\chapter{Advanced Cognitive Modeling
Notes}\label{advanced-cognitive-modeling-notes}

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Advanced Cognitive Modeling
Notes}\label{advanced-cognitive-modeling-notes-1}

These course notes support the Advanced Cognitive Modeling course taught
in the Master's program in Cognitive Science at Aarhus University. The
course represents a journey into how we can understand cognitive
processes through the formalization and implementation of hypothesized
mechanisms, their testing and validation.

\section{Course Philosophy and
Approach}\label{course-philosophy-and-approach}

Advanced cognitive modeling focuses on three interrelated objectives
that shape how we approach the modeling of cognitive processes:

The first objective centers on understanding the thought process behind
model development. Rather than simply providing a toolbox of existing
scripts, we explore how cognitive models are conceptualized and
constructed from the ground up. This approach ensures you develop the
skills to create novel models for unique research questions.

The second objective emphasizes mastering the Bayesian workflow
essential for robust model development. This workflow encompasses
simulation design, prior assessment, parameter recovery testing, and
thorough model fit evaluation. These skills ensure your models are not
just theoretically sound but also practically reliable and generalize
way beyond cognitive modeling.

The third objective focuses on developing advanced probabilistic
modeling capabilities. Through hands-on experience with Stan, you will
learn to implement increasingly sophisticated models while maintaining
scientific rigor.

\section{Course Structure and Learning
Path}\label{course-structure-and-learning-path}

The course follows a carefully structured progression that builds your
modeling capabilities step by step:

After a deepdive into the physics of pizza ovens, we begin with simple
scenarios that introduce fundamental modeling concepts. Each subsequent
chapter introduces new modeling techniques while building upon previous
knowledge. This cumulative approach ensures you develop a deep
understanding of both basic principles and advanced applications.

The chapters include theoretical discussions paired with practical
coding exercises. During practical sessions, we work with real datasets,
design models collaboratively, and implement them using modern
statistical tools. This hands-on approach provides ample opportunity for
questions and exploration.

The course schedule maintains flexibility to adapt to the collective
learning pace of each cohort. While we have clear learning objectives,
we ensure everyone develops a solid foundation before moving to more
advanced topics.

\section{Prerequisites and
Preparation}\label{prerequisites-and-preparation}

To make the most of this course, students should prepare their technical
environment and review fundamental concepts:

Software Requirements: - R (version 4.4 or above) - RStudio (version
2024.12.0 or above) - brms package with proper configuration - cmdstanr
package with complete installation

Technical Prerequisites: - Working knowledge of R programming - Basic
understanding of Bayesian statistics - Familiarity with cognitive
science fundamentals

Additional Resources: - Introduction to R and tidyverse:
https://r4ds.had.co.nz/ - A condensed Bayesian statistics primer (by
Chris Cox and me): https://4ccoxau.github.io/PriorsWorkshop/ - A
condensed primer on all my wisdom on priors:
https://youtu.be/kMSrRd4f2As

\section{Course Resources}\label{course-resources}

The course materials include: - Lecture notes and presentations -
Practical exercise guides - Example code and solutions - Additional
readings and references

For comprehensive information: - Course syllabus: {[}TBA{]} - Lecture
videos:
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL\_f3yDs5oZx5Ff-rRIZtghpE2tPfyAzMk

\section{About These Notes}\label{about-these-notes}

These notes represent an evolving resource that builds upon previous
iterations of the course while incorporating new developments in the
field. They are designed to serve both as a learning guide during the
course and as a reference for your future research endeavors.

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{The Pizza Experiment}\label{the-pizza-experiment}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} In these
course notes we will introduce and consistently use a Bayesian Workflow
Before we model cognition, we need to understand what modeling \emph{is}
--- building a mechanism-based account of a system, fitting it to data,
and validating it. Every move we make here (theory → math → code →
fitting → checking) will repeat in every chapter that follows, now
applied to minds instead of pizza stones. The full course roadmap is in
the section below.
\end{quote}

\section{From Pizza to Cognitive Models: An
Introduction}\label{from-pizza-to-cognitive-models-an-introduction}

This chapter introduces core modeling concepts through a delicious lens:
the physics of pizza stone heating. While this might seem far removed
from cognitive science, it provides an insightful introduction to the
challenges and methodologies of modeling complex phenomena.

\section{Why Start with Pizza?}\label{why-start-with-pizza}

Do I even need to answer that question? Because pizza, obviously.

Beyond the universal appeal of pizza, starting with a physical system
like a heating pizza stone offers a tangible way to grasp core
principles fundamental to cognitive modeling. Understanding human
cognition involves tackling immense complexity -- often dealing with
hidden mental states, noisy behavioral data, and competing theories.
Modeling a pizza stone allows us to isolate and understand key
methodological challenges in a more constrained environment.

First, it demonstrates how to bridge theory and math. We rely on
\emph{Prior Knowledge} --- using Newton's Law of Cooling to structure
our equations --- just as cognitive scientists use theories of memory or
attention to guide their models. This forces us to critically determine
our \emph{Levels of Analysis}: just as we simplify complex
thermodynamics into a basic cooling formula, cognitive scientists must
decide whether to model neurons, beliefs, or behavioral choices.

It also highlights the problem of \emph{Latent Variables}. Just as we
cannot ``see'' a heating coefficient---only the noisy temperatures it
produces---we cannot see a ``learning rate'' in the brain, only the
choices it drives.

Finally, this example acts as a sandbox for \emph{Model Complexity} and
\emph{Validation}. We will explicitly compare detailed physics-based
models against simpler statistical approximations to see which performs
better on empirical data. This mirrors the central trade-off in
cognitive science: balancing theoretical completeness with the practical
utility of generating testable predictions from available data.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives}

This chapter uses the seemingly simple example of a heating pizza stone
to introduce fundamental principles of computational modeling that are
directly applicable to cognitive science. While the specific physics and
code are illustrative, the core goals are to understand the
\emph{process} of modeling.

By completing this chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Appreciate Generative Modeling:} Understand the value of
  building models based on underlying mechanisms (like physics or
  cognitive processes) rather than just describing data patterns.
\item
  \textbf{Connect Theory and Data:} See how theoretical knowledge
  (physics laws) informs model structure and how data is used to
  estimate unknown model parameters (like the heating coefficient).
\item
  \textbf{Implement and Fit Models:} Gain initial experience
  implementing mathematical models in code (R and Stan) and using
  Bayesian inference (\texttt{brms}, \texttt{cmdstanr}) to fit them to
  empirical data.
\item
  \textbf{Evaluate Model Fit:} Learn basic techniques for visualizing
  model predictions against data and understand the limitations of
  purely statistical models compared to theory-driven ones.
\item
  \textbf{Recognize Modeling Trade-offs:} Understand the balance between
  model complexity, theoretical grounding, and practical utility through
  comparing statistical vs.~physics-based approaches.
\item
  \textbf{Apply Modeling Principles:} Recognize how the steps taken here
  -- data exploration, model building, parameter estimation, validation
  -- form a general workflow applicable to cognitive modeling problems
  explored later in the course.
\end{itemize}

Oh, and you'll probably get hungry as well!

Required Packages

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Ensure \textquotesingle{}pacman\textquotesingle{} is installed}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{require}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"pacman"}\NormalTok{)) }\FunctionTok{install.packages}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"pacman"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Load packages (pacman will verify, install if missing, and load them)}
\NormalTok{pacman}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{p\_load}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  tidyverse,  }\CommentTok{\# Core package for data manipulation and visualization}
\NormalTok{  brms,       }\CommentTok{\# Interface for Bayesian regression models using Stan}
\NormalTok{  bayesplot,  }\CommentTok{\# Convenient functions for plotting Bayesian model results}
\NormalTok{  tidybayes,  }\CommentTok{\# Utilities for working with Bayesian model outputs in a tidy format}
\NormalTok{  cmdstanr,    }\CommentTok{\# Backend interface for running Stan models efficiently}
\NormalTok{  here        }\CommentTok{\# For constructing file paths relative to project root}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Set to TRUE to rerun everything (takes time!), FALSE to load saved results.}
\NormalTok{regenerate\_simulations }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{FALSE} 
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Part 1: Exploring the Pizza Stone Temperature
Data}\label{part-1-exploring-the-pizza-stone-temperature-data}

In this study, we collected temperature measurements from a pizza stone
in a gas-fired oven using an infrared temperature gun. Three different
raters (N, TR, and R) took measurements over time to track how the stone
heated up. Understanding how pizza stones heat up is crucial for
achieving the perfect pizza crust, as consistent and sufficient stone
temperature is essential for proper baking.

The measurements were taken as follows:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Load and examine the data}
\NormalTok{data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Order =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{18}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Seconds =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{175}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{278}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{333}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{443}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{568}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{731}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{773}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{851}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{912}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{980}\NormalTok{, }
                  \DecValTok{1040}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1074}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1124}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1175}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1237}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1298}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1359}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1394}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Temperature =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{15.1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{233}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{244}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{280}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{289}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{304}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{343}\NormalTok{, }\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{333}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{341}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{320}\NormalTok{, }
                  \DecValTok{370}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{325}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{362}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{363}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{357}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{380}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{376}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{380}\NormalTok{,}
                  \FloatTok{14.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{139.9}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{153}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{36.1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{254}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{459}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{263}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{369}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{11}\NormalTok{),}
                  \FloatTok{12.9}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{149.5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{159}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{179.4}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{191.7}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{201}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{210}\NormalTok{, }\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{256}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{257}\NormalTok{, }
                  \DecValTok{281}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{293}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{297}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{309}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{318}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{321}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)),}
  \AttributeTok{Rater =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"N"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"TR"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"R"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{each =} \DecValTok{19}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}



\CommentTok{\# Create summary statistics}
\NormalTok{summary\_stats }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(Rater) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{summarize}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_measurements =} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(Temperature)),}
    \AttributeTok{mean\_temp =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(Temperature, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{sd\_temp =} \FunctionTok{sd}\NormalTok{(Temperature, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{min\_temp =} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(Temperature, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{max\_temp =} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(Temperature, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Display summary statistics}
\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(summary\_stats, }\AttributeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrrrr@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
Rater & n\_measurements & mean\_temp & sd\_temp & min\_temp &
max\_temp \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
N & 18 & 312.0 & 86.4 & 15.1 & 380 \\
R & 15 & 229.0 & 83.9 & 12.9 & 321 \\
TR & 8 & 211.1 & 155.2 & 14.5 & 459 \\

\subsection{Initial Data
Visualization}\label{initial-data-visualization}

Let's visualize how the temperature evolves over time for each rater:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Temperature, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ Rater)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Pizza Stone Temperature Evolution"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Measurements by three different raters"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Time (minutes)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Temperature (°C)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Rater"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_visualize_data-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Key Observations}\label{key-observations}

Several interesting patterns emerge from our data:

\emph{Heating Patterns}: The temperature generally increases over time,
but not uniformly. We observe some fluctuations that might be due to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Variation in gas flame intensity
\item
  Different measurement locations on the stone
\item
  Measurement technique differences between raters
\end{itemize}

\emph{Measurement Patterns by Rater}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Rater N maintained consistent measurements throughout the experiment
\item
  Rater TR shows more variability and fewer total measurements
\item
  Rater R shows a more gradual temperature increase pattern
\end{itemize}

\emph{Missing Data}: Some measurements are missing (NA values),
particularly in the later time points for Rater TR. This is common in
real-world data collection and needs to be considered in our analysis.

Let's examine the rate of temperature change:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Calculate Rate of Temperature Change {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# To understand how quickly the stone heats up, calculate the change rate per minute.}
\NormalTok{data\_with\_rate }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(Rater) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \CommentTok{\# Perform calculation separately for each rater}
  \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(Seconds) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \CommentTok{\# Ensure data is sorted chronologically for lag function}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Calculate difference in temperature from previous measurement}
    \AttributeTok{delta\_temp =}\NormalTok{ Temperature }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{lag}\NormalTok{(Temperature),}
    \CommentTok{\# Calculate time elapsed since previous measurement}
    \AttributeTok{delta\_secs =}\NormalTok{ Seconds }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{lag}\NormalTok{(Seconds),}
    \CommentTok{\# Calculate rate in degrees C per second, then convert to degrees C per minute}
    \AttributeTok{temp\_change\_per\_min =}\NormalTok{ (delta\_temp }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ delta\_secs) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{,}
    \CommentTok{\# Convert time to minutes for plotting}
    \AttributeTok{minutes =}\NormalTok{ Seconds }\SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{60}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \CommentTok{\# Remove rows where rate couldn\textquotesingle{}t be calculated (i.e., the first row for each rater)}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(temp\_change\_per\_min))}

\CommentTok{\# Visualize temperature change rate}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(data\_with\_rate, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ minutes, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ temp\_change\_per\_min, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ Rater)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Rate of Temperature Change Over Time"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Degrees Celsius per minute"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Time (minutes)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Temperature Change Rate (°C/min)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Rater"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_temp_change_rate-1.pdf}
\end{center}

This visualization reveals that the heating rate is (likely) highest in
the first few minutes and gradually decreases as the stone temperature
approaches the oven temperature. This aligns with Newton's Law of
Cooling/Heating, which we will explore in the next section.

\section{Part 2: Initial Statistical
Modeling}\label{part-2-initial-statistical-modeling}

Before developing our physics-based model, let's explore how standard
statistical approaches perform in modeling our temperature data. We'll
implement two types of models using the brms package: a linear
mixed-effects model and a lognormal mixed-effects model. Both models
will account for variations between raters.

\subsection{Model Setup and Priors}\label{model-setup-and-priors}

First, let's ensure we have a directory for our models and set up our
computational parameters:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Model Fitting Setup {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Define relative paths for saving model objects and Stan code.}
\NormalTok{linear\_model\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch02\_pizza\_linear\_model"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{lognormal\_model\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch02\_pizza\_lognormal\_model"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{stan\_model\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch02\_pizza\_physics.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Create the required directories if they don\textquotesingle{}t already exist}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Define computational parameters}
\NormalTok{mc\_settings }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter =} \DecValTok{6000}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{backend =} \StringTok{"cmdstanr"}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Linear Mixed-Effects
Model}\label{linear-mixed-effects-model}

We begin with a linear mixed-effects model, which assumes that
temperature increases linearly with time but allows for different
patterns across raters. This model includes both fixed effects (overall
time trend) and random effects (rater-specific variations).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{data\_scaled }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Minutes =}\NormalTok{ Seconds }\SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Define priors for linear model}
\NormalTok{linear\_priors }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"Intercept"}\NormalTok{),  }\CommentTok{\# Centered around room temperature}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"b"}\NormalTok{),          }\CommentTok{\# Expected temperature change per minute}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"sigma"}\NormalTok{),         }\CommentTok{\# Residual variation}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"sd"}\NormalTok{),            }\CommentTok{\# Random effects variation}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{lkj}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"cor"}\NormalTok{)                    }\CommentTok{\# Random effects correlation}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Linear Model {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{linear\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{brm}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  Temperature }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ Minutes }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Minutes }\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{ Rater),}
  \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_scaled,}
  \AttributeTok{family =}\NormalTok{ gaussian,}
  \AttributeTok{prior =}\NormalTok{ linear\_priors,}
  \AttributeTok{chains =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{chains,}
  \AttributeTok{iter =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{iter,}
  \AttributeTok{refresh =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{refresh,}
  \AttributeTok{silent =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{seed, }\CommentTok{\# Ensures reproducibility of MCMC sampling}
  \AttributeTok{backend =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{backend,}
  \AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ linear\_model\_path, }\CommentTok{\# Use relative path}
  \AttributeTok{file\_refit =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(regenerate\_simulations, }\StringTok{"always"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"on\_change"}\NormalTok{), }\CommentTok{\# Refit if model code or data changes}
  \AttributeTok{cores =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\CommentTok{\# Specify cores used for parallel chains}
  \CommentTok{\# Increased adapt\_delta and max\_treedepth help prevent divergent transitions}
  \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{20}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Display model summary}
\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(linear\_model)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
 Family: gaussian 
  Links: mu = identity 
Formula: Temperature ~ Minutes + (1 + Minutes | Rater) 
   Data: data_scaled (Number of observations: 41) 
  Draws: 2 chains, each with iter = 6000; warmup = 3000; thin = 1;
         total post-warmup draws = 6000

Multilevel Hyperparameters:
~Rater (Number of levels: 3) 
                       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS
sd(Intercept)             57.01     28.21     7.42   120.86 1.00     2014
sd(Minutes)               31.39     15.76    11.83    71.91 1.00     1655
cor(Intercept,Minutes)     0.00      0.35    -0.65     0.65 1.00     2075
                       Tail_ESS
sd(Intercept)              1104
sd(Minutes)                2789
cor(Intercept,Minutes)     3209

Regression Coefficients:
          Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
Intercept    84.92     45.64   -13.93   166.45 1.00     1809     2567
Minutes      -5.54      4.21   -13.23     3.41 1.00     1816     2506

Further Distributional Parameters:
      Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
sigma    57.45      6.87    45.63    72.47 1.00     3734     3283

Draws were sampled using sample(hmc). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Generate predictions}
\NormalTok{linear\_preds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{fitted}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  linear\_model,}
  \AttributeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ data\_scaled,}
  \AttributeTok{probs =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.025}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.975}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{bind\_cols}\NormalTok{(data)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Lognormal Mixed-Effects
Model}\label{lognormal-mixed-effects-model}

The lognormal model accounts for the fact that temperature changes might
be proportional rather than additive, and ensures predictions cannot go
below zero (I don't bring my oven out in the freezing cold!).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Define priors for lognormal model}
\NormalTok{lognormal\_priors }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.7}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"Intercept"}\NormalTok{),     }\CommentTok{\# Log scale for room temperature}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"b"}\NormalTok{),     }\CommentTok{\# Expected log{-}scale change per minute}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"sigma"}\NormalTok{),        }\CommentTok{\# Log{-}scale residual variation}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{normal}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"sd"}\NormalTok{),           }\CommentTok{\# Random effects variation}
  \FunctionTok{prior}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{lkj}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{class =} \StringTok{"cor"}\NormalTok{)                   }\CommentTok{\# Random effects correlation}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Lognormal Model {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{lognormal\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{brm}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  Temperature }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ Minutes }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Minutes }\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{ Rater),}
  \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_scaled,}
  \AttributeTok{family =}\NormalTok{ lognormal, }\CommentTok{\# Use lognormal distribution}
  \AttributeTok{prior =}\NormalTok{ lognormal\_priors,}
  \AttributeTok{chains =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{chains,}
  \AttributeTok{cores =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{iter,}
  \AttributeTok{refresh =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{refresh,}
  \AttributeTok{silent =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{seed, }\CommentTok{\# Use the same seed for comparison if desired}
  \AttributeTok{backend =}\NormalTok{ mc\_settings}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{backend,}
  \AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ lognormal\_model\_path, }\CommentTok{\# Use relative path}
  \AttributeTok{file\_refit =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(regenerate\_simulations, }\StringTok{"always"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"on\_change"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Generate predictions}
\NormalTok{lognormal\_preds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{fitted}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  lognormal\_model,}
  \AttributeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ data\_scaled,}
  \AttributeTok{probs =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.025}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.975}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{bind\_cols}\NormalTok{(data)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Model Comparison and
Visualization}\label{model-comparison-and-visualization}

Let's compare how these models fit our data:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Compare models using LOO}
\NormalTok{model\_comparison }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{loo\_compare}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{(linear\_model),}
  \FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{(lognormal\_model)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Create comparison plot}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Raw data points}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data, }
            \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Temperature, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ Rater),}
            \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Linear model predictions}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ linear\_preds,}
            \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Estimate, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Linear"}\NormalTok{),}
            \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ linear\_preds,}
              \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ Q2}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ Q97}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Lognormal model predictions}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ lognormal\_preds,}
            \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Estimate, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Lognormal"}\NormalTok{),}
            \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ lognormal\_preds,}
              \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ Q2}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ Q97}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Formatting}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Rater) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Comparison of Statistical Models"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Linear vs Lognormal Mixed{-}Effects Models"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Time (minutes)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Temperature (°C)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Model Type"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_model_comparison-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Create comparison plot but capping the y axis}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Raw data points}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data, }
            \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Temperature, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ Rater),}
            \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Linear model predictions}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ linear\_preds,}
            \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Estimate, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Linear"}\NormalTok{),}
            \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ linear\_preds,}
              \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ Q2}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ Q97}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Lognormal model predictions}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ lognormal\_preds,}
            \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Estimate, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Lognormal"}\NormalTok{),}
            \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ lognormal\_preds,}
              \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ Q2}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ Q97}\FloatTok{.5}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Formatting}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Rater) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Comparison of Statistical Models"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Linear vs Lognormal Mixed{-}Effects Models"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Time (minutes)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Temperature (°C)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Model Type"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_model_comparison-2.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Model Assessment}\label{model-assessment}

I have seen worse models in my time, but they do seem to have important
issues:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The linear mixed-effects model assumes a constant rate of temperature
  change, which we can see is not at all accurate. The actual
  temperature increase is fast at the beginning and appears to slow down
  over time, particularly at higher temperatures. While this model has
  the advantage of simplicity, it is not likely to produce accurate
  predictions as it seem to fail to capture the underlying physics of
  heat transfer.
\item
  The lognormal mixed-effects model is completely off.
\end{itemize}

Further, the lognormal model produce some divergences. In a Bayesian
workflow, divergences indicate that the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampler
is exploring pathological posterior geometry. Because purely statistical
models lack mechanistic asymptotes, they explore parameter spaces that
lead to mathematically unstable extrapolations. The core issue is that
neither model incorporates our knowledge of heat transfer physics: e.g
the flame temperature limiting the temperature the stone can reach and
the exponential approach to equilibrium temperature. This limitation
motivates our next section, where we'll develop a physics-based model.

\section{Part 3: Understanding the Physics
Model}\label{part-3-understanding-the-physics-model}

Temperature evolution in a pizza stone follows Newton's Law of
Cooling/Heating. We'll start by exploring this physical model before
applying it to real data.

\subsection{The Basic Temperature Evolution
Equation}\label{the-basic-temperature-evolution-equation}

The temperature evolution of a pizza stone in a gas-fired oven is
governed by the heat diffusion equation, which describes how heat flows
through solid materials:

\[\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k\nabla^2T + Q\]

where: \(\rho\) represents the stone's density (kg/m³) \(c_p\) denotes
specific heat capacity (J/kg·K) \(T\) is temperature (K) \(t\)
represents time (s) \(k\) is thermal conductivity (W/m·K) \(\nabla^2\)
is the Laplacian operator \(Q\) represents heat input from the oven
(W/m³)

While this equation provides a complete description of heat flow, we can
significantly simplify our analysis by applying the lumped capacitance
model. his simplification assumes that the stone conducts heat
internally much faster than it absorbs it from the surface. This allows
us to ignore the complex internal gradients (\(\nabla^2T\)) and focus
purely on the surface heat transfer, reducing our model to:

\[\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{hA}{mc_p}(T_{\infty} - T)\]

where: \(h\) is the heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) \(A\) is the
surface area exposed to heat (m²) \(m\) is the stone's mass (kg)
\(T_{\infty}\) is the oven temperature (K)

This simplified equation relates the rate of temperature change to the
difference between the current stone temperature T and the ambient
temperature T∞ (as determined by the flame). The coefficient h
represents the heat transfer coefficient between the ambient and stone,
A is the stone's surface area exposed to heat, m is its mass, and cp
remains the specific heat capacity.

To solve this differential equation, we begin by separating variables:

\[\frac{dT}{T_{\infty} - T} = \left(\frac{hA}{mc_p}\right)dt\]

Integration of both sides yields:

\[-\ln|T_{\infty} - T| = \left(\frac{hA}{mc_p}\right)t + C\]

where C is an integration constant.

Using the initial condition \(T = T_i\) at \(t = 0\), we can determine
the integration constant:

\[C = -\ln|T_{\infty} - T_i|\]

Substituting this back and solving for temperature gives us:

\[T = T_{\infty} + (T_i - T_{\infty})\exp\left(-\frac{hA}{mc_p}t\right)\]

For practical reasons, we combine physical parameters into a single
coefficient, which we will call HOT:

\[HOT = \frac{hA}{mc_p}\] Giving our working equation:
\[T = T_{\infty} + (T_i - T_{\infty})\exp(-HOT \cdot t)\]

This equation retains the essential physics while providing a practical
model for analyzing our experimental data. The HOT coefficient
encapsulates the combined effects of heat transfer efficiency, stone
geometry, and material properties into a single parameter that
determines how quickly the stone approaches the flame temperature.

\section{Part 4: Implementing the Physics-Based
Model}\label{part-4-implementing-the-physics-based-model}

Having established the theoretical foundation, we now face a practical
challenge: we cannot measure \(h\), \(A\), or \(c_p\) directly during
the bake. Instead, we must infer the aggregate parameter HOT from the
noisy temperature data collected by our raters. This is where Stan comes
in, enabling us to create a Bayesian implementation of our physics-based
model, and to account for measurement uncertainty and variation between
raters. First, we prepare our data for the Stan model. Our model
requires initial temperatures, time measurements, and observed
temperatures from each rater:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Create data structure for Stan}
\NormalTok{stan\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(Temperature))),}
  \AttributeTok{time =}\NormalTok{ data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(Temperature)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{pull}\NormalTok{(Seconds),}
  \AttributeTok{temp =}\NormalTok{ data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(Temperature)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{pull}\NormalTok{(Temperature),}
  \AttributeTok{n\_raters =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{rater =} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} 
                           \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(Temperature)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} 
                           \FunctionTok{pull}\NormalTok{(Rater))),}
  \AttributeTok{Ti =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{15.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{14.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{12.9}\NormalTok{),  }\CommentTok{\# Initial temperature estimates}
  \AttributeTok{Tinf =} \DecValTok{450}              \CommentTok{\# Flame temperature estimate}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Next, we implement our physics-based model in Stan. The model
incorporates our derived equation while allowing for rater-specific
heating coefficients:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_code }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} N;                   // Number of observations}
\StringTok{  vector[N] time;                   // Time points}
\StringTok{  vector[N] temp;                   // Observed temperatures}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} n\_raters;           // Number of raters}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=1,upper=n\_raters\textgreater{} rater;  // Rater indices}
\StringTok{  vector[n\_raters] Ti;             // Initial temperatures}
\StringTok{  real Tinf;                       // Flame temperature}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}[n\_raters] HOT;   // Heating coefficients}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}[n\_raters] sigma; // Measurement error}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Physics{-}based temperature prediction}
\StringTok{  vector[N] mu = Tinf + (Ti[rater] {-} Tinf) .* exp({-}HOT[rater] .* time);}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  // Prior distributions}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(HOT | 0.005, 0.005);    // Prior for heating rate}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma | 1);         // Prior for measurement error}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  // Likelihood}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(temp | mu, sigma[rater]);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\CommentTok{\# Save the Stan model code using the relative path}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_code, stan\_model\_path)}

\CommentTok{\# Compile and fit the Stan model using the relative path}
\CommentTok{\# Compilation happens once; subsequent runs load the compiled object if unchanged.}
\NormalTok{mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan/ch02\_pizza\_physics.stan"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{quiet =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Prior Predictive Check}\label{prior-predictive-check}

Before fitting our model to empirical data, we must ensure our priors
generate physically plausible heating curves (Simulation Validation). If
our \texttt{normal(0.005,\ 0.005)} prior on \texttt{HOT} is appropriate,
simulated temperatures should gradually approach \(T_{\infty}\) without
exceeding it or dropping below absolute zero.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Simulate HOT parameters from the prior}
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{prior\_hot }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{mean =} \FloatTok{0.005}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{sd =} \FloatTok{0.005}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{prior\_hot }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prior\_hot[prior\_hot }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] }\CommentTok{\# Truncate at 0 as per Stan lower=0 bound}

\CommentTok{\# Simulate curves}
\NormalTok{time\_seq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{time), }\AttributeTok{length.out =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{prior\_preds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sim\_id =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(prior\_hot), }\AttributeTok{time =}\NormalTok{ time\_seq) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{hot =}\NormalTok{ prior\_hot[sim\_id],}
    \AttributeTok{temp =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Tinf }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{15.0} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Tinf) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{hot }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ time)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_preds, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ time}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ temp, }\AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ sim\_id)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior Predictive Check: Heating Curves"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Time (minutes)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Temperature (°C)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_prior_predictive-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit\_physics\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch02\_pizza\_physics\_fit.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(fit\_physics\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{show\_messages =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(fit\_physics\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(fit\_physics\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The Stan implementation translates our mathematical model into a
computational framework. We assign informative priors to our parameters
based on physical understanding: the heating coefficient (HOT) is
expected to be small but positive, while measurement error (sigma)
follows an exponential distribution to ensure positivity while allowing
for varying levels of uncertainty between raters. To visualize our
model's predictions and assess its performance, we extract posterior
samples and generate predictions across our time range:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract draws}
\NormalTok{post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"HOT"}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"sigma"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{slice\_sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Create prediction grid}
\NormalTok{pred\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ tidyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{crossing}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{time =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{time), }\AttributeTok{length.out =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{rater =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_raters}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{Ti =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Ti[rater],}
    \AttributeTok{Tinf =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Tinf}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Generate predictions}
\NormalTok{pred\_matrix }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(pred\_data), }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(pred\_data)) \{}
\NormalTok{  pred\_matrix[i,] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{with}\NormalTok{(pred\_data[i,], }
\NormalTok{                         Tinf }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (Ti }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ Tinf) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(post)[,rater] }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ time))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Summarize predictions}
\NormalTok{predictions }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ pred\_data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mean =} \FunctionTok{rowMeans}\NormalTok{(pred\_matrix),}
    \AttributeTok{lower =} \FunctionTok{apply}\NormalTok{(pred\_matrix, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, quantile, }\FloatTok{0.025}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{upper =} \FunctionTok{apply}\NormalTok{(pred\_matrix, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, quantile, }\FloatTok{0.975}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Create visualization}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(predictions, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ time}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ lower, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ upper), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} 
      \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(Temperature)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
      \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{rater =} \FunctionTok{case\_when}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{        Rater }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"N"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{        Rater }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"TR"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{        Rater }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"R"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \DecValTok{3}
\NormalTok{      )), }
    \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Seconds}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Temperature)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{rater, }\AttributeTok{labeller =} \FunctionTok{labeller}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{rater =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
    \StringTok{"1"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Rater N"}\NormalTok{, }
    \StringTok{"2"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Rater TR"}\NormalTok{, }
    \StringTok{"3"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Rater R"}
\NormalTok{  ))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Physics{-}Based Model Predictions"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Time (minutes)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Temperature (°C)"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_physics_model_preds-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Our implementation combines the theoretical understanding developed in
Part 3 with practical considerations for real-world data analysis. The
model accounts for measurement uncertainty while maintaining the
fundamental physics of heat transfer, providing a robust framework for
understanding pizza stone temperature evolution.

\section{Part 5: Model Analysis and Practical
Applications}\label{part-5-model-analysis-and-practical-applications}

Having fitted our physics-based model, we must first verify that it
actually describes the data well (Validation) before we can use it to
make predictions. Once we trust our estimates of the HOT parameter, we
can develop practical insights for pizza stone management. A key
question for any pizzaiolo is: How long must I wait before the stone is
ready? To answer this, we don't need a new model; we simply need to look
at our physics equation from a different angle. By rearranging our
original cooling law to solve for time (\(t\)) instead of temperature
(\(T\)), we create a predictive tool:

\[t = -\frac{1}{HOT} \ln\left( \frac{T_{\text{target}} - T_{\infty}}{T_{\text{initial}} - T_{\infty}} \right)\]

We will now implement this function in R to calculate precise waiting
times under different conditions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{time\_to\_temp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(target\_temp, HOT, Ti, Tinf) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Solve: target = Tinf + (Ti {-} Tinf) * exp({-}HOT * t)}
  \CommentTok{\# for t}
\NormalTok{  t }\OtherTok{=} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{HOT }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((target\_temp }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ Tinf)}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{(Ti }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ Tinf))}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(t}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{)  }\CommentTok{\# Convert seconds to minutes}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

To understand heating times across different oven conditions, we examine
how varying flame temperatures affect the time needed to reach
pizza-making temperatures. We extract the heating coefficients from our
fitted model and analyze temperature scenarios:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract HOT samples from our posterior}
\NormalTok{hot\_samples }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"HOT"}\NormalTok{))}

\CommentTok{\# Create prediction grid for different flame temperatures}
\NormalTok{pred\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ tidyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{crossing}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Tinf =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{450}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1200}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{),  }\CommentTok{\# Range of flame temperatures}
  \AttributeTok{rater =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{3}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{Ti =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Ti[rater],}
    \AttributeTok{target\_temp =} \DecValTok{400}  \CommentTok{\# Target temperature for pizza cooking}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Calculate heating times across conditions}
\NormalTok{n\_samples }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{100}
\NormalTok{time\_preds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(pred\_data), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) \{}
\NormalTok{  times }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_samples, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(j) \{}
\NormalTok{    hot }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ hot\_samples[j, }\FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"HOT["}\NormalTok{, pred\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rater[i], }\StringTok{"]"}\NormalTok{)][[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]]}
    \FunctionTok{time\_to\_temp}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{      pred\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{target\_temp[i], }
\NormalTok{      hot, }
\NormalTok{      pred\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Ti[i], }
\NormalTok{      pred\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Tinf[i]}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  \})}
  
  \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{rater =}\NormalTok{ pred\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rater[i],}
    \AttributeTok{Tinf =}\NormalTok{ pred\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Tinf[i],}
    \AttributeTok{mean\_time =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(times),}
    \AttributeTok{lower =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(times, }\FloatTok{0.025}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{upper =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(times, }\FloatTok{0.975}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\})}

\CommentTok{\# Visualize heating time predictions}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(time\_preds, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Tinf)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ lower, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ upper), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mean\_time)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{rater, }\AttributeTok{labeller =} \FunctionTok{labeller}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{rater =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
    \StringTok{"1"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Rater N"}\NormalTok{, }
    \StringTok{"2"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Rater TR"}\NormalTok{, }
    \StringTok{"3"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Rater R"}
\NormalTok{  ))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Time Required to Reach Pizza{-}Making Temperature"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Target temperature: 400°C"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Flame Temperature (°C)"}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Minutes to reach target"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_bw}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{01-PizzaExperiment_files/figure-pdf/ch02_heating_time_preds-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Our analysis reveals several important insights for practical pizza
making. First, the heating time decreases non-linearly with flame
temperature, showing diminishing returns at very high temperatures. We
also observed differences between raters in their measured heating
times. These variations likely stem from differences in measurement
technique and location on the stone, highlighting the importance of
consistent temperature monitoring practices. E.g. N measure the
temperature at the boundary between flame and stone, which matches the
model's assumptions better than R, who measures at the center of the
stone.

For practical application, we can provide specific heating guidelines
based on our model. At a typical flame temperature of 800°C, the model
predicts it will take approximately 20-30 minutes to reach optimal
pizza-making temperature, assuming room temperature start. However, this
time can vary significantly based on:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Initial stone temperature
\item
  Flame temperature and consistency
\item
  Environmental conditions.
\end{itemize}

However, we should also consider that the model best fits the
measurements from Rater N, who measured at the boundary between flame
and stone. This suggests that our predictions are most applicable to the
border of the stone, and less to the center (measured by R). Yet, it is
the center of the stone that is a better index of the temperature all of
our pizza will receive. Therefore we should add a few minutes to the
model estimates to ensure best real-world outcomes.

Can we really wait that long? Well, there are no shortcuts to the
perfect crust! Just like there is no shortcut to understanding human
cognition, and models need always to be critically contextualized in the
real world.

\section{Conclusion: From Pizza to Cognitive
Principles}\label{conclusion-from-pizza-to-cognitive-principles}

The journey from modeling a heating pizza stone to understanding
cognitive processes might seem unusual, but it illustrates fundamental
principles that will guide us throughout this course.

Through this seemingly simple physics problem, we have encountered core
challenges faced daily by cognitive modelers:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Levels of Analysis}: We saw how simplifying complex equations
  (heat diffusion) mirrors the need to choose the right level of
  abstraction when modeling cognition (e.g., neural vs.~behavioral).
\item
  \emph{Prior Knowledge}: We used physics laws to build a generative
  model, much like cognitive theories guide model development.
\item
  \emph{Validation}: We confronted the necessity of comparing model
  predictions to real data, a process central to testing theories.
\item
  \emph{Parameter Estimation}: We used the model to quantify an
  unobservable property (HOT) from noisy data, analogous to estimating
  latent cognitive parameters like learning rates or decision
  thresholds.
\end{itemize}

Crucially, the comparison between purely statistical models
(linear/lognormal) and the physics-based Stan model highlighted the
value of mechanistic understanding. While the statistical models
struggled to capture the saturation curve --- often predicting
impossible temperatures via extrapolation --- the physics model offered
deeper insight and robust predictions. This is a primary goal in
cognitive modeling: we do not simply to describe \emph{which} decisions
were made, but we want to capture the underlying mechanism that can
increase our understanding of \emph{how} decisions were made, and
generalize to novel unseen decisions.

The pizza stone experiment also demonstrated the importance of rigorous
methodology. We saw how multiple measurements from different raters
revealed variability in our data, leading us to consider models
assumptions, measurement error and individual differences---themes that
will become crucial when studying human behavior.

\section{The Course Roadmap}\label{the-course-roadmap}

As we progress through the course, the principles from this chapter will
resurface at every step: a generative model driven by theory (Ch. 3),
parameter estimation via Bayesian inference (Ch. 5), validation of that
inference (Ch. 6), and scaling to many participants (Ch. 7). The pizza
stone is not a detour --- it is the template.

Every chapter is one step in a single pipeline {[}NOW COVERING UP TO
MODEL COMPARISON. MORE TO COME{]}. Use this table to orient yourself
whenever you feel lost.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2812}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.1875}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5312}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Chapter
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Step
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Central question
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{1 --- Pizza Experiment} & Why model? & What is a generative
model and what does it mean to fit one to data? \\
\textbf{2 --- Building Models} & Observe \& theorize & What behaviors do
we see, and what verbal strategies could explain them? \\
\textbf{3 --- Formal Models} & Formalize \& simulate & Can we turn a
verbal strategy into code that \emph{generates} realistic data? \\
\textbf{4 --- Inferring Rates} & Fit a single dataset & Given observed
data, can we infer the parameters that produced it? \\
\textbf{5 --- Model Quality} & Validate the engine & Are our parameter
estimates actually trustworthy? (Prior checks, recovery, SBC, posterior
checks) \\
\textbf{6 --- Multilevel Models} & Scale to populations & How do
individuals differ, and how do we pool information across them? \\
\textbf{7 --- Model Comparison} & Choose between theories & Of several
plausible models, which one best predicts unseen data? \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{The key insight:} steps 1--3 build models; steps 4--7 test them.
A model only earns the right to touch real human data after passing the
validation battery in Chapter 6.

\textbf{Coming up in Chapter 2:} We shift focus from stones to minds.
You have already played the Matching Pennies game in class. Chapter 2
helps you translate what you observed --- your own strategies, your
opponent's patterns --- into candidate verbal models that Chapter 3 will
then formalize in code.

I hope this has whetted your appetite -- both for pizza and for
computational modeling!

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Building Models of Strategic
Decision-Making}\label{building-models-of-strategic-decision-making}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Ch. 1
established \emph{what} modeling means. Now we need something to model.
This chapter gives us our cognitive task (Matching Pennies) and has us
build theories \emph{in words} --- no code yet. These verbal models are
the raw material that Ch. 3 translates into simulatable algorithms and
Ch. 5 fits to real data.
\end{quote}

You've just played Matching Pennies and discussed strategies with your
classmates. You probably noticed patterns in your opponent's play, tried
to be unpredictable, and maybe even changed your strategy mid-game. This
chapter helps you translate those observations and intuitions into the
language of cognitive modeling---preparing you to implement formal
models in Chapter 3.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-1}

This chapter bridges the gap between observing behavior and developing
testable theories. By the end of this chapter, using the Matching
Pennies game as a case study, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Identify Key Modeling Steps:} Understand the process of moving
  from behavioral observations and participant reflections to
  formulating initial verbal theories of underlying cognitive
  strategies.
\item
  \textbf{Appreciate Theory Building Challenges:} Recognize common
  issues in theory development, such as the participant vs.~researcher
  perspective, the need for simplification, and incorporating known
  cognitive constraints.
\item
  \textbf{Conceptualize Learning Mechanisms:} Propose distinct models
  (e.g., random choice, heuristics, RL) and organize them into a unified
  framework based on how they process prediction errors.
\item
  \textbf{Connect to Formalization:} Understand the ``Update Rule''
  (\(\text{New} = \text{Old} + \text{Learning Rate} \times \text{Error}\))
  as a universal grammar of learning that connects simple heuristics to
  complex cognitive strategies.
\end{itemize}

\section{Introduction: Observing Behavior to Theorize
Mechanisms}\label{introduction-observing-behavior-to-theorize-mechanisms}

Chapter 1 emphasized the importance of modeling underlying generative
mechanisms. To do this for cognition, we first need a behavior to
explain. This chapter uses the \textbf{Matching Pennies game} as our
initial cognitive phenomenon. It's a simple strategic interaction, yet
rich enough to illustrate the process of developing and refining
cognitive models.

Our goal here is not yet to build the final computational models, but to
practice the crucial preceding steps:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Observing behavior in a specific task (through experiments and data
  exploration).
\item
  Reflecting on potential cognitive strategies and constraints (drawing
  on observations, participant reports, and cognitive science
  principles).
\item
  Formulating initial \emph{verbal} theories or candidate models that
  describe the potential underlying mechanisms.
\end{enumerate}

This process lays the groundwork for Chapter 3, where we will translate
these verbal ideas into precise, formal models ready for simulation and
testing.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why build verbal models before writing any code?} Jumping straight
to code forces you to make dozens of implicit decisions (What counts as
a win? Does memory last 1 trial or 10?) without realizing you're making
them. Working through the verbal model first surfaces those hidden
assumptions so you can make them deliberately. Every ambiguity you
resolve here saves you a debugging session in Ch. 3.
\end{quote}

\section{The Matching Pennies Game}\label{the-matching-pennies-game}

In the matching pennies game, two players engage in a series of choices.
One player attempts to match the other's choice, while the other player
aims to achieve a mismatch, and they repeatedly play with each other.
This is a prototypical example of interacting behaviors that are usually
tackled by game theory, and bring up issues of theory of mind and
recursivity.

For an introduction see the paper: Waade, Peter T., et al.~``Introducing
tomsup: Theory of mind simulations using Python.'' Behavior Research
Methods 55.5 (2023): 2197-2231.

For fun data involving different kinds of primates playing the game,
see: Devaine, M., San-Galli, A., Trapanese, C., Bardino, G., Hano, C.,
Saint Jalme, M., \ldots{} \& Daunizeau, J. (2017). Reading wild minds: a
computational assay of theory of mind sophistication across seven
primate species. PLoS computational biology, 13(11), e1005833. The data
is available in Assignment 2 (for the students at AU/CogSci).

\section{Game Structure}\label{game-structure}

The game proceeds as follows:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Two players sit facing each other
\item
  Each round, both players choose either ``left'' or ``right'' to
  indicate where they believe a penny is hidden
\item
  The matcher wins by choosing the same hand as their opponent
\item
  The hider wins by choosing the opposite hand
\item
  Points are awarded: +1 for winning, -1 (or 0, depending on the
  version) for losing
\item
  Repeat
\end{enumerate}

This simple structure creates a rich environment for studying
decision-making strategies, learning, and adaptation.

\section{Empirical Investigation}\label{empirical-investigation}

\subsection{Data Collection Protocol}\label{data-collection-protocol}

{[}NOTES FOR FUTURE YEARS: MAYBE REPLACE WITH THE PRIMATE DATA?{]}

If you are attending my class you have been (or will be) asked to
participate in a matching pennies game. This game provides the
foundation for our modeling efforts. By observing gameplay and
collecting data, we can develop models that capture the cognitive
processes underlying decision-making in strategic situations.

Participants play 30 rounds as the matcher and 30 rounds as the hider,
allowing us to observe behavior in both roles. While playing,
participants track their scores, which can provide quantitative data for
later analysis. Participants are also asked to reflect on their
strategies and the strategies they believe their opponents are using, as
that provides valuable materials to build models on.

\subsection{Initial Observations}\label{initial-observations}

Through the careful observation and discussion of gameplay we do in
class, several patterns typically emerge. For instance, players often
demonstrate strategic adaptation, adjusting their choices based on their
opponent's previous moves. They may attempt to identify patterns in
their opponent's behavior while trying to make their own choices less
predictable. The tension between exploitation of perceived patterns and
maintenance of unpredictability creates fascinating dynamics for
modeling.

\section{Empirical explorations}\label{empirical-explorations}

Below you can observe how a previous year of CogSci did against bots
(computational agents) playing according to different strategies. Look
at the plots below, where the x axes indicate trial, the y axes how many
points the CogSci'ers scored (0 being chance, negative means being
completely owned by the bots, positive owning the bot) and the different
colors indicate different strategies employed by the bots. Strategy
``-2'' was a Win-Stay-Lose-Shift bot: when it got a +1, it repeated its
previous move (e.g.~right if it had just played right), otherwise it
would perform the opposite move (e.g.~left if it had just played right).
Strategy ``-1'' was a biased Nash both, playing ``right'' 80\% of the
time. Strategy ``0'' indicates a reinforcement learning bot; ``1'' a bot
assuming you were playing according to a reinforcement learning strategy
and trying to infer your learning and temperature parameters; ``2'' a
bot assuming you were following strategy ``1'' and trying to accordingly
infer your parameters.

\NormalTok{data\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{file.path}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"data"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"MP\_MSc\_CogSci22.csv"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{bot\_labels }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}

\NormalTok{d\_clean }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}

\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{02-BuildingModels_files/figure-pdf/ch03_plot_individual_performance-1.pdf}

\subsection{From Observation to Theory: Identifying Potential
Mechanisms}\label{from-observation-to-theory-identifying-potential-mechanisms}

The plots above reveal patterns: average performance changes over time,
varies by opponent, and differs across individuals. Gameplay
observations and participant reflections (from class discussion or
collected data) add qualitative insights -- perhaps players mention
trying to be unpredictable, guessing opponent biases, or repeating
winning moves.

The crucial next step is to distill these rich, complex observations
into simplified, plausible \textbf{mechanisms} or \textbf{strategies}.
This involves abstraction:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Identifying Core Patterns:} What recurring behaviors seem most
  important? (e.g., reacting to wins/losses, tracking opponent
  frequencies).
\item
