  \textbf{Simplifying:} Can we capture the essence of a strategy without
  modeling every detail of a player's thought process or interaction?
  (e.g., modeling just previous behaviors as possible inputs, instead of
  complex patterns in face expressions or movements that real humans
  likely use when trying to guess which hand has the penny).
\item
  \textbf{Drawing on Cognitive Principles:} How do known cognitive
  constraints (like limited memory or processing errors) shape plausible
  strategies?
\end{itemize}

For instance, observing that players often change their choice after a
loss might lead us to propose a ``If Lose Then Shift'' component as part
of a candidate model. Observing that performance differs against biased
vs.~adaptive bots suggests players might be trying to learn or adapt,
leading to memory-based or learning models.

This process generates \emph{verbal models} -- initial hypotheses about
the strategies at play. Key modeling considerations guide this
translation:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  What information do players likely use? (Own past choices? Opponent's
  choices? Payoffs?)
\item
  How far back does memory plausibly extend? (Last trial? Last 5 trials?
  Exponential decay?)
\item
  What is the role of randomness? (True randomness? Exploration?
  Implementation errors?)
\item
  How might strategies adapt over time or differ between individuals?
\end{itemize}

Answering these helps refine our verbal models, paving the way for
formalization. The goal isn't to capture everything, but to propose
distinct, testable mechanisms.

\subsection{The distinction between participant and researcher
perspectives}\label{the-distinction-between-participant-and-researcher-perspectives}

As participants we might not be aware of the strategy we use, or we
might believe something erroneous. The exercise here is to act as
researchers: what are the principles underlying the participants'
behaviors, no matter what the participants know or believe? Note that
talking to participants and being participants helps developing ideas,
but it's not the end point of the process. Also note that as cognitive
scientists we can rely on what we have learned about cognitive processes
(e.g.~memory).

Another important component of the distinction is that participants
leave in a rich world: they rely on facial expressions and bodily
posture, the switch strategies, etc. On the other hand, the researcher
is trying to identify one or few at most ``simple'' strategies. Rich
bodily interactions and mixtures or sequences of multiple strategies are
not a good place to start modeling. These aspects are a poor starting
point for building your first model, and are often pretty difficult to
fit to empirical data. Nevertheless, they are important intuitions that
the researcher should (eventually?) accommodate.

\section{Candidate Models: A Hierarchy of Cognitive
Complexity}\label{candidate-models-a-hierarchy-of-cognitive-complexity}

Based on behavioral observations and cognitive principles, the many
discussions in class during the years have advanced a variety of models,
which I'm here organizing into a hierarchy based on their conception of
learning. We start with simple agents that ignore history, move to
learning agents that track first average outcomes, then also uncertainty
and volatility, and conclude with strategic agents that model the
opponent's mind. To make their conceptual continuity more explicit, I
couldn't resist adding some discussion of the math behind the verbal
intuitions and pointing to how each model can be recast in terms of an
update rule:
\[\text{New Belief} = \text{Old Belief} + \text{Learning Rate} \cdot \text{Predictive Error}\].
This format unifies the different models under a common mathematical
framework, making it easier to understand their relationships and
differences.

Also note that in the formula below, `Belief' is a placeholder. If we
are modeling a coin flipper, the belief is a probability (\(\theta\)).
If we are modeling betting, it might be an expected reward value
(\(V\)). If we are modeling playing strategies, it might be a prediction
of the opponent's move. As it should be obvious, those are partially
overlapping conceptualizations.

\subsection{Level 0: The Static Agent (No
Learning)}\label{level-0-the-static-agent-no-learning}

The simplest assumption is that the agent does not learn or adapt to the
opponent at all. They simply act according to a fixed internal
preference.

\subsection{The Biased Agent (Random
Choice):}\label{the-biased-agent-random-choice}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: The agent is a biased coin flipper. They have a static
  preference (e.g., ``I generally prefer Right'') that does not change
  over time.
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (Bernoulli Process)}: We model the
  choice \(y_t\) at trial \(t\) as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution
  with a fixed rate parameter \(\theta\):
  \[y_t \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\]
\item
  \emph{The ``Update'' Rule}: Because this agent is static, there is no
  update rule, in other words, a learning of 0. The parameter \(\theta\)
  remains constant regardless of wins or losses.
  \[\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t} + \text{Learning Rate} \cdot \text{Predictive Error}\]
  where \(\text{Learning Rate} = 0\) and therefore the formula reduces
  to \[\theta_{t+1} = \theta_{t}\].
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \(y_t \in \{0, 1\}\): The choice (e.g., Left/Right).
  \item
    \(\theta \in [0, 1]\): The fixed probability of choosing 1 (bias).
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Looking Ahead}: This model might seem too simple to be useful,
  but never underestimate the power of null models! In a study using
  k-ToM models (see below), we realized that for k = 6 or above, the
  model behaved in a way that was practically indistinguishabe from a
  biased agent. Also, a simple model allows us to build skills in
  reasonable steps. In the next chapter, we will write code to simulate
  data according to these models and in the following chapter, we will
  then learn how to take a sequence of choices and mathematically work
  backwards to find the most likely value of the model parameters. In
  all these cases, being able to start with a simple model is crucial to
  build intuition and skills before moving to more complex models.
\end{itemize}

{[}FIND REF OF PAPER USING THIS{]}

\subsection{Level 1: Heuristics \& The Immediate
Past}\label{level-1-heuristics-the-immediate-past}

The next step up is an agent that learns minimally: it reacts to
feedback but has a ``memory'' of only one trial.

\subsubsection{Deterministic Win-Stay-Lose-Shift
(WSLS):}\label{deterministic-win-stay-lose-shift-wsls}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: A rigid heuristic: ``If I won, I do the same thing. If
  I lost, I switch.'' This can be viewed as an agent with zero memory
  who effectively ``re-learns'' the world from scratch after every
  single feedback.
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (Conditional Probability)}:
  \[P(y_t = y_{t-1} | outcome_{t-1}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } outcome_{t-1} = \text{win} \\ 0 & \text{if } outcome_{t-1} = \text{loss} \end{cases}\]
\item
  \emph{The ``Update'' Rule}: We can treat the ``Probability of
  Staying'' (\(P_{stay}\)) as a value that is updated
  trial-by-trial.\[P_{stay, t+1} = P_{stay, t} + \text{Learning Rate} \cdot (Outcome_t - P_{stay, t})\]
  Where \(\text{Learning Rate} = 1\). Because the agent learns instantly
  from the immediate past, the formula reduces to:
  \[P_{stay, t+1} = P_{stay, t} + 1 \cdot (Outcome_t - P_{stay, t})\]\[P_{stay, t+1} = Outcome_t\]

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    Implication: If the outcome was a Win (\(1\)), the probability of
    staying becomes \(1\). If the outcome was a Loss (\(0\)), the
    probability of staying becomes \(0\). This mathematically formalizes
    the idea of ``instant forgetting.''
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:\(outcome_{t-1}\):
  \(outcome_{t-1} \in \{0, 1\}\): The feedback from the previous trial,
  where 1 = Win and 0 = Loss
\item
  \emph{Note: The Physics View (Ising Formulation)} {[}only for
  nerds{]}: While we typically code choices as 0/1 for logistic
  regression, physicists and network theorists often code binary states
  as ``spins'': -1 (Left) and +1 (Right). If we also code the outcome as
  -1 (Loss) and +1 (Win), the WSLS update rule becomes mathematically
  elegant: \[y_t = outcome_{t-1} \cdot y_{t-1}\] Why does this work? If
  Win (+1): The equation becomes \(y_t = 1 \cdot y_{t-1}\). The sign
  stays the same (Stay). If Loss (-1): The equation becomes
  \(y_t = -1 \cdot y_{t-1}\). The sign flips (Shift). This formulation
  highlights that ``shifting'' is mathematically equivalent to sign
  inversion. This connects cognitive shifting to ``spin glass'' or ising
  models in physics (e.g., Stephens \& Bialek, 2010), and is useful to
  know of it as it sometimes simplifies the mathematical equations of
  your models.
\end{itemize}

{[}FIND REF OF PAPERS USING THIS MODES{]}

\subsubsection{Probabilistic Win-Stay-Lose-Shift (Logistic
Regression):}\label{probabilistic-win-stay-lose-shift-logistic-regression}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: We relax the strict rule. The agent is more likely to
  stay after a win and shift after a loss, but real behavior is noisy
  (stochastic, probabilistic).
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (GLM)}: We define the probability of
  ``staying'' (\(P_{stay}\)) as a function of the previous outcome using
  a logistic curve.
  \[P(stay_t) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot outcome_{t-1})\].
  Note that we can think of the reactions to feedback as symmetric
  (probability of staying when winning equal to the possibility of
  shifting when losing), or asymmetric (e.g.~the agent might be more
  sensitive to losses than wins, or vice versa). In the latter case,
  we'd have an equation like:
  \[P(stay_t) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot win_{t-1} + \beta_2 \cdot loss_{t-1})\]
\item
  \emph{The ``Update'' Rule}: To fit our hierarchy, we treat the agent's
  internal Decision Value (\(Q\)) as the state being
  updated.\[Q_{t+1} = Q_{t} + \text{Learning Rate} \cdot (\text{Target}_t - Q_{t})\]Where
  \(\text{Learning Rate} = 1\) (Instant Update) and the Target is the
  weighted outcome
  (\(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot outcome_t\)).Reduction:\[Q_{t+1} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot outcome_t\]Implication:
  Just like the Deterministic agent, this agent ``forgets'' the distant
  past instantly. However, a ``Win'' does not guarantee a Stay
  (\(P=1\)); it simply pushes the decision value to a high---but not
  infinite---number.
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    \(Q\): The internal log-odds of staying.
  \item
    \(\text{logit}^{-1}(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}\): The sigmoid function
    mapping value to probability.
  \item
    \(\beta_1\): Sensitivity to feedback. High \(\beta_1\) approximates
    the deterministic heuristic; \(\beta_1 \approx 0\) implies the agent
    ignores feedback.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

{[}FIND REF OF PAPER USING THIS e.g., Zhang \& Lee, 2010; Worthy et al.,
2013{]}

\subsection{Level 2: Integrating History (The Moving
Average)}\label{level-2-integrating-history-the-moving-average}

Real agents usually care about more than just the last trial. They
integrate history over time to form an expectation.

\subsubsection{The Moving Average (Windowed
Memory):}\label{the-moving-average-windowed-memory}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: ``I think the future will look like the average of the
  recent past.''
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (Batch Average)}:
  \[V_{t} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} Outcome_{t-i}\]
\item
  \emph{Transformation to Update Rule}: To avoid recalculating the sum
  from scratch, we can update the average by adding the new outcome and
  removing the oldest
  outcome.\[V_{t} = V_{t-1} + \frac{1}{N} \cdot (Outcome_{t} - Outcome_{t-N})\]
\item
  \emph{Critique (The Memory Problem)}: This formulation reveals a
  massive cognitive cost. To perform this update, the agent cannot just
  store the current average (\(V_{t-1}\)); they must remember exactly
  what happened \(N\) trials ago (\(Outcome_{t-N}\)) to ``remove'' it
  from the sum. Further, a memory from 10 trials ago is recalled
  perfectly, but a memory from 11 trials ago vanishes instantly. This is
  biologically implausible.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    The Mismatch: This does not fit the standard ``Prediction Error''
    update rule. It compares the new reality (\(Outcome_t\)) to the
    oldest reality (\(Outcome_{t-N}\)), rather than comparing reality to
    expectation.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \(V_t\): The estimated value (probability of winning/Right) at trial
    \(t\).
  \item
    \(N\): The window size (e.g., 10 trials).
  \item
    \(Outcome_t \in \{0, 1\}\): The outcome at trial \(t\).
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Level 3: A more elegant memory decay
mechanism}\label{level-3-a-more-elegant-memory-decay-mechanism}

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is mathematically the elegant solution to
the Moving Average's memory problem. It replaces the ``perfect memory of
\(N\) trials'' with a ``fading memory of everything''.

\subsubsection{From Moving Average to Reinforcement Learning
(Rescorla-Wagner):}\label{from-moving-average-to-reinforcement-learning-rescorla-wagner}

How do we fix the memory problem in Level 2? The agent cannot remember
the specific outcome \(Outcome_{t-N}\). So, they make a guess: ``The
outcome \(N\) trials ago was probably similar to my current average
value.'' Mathematically, we substitute
\(Outcome_{t-N} \approx V_{t-1}\).
\[V_{t} = V_{t-1} + \frac{1}{N} (Outcome_{t} - \mathbf{V_{t-1}})\] If we
rename \(\frac{1}{N}\) to \(\alpha\) (alpha), we get the standard RL
rule.

Note that the update rule only applies to the value of the action that
was actually taken. For instance, if we are modeling the value of
picking ``Right'', the outcome should be 1 if we picked ``Right'' and
won, 0 if we picked ``Right'' and lost, and it should not be updated at
all if we picked ``Left''. In the context of the matching pennies we can
assume that increased expected value for left will go hand in hand with
decreased expected value for right (and so when the participant chooses
``Left'', use \(1-Outcome\) to update the value of ``Right'').

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{``Traditional Formulation (Exponential Weighted Average)}:
  Instead of a sum over \(N\), value is a weighted sum of all past
  history, where weights decay geometrically.
  \[V_{t} = (1-\alpha) \cdot V_{t-1} + \alpha \cdot Reward_{t}\]
\item
  \emph{Transformation to Update Rule (The Delta Rule)}: By rearranging
  terms, we get the update format I used as template for all models:
  \[V_{t} = V_{t-1} + \alpha \cdot (Reward_{t} - V_{t-1})\]
\item
  \emph{Why is this ``Learning''?} The term \((Reward_{t} - V_{t-1})\)
  is the Prediction Error (PE). The agent compares reality (\(Reward\))
  to their expectation (\(V\)) and nudges their belief by a step size
  \(\alpha\).
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \(V_t\): The estimated value (e.g., probability of win) at trial
    \(t\).
  \item
    \(\alpha \in [0, 1]\): The Learning Rate. High \(\alpha\) = fast
    forgetting; Low \(\alpha\) = long memory.
  \item
    \(PE\): The difference between what happened and what was expected.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Historical Context: From Behaviorism to Bayesian Belief --- The
Prediction-Error Lineage}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The simple update rule
\(V_t = V_{t-1} + \alpha \cdot (Outcome_t - V_{t-1})\) looks almost
trivially obvious once written down. Getting there took cognitive
science roughly four decades and required overturning an entire
paradigm.

\subsubsection{The Behaviorist
Prohibition}\label{the-behaviorist-prohibition}

For the first half of the twentieth century, scientific psychology
largely banned the internal state variable \(V_t\) from its vocabulary.
The dominant framework --- Hullian behaviorism (Hull 1943) and its
variants --- insisted that a rigorous account of learning had to be
stated purely in terms of observable stimuli and responses, with no
reference to unobservable mental states. Tolman's (Tolman 1948) proposal
that rats form ``cognitive maps'' --- internal representations of their
environment --- was widely regarded as an embarrassing lapse into
mentalism. The prediction error \((Outcome - V)\) cannot exist without
\(V\), and \(V\) is a latent belief. The entire framework of the models
in this chapter was conceptually prohibited.

\subsubsection{The Rescorla-Wagner
Pivot}\label{the-rescorla-wagner-pivot}

The rehabilitation came from classical conditioning research, and
specifically from the realisation that conditioning depends not on the
raw presence of the unconditioned stimulus but on \emph{how surprising}
it is. Rescorla and Wagner (1972) introduced the prediction-error rule
into learning theory to explain blocking (Kamin 1969): if one cue
already perfectly predicts an outcome, adding a second cue produces no
conditioning to it, because the outcome is not surprising. This
``blocking effect'' is exactly what the prediction-error term captures
--- when \(V_{t-1}\) already equals \(Outcome_t\), the error is zero and
no updating occurs. Rescorla and Wagner's model was the first to make
the internal expectation \(V_t\) an explicit, load-bearing quantity in a
learning equation.

The impact was immediate and lasting. The prediction-error rule turned
out to be the correct description of dopamine neuron firing (Schultz,
Dayan, and Montague 1997): dopamine neurons respond to unpredicted
rewards (positive prediction error), are suppressed by
predicted-but-absent rewards (negative prediction error), and are silent
when rewards are fully predicted (zero error). The same equation that
Rescorla and Wagner derived from behavioral blocking data in 1972 became
the neural currency of reinforcement learning two decades later.

\subsubsection{From Fixed to Adaptive Learning Rates: the Kalman
Filter}\label{from-fixed-to-adaptive-learning-rates-the-kalman-filter}

Rescorla-Wagner's \(\alpha\) is fixed --- the same step size applies on
trial 1 as on trial 1000. This is cognitively unrealistic: a rational
agent should trust new observations more when they are uncertain about
the current state, and less when they are confident. Dayan, Kakade, and
Montague (2000) showed that Rescorla-Wagner is a special case of the
\textbf{Kalman filter} (Kalman 1960) obtained by setting the process
noise to zero (a static environment). In the full Kalman filter, the
learning rate becomes the Kalman Gain
\(K_t = \sigma^2_{t-1} / (\sigma^2_{t-1} + \sigma^2_{noise})\) --- a
quantity that starts high (large prior uncertainty) and shrinks as
evidence accumulates. The same update rule, but now the step size is
endogenous and principled rather than fixed and arbitrary.

\subsubsection{Hierarchical Uncertainty: the
HGF}\label{hierarchical-uncertainty-the-hgf}

The Kalman filter still assumes that \(\sigma^2_{noise}\) --- the
volatility of the environment --- is known and fixed. Real environments
are not: sometimes the rules change slowly, sometimes rapidly. Mathys et
al. (2011) introduced the \textbf{Hierarchical Gaussian Filter (HGF)} to
handle this: a hierarchy of Kalman filters where the volatility at one
level is itself tracked and updated at the level above. The learning
rate for beliefs about the world now adapts continuously to inferred
volatility, and there is a separate meta-learning rate \(\kappa\)
governing how quickly volatility estimates are revised. The HGF is,
formally, just another application of the prediction-error principle ---
but applied recursively across levels of abstraction.

The three-decade arc from R-W (1972) to the Kalman filter connection
(2000) to the HGF (2011) is a story about the same core idea ---
\emph{update in proportion to surprise} --- being successively liberated
from artificial constraints: first the prohibition on latent states,
then the constraint of a fixed learning rate, then the assumption of
known and constant volatility.

\end{tcolorbox}

\subsection{Level 4: The Bayesian Update (Static
Uncertainty)}\label{level-4-the-bayesian-update-static-uncertainty}

In RL, the learning rate \(\alpha\) is a fixed number. In Bayesian
inference, the ``learning rate'' becomes adaptive based on uncertainty.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: The agent tracks not just the value, but the
  uncertainty in estimating that value. They weigh new evidence against
  their prior belief based on how noisy the evidence is versus how solid
  their prior is.
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation}: We combine a Prior distribution
  (\(\mathcal{N}(\mu_{prior}, \sigma^2_{prior})\)) with Likelihood
  (\(\mathcal{N}(Outcome, \sigma^2_{noise})\)) to get a
  Posterior.\[\text{Posterior Mean} = \frac{\sigma^2_{noise}\mu_{t-1} + \sigma^2_{t-1}Outcome_t}{\sigma^2_{noise} + \sigma^2_{t-1}}\]
\item
  \emph{Transformation to Update Rule (Kalman Gain)}: With some algebra,
  we can rearrange the posterior mean into our standard ``Update +
  Error''
  format.\[\mu_{t} = \mu_{t-1} + K_t \cdot (Outcome_t - \mu_{t-1})\]
  Here, \(K\), what we called learning rate is often called
  \(Kalman Gain\):
  \[K_t = \frac{\sigma^{2}_{t-1}}{\sigma^{2}_{t-1} + \sigma^{2}_{noise}}\]
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \(\mu_t\): The estimated value (mean belief) at trial \(t\).
  \item
    \(\sigma^2_{t-1}\): The uncertainty of the agent's belief before
    seeing the outcome (Prior Variance).
  \item
    \(\sigma^2_{noise}\): The uncertainty/noise of the environment
    (Likelihood Variance).
  \item
    \(Outcome_t\): The observation at trial \(t\).
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Critique (The Stopping Problem)}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    The Match: This looks exactly like RL, where \(K_t\) replaces
    \(\alpha\).
  \item
    The Difference: In this model, the uncertainty \(\sigma^2_{t-1}\)
    decreases after every observation (the agent gets more confident).
    As \(\sigma^2_{t-1} \to 0\), the gain \(K_t \to 0\).
  \item
    Implication: The agent eventually stops learning because they become
    ``sure'' they know the truth. This is optimal for static worlds, but
    disastrous if the opponent changes strategy.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Level 5: The Kalman Filter (Dynamic
Uncertainty)}\label{level-5-the-kalman-filter-dynamic-uncertainty}

Real environments change (volatility). The Kalman Filter extends the
Bayesian update by adding a Prediction Step that prevents the agent from
becoming ``too sure.''

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: ``The world is not static; it drifts. Even if I was
  sure yesterday, my knowledge has degraded by today.''
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (State Space Model)}: We assume the true
  hidden value (\(x\)) drifts over time, and that our observations
  (\(y\)) are noisy versions of that drifting value.
  \[x_t = x_{t-1} + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)\]\[y_t = x_t + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)\]
\item
  \emph{Transformation to Update Rule (Two-Step Process)}: Unlike the
  static Bayesian agent, the Kalman agent ``inflates'' their uncertainty
  before looking at the data.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    Predict (Inflate Uncertainty): Before seeing data, the agent assumes
    the world might have changed. They add Process Noise (\(Q\)) to
    their existing uncertainty.
    \[\sigma^2_{prediction} = \sigma^2_{previous} + Q\]
  \item
    Calculate Gain: The ``Learning Rate'' (\(K_t\)) is calculated using
    this inflated
    uncertainty.\[K_{t} = \frac{\sigma^2_{prediction}}{\sigma^2_{prediction} + R}\]
  \item
    Update (The Standard Rule): Finally, we apply the standard
    error-correction rule.
    \[\mu_{t} = \mu_{t-1} + K_t \cdot (Outcome_t - \mu_{t-1})\]
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    \(Q\): Process Noise (Volatility). The variance of the drift
    \(w_t\). High \(Q\) means the world changes largely/frequently.
  \item
    \(R\): Measurement Noise (Observation Uncertainty). Equivalent to
    \(\sigma^2_{noise}\) in Level 4.
  \item
    \(\mu_t\): The agent's estimate of the true state \(x_t\).
  \item
    \(\sigma^2_{prediction}\): The agent's uncertainty after accounting
    for volatility but before seeing the new outcome.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Why this fixes the ``Stopping Problem''}: In Level 4,
  \(\sigma^2\) shrank to zero, causing learning to stop. Here, because
  we add \(Q\) at every step, \(\sigma^2_{prediction}\) never hits zero.
  Consequently, the learning rate \(K_t\) never hits zero. It settles at
  a dynamic equilibrium---a sweet spot where the agent stays permanently
  ``alert'' to changes without over-reacting to noise.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Level 6: The Hierarchical Gaussian Filter
(Meta-Learning)}\label{level-6-the-hierarchical-gaussian-filter-meta-learning}

The Kalman Filter assumes the volatility (\(Q\)) is constant. But what
if the opponent plays steadily for 20 rounds, then suddenly starts
switching wildly? The HGF introduces a hierarchy to track this.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    Level 1 (\(x_1\)): The value of the stimulus (e.g., probability of
    ``Right'').
  \item
    Level 2 (\(x_2\)): The volatility of Level 1.
  \item
    N.B. we could build more levels (e.g.~level 3 would be the
    meta-volatility, the model belief of how fast to update estimates of
    volatility)
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Transformation to Update Rule (Volatile Learning Rates)}: The
  update rule for Level 1 looks standard, but the Learning Rate is now a
  function of Level
  2.\[\mu_{1,t} = \mu_{1,t-1} + K_1 \cdot (Outcome_t - \mu_{1,t-1})\]
  Where the Gain \(K_1\) depends on the volatility estimate from Level
  2:\[K_1 \approx \frac{\exp(\mu_{2})}{\exp(\mu_{2}) + \sigma^2_{noise}}\]
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \(\mu_{1}\): Estimated value (Probability).
  \item
    \(\mu_{2}\): Estimated volatility (Log-volatility).
  \item
    \(\exp(\mu_2)\): The ``phasic volatility''---how much the agent
    expects the world to change right now.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Implication}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    If Level 2 detects high volatility (\(x_2 \uparrow\)), the learning
    rate \(K_1\) spikes \(\to 1\).
  \item
    If Level 2 detects stability (\(x_2 \downarrow\)), the learning rate
    \(K_1\) drops \(\to 0\).
  \item
    Meta-Learning: The agent is not just learning the value; they are
    learning how fast to learn.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Looking Ahead}: This model represents the current
  state-of-the-art in ``Ideal Observer'' models and has had many
  interesting applications in cognitive neurosciences (REFS). While the
  math for inverting this model (Variational Bayes) is complex, the
  intuition is simple: it is a dynamic learning rate manager. Future
  chapters (not yet written) might explore the model and its
  implications and limitations.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Level 7: Recursive Strategies (Theory of
Mind)}\label{level-7-recursive-strategies-theory-of-mind}

Here is the revised and expanded text for the final sections. I have
aligned Level 7 (ToM) with the ``Update Rule'' framework by clarifying
that while the decision is recursive, the learning is a Bayesian update
of the opponent's parameters. I also kept the Mixture Models section as
a necessary pedagogical conclusion.

Level 7: Recursive Strategies (Theory of Mind)

Finally, we move from learning about the environment to learning about
the agent. This is Theory of Mind (ToM). In our framework, this is
formally framed as Recursive Bayesian Learning.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: The agent (\(k\)-ToM) does not simply track the
  pattern of outcomes; they attempt to track the opponent's hidden
  strategy.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    A 0-ToM agent is a simple learner (e.g., the Biased Agent or RL).
  \item
    A 1-ToM agent simulates a 0-ToM opponent. They ask: ``If I were a
    simple learner observing this history, what would I play next?''
  \item
    A 2-ToM agent simulates a 1-ToM opponent. They ask: ``What does the
    opponent think I will do?''
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (Recursive Simulation)}: The agent holds
  a ``shadow model'' of the opponent. If I am Level \(k\), I assume my
  opponent is Level \(k-1\). I feed the game history into my internal
  simulation of them to predict their next move probability
  (\(\hat{p}_{op}\)).
\item
  \emph{Update Rule (Bayesian Meta-Learning)}: Unlike RL, where we
  update a simple value \(V\), here we update the parameters of the
  simulated opponent.
  \[\text{OpponentModel}_{new} = \text{OpponentModel}_{old} + \text{Learning Rate} \cdot (\text{Prediction Error})\]

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    Specifics: If I am 1-ToM modeling a 0-ToM opponent (Bias), I use a
    Bayesian update to refine my estimate of their bias \(\theta\).
  \item
    Decision Value: Once the opponent model is updated, the value of my
    action \(a\) is derived from their predicted move:
    \[V_{k}(a) \propto P(\text{Opponent plays } \hat{a}_{op} | \text{Opponent is } k-1)\]
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \(k\): The sophistication level. \(k=0\) is the baseline; \(k=1\) is
    strategic; \(k=2\) is meta-strategic.
  \item
    \(\hat{a}_{op}\): The predicted action of the opponent.(Ref:
    Devaine, et al., 2014; Waade et al., 2023)
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Handling Heterogeneity: Mixture
Models}\label{handling-heterogeneity-mixture-models}

Real behavior is rarely pure. A player might be ``Random'' when tired
but ``Strategic'' when focused. Or they might switch strategies to
confuse the opponent.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept}: We assume the data is not generated by one single
  model, but by a probabilistic blend of several.
\item
  \emph{Traditional Formulation (The Weighted Likelihood)}: The
  likelihood of the observed data \(D\) is the weighted sum of the
  likelihoods from \(M\) different candidate strategies.
  \[P(D | \Theta) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_m \cdot P(D | \text{Model}_m, \theta_m)\]
\item
  \emph{Variable Definitions}:\(w_m\): The mixing weight (e.g., 70\% RL,
  30\% Random). Note that \(\sum w_m = 1\).\(P(D|\text{Model}_m)\): How
  well the specific strategy (e.g., RL) explains the data.
\item
  \emph{Critique} (The Complexity Trap): While realistic, these models
  are hard to fit. If we allow an agent to switch between 5 different
  strategies at any moment, the possible combinations of parameter
  values explaining the same patterns of behavior could explode, making
  the model underdefined for the data. Sometimes one can be lucky and
  integrate the ``mixed'' models into one equation (see an example here:
  https://betanalpha.github.io/assets/chapters\_html/reading\_times.html)
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Plausibility Check: Cognitive
Constraints}\label{plausibility-check-cognitive-constraints}

You probably noticed you couldn't remember every single move your
opponent made. Maybe you lost track of older trials, and recent trials
felt more important. These aren't failures: they're features of human
cognition. Our models should reflect these constraints to be cognitively
realistic. Let's see what difference these constraints make for
predictions.

\subsubsection{Memory Limitations}\label{memory-limitations}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \emph{Constraint:} Humans have limited working memory and exhibit
  forgetting, often approximated by exponential decay. Perfect recall of
  long trial sequences is unrealistic.
\item
  \emph{Modeling Implication:} This favors models incorporating memory
  decay or finite history windows (like imperfect memory models or RL
  with a learning rate \textless{} 1) over perfect memory models. It
  suggests that even bias-tracking models should discount older
  information.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Perseveration Tendencies}\label{perseveration-tendencies}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \emph{Constraint:} People sometimes exhibit perseveration -- repeating
  a previous action, especially if it was recently successful or chosen,
  even if a different strategy might suggest otherwise. This can be
  distinct from rational ``win-stay''.
\item
  \emph{Modeling Implication:} This might be incorporated as an
  additional bias parameter influencing the choice probability (e.g., a
  small added probability of repeating the last action \(a_{t-1}\)
  regardless of outcome) or interact with feedback processing (e.g.,
  strengthening the `stay' tendency after wins).
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Noise and Errors}\label{noise-and-errors}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \emph{Constraint:} Human behavior is inherently noisy. People make
  mistakes, have attentional lapses, press the wrong button, or
  misunderstand feedback. Behavior rarely perfectly matches a
  deterministic strategy.
\item
  \emph{Modeling Implication:} Models should almost always include a
  ``noise'' component. This can be implemented in several ways:

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \textbf{Lapse Rate:} A probability (e.g., \(\epsilon\)) that on any
    given trial, the agent makes a random choice instead of following
    their primary strategy (as used in the Mixture Model chapter).
  \item
    \textbf{Decision Noise (Softmax):} In models where choices are based
    on comparing values (like RL), a `temperature' parameter can control
    the stochasticity. High temperature leads to more random choices,
    low temperature leads to more deterministic choices based on values.
  \item
    \textbf{Imperfect Heuristics:} Parameters within a strategy might
    reflect imperfect application (e.g., in WSLS, \(p_{stay\_win} < 1\)
    or \(p_{shift\_loss} < 1\)). This can also capture asymmetric
    responses to feedback (e.g., being more likely to shift after a loss
    than stay after a win).

    \begin{itemize}
    \tightlist
    \item
      \emph{Exploration:} Note that we talked about noise and errors,
      but random deviations can also be framed as adaptive exploration,
      allowing the agent to test actions that their current strategy
      deems suboptimal.
    \end{itemize}
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{A Note on Ideal
Observers}\label{a-note-on-ideal-observers}

It is important to distinguish between \textbf{Heuristic Models} (like
WSLS) and \textbf{Ideal Observer Models} (like the Kalman Filter or
HGF). Heuristics attempt to describe the \emph{process} a human uses.
Ideal observers describe the \emph{optimal computation} given the
uncertainty. When we fit models like the HGF to human data, we are
effectively asking: ``In what specific ways does the human deviate from
optimality?'' (e.g., do they overestimate how volatile the opponent
is?).

\subsection{Relationships Between
Models}\label{relationships-between-models}

It's useful to note that these candidate models aren't always entirely
distinct. Often, simpler models emerge as special cases of more complex
ones:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  A Random Choice model is like a Memory-Based model where the influence
  of memory is zero.
\item
  WSLS can be seen as a specific type of RL model with a very high
  learning rate and sensitivity only to the immediately preceding
  trial's outcome.
\item
  A 0-ToM model might resemble a Bias Tracking (moving average or RL
  model).
\end{itemize}

Recognizing these connections can guide a principled modeling approach,
starting simple and adding complexity only as needed and justified by
data or theory.

This is what we called ``model nesting'' in a future chapter. It's very
elegant in that it allows to directly compare several models in a
non-exclusive fashion, simply based on parameter values. A good example
is also provided in Betancourt's case study on reading times:
https://betanalpha.github.io/assets/chapters\_html/reading\_times.html

\subsection{Handling Heterogeneity: Mixture
Models}\label{handling-heterogeneity-mixture-models-1}

Sometimes the models cannot be nested, or we might want to capture the
possibility that different participants (or even the same participant at
different times) use different strategies. For instance, some players
might predominantly use WSLS, while others rely more on bias tracking
(moving average or RL). This is where \emph{mixture models} become
relevant (explored in detail in a future chapter).

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \emph{Concept:} Instead of assuming \emph{one} model generated all the
  data, a mixture model assumes the data is a probabilistic blend from
  \emph{multiple} candidate models (e.g., 70\% of choices from WSLS,
  30\% from Random Bias).
\item
  \emph{Purpose:} Allows capturing heterogeneity within or across
  individuals without needing to know \emph{a priori} which strategy was
  used on which trial or by which person. The model estimates the
  \emph{probability} that each data point came from each component
  strategy.
\item
  \emph{Challenge:} Mixture models often require substantial data to
  reliably distinguish between components and estimate their mixing
  proportions.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Cognitive Modeling vs.~Traditional Statistical Approaches
(e.g.,
GLM)}\label{cognitive-modeling-vs.-traditional-statistical-approaches-e.g.-glm}

How does this modeling approach differ from standard statistical
analyses you might have learned, like ANOVAs or the General Linear Model
- GLM?

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Focus:} GLM approaches typically focus on identifying
  \emph{statistical effects}: Does factor X significantly influence
  outcome Y? (e.g., Does the opponent's strategy affect the player's win
  rate?). Cognitive modeling focuses on identifying the underlying
  \emph{process or mechanism}: \emph{How} does the opponent's strategy
  lead to changes in the player's choices via specific computations
  (like learning, memory updating, or strategic reasoning)?
\item
  \textbf{Theory:} Cognitive models are usually derived from theories
  about mental processes. GLMs are more general statistical tools, often
  used agnostically regarding the specific cognitive mechanism.
\item
  \textbf{Parameters:} Cognitive models estimate parameters that often
  have direct psychological interpretations (e.g., learning rate, memory
  decay, decision threshold, bias weight). GLM parameters represent
  statistical associations (e.g., regression coefficients).
\item
  \textbf{Data Level:} Cognitive models often predict behavior at the
  trial level (e.g., predicting the choice on trial \emph{t} based on
  history up to \emph{t-1}). GLM analyses often aggregate data (e.g.,
  comparing average win rates across conditions).
\item
  \textbf{Prediction vs.~Explanation:} While both aim to explain data,
  cognitive modeling often places a stronger emphasis on generating the
  observed behavior pattern from the hypothesized mechanism, allowing
  for simulation and prediction of fine-grained details.
\end{itemize}

\emph{Example Revisited:} In the Matching Pennies game: * A GLM approach
might test if
\texttt{Payoff\ \textasciitilde{}\ BotStrategy\ *\ Role\ +\ (1\textbar{}ID)}
shows a significant effect of \texttt{BotStrategy}. * A cognitive
modeling approach would fit different strategy models (WSLS, RL, etc.)
to the choice data and compare them (using methods from Ch 7) to see
which \emph{mechanism} best explains the choices made against different
bots, potentially revealing \emph{why} performance differs (e.g., due to
changes in estimated learning rates or strategy weights).

Both approaches are valuable, but cognitive modeling aims for a deeper,
mechanistic level of explanation about the underlying cognitive
processes.

\section{Conclusion: From Observations to Verbal
Theories}\label{conclusion-from-observations-to-verbal-theories}

This chapter took us from observing behavior in a specific task -- the
Matching Pennies game -- to the crucial stage of formulating initial
theories about the cognitive processes involved. We explored how
analyzing gameplay data, considering participant reports, applying
cognitive principles (like memory limits and error proneness), and
contrasting different potential strategies (Random, WSLS, Memory-based,
RL, k-ToM) helps us generate plausible \emph{verbal models}.

We saw that the path from raw behavior to a testable model involves
significant abstraction and simplification. We also highlighted the
importance of distinguishing between the participant's experience and
the researcher's theoretical stance, and how cognitive modeling differs
from traditional statistical approaches by focusing on underlying
mechanisms.

You now have a conceptual map of candidate models and understand why
cognitive constraints matter. But verbal descriptions like
`win-stay-lose-shift' hide crucial ambiguities: Does `stay' mean always
stay or usually stay? How do we handle the first trial? In Chapter 3,
you'll implement these models in code, forcing you to make every
assumption explicit. This is where modeling becomes rigorous and often
reveals that our verbal intuitions were vaguer than we thought.

The next chapter, ``From verbal descriptions to formal models,'' tackles
exactly this challenge. We will take some of the candidate models
discussed here (like Random Choice and WSLS) and translate them into
precise mathematical algorithms and R functions. This formalization will
force us to be explicit about our assumptions and enable us to simulate
agent behavior. That simulation is itself a prerequisite: in Ch. 5 we
flip the direction of the pipeline --- instead of generating data from
known parameters, we will \emph{infer} parameters from observed data.
And in Ch. 6, before we trust any of those estimates, we will put the
entire inference procedure through a rigorous validation battery.

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{From verbal descriptions to formal
models}\label{from-verbal-descriptions-to-formal-models}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} We have
verbal theories (Ch. 2). This chapter turns them into runnable code ---
\textbf{forward models} that generate data \emph{from} parameters. This
is a prerequisite for Ch. 5, where we reverse the direction: given
observed data, we will \emph{infer} the parameters. You cannot run that
inversion without first knowing how to run the model forward.
\end{quote}

In Chapter 1, we explored how the ``physics'' of a pizza
stone---Newton's Law of Cooling---could generate temperature data. We
called this a \textbf{generative model}. In Chapter 2, you observed your
own behavior in the Matching Pennies game and proposed verbal theories
like ``Win-Stay-Lose-Shift'' or ``Random Choice.''

Now, we bridge the gap. In this chapter, we will build ``models of the
mind.'' We will translate the verbal strategies from Chapter 2 into
precise algorithms---creating agents, or \textbf{Synthetic Subjects}.

Just as we used a formula to simulate the pizza stone heating up, we
will write code to simulate a cognitive agent playing a game. If our
code can generate choice data that looks like human data, we have a
candidate model for how the mind works. If it cannot, our theory is
wrong.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-2}

This chapter focuses on translating the verbal theories from Chapter 2
into concrete, testable computational models. After completing this
chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Formalize Verbal Models:} Translate abstract strategy
  descriptions (like ``Random Choice'' or ``Win-Stay-Lose-Shift'') into
  precise algorithms.
\item
  \textbf{Implement Agent-Based Simulations:} Create simple software
  ``agents'' that execute these algorithms within a simulated
  environment (the Matching Pennies game).
\item
  \textbf{Run Virtual Experiments:} Use simulation to see how different
  strategies perform against different opponents \emph{before} we ever
  touch real data.
\item
  \textbf{Analyze Simulated Behavior:} Generate and visualize simulation
  data to understand how different strategies perform and interact under
  controlled conditions.
\item
  \textbf{Prepare for Model Fitting:} Appreciate why simulating models
  is a crucial step prior to estimating parameters from empirical data
  (covered in Chapter 5).
\end{itemize}

\section{The Value of Formalization and
Simulation}\label{the-value-of-formalization-and-simulation}

Verbal models are comfortable but dangerous. In Chapter 2, we might have
said: \emph{``The agent stays after a win.''}

But formalization forces us to answer uncomfortable questions, for
instance:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{The Persistence Question:} If they win 10 times, do they stay
  forever? Or is there a limit?
\item
  \textbf{The Error Question:} Do they \emph{always} stay, or do they
  sometimes press the wrong button by mistake?
\item
  \textbf{The First Trial Question:} What do they do before they have
  any feedback?
\end{itemize}

Writing code forces us to strip away ambiguity. It reveals hidden
assumptions and forces us to be explicit about the mechanisms driving
behavior. However, the value of this process extends beyond just
clarity. Once formalized, we can create computational agents that act
out the strategy. Simulation allows us to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Understand Emergent Behavior}: See the consequences of our
  model's rules over many trials or interactions. Does the strategy lead
  to expected patterns? Are there surprising outcomes that weren't
  obvious from the verbal description?
\item
  \textbf{Explore Parameter Space}: How does behavior change if we alter
  a model parameter (e.g., the bias in a random agent, or the stay
  probability in WSLS)?
\item
  \textbf{Generate Testable Predictions}: What specific behavioral
  patterns (e.g., choice sequences) does the model predict under
  different conditions? These predictions can then be compared to real
  data.
\item
  \textbf{Debug Model Logic}: Does the simulated behavior make sense? If
  not, it might indicate a flaw in our formalization of the verbal
  theory.
\end{itemize}

In this chapter, we will:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Formalize:} Define precise algorithms for two key strategies
  (Random Choice and WSLS).
\item
  \textbf{Implement:} Code these algorithms as reusable R functions.
\item
  \textbf{Simulate:} Run these agents in a ``Virtual Arena'' against
  different opponents.
\item
  \textbf{Scale:} Simulate many agents to understand average behavior
  and visualize the results to compare strategy effectiveness.
\end{enumerate}

\section{Defining general conditions}\label{defining-general-conditions}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Load necessary packages}
\CommentTok{\# patchwork helps combine multiple plots}
\NormalTok{pacman}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{p\_load}\NormalTok{(tidyverse, patchwork, here)}

\CommentTok{\# Number of trials per simulation}
\NormalTok{trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{120}  

\CommentTok{\# Number of agents to simulate}
\NormalTok{agents }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{100}  

\CommentTok{\# Set to TRUE to rerun everything (takes time!), FALSE to load saved results.}
\NormalTok{regenerate\_simulations }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{FALSE} 

\CommentTok{\# Optional: Set random seed for reproducibility}
\CommentTok{\# set.seed(123)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Implementing a Random Agent}\label{implementing-a-random-agent}

Our simplest model from Chapter 2 was the Random Choice agent. In
cognitive science, this serves as our \textbf{Null Hypothesis}.

A Random Agent is not just ``acting crazily''; it represents a specific,
strong theoretical claim: \textbf{The agent has no memory and no
learning.} It does not care about the past (history) or the future
(planning). It only has a bias.

\textbf{Algorithm:} On every trial \(t\), independently of what happened
at \(t-1\), choose `right' (1) with probability \(\theta\) and `left'
(0) with probability \(1-\theta\).

Let's implement this synthetic subject.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Simulation Parameters {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{120} \CommentTok{\# Number of trials for a single simulation run}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Basic Implementation {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Simulate an unbiased random agent (rate = 0.5)}
\NormalTok{rate\_unbiased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}
\NormalTok{choices\_unbiased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(trials, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ rate\_unbiased)}
\CommentTok{\# rbinom generates random draws from a binomial distribution.}
\CommentTok{\# size = 1 makes it a Bernoulli trial (0 or 1 outcome).}
\CommentTok{\# prob = rate specifies the probability of getting a 1.}

\CommentTok{\# Simulate a biased random agent (rate = 0.8, chooses \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{} 80\% of the time)}
\NormalTok{rate\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.8}
\NormalTok{choices\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(trials, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ rate\_biased)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Visualization 1: Raw Choices {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Create data frames for plotting}
\NormalTok{d\_unbiased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials, }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choices\_unbiased, }\AttributeTok{rate =}\NormalTok{ rate\_unbiased)}
\NormalTok{d\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials, }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choices\_biased, }\AttributeTok{rate =}\NormalTok{ rate\_biased)}

\CommentTok{\# Plot raw choice sequence (0s and 1s)}
\NormalTok{p1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(d\_unbiased, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ choice)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Unbiased Random Agent (Rate = 0.5)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Choice (0/1)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(d\_biased, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ choice)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Biased Random Agent (Rate = 0.8)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Choice (0/1)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.1}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Show plots side{-}by{-}side}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p1 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ p2 }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{plot\_layout}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{guides =} \StringTok{"collect"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{03-BuildingFormalModels_files/figure-pdf/ch04_implement_random_agent-1.pdf}
\end{center}

This first visualization shows the behavior of a purely random agent -
one that chooses between options with equal probability (rate = 0.5).
Looking at the jagged line jumping between 0 and 1, we can see that the
agent's choices appear truly random, with no discernible pattern. This
represents what we might expect from a player who is deliberately trying
to be unpredictable in the matching pennies game. However, this raw
choice plot can be hard to interpret. A more informative way to look at
the agent's behavior is to examine how its average rate of choosing
option 1 evolves over time:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Visualization 2: Cumulative Rate {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Calculate the cumulative proportion of \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{} choices (1s)}
\NormalTok{d\_unbiased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_unbiased }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cumulative\_rate =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(choice) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{())}
\NormalTok{d\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_biased }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cumulative\_rate =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(choice) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# Combine data for comparison plot}
\NormalTok{d\_combined }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(d\_unbiased, d\_biased) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
                \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Agent =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Rate ="}\NormalTok{, rate))}

\CommentTok{\# Plot cumulative rates}
\NormalTok{p3 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(d\_combined, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ cumulative\_rate, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ Agent)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# Reference line for 50\%}
  \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Cumulative Choice Rate Over Time"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Shows convergence towards the agent\textquotesingle{}s underlying bias"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial Number"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cumulative Proportion of Choosing \textquotesingle{}Right\textquotesingle{}"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Agent Type"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p3)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{03-BuildingFormalModels_files/figure-pdf/ch04_cumulative_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

We can clearly see how bias affects choice behavior. The unbiased agent
(rate = 0.5) stabilizes around choosing each option equally often, while
the biased agent (rate = 0.8) shows a strong preference for option 1,
choosing it approximately 80\% of the time. This comparison helps us
understand how we might detect biases in real players' behavior -
consistent deviation from 50-50 choice proportions could indicate an
underlying preference or strategy.

\subsection{Encapsulating the Agent in a
Function}\label{encapsulating-the-agent-in-a-function}

To make our agent reusable, we'll wrap the logic in a function. This is
good practice for building more complex simulations.

Note that the function is preceded by a number of commented lines. They
are called Roxygen comments, a special documentation format used in R
packages. By using these comments, we keep the code and its manual in
the same place (and if you build a package, the format allows for
automatically generating the help pages).

\subsubsection{How to read it:}\label{how-to-read-it}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Description: The first lines describe what the function does.
\item
  \texttt{@param}: Describes the inputs (arguments) the function expects
  (e.g., n\_trials, rate).
\item
  \texttt{@return}: Describes the output the function produces (e.g., a
  vector of 0s and 1s).
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Random Agent Function}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Generates a sequence of choices based on a fixed probability (rate).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Includes optional noise causing random 50/50 choices.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_trials Integer, the number of choices to generate.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param rate Numeric, the probability (0{-}1) of choosing option 1 (\textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{}).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param noise Numeric, the probability (0{-}1) of making a random 50/50 choice}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}   instead of following the rate. Default is 0 (no noise).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return A numeric vector of choices (0s and 1s).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{RandomAgent\_f }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, }\AttributeTok{rate =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Input validation}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.numeric}\NormalTok{(rate) }\SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ rate }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ rate }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
    \FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Rate must be a probability between 0 and 1."}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.numeric}\NormalTok{(noise) }\SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ noise }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ noise }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
    \FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Noise must be a probability between 0 and 1."}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \CommentTok{\# Generate base choices according to the rate}
\NormalTok{  choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ rate)}

  \CommentTok{\# Apply noise: identify trials where noise occurs}
\NormalTok{  noise\_trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ noise) }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}

  \CommentTok{\# Replace choices with random 50/50 on noise trials}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{any}\NormalTok{(noise\_trials)) \{}
\NormalTok{    choices[noise\_trials] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(noise\_trials), }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(choices)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Example usage:}
\NormalTok{example\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{RandomAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{rate =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Example choices (rate=0.7, noise=0.1):"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(example\_choices, }\AttributeTok{collapse =} \StringTok{" "}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "Example choices (rate=0.7, noise=0.1): 1 1 1 1 0 1 1 1 0 1"
\end{verbatim}

\section{Implementing a Win-Stay-Lose-Shift (WSLS)
Agent}\label{implementing-a-win-stay-lose-shift-wsls-agent}

The Random Agent was memoryless. The Win-Stay-Lose-Shift (WSLS) agent is
our first step into \textbf{strategic behavior}: it reacts to the
immediate past.

\textbf{Algorithm:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Initialize:} Make a random guess on the first trial.
\item
  \textbf{Loop (Trial \(t > 1\)):}

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    Check feedback from trial \(t-1\).
  \item
    If \textbf{Win}: Repeat the previous choice.
  \item
    If \textbf{Loss}: Switch to the opposite choice.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Noise (The ``Trembling Hand''):} With a small probability
  \texttt{noise}, ignore the strategy and pick randomly. This accounts
  for attention lapses or motor errors (often called ``trembling hand''
  in game theory).
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Implementing the WSLS
Function}\label{implementing-the-wsls-function}

\emph{Note on Programming:} In R, we usually prefer vectorization (doing
everything at once) over loops. However, many cognitive models are
inherently \textbf{sequential}: what I do now depends on what I just
saw. Therefore, we use an explicit \texttt{if/else} logic here to mirror
the cognitive process.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Win{-}Stay{-}Lose{-}Shift Agent Function}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Determines the next choice based on the previous choice and its outcome (feedback).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Includes optional noise.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param prevChoice Numeric, the agent\textquotesingle{}s choice on the previous trial (0 or 1).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param feedback Numeric, the outcome of the previous trial (1 for win, 0 for loss).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param noise Numeric, the probability (0{-}1) of making a random 50/50 choice. Default is 0.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return Numeric, the agent\textquotesingle{}s next choice (0 or 1).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{WSLSAgent\_f }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(prevChoice, feedback, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Input validation}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{prevChoice }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Previous choice must be 0 or 1."}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{feedback }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Feedback must be 0 or 1."}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.numeric}\NormalTok{(noise) }\SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ noise }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ noise }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Noise must be a probability between 0 and 1."}\NormalTok{)}

  \CommentTok{\# Core WSLS logic:}
  \CommentTok{\# If feedback is 1 (win), stay: choice = prevChoice}
  \CommentTok{\# If feedback is 0 (loss), shift: choice = 1 {-} prevChoice}
\NormalTok{  choice }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(feedback }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, prevChoice, }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ prevChoice)}

  \CommentTok{\# Apply noise if specified}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (noise }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{\&\&} \FunctionTok{runif}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ noise) \{}
    \CommentTok{\# Override with a random 50/50 choice}
\NormalTok{    choice }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(choice)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Example usage:}
\CommentTok{\# Won previous trial (feedback=1) after choosing 1:}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Next choice after win (chose 1):"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{prevChoice =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{feedback =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "Next choice after win (chose 1): 1"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Lost previous trial (feedback=0) after choosing 1:}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Next choice after loss (chose 1):"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{prevChoice =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{feedback =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "Next choice after loss (chose 1): 0"
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Simulating WSLS
vs.~Opponents}\label{simulating-wsls-vs.-opponents}

Now, let's simulate our WSLS agent playing against different opponents
to see how it performs.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Simulation Setup {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{120}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Simulation 1: WSLS vs. Biased Random Agent {-}{-}{-}}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Simulating WSLS vs. Biased Random (Rate = 0.8)...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Simulating WSLS vs. Biased Random (Rate = 0.8)...
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Opponent always chooses \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{} with 80\% probability}
\NormalTok{opponent\_choices\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{RandomAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_trials =}\NormalTok{ trials, }\AttributeTok{rate =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Initialize vectors for WSLS agent}
\NormalTok{wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, trials)}
\NormalTok{feedback\_vs\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, trials)}

\CommentTok{\# First choice is random}
\NormalTok{wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Simulation loop}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{2}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Determine feedback from previous trial (WSLS wins if choices match opponent)}
  \CommentTok{\# Note: In Matching Pennies, win condition depends on role (Matcher/Hider).}
  \CommentTok{\# Here we assume the WSLS agent is the \textquotesingle{}Matcher\textquotesingle{} for simplicity.}
\NormalTok{  prev\_feedback }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices\_biased[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  feedback\_vs\_biased[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prev\_feedback }\CommentTok{\# Store feedback for analysis}

  \CommentTok{\# WSLS agent makes choice based on previous feedback}
\NormalTok{  wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{prevChoice =}\NormalTok{ wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{],}
    \AttributeTok{feedback =}\NormalTok{ prev\_feedback,}
    \AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0} \CommentTok{\# Assuming no noise for this simulation}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}
\CommentTok{\# Record feedback for the last trial}
\NormalTok{feedback\_vs\_biased[trials] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased[trials] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices\_biased[trials], }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Create dataframe for analysis}
\NormalTok{df\_vs\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials,}
  \AttributeTok{Self\_WSLS =}\NormalTok{ wsls\_choices\_vs\_biased,}
  \AttributeTok{Opponent\_Random =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices\_biased,}
  \AttributeTok{Feedback =}\NormalTok{ feedback\_vs\_biased }\CommentTok{\# 1 if WSLS won}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Cumulative\_Performance =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(Feedback) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Simulation 2: WSLS vs. WSLS {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# (Opponent plays WSLS but tries to mismatch {-} feedback is inverted)}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Simulating WSLS vs. WSLS...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Simulating WSLS vs. WSLS...
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{wsls1\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, trials)}
\NormalTok{wsls2\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, trials)}
\NormalTok{feedback\_wsls1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, trials) }\CommentTok{\# Feedback for agent 1}

\NormalTok{wsls1\_choices[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{wsls2\_choices[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{2}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Feedback for agent 1 from previous trial (wins if match)}
\NormalTok{  prev\_feedback1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(wsls1\_choices[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ wsls2\_choices[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  feedback\_wsls1[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prev\_feedback1}

  \CommentTok{\# Feedback for agent 2 (opponent) is the opposite (wins if mismatch)}
\NormalTok{  prev\_feedback2 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ prev\_feedback1}

  \CommentTok{\# Both agents choose based on their own previous feedback}
\NormalTok{  wsls1\_choices[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(wsls1\_choices[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], prev\_feedback1, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  wsls2\_choices[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(wsls2\_choices[t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], prev\_feedback2, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{feedback\_wsls1[trials] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(wsls1\_choices[trials] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ wsls2\_choices[trials], }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Create dataframe}
\NormalTok{df\_vs\_wsls }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials,}
  \AttributeTok{Self\_WSLS =}\NormalTok{ wsls1\_choices,}
  \AttributeTok{Opponent\_WSLS =}\NormalTok{ wsls2\_choices,}
  \AttributeTok{Feedback =}\NormalTok{ feedback\_wsls1 }\CommentTok{\# 1 if Self\_WSLS won}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Cumulative\_Performance =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(Feedback) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Visualize Simulation Results {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Plot choices over time}
\NormalTok{p\_choices\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df\_vs\_biased, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Self\_WSLS, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"WSLS Agent"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Opponent\_Random }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Biased Opponent"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# Offset slightly}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"WSLS vs. Biased Random"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Choice (0/1)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_choices\_wsls }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df\_vs\_wsls, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Self\_WSLS, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"WSLS Agent 1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Opponent\_WSLS }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"WSLS Agent 2"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# Offset slightly}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"WSLS vs. WSLS"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Choice (0/1)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.1}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Plot cumulative performance}
\NormalTok{p\_perf\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df\_vs\_biased, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Cumulative\_Performance)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"WSLS Performance vs. Biased Random"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Proportion Wins"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_perf\_wsls }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df\_vs\_wsls, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Cumulative\_Performance)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"purple"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"WSLS Performance vs. WSLS"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Proportion Wins"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Arrange plots}
\NormalTok{(p\_choices\_biased }\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{ p\_choices\_wsls) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (p\_perf\_biased }\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{ p\_perf\_wsls) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{plot\_layout}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{guides =} \StringTok{"collect"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\&} \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{03-BuildingFormalModels_files/figure-pdf/ch04_wsls_vs_biased-1.pdf}
\end{center}

This cumulative performance plot reveals the overall effectiveness of
the WSLS strategy. By tracking the running average of successes, we can
see whether the strategy leads to above-chance performance in the long
run. When playing against a biased random opponent, the WSLS agent can
potentially exploit the opponent's predictable tendencies, though
success depends on how strong and consistent the opponent's bias is.
When we pit the WSLS agent against another WSLS agent, the dynamics
become more complex. Both agents are now trying to adapt to each other's
adaptations, creating a more sophisticated strategic interaction. The
resulting behavior often shows interesting patterns of mutual
adaptation, where each agent's attempts to exploit the other's strategy
leads to evolving patterns of play.

\section{Scaling Up: The Virtual
Experiment}\label{scaling-up-the-virtual-experiment}
