Now that we have our agents defined as functions, we can run
\textbf{virtual Experiments}, to better understand our models.

We will build a ``Simulation Engine'' function that:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Takes two agents (e.g., WSLS vs.~Random).
\item
  Makes them play for \texttt{N} trials.
\item
  Records every move.
\end{enumerate}

To understand the average behavior and robustness of the WSLS strategy,
we need to simulate many agents playing against opponents with varying
biases.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why run a virtual experiment instead of just testing one agent?} A
single simulation is like flipping a coin once --- you cannot tell if
the coin is fair from one flip. Running 100 simulated agents lets us
separate the signal (the model's actual tendency) from the noise (random
variation in any one run). It also lets us sweep across parameter values
(e.g., varying opponent bias from 0.5 to 1.0) to understand \emph{when}
WSLS works and when it fails --- building intuition before we ever see
real human data.
\end{quote}

First, let's create a function that runs one simulation (one WSLS agent
vs.~one opponent) and returns the results. This avoids repeating code.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Run Matching Pennies Simulation}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Simulates a full game between an Agent (Subject) and an Opponent.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_trials Integer, number of trials.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param agent\_strategy String, "WSLS" or "Random".}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param agent\_params List, parameters for the agent (e.g., list(noise=0.1)).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param opponent\_strategy String, "WSLS" or "Random".}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param opponent\_params List, parameters for the opponent (e.g., list(rate=0.8)).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return A tibble with trial{-}by{-}trial choices and outcomes.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\NormalTok{run\_simulation }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, agent\_strategy, agent\_params, opponent\_strategy, opponent\_params) \{}

  \CommentTok{\# Pre{-}allocate vectors for speed}
\NormalTok{  agent\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{  opponent\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{  agent\_feedback }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, n\_trials) }

  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Trial 1 (Initialization) {-}{-}{-}}
  \CommentTok{\# Agents behave randomly or use a specific start bias on trial 1}
\NormalTok{  agent\_choices[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  opponent\_choices[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Check winner (Assuming Agent is Matcher: Win if choices match)}
\NormalTok{  agent\_feedback[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(agent\_choices[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Loop through Game {-}{-}{-}}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{2}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_trials) \{}
    
    \CommentTok{\# 1. Agent makes a choice based on history (t{-}1)}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (agent\_strategy }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"WSLS"}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{      agent\_choices[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(agent\_choices[t}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{], agent\_feedback[t}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{], agent\_params}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{noise)}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (agent\_strategy }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Random"}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{      agent\_choices[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{RandomAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, agent\_params}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rate, agent\_params}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{noise)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
    
    \CommentTok{\# 2. Opponent makes a choice}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (opponent\_strategy }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Random"}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{      opponent\_choices[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{RandomAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, opponent\_params}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rate, opponent\_params}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{noise)}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (opponent\_strategy }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"WSLS"}\NormalTok{) \{}
      \CommentTok{\# Opponent is Hider: They win if choices MISMATCH. }
      \CommentTok{\# So their feedback is the opposite of the Agent\textquotesingle{}s feedback.}
\NormalTok{      op\_feedback }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_feedback[t}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{]}
\NormalTok{      opponent\_choices[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{WSLSAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(opponent\_choices[t}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{], op\_feedback, opponent\_params}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{noise)}
\NormalTok{    \}}

    \CommentTok{\# 3. Determine Outcome}
\NormalTok{    agent\_feedback[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(agent\_choices[t] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices[t], }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \CommentTok{\# Return data}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_trials,}
    \AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ agent\_choices,}
    \AttributeTok{opponent =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices,}
    \AttributeTok{win =}\NormalTok{ agent\_feedback}
\NormalTok{  ))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Running Scaled Simulations {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simdata"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch03\_scaled\_simulations.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_file)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Running scaled simulations...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}

  \CommentTok{\# Default WSLS parameters (Only \textquotesingle{}noise\textquotesingle{} is used by our simple WSLS agent)}
\NormalTok{  wsls\_params\_default }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

  \CommentTok{\# Simulation 1: WSLS vs. Random agents with varying biases}
\NormalTok{  opponent\_rates }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{from =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{to =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  results\_vs\_random }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{() }

  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (rate }\ControlFlowTok{in}\NormalTok{ opponent\_rates) \{}
    \CommentTok{\# Create a list to store runs for this specific rate}
\NormalTok{    rate\_runs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{()}
    
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (agent\_i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{agents) \{}
\NormalTok{       opponent\_params\_rand }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{rate =}\NormalTok{ rate, }\AttributeTok{noise =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
       
       \CommentTok{\# CORRECTED CALL: Added "WSLS" as the second argument}
\NormalTok{       sim\_run }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{run\_simulation}\NormalTok{(trials, }\StringTok{"WSLS"}\NormalTok{, wsls\_params\_default, }\StringTok{"Random"}\NormalTok{, opponent\_params\_rand)}
       
\NormalTok{       sim\_run}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_i}
\NormalTok{       sim\_run}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opponent\_rate }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ rate}
\NormalTok{       rate\_runs[[agent\_i]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_run}
\NormalTok{    \}}
    \CommentTok{\# Combine all agents for this rate and store in main list}
\NormalTok{    results\_vs\_random[[}\FunctionTok{as.character}\NormalTok{(rate)]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(rate\_runs)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \CommentTok{\# Combine everything into one big dataframe}
\NormalTok{  df\_scaled\_vs\_random }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(results\_vs\_random)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(df\_scaled\_vs\_random, sim\_data\_file)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  df\_scaled\_vs\_random }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_file)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Visualizing Results}\label{visualizing-results}

Now let's visualize the average performance of the WSLS agent against
random opponents with different biases.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Calculate cumulative performance for each simulation run}
\NormalTok{df\_scaled\_vs\_random }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ df\_scaled\_vs\_random }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent, opponent\_rate) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \CommentTok{\# CHANGED \textquotesingle{}Feedback\textquotesingle{} to \textquotesingle{}win\textquotesingle{} here:}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Cumulative\_Performance =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(win) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}

\CommentTok{\# Plot average performance over time, grouped by opponent bias rate}
\NormalTok{p\_scaled\_perf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df\_scaled\_vs\_random,}
       \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Cumulative\_Performance,}
           \AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_rate, }\AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{as.factor}\NormalTok{(opponent\_rate))) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# made color a factor for cleaner legend}
  \FunctionTok{stat\_summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fun =}\NormalTok{ mean, }\AttributeTok{geom =} \StringTok{"line"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# Discrete scale since we made rate a factor}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Average WSLS Performance vs. Random Opponents"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Performance improves as opponent\textquotesingle{}s bias increases"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial Number"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Average Proportion Wins"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Opponent Bias"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p\_scaled\_perf)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{03-BuildingFormalModels_files/figure-pdf/ch04_visualizing_results-1.pdf}
\end{center}

This plot clearly shows that the WSLS strategy becomes more effective
(higher average win rate) as the opponent becomes more predictable
(i.e., their bias rate moves further away from 0.5 towards 1.0). Against
a purely random opponent (rate=0.5), WSLS performs at chance level on
average. This highlights WSLS's ability to exploit predictability.

\section{Conclusion: The Forward
Model}\label{conclusion-the-forward-model}

In this chapter, we successfully built a \textbf{Forward Model}. * We
started with a verbal theory (WSLS). * We translated it into code
(parameters + algorithm). * We ran the code to \textbf{generate}
artificial data.

This is the ``models of the Mind'' in action. We demonstrated that a
simple mechanism (Win-Stay-Lose-Shift) can generate complex success
rates depending on the environment (the opponent).

\subsection{The Problem of Inference}\label{the-problem-of-inference}

However, in real science, we don't start with the parameters. We start
with the data (the choices participants made) and we want to find the
parameters (did they use WSLS? How much noise?).

We need to run our machine in reverse.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Forward (Simulation)}: Parameters \(\rightarrow\) Data
\item
  \textbf{Inverse (Fitting)}: Data \(\rightarrow\) Parameters
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Coming up in Chapter 5:} We reverse the arrow. The simulated
data we just created --- sequences of choices generated by agents with
\emph{known} parameters --- become our test cases. We will learn to
infer those parameters back from the choices using Bayesian inference in
Stan. This single-dataset fit is the foundation; in Chapter 6, we will
then stress-test the entire inference procedure (prior predictive
checks, parameter recovery, SBC, posterior predictive checks) before the
model is allowed anywhere near real human data.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Open Science and the Case for Transparent Implementations}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

You have just written your first formal cognitive model: a function that
takes parameters, runs an algorithm, and produces data. It is tempting
to treat this as a private computational tool --- something you run,
extract a result from, and move on. The history of cognitive modeling
suggests this would be a mistake.

A persistent challenge in the field is what we might call the
\textbf{hidden implementation problem}. Two research groups both claim
to implement the same model --- say, a Win-Stay-Lose-Shift agent, or
later in the course, an exemplar model or a reinforcement learner. Their
verbal descriptions match. Their equations look identical on paper. But
their code differs in subtle ways: how they handle the first trial,
whether they normalise probabilities before or after applying a noise
parameter, how they deal with edge cases. The result is that ``the same
model'' produces different predictions, and conflicting empirical
conclusions are blamed on the data rather than the implementation. The
field accumulates apparent contradictions that are really implementation
artifacts.

The solution is not to standardise everyone onto a single package ---
though shared, collaboratively verified implementations are genuinely
valuable. A pioneering example is the \texttt{catlearn} R package (Wills
et al. 2017), which provides formally verified implementations of
classic categorization models alongside canonical benchmark datasets, so
that results from different labs are directly comparable. The broader
principle extends to every domain of cognitive modeling: when
implementations are open and documented, errors can be found and fixed;
when they are private, the same errors propagate silently.

These course notes are designed as a contribution to this open-science
ethos. Rather than handing you pre-built scripts to run, we ask you to
implement each model yourself, document every assumption explicitly,
validate the implementation against known inputs and outputs, and
produce code you could share. You will not struggle with an equation in
silence and then quietly shelve the script because you are not sure it
is correct. You will build the habits --- transparent code, documented
choices, validated outputs --- that make science cumulative rather than
siloed. Every chapter that follows applies this principle: forward
simulator, Stan model, diagnostic battery, and validation checks, all in
open, reproducible code.

\end{tcolorbox}

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{From simulation to model fitting}\label{sec-inferring-rates}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Ch. 3 built
forward models --- code that generates data \emph{from} known
parameters. This chapter reverses the arrow: given observed choices, we
infer the parameters that most plausibly produced them. This is
\textbf{Bayesian parameter estimation}, and it is the engine at the
heart of all remaining chapters. We fit to \emph{simulated} data here
precisely because we know the ground truth --- a sanity check before the
engine is handed real human data in Ch. 6.
\end{quote}

In Chapter 3, we formalized verbal models into computational agents and
explored their behavior through simulation by \emph{setting} their
parameters (like bias \texttt{rate} or WSLS rules). However, when
analyzing real experimental data, we don't know the true parameter
values underlying a participant's behavior. The core task shifts from
simulation to \textbf{parameter estimation}: inferring the plausible
values of model parameters \emph{given} the observed data.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-3}

This chapter transitions from simulating predefined models (Chapter 3)
to inferring model parameters from data using Bayesian methods. By the
end of this chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Model Cognitive Processes:} Apply these techniques to estimate
  parameters for simple cognitive strategies, including biased choice
  and different types of memory models.
\item
  \textbf{Master Reparameterization:} Learn to rethink the mathematical
  form of models. You will appreciate the utility of the log-odds
  transformation for mapping bounded probability parameters into
  unbounded spaces.
\item
  \textbf{Read MCMC Diagnostics:} Learn the mandatory diagnostic battery
  for Bayesian inference, specifically focusing on chain convergence
  (\(\hat{R}\)) and Effective Sample Size (ESS).
\end{itemize}

In a rigorous Bayesian workflow, we \emph{never} fit a model to
empirical data without validating it first. Therefore, throughout this
chapter, we will fit our models to a single \textbf{simulated dataset}
where we know the ground truth. This allows us to learn the mechanics of
Stan and verify our single-fit posterior. In the next chapter, we will
scale this up into a full rigorous validation pipeline (Parameter
Recovery and predictive checks).

\section{The Challenge: Inferring Latent
Parameters}\label{the-challenge-inferring-latent-parameters}

Why is this a challenge?

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Parameters are Unseen:} Cognitive parameters (like bias
  strength, memory decay, learning rate) are not directly observable; we
  must infer them from behavior (choices, RTs, etc.).
\item
  \textbf{Behavior is Noisy:} Human behavior is variable. Our models
  need to account for this noise while extracting the underlying
  parameter signal.
\item
  \textbf{Model Plausibility:} We need to determine not just \emph{if} a
  model fits, but \emph{how well} it fits, what are its strengths and
  limitations, and whether the estimated parameter values are
  theoretically meaningful.
\end{enumerate}

Consider the biased agent from Chapter 3. We simulated agents with a
known \texttt{rate}. Now, imagine you only have the sequence of choices
(the \texttt{h} data) from an unknown agent. How can you estimate the
underlying \texttt{rate} that most likely generated those choices?

\textbf{Our Approach: Bayesian Inference with Stan}

This chapter introduces \textbf{Bayesian inference} as our primary tool
for parameter estimation. The Bayesian approach allows us to combine
prior knowledge about parameters with information from the observed data
(via the likelihood function) to arrive at an updated understanding
(posterior distribution) of the parameters. We will use \textbf{Stan}, a
powerful probabilistic programming language, implemented via the
\texttt{cmdstanr} R package, to:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Specify Models:} Define our cognitive models formally,
  including parameters and their prior distributions.
\item
  \textbf{Fit Models:} Use Stan's algorithms (primarily Hamiltonian
  Monte Carlo - HMC, and a faster but more risky approximation method
  called pathfinder) to sample from the posterior distribution of
  parameters given the data.
\item
  \textbf{Evaluate Fits:} Examine the results to understand parameter
  estimates and their uncertainty.
\end{enumerate}

As a crucial validation step, we will first apply these methods to
\textbf{simulated data}, where we know the true parameters. This allows
us to check if our models and fitting procedures work correctly -- a
process called \textbf{parameter recovery}.

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Terminology note --- ``parameter recovery'' used carefully:}
Here and in the conclusion of this chapter, we use ``parameter
recovery'' loosely to mean: \emph{fitting a model to one simulated
dataset and checking whether the posterior lands near the true value.}
This is a useful first sanity check. In Ch. 6 we will use the term more
precisely to mean a \emph{systematic} study that repeats this across
dozens of datasets at many different true parameter values and noise
levels. That is a qualitatively different (and stronger) validation. The
even stricter test --- Simulation-Based Calibration Checks (SBC) ---
checks whether posterior \emph{intervals} are correctly calibrated
across the entire prior space. These distinctions will matter a lot in
Ch. 6, so don't worry about them too much now, you'll hear more about
them.
\end{quote}

\section{Simulating data}\label{simulating-data}

As usual we start with simulated data, where we know the underlying
mechanisms and parameter values.

Simulated data are rarely enough (empirical data often offer unexpected
challenges), but they are a great starting point to stress test your
model: does the model reconstruct the right parameter values? Does it
reproduce the overall patterns in the data?

Here we build a new simulation of random agents with bias and noise. The
code and visualization is really nothing different from last chapter.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Flag to control whether to regenerate simulation/fitting results for the knitted files}
\CommentTok{\# Set to TRUE to rerun everything (takes time!), FALSE to load saved results.}
\NormalTok{regenerate\_simulations }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{FALSE} 

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Simulate Data for Fitting {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Goal: Generate data from known models/parameters to test our fitting procedures.}

\NormalTok{trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{120} \CommentTok{\# Number of trials per simulated agent}

\CommentTok{\# Define the agent function (copied from previosu chapter)}
\CommentTok{\# This agent chooses \textquotesingle{}1\textquotesingle{} with probability \textquotesingle{}rate\textquotesingle{}, potentially adding noise.}
\NormalTok{RandomAgentNoise\_f }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(rate, noise) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Ensure rate is probability (in case log{-}odds are passed, though not here)}
\NormalTok{  rate\_prob }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(rate) }\CommentTok{\# Assumes rate might be log{-}odds; use identity if rate is already probability}
\NormalTok{  choice }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, rate\_prob) }\CommentTok{\# Base choice}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (noise }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{\&} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, noise) }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{ }\CommentTok{\# Check if noise occurs}
\NormalTok{    choice }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\CommentTok{\# Override with random 50/50 choice}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(choice)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Generate Data Across Different Conditions {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Define relative paths for saving/loading simulation data}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_dir }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simdata"} \CommentTok{\# Assumes a \textquotesingle{}simdata\textquotesingle{} subdirectory}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch06\_randomnoise.csv"}\NormalTok{) }

\CommentTok{\# Create directory if it doesn\textquotesingle{}t exist}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_file)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generating new simulation data...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
  \CommentTok{\# Use expand\_grid for cleaner parameter combinations}
\NormalTok{  param\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{noise =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{), }\CommentTok{\# Noise levels}
    \AttributeTok{rate =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)     }\CommentTok{\# Bias rate levels (probability scale)}
\NormalTok{  )}

  \CommentTok{\# Use map\_dfr for efficient simulation across parameters}
\NormalTok{  d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(param\_grid, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(noise, rate) \{}
    \CommentTok{\# Simulate choices for one agent/condition}
\NormalTok{    choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_int}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials, }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\FunctionTok{RandomAgentNoise\_f}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(rate), noise)) }\CommentTok{\# Convert rate to log{-}odds for function if needed}
    \CommentTok{\# Create tibble for this condition}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials,}
      \AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choices,}
      \AttributeTok{true\_rate =}\NormalTok{ rate, }\CommentTok{\# Store the true rate used for generation}
      \AttributeTok{true\_noise =}\NormalTok{ noise }\CommentTok{\# Store the true noise used}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  \}) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \CommentTok{\# Calculate cumulative rate for visualization}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(true\_rate, true\_noise) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cumulative\_rate =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(choice) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}

  \CommentTok{\# Save the generated data}
  \FunctionTok{write\_csv}\NormalTok{(d, sim\_data\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generated new simulation data and saved to"}\NormalTok{, sim\_data\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing simulation data}
\NormalTok{  d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{read\_csv}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing simulation data from"}\NormalTok{, sim\_data\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Loaded existing simulation data from /Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/simdata/ch06_randomnoise.csv 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Visualize Simulated Data {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Plot cumulative rate, faceted by noise level}
\NormalTok{p1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(d, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ cumulative\_rate, }\AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ true\_rate, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ true\_rate)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_viridis\_c}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# Use perceptually uniform color scale}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Noise ="}\NormalTok{, true\_noise)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \CommentTok{\# Facet by noise level}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Simulated Choice Behavior (Cumulative Rate)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Agents with different bias rates and noise levels"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial Number"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cumulative Proportion Choosing \textquotesingle{}Right\textquotesingle{}"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"True Bias Rate"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p1)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/ch05_simulating data-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\section{How the Posterior Is Actually
Computed}\label{how-the-posterior-is-actually-computed}

Before we hand this problem to Stan, it is worth understanding what Stan
is actually \emph{doing} --- because ``posterior ∝ prior × likelihood''
is easy to say and hard to see. This section builds a posterior by hand
using a \textbf{grid approximation}: a brute-force method that works
when there is only one parameter and we are willing to evaluate every
possible value of it.

\textbf{The setup.} Suppose an agent made 3 choices, picking ``right''
on trials 1 and 3 and ``left'' on trial 2 (\texttt{h\ =\ c(1,\ 0,\ 1)}).
We want to know: what is the most plausible value of θ --- the agent's
underlying bias toward ``right''?

\textbf{Bayes' rule} tells us:

\[P(\theta \mid \text{data}) \propto P(\theta) \times P(\text{data} \mid \theta)\]

In words: the posterior is proportional to the prior (what we believed
before seeing the data) multiplied by the likelihood (how probable the
data is under each value of θ). We then re-normalize so the result sums
to 1.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Define a coarse grid of 10 candidate values for theta}
\NormalTok{theta\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Prior: uniform — Beta(1,1) — every value equally plausible before seeing data}
\NormalTok{prior }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid))}
\NormalTok{prior }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prior }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(prior)   }\CommentTok{\# Normalize to sum to 1}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Observed data}
\NormalTok{h }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)   }\CommentTok{\# 2 "right" out of 3 trials}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Likelihood: P(data | theta)}
\CommentTok{\# Each choice is Bernoulli(theta), so the joint likelihood is the product}
\CommentTok{\# across trials}
\NormalTok{likelihood }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sapply}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(th) \{}
  \FunctionTok{prod}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{dbinom}\NormalTok{(h, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ th))}
\NormalTok{\})}

\CommentTok{\# 5. Unnormalized posterior: prior * likelihood (pointwise)}
\NormalTok{posterior\_unnorm }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prior }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ likelihood}

\CommentTok{\# 6. Normalize so it sums to 1}
\NormalTok{posterior }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ posterior\_unnorm }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(posterior\_unnorm)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Display as a table {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{grid\_table }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{θ}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}           \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ theta\_grid,}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{Prior}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}       \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(prior, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{Likelihood}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}  \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(likelihood, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{),}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{Posterior}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}   \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(posterior, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(grid\_table,}
             \AttributeTok{caption =} \StringTok{"Grid approximation: prior × likelihood → posterior (3 observations)"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}rrrr@{}}
\caption{Grid approximation: prior × likelihood → posterior (3
observations)}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
θ & Prior & Likelihood & Posterior \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
θ & Prior & Likelihood & Posterior \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
0.1 & 0.1 & 0.009 & 0.011 \\
0.2 & 0.1 & 0.032 & 0.039 \\
0.3 & 0.1 & 0.063 & 0.076 \\
0.4 & 0.1 & 0.096 & 0.116 \\
0.5 & 0.1 & 0.125 & 0.152 \\
0.6 & 0.1 & 0.144 & 0.175 \\
0.7 & 0.1 & 0.147 & 0.178 \\
0.8 & 0.1 & 0.128 & 0.155 \\
0.9 & 0.1 & 0.081 & 0.098 \\
1.0 & 0.1 & 0.000 & 0.000 \\
\end{longtable}

Grid approximation with 3 observed choices. The posterior (blue) is the
pointwise product of the prior (red) and the likelihood (green),
re-normalized. Notice that the prior is flat --- every value of θ was
equally plausible beforehand --- and the posterior peaks near θ = 0.7,
which best explains seeing 2 `right' choices out of 3.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Plot all three distributions {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{grid\_long }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ grid\_table }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{rename}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{theta =} \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{θ}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prior =}\NormalTok{ Prior, }\AttributeTok{likelihood =}\NormalTok{ Likelihood, }\AttributeTok{posterior =}\NormalTok{ Posterior) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(prior, likelihood, posterior),}
               \AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"distribution"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"mass"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{distribution =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(distribution,}
                                \AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"prior"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"likelihood"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"posterior"}\NormalTok{),}
                                \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Likelihood"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Posterior"}\NormalTok{)))}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(grid\_long, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mass, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ distribution)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_col}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{position =} \StringTok{"dodge"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{width =} \FloatTok{0.08}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Likelihood"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"darkgreen"}\NormalTok{,}
                                \StringTok{"Posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{breaks =}\NormalTok{ theta\_grid) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Bayes by Hand: Prior × Likelihood → Posterior"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Data: h = c(1, 0, 1) — two \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{} choices out of three trials"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"θ  (probability of choosing \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{})"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Probability mass"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{fill     =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"top"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/04-bayes-by-hand-1.pdf}

}

\caption{Grid approximation with 3 observed choices. The posterior
(blue) is the pointwise product of the prior (red) and the likelihood
(green), re-normalized. Notice that the prior is flat --- every value of
θ was equally plausible beforehand --- and the posterior peaks near θ =
0.7, which best explains seeing 2 `right' choices out of 3.}

\end{figure}%

Three things to notice from the table and the plot:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The prior is flat.} Before seeing any data, every value of θ
  is equally plausible. This is what \texttt{Beta(1,\ 1)} means ---
  maximal prior ignorance.
\item
  \textbf{The likelihood peaks near θ = 0.7.} The agent chose ``right''
  2 out of 3 times, so θ = 0.7 makes the observed sequence most
  probable. Values near 0 or 1 make it very improbable.
\item
  \textbf{The posterior is the pointwise product, re-normalized.} It is
  narrower than the prior (the data ruled out extreme values) and
  centred on the likelihood peak. This is exactly the ``update'': the
  data shifted our beliefs from flat to concentrated.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Why Stan instead of a grid?} This worked with 1 parameter and 10
grid points. With 2 parameters and 100 grid points each, we need 100 ×
100 = 10,000 evaluations. With 5 parameters: 100⁵ = \textbf{10 billion}.
The grid grows exponentially --- this is the \emph{curse of
dimensionality}. Stan's Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) sampler sidesteps
this by \emph{sampling} from regions of high posterior probability
rather than evaluating every possible combination. The samples it
returns are the blue histogram you will see in the next section --- same
idea, just no grid required.

\section{Building our First Stan Model: Inferring Bias
Rate}\label{building-our-first-stan-model-inferring-bias-rate}

N.B. Refer to the video and slides for the step by step build-up of the
Stan code.

Let's use Stan to estimate the underlying bias \texttt{rate} (which
we'll call \texttt{theta} in the model) from just the sequence of
choices (\texttt{h}) generated by one of our simulated agents. This
model is represented in the

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png}

}

\caption{Binomial Model with Formulas}

\end{figure}%

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png}

}

\caption{Kruschke-Style Binomial Model}

\end{figure}%

We'll start with a simple case: an agent with \texttt{rate\ =\ 0.8} and
\texttt{noise\ =\ 0}.

\textbf{Preparing Data for Stan} Why a list? Well, dataframes (now
tibbles) are amazing. But they have a big drawback: they require each
variable to have the same length. Lists do not have that limitation,
they are more flexible. So, lists. We'll have to learn how to live with
them.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Subset data for one specific agent simulation (rate=0.8, noise=0)}
\NormalTok{d1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(true\_rate }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, true\_noise }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{) }\CommentTok{\# Make sure noise level matches}

\CommentTok{\# Stan requires data in a list format}
\NormalTok{data\_for\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n =}\NormalTok{ trials,     }\CommentTok{\# Number of trials (integer)}
  \AttributeTok{h =}\NormalTok{ d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice   }\CommentTok{\# Vector of choices (0s and 1s) for this agent}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{str}\NormalTok{(data\_for\_stan) }\CommentTok{\# Show the structure of the list}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
List of 2
 $ n: num 120
 $ h: num [1:120] 1 1 0 1 1 0 1 1 1 1 ...
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Model}\label{model}

We write the stan code within the R code (so I can show it to you more
easily), then we save it as a stan file, which can be loaded at a later
stage in order to compile it. {[}Missing: more info on compiling etc.{]}

Remember that the minimal Stan model requires 3 chunks, one specifying
the data it will need as input; one specifying the parameters to be
estimated; one specifying the model within which the parameters appear,
and the priors for those parameters.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# This R chunk defines the Stan code as a string and writes it to a file.}
\CommentTok{\# It\textquotesingle{}s marked eval=FALSE because we don\textquotesingle{}t run the R code defining the string here,}
\CommentTok{\# but the Stan code itself IS the important content.}

\NormalTok{stan\_model\_code }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Stan Model: Simple Bernoulli for Bias Estimation}
\StringTok{// Goal: Estimate the underlying probability (theta) of choosing 1 (\textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{})}
\StringTok{//       given a sequence of binary choices.}

\StringTok{// 1. Data Block: Declares the data Stan expects from R}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;        // Number of trials (must be at least 1)}
\StringTok{  array[n] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h; // Array \textquotesingle{}h\textquotesingle{} of length \textquotesingle{}n\textquotesingle{} containing choices (0 or 1)}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{// 2. Parameters Block: Declares the parameters the model will estimate}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} theta; // The bias parameter (probability), constrained between 0 and 1}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{// 3. Model Block: Defines the priors and the data model (used as likelihood during inference)}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Prior: Our belief about theta *before* seeing the data.}
\StringTok{  // We use a Beta(1, 1) prior, which is equivalent to a Uniform(0, 1) distribution.}
\StringTok{  // This represents maximal prior ignorance about the bias.}
\StringTok{  // \textquotesingle{}target +=\textquotesingle{} adds the log{-}probability density to the overall model log{-}probability.}
\StringTok{  target += beta\_lpdf(theta | 1, 1); // lpdf = log probability density function}

\StringTok{  // Likelihood: How the data \textquotesingle{}h\textquotesingle{} depend on the parameter \textquotesingle{}theta\textquotesingle{}.}
\StringTok{  // We model each choice \textquotesingle{}h\textquotesingle{} as a Bernoulli trial with success probability \textquotesingle{}theta\textquotesingle{}.}
\StringTok{  // The model assesses how likely the observed sequence \textquotesingle{}h\textquotesingle{} is given a value of \textquotesingle{}theta\textquotesingle{}.}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_lpmf(h | theta); // lpmf = log probability mass function (for discrete data)}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{// 4. Generated Quantities Block (Optional but useful)}
\StringTok{// Code here is executed *after* sampling, using the estimated parameter values.}
\StringTok{// Useful for calculating derived quantities or predictions.}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // let\textquotesingle{}s generate the prior for visualization}
\StringTok{  // first we define it}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower = 0, upper = 1\textgreater{} prior\_theta; }
\StringTok{  // then we generate it}
\StringTok{  prior\_theta = beta\_rng(1, 1); // Draw a random sample from the prior distribution for theta}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\CommentTok{\# Define relative path for Stan model file}
\NormalTok{stan\_model\_dir }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"stan"}
\NormalTok{stan\_file\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Create directory if needed}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir)}

\CommentTok{\# Write the Stan code to the file}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_code, stan\_file\_bernoulli)}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Stan model written to:"}\NormalTok{, stan\_file\_bernoulli, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Stan model written to: /Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch06_SimpleBernoulli.stan 
\end{verbatim}

Compiling and Fitting the Stan Model

Now we use cmdstanr to compile this model (translating it into efficient
C++ code) and then run the MCMC sampler to get posterior estimates for
theta.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# {-}{-}{-} Compile and Fit {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Specify model file path}
\NormalTok{stan\_file\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\CommentTok{\# Define path for saving fitted model object}
\NormalTok{stan\_results\_dir }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simmodels"}
\NormalTok{model\_file\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(stan\_results\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(stan\_results\_dir)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(stan\_results\_dir)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file\_bernoulli)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Compiling and fitting Stan model (Bernoulli)...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
  \CommentTok{\# Compile the Stan model (only needs to be done once unless code changes)}
\NormalTok{  mod\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_bernoulli,}
                                 \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{), }\CommentTok{\# Enable threading}
                                 \AttributeTok{stanc\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"O1"}\NormalTok{)) }\CommentTok{\# Basic optimization}

  \CommentTok{\# Sample from the posterior distribution using MCMC}
\NormalTok{  fit\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_bernoulli}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_for\_stan,          }\CommentTok{\# The data list we prepared}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,                    }\CommentTok{\# For reproducible MCMC sampling}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,                    }\CommentTok{\# Number of parallel Markov chains (recommend 4)}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\CommentTok{\# Run chains in parallel}
    \CommentTok{\#threads\_per\_chain = 1,         \# For within{-}chain parallelization (usually 1 is fine)}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,            }\CommentTok{\# Number of warmup iterations per chain (discarded)}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,          }\CommentTok{\# Number of sampling iterations per chain (kept)}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,                   }\CommentTok{\# How often to print progress updates. 0 means never since the model is so fast}
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{,            }\CommentTok{\# Controls complexity of MCMC steps (adjust if needed)}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.8}              \CommentTok{\# Target acceptance rate (adjust if divergences occur)}
\NormalTok{  )}

  \CommentTok{\# Save the fitted model object}
\NormalTok{  fit\_bernoulli}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file\_bernoulli)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Model fit completed and saved to:"}\NormalTok{, model\_file\_bernoulli, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing fitted model object}
\NormalTok{  fit\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file\_bernoulli)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing model fit from:"}\NormalTok{, model\_file\_bernoulli, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Loaded existing model fit from: /Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/simmodels/ch06_SimpleBernoulli.rds 
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Assessing MCMC Health (The Mandatory
Diagnostics)}\label{assessing-mcmc-health-the-mandatory-diagnostics}

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why check diagnostics before looking at the estimates?} The
posterior distribution Stan returns is only valid if the sampler
explored the parameter space correctly. If it did not --- due to
divergent transitions, poor mixing, or insufficient samples --- the
numbers in the posterior are artifacts of the algorithm, not properties
of the model. Looking at estimates from a broken sampler is like reading
a map drawn by a robot that kept falling over: the map looks plausible
but cannot be trusted. Diagnostics are the check that the robot walked
properly before you navigate with its map.
\end{quote}

Before we look at the parameter estimates, we MUST check whether the
model was able to sample the posterior space: the health of the MCMC
chains. Fitting a model is like sending a robotic explorer (the sampler)
to map a landscape (the posterior). If the robot's tracks are broken,
the map is mathematically invalid. We must check:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Divergences:} Did the simulation crash? (Zero tolerance).
\item
  \textbf{\(\hat{R}\) (R-hat):} Did our 4 independent chains map the
  exact same space? (Must be strictly \(< 1.01\)).
\item
  \textbf{Effective Sample Size (ESS):} Do we have enough \emph{truly
  independent} draws? (Bulk and Tail ESS should be \(> 400\)).
\item
  \textbf{Visual Diagnostics:} Trace plots (should look like a ``hairy
  caterpillar'') and Rank Histograms (should be uniform across chains).
\end{enumerate}

Let's run this mandatory diagnostic battery on our first fit:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Check for Divergent Transitions}
\NormalTok{diagnostics }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_bernoulli}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Divergent transitions per chain:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{num\_divergent, }\AttributeTok{collapse =} \StringTok{", "}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Divergent transitions per chain: 0, 0, 0, 0 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 2. Check R{-}hat and ESS metrics}
\NormalTok{fit\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_bernoulli}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{variables =} \StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{)}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(fit\_summary }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(variable, mean, rhat, ess\_bulk, ess\_tail))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 1 x 5
  variable  mean  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail
  <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 theta    0.836  1.00    2661.    3744.
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 3. Visual Checks (Trace and Rank Plots)}
\NormalTok{draws\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_bernoulli}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# A healthy trace plot is well{-}mixed with no wandering}
\NormalTok{p\_trace }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mcmc\_trace}\NormalTok{(draws\_df, }\AttributeTok{pars =} \StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{ggtitle}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Trace Plot"}\NormalTok{, }
          \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = all chains overlap and fluctuate around the same stable band"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# A healthy rank histogram has uniform bars across all chains}
\NormalTok{p\_rank }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mcmc\_rank\_overlay}\NormalTok{(draws\_df, }\AttributeTok{pars =} \StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ref\_line =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{ggtitle}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Trank plot"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = chains interleave uniformly around the reference line — no chain dominates any rank band"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_trace }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_rank}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/04 biased model quality-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 4. Look at the Posterior Estimate}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(draws\_df) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(theta, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Posterior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(prior\_theta, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Prior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prior{-}Posterior Update: Bias Parameter (θ)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = posterior (blue) is narrower than prior (red) and shifted toward the true value (dashed)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"θ  (Probability of Choosing Right)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{fill     =} \StringTok{"Distribution"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/04 biased model quality-2.pdf}
\end{center}

The posterior (blue) is substantially narrower than the prior (red) and
has shifted toward the true value of 0.8 (dashed line). The posterior
mean is approximately 0.755, not exactly 0.8 --- this is expected with
only 120 trials and is not a sign that something is wrong. What matters
is that (a) the posterior is much more informative than the prior,
confirming the data were indeed informative, and (b) the true value 0.8
sits comfortably within the credible interval. If the posterior were
identical to the prior, the data would be telling us nothing; if the
posterior were centered far from the true value and excluded it, the
model would be misspecified.

Now we build the same model, but using the log odds scale for the theta
parameter, which will become useful later when we condition theta on
variables and build multilevel models (as we can do what we want in a
log odds space and it will always be bound between 0 and 1).

\textbf{Alternative Parameterization: Log-Odds Scale}

While estimating theta directly as a probability (0-1) is intuitive, it
can sometimes be computationally advantageous or necessary for more
complex models (like multilevel models in a later chapter) to estimate
parameters on an unbounded scale. The logit (log-odds) transformation
converts a probability \(p\) (from 0 to 1) to log-odds (from \(-\infty\)
to \(+\infty\)): \[\text{logit}(p) = \log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)\]
The inverse transformation (logistic function or inv\_logit) converts
log-odds back to probability:
\[\text{inv\_logit}(x) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-x)}\] We can rewrite our
Stan model to estimate theta on the log-odds scale. However, we must be
careful with our priors, as the non-linear transformation distorts the
probability mass.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Too Narrow: A standard normal prior
  \(\lambda \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1)\) concentrates mass around
  \(p=0.5\), biasing the model toward chance.
\item
  Too Wide: A wide prior (e.g., \(\lambda \sim \text{Normal}(0, 5)\))
  pushes mass to the extremes (0 and 1), implying we expect
  deterministic behavior.
\item
  Just Right: To approximate a uniform distribution on the probability
  scale (like our Beta(1,1)), we typically use
  \(\lambda \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\). This is the specific ``sweet
  spot'' that is essentially flat, avoiding both the center-bias of
  narrow priors and the extreme-bias of wide priors.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Stan code using logit parameterization}
\NormalTok{stan\_model\_logit\_code }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Stan Model: Simple Bernoulli (Logit Parameterization)}
\StringTok{// Estimate theta on the log{-}odds scale}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real theta\_logit; // Parameter is now on the unbounded log{-}odds scale}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Prior on log{-}odds scale (e.g., Normal(0, 1.5))}
\StringTok{  // Normal(0, 1.5) on log{-}odds corresponds roughly to a diffuse prior on probability scale}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(theta\_logit | 0, 1.5);}

\StringTok{  // Likelihood using the logit version of the Bernoulli PMF}
\StringTok{  // This tells Stan that theta\_logit is on the log{-}odds scale}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h | theta\_logit);}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // Generate the prior for viz purposes}
\StringTok{  real prior\_theta = inv\_logit(normal\_rng(0, 1.5)); // Sample from the log{-}odds prior}
\StringTok{  // Convert estimate back to probability scale for easier interpretation}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} theta = inv\_logit(theta\_logit);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\CommentTok{\# Define file path}
\NormalTok{stan\_file\_logit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli\_logodds.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\CommentTok{\# Write the Stan code to the file}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_logit\_code, stan\_file\_logit)}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Stan model (logit) written to:"}\NormalTok{, stan\_file\_logit, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Stan model (logit) written to: /Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch06_SimpleBernoulli_logodds.stan 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Efficiency --- Sufficient Statistics: Aggregate IID Trials}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

When all trials are \textbf{independently and identically distributed}
(no memory, no sequential dependency, one \(\theta\) for the whole
session), you can replace a loop of \(N\) Bernoulli calls with a single
Binomial call on the \textbf{sufficient statistic} --- the total number
of successes.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Slower: N separate Bernoulli evaluations (one autodiff node each)}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N)}
  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | theta\_logit);}

\CommentTok{// Faster: one Binomial evaluation — mathematically identical for IID data}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ binomial\_logit\_lpmf(sum(h) | N, theta\_logit);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Pass \texttt{sum(h)} and \texttt{N} as integers rather than the full
array --- Stan never sees the individual trial values.

\textbf{When this applies:} single-subject models with one rate
parameter per session (Chapters 4--5).

\textbf{When it does not apply:} any model where the likelihood varies
by trial --- memory models, multilevel models with per-subject
parameters indexed by \texttt{subj\_id}, sequential belief-updating
models. For those, the per-trial loop is necessary.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# With the logit format}
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Specify where the model is}
\NormalTok{file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli\_logodds.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# File path for saved model}
\NormalTok{model\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli\_logodds.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Check if we need to rerun the simulation}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file)) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Compile the model}
\NormalTok{  mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(file, }
                       \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{),}
                       \AttributeTok{stanc\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"O1"}\NormalTok{))}
  
  \CommentTok{\# The following command calls Stan with specific options.}
\NormalTok{  samples\_biased\_logodds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_for\_stan,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \CommentTok{\#threads\_per\_chain = 1,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Save the fitted model}
\NormalTok{  samples\_biased\_logodds}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generated new model fit and saved to"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing results}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loading biased model (log{-}odds) samples...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  samples\_biased\_logodds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Available parameters:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{colnames}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(samples\_biased\_logodds}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{())), }\AttributeTok{collapse =} \StringTok{", "}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing model fit from"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Loading biased model (log-odds) samples...
Available parameters: lp__, theta_logit, prior_theta, theta, .chain, .iteration, .draw 
Loaded existing model fit from /Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/simmodels/ch06_SimpleBernoulli_logodds.rds 
\end{verbatim}

\section{Bonus: Pathfinder Variational Inference vs.~Full
MCMC}\label{bonus-pathfinder-variational-inference-vs.-full-mcmc}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Optimization: Pathfinder as Warm-Start Initialization}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Stan's default NUTS-HMC sampler draws posterior samples by simulating a
Hamiltonian dynamical system. During \textbf{warm-up}, the sampler
adapts its step size and metric --- a phase that can take hundreds of
iterations if chains start far from the posterior mass.
\textbf{Pathfinder} (Zhang et al. 2022) is a fast variational
approximation that traces the L-BFGS optimization path from an initial
point to the posterior mode and fits a series of multivariate normal
approximations along the way. It is not a gold-standard posterior, but
it tells HMC \emph{roughly where the posterior lives} before NUTS
begins.

Three strategies, in order of increasing accuracy and cost:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Strategy
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
When to use
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Typical speed
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Pathfinder alone} & Quick exploration, prior sensitivity checks
& \textasciitilde10--50× faster than NUTS \\
\textbf{NUTS with default init} & Standard analysis & Baseline \\
\textbf{Pathfinder → NUTS warm-start} & Hierarchical or complex models
where warm-up is slow & 20--60\% faster than default NUTS \\
\end{longtable}

The warm-start trick: run \texttt{mod\$pathfinder(...)} to get a
posterior approximation, then pass it as \texttt{init} to
\texttt{mod\$sample(...)}. This replaces the random Uniform(−2, 2)
starting points with draws from the Pathfinder approximation, letting
the sampler skip most of the warm-up trajectory. We will use this
pattern throughout the book for models with challenging geometry or many
parameters.

\textbf{When Pathfinder fails:} Near-degenerate posteriors (e.g., hard
funnels, flat ridges) can cause Pathfinder's variational approximation
to collapse. Always wrap it in
\texttt{tryCatch(...,\ error\ =\ function(e)\ NULL)} and fall back to
explicit per-chain inits centered on prior medians if it returns
\texttt{NULL}.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Bonus content: Benchmarking NUTS, Pathfinder, and Pathfinder{-}initialized NUTS}

\CommentTok{\# We will compare three methods:}
\CommentTok{\# 1. Standard NUTS (MCMC)}
\CommentTok{\# 2. Pathfinder (Variational Inference)}
\CommentTok{\# 3. NUTS initialized with Pathfinder}

\CommentTok{\# Define file paths for all three models}
\NormalTok{file\_nuts\_std  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_benchmark\_nuts\_std.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{file\_pathfinder }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_benchmark\_pathfinder.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{file\_nuts\_init  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_benchmark\_nuts\_init.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{stan\_file\_logit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_SimpleBernoulli\_logodds.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  stan\_file\_logit, }
  \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{stanc\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"O1"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} 1. Run Standard NUTS (Baseline) {-}{-}{-}}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(file\_nuts\_std)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Running Standard NUTS...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  fit\_nuts\_std }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_for\_stan,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\CommentTok{\# Standard warmup}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_nuts\_std}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(file\_nuts\_std)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_nuts\_std }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(file\_nuts\_std)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} 2. Run Pathfinder {-}{-}{-}}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(file\_pathfinder)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Running Pathfinder...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  fit\_pathfinder }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{pathfinder}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_for\_stan,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{draws =} \DecValTok{4000}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{num\_paths =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{single\_path\_draws =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{history\_size =} \DecValTok{50}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_pathfinder}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(file\_pathfinder)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_pathfinder }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(file\_pathfinder)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} 3. Run NUTS initialized with Pathfinder {-}{-}{-}}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(file\_nuts\_init)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Running NUTS initialized with Pathfinder...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  fit\_nuts\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_for\_stan,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,    }\CommentTok{\# Reduced warmup allowed by better init!}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{init =}\NormalTok{ fit\_pathfinder, }\CommentTok{\# \textless{}{-}{-}{-} The Magic Line}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_nuts\_init}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(file\_nuts\_init)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_nuts\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(file\_nuts\_init)}
\NormalTok{\}}


\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} COMPARISON: TIMING {-}{-}{-}}

\CommentTok{\# Extract execution times (in seconds)}
\CommentTok{\# Note: $time()$total gives the wall clock time for the computation}
\NormalTok{time\_nuts\_std  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_nuts\_std}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{time}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total}
\NormalTok{time\_pathfinder }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_pathfinder}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{time}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total}
\CommentTok{\# For the initialized model, the total workflow time is Pathfinder + NUTS}
\NormalTok{time\_nuts\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ time\_pathfinder }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ fit\_nuts\_init}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{time}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total }

\CommentTok{\# Create a summary table}
\NormalTok{benchmarks }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Method =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Standard NUTS"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Pathfinder"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Pathfinder + NUTS"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Time\_Sec =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(time\_nuts\_std, time\_pathfinder, time\_nuts\_init),}
  \AttributeTok{Speedup\_vs\_Std =}\NormalTok{ time\_nuts\_std }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ Time\_Sec}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(benchmarks)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
