  Method            Time_Sec Speedup_vs_Std
  <chr>                <dbl>          <dbl>
1 Standard NUTS        0.312          1    
2 Pathfinder           0.270          1.16 
3 Pathfinder + NUTS    0.798          0.391
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} COMPARISON: POSTERIORS {-}{-}{-}}

\CommentTok{\# Extract draws}
\NormalTok{draws\_std  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_nuts\_std}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Method =} \StringTok{"Standard NUTS"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{draws\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_pathfinder}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Method =} \StringTok{"Pathfinder"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{draws\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_nuts\_init}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Method =} \StringTok{"Pathfinder + NUTS"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Combine}
\NormalTok{all\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(draws\_std, draws\_path, draws\_init)}

\CommentTok{\# Plot}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(all\_draws, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ Method)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Posterior Comparison"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Pathfinder approximates well; Initialized NUTS refines it faster than Standard NUTS"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Theta (Rate)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/ch05_intro to pathfinded-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\section{Moving Beyond Simple Bias: Memory
Models}\label{sec-memory-models}

The simple biased agent model assumes choices are independent over time,
influenced only by a fixed \texttt{theta}. However, behavior is often
history-dependent. Let's explore models where the choice probability
\texttt{theta} on trial \emph{t} depends on previous events.

\subsection{Memory Model 1: GLM-like Approach (External
Predictor)}\label{memory-model-1-glm-like-approach-external-predictor}

One way to incorporate memory is to treat a summary of past events as an
external predictor influencing the current choice probability, similar
to a predictor in a Generalized Linear Model (GLM).

Let's assume the choice probability \texttt{theta} depends on the
\textbf{cumulative rate of the opponent's `right' choices} observed up
to the \emph{previous} trial.To make the variable more intuitive we code
previous rate - which is bound to a probability 0-1 space - into
log-odds via a logit link/transformation. In this way a previous rate
with more left than right choices will result in a negative value,
thereby decreasing our propensity to choose right; and one with more
right than left choices will result in a positive value, thereby
increasing our propensity to choose right.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# We subset to only include no noise and a specific rate}
\NormalTok{d1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} 
  \FunctionTok{subset}\NormalTok{(true\_noise }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{ true\_rate }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} 
  \FunctionTok{rename}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Other =}\NormalTok{ choice) }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} 
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cumulativerate =} \FunctionTok{lag}\NormalTok{(cumulative\_rate, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \CommentTok{\# no prior info at first trial}
\NormalTok{d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate[d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.01}
\NormalTok{d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate[d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.99}

\CommentTok{\# Now we create the memory agent with a coefficient of 1 (in log odds)}
\NormalTok{MemoryAgent\_f }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(bias, beta, cumulativerate)\{}
\NormalTok{    choice }\OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(bias }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(cumulativerate)))}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(choice)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Self[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{RandomAgentNoise\_f}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\CommentTok{\# 0.5 in the transformed space}

\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{2}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{trials) \{}
\NormalTok{  d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Self[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{MemoryAgent\_f}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{bias =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{beta =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate[i])}
\NormalTok{\}}

\DocumentationTok{\#\# Create the data}
\NormalTok{data\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{h =}\NormalTok{ d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Self,}
  \AttributeTok{memory =}\NormalTok{ d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{cumulativerate }\CommentTok{\# this creates the new parameter: the rate of right hands so far in log{-}odds}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// The input (data) for the model. n of trials and h for (right and left) hand}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{ int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;}
\StringTok{ array[n] int h;}
\StringTok{ vector[n] memory; // here we add the new variable between 0.01 and .99}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{// The parameters accepted by the model. }
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real bias; // how likely is the agent to pick right when the previous rate has no information (50{-}50)?}
\StringTok{  real beta; // how strongly is previous rate impacting the decision?}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{// The model to be estimated. }
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // priors}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(bias | 0, .3);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(beta | 0, .5);}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  // model}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h | bias + beta * logit(memory));}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // Generate prior samples for Prior{-}Posterior Update checks}
\StringTok{  real bias\_prior = normal\_rng(0, 0.3);}
\StringTok{  real beta\_prior = normal\_rng(0, 0.5);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  stan\_model,}
  \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch06\_MemoryBernoulli.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch06_MemoryBernoulli.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Specify where the model is}
\NormalTok{file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch06\_MemoryBernoulli.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# File path for saved model}
\NormalTok{model\_file\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_MemoryBernoulli.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Check if we need to rerun the simulation}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file\_memory)) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Compile the model}
\NormalTok{  mod\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(file)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# The following command calls Stan with specific options.}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_memory,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{threads\_per\_chain =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Save the fitted model}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file\_memory)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generated new model fit and saved to"}\NormalTok{, model\_file\_memory, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing results}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loading memory model samples...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file\_memory)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Available parameters:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{colnames}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(samples\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{())), }\AttributeTok{collapse=}\StringTok{", "}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing model fit from"}\NormalTok{, model\_file\_memory, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Available parameters: lp__, bias, beta, bias_prior, beta_prior, .chain, .iteration, .draw 
Loaded existing model fit from /Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/simmodels/ch06_MemoryBernoulli.rds 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Check if samples\_memory exists}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{exists}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"samples\_memory"}\NormalTok{)) \{}

\NormalTok{\}}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 5 x 10
  variable     mean   median    sd   mad      q5     q95  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail
  <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 lp__     -70.3    -70.0    0.971 0.736 -72.2   -69.4   1.000     644.     733.
2 bias       0.304    0.311  0.252 0.244  -0.120   0.717 1.01      383.     555.
3 beta       0.518    0.513  0.222 0.225   0.160   0.882 1.01      427.     474.
4 bias_pr~  -0.0138  -0.0106 0.300 0.292  -0.509   0.466 1.000    2201.    1758.
5 beta_pr~   0.0302   0.0395 0.491 0.496  -0.770   0.814 1.000    1861.    1940.
\end{verbatim}

The trace plots should show the same ``hairy caterpillar'' pattern as
before --- well-mixed chains with no systematic drift. The
prior-posterior update plots tell the story: the posterior for
\texttt{bias} should be narrow and centred near 0 (log-odds of 0.5 =
unbiased), and the posterior for \texttt{beta} should be centred near 1
(the true generating value). The data have meaningfully updated both
parameters away from their priors. Formal quality checks --- prior
predictive checks, systematic parameter recovery, and SBC --- come in
Ch. 6.

\subsection{Memory Model 2: Internal State
Variable}\label{memory-model-2-internal-state-variable}

Instead of feeding memory as external data, we can model it as an
internal state that updates within the model.

For this specific model --- where memory is simply a running average of
the opponent's choices --- the update rule depends only on the observed
data (\texttt{other}), not on any parameter being estimated. We should
therefore calculate it in Stan's \texttt{transformed\ data} block.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Efficiency Rule 1 --- Block Placement: Run it Once, Not Thousands
of Times}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Every Stan model has blocks that execute at very different frequencies.
Choosing the \textbf{right block} is the most impactful single
optimization:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Block
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Executes
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Use for
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{transformed\ data} & \textbf{Once}, before sampling & Any
quantity derived \textbf{only from data} --- precomputed distances,
normalized inputs, log-transforms of fixed constants \\
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} & \textbf{Every leapfrog step} &
Deterministic functions of parameters whose gradients must flow backward
--- recursive states, reparameterizations, predicted values used in the
likelihood \\
\texttt{model} (local \texttt{\{\}} block) & Every leapfrog step,
\textbf{not saved} & Scratch variables for accumulating
\texttt{target\ +=}; no overhead from the saved-draw tape \\
\texttt{generated\ quantities} & \textbf{Once per saved draw}
(post-warmup only) & PPCs, log-likelihoods, prior samples --- expensive
quantities you only need in the final posterior \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Concrete cost of misplacement.} A typical fit runs 4 chains ×
1000 warmup × \textasciitilde6 leapfrog steps per draw =
\textasciitilde24 000 leapfrog evaluations. Any computation in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} runs \textasciitilde24 000 times. The
same computation in \texttt{transformed\ data} runs exactly once. For a
model with 500 trials, precomputing 500 memory values in
\texttt{transformed\ data} rather than \texttt{transformed\ parameters}
saves \textasciitilde12 million floating-point operations per fit.

\textbf{The decision tree:}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Does this depend \textbf{only on data} (not on any parameter)? →
  \texttt{transformed\ data}
\item
  Does this depend on \textbf{parameters} and does the likelihood or
  another parameter need it? → \texttt{transformed\ parameters}
\item
  Is this needed \textbf{only after sampling} (PPC draws, LOO
  log-likelihoods)? → \texttt{generated\ quantities}, gated by
  \texttt{run\_diagnostics} (introduced properly in Ch. 5)
\item
  Is this just a temporary accumulator inside a loop? → Local variable
  inside \texttt{\{\}} in \texttt{model} or
  \texttt{transformed\ parameters}
\end{enumerate}

In the memory model below, \texttt{memory} depends only on
\texttt{other} (data) → \texttt{transformed\ data}. In the forgetting
model that follows, \texttt{memory} depends on the free parameter
\texttt{forgetting} → local variable inside \texttt{model}.

\textbf{Corollary --- pre-compute in R when possible.} If a quantity
depends only on data and will never change between fits, computing it in
R and passing it as a data field is even faster than
\texttt{transformed\ data}: Stan never sees the raw computation at all.
For example, the multilevel memory model in Chapter 6 pre-computes the
running opponent rate in R and passes \texttt{opp\_rate\_prev} as a
vector, rather than recomputing it inside Stan on every chain.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Efficiency --- Vectorize the Likelihood Call}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Stan evaluates each \texttt{\textasciitilde{}} or \texttt{target\ +=}
statement as a separate autodiff node. Replacing a trial-level loop with
a \textbf{vectorized distribution call} collapses \(N\) nodes into one,
reducing autodiff graph size and improving cache locality.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Slower: N separate nodes in the autodiff graph}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N)}
  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | theta\_logit);}

\CommentTok{// Faster: one vectorized call — h and theta\_logit are both length{-}N vectors}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h | theta\_logit);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The vectorized form works whenever \texttt{theta\_logit} is either a
\textbf{scalar} (broadcasts to all trials) or a \textbf{vector of the
same length as \texttt{h}}. In the multilevel model of Chapter 6,
\texttt{target\ +=\ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y\ \textbar{}\ theta\_logit{[}subj\_id{]})}
vectorizes the full N\_total trial loop by scatter-indexing the
per-subject parameters.

\textbf{Caveat:} vectorization is not available for mixture likelihoods
(\texttt{log\_mix}) because each observation requires its own separate
\texttt{log\_mix} call. The loop is unavoidable there (Ch. 8).

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Create the data — run\_diagnostics = 1 for the single validation fit}
\NormalTok{data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{h =}\NormalTok{ d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Self,}
  \AttributeTok{other =}\NormalTok{ d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{Other,}
  \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{stan\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Memory{-}based choice model with prior and posterior predictions.}
\StringTok{// memory[] is data{-}derived and lives in transformed data (computed once).}
\StringTok{// run\_diagnostics gates expensive generated quantities for SBC speed.}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{ int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;}
\StringTok{ array[n] int h;}
\StringTok{ array[n] int other;}
\StringTok{ int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics; // 1 = compute PPC + log\_lik; 0 = skip (use for SBC)}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed data \{}
\StringTok{  // memory depends only on other[] (data), so it runs ONCE here.}
\StringTok{  // Moving this to transformed parameters would recompute it \textasciitilde{}24 000 times per fit.}
\StringTok{  vector[n] memory;}
\StringTok{  memory[1] = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  for (trial in 1:(n{-}1)) \{}
\StringTok{     memory[trial + 1] = memory[trial] + ((other[trial] {-} memory[trial]) / (trial + 1));}
\StringTok{     if (memory[trial + 1] \textless{} 0.01) memory[trial + 1] = 0.01;}
\StringTok{     if (memory[trial + 1] \textgreater{} 0.99) memory[trial + 1] = 0.99;}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real bias;}
\StringTok{  real beta;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(bias | 0, .3);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(beta | 0, .5);}
\StringTok{  // Vectorized likelihood — no loop needed because memory is precomputed.}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h | bias + beta * logit(memory));}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // Prior samples are always generated (cheap).}
\StringTok{  real bias\_prior = normal\_rng(0, 0.3);}
\StringTok{  real beta\_prior = normal\_rng(0, 0.5);}
\StringTok{  // Expensive diagnostics are gated — set run\_diagnostics = 0 during SBC.}
\StringTok{  vector[n] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int y\_rep;}
\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    for (trial in 1:n) \{}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[trial] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[trial] | bias + beta * logit(memory[trial]));}
\StringTok{      y\_rep[trial]   = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(bias + beta * logit(memory[trial]));}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  stan\_model,}
  \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch06\_InternalMemory.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch06_InternalMemory.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Specify where the model is}
\NormalTok{file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_InternalMemory.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# File path for saved model}
\NormalTok{model\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_InternalMemory.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Check if we need to rerun the simulation}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file)) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Compile the model}
\NormalTok{  mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(file, }
                       \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{),}
                       \AttributeTok{stanc\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"O1"}\NormalTok{))}
  
  \CommentTok{\# The following command calls Stan with specific options.}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_internal }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{threads\_per\_chain =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Save the fitted model}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_internal}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generated new model fit and saved to"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing results}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_internal }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing model fit from"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 2. Prior{-}Posterior Update Check}
\NormalTok{p1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ draws\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(bias, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Posterior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ draws\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(bias\_prior, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Prior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}

  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ draws\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(beta, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Posterior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ draws\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(beta\_prior, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Prior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"red"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}

  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ draws\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(bias, beta), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{04-InferringRates_files/figure-pdf/ch05_internal memory stan model-3.pdf}
\end{center}

Now that we know how to model memory as an internal state, we can play
with making the update discount the past, setting a parameter that
indicates after how many trials memory is lost, etc.

\subsection{Memory Model 3: Exponential Forgetting (similar to
RL)}\label{memory-model-3-exponential-forgetting-similar-to-rl}

A more cognitively plausible memory model incorporates forgetting, where
recent events have more influence than distant ones. This can be
implemented by adding a \texttt{forgetting} parameter (often called a
learning rate, α, in RL) that controls the weight of the most recent
outcome versus the previous memory state.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Memory model with exponential forgetting (learning{-}rate style).}
\StringTok{// memory[] depends on the free parameter \textquotesingle{}forgetting\textquotesingle{}, so it cannot go in}
\StringTok{// transformed data. It lives as a local vector inside the model block —}
\StringTok{// computed at every leapfrog step but never saved to the posterior draws.}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int h;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int other;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real bias;}
\StringTok{  real beta;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} forgetting;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += beta\_lpdf(forgetting | 1, 1);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(bias | 0, .3);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(beta | 0, .5);}

\StringTok{  // memory is a LOCAL variable — declared inside model, so it lives on the}
\StringTok{  // stack and is discarded after each leapfrog step without touching the saved{-}}
\StringTok{  // draw tape. This is the right pattern when memory depends on a parameter.}
\StringTok{  vector[n] memory;}
\StringTok{  for (trial in 1:n) \{}
\StringTok{    if (trial == 1) memory[trial] = 0.5;}
\StringTok{    target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[trial] | bias + beta * logit(memory[trial]));}
\StringTok{    if (trial \textless{} n) \{}
\StringTok{      memory[trial + 1] = (1 {-} forgetting) * memory[trial] + forgetting * other[trial];}
\StringTok{      if (memory[trial + 1] \textless{}= 0) memory[trial + 1] = 0.01;}
\StringTok{      if (memory[trial + 1] \textgreater{}= 1) memory[trial + 1] = 0.99;}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  real bias\_prior      = normal\_rng(0, 0.3);}
\StringTok{  real beta\_prior      = normal\_rng(0, 0.5);}
\StringTok{  real forgetting\_prior = beta\_rng(1, 1);}
\StringTok{  vector[n] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int y\_rep;}
\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    // Recompute memory for the saved draw — runs once per posterior sample.}
\StringTok{    vector[n] memory\_gq;}
\StringTok{    for (trial in 1:n) \{}
\StringTok{      if (trial == 1) memory\_gq[trial] = 0.5;}
\StringTok{      real lin = bias + beta * logit(memory\_gq[trial]);}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[trial] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[trial] | lin);}
\StringTok{      y\_rep[trial]   = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(lin);}
\StringTok{      if (trial \textless{} n) \{}
\StringTok{        memory\_gq[trial + 1] = (1 {-} forgetting) * memory\_gq[trial] + forgetting * other[trial];}
\StringTok{        if (memory\_gq[trial + 1] \textless{}= 0) memory\_gq[trial + 1] = 0.01;}
\StringTok{        if (memory\_gq[trial + 1] \textgreater{}= 1) memory\_gq[trial + 1] = 0.99;}
\StringTok{      \}}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  stan\_model,}
  \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch06\_InternalMemory2.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch06_InternalMemory2.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Specify where the model is}
\NormalTok{file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch06\_InternalMemory2.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# File path for saved model}
\NormalTok{model\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch06\_InternalMemory2.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Check if we need to rerun the simulation}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file)) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Compile the model}
\NormalTok{  mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(file, }
                       \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{),}
                       \AttributeTok{stanc\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"O1"}\NormalTok{))}
  
\NormalTok{  data\_forgetting }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{modifyList}\NormalTok{(data, }\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L))}
  \CommentTok{\# The following command calls Stan with specific options.}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_forgetting }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_forgetting,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.95}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Save the fitted model}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_forgetting}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generated new model fit and saved to"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing results}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_forgetting }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing model fit from"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{samples\_memory\_forgetting}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{()}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 4 x 10
  variable    mean   median    sd   mad       q5     q95  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail
  <chr>      <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 lp__     -75.3   -74.9    1.42  1.18  -78.2    -73.5    1.00     219.     283.
2 bias       0.668   0.674  0.256 0.278   0.242    1.08   1.01     157.     168.
3 beta       0.108   0.0603 0.199 0.162  -0.136    0.478  1.01     109.     322.
4 forgett~   0.243   0.141  0.241 0.157   0.0108   0.802  1.02     165.     262.
\end{verbatim}

The memory model we've implemented can be seen as part of a broader
family of models that track and update beliefs based on incoming
evidence. Let's explore how it relates to some key frameworks.

\section{Memory Model 4: Bayesian Agent (Optimal
Updating)}\label{memory-model-4-bayesian-agent-optimal-updating}

A fully Bayesian agent wouldn't just track the rate but maintain a full
probability distribution (a Beta distribution) over the likely bias of
the opponent, updating it optimally according to Bayes' rule after each
observation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;  // number of trials}
\StringTok{  array[n] int h;  // agent\textquotesingle{}s choices (0 or 1)}
\StringTok{  array[n] int other;  // other player\textquotesingle{}s choices (0 or 1)}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} alpha\_prior;  // Prior alpha parameter}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} beta\_prior;   // Prior beta parameter}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[n] alpha;  // Alpha parameter at each trial}
\StringTok{  vector[n] beta;   // Beta parameter at each trial}
\StringTok{  vector[n] rate;   // Expected rate at each trial}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  // Initialize with prior}
\StringTok{  alpha[1] = alpha\_prior;}
\StringTok{  beta[1] = beta\_prior;}
\StringTok{  rate[1] = alpha[1] / (alpha[1] + beta[1]);}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  // Sequential updating of Beta distribution}
\StringTok{  for(t in 2:n) \{}
\StringTok{    // Update Beta parameters based on previous observation}
\StringTok{    alpha[t] = alpha[t{-}1] + other[t{-}1];}
\StringTok{    beta[t] = beta[t{-}1] + (1 {-} other[t{-}1]);}
\StringTok{    }
\StringTok{    // Calculate expected rate}
\StringTok{    rate[t] = alpha[t] / (alpha[t] + beta[t]);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Priors on hyperparameters}
\StringTok{  target += gamma\_lpdf(alpha\_prior | 2, 1);}
\StringTok{  target += gamma\_lpdf(beta\_prior | 2, 1);}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  // Agent\textquotesingle{}s choices follow current rate estimates}
\StringTok{  for(t in 1:n) \{}
\StringTok{    target += bernoulli\_lpmf(h[t] | rate[t]);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // We strictly reserve posterior predictive checks for Chapter 6.}
\StringTok{  // Here, we just calculate the initial expected rate for reference.}
\StringTok{  real initial\_rate = alpha\_prior / (alpha\_prior + beta\_prior);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  stan\_model,}
  \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch06\_BayesianMemory.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch06_BayesianMemory.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DocumentationTok{\#\# Specify where the model is}
\NormalTok{file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch06\_BayesianMemory.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# File path for saved model}
\NormalTok{model\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch06\_BayesianMemory.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Check if we need to rerun the simulation}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file)) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Compile the model}
\NormalTok{  mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(file)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# The following command calls Stan with specific options.}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_bayes }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{threads\_per\_chain =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Save the fitted model}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_bayes}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Generated new model fit and saved to"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Load existing results}
\NormalTok{  samples\_memory\_bayes }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing model fit from"}\NormalTok{, model\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}

\section{Relationship to
Rescorla-Wagner}\label{relationship-to-rescorla-wagner}

The Rescorla-Wagner model of learning---which drives the Reinforcement
Learning agents we will build in in a later chapter follows the form:

\[V_{t+1} = V_{t} + \alpha (R_{t} - V_{t})\]

where:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \(V_{t}\) is the current Value estimate (in our code: memory{[}t{]}).
\item
  \(\alpha\) is the Learning Rate (in our code: forgetting).
\item
  \(R_{t}\) is the Reward or Outcome (in our code: other{[}t{]}).
\item
  \((R_{t} - V_{t})\) is the Prediction Error.
\end{itemize}

Our memory model with a forgetting parameter follows a very similar
structure:\[\text{memory}_{t+1} = (1 - \text{forgetting}) \times \text{memory}_{t} + \text{forgetting} \times \text{outcome}_{t}\]If
we expand this, we can see it is mathematically identical to the
Rescorla-Wagner update rule:\[\begin{aligned}
\text{memory}_{t+1} &= \text{memory}_{t} - \text{forgetting} \times \text{memory}_{t} + \text{forgetting} \times \text{outcome}_{t} \\
\text{memory}_{t+1} &= \text{memory}_{t} + \text{forgetting} \times (\text{outcome}_{t} - \text{memory}_{t})
\end{aligned}\]

This reveals that our ``exponential forgetting'' memory model is
actually a standard Reinforcement Learning agent with a big fake
moustache. The forgetting parameter dictates how quickly the agent
updates their beliefs (value estimate) based on new prediction errors.

\subsection{Connection to Kalman
Filters}\label{connection-to-kalman-filters}

Our memory model updates beliefs about the probability of right-hand
choices using a weighted average of past observations. This is
conceptually similar to how a Kalman filter works, though simpler:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Kalman filters maintain both an estimate and uncertainty about that
  estimate
\item
  They optimally weight new evidence based on relative uncertainty
\item
  Our forgetting model uses a fixed weighting scheme (1/trial or the
  forgetting parameter)
\item
  The Bayesian Agent (\texttt{ch06\_BayesianMemory.stan}) captures
  uncertainty via the Beta distribution, similar in spirit but simpler
  than a Kalman filter.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Connection to Hierarchical Gaussian Filter
(HGF)}\label{connection-to-hierarchical-gaussian-filter-hgf}

The HGF extends these ideas by:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Tracking beliefs at multiple levels
\item
  Allowing learning rates to vary over time
\item
  Explicitly modeling environmental volatility
\end{itemize}

Our model could be seen as the simplest case of an HGF where:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  We only track one level (probability of right-hand choice)
\item
  Have a fixed learning rate (forgetting parameter)
\item
  Don't explicitly model environmental volatility
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Implications for Model
Development}\label{implications-for-model-development}

Understanding these relationships helps us think about how models relate
to each other and to extend our model:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  We could add uncertainty estimates to get Kalman-like behavior
\item
  We could make the forgetting parameter dynamic to capture changing
  learning rates
\item
  We could add multiple levels to track both immediate probabilities and
  longer-term trends
\end{itemize}

Each extension would make the model more flexible but also more complex
to fit to data. The choice depends on our specific research questions
and available data.

\section{Conclusion: Estimating Parameters and Exploring
Memory}\label{conclusion-estimating-parameters-and-exploring-memory}

This chapter marked a crucial transition from simulating models with
known parameters to the core task of \textbf{parameter estimation}:
inferring plausible parameter values from observed data using Bayesian
inference and Stan.

We learned how to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Specify Bayesian models} in Stan, defining data, parameters,
  priors, and data models.
\item
  \textbf{Fit models} using \texttt{cmdstanr} to obtain posterior
  distributions for parameters like choice bias (\texttt{theta}).
\item
  \textbf{Utilize transformations} like log-odds for computational
  benefits and flexible prior specification.
\item
  \textbf{Validate our fitting procedure} through \textbf{parameter
  recovery}, ensuring our models can retrieve known values from
  simulated data.
\item
  \textbf{Implement different cognitive models} for history-dependent
  choice, exploring various ways to represent \textbf{memory}: as an
  external predictor, an internal updating state, incorporating
  exponential forgetting (linking to Reinforcement Learning principles),
  and even as a fully Bayesian belief update.
\item
  \textbf{Recognize connections} between these models and broader
  frameworks like Kalman filters and HGF.
\end{itemize}

By fitting these models, we moved beyond simply describing behavior
(like the cumulative rates in Chapter 3) to quantifying underlying
latent parameters (like \texttt{bias}, \texttt{beta},
\texttt{forgetting}).

However, a single fit to a single simulated dataset is only a
preliminary sanity check --- the equivalent of flipping a coin once to
check if it's fair. It does not tell us whether the model behaves well
across a wide range of parameter values, whether our credible intervals
are correctly calibrated, or whether the fitted model can actually
reproduce the patterns in the data.

\textbf{Coming up in Chapter 6:} We take the Stan models built in this
chapter and subject them to a four-phase validation battery before they
are allowed near real human data:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Prior Predictive Check} --- do the priors allow only
  cognitively plausible behavior?
\item
  \textbf{Parameter Recovery} --- does the model find the right answer
  across many simulated datasets and noise levels?
\item
  \textbf{Simulation-Based Calibration Checks (SBC)} --- are our
  posterior intervals actually calibrated (does a 95\% CI contain the
  truth 95\% of the time)?
\item
  \textbf{Posterior Predictive Check} --- does the fitted model
  reproduce key patterns in the observed data?
\end{enumerate}

Only after passing all four tests does a model earn the right to touch
real empirical data.

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Assessing Model Quality}\label{sec-model-quality-checks}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Ch. 5 fit a
Stan model to one simulated dataset. Obtaining a posterior is not the
same as trusting it. This chapter is the validation gate: a six-phase
battery the model must pass before touching real human data. These
techniques reappear in every subsequent chapter --- learn them once
here, apply them everywhere.
\end{quote}

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-4}

In Chapter 5, we learned how to write Stan code and infer parameters for
a single simulated dataset. However, obtaining a posterior distribution
does not mean the model is correct or the inference is reliable.

This chapter introduces the strict diagnostic battery required to
validate any cognitive model. After completing this chapter, you will be
able to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Assess Generative Plausibility (Prior Predictive Checks):}
  Simulate data from your model's priors \emph{before} fitting to ensure
  the mathematical bounds and prior assumptions are physically and
  cognitively plausible, that is, generate plausible behaviors.
\item
  \textbf{Validate Identifiability (Parameter Recovery):} Show that your
  model can accurately recover known ground-truth parameters across
  different noise levels.
\item
  \textbf{Calibrate Uncertainty (Simulation-Based Calibration Checks):}
  Use SBC to guarantee your computational engine is unbiased and your
  posterior uncertainties are mathematically sound.
\item
  \textbf{Perform Posterior Predictive Checks:} Simulate data from your
  \emph{fitted} model to assess whether it can reproduce key patterns
  observed in the actual data.
\item
  \textbf{Visualize Prior-Posterior Updates:} Compare prior and
  posterior distributions to understand how the data informed parameter
  estimates.
\item
  \textbf{Test Robustness (Sensitivity Analysis):} Evaluate how
  sensitive your parameter estimates are to the choice of prior
  distributions using both manual methods and the \texttt{priorsense}
  package.
\end{itemize}

Before going into each phase in detail, here is a one-row summary of
what each check does and what a failure looks like. Return to this table
whenever you feel lost.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Phase
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Check
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Core question
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What failure looks like
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & Mathematical formulation & Is the Stan code doing what we think? &
Model block and generated quantities disagree \\
2 & Prior Predictive & Do the priors allow only plausible behaviour? &
Simulated data saturates impossible boundaries \\
3 & Posterior Predictive & Does the fitted model reproduce observed
patterns? & Observed statistic falls in the tail of replicates \\
4 & Prior-Posterior Update & Did the data teach the model anything? &
Posterior ≈ prior; or posterior far from truth \\
5 & Sensitivity & Do conclusions change if we tweak the prior? & Large
posterior shift under small prior perturbation \\
6 & Parameter Recovery + SBC & Can the engine find the truth? Are
credible intervals calibrated? & Recovery off the diagonal; rank
histogram U-shaped or skewed \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Note on terminology:} In Ch. 5 we used ``parameter recovery''
loosely to mean fitting one simulated dataset and checking whether the
posterior landed near the true value. Phases 6a--b here are the
\emph{rigorous} version: systematic recovery across many parameter
values (6a) and formal calibration of the posterior intervals across the
full prior space (SBC, 6b).

\section{Introduction: Why Parameter Estimates Aren't
Enough}\label{introduction-why-parameter-estimates-arent-enough}

In the previous chapters, we journeyed from observing behavior (Chapter
2), formalizing strategies (Chapter 3), to fitting models and estimating
parameters (Chapter 5).

But how confident are we about the model, its assumptions, the way we
coded it? The model itself, the structure we assumed could be
mathematically misspecified (not do what we think it does),
unidentifiable (produce similar behaviors with an infinite set of
combinations of parameter values), and/or simply unable to capture the
data (sometimes even the data that we simulated from it!).

While rigorous Bayesian workflows can take many forms, here we structure
our validations in \textbf{six sequential phases}, each answering a
different question before we are allowed to proceed:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Phase 1} confirms the Stan code is mathematically well-formed.
\item
  \textbf{Phase 2} (Prior Predictive) checks that our priors generate
  plausible data before we see any observations.
\item
  \textbf{Phase 3} (Posterior Predictive) checks that the fitted model
  can reproduce the key patterns in the data it was trained on.
\item
  \textbf{Phase 4} (Prior-Posterior Update) visualises how much the data
  actually moved our beliefs.
\item
  \textbf{Phase 5} (Sensitivity) tests whether conclusions hold under
  small changes to the prior.
\item
  \textbf{Phase 6} (Parameter Recovery + SBC) proves the computational
  engine is unbiased and that credible intervals are correctly
  calibrated.
\end{itemize}

This chapter executes all six phases on the simple biased agent, then
repeats the workflow on the memory model --- where we will discover a
real identifiability problem and fix it.

\section{Loading Packages and Previous
Results}\label{loading-packages-and-previous-results}

Let's start by loading the necessary packages and the fitted model
object from Chapter 5. This avoids re-running the fitting process
unnecessarily and ensures consistency.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Load required packages using pacman for efficient package management}
\CommentTok{\# pacman::p\_load ensures packages are installed if missing, then loads them}
\NormalTok{pacman}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{p\_load}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  tidyverse,    }\CommentTok{\# Core suite for data manipulation (dplyr) and plotting (ggplot2)}
\NormalTok{  here,         }\CommentTok{\# For creating robust file paths relative to the project root (optional but good practice)}
\NormalTok{  posterior,    }\CommentTok{\# Provides tools for working with posterior distributions}
\NormalTok{  cmdstanr,     }\CommentTok{\# The R interface to Stan (via CmdStan)}
\NormalTok{  bayesplot,    }\CommentTok{\# Specialized plotting functions for Bayesian models}
\NormalTok{  patchwork,    }\CommentTok{\# For easily combining multiple ggplot plots}
\NormalTok{  priorsense,   }\CommentTok{\# For automated prior sensitivity analysis}
\NormalTok{  SBC,          }\CommentTok{\# Simulation{-}Based Calibration Checks}
\NormalTok{  future,       }\CommentTok{\# Parallel processing}
\NormalTok{  furrr         }\CommentTok{\# Parallel mapping}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Set a default theme for ggplot for consistency}
\FunctionTok{theme\_set}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()) }\CommentTok{\# Use a classic theme with white background}

\CommentTok{\# Define relative paths }
\NormalTok{stan\_model\_dir }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"stan"}
\NormalTok{stan\_results\_dir }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simmodels"}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_dir }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simdata"}

\CommentTok{\# Create directories if they don\textquotesingle{}t exist (important for first run)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(stan\_results\_dir)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(stan\_results\_dir)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{dir.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir)) }\FunctionTok{dir.create}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Phase 1: Mathematical Formulation and Stan
implementation}\label{phase-1-mathematical-formulation-and-stan-implementation}

Just as a reminder, for our Simple Biased Agent, the generative process
across \(n\) trials is defined as: \[h_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\]
\[\theta = \text{logit}^{-1}(\theta_{\text{logit}})\]
\[\theta_{\text{logit}} \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\]

Notice our parameterization. By operating on the unconstrained space
(\(\theta_{\text{logit}} \in [-\infty, \infty]\)) rather than the
constrained probability space (\(\theta \in [0, 1]\)), we avoid boundary
effects that can cause the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) to pathologically
bounce against parameter limits.

Stan's default initialization draws uniformly from \([-2, 2]\) on the
unconstrained (logit) space. When our models will grow more complex,
this arbitrary initialization might be less than optimal and we'll have
to explicitly initialize from the prior or use multi-path variational
inference (\texttt{Pathfinder}) if the chains struggle to start.

We will now reproduce the Stan code for the model, and modify it to
include the generated quantities block. This block executes after the
model has been fitted, and is very useful in our checks as we can
generate and save several variables to be used in our model assessment:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  the prior (e.g.~for visualization in prior-posterior update checks),
\item
  simulations of model expectations from the prior only (before exposure
  to data) for prior predictive checks
\item
  simulations of model expectations from the posteriors (after exposure
  to data), for posterior predictive checks
\item
  the log likelihood for each data point, to assess how well they are
  captured by the model
\item
  the joint log prior, which tells us how likely the posterior is given
  the prior and will be used to assess the sensitivity of the posterior
  to the choice of priors
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Design Pattern --- Priors as Data}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Notice that we declare the prior hyperparameters
(\texttt{prior\_theta\_mu}, \texttt{prior\_theta\_sigma}) in the
\textbf{\texttt{data} block} rather than hardcoding them in the
\texttt{model} block. This separation has two concrete benefits:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{No recompilation when exploring priors.} Changing a hardcoded
  \texttt{normal(0,\ 1.5)} to \texttt{normal(0,\ 2.0)} in Stan requires
  recompiling the C++ model binary (\textasciitilde10--30 seconds).
  Passing them as data means you change a single R list entry and re-run
  instantly.
\item
  \textbf{SBC priors can differ from the fitted model's priors.} When
  SBC simulates from the prior, it draws hyperparameters from the same
  distribution specified in the data list. Keeping this in sync is
  trivial when priors are data; it is easy to introduce accidental
  mismatches when they are hardcoded.
\end{enumerate}

We use this pattern for every model with tunable priors from this
chapter onward.

\end{tcolorbox}

Note that we are adding data for the prior parameters, so we can quickly
change them without recompiling the model, and that we will explain the
different additional variables in detail throughout the chapter.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Efficiency Rule 2 --- Conditional Diagnostics: The
\texttt{run\_diagnostics} Flag}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The six-phase validation pipeline requires \textbf{thousands of Stan
fits} during SBC (Simulation-Based Calibration Checks). Each SBC
iteration only needs to verify posterior calibration --- it does
\textbf{not} need:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  PPC draws (\texttt{y\_rep}) --- these require one
  \texttt{bernoulli\_rng()} call per observation per draw
\item
  Pointwise log-likelihoods (\texttt{log\_lik}) --- needed for LOO-CV,
  but LOO is not run on SBC fits
\item
  Prior predictive samples --- only needed for the visual prior check,
  not for calibration
\end{itemize}

Generating these quantities inside \texttt{generated\ quantities} on
every SBC iteration wastes substantial compute. We gate them with a
single integer flag in the data block:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{upper}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;  }\CommentTok{// 1 = full diagnostics; 0 = calibration only}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{generated quantities}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[N\_trials] log\_lik;}
  \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[N\_trials] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ y\_rep;}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (run\_diagnostics) \{}
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N\_trials) \{}
\NormalTok{      log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | theta\_logit);}
\NormalTok{      y\_rep[i]   = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(theta\_logit);}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Then in R, the main validation fit uses \texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 1}
and SBC backends use \texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 0}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_data\_main }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L)   }\CommentTok{\# single model check}
\NormalTok{stan\_data\_sbc  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{L)   }\CommentTok{\# every SBC iteration}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Why it matters at scale.} For a model with 120 trials and 100
SBC datasets × 4 chains × 1000 draws: gating \texttt{log\_lik} and
\texttt{y\_rep} eliminates 100 × 4 × 1000 × 120 ≈ \textbf{48 million}
log-probability evaluations, typically cutting SBC wall-clock time by
20--40\%.

Note that \textbf{prior samples} (e.g.,
\texttt{bias\_prior\ =\ normal\_rng(0,\ 0.3)}) are cheap scalar draws
and can remain ungated --- they don't loop over observations.

We introduced this flag in simplified form in Chapter 4's memory model.
From this chapter onward it appears in every Stan model in the book.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Efficiency --- Post-Hoc Generated Quantities with
\texttt{\$generate\_quantities()}}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The \texttt{run\_diagnostics} flag trades off between fitting speed and
diagnostic richness. A complementary pattern avoids the trade-off
entirely: \textbf{fit once with \texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 0}, then
replay the saved draws through the \texttt{generated\ quantities} block
on demand} using \texttt{\$generate\_quantities()}.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Fast fit — no log\_lik or y\_rep computed during sampling}
\NormalTok{fit\_fast }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{L), ...)}

\CommentTok{\# Later: compute log\_lik and y\_rep from the saved draws without re{-}running MCMC}
\NormalTok{fit\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{generate\_quantities}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{fitted\_params =}\NormalTok{ fit\_fast,}
  \AttributeTok{data          =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Extract as usual}
\NormalTok{log\_lik }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{y\_rep   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"y\_rep"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{\$generate\_quantities()} runs only the
\texttt{generated\ quantities} block --- it never touches the sampler.
This means:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{LOO and PPC can be deferred} until you actually need them,
  without re-running MCMC.
\item
  \textbf{SBC backends always run with \texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 0}}
  and you call \texttt{\$generate\_quantities()} only on the subset of
  fits you want to inspect.
\item
  The saved \texttt{CmdStanFit} object from a previous session can be
  replayed by loading it with \texttt{readRDS()} and passing it as
  \texttt{fitted\_params}.
\end{itemize}

The only requirement is that the \texttt{data} list passed to
\texttt{\$generate\_quantities()} matches the data block of the compiled
model --- the same model object must be used for both calls.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Stan code of the model, adding generated quantities for predictive checks}
\NormalTok{stan\_model\_gq\_code }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_trials;               // Total number of trials}
\StringTok{  array[N\_trials] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} y; // Observed choices}
\StringTok{  real prior\_theta\_mu;                 // Mean of the prior for theta\_logit}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_theta\_sigma;     // SD of the prior for theta\_logit}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics; // Flag: 1 to compute PPC/log\_lik, 0 to skip}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real theta\_logit; // Represents the log{-}odds of choosing option 1 (right)}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(theta\_logit | prior\_theta\_mu, prior\_theta\_sigma); // prior}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | theta\_logit); // likelihood}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // Create the prior for theta}
\StringTok{  real theta\_logit\_prior = normal\_rng(prior\_theta\_mu, prior\_theta\_sigma); }
\StringTok{  // {-}{-}{-} Parameter Transformation {-}{-}{-}}
\StringTok{  real theta\_prior = inv\_logit(theta\_logit\_prior); }
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} theta = inv\_logit(theta\_logit);}

\StringTok{  // {-}{-}{-} Conditional Diagnostics {-}{-}{-}}
\StringTok{  // These blocks are expensive to run and store during SBC.}
\StringTok{  // We wrap them in a conditional flag.}
\StringTok{  array[N\_trials] int y\_prior\_rep;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=N\_trials\textgreater{} prior\_rep\_sum;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_trials] int y\_post\_rep;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=N\_trials\textgreater{} post\_rep\_sum;}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_trials] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  real lprior;}

\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    // {-}{-}{-} Prior Predictive Check Simulation {-}{-}{-}}
\StringTok{    y\_prior\_rep = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(rep\_vector(theta\_logit\_prior, N\_trials));}
\StringTok{    prior\_rep\_sum = sum(y\_prior\_rep);}

\StringTok{    // {-}{-}{-} Posterior Predictive Check Simulation {-}{-}{-}}
\StringTok{    y\_post\_rep = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(rep\_vector(theta\_logit, N\_trials));}
\StringTok{    post\_rep\_sum = sum(y\_post\_rep); }
\StringTok{    }
\StringTok{    // {-}{-}{-} Log{-}likelihood {-}{-}{-}}
\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N\_trials) log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | theta\_logit);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{  // joint log prior (useful for sensitivity even if run\_diagnostics=0)}
\StringTok{  lprior = normal\_lpdf(theta\_logit | prior\_theta\_mu, prior\_theta\_sigma);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\NormalTok{stan\_file\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{file.path}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch07\_SimpleBernoulli\_GQ.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_model\_gq\_code, stan\_file\_gq)}

\CommentTok{\# Define path for saving/loading the fitted model object that includes the generated quantities}
\NormalTok{model\_file\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{file.path}\NormalTok{(stan\_results\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch07\_SimpleBernoulli\_GQ.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Generate the required data}

\CommentTok{\# Load the simulation data used in Chapter 5}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{file.path}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch06\_randomnoise.csv"}\NormalTok{) }
\NormalTok{d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{read\_csv}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_file, }\AttributeTok{show\_col\_types =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{) }

\CommentTok{\# Subset data for the specific agent fitted in Chapter 5 (rate=0.8, noise=0)}
\NormalTok{d1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d }\SpecialCharTok{\%\textgreater{}\%} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(true\_rate }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, true\_noise }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{N\_trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d1)}

\CommentTok{\# Prepare the observed data list in the format Stan expects}
\NormalTok{observed\_data\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N\_trials =}\NormalTok{ N\_trials,}
  \AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ d1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_theta\_mu =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_theta\_sigma =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{)}


\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# mod\_gq is compiled OUTSIDE the cache conditional so it is always available —}
\CommentTok{\# for fitting, for the sensitivity analysis, and for the SBC backend.}
\CommentTok{\# Compilation is fast (C++ is cached by CmdStan after the first run).}
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{mod\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_gq)}

\NormalTok{.run\_sampling }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(model\_file\_gq)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{.run\_sampling) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(model\_file\_gq)}
  \CommentTok{\# Detect stale cache: if y\_post\_rep is missing, the model was saved before PPC was added}
\NormalTok{  .has\_ppc }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(\{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"y\_post\_rep"}\NormalTok{); }\ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{ \}, }\AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) }\ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{.has\_ppc) .run\_sampling }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{TRUE}
\NormalTok{\}}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (.run\_sampling) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data             =}\NormalTok{ observed\_data\_list,}
    \AttributeTok{seed             =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains           =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains  =} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{availableCores}\NormalTok{()),}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup      =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling    =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh          =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth    =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta      =} \FloatTok{0.8}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{file =}\NormalTok{ model\_file\_gq)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{The Mandatory MCMC Diagnostic
Battery}\label{the-mandatory-mcmc-diagnostic-battery}

Before we even look at a posterior distribution, we must prove the
geometry of the posterior was successfully explored. We have zero
tolerance for divergences. If a divergence occurs, the NUTS trajectory
has departed from the true posterior manifold, indicating a biased
approximation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract the summary and diagnostics from our fitted model object}
\NormalTok{fit\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{diagnostic\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}

\CommentTok{\# 1. Check for Divergent Transitions (Zero Tolerance)}
\NormalTok{divergences }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(diagnostic\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{num\_divergent)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (divergences }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"CRITICAL FAILURE:"}\NormalTok{, divergences, }\StringTok{"divergences detected. The posterior geometry is pathological. Reparameterization required. Do not interpret these results."}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Check E{-}BFMI (Energy{-}Bayesian Fraction of Missing Information)}
\CommentTok{\# Threshold is typically 0.3. Values below this indicate poor exploration.}
\NormalTok{min\_ebfmi }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(diagnostic\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ebfmi)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (min\_ebfmi }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{warning}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"WARNING: Low E{-}BFMI detected (Min ="}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(min\_ebfmi, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{), }
                \StringTok{"). The sampler may struggle to explore the tails of the posterior."}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Check for Convergence (Rhat \textless{} 1.01)}
\NormalTok{max\_rhat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(fit\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rhat, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (max\_rhat }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{1.01}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{stop}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"CRITICAL FAILURE: Chains did not converge. Max Rhat ="}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(max\_rhat, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Check Effective Sample Size (ESS \textgreater{} 400 for both bulk and tail)}
\NormalTok{min\_ess\_bulk }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(fit\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ess\_bulk, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{min\_ess\_tail }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(fit\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ess\_tail, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (min\_ess\_bulk }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{400} \SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ min\_ess\_tail }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{400}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{warning}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"WARNING: Insufficient Effective Sample Size. Autocorrelation is too high. Run longer chains."}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Phase 2: Generative Plausibility (Prior Predictive
Checks)}\label{phase-2-generative-plausibility-prior-predictive-checks}

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why run a prior predictive check before seeing any data?} Priors
are not just regularizers --- they are part of the generative model. A
prior that assigns high probability to θ = 0.999 (choosing ``right''
almost every single trial) might corrupt the posterior even with 1,000
observations, and/or make the MCMC much slower. Catching an implausible
prior here costs minutes; catching it throughout the fitting process
might waste you much longer.
\end{quote}

Before fitting the model, we specified prior distributions for our
parameters (e.g., \(\theta_{\text{logit}} \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\)).
Priors do not merely encode our initial uncertainty before seeing the
data; they are fundamental constraints on the data-generating process.
Prior predictive checks help us understand the implications of these
choices. If we simulate data using only parameters drawn from our
priors, what kind of data does the model expect before seeing any actual
observations?

\subsection{The Generative Algorithm:}\label{the-generative-algorithm}

To assess generative plausibility, we explicitly simulate from the joint
distribution of the model:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Draw from the Prior: Sample \(S\) parameter values from their
  respective prior distributions. For example:
  \(\theta_{\text{logit}}^{(s)} \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\).
\item
  Transform to Constrained Space: Push these unconstrained values
  through the inverse-link function to map them to the cognitive
  parameter space (probability):
  \(\theta^{(s)} = \text{logit}^{-1}(\theta_{\text{logit}}^{(s)})\).
\item
  Simulate the Likelihood: Using these prior parameter values, simulate
  a replicated dataset from the data-generating function:
  \(y_{\text{prior}}^{(s)} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta^{(s)})\).
\item
  Evaluate Summary Statistics: Calculate theoretically relevant summary
  statistics, \(T(y_{\text{prior}}^{(s)})\), and plot their
  distribution.
\end{itemize}

This protocol reveals the limits of our assumptions. We must interrogate
this space: Are we inadvertently generating boundary-saturating
behaviors? If our prior allows the agent to generate 120 ``left''
choices and 0 ``right'' choices with high frequency, we have likely
specified a prior that is too wide on the logit scale, pushing the
probability mass into the extreme tails of 0 and 1.

\subsection{Performing the Prior Predictive
Check:}\label{performing-the-prior-predictive-check}

We have already embedded the generative simulation (h\_prior\_rep) and
the summary statistic calculation (prior\_rep\_sum) directly into the
Stan generated quantities block. We will now extract these draws and
visualize the geometry of our prior predictions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract the summary statistic calculated from prior predictive simulations}
\CommentTok{\# \textquotesingle{}prior\_rep\_sum\textquotesingle{} was calculated within the generated quantities block}
\NormalTok{draws\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()) }\CommentTok{\# Ensure we have the draws as a dataframe}
