    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Correlation r = "}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(cor\_reversal, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
                      \StringTok{"   (compare to r ≈ 0.9 with constant{-}bias design)"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Baseline Bias"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Memory Strength (Beta)"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{label =} \StringTok{"Ground Truth"}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{fontface =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p\_joint\_reversal)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{05-ModelQualityChecks_files/figure-pdf/ch06_fit_memory_reversal-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{Correlation with constant{-}bias design: \textasciitilde{}0.9}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{,}
  \StringTok{"Correlation with reversal design:       \%.3f}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{  cor\_reversal}
\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

Correlation with constant-bias design: ~0.9
\end{verbatim}

The joint posterior is now compact and approximately circular around the
true parameter values --- \texttt{bias} and \texttt{beta} are
identified. The reversal in the opponent's preference provides the
critical contrast that lets the model distinguish ``the agent is
generally biased right'' from ``the agent tracks the opponent and
follows them.''

\textbf{Saving the reversal dataset for Chapters 6 and 7.} Chapters 6
(multilevel models) and 7 (model comparison) both need a well-identified
memory dataset as their running example. We save the full reversal
simulation object here so those chapters can load it directly without
regenerating it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{reversal\_data\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{file.path}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_dir, }\StringTok{"ch07\_reversal\_data.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{stan\_data        =}\NormalTok{ data\_reversal,    }\CommentTok{\# Ready{-}to{-}use Stan data list}
    \AttributeTok{sim\_tibble       =}\NormalTok{ d\_reversal,       }\CommentTok{\# Full trial{-}level tibble}
    \AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_reversal,}\CommentTok{\# Raw opponent sequence}
    \AttributeTok{true\_bias        =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{true\_beta        =} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials         =}\NormalTok{ n\_reversal,}
    \AttributeTok{description      =} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}
      \StringTok{"Reversal learning paradigm: opponent plays 80\% right (trials 1{-}120) "}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"then 20\% right (trials 121{-}240). Agent: bias=0, beta=0.9."}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  ),}
\NormalTok{  reversal\_data\_file}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Reversal dataset saved to:"}\NormalTok{, reversal\_data\_file, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Reversal dataset saved to: simdata/ch07_reversal_data.rds 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Load in Ch. 7 and Ch. 8 with: readRDS(here(\textquotesingle{}simdata\textquotesingle{}, \textquotesingle{}ch07\_reversal\_data.rds\textquotesingle{}))}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Load in Ch. 7 and Ch. 8 with: readRDS(here('simdata', 'ch07_reversal_data.rds'))
\end{verbatim}

Finally, we run Simulation-Based Calibration Checks. We use the SBC
package to guarantee that our logit(memory) parameterization doesn't
introduce hidden biases in the sampler's rank statistics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Define the Generator using our simulate\_function}
\NormalTok{sbc\_memory\_generator\_fn }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{() \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Draw true params from priors}
\NormalTok{  t\_bias }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  t\_beta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Use fixed opponent choices for simplicity in this check}
\NormalTok{  opp\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{)}
  
\NormalTok{  sim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_memory}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{bias =}\NormalTok{ t\_bias, }\AttributeTok{beta =}\NormalTok{ t\_beta, }\AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =}\NormalTok{ opp\_choices)}
  
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{bias =}\NormalTok{ t\_bias, }\AttributeTok{beta =}\NormalTok{ t\_beta),}
    \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N\_trials =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ sim}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice, }\AttributeTok{other =}\NormalTok{ opp\_choices,}
                     \AttributeTok{prior\_bias\_mu =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prior\_bias\_sigma =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
                     \AttributeTok{prior\_beta\_mu =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prior\_beta\_sigma =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
                     \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{L)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Execute SBC (1000 simulations)}
\NormalTok{sbc\_mem\_obj }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(sbc\_memory\_generator\_fn)}
\NormalTok{sbc\_mem\_backend }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(mod\_mem\_gq, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Run parallel fits}
\NormalTok{future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{sequential)}
\NormalTok{sbc\_mem\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_mem\_obj, }\AttributeTok{n\_sims =} \DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{), sbc\_mem\_backend)}
\NormalTok{future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{sequential)}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Visualize calibration}
\NormalTok{p\_mem\_sbc }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plot\_rank\_hist}\NormalTok{(sbc\_mem\_results) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p\_mem\_sbc)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{05-ModelQualityChecks_files/figure-pdf/ch06_memory_sbc-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\section{Conclusion: The Validation Workflow as a Reusable
Checklist}\label{conclusion-the-validation-workflow-as-a-reusable-checklist}

Fitting a model and obtaining parameter estimates is just the beginning.
This chapter introduced a six-phase validation battery that every model
must pass before its estimates can be trusted:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Mathematical formulation} --- confirm the Stan code does what
  you think.
\item
  \textbf{Prior predictive check} --- priors must generate plausible
  data.
\item
  \textbf{Posterior predictive check} --- fitted model must reproduce
  observed patterns.
\item
  \textbf{Prior-posterior update} --- data must genuinely update
  beliefs.
\item
  \textbf{Sensitivity analysis} --- conclusions must survive small prior
  perturbations.
\item
  \textbf{Parameter recovery + SBC} --- the engine must find the truth
  and intervals must be calibrated.
\end{enumerate}

Failing any check is not a setback --- it is information. The memory
model's \texttt{bias}/\texttt{beta} correlation failure in Phase 6 told
us exactly what was wrong with the experimental design and how to fix
it. That is the validation workflow working correctly: it caught a real
problem before the model touched empirical data.

The reversal learning dataset generated in this chapter
(\texttt{simdata/ch07\_reversal\_data.rds}) is the running example for
the next two chapters. It is well-identified, validated against all six
checks, and ready to be extended.

\textbf{Coming up in Chapter 7:} We take the memory model --- now
validated on the reversal paradigm --- and scale it to many simulated
participants using a hierarchical (multilevel) model. The central
question shifts from ``can we estimate parameters for one agent?'' to
``how do individuals vary, and how do we share information across
them?''

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Individual Differences in Cognitive Strategies: The
Hierarchical Architecture}\label{sec-multilevel-models}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chs. 1--5
fitted models to a single agent and validated the inference engine. Now
we scale up: real experiments involve many participants who differ from
each other. This chapter builds hierarchical (multilevel) models that
estimate both individual parameters \emph{and} the population
distribution they are drawn from --- and validates the whole pipeline
with the same six-phase battery from Ch. 6. The memory model fitted here
uses the \textbf{reversal learning dataset from Ch. 6}, which was the
first dataset that cleanly separated \texttt{bias} from \texttt{beta}.
The fitted model and its LOO score are saved for Ch. 8.
\end{quote}

\section{Introduction}\label{introduction}

Our exploration of decision-making models has so far focused on single
agents or averaged behavior. However, individuals differ systematically
in how they approach tasks and process information. Some people may be
more risk-averse, have better memory, learn faster, or employ entirely
different strategies than others. Ignoring these individual differences
can lead to misleading conclusions about cognitive processes. From a
statistical perspective, if we assume a single set of parameters
generates all data, our model is mathematically misspecified, which
leads to pathological posterior geometries and misleading inferential
results.

This chapter introduces hierarchical modeling (also called multilevel or
mixed effects modeling) as a powerful framework for capturing these
individual differences while still identifying population-level
patterns.

\subsection{The Epistemic Challenge: Pooling
Information}\label{the-epistemic-challenge-pooling-information}

Traditional statistical approaches to handling data from multiple
individuals force a false dichotomy, imposing rigid, unjustified
structural assumptions on the data:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Complete Pooling (Underfitting)}: Treats all participants as
  identical: it estimates a single set of parameters for the entire
  group, completely ignoring potentially meaningful individual
  variation. This assumes the population variance (the average error in
  predicting an individual from the population) is strictly zero
  (\(\sigma \to 0\)).

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \emph{The Pathology}: Systematically fails to capture cognitive
    heterogeneity, describing an ``average'' participant that does not
    necessarily exist.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{No Pooling (Overfitting)}: Analyzes each participant in
  complete isolation, estimating independent parameters (\(\theta_j\))
  for each individual. No pooling corresponds to fitting \(J\) separate
  models to \(J\) individual participants. This assumes the population
  variance is infinite (\(\sigma \to \infty\)).

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \emph{The Pathology}: Severely inefficient and vulnerable to noise.
    For individuals with sparse or noisy data, the posterior estimate
    will be excessively wide or completely dominated by the random
    fluctuations of their specific likelihood.
  \end{itemize}
\end{enumerate}

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why does this dichotomy matter in practice?} With 120 trials per
agent, complete pooling produces one parameter estimate that represents
nobody in particular. No pooling produces 50 estimates, many of which
are dominated by noise for agents with only 20--30 trials. Partial
pooling is not a philosophical compromise --- it produces estimates that
are demonstrably \emph{closer to the truth} for low-data individuals, as
the shrinkage plot at the end of this chapter will show directly.
\end{quote}

\subsection{The Solution: Partial Pooling (Multilevel or Hierarchical
Modeling)}\label{the-solution-partial-pooling-multilevel-or-hierarchical-modeling}

Hierarchical modeling offers a principled, data-driven middle ground.
Instead of forcing \(\sigma\) to be \(0\) or \(\infty\), we treat the
population variance as a free parameter estimated from the data. The
model is structured hierarchically:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Individual Level}: Each individual agent \(j\) has a specific
  parameter vector (e.g., their intrinsic bias \(\theta_j\) or memory
  decay \(\beta_j\)).
\item
  \textbf{Population Level}: These individual parameters are explicitly
  modeled as draws from an overarching continuous population
  distribution (e.g., a Gaussian characterized by a population mean
  \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\)).
\item
  \textbf{The Bayesian Compromise}: The estimation of an individual's
  parameter (\(\theta_j\)) is a precision-weighted combination of their
  own empirical data (the likelihood) and the population distribution
  (the hierarchical prior).
\item
  \textbf{Shrinkage}: Estimates for individuals with sparse or noisy
  data are ``shrunk'' toward the population mean \(\mu\). This is a
  mathematical mechanism that regularizes inference, preventing
  overfitting and yielding more robust out-of-sample estimates.
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Historical Context: Shrinkage, Partial Pooling, and the Recovery of
Individual Differences}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The hierarchical model is now a standard tool, but the intellectual path
to it was surprisingly contentious. Accepting partial pooling required
overturning the intuition --- deeply held by statisticians trained in
classical estimation theory --- that combining information from
different units is always a form of cheating.

\subsubsection{Mixed Models in the
Fields}\label{mixed-models-in-the-fields}

The mathematical foundations were laid not in cognitive science but in
animal breeding. Henderson (1950) needed to estimate the genetic merit
of individual bulls from data on their offspring, spread across farms
that differed in management and feeding. The complete-pooling solution
(assume all farms are identical) was agronomically wrong; the no-pooling
solution (analyse each farm in isolation) wasted the information that
herds from the same breed share a common distribution of genetic merit.
Henderson's \textbf{Best Linear Unbiased Prediction} (BLUP) formalised
the middle path: individual estimates are shrunk toward the population
mean by an amount that depends on how much data each individual
contributes. The same equations appear, under different names, as the
core of the hierarchical models in this chapter.

\subsubsection{Stein's Paradox and the Optimality of
Shrinkage}\label{steins-paradox-and-the-optimality-of-shrinkage}

The theoretical justification arrived from a different direction, and it
was deeply counterintuitive. James and Stein (1961) proved that the
ordinary (no-pooling) estimator of three or more means is
\emph{inadmissible} under squared-error loss: there always exists a
shrinkage estimator --- one that pulls all estimates toward a common
value --- with uniformly lower expected loss. This is Stein's paradox.
It implies that, regardless of whether the true means are related to
each other, you are statistically better off treating them as
exchangeable draws from a common distribution than estimating each
independently. The result was controversial because it seemed to say
that knowing the speed of light should improve your estimate of the
boiling point of water --- which is absurd --- but the paradox dissolves
once you recognise that the admissibility comparison is over the
\emph{joint} vector of estimates, not individual components.

\subsubsection{Bayesian Formalisation}\label{bayesian-formalisation}

Lindley and Smith (1972) showed that the James-Stein estimator is a
Bayesian posterior mean under the assumption that the true parameters
are drawn from a common normal distribution --- exactly the hierarchical
prior structure used in this chapter. This connected the frequentist
inadmissibility result to a principled Bayesian generative story:
shrinkage is not a trick but the natural consequence of treating
individuals as exchangeable members of a population. Gelman and Rubin
(1992) and Gelman et al. (1995) developed the computational
infrastructure (via MCMC) that made this formalism applicable to
realistic psychological datasets.

\subsubsection{Into Cognitive Science}\label{into-cognitive-science}

The adoption of hierarchical models in cognitive science was slow. For
much of the 1990s and early 2000s, individual-differences research
either fitted separate models to each participant and then performed a
second-stage group analysis --- the ``two-stage'' procedure that
discards uncertainty --- or fitted a single population model that
ignored individual variation entirely. Rouder et al. (2005) and Rouder
and Lu (2008) demonstrated that hierarchical models substantially
improve parameter recovery in reaction-time and signal-detection
contexts: individual estimates derived from partial pooling are closer
to the true generating values than either the complete-pooling or
no-pooling alternatives, especially for participants who contribute few
trials. The shrinkage plot at the end of this chapter makes this
advantage visible directly: you can watch the model borrow strength from
the population to regularise noisy individuals.

\subsubsection{Non-Centred Parameterisation and Neal's
Funnel}\label{non-centred-parameterisation-and-neals-funnel}

The one purely computational contribution to this story came from Neal
(2003) and was adopted as standard practice by the Stan community via
Betancourt and Girolami (2013). When individual parameters are
parameterised as \(\theta_j = \mu + \sigma \cdot z_j\) with
\(z_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\) --- the non-centred form --- the geometry
of the posterior becomes approximately Gaussian and Hamiltonian Monte
Carlo can traverse it efficiently. In the centred form
\(\theta_j \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\), the posterior develops a
funnel geometry near \(\sigma \approx 0\) that causes HMC to diverge.
The non-centred parameterisation is the single most important practical
technique in hierarchical Bayesian computation, and the reason the
models in this chapter spend time on it.

\end{tcolorbox}

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-5}

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Explain Partial Pooling}: Understand how multilevel modeling
  balances individual and group-level information through shrinkage.
\item
  \textbf{Distinguish Pooling Approaches}: Articulate the differences
  and trade-offs between complete pooling (\(\sigma=0\)), no pooling
  (\(\sigma=\infty\)), and partial pooling.
\item
  \textbf{Implement Hierarchical Models}: Write Stan code for
  hierarchical models with varying intercepts and/or slopes.
\item
  \textbf{Reparameterize a model}: Understand why and how to
  reparameterize a model and in particular how to use non-centered
  parameterization to improve sampling efficiency in hierarchical
  models.
\item
  \textbf{Model Parameter Correlations}: Implement and interpret models
  that estimate correlations between individual-level parameters (via
  Cholesky-factorized correlation matrices \(\mathbf{L}_\Omega\)).
\item
  \textbf{Perform Comprehensive Model Checks}: Apply a rigorous Bayesian
  workflow to hierarchical models (Predictive Checks, Prior-Posterior
  Update Checks, Sensitivity Analysis, Parameter Recovery,
  Simulation-Based Calibration Checks, etc).
\item
  \textbf{Apply to Cognitive Questions}: Use multilevel modeling to
  investigate individual differences in cognitive strategies (e.g.,
  bias, memory, WSLS).
\end{itemize}

This chapter has two parts. The first applies the full Bayesian workflow
to the \textbf{Hierarchical Biased Agent} --- one parameter per person,
one population distribution --- and uses it to expose and fix the
Centered Parameterization's pathological geometry (Neal's Funnel). The
second applies the same workflow to the \textbf{Hierarchical Memory
Agent}, which has two correlated parameters per person, requires a
bivariate population distribution, and uses the reversal learning
dataset from Ch. 6 that we validated there. The LOO score from the
fitted memory model is saved for Ch. 8's model comparison.

\subsection{Biased Agent Model
(Multilevel)}\label{biased-agent-model-multilevel}

In this architecture, each agent \(j \in \{1, \dots, J\}\) possesses an
unobserved, individual-level bias parameter \(\theta_j\). This
individual parameter is generated by a latent population distribution,
parameterized by a global mean \(\mu_\theta\) and global standard
deviation \(\sigma_\theta\). Finally, the empirical choices \(h_{ij}\)
for trial \(i\) are generated conditionally on the individual parameter
\(\theta_j\).

Mathematically, this translates to the following generative structure on
the unconstrained (logit)
space:\[h_{ij} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\text{logit}^{-1}(\theta_{\text{logit}, j}))\]\[\theta_{\text{logit}, j} \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_{\theta}, \sigma_{\theta})\]\[\mu_{\theta} \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\]\[\sigma_{\theta} \sim \text{Exponential}(1)\]

By mapping this DAG, we have strictly defined our generative
assumptions. We are now ready to translate this architecture into Stan
code and subject it to Prior Predictive Checks.

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/hierarchical_biased_dags-1.png}

}

\caption{Standard Plate Notation (Left) and Kruschke-Style Generative
Notation (Right) for the Hierarchical Biased Agent.}

\end{figure}%

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Computational Note: The Geometry of Hierarchical Models} The
mathematical definition provided above---specifically
\(\theta_{\text{logit}, j} \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_{\theta}, \sigma_{\theta})\)---is
known as the \textbf{Centered Parameterization}. It is the most direct
conceptual translation of our theory. However, Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
(NUTS) often struggles with this exact form. As \(\sigma_\theta\)
approaches zero, the target geometry constrains into a region of extreme
curvature known as ``Neal's Funnel.'' In the next phase, we will
purposefully implement this naive form to diagnose its pathological
failures before introducing the coordinate-transform solution
(Non-Centered Parameterization).
\end{quote}

\section{Phase 1: Generative Plausibility of the Hierarchical Biased
Agent}\label{phase-1-generative-plausibility-of-the-hierarchical-biased-agent}

Before we write the inference algorithm in Stan, we must construct the
forward generative model in R. This code must strictly mirror the
mathematical dependencies formalized in our DAG.

We model the parameters on the logit (log-odds) scale. As discussed in
previous chapters, probabilities are bounded between \(0\) and \(1\). If
we attempt to place a hierarchical Normal distribution directly on the
probability space, the probability mass will inevitably spill over the
bounds, creating mathematically impossible values (e.g., a probability
of \(1.2\)). The logit transform
\(\text{logit}(p) = \log(\frac{p}{1-p})\) maps the bounded \([0, 1]\)
interval to the unbounded real line \((-\infty, \infty)\). The HMC
sampler traverses this unbounded space smoothly, and we use the
inverse-logit function (plogis() in R, inv\_logit() in Stan) to map the
inferences back to probabilities at the likelihood level.

Let us build a unified generative function. In accordance with the
rigorous Bayesian workflow, this function is designed to either:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Draw hyperparameters directly from our stated priors (for Prior
  Predictive Checks).
\item
  Accept fixed ``ground-truth'' hyperparameters (for Parameter Recovery
  in Phase 2).
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Simulate Hierarchical Biased Agents}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_agents Integer, number of agents (J).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_trials\_min Integer, minimum number of trials per agent.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_trials\_max Integer, maximum number of trials per agent.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param mu\_theta Numeric, population mean on the logit scale. If NULL, draws from prior.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param sigma\_theta Numeric, population SD on the logit scale. If NULL, draws from prior.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param seed Integer, for exact computational reproducibility.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return A tidy tibble containing both latent parameters and observed choices.}
\NormalTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }
                                         \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }
                                         \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{, }
                                         \AttributeTok{mu\_theta =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }
                                         \AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
                                         \AttributeTok{seed =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) \{}
  
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(seed)) }\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 1. Sample Population Hyperparameters (if not provided)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(mu\_theta)) mu\_theta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{sd =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(sigma\_theta)) sigma\_theta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{rate =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 2. Sample Individual Latent Parameters and Trial Counts}
  \CommentTok{\# We purposefully vary N\_j to demonstrate shrinkage later.}
\NormalTok{  agent\_params }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{agent\_id =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_agents,}
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(n\_trials\_min}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_trials\_max, n\_agents, }\AttributeTok{replace =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{theta\_logit =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_agents, }\AttributeTok{mean =}\NormalTok{ mu\_theta, }\AttributeTok{sd =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_theta),}
    \AttributeTok{theta\_prob =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(theta\_logit) }
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 3. Generate Observed Data (Likelihood)}
  \CommentTok{\# h\_ij \textasciitilde{} Bernoulli(inv\_logit(theta\_logit\_j))}
\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_params }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{trial\_data =} \FunctionTok{map2}\NormalTok{(theta\_prob, n\_trials, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(prob, n) \{}
        \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
          \AttributeTok{trial =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n,}
          \AttributeTok{choice =} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ prob)}
\NormalTok{        )}
\NormalTok{      \})}
\NormalTok{    ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{unnest}\NormalTok{(trial\_data)}
  
  \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{) }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_theta =}\NormalTok{ mu\_theta,}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_theta}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(sim\_data)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Executing the Prior Predictive Check: The Logit-Normal
Artifact}\label{executing-the-prior-predictive-check-the-logit-normal-artifact}

When placing priors on an unbounded scale (logit) that will be
transformed to a bounded scale (probability), our intuition often fails.
A seemingly ``uninformative'' prior like
\(\mu \sim \text{Normal}(0, 10)\) on the logit scale actually forces
almost all probability mass to exactly \(0\) or \(1\) on the outcome
scale, implying that all agents are entirely deterministic.

Let us generate 12 datasets drawn entirely from our chosen priors
(\(\mu_\theta \sim N(0, 1.5), \sigma_\theta \sim \text{Exp}(1)\)) to
verify they generate scientifically plausible, heterogeneous
populations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Generate 12 distinct "universes" from the priors}
\NormalTok{prior\_sims }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sim\_id) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# We use a fixed seed per universe for stable workflow mapping}
\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_biased}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\CommentTok{\# Only need 1 trial to extract parameters}
    \AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ sim\_id }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{10} 
\NormalTok{  )}
  
\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, theta\_prob) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{simulation\_id =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior Universe"}\NormalTok{, sim\_id))}
\NormalTok{\})}

\CommentTok{\# Visualize the generative plausibility}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_sims, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta\_prob)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{simulation\_id, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{breaks =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior Predictive Check: Implied Population Distributions"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = populations spread across (0,1), not piled at 0 or 1. Each panel is one prior universe."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Agent Bias (Probability Scale)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_prior_predictive_check_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\section{Phase 2: Data Preparation and the Centered Stan
Model}\label{phase-2-data-preparation-and-the-centered-stan-model}

Before we unleash the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) engine, we must
format our simulated data structure to match the static typing of the
Stan compiler. Because our agents have varying numbers of trials
(\(N_j\)), we cannot pass a simple \(J \times N\) matrix. Instead, we
use a ``long format'' array indexing strategy, where a specific vector
maps every trial \(n \in \{1, \dots, N_{\text{total}}\}\) to its
generating agent \(j\).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Prepare Data for Stan}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Maps a tidy tibble into the strictly typed list required by CmdStanR.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} }
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param sim\_data Tidy tibble generated by simulate\_hierarchical\_biased}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return A named list formatted for CmdStanR}
\NormalTok{prep\_stan\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sim\_data) \{}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{N\_total                  =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(sim\_data),}
    \AttributeTok{N\_subjects               =} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{unique}\NormalTok{(sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id)),}
    \AttributeTok{subj\_id                  =}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
    \AttributeTok{y                        =}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_theta\_mu        =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_theta\_sigma     =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_sigma\_theta\_lambda =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics          =} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Execute the preparation on a specifically generated universe for recovery}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_centered }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_biased}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }
  \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{\# Intentional sparsity to force shrinkage}
  \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_theta =} \FloatTok{1.38}\NormalTok{,}\CommentTok{\# slightly below .8 in prob}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{,  }
  \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{1981}
\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{stan\_data\_centered }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{prep\_stan\_data}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_centered)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Stan Model: Centered Parameterization
(CP)}\label{stan-model-centered-parameterization-cp}

We will now implement the Centered Parameterization (CP) of the
hierarchical biased agent. In CP, we explicitly model the individual
latent parameters (\(\theta_{\text{logit}, j}\)) - determining their
probability of choosing ``right'' versus ``left'' - as being drawn
directly from a population distribution with mean \texttt{thetaM} and
standard deviation \texttt{thetaSD}.

Mathematically, this translates to the following hierarchical structure,
which strictly mirrors our Data Generating Process (DGP) from Phase 1:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Population Level}: \[\mu_\theta \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1.5)\]
  \[\sigma_\theta \sim \text{Exponential}(1)\]
\item
  \textbf{Individual Level}:
  \[\theta_{\text{logit}, j} \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta)\]
\item
  \textbf{Data Level (Likelihood)}:
  \[h_{ij} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\text{logit}^{-1}(\theta_{\text{logit}, j}))\]
\end{itemize}

This architecture mathematically instantiates partial pooling: the
estimation of an individual's bias balances their specific choice
history (\(h_{ij}\)) with the population's overarching distributional
geometry (\(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta\)).

\begin{quote}
The Anatomy of a Potential Pathology: Look closely at the
individual-level formulation:
\(\theta_{\text{logit}, j} \sim \text{Normal}(\mu_\theta, \sigma_\theta)\).
The permissible geometric space for \(\theta_{\text{logit}, j}\) is
dynamically dictated by \(\sigma_\theta\). If the data suggests the
population is highly homogenous, the sampler will push
\(\sigma_\theta \to 0\). As it does, the parameter space for
\(\theta_{\text{logit}, j}\) is violently crushed toward \(\mu_\theta\),
creating a funnel of infinite curvature. We are fitting this model
precisely to watch the NUTS sampler fail in this region.
\end{quote}

Let us write the Stan code.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_biased\_ml\_cp\_code }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Multilevel Biased Agent Model: Centered Parameterization (CP)}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_total;                 // Total number of observations}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_subjects;              // Total number of agents}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=N\_subjects\textgreater{} subj\_id; // Agent ID index}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} y;     // Observed choices}
\StringTok{  real prior\_mu\_theta\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_mu\_theta\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_sigma\_theta\_lambda;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  // Population{-}level hyperparameters}
\StringTok{  real mu\_theta;                        // Population mean bias (logit scale)}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} sigma\_theta;            // Population SD of bias (logit scale)}

\StringTok{  // Individual{-}level parameters (CENTERED)}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_subjects] theta\_logit;       // Agent{-}specific biases }
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // 1. Population{-}level hyperpriors}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_theta | prior\_mu\_theta\_mu, prior\_mu\_theta\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma\_theta | prior\_sigma\_theta\_lambda);}

\StringTok{  // 2. Hierarchical prior (CENTERED)}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(theta\_logit | mu\_theta, sigma\_theta);}

\StringTok{  // 3. Likelihood}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | theta\_logit[subj\_id]); }
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  // Transform hyperparameters back to the probability (scale)}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} mu\_theta\_prob = inv\_logit(mu\_theta);}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{}[N\_subjects] theta\_prob = inv\_logit(theta\_logit);}

\StringTok{  // Prior samples — cheap scalar draws; declared at top level so they appear in draws output}
\StringTok{  real mu\_theta\_prior    = normal\_rng(prior\_mu\_theta\_mu, prior\_mu\_theta\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  real sigma\_theta\_prior = exponential\_rng(prior\_sigma\_theta\_lambda);}

\StringTok{  // Initialize arrays for conditional diagnostics}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int y\_post\_rep;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int y\_prior\_rep;}

\StringTok{  // Accumulator for joint prior log{-}density}
\StringTok{  real lprior;}
\StringTok{  lprior = normal\_lpdf(mu\_theta | prior\_mu\_theta\_mu, prior\_mu\_theta\_sigma) +}
\StringTok{           exponential\_lpdf(sigma\_theta | prior\_sigma\_theta\_lambda) +}
\StringTok{           normal\_lpdf(theta\_logit | mu\_theta, sigma\_theta);}

\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | theta\_logit[subj\_id[i]]);}
\StringTok{      y\_post\_rep[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(theta\_logit[subj\_id[i]]);}
\StringTok{      real theta\_logit\_prior\_i = normal\_rng(mu\_theta\_prior, sigma\_theta\_prior);}
\StringTok{      y\_prior\_rep[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(theta\_logit\_prior\_i);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\CommentTok{\# Save}
\NormalTok{stan\_file\_biased\_ml\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_multilevel\_biased\_cp.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_biased\_ml\_cp\_code, stan\_file\_biased\_ml\_cp)}

\CommentTok{\# Compile the CP model utilizing the CmdStan C++ backend}
\NormalTok{mod\_biased\_ml\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_biased\_ml\_cp)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Fitting the model}\label{fitting-the-model}

This not really necessary yet, but a rigorous workflow demands an
explicit initialization strategy, so we have all the pieces in place for
more complex models. We will initialize the NUTS algorithm by drawing
starting values directly from our generative priors. This ensures the
chains begin in scientifically plausible regions of the parameter space,
rather than Stan's arbitrary default \(U[-2, 2]\) on the unconstrained
space.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit\_cp\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_fit\_cp.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{init\_cp\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) \{}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_theta    =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{theta\_logit =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(stan\_data\_centered}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{N\_subjects, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\})}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(fit\_cp\_path)) \{}
  
\NormalTok{  fit\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_biased\_ml\_cp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data            =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data\_centered,}
    \AttributeTok{seed            =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains          =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup     =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling   =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{init            =}\NormalTok{ init\_cp\_list, }
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta     =} \FloatTok{0.85}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
\NormalTok{  fit\_cp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(fit\_cp\_path)}
  
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(fit\_cp\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Assessing the fitting
process}\label{assessing-the-fitting-process}

Even if the Stan compiler finishes without throwing explicit warning
messages, our workflow demands that we systematically verify the
geometry of the posterior and the efficiency of the sampler. Hamiltonian
Monte Carlo (HMC) is a physical simulation---it explores the posterior
by simulating a particle gliding over a frictionless manifold dictated
by the joint log-probability density. We must prove the numerical
integration of this physics engine remained stable.

Let us systematically unpack the computational diagnostics of our
Centered Parameterization fit.

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  The Diagnostic Summary: Integration Stability First, we ask cmdstanr
  for a high-level summary of the NUTS sampler's performance.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#fit\_cp$cmdstan\_diagnose()}
\NormalTok{fit\_cp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
$num_divergent
[1] 0 0 0 0

$num_max_treedepth
[1] 0 0 0 0

$ebfmi
[1] 0.9499418 1.0854016 0.9723112 0.9770850
\end{verbatim}

We are looking for strict zeros in num\_divergent. A divergent
transition occurs when the numerical integration process (the leapfrog
steps) fails to accurately map the curvature of the posterior manifold,
causing the simulated trajectory to fly off the energy level. Even one
divergence compromises the mathematical guarantee of the posterior.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Chain Mixing and Stationarity
(\(\hat{R}\))}{Chain Mixing and Stationarity (\textbackslash hat\{R\})}}\label{chain-mixing-and-stationarity-hatr}

Did our independent Markov chains converge to the same stationary
distribution? We visualize this using trace plots and quantify it using
\(\hat{R}\) (R-hat).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract the posterior array}
\NormalTok{draws\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_cp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()}

\CommentTok{\# Visual inspection of chain mixing}
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_trace}\NormalTok{(draws\_cp, }\AttributeTok{pars =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{np =} \FunctionTok{nuts\_params}\NormalTok{(fit\_cp)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Trace Plots: Hyperparameters (CP Model)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = chains overlap and fluctuate around a stable band. Divergent red triangles mark sampler failures."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_diagnostics_trace-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Quantitative assessment of convergence and efficiency}
\NormalTok{cp\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_cp }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{subset\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"theta\_logit"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{default\_convergence\_measures}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# We require R{-}hat \textless{} 1.01 for all parameters}
\NormalTok{cp\_summary }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"theta\_logit[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"theta\_logit[2]"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrr@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
variable & rhat & ess\_bulk & ess\_tail \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
mu\_theta & 1.000 & 4407.162 & 2910.797 \\
sigma\_theta & 1.000 & 3472.594 & 3048.045 \\
theta\_logit{[}1{]} & 1.001 & 4648.021 & 2779.811 \\
theta\_logit{[}2{]} & 1.001 & 4760.858 & 3107.729 \\
\end{longtable}

The potential scale reduction factor (\(\hat{R}\)) compares the variance
within a single chain to the variance between all chains. If the chains
are exploring the same typical set, this ratio converges to 1. Our
strict threshold is \(\hat{R} < 1.01\).

\subsubsection{Effective Sample Size
(ESS)}\label{effective-sample-size-ess}

Markov chains are inherently autocorrelated. Stan might draw 4000
samples, but we need to know the Effective Sample Size---the equivalent
number of completely independent draws.In your summary table, look at
ess\_bulk (efficiency of the posterior mean estimate) and ess\_tail
(efficiency of the posterior interval estimates). We strictly require
ESS \textgreater{} 400 per parameter. If the geometry is difficult, ESS
plummets, indicating the sampler is taking highly correlated,
inefficient steps.

\subsubsection{Energy and E-BFMI}\label{energy-and-e-bfmi}

Finally, we evaluate the Energy-Bayesian Fraction of Missing Information
(E-BFMI). HMC relies on injecting kinetic energy (momentum) into the
system to explore the tails of the target distribution.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Assess the momentum resampling efficiency}
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_nuts\_energy}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{nuts\_params}\NormalTok{(fit\_cp)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Energy Transitions"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Marginal and transition energy distributions must align."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_diagnostics_energy-1.pdf}
\end{center}

If the model geometry is well-behaved, the energy transition
distribution (the energy proposed by the momentum resampling) will
neatly match the marginal energy distribution (the actual energy of the
posterior). An E-BFMI below 0.2 indicates the sampler is taking tiny,
localized steps and failing to explore the global geometry.

\subsection{Prior-Posterior Update Checks: Quantifying
Learning}\label{prior-posterior-update-checks-quantifying-learning}

Bayesian inference is the mathematical process of allocating credibility
across parameter values. The posterior is simply the prior updated by
the likelihood. If the posterior is identical to the prior, the data
contained no information. If the posterior is violently pushed against
the boundaries of the prior, our priors were likely misspecified.

Let us visualize how much the data narrowed our uncertainty about the
population parameters. Because we explicitly generated prior draws in
our Stan generated quantities block, this comparison is trivial.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract prior and posterior draws for the population mean and variance}
\NormalTok{draws\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(draws\_cp)}

\CommentTok{\# Reshape for ggplot}
\NormalTok{pp\_update\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_posterior =}\NormalTok{ mu\_theta, }\AttributeTok{mu\_prior =}\NormalTok{ mu\_theta\_prior,}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_posterior =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_theta, }\AttributeTok{sigma\_prior =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_theta\_prior}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"parameter"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"distribution"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{names\_sep =} \StringTok{"\_"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Extract the true generative hyperparameters we saved as attributes}
\NormalTok{true\_mu }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_centered, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu\_theta}
\NormalTok{true\_sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_centered, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma\_theta}

\CommentTok{\# Create a reference dataframe for the ground truth matching the facet names}
\NormalTok{true\_pop\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{parameter =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{true\_value =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(true\_mu, true\_sigma)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Visualize the update}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(pp\_update\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ value)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ distribution), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Add the true values as dashed lines}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ true\_pop\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =}\NormalTok{ true\_value, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"True Value"}\NormalTok{), }
             \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"darkred"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}
    \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{parameter, }
    \AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{,}
    \CommentTok{\# Clean up the facet labels for publication quality}
    \AttributeTok{labeller =} \FunctionTok{as\_labeller}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Population Mean (\textbackslash{}u03BC)"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Population SD (\textbackslash{}u03C3)"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Distribution"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"gray70"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_linetype\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Ground Truth"}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"True Value"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior{-}Posterior Update Checks: Population Hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = posterior (blue) narrower than prior (grey) and centred on true value (dashed red)."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Parameter Value (Logit Scale)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_pp_update_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

A healthy update shows a posterior that is significantly narrower than
the prior (indicating learning) and comfortably contained within the
prior's mass (indicating the prior did not inappropriately censor the
likelihood).

\subsection{Posterior Parameter
Correlations}\label{posterior-parameter-correlations}

Parameters in hierarchical models are structurally correlated. By
plotting the joint posterior geometry of multiple parameters, we can
detect pathological collinearity or explicitly visualize the boundary of
possible Neal's Funnel.

We will use a pairs plot to examine the joint distribution of the
population mean (\(\mu_\theta\)), population standard deviation
(\(\sigma_\theta\)), and a single agent's latent bias
(\(\theta_{\text{logit}, 1}\)).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Visualize joint posterior geometries and identify structural correlations}
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_pairs}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  draws\_cp, }
  \AttributeTok{pars =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"theta\_logit[1]"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{np =} \FunctionTok{nuts\_params}\NormalTok{(fit\_cp), }\CommentTok{\# Injects divergence data (red crosses) if any exist}
  \AttributeTok{diag\_fun =} \StringTok{"dens"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{off\_diag\_fun =} \StringTok{"hex"}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_pairs_plot-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Posterior Predictive Checks (PPC): Generative
Adequacy}\label{posterior-predictive-checks-ppc-generative-adequacy}

Can our fitted model actually generate the reality we observed? If we
simulate data from the posterior distribution, does it look like the
empirical data? If not, the model is structurally misspecified,
regardless of how well the chains mixed.

We will compare the observed choice rate for each agent against the
distribution of choice rates predicted by the posterior.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Safely extract vectors}
\NormalTok{y\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(stan\_data\_centered}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{y)}
\NormalTok{agent\_idx }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(stan\_data\_centered}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{subj\_id)}

\CommentTok{\# Use cmdstanr\textquotesingle{}s native extraction to guarantee a strict S x N matrix}
\NormalTok{y\_rep }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_cp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"y\_post\_rep"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Select a random subset of agents to avoid overcrowded, illegible plots}
\NormalTok{n\_sample }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{12}
\NormalTok{sampled\_agents }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{unique}\NormalTok{(agent\_idx), n\_sample)}

\CommentTok{\# Create a logical mask mapping which trials belong to the sampled agents}
\NormalTok{keep\_mask }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_idx }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%}\NormalTok{ sampled\_agents}

\CommentTok{\# Subset the data exactly}
\NormalTok{y\_obs\_sub }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ y\_obs[keep\_mask]}
\NormalTok{agent\_idx\_sub }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_idx[keep\_mask]}
\CommentTok{\# For y\_rep, we subset the columns (trials)}
\NormalTok{y\_rep\_sub }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ y\_rep[ , keep\_mask] }

\CommentTok{\# Define a custom statistic to explicitly state our cognitive metric }
\CommentTok{\# (and simultaneously silence the bayesplot warning)}
\NormalTok{prop\_right }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(x) }\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(x)}

\CommentTok{\# Execute the grouped Posterior Predictive Check}
\FunctionTok{ppc\_stat\_grouped}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y\_obs\_sub, }
  \AttributeTok{yrep =}\NormalTok{ y\_rep\_sub, }
  \AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ agent\_idx\_sub, }
  \AttributeTok{stat =} \StringTok{"prop\_right"}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Posterior Predictive Check: Agent{-}Level Choice Rates"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Healthy = dark line (observed) inside histogram (predicted). Showing "}\NormalTok{, n\_sample, }\StringTok{" agents."}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Proportion of \textquotesingle{}Right\textquotesingle{} Choices"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_ppc_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Calibration and Recovery}\label{calibration-and-recovery}

We have proven the model fits the data and generates plausible
predictions. Now, we must prove that the parameters we extract are
actually the true parameters that generated the data. Because this is a
hierarchical model, we must assess recovery at both the population and
individual levels.

\subsubsection{Individual-Level
Recovery}\label{individual-level-recovery}

Having validated the top of the hierarchy (in the prior-posterior update
check), we move to the individual agents. We plot the posterior
estimates against the ground truth. We expect the estimates to align
with the diagonal identity line, but crucially, we expect agents with
fewer trials to deviate from the raw truth, demonstrating shrinkage
toward the estimated population mean.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Extract the full summary (which defaults to including median, q5, and q95)}
\NormalTok{recovery\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_cp }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
\NormalTok{  posterior}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{subset\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{variable =} \StringTok{"theta\_logit"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
\NormalTok{  posterior}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{summarise\_draws}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{str\_extract}\NormalTok{(variable, }\StringTok{"(?\textless{}=}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{[)}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{d+(?=}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{])"}\NormalTok{)))}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Extract the true generative parameters directly from the simulated dataset}
\NormalTok{true\_agent\_params }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_data\_centered }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, n\_trials, theta\_logit, theta\_prob)}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Join the posterior estimates with the ground truth}
\NormalTok{recovery\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ true\_agent\_params }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(recovery\_summary, }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Plot Recovery with Shrinkage Dynamics}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta\_logit, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ median)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Parameter Recovery: Hierarchical Biased Agent"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = points near diagonal (y = x). Small dots shrink more — that is partial pooling working correctly."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True Parameter Value (Logit Scale)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median Estimate (Logit Scale)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{size =} \StringTok{"Number of Trials (N\_j)"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_recovery_shrinkage-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Prior sensitivity}\label{prior-sensitivity}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Calculate the sensitivity diagnostics}
\CommentTok{\# We target the hyperparameters, as they govern the hierarchical shrinkage}
\NormalTok{sens\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{powerscale\_sensitivity}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_cp, }\CommentTok{\# or fit\_ncp, provided lprior is defined}
  \AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"=== PRIOR SENSITIVITY DIAGNOSTICS ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
=== PRIOR SENSITIVITY DIAGNOSTICS ===
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(sens\_results)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Sensitivity based on cjs_dist
Prior selection: all priors
Likelihood selection: all data

    variable prior likelihood                                diagnosis
    mu_theta 0.165      0.050 potential strong prior / weak likelihood
 sigma_theta 0.354      0.091            potential prior-data conflict
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{ps\_seq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_sequence}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_cp, }
  \AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Plot the density shifts}
\NormalTok{priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_dens}\NormalTok{(ps\_seq) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior Sensitivity: Density Perturbation"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Does scaling the prior/likelihood drastically alter our geometric beliefs?"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_prior_sensitivity-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Plot the quantitative estimate shifts}
\NormalTok{priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_quantities}\NormalTok{(ps\_seq) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior Sensitivity: Parameter Estimates"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Horizontal lines indicate the baseline posterior bounds. Deviations indicate sensitivity."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_cp_prior_sensitivity-2.pdf}
\end{center}

The sensitivity analysis reveals that our population variance
(\(\sigma_\theta\)) exhibits a prior-data conflict. This is a feature,
not a bug, of partial pooling: our Exponential prior applies
regularizing pressure toward zero variance (complete pooling), while the
empirical data resists this pull to account for individual differences.

\subsection{Simulation-Based Calibration Checks
(SBC)}\label{simulation-based-calibration-checks-sbc}

Parameter recovery on a single dataset is anecdotal. A single success
might just mean we got lucky with a benign region of the parameter
space. To mathematically guarantee that our NUTS sampler is unbiased and
our posterior uncertainties are perfectly calibrated across the entire
prior space, we must perform Simulation-Based Calibration Checks (SBC).
The logic of SBC is structurally elegant: if we generate a ground-truth
parameter \(\theta_{\text{true}}\) from the prior, generate data from
the data model conditional on \(\theta_{\text{true}}\), and compute the
posterior, then the posterior must not systematically over- or
under-estimate \(\theta_{\text{true}}\). Under a well-calibrated
sampler, the rank of the true parameter value within the sorted
posterior draws will be uniformly
distributed:\[r \sim \text{Uniform}(0, S)\]where \(S\) is the number of
posterior samples. If the model is overconfident (the posterior is too
narrow and misses the truth), the rank histogram will be U-shaped. If it
is underconfident (the posterior is too wide), it will form an inverted
U-shape. Asymmetries indicate directional bias.

\subsection{Setting up the SBC
Pipeline}\label{setting-up-the-sbc-pipeline}

The SBC package requires a strict data structure. It expects a generator
function that returns a list of two components:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  variables: A named list of the true parameter values.
\item
  generated: The formatted data list required by our Stan model.
\end{itemize}

Let us build a strict wrapper around our simulation and data preparation
functions to satisfy this requirement.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Define the strict SBC generator wrapper}
\NormalTok{generate\_sbc\_dataset }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{) \{}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 1. Simulate the universe (draws hyperparams and agent params from priors)}
\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_biased}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_agents =}\NormalTok{ n\_agents, }
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials\_min, }
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials\_max}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 2. Extract the true generative hyperparameters}
\NormalTok{  mu\_true }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu\_theta}
\NormalTok{  sigma\_true }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma\_theta}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 3. Extract the true individual latent parameters (must preserve agent order)}
\NormalTok{  theta\_true }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_data }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, theta\_logit) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
    \FunctionTok{pull}\NormalTok{(theta\_logit)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 4. Prepare the strictly typed list for CmdStanR}
\NormalTok{  stan\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{prep\_stan\_data}\NormalTok{(sim\_data)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 5. Return the exact structure required by the SBC package}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{mu\_theta =}\NormalTok{ mu\_true,}
      \AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_true,}
      \AttributeTok{theta\_logit =}\NormalTok{ theta\_true}
\NormalTok{    ),}
    \AttributeTok{generated =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

A production-grade SBC requires \(N_{\text{sim}} \ge 1000\) to smooth
the rank histograms. For computational feasibility in this chapter, we
will run 200 simulations. Because we established our future parallel
processing plan in the setup chunk, the SBC package will automatically
distribute these models across your CPU cores.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbc\_cp\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_sbc\_cp.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{generator\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  generate\_sbc\_dataset,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{120}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{backend\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  mod\_biased\_ml\_cp,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sbc\_cp\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{multisession, }\AttributeTok{workers =} \DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_datasets\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(generator\_cp, }\DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results\_cp  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{datasets =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_datasets\_cp, }\AttributeTok{backend =}\NormalTok{ backend\_cp,}
  \AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{datasets =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_datasets\_cp, }\AttributeTok{results =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results\_cp),}
\NormalTok{    sbc\_cp\_path}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_obj        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sbc\_cp\_path)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_datasets\_cp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{datasets}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results\_cp  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{results}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Visualize}
\FunctionTok{plot\_rank\_hist}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results\_cp, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"SBC Rank Histograms: Centered Parameterization"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = bars flat within grey band. U{-}shape = overconfident. Here, U{-}shapes reveal the funnel pathology."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_sbc_cp_execution-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results\_cp, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"SBC ECDF Difference: Centered Parameterization"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Healthy = blue line stays near zero. Excursions here correlate with the divergence failures shown below."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_sbc_cp_execution-2.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Extract the lightweight stats dataframe from the SBC object}
\NormalTok{sbc\_stats\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results\_cp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{stats}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Filter for the population{-}level parameters you care about}
\CommentTok{\# (e.g., mu\_alpha, mu\_beta, rho, or even sigma[1])}
\NormalTok{recovery\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_stats\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{))}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Create the Parameter Recovery Identity Plot}
\NormalTok{p\_sbc\_recovery }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ median)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# The Identity Line (Perfect Recovery)}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# The Posterior Estimates with 90\% Credible Intervals}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Facet by parameter}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{variable, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Parameter Recovery (Extracted Directly from SBC)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Points represent the posterior median. Bars are 90\% Credible Intervals."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True Generative Value (Drawn from Prior)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median Estimate"}
