\NormalTok{    partial\_pool\_summary }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, theta\_partial\_pool), }
    \AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \CommentTok{\# Bring in the ground truth}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    true\_agent\_params }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, n\_trials, }\AttributeTok{theta\_true =}\NormalTok{ theta\_logit),}
    \AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \CommentTok{\# Sort agents by their No Pooling estimate for a cleaner visual curve}
  \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(theta\_no\_pool) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{rank =} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# The Ultimate Epistemic Plot}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(comparison\_df) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} POPULATION AVERAGES (Mapped to linetype for a clean legend) {-}{-}{-}}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =}\NormalTok{ no\_pool\_mean\_est, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"No Pooling Mean"}\NormalTok{), }
             \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"darkorange"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =}\NormalTok{ full\_pool\_est, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Complete Pooling Mean"}\NormalTok{), }
             \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"darkred"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =}\NormalTok{ mu\_partial\_pool\_est, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"Partial Pooling Mean (\textbackslash{}U03BC)"}\NormalTok{), }
             \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =}\NormalTok{ true\_mean\_est, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"True Generative Mean"}\NormalTok{), }
             \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} INDIVIDUAL ESTIMATES {-}{-}{-}}
  \CommentTok{\# Shrinkage Vectors (from No Pool to Partial Pool)}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_segment}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ rank, }\AttributeTok{xend =}\NormalTok{ rank, }
                   \AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ theta\_no\_pool, }\AttributeTok{yend =}\NormalTok{ theta\_partial\_pool), }
               \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray65"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{arrow =} \FunctionTok{arrow}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{length =} \FunctionTok{unit}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"cm"}\NormalTok{))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# True Generative Parameters (Black Crosses)}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ rank, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ theta\_true, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"True Value"}\NormalTok{), }
             \AttributeTok{shape =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{stroke =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
             
  \CommentTok{\# No Pooling Estimates (Open Circles)}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ rank, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ theta\_no\_pool, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"No Pooling"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials), }
             \AttributeTok{shape =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{stroke =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
             
  \CommentTok{\# Partial Pooling Estimates (Solid Points)}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ rank, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ theta\_partial\_pool, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Partial Pooling"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials), }
             \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} SCALES \& THEMES {-}{-}{-}}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_linetype\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Population Averages"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
      \StringTok{"True Generative Mean"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"solid"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"Partial Pooling Mean (\textbackslash{}U03BC)"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"Complete Pooling Mean"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"No Pooling Mean"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dotdash"}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Individual Estimates"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
      \StringTok{"True Value"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"No Pooling"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"darkorange"}\NormalTok{, }
      \StringTok{"Partial Pooling"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Bayesian Shrinkage: Partial Pooling vs. the Extremes"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Arrows show shrinkage from No Pooling → Partial Pooling. Longest arrows = fewest trials. Partial pooling lands closest to truth (black solid line)."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Agents (Sorted by No{-}Pooling Estimate)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Estimated Bias Parameter (Logit Scale)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{size =} \StringTok{"Trial Count (N\_j)"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{axis.text.x =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{(), }
    \AttributeTok{axis.ticks.x =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{(),}
    \AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"right"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_shrinkage_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Study this plot carefully. It contains the entire epistemological
justification for hierarchical modeling, visible across two dimensions:
the horizontal population averages and the individual agent estimates.

\subsection{The Horizontal Lines: The Quartet of Population
Means}\label{the-horizontal-lines-the-quartet-of-population-means}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  True Generative Mean (Solid Black): The actual center of our simulated
  universe. This is the structural reality we are trying to recover.
\item
  No Pooling Mean (Dot-dash Orange): The unweighted arithmetic average
  of the isolated estimates. Because it treats all agents equally, it is
  highly susceptible to being dragged away from the truth by extreme
  outliers with very little data.
\item
  Complete Pooling Mean (Dashed Red): The unweighted average of all
  trials. Because it ignores agent boundaries, it allows participants
  who completed massive numbers of trials to disproportionately pull the
  center of gravity toward themselves.
\item
  Partial Pooling Mean (\(\mu_\theta\), Dotted Blue): The
  precision-weighted compromise. By mathematically down-weighting the
  noisy, low-\(N_j\) agents, it reliably lands closest to the true
  generative mean.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The Points and Arrows: Information-Weighted
Regularization}\label{the-points-and-arrows-information-weighted-regularization}

\begin{itemize}
\item
  The Overfitting Extremes: Look at the tails of the graph. The orange
  open circles (No Pooling) are extreme. If we trusted No Pooling, we
  would conclude these agents have massive, almost deterministic biases.
\item
  The Power of the Prior: The solid blue dots (Partial Pooling) are
  systematically pulled inward. The hierarchical model ``knows'' that
  extremely high or low values are mathematically unlikely given the
  behavior of the rest of the cohort.
\item
  The Calibration of Skepticism: Look at the size of the dots,
  representing the trial count (\(N_j\)). The longest arrows (most
  aggressive shrinkage) almost exclusively belong to the smallest dots.
  When an agent has sparse data, the posterior relies heavily on the
  population prior. The largest dots barely move; when an agent has
  massive amounts of data, the likelihood overwhelms the prior, allowing
  them to remain safely extreme.
\end{itemize}

\section{The Memory Agent: Two Correlated Individual
Differences}\label{the-memory-agent-two-correlated-individual-differences}

\subsection{Introduction}\label{introduction-1}

The Biased Agent established the full Bayesian workflow for a single
individual-level parameter: simulate, fit, diagnose, recover, calibrate.
It also taught us, the hard way, that the Centered Parameterization
fails when population variance is small, and that the Non-Centered
Parameterization resolves this by decoupling individual and population
parameters geometrically. We proceed to the Memory Agent with both of
those lessons in hand.

The Memory Agent is structurally richer. Recall from Chapter 3 its
decision rule. On each trial \(i\), agent \(j\) maintains a running
average of the opponent's past choices \(m_{ij}\) and uses it as a
predictive signal:

\[h_{ij} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\text{logit}^{-1}(\alpha_j + \beta_j \cdot \text{logit}(m_{ij})))\]

\[m_{ij} = \frac{1}{i-1}\sum_{k=1}^{i-1} o_{kj}\]

There are now two individual-level parameters per agent: \(\alpha_j\),
the baseline directional bias, and \(\beta_j\), the sensitivity to
opponent history. They need not vary independently across the
population. An agent who has a strong directional tendency might also
track the opponent more aggressively --- or might not. Fitting two
separate univariate hierarchical models forecloses this question before
the data can answer it. We model the parameters jointly.

\textbf{Experimental design: the reversal paradigm.} In Ch. 6 we
discovered that fitting the memory model to a \emph{constant-bias}
opponent produces a strong positive correlation between \texttt{bias}
and \texttt{beta} in the posterior --- both parameters explain the same
rightward tendency and cannot be told apart. The reversal learning
design (opponent plays 80\% right for trials 1--120, then 20\% right for
trials 121--240) breaks this confound: in the second phase, a pure-bias
agent continues right while a memory agent flips left, providing the
discriminating contrast the two-parameter model needs. We therefore use
the reversal schedule saved in Ch. 6 as the shared opponent sequence for
all J agents here.

\subsubsection{The Bivariate Population
Distribution}\label{the-bivariate-population-distribution}

\[\begin{pmatrix} \alpha_j \\ \beta_j \end{pmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}_2\!\left(\boldsymbol{\mu},\, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}\right), \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \operatorname{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma})\, \Omega\, \operatorname{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}), \quad \Omega = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}\]

This decomposition separates how much individuals differ on each
cognitive dimension (\(\sigma_\alpha\), \(\sigma_\beta\)) from how those
dimensions covary (\(\rho\)). The scalar \(\rho \in (-1, 1)\) is the key
new inferential target.

\subsubsection{The LKJ Prior}\label{the-lkj-prior}

We need a prior on the correlation matrix \(\Omega\). Stan provides the
LKJ distribution (Lewandowski, Kurowicka, and Joe, 2009), with a single
shape parameter \(\eta > 0\):

\[\Omega \sim \text{LKJ}(\eta) \quad \propto \quad (\det \Omega)^{\eta - 1}\]

When \(\eta = 1\) the distribution is uniform over valid correlation
matrices. When \(\eta > 1\) mass concentrates toward the identity,
encoding the prior belief that parameters tend to vary independently
unless the data argue otherwise. For a \(2 \times 2\) matrix, the
marginal on \(\rho\) reduces to a rescaled Beta:
\(\rho \sim 2 \cdot \text{Beta}(\eta, \eta) - 1\). With our default
\(\eta = 2\), the prior is unimodal at zero and weakly regularizes
against extreme correlations.

Stan samples the lower-triangular \textbf{Cholesky factor}
\(\mathbf{L}_\Omega\) (declared as
\texttt{cholesky\_factor\_corr{[}2{]}}) under the
\texttt{lkj\_corr\_cholesky(eta)} prior, recovering
\(\Omega = \mathbf{L}_\Omega \mathbf{L}_\Omega^\top\) in
\texttt{generated\ quantities}. This parameterization is unconstrained
on its natural manifold.

\subsubsection{Non-Centered
Parameterization}\label{non-centered-parameterization}

The scalar NCP from the biased agent
(\(\theta_j = \mu + z_j \cdot \sigma\)) generalizes directly. We
introduce a \(2 \times J\) matrix of independent standard normals:

\[z_{kj} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \text{ i.i.d.}, \qquad \begin{pmatrix}\alpha_j \\ \beta_j\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}\mu_\alpha \\ \mu_\beta\end{pmatrix} + \operatorname{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma})\,\mathbf{L}_\Omega\,\mathbf{z}_j\]

The sampler traverses the flat, isotropic space of \(\mathbf{Z}\), whose
geometry is entirely independent of \(\boldsymbol{\sigma}\) and
\(\Omega\). Neal's Funnel is structurally impossible.

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/hierarchical_memory_dag-1.png}

}

\caption{Standard Plate Notation (left) and Kruschke-Style Notation
(right) for the Hierarchical Memory Agent. The correlation node Omega
and the bivariate MVN distinguish this model from the biased agent.}

\end{figure}%

\subsection{Phase 1: Generative Plausibility of the Memory
Agent}\label{phase-1-generative-plausibility-of-the-memory-agent}

Before writing any Stan code, we construct the forward generative model
in R. This code must strictly mirror the mathematical hierarchy above.

One design decision deserves explicit justification. The memory signal
\(m_{ij}\) is a deterministic function of the opponent's past choices.
We could compute it inside Stan's \texttt{transformed\ data} block, but
this executes a nested loop on every leapfrog step of every chain.
Instead, we pre-compute the vector \texttt{opp\_rate\_prev} in R once
and pass it as data. This reduces the memory calculation to a single
vectorized pass before fitting begins, at zero loss of model fidelity.

The simulation function must also mirror the NCP Stan model exactly. We
draw \(\mathbf{z}_j \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}_2)\) and
apply the Cholesky transform, exactly as the Stan model will. For a
\(2 \times 2\) correlation matrix with off-diagonal \(\rho\), the lower
Cholesky factor is:

\[\mathbf{L}_\Omega = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ \rho & \sqrt{1-\rho^2} \end{pmatrix}\]

We sample \(\rho\) from the LKJ(2) marginal:
\(\rho \sim 2 \cdot \text{Beta}(2, 2) - 1\).

In accordance with the rigorous Bayesian workflow, the simulation
function is designed to either:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Draw all hyperparameters directly from the stated priors (for Prior
  Predictive Checks).
\item
  Accept fixed ground-truth hyperparameters (for Parameter Recovery).
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Simulate Hierarchical Memory Agents}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_agents Integer, number of agents (J).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_trials\_min Integer, minimum trials per agent.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param n\_trials\_max Integer, maximum trials per agent.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param mu\_alpha Numeric, population mean baseline bias (logit scale). NULL draws from prior.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param mu\_beta Numeric, population mean memory sensitivity. NULL draws from prior.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param sigma\_alpha Numeric, population SD of alpha. NULL draws from prior.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param sigma\_beta Numeric, population SD of beta. NULL draws from prior.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param rho Numeric, population correlation alpha{-}beta. NULL draws from LKJ(2).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param opponent\_choices Integer vector of length \textgreater{}= max(n\_trials\_max).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}   If provided, every agent plays a consecutive prefix of this fixed}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}   sequence (realistic: all participants face the same opponent).}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}   If NULL, generates independent Bernoulli draws using opponent\_rate.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param opponent\_rate Numeric in (0,1). Used only when opponent\_choices}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}   is NULL. Default 0.7.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param seed Integer, for exact computational reproducibility.}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return A tidy tibble containing both latent parameters and observed choices.}
\NormalTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n\_agents      =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min  =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max  =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha      =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_beta       =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_alpha   =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_beta    =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{rho           =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{opponent\_rate    =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{seed          =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(seed)) }\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}

  \CommentTok{\# 1. Sample hyperparameters from priors if not provided}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(mu\_alpha))    mu\_alpha    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(mu\_beta))     mu\_beta     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(sigma\_alpha)) sigma\_alpha }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(sigma\_beta))  sigma\_beta  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  \CommentTok{\# LKJ(2) marginal on rho for 2x2 = 2*Beta(2,2) {-} 1}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(rho))         rho         }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{rbeta}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}

  \CommentTok{\# 2. Construct the lower{-}triangular Cholesky factor L\_Omega}
\NormalTok{  L\_Omega }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, rho, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ rho}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)), }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}

  \CommentTok{\# 3. Sample individual parameters via the NCP transform (mirrors Stan exactly)}
  \CommentTok{\# Z is 2 x J; Scale = diag(sigma) \%*\% L\_Omega}
\NormalTok{  Z            }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ n\_agents), }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  Scale        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(sigma\_alpha, sigma\_beta)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ L\_Omega}
  \CommentTok{\# indiv\_params is J x 2: column 1 = alpha\_j, column 2 = beta\_j}
\NormalTok{  indiv\_params }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{t}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(mu\_alpha, mu\_beta) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Scale }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ Z)}

  \CommentTok{\# 4. Simulate trial{-}level data}
\NormalTok{  n\_trials\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(n\_trials\_min}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_trials\_max, n\_agents, }\AttributeTok{replace =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_agents), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(j) \{}
\NormalTok{    n\_t     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ n\_trials\_vec[j]}
\NormalTok{    alpha\_j }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ indiv\_params[j, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]}
\NormalTok{    beta\_j  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ indiv\_params[j, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{]}

    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(opponent\_choices)) \{}
      \CommentTok{\# Shared reversal schedule: agent plays the first n\_t steps of the}
      \CommentTok{\# fixed sequence. Agents who leave early see only the first phase.}
      \FunctionTok{stopifnot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(opponent\_choices) }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\NormalTok{ n\_t)}
\NormalTok{      opp\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices[}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_t)]}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{      opp\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_t, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, opponent\_rate)}
\NormalTok{    \}}

    \CommentTok{\# Pre{-}compute running opponent rate BEFORE each trial (strict t{-}1 history).}
    \CommentTok{\# Trial 1 has no history: initialize to 0.5 (uninformative).}
    \CommentTok{\# Clip to [0.01, 0.99] so logit(opp\_rate\_prev) is always finite.}
\NormalTok{    opp\_rate\_prev    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(n\_t)}
\NormalTok{    opp\_rate\_prev[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (n\_t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{      raw\_rates         }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(opp\_choices[}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{n\_t]) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      opp\_rate\_prev[}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.01}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{, raw\_rates))}
\NormalTok{    \}}

\NormalTok{    logit\_p       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_j }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_j }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(opp\_rate\_prev)}
\NormalTok{    agent\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_t, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(logit\_p))}

    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{agent\_id      =}\NormalTok{ j,}
      \AttributeTok{trial         =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_t),}
      \AttributeTok{n\_trials      =}\NormalTok{ n\_t,}
      \AttributeTok{choice        =}\NormalTok{ agent\_choices,}
      \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ opp\_rate\_prev,}
      \AttributeTok{true\_alpha    =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_j,}
      \AttributeTok{true\_beta     =}\NormalTok{ beta\_j}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  \})}

  \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{) }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha    =}\NormalTok{ mu\_alpha,    }\AttributeTok{mu\_beta     =}\NormalTok{ mu\_beta,}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_alpha =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_alpha, }\AttributeTok{sigma\_beta  =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_beta,}
    \AttributeTok{rho         =}\NormalTok{ rho}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  sim\_data}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsubsection{Executing the Prior Predictive Check: The Joint
Population
Cloud}\label{executing-the-prior-predictive-check-the-joint-population-cloud}

For a bivariate model, the most revealing prior predictive display is a
scatter of individual \((\alpha_j, \beta_j)\) pairs across multiple
prior universes. This simultaneously reveals how dispersed individual
differences are (\(\sigma_\alpha\), \(\sigma_\beta\)), how correlated
the two cognitive dimensions tend to be (\(\rho\)), and whether any
prior universe generates implausible populations (e.g.,
\(|\beta_j| > 8\), meaning the memory signal completely overrides
choice).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Generate 12 prior universes}
\NormalTok{prior\_sims\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sim\_id) \{}
\NormalTok{  sim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_memory}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ sim\_id }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{13}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  hp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sim }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, true\_alpha, true\_beta) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{simulation\_id =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior Universe"}\NormalTok{, sim\_id))}
\NormalTok{\})}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_sims\_mem, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ true\_alpha, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ true\_beta)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray60"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray60"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{simulation\_id, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prior Predictive Checks: Implied Joint Population Distributions"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Populations generated by mu \textasciitilde{} N(0,1), sigma \textasciitilde{} Exp(1), Omega \textasciitilde{} LKJ(2)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Baseline bias  "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ alpha[j] }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{"  (logit scale)"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Memory sensitivity  "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ beta[j])}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_prior_predictive_scatter-1.pdf}
\end{center}

A healthy prior generates a range of cloud shapes --- some circular (low
\(|\rho|\)), some elongated and tilted (high \(|\rho|\)), some compact
(low \(\sigma\)), some diffuse (high \(\sigma\)). No prior universe
should collapse all agents to a point or place all agents at implausibly
extreme values.

\subsection{Phase 2: Data Preparation and the NCP Stan
Model}\label{phase-2-data-preparation-and-the-ncp-stan-model}

Having established from the biased agent workflow that NCP is our
default architecture for cognitive tasks with sparse per-agent data, we
implement it directly. We skip the Centered Parameterization entirely.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Prepare Memory Agent Data for Stan}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{}}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @param sim\_data Tibble from simulate\_hierarchical\_memory().}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} @return A named list formatted for CmdStanR.}
\NormalTok{prep\_stan\_data\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sim\_data) \{}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{N\_total                  =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(sim\_data),}
    \AttributeTok{N\_subjects               =} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{unique}\NormalTok{(sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id)),}
    \AttributeTok{subj\_id                  =}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
    \AttributeTok{y                        =}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
    \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev            =}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_alpha\_mu        =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_alpha\_sigma     =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_beta\_mu         =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_beta\_sigma      =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_sigma\_lambda       =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_Omega\_shape        =} \FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics          =} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}


\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Load the reversal learning schedule from Ch. 6 {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# This 240{-}trial sequence (80\% right → reversal → 20\% right) was validated}
\CommentTok{\# in Ch. 6 to cleanly identify bias and beta. All agents play the same}
\CommentTok{\# opponent schedule, with variable trial counts (80–240 trials).}
\NormalTok{reversal\_obj      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simdata"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch06\_reversal\_data.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{opponent\_reversal }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ reversal\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opponent\_choices  }\CommentTok{\# 240{-}element integer vector}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded reversal schedule from Ch. 6:"}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(opponent\_reversal), }\StringTok{"trials |"}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(opponent\_reversal[}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{]), }\StringTok{"right in phase 1 |"}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(opponent\_reversal[}\DecValTok{121}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{240}\NormalTok{]), }\StringTok{"right in phase 2}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Loaded reversal schedule from Ch. 6: 240 trials | 93 right in phase 1 | 23 right in phase 2
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Generate hierarchical memory data using the reversal schedule {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{sim\_data\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_memory}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n\_agents         =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min     =} \DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{,    }\CommentTok{\# Minimum: agents see at least the first phase}
  \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max     =} \DecValTok{240}\NormalTok{,   }\CommentTok{\# Maximum: agents complete both phases}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha         =}  \FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{\# No systematic directional baseline}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_beta          =}  \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{\# Moderate positive memory tracking}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_alpha      =}  \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_beta       =}  \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{rho              =}  \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{,  }\CommentTok{\# Mild positive correlation}
  \AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_reversal,  }\CommentTok{\# \textless{}{-}{-} reversal schedule from Ch. 6}
  \AttributeTok{seed             =} \DecValTok{1981}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{stan\_data\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{prep\_stan\_data\_memory}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_memory)}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{"Hierarchical memory data: \%d agents, \%d total trials, \%d–\%d trials per agent}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{  stan\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{N\_subjects,}
\NormalTok{  stan\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{N\_total,}
  \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_trials),}
  \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Hierarchical memory data: 50 agents, 8058 total trials, 84–237 trials per agent
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{Stan Model: Hierarchical Memory Agent
(NCP)}\label{stan-model-hierarchical-memory-agent-ncp}

The only new structural element relative to the biased agent NCP is the
bivariate extension. Instead of a scalar \(z_j\), we have a
\(2 \times J\) matrix \(\mathbf{Z}\). Instead of scaling by a single
\(\sigma\), we apply \texttt{diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma,\ L\_Omega)} ---
which computes
\(\operatorname{diag}(\boldsymbol{\sigma})\mathbf{L}_\Omega\) and
produces the full Cholesky scale matrix encoding both marginal SDs and
the correlation structure.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_memory\_ncp\_code }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Hierarchical Memory Agent Model (Non{-}Centered Parameterization)}
\StringTok{// alpha\_j (baseline bias) and beta\_j (memory sensitivity) are jointly}
\StringTok{// drawn from a bivariate Normal. NCP decouples sampling geometry from}
\StringTok{// the population parameters, preventing Neal\textquotesingle{}s Funnel.}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_total;                    // Total observations}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_subjects;                 // Number of agents}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=N\_subjects\textgreater{}  subj\_id;   // Agent index}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{}  y;       // Observed choices}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0.01, upper=0.99\textgreater{}[N\_total] opp\_rate\_prev;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  real prior\_mu\_alpha\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_mu\_alpha\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_mu\_beta\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_mu\_beta\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_sigma\_lambda;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_Omega\_shape;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  // Population{-}level parameters}
\StringTok{  real mu\_alpha;              // Population mean baseline bias (logit scale)}
\StringTok{  real mu\_beta;               // Population mean memory sensitivity}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}[2] sigma;   // Marginal SDs}

\StringTok{  // Cholesky factor of the correlation matrix}
\StringTok{  cholesky\_factor\_corr[2] L\_Omega;}

\StringTok{  // Individual{-}level parameters (NCP)}
\StringTok{  matrix[2, N\_subjects] z;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[N\_subjects, 2] indiv\_params;}
\StringTok{  \{}
\StringTok{    matrix[2, N\_subjects] deviations = diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma, L\_Omega) * z;}
\StringTok{    for (j in 1:N\_subjects) \{}
\StringTok{      indiv\_params[j, 1] = mu\_alpha + deviations[1, j];}
\StringTok{      indiv\_params[j, 2] = mu\_beta  + deviations[2, j];}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Hyperpriors}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_alpha | prior\_mu\_alpha\_mu, prior\_mu\_alpha\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_beta  | prior\_mu\_beta\_mu,  prior\_mu\_beta\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma | prior\_sigma\_lambda);}
\StringTok{  target += lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega | prior\_Omega\_shape);}

\StringTok{  // Individual{-}level prior (NCP)}
\StringTok{  target += std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z));}

\StringTok{  // Likelihood}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] logit\_p;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{    logit\_p[i] = indiv\_params[subj\_id[i], 1]}
\StringTok{               + indiv\_params[subj\_id[i], 2] * logit(opp\_rate\_prev[i]);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | logit\_p);}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[2, 2] Omega = multiply\_lower\_tri\_self\_transpose(L\_Omega);}
\StringTok{  real rho = Omega[1, 2];}

\StringTok{  vector[N\_subjects] alpha    = indiv\_params[, 1];}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_subjects] beta\_mem = indiv\_params[, 2];}

\StringTok{  // Prior log{-}density}
\StringTok{  real lprior = normal\_lpdf(mu\_alpha | prior\_mu\_alpha\_mu, prior\_mu\_alpha\_sigma)}
\StringTok{              + normal\_lpdf(mu\_beta  | prior\_mu\_beta\_mu,  prior\_mu\_beta\_sigma)}
\StringTok{              + exponential\_lpdf(sigma | prior\_sigma\_lambda)}
\StringTok{              + lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega | prior\_Omega\_shape)}
\StringTok{              + std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z));}

\StringTok{  // Predictive checks and pointwise log{-}likelihood}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int y\_post\_rep;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int y\_prior\_rep;}

\StringTok{  // Prior draws for prior{-}posterior update plots}
\StringTok{  real mu\_alpha\_prior    = normal\_rng(prior\_mu\_alpha\_mu, prior\_mu\_alpha\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  real mu\_beta\_prior     = normal\_rng(prior\_mu\_beta\_mu, prior\_mu\_beta\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  real sigma\_alpha\_prior = exponential\_rng(prior\_sigma\_lambda);}
\StringTok{  real sigma\_beta\_prior  = exponential\_rng(prior\_sigma\_lambda);}
\StringTok{  // LKJ(2) marginal on rho for 2x2: 2*Beta(2,2) {-} 1}
\StringTok{  real rho\_prior         = 2.0 * beta\_rng(2.0, 2.0) {-} 1.0;}

\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    matrix[2, 2] L\_Omega\_prior = lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_rng(2, prior\_Omega\_shape);}
\StringTok{    vector[2] sigma\_prior = [sigma\_alpha\_prior, sigma\_beta\_prior]\textquotesingle{};}
\StringTok{    matrix[2, N\_subjects] z\_prior;}
\StringTok{    for (j in 1:N\_subjects) \{ }
\StringTok{      z\_prior[1,j] = normal\_rng(0,1); }
\StringTok{      z\_prior[2,j] = normal\_rng(0,1); }
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{    matrix[N\_subjects, 2] indiv\_prior;}
\StringTok{    \{}
\StringTok{      matrix[2, N\_subjects] dev\_prior = diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma\_prior, L\_Omega\_prior) * z\_prior;}
\StringTok{      for (j in 1:N\_subjects) \{}
\StringTok{        indiv\_prior[j, 1] = mu\_alpha\_prior + dev\_prior[1, j];}
\StringTok{        indiv\_prior[j, 2] = mu\_beta\_prior  + dev\_prior[2, j];}
\StringTok{      \}}
\StringTok{    \}}

\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      int j = subj\_id[i];}
\StringTok{      real lp\_post  = indiv\_params[j,1] + indiv\_params[j,2] * logit(opp\_rate\_prev[i]);}
\StringTok{      real lp\_prior = indiv\_prior[j,1]  + indiv\_prior[j,2]  * logit(opp\_rate\_prev[i]);}

\StringTok{      log\_lik[i]     = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | lp\_post);}
\StringTok{      y\_post\_rep[i]  = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(lp\_post);}
\StringTok{      y\_prior\_rep[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(lp\_prior);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\NormalTok{stan\_file\_memory\_ncp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_multilevel\_memory\_ncp.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_memory\_ncp\_code, stan\_file\_memory\_ncp)}
\NormalTok{mod\_memory\_ncp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_memory\_ncp)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Two Stan details are worth noting. First, the local block
\texttt{\{\ matrix{[}2,J{]}\ deviations\ =\ ...;\ \}} in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} confines the intermediate matrix to
local scope, preventing it from appearing in the parameter output.
Second, \texttt{lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_rng(2,\ 2.0)} in
\texttt{generated\ quantities} draws a full Cholesky factor from the
LKJ(2) prior. This is the correct way to generate prior predictive data
--- unlike the Beta marginal trick, it generalizes to the \(3 \times 3\)
case we will need for the WSLS agent.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Efficiency --- Cholesky Factorization for Correlation Matrices}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Whenever a model includes a \textbf{correlation matrix} \(\Omega\) over
individual-level parameters, use the Cholesky-factorized
parameterization rather than sampling \(\Omega\) directly.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Parameterization
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Why avoid
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{corr\_matrix{[}K{]}\ Omega} + \texttt{lkj\_corr\_lpdf} & Direct
& Requires \(O(K^3)\) matrix square root and positive-definiteness
checks at every leapfrog step \\
\texttt{cholesky\_factor\_corr{[}K{]}\ L\_Omega} +
\texttt{lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf} & Cholesky & \(L_\Omega\) is already
lower-triangular; lies directly on its natural constrained manifold;
\(O(K^2)\) operations \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Avoid}
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{ }\DataTypeTok{corr\_matrix}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] Omega; \}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{     \{ Omega \textasciitilde{} lkj\_corr(}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{); \}}

\CommentTok{// Prefer}
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{ }\DataTypeTok{cholesky\_factor\_corr}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] L\_Omega; \}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{      \{ L\_Omega \textasciitilde{} lkj\_corr\_cholesky(}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{); \}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{// Apply the Cholesky scale to NCP offsets in O(K\^{}2) — no matrix inversion}
  \DataTypeTok{matrix}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, J] deviations = diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma, L\_Omega) * z;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{generated quantities}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{// Recover the full correlation matrix once per saved draw (not every leapfrog step)}
  \DataTypeTok{matrix}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] Omega = multiply\_lower\_tri\_self\_transpose(L\_Omega);}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ rho = Omega[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{];}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Key point:} the matrix multiply
\texttt{diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma,\ L\_Omega)\ *\ z} gives the
individual-level deviations directly --- no matrix square-root needed at
run time. The correlation matrix \(\Omega\) is only reconstructed in
\texttt{generated\ quantities}, where it runs once per saved draw
instead of once per leapfrog step.

For \(K = 2\) parameters the saving is modest; for \(K = 5\) or more
(e.g., five per-subject RL parameters in a full model) it becomes
substantial.

\end{tcolorbox}

\subsubsection{Fitting the model}\label{fitting-the-model-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit\_memory\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_fit\_memory\_ncp.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Initialize at prior means.}
\NormalTok{N\_subj\_val\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ stan\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{N\_subjects}

\NormalTok{init\_memory\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) \{}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{), }
    \AttributeTok{mu\_beta =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{), }
    \AttributeTok{sigma =} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{L\_Omega =} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{nrow=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }
    \AttributeTok{z =} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{nrow=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol=}\NormalTok{N\_subj\_val\_mem)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\})}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_memory\_ncp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data            =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data\_memory,}
    \AttributeTok{seed            =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains          =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup     =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling   =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{init            =}\NormalTok{ init\_memory\_list, }
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta     =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{,   }
    \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth   =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh         =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

We raise \texttt{adapt\_delta} to 0.90. The bivariate NCP is
well-behaved globally, but the correlation parameter \(\rho\) introduces
a curved direction that can require longer leapfrog trajectories. The
higher target acceptance rate forces the dual-averaging algorithm to
find a smaller step size, reducing integration error along this
direction. If \texttt{max\_treedepth} warnings appear (distinct from
divergences), raise \texttt{max\_treedepth} further --- do not raise
\texttt{adapt\_delta} in response to those.

\subsubsection{Assessing the fitting
process}\label{assessing-the-fitting-process-1}

Even if the Stan compiler finishes without explicit warnings, our
workflow demands that we systematically verify the geometry of the
posterior and the efficiency of the sampler.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#fit\_memory$cmdstan\_diagnose()}
\NormalTok{fit\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
$num_divergent
[1] 0 0 0 0

$num_max_treedepth
[1] 0 0 0 0

$ebfmi
[1] 0.7753663 0.6990873 0.7302008 0.7121430
\end{verbatim}

We are looking for strict zeros in \texttt{num\_divergent}. With the
NCP, this should hold. Any divergences here are a signal that
\texttt{adapt\_delta} needs further adjustment, the priors are
misspecified, or there is a structural model issue.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Chain Mixing and Stationarity
(\(\hat{R}\))}{Chain Mixing and Stationarity (\textbackslash hat\{R\})}}\label{chain-mixing-and-stationarity-hatr-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{draws\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()}

\FunctionTok{mcmc\_trace}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  draws\_memory,}
  \AttributeTok{pars =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{np   =} \FunctionTok{nuts\_params}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Trace Plots: Population Parameters (Memory Agent NCP)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"All five population{-}level parameters, including rho. Stationary chains expected throughout."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_chain_mixing-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{draws\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{subset\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{,}
                            \StringTok{"alpha[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"beta\_mem[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"beta\_mem[2]"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{default\_convergence\_measures}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrr@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
variable & rhat & ess\_bulk & ess\_tail \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
mu\_alpha & 1.003 & 4536.130 & 3219.616 \\
mu\_beta & 1.000 & 5530.353 & 3365.868 \\
sigma{[}1{]} & 1.001 & 3094.326 & 2956.688 \\
sigma{[}2{]} & 1.000 & 1998.533 & 2940.560 \\
rho & 1.000 & 1646.126 & 2270.844 \\
alpha{[}1{]} & 1.002 & 5728.964 & 3161.287 \\
alpha{[}2{]} & 1.002 & 6427.232 & 3574.001 \\
beta\_mem{[}1{]} & 1.001 & 5303.794 & 2973.740 \\
beta\_mem{[}2{]} & 1.000 & 5467.761 & 3269.354 \\
\end{longtable}

The potential scale reduction factor (\(\hat{R}\)) must be below 1.01
for all parameters. The correlation parameter \texttt{rho} is informed
only by covariation \emph{across} agents, not within any individual
agent's trial sequence, so its ESS will be lower than the marginal
parameters. ESS \textgreater{} 400 remains our threshold. An ESS below
200 for \texttt{rho} warrants investigation of the correlation manifold
geometry.

\paragraph{Effective Sample Size
(ESS)}\label{effective-sample-size-ess-1}

Monitor \texttt{ess\_tail} for \texttt{sigma{[}1{]}} and
\texttt{sigma{[}2{]}}. Population SDs can develop mild funnel-like
behavior even under NCP when the likelihood is highly informative about
individual parameters, because the \(z_j\) draws must simultaneously
explain many agents. If \texttt{ess\_tail} is low for the sigmas but
\texttt{ess\_bulk} is acceptable, raising \texttt{adapt\_delta} further
or running more warmup iterations is the correct response.

\paragraph{Energy (E-BFMI)}\label{energy-e-bfmi}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_nuts\_energy}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{nuts\_params}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Energy Transitions: Memory Agent NCP"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Marginal and transition energy distributions must align. E{-}BFMI \textgreater{} 0.2 required."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_energy-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsubsection{Posterior Parameter
Correlations}\label{posterior-parameter-correlations-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_pairs}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  draws\_memory,}
  \AttributeTok{pars         =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{np           =} \FunctionTok{nuts\_params}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory),}
  \AttributeTok{diag\_fun     =} \StringTok{"dens"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{off\_diag\_fun =} \StringTok{"hex"}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_posterior_correlations-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The critical panels are \((\sigma_\alpha, \mu_\alpha)\) and
\((\sigma_\beta, \mu_\beta)\). Under the CP biased agent, these showed
triangular wedges --- the Neal's Funnel signature. Under NCP, they
should be roughly elliptical. Any triangular shape here would be
unexpected and would require investigation.

\subsection{Prior-Posterior Update Checks: Quantifying
Learning}\label{prior-posterior-update-checks-quantifying-learning-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{draws\_df\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(draws\_memory)}

\CommentTok{\# Extract true generative hyperparameters saved as attributes}
\NormalTok{true\_hp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_memory, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{true\_pop\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{parameter  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{true\_value =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(true\_hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu\_alpha, true\_hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu\_beta,}
\NormalTok{                 true\_hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma\_alpha, true\_hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma\_beta, true\_hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rho)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Reshape: split on the underscore immediately before "posterior" or "prior"}
\NormalTok{pp\_update\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_df\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha\_posterior  =}\NormalTok{ mu\_alpha,        mu\_alpha\_prior,}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_beta\_posterior   =}\NormalTok{ mu\_beta,         mu\_beta\_prior,}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_alpha\_posterior =} \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{sigma[1]}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{,    sigma\_alpha\_prior,}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_beta\_posterior  =} \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{sigma[2]}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{,    sigma\_beta\_prior,}
    \AttributeTok{rho\_posterior       =}\NormalTok{ rho,             rho\_prior}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(),}
    \AttributeTok{names\_to  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"parameter"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"distribution"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{names\_sep =} \StringTok{"\_(?=posterior|prior)"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(pp\_update\_mem, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ value)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ distribution), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ true\_pop\_mem,}
    \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =}\NormalTok{ true\_value, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"True Value"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"darkred"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{parameter, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{name   =} \StringTok{"Distribution"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"gray70"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_linetype\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{name   =} \StringTok{"Ground Truth"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"True Value"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior{-}Posterior Update Checks: Memory Agent"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x     =} \StringTok{"Parameter Value"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y     =} \StringTok{"Count"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_pp_update-1.pdf}
\end{center}

A healthy update shows a posterior significantly narrower than the prior
and comfortably contained within the prior's mass. The \texttt{rho}
panel carries the central new scientific information. If the posterior
on \(\rho\) is essentially indistinguishable from the LKJ(2) prior, the
data do not contain sufficient information to identify the
population-level correlation --- this can happen with small \(J\) or
short trial sequences. In that case, report the parameter as
unidentified, not the prior as the conclusion. A clearly narrowed
posterior constitutes genuine evidence about the cognitive architecture
of the population.

\subsection{Posterior Predictive Checks (PPC): Generative
Adequacy}\label{posterior-predictive-checks-ppc-generative-adequacy-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{y\_obs\_mem     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(stan\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{y)}
\NormalTok{agent\_idx\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(stan\_data\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{subj\_id)}
\NormalTok{y\_rep\_mem     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"y\_post\_rep"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{n\_sample\_mem       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{12}
\NormalTok{sampled\_agents\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{unique}\NormalTok{(agent\_idx\_mem), n\_sample\_mem)}
\NormalTok{keep\_mask\_mem      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_idx\_mem }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%}\NormalTok{ sampled\_agents\_mem}

\NormalTok{prop\_right }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(x) }\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(x)}

\FunctionTok{ppc\_stat\_grouped}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{y     =}\NormalTok{ y\_obs\_mem[keep\_mask\_mem],}
  \AttributeTok{yrep  =}\NormalTok{ y\_rep\_mem[, keep\_mask\_mem],}
  \AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ agent\_idx\_mem[keep\_mask\_mem],}
  \AttributeTok{stat  =} \StringTok{"prop\_right"}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Posterior Predictive Checks: Agent{-}Level Choice Rates (Subset)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Showing"}\NormalTok{, n\_sample\_mem, }\StringTok{"randomly sampled agents. Dark line: Observed. Histogram: Predicted."}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"Proportion of \textquotesingle{}Right\textquotesingle{} Choices"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_ppc-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsubsection{Calibration and
Recovery}\label{calibration-and-recovery-1}

We have proven the model fits the data and generates plausible
predictions. Now we must prove the parameters we extract are actually
the true parameters that generated the data. For a bivariate model,
recovery must be assessed at both levels: all five population
parameters, and the individual \((\alpha_j, \beta_j)\) pairs jointly.

\paragraph{Individual-Level Recovery}\label{individual-level-recovery-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{recovery\_alpha }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{subset\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{variable =} \StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_draws}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{str\_extract}\NormalTok{(variable, }\StringTok{"(?\textless{}=}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{[)}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{d+(?=}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{])"}\NormalTok{)))}

\NormalTok{recovery\_beta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{subset\_draws}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{variable =} \StringTok{"beta\_mem"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_draws}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{str\_extract}\NormalTok{(variable, }\StringTok{"(?\textless{}=}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{[)}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{d+(?=}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{])"}\NormalTok{)))}

\NormalTok{true\_agent\_params\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_data\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, n\_trials, true\_alpha, true\_beta)}

\NormalTok{recovery\_mem\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ true\_agent\_params\_mem }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(recovery\_alpha }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_est =}\NormalTok{ median, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_q5 =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_q95 =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(recovery\_beta  }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, }\AttributeTok{beta\_est  =}\NormalTok{ median, }\AttributeTok{beta\_q5  =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{beta\_q95  =}\NormalTok{ q95),  }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_rec\_alpha }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_mem\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ true\_alpha, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_est)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Recovery: Baseline Bias "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ alpha[j]),}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True alpha (logit scale)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \StringTok{"N trials"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{p\_rec\_beta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_mem\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ true\_beta, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ beta\_est)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ beta\_q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ beta\_q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Recovery: Memory Sensitivity "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ beta[j]),}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True beta"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \StringTok{"N trials"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{p\_rec\_alpha }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ p\_rec\_beta }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{plot\_layout}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{guides =} \StringTok{"collect"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_recovery-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Recovery for \(\beta_j\) is typically noisier than for \(\alpha_j\) at
any given trial count. The memory signal \(\text{logit}(m_{ij})\) starts
at zero (the \(\text{logit}(0.5) = 0\) initialization) and becomes
informative only as the cumulative opponent history diverges from 0.5.
Agents with few trials show stronger shrinkage toward
\(\mu_\beta = 1.5\) --- not toward zero, but toward the estimated
population mean, which is the appropriate regularization given what the
rest of the cohort tells us about the population.

\subsubsection{Prior sensitivity}\label{prior-sensitivity-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sens\_results\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{powerscale\_sensitivity}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_memory,}
  \AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"=== PRIOR SENSITIVITY DIAGNOSTICS: MEMORY AGENT ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
=== PRIOR SENSITIVITY DIAGNOSTICS: MEMORY AGENT ===
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(sens\_results\_mem)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Sensitivity based on cjs_dist
Prior selection: all priors
Likelihood selection: all data

 variable prior likelihood                     diagnosis
 mu_alpha 0.033      0.075                             -
  mu_beta 0.058      0.076 potential prior-data conflict
 sigma[1] 0.361      0.208 potential prior-data conflict
 sigma[2] 0.517      0.124 potential prior-data conflict
      rho 0.034      0.197                             -
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{ps\_seq\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{powerscale\_sequence}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_memory,}
  \AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_dens}\NormalTok{(ps\_seq\_mem) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prior Sensitivity: Density Perturbation (Memory Agent)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Does scaling the prior or likelihood drastically alter our beliefs about the population?"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_prior_sensitivity-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_quantities}\NormalTok{(ps\_seq\_mem) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prior Sensitivity: Parameter Estimates (Memory Agent)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Horizontal lines: baseline posterior bounds. Deviations indicate sensitivity."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_memory_prior_sensitivity-2.pdf}
\end{center}

Pay particular attention to the \texttt{rho} panel. Because the
correlation is identified only from across-agent covariation, it has
fewer effective observations than the marginal population parameters. If
\texttt{rho} shows strong sensitivity to prior scaling, the data contain
limited information about the correlation structure and the LKJ prior is
doing meaningful regularization.

\subsubsection{Simulation-Based Calibration Checks
(SBC)}\label{simulation-based-calibration-checks-sbc-1}

Parameter recovery on a single dataset is anecdotal. We must prove that
the sampler is globally unbiased across the entire prior space.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbc\_memory\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_sbc\_memory.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{generate\_sbc\_dataset\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{240}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Use the same reversal schedule as the main fit.}
  \CommentTok{\# opponent\_reversal is available in the chapter environment from Edit 6.16.}
\NormalTok{  sim  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_hierarchical\_memory}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_agents         =}\NormalTok{ n\_agents,}
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min     =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials\_min,}
    \AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max     =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials\_max,}
    \AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_reversal}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  hp   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{attr}\NormalTok{(sim, }\StringTok{"hyperparameters"}\NormalTok{)}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha   =}\NormalTok{ hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu\_alpha,}
      \AttributeTok{mu\_beta    =}\NormalTok{ hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu\_beta,}
      \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{sigma[1]}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma\_alpha,}
      \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{sigma[2]}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma\_beta,}
      \AttributeTok{rho        =}\NormalTok{ hp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rho,}
      \AttributeTok{alpha      =}\NormalTok{ sim }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, true\_alpha) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{pull}\NormalTok{(true\_alpha),}
      \AttributeTok{beta\_mem   =}\NormalTok{ sim }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, true\_beta)  }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{pull}\NormalTok{(true\_beta)}
\NormalTok{    ),}
    \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{prep\_stan\_data\_memory}\NormalTok{(sim)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{generator\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  generate\_sbc\_dataset\_memory,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_agents =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_min =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials\_max =} \DecValTok{120}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{backend\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  mod\_memory\_ncp,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sbc\_memory\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_datasets\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(generator\_memory, }\DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(sequential)}
  \CommentTok{\#future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 4)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results\_memory  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{datasets =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_datasets\_memory, }\AttributeTok{backend =}\NormalTok{ backend\_memory,}
    \AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{datasets =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_datasets\_memory, }\AttributeTok{results =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results\_memory),}
\NormalTok{    sbc\_memory\_path}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_mem\_obj         }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sbc\_memory\_path)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_datasets\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_mem\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{datasets}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results\_memory  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_mem\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{results}
\NormalTok{\}}

\FunctionTok{plot\_rank\_hist}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results\_memory, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"SBC Rank Histograms: Memory Agent NCP"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results\_memory, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"SBC ECDF Difference: Memory Agent NCP"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Deviation from the diagonal indicates miscalibration."}
\NormalTok{  )}
