\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Extract the lightweight stats dataframe from the SBC object}
\NormalTok{sbc\_stats\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{stats}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Filter for the population{-}level parameters you care about}
\CommentTok{\# (e.g., mu\_alpha, mu\_beta, rho, or even sigma[1])}
\NormalTok{recovery\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_stats\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"rho"}\NormalTok{))}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Create the Parameter Recovery Identity Plot}
\NormalTok{p\_sbc\_recovery }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ median)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# The Identity Line (Perfect Recovery)}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# The Posterior Estimates with 90\% Credible Intervals}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Facet by parameter}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{variable, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Parameter Recovery (Extracted Directly from SBC)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Points represent the posterior median. Bars are 90\% Credible Intervals."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True Generative Value (Drawn from Prior)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median Estimate"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{strip.background =} \FunctionTok{element\_rect}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"gray95"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{))}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(p\_sbc\_recovery)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_sbc_memory-3.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 4. Quantify the Recovery (Correlation and RMSE)}
\NormalTok{recovery\_metrics }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ recovery\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(variable) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarize}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{Pearson\_r =} \FunctionTok{cor}\NormalTok{(simulated\_value, median),}
    \AttributeTok{RMSE =} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{((simulated\_value }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ median)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"=== PARAMETER RECOVERY QUANTIFICATION ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{diags\_memory  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{backend\_diagnostics}
\NormalTok{mem\_fail\_rate }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(diags\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_divergent }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{100}

\NormalTok{diags\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ n\_divergent)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{binwidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"darkred"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}

    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\%.0f\%\% of universes produced at least one divergence."}\NormalTok{, mem\_fail\_rate),}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"Number of Divergent Transitions"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Number of SBC Universes"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_sbc_memory_diagnostics-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Does any failure concentrate at small sigma values?}
\NormalTok{posterior}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(sbc\_datasets\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{variables) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sim\_id =} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    diags\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sim\_id =} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(sim\_id, n\_divergent),}
    \AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"sim\_id"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{had\_divergence =}\NormalTok{ n\_divergent }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{sigma[1]}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{sigma[2]}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ had\_divergence)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"TRUE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"darkred"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Clean"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"TRUE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Divergent"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{name   =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Memory NCP: Where Do Divergences Occur?"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Any divergences should not concentrate at small sigma values (that would indicate residual funnel geometry)."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(sigma[alpha]), }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(sigma[beta])}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{06-MultilevelModels_files/figure-pdf/ch07_sbc_memory_diagnostics-2.pdf}
\end{center}

The \texttt{rho} rank histogram will typically have wider confidence
bands than the marginal mean and SD histograms at \(N = 200\). This is
expected: \(\rho\) is identified only from covariation across \(J = 50\)
agents, so each SBC universe contributes one effective draw of \(\rho\)
to the calibration check. Mild irregularity within the confidence
envelope is statistical noise. Systematic U-shape or strong asymmetry
would indicate a genuine calibration failure.

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Cognitive Interpretation of
\(\hat{\rho}\)}{Cognitive Interpretation of \textbackslash hat\{\textbackslash rho\}}}\label{cognitive-interpretation-of-hatrho}

The posterior on \(\rho\) is the inferential result that distinguishes
this model from two independent univariate hierarchical models.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{\(\rho \approx 0\)}: Baseline tendency and memory-based
  opponent tracking are orthogonal individual differences. Studying one
  tells us nothing about the other.
\item
  \textbf{\(\rho > 0\)}: Agents with a stronger directional baseline
  also tend to weight memory more heavily --- consistent with a general
  exploitation tendency, where both parameters index willingness to
  depart from 50/50 in response to a signal.
\item
  \textbf{\(\rho < 0\)}: Agents with a strong directional baseline tend
  to suppress memory use --- a compensatory account in which prior
  commitment crowds out sensitivity to the opponent's history.
\end{itemize}

None of these interpretations follow from the model alone. The model
provides a calibrated posterior over \(\rho\). Cognitive theory provides
the framework for deciding what that value means.

\subsubsection{LOO for Model Comparison}\label{loo-for-model-comparison}

We retain the LOO-CV score here for the model comparison section at the
end of the chapter.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{loo\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{(fit\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{))}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(loo\_memory)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}

\section{Conclusion: From One Agent to a
Population}\label{conclusion-from-one-agent-to-a-population}

This chapter extended the Bayesian modeling workflow to multiple
participants. The core ideas are:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Partial pooling} is not a compromise --- it is the
  statistically correct way to combine information across individuals
  with unequal data, and it provably outperforms both extremes (as the
  shrinkage plot showed).
\item
  \textbf{Centered vs.~non-centered parameterization} is a geometric
  choice, not a philosophical one. CP fails when population variance is
  small (Neal's Funnel); NCP fixes the global geometry but can be
  locally inefficient when data are very informative. SBC revealed which
  regime we are in.
\item
  \textbf{The reversal learning design} is essential for the memory
  model. A constant-bias opponent makes \texttt{bias} and \texttt{beta}
  statistically inseparable; the reversal schedule provides the contrast
  that identifies them.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Coming up in Chapter 8:} We have now validated two models ---
the hierarchical biased agent and the hierarchical memory agent --- and
computed LOO scores for both. Chapter 8 introduces model comparison:
given that both models have passed validation, which one better predicts
held-out data? The answer will use the LOO scores and the
\texttt{loo\_compare()} function from the \texttt{loo} package.

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Deciding Between Competing Theories: Model
Comparison}\label{deciding-between-competing-theories-model-comparison}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chs. 1--6
built models, validated them, and scaled them to populations. Every
model passed a six-phase quality battery before fitting real data. This
chapter asks the next question: when two validated models both fit the
data, which one better \emph{predicts} data it has never seen? The
answer uses PSIS-LOO cross-validation and requires the validated fits
from Ch. 7. The reversal learning design introduced in Ch. 6 and used in
Ch. 7 is essential here too --- it is the design that makes the two
models distinguishable in the first place.
\end{quote}

By Chapter 7 we had a working Bayesian workflow: specify a generative
model, validate it with SBC and parameter recovery, fit it to data, and
diagnose the posterior geometry, both in the case of single agent and
hierarchical models. We now face a different question: we rarely have
only one possible model of the cognitive processes underlying the
observed behaviors.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Sometimes we have different models representing competing theoretical
  accounts of cognitive processes. For instance, a Gaussian filter model
  of learning regularities in one's opponent's behavior in the Matching
  Pennies game vs a Theory of Mind model of learning the model used by
  the opponent vs.~an active inference model trying to actively deceive
  the opponent.
\item
  Sometimes we have simpler and more complex versions of the same model:
  should we assume we equally learn from all feedback (using a
  reinforcement learning model) or should we assume different learning
  from positive and negative feedback (using a dual learning rate
  model)?
\end{itemize}

In either case, once we have ensured that all models involved pass their
individual quality checks, how do we decide which better accounts for
the observed data?

There are many ways to compare models, but here we develop a Bayesian
predictive approach consistent with our approach in previous chapters.
The core argument is that a model should be judged not by how well it
explains the data it was \emph{trained} on, but by how well it predicts
data it has \emph{never seen}. We formalize this as the Expected Log
Predictive Density (ELPD), build an efficient computational
approximation via PSIS-LOO, and apply the full workflow to the biased
and memory agent models from Chapter 7. This approach also gives us a
principled way to identify difficult to predict data points (sometimes
called influential observations), which can tell us much about the
limits of our models and different mechanisms at work in different
portions of the data. However, note that focusing on predictive
abilities does not directly equate to model most likely to be true! It
is a useful pragmatic measure and we should keep it in mind.

As in previous chapters, we heavily rely on simulations to understand
these techniques and validate our model comparison procedure. Because we
control the data-generating mechanism, we can evaluate not just whether
model comparison produces a preference, but whether it produces the
\emph{correct} preference --- the model recovery test.

Remember, model comparison is not a fail-safe procedure to determine the
absolute ``truth''. All models are simplifications. Instead, it helps us
identify which of our candidate models provides the most useful or most
plausible account of the data, given our current understanding and the
available evidence. Model comparison should always be part of a larger
principled scientific workflow using models as stepping stones to deeper
understanding, not as final arbiters of truth.

\begin{quote}
CRITICAL PRECONDITION: Model comparison is only scientifically
meaningful if all candidate models have already passed the individual
quality checks established in Chapter 6. If Model A converges perfectly
but Model B fails its SBC calibration or has persistent divergences, an
ELPD comparison is a ``garbage-in, garbage-out'' exercise. We only
compare models that have earned the right to be taken seriously.
\end{quote}

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-6}

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Explain the failure mode of in-sample fit: Understand why simply
  choosing the model that fits the training data best is misleading and
  leads to the ``memorization'' of noise.
\item
  Understand Cross-Validation: Grasp the core concept of partitioning
  data into training and test sets to estimate generalization
  performance.
\item
  Define ELPD rigorously: State the Expected Log Predictive Density as
  the formal Bayesian target for predictive accuracy.
\item
  Implement PSIS-LOO: Compute ELPD estimates from Stan fits and use
  Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) to identify when the importance-sampling
  approximation is unreliable.
\item
  Characterize influential observations: Link high \(\hat{k}\) values
  back to the cognitive structure of the task, distinguishing between
  person-level outliers and trial-level surprises.
\item
  Perform exact agent-level K-Fold CV: Implement a parallelized
  cross-validation pipeline to test how models generalize to completely
  new participants.
\item
  Diagnose LOO-PIT calibration: Evaluate whether a model's predictive
  uncertainty matches reality using randomized PIT values and the formal
  POT-C test.
\item
  Interpret loo\_compare() and Bayesian Stacking: Reason about the
  \(|elpd\_diff|/se\_diff\) ratio and use stacking weights to find the
  optimal predictive mixture of models.
\item
  Conduct Power Analysis for Model Recovery: Use 2x2 confusion matrices
  and 2D trade-off plots (Trials vs.~Participants) to determine if an
  experimental design is discriminative enough to identify the true
  cognitive mechanism.
\item
  Evaluate Model Calibration via SBC: Move beyond binary ``wins'' to
  assess the probabilistic calibration of model weights using
  Simulation-Based Calibration Checks for Models.
\item
  Identify sequentially dependent data: Recognize when trial-level
  exchangeability is violated and describe the Leave-Future-Out (LFO-CV)
  remedy.
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Historical Context: From Fit to Prediction --- The History of Model
Selection}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

How to choose among competing models is one of the oldest unsolved
problems in statistics. The modern answer --- estimate out-of-sample
predictive accuracy --- is less obvious than it sounds, and the path to
it passed through several influential wrong turns.

\subsubsection{The In-Sample Era: Likelihood and
R²}\label{the-in-sample-era-likelihood-and-ruxb2}

The classical approach was to ask which model assigns the highest
probability to the observed data: the maximum likelihood. This criterion
was the backbone of hypothesis testing (Fisher, 1922; Neyman \& Pearson,
1933) and dominated empirical science throughout the twentieth century.
Its failure mode was known --- a model with enough free parameters can
fit any finite dataset perfectly --- but was treated as a matter of
scientific judgment rather than a formal problem. The coefficient of
determination \(R^2\) shared the same flaw: it increases monotonically
with model complexity and therefore cannot serve as a principled
selection criterion.

\subsubsection{Information Criteria: AIC and
BIC}\label{information-criteria-aic-and-bic}

The first principled solution came from information theory. Akaike
(1974) derived the \textbf{Akaike Information Criterion} (AIC) by asking
a precise question: what is the expected Kullback-Leibler divergence
between the true data-generating distribution and the distribution
implied by a fitted model, evaluated on \emph{new} data from the same
process? The answer, to leading order, is \(-2\log\hat{L} + 2k\), where
\(\hat{L}\) is the maximized likelihood and \(k\) is the number of free
parameters. The penalty term \(2k\) is a correction for the optimism of
in-sample fit: a model that uses \(k\) extra parameters gains
approximately \(2k\) units of log-likelihood purely by fitting noise,
and AIC subtracts this back out.

Schwarz (1978) derived the \textbf{Bayesian Information Criterion} (BIC)
from a different starting point --- approximating the log marginal
likelihood under a diffuse prior --- and obtained a penalty of
\(k \log n\) that grows with sample size and more aggressively penalises
complexity. AIC and BIC dominated applied statistics through the 1980s
and 1990s, but both inherit a key limitation: they are computed on the
training data and require asymptotic approximations whose validity in
small, heterogeneous cognitive datasets is difficult to verify.

\subsubsection{Cross-Validation and the Equivalence
Theorem}\label{cross-validation-and-the-equivalence-theorem}

Stone (1977) proved a result that initially seemed surprising: AIC and
leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-CV) are \textbf{asymptotically
equivalent}. Cross-validation --- holding out each observation in turn,
refitting on the rest, and evaluating predictive accuracy on the
held-out point --- gives the same answer as AIC in large samples, but
without requiring asymptotic approximations or a count of free
parameters. This equivalence is important because cross-validation is
model-agnostic: it works on any model that can generate predictions,
whether or not its parameter count is well-defined (as is the case for
hierarchical Bayesian models where effective complexity is
data-dependent).

\subsubsection{PSIS-LOO: Making Cross-Validation
Affordable}\label{psis-loo-making-cross-validation-affordable}

The practical obstacle to LOO-CV was computational: for a model fit to
\(N\) observations, exact LOO requires \(N\) separate refits. Vehtari,
Gelman, and Gabry (2017) solved this with \textbf{Pareto-Smoothed
Importance Sampling LOO} (PSIS-LOO). The key insight is that a model fit
to all \(N\) observations already contains, implicitly, the posterior
for each leave-one-out model --- the left-out observation simply
receives zero weight. Importance sampling reweights the full-data
posterior to approximate the leave-one-out posterior, and
Pareto-smoothing of the importance weights stabilises the estimator and,
crucially, provides a per-observation diagnostic (the \(\hat{k}\)
statistic) that flags when the approximation is unreliable. This
diagnostic turned model comparison from a black-box number into an
interpretable workflow: high \(\hat{k}\) observations are not just
nuisances but windows into the structure of the data that the model is
struggling to predict.

The arc from AIC (1974) to PSIS-LOO (2017) is a story about a single
conceptual shift --- from \emph{how well does the model fit what we have
seen?} to \emph{how well does the model predict what we have not yet
seen?} --- being made progressively more rigorous, more computationally
feasible, and more transparent about its own failure modes.

\end{tcolorbox}

\section{Why In-Sample Fit Fails: The Overfitting
Trap}\label{why-in-sample-fit-fails-the-overfitting-trap}

The most naive approach to comparing two models is to ask which fits the
training data better. The problem with this criterion is that it is
systematically biased toward more complex models. A model with enough
free parameters can memorize any finite dataset. But memorization is not
understanding --- a model that has memorized the noise in its training
data will make poor predictions on new data it has never seen.

To make this concrete, we generate data from a simple quadratic curve
with added noise, then fit three polynomial models of increasing
complexity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{101}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{n      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{30}
\NormalTok{x      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{length.out =}\NormalTok{ n)}
\NormalTok{y\_true }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ x }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.8} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ x}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}
\NormalTok{y\_obs  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ y\_true }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{df\_fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y\_obs)}

\NormalTok{fit\_p1  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ x,           }\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ df\_fit)}
\NormalTok{fit\_p2  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \FunctionTok{poly}\NormalTok{(x, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),  }\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ df\_fit)}
\NormalTok{fit\_p10 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \FunctionTok{poly}\NormalTok{(x, }\DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ df\_fit)}

\NormalTok{x\_pred  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{length.out =} \DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{pred\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{x    =}\NormalTok{ x\_pred,}
  \AttributeTok{lin  =} \FunctionTok{predict}\NormalTok{(fit\_p1,  }\AttributeTok{newdata =} \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x\_pred)),}
  \AttributeTok{quad =} \FunctionTok{predict}\NormalTok{(fit\_p2,  }\AttributeTok{newdata =} \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x\_pred)),}
  \AttributeTok{high =} \FunctionTok{predict}\NormalTok{(fit\_p10, }\AttributeTok{newdata =} \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x\_pred))}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{x, }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"model"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"y\_pred"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{model =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(model,}
    \AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"lin"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"quad"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"high"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Degree 1 (underfit)"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Degree 2 (true DGP)"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Degree 10 (overfit)"}\NormalTok{)))}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df\_fit, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ pred\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y\_pred, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ model), }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Model"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"The Overfitting Trap: In{-}Sample Fit Favours Complexity"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Degree{-}10 polynomial fits training data perfectly but would fail on new data. Degree{-}2 (true DGP) generalizes best."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Predictor"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Outcome"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-overfitting-demo-1.pdf}
\end{center}

In this plot, we generated data from a quadratic curve with added noise.
A linear model (red) underfits, missing a crucial feature of the data:
the curvature. A degree-10 polynomial (green) overfits the data: it
captures the curvature, but also the noise, deviating here and there to
capture each stray datapoint. The quadratic model (blue) captures the
underlying trend without fitting every wiggle, representing a better
balance.

The error these models make on the training data (e.g.~root mean square
error) monotonically decreases as we increase the complexity of the
model (and the variance explained, \(R^2\), monotonically increases),
and therefore we cannot use it to choose between models. We need a
criterion that rewards genuine generalization: models that capture the
signal without overfitting the noise.

\subsection{The Concept of
Cross-Validation}\label{the-concept-of-cross-validation}

To find this balance between fitting and generalizing, we need a method
to test how well our model performs on data it hasn't seen yet. Since we
usually only have one dataset, we artificially create ``unseen'' data by
hiding parts of our dataset from the model during the training phase.
This is the core concept of \textbf{Cross-Validation (CV)}.

The general process is:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Split your data into a \emph{training set} and a \emph{test set},
  respecting the structure in the data. E.g. if you have multiple
  participants, you might want to hold out entire participants for
  testing, rather than random trials, to test generalization to new
  individuals.
\item
  Fit (train) your model using only the training data.
\item
  Evaluate the model's predictions on the hidden test data.
\item
  Repeat this process with different splits and average the performance.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection{K-Fold Cross-Validation}\label{k-fold-cross-validation}

In K-Fold cross-validation, we divide the data into \(K\) equally sized
subsets (folds). We train the model on \(K-1\) folds and test it on the
remaining fold, repeating this until every fold has served as the test
set exactly once.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Visualization of k{-}fold cross{-}validation}
\NormalTok{n\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{20}
\NormalTok{cv\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{index =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_data, }\AttributeTok{value =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_data))}
\NormalTok{cv\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{fold }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{length.out =}\NormalTok{ n\_data))}

\NormalTok{cv\_viz\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{iteration =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_data),}
  \AttributeTok{data\_point =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_data, }\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{role =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(cv\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{fold[data\_point] }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ iteration, }\StringTok{"test"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"train"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(cv\_viz\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ data\_point, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ iteration, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ role)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_tile}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"train"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"test"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{), }
                    \AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Data Role"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"K{-}Fold Cross{-}Validation (k=5)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Each row is one iteration; the model must predict the red tiles."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Data Point"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Iteration"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{panel.grid =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-kfold-viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsubsection{Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
(LOO-CV)}\label{leave-one-out-cross-validation-loo-cv}

Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOO-CV) is the extreme limit of K-Fold,
where \(K\) equals the total number of data points (\(N\)). In each
iteration, we hold out exactly one observation for testing and train the
model on the remaining \(N-1\) observations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{loo\_viz\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{iteration =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_data, }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_data),}
  \AttributeTok{data\_point =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_data, n\_data),}
  \AttributeTok{role =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(iteration }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ data\_point, }\StringTok{"test"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"train"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(loo\_viz\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ data\_point, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ iteration, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ role)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_tile}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"train"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"test"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{), }
                    \AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Data Role"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Leave{-}One{-}Out Cross{-}Validation"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Maximum training data used, but requires fitting the model N times."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Data Point"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Iteration"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{panel.grid =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-loo-cv-viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Cross-validation is especially important in cognitive Bayesian modeling
for several reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Bayesian models can be highly parameterized (especially hierarchical
  ones), making them prone to overfitting.
\item
  Prior choices can significantly influence a model's predictive
  performance.
\item
  We often need to compare competing theoretical accounts of the same
  behavior.
\end{enumerate}

However, implementing cross-validation for Bayesian models presents two
key challenges that we must solve:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Proper Scoring}: In standard machine learning, we might just
  calculate the mean squared error or accuracy on the test fold. But
  Bayesian models output \emph{probability distributions}, not just
  point estimates. We need an appropriate metric for evaluating the
  quality of the full predictive distribution.
\item
  \textbf{Computational Cost}: For a dataset with 6,000 observations,
  true LOO-CV would require fitting our Stan model 6,000 separate times.
  Because MCMC sampling can take hours, exact LOO-CV is usually
  practically impossible.
\end{enumerate}

In the next sections, we will solve the first challenge using the
\textbf{Expected Log Predictive Density (ELPD)}, and the second
challenge using an elegant mathematical shortcut called
\textbf{PSIS-LOO}.

\section{Formalizing Predictive Accuracy: The
ELPD}\label{formalizing-predictive-accuracy-the-elpd}

\subsection{The Log-Score Proper Scoring
Rule}\label{the-log-score-proper-scoring-rule}

Any model \(M\) can generate a predictive distribution: how likely
different values of the outcome variable are estimated to be given the
data it has seen and the model's assumptions.

When an actual new outcome \(\tilde{y}_0\) is observed, we evaluate the
model by checking exactly how much probability mass it assigned to the
event that actually happened: \(p(\tilde{y}_0 \mid y, M)\).

We take the natural logarithm of this probability to compute the
log-score:
\[\text{log-score}(M, \tilde{y}_0) = \log p(\tilde{y}_0 \mid y, M)\]

We use the log scale for two primary reasons:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Additivity}: The joint probability of independent future
  observations is the product of their individual probabilities. On the
  log scale, this becomes a sum, which is mathematically tractable and
  avoids computational underflow: probabilities being smaller than one
  being multiplied with each other become small very quickly and run
  into approximation issues, because computers use only a fixed number
  of decimals.
\item
  \textbf{Information Theory}: The negative log-score,
  \(-\log p(\tilde{y}_0 \mid y, M)\), is the \emph{surprisal} or Shannon
  information content of the outcome under the model. Maximizing the
  log-score is equivalent to minimizing the model's surprise.
\end{itemize}

We call the log-score a \emph{scoring rule}: a function that provides a
summary measure for the evaluation of probabilistic forecasts. A scoring
rule is strictly \emph{proper} if the model maximizes its expected score
if and only if the probability distribution it outputs exactly matches
the true data-generating distribution. This is a desirable property: a
model cannot ``game'' the score by being strategically overconfident or
underconfident. Overconfident models - those that assign near-zero
probability to outcomes that then occur - incur unbounded penalties. A
model that says ``I am 99\% certain this participant will choose right''
and the participant chooses left will be penalized far more than a model
that honestly said ``I think it's about 60\%.''

You may have encountered Bayes Factors as a method for model comparison.
Bayes Factors rely on the marginal likelihood---the probability of the
data integrated over the entire prior. While theoretically elegant,
Bayes Factors are notoriously sensitive to the exact width of priors and
are often computationally impossible to calculate for the complex
hierarchical models used in cognitive science. In this course, we rely
on ELPD and Cross-Validation because they focus on predictive
generalization, are more robust to prior choices, and provide
trial-level diagnostics that Bayes Factors lack.

\subsection{The Expected Log Predictive
Density}\label{the-expected-log-predictive-density}

The log-score evaluates a model on a single new observation. But what we
really want is a summary over \emph{all possible} future observations -
a measure of how good the model would be in general, not just on one
lucky or unlucky draw.

The Expected Log Predictive Density (ELPD) does exactly this: it
averages the log-score over all possible future observations, weighted
by how likely those observations are under the true data-generating
distribution \(p_t\):

\[\text{ELPD} = \int p_t(\tilde{y}) \log p(\tilde{y} \mid y, M) \, d\tilde{y}\]

where
\(p(\tilde{y} \mid y, M) = \int p(\tilde{y} \mid \theta) \, p(\theta \mid y) \, d\theta\)
is the posterior predictive distribution - the model's prediction for a
new observation, averaged over the uncertainty in its parameters. The
model with the highest ELPD makes the best average predictions across
the full range of possible future data.

There is one crucial catch: we do not know \(p_t\) - the true
distribution of future observations. If we did, we would not need a
model in the first place! So we have to \emph{estimate} the ELPD from
the data we have. Leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-CV) provides an
approach that is asymptotically unbiased:

\[\widehat{\text{ELPD}}_{\text{LOO}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(y_i \mid y_{-i}, M)\]

The notation \(y_{-i}\) means ``all observations except observation
\(i\)''. So for each data point, we ask: if the model had never seen
this observation, how well would it predict it? A model that memorized
\(y_i\) to reduce its training loss will be penalized here, because the
LOO posterior is computed \emph{without} \(y_i\). The prediction must
generalize - it cannot cheat by peeking at the answer.

But how do we calculate it?

\subsection{The Importance-Sampling
Shortcut}\label{the-importance-sampling-shortcut}

Computing \(\widehat{\text{ELPD}}_{\text{LOO}}\) exactly would require
fitting the model \(N\) times - once with each observation left out. For
our hierarchical models with 50 agents and 120 trials each, that is
6,000 separate MCMC runs. This is not feasible in practice, especially
when we get to more complex models.

Fortunately, there is an elegant mathematical shortcut, which is
analogous to what we have seen when assessing prior sensitivity. The
idea is to use the \emph{single} full-data posterior we already have and
to \emph{reweight} its draws to approximate what the posterior would
have looked like had we omitted observation \(i\). .

The mathematical weights that accomplish this reweighting are the
\emph{inverse} of the likelihood contributions: observations that the
model fits well (high likelihood) get down-weighted when computing what
the model ``would have'' predicted without them. These log-scale
importance weights are simply the negative pointwise log-likelihoods:

\[\log r_i^{(s)} = -\log p(y_i \mid \theta^{(s)})\]

These are already stored in our Stan \texttt{log\_lik} block from
Chapter 7. So no additional computation is required: everything we need
is already sitting in our existing model fits.

However, we should be careful. If the model's estimate would barely
change when we removing observation \(i\), then \(i\) is not very
influential, and we can approximate the LOO estimate cheaply. If the
model's estimate would change a lot, then \(i\) is influential, and our
approximation is less reliable. We therefore need to use a diagnostic to
flag such cases: the Pareto-\(\hat{k}\), which we cover in detail in a
later paragraph.

This combination of LOO cross-validation with Pareto-Smoothed Importance
Sampling is called \textbf{PSIS-LOO} (Vehtari, Gelman \& Gabry, 2017),
and it is our primary tool for model comparison, so we'll go through it
step by step.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why learn the mechanics before using \texttt{loo()}?} The
\texttt{loo()} function is one line of R. But knowing what it is doing
--- reweighting posterior draws to approximate leave-one-out
predictions, then checking whether those weights are stable via the
Pareto tail --- lets you diagnose failures and interpret the
Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) diagnostic as a \emph{cognitive} signal (which
observations is the model surprised by?) rather than just a warning
light. The under-the-hood demonstration on the next page is worth
reading once carefully; after that, \texttt{loo()} will never be a black
box.
\end{quote}

To really understand what the \texttt{loo()} function will do for us
later, let's look under the hood. We can simulate the PSIS-LOO process
directly using the \texttt{loo} package's internal engine. We will
generate 1,000 simulated log-likelihood values, inject some extreme
outliers to simulate a ``shocked'' model, and feed them directly into
the actual \texttt{loo::psis()} algorithm.

We can then extract the smoothed weights and compare them to the raw
weights to see exactly how the algorithm protects us from extreme MCMC
draws.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{n\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1000}

\NormalTok{n\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1000}

\CommentTok{\# 1. Simulate realistic raw log{-}importance weights}
\CommentTok{\# 995 well{-}behaved draws, and 5 massive breakaway outliers}
\NormalTok{log\_w\_raw }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{995}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{mean =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{sd =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{14}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{18}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Apply the ACTUAL PSIS algorithm}
\NormalTok{psis\_result }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{psis}\NormalTok{(log\_w\_raw, }\AttributeTok{r\_eff =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Print the diagnostic to prove we triggered the smoothing}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Pareto{-}k diagnostic from the real algorithm:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(psis\_result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Pareto-k diagnostic from the real algorithm: 1.76 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Print the diagnostic to prove we triggered the smoothing}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Pareto{-}k diagnostic from the real algorithm:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(psis\_result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Pareto-k diagnostic from the real algorithm: 1.76 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 3. Extract and normalize weights for visualization}
\CommentTok{\# Raw weights}
\NormalTok{w\_raw }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_w\_raw)}
\NormalTok{w\_raw\_norm }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ w\_raw }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(w\_raw)}

\CommentTok{\# Smoothed weights (must also be explicitly normalized)}
\NormalTok{w\_smooth\_raw }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(psis\_result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{log\_weights))}
\NormalTok{w\_smooth\_norm }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ w\_smooth\_raw }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(w\_smooth\_raw)}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Sort the weights from smallest to largest to visualize the "tail"}
\CommentTok{\# We sort by the raw weights so we can see how the specific extreme indices change}
\NormalTok{sort\_order }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{order}\NormalTok{(w\_raw\_norm)}
\NormalTok{weight\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{raw =}\NormalTok{ w\_raw\_norm[sort\_order],}
  \AttributeTok{smoothed =}\NormalTok{ w\_smooth\_norm[sort\_order]}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# The PSIS algorithm smooths the top 3 * sqrt(S) weights. }
\CommentTok{\# For S=1000, that is the top \textasciitilde{}95 weights.}
\NormalTok{weight\_data\_top }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tail}\NormalTok{(weight\_data, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{weight\_data\_top}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rank }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{150}
\NormalTok{tail\_start\_x }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{150} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{95} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.5}

\CommentTok{\# PLOT: Importance weights before and after Pareto smoothing}
\NormalTok{p\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(weight\_data\_top, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ rank)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Arrows showing the shift computed by the real algorithm}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_segment}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xend =}\NormalTok{ rank, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ raw, }\AttributeTok{yend =}\NormalTok{ smoothed),}
               \AttributeTok{arrow =} \FunctionTok{arrow}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{length =} \FunctionTok{unit}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"cm"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{type =} \StringTok{"closed"}\NormalTok{), }
               \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"purple"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
               
  \CommentTok{\# Points drawn on top}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ raw), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ smoothed), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"purple"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Add a vertical line to show where the algorithm began smoothing}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =}\NormalTok{ tail\_start\_x, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ tail\_start\_x }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(weight\_data\_top}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{raw) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{, }
           \AttributeTok{label =} \StringTok{"Algorithm begins}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{smoothing here"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{hjust =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray30"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Labels and styling}

       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"The loo::psis() function compresses the extreme tail; remaining weights shift up to absorb the mass."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Rank of Weight (Zoomed in on the top 15\%)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Normalized Weight"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{()}

\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-psis-under-the-hood-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Notice how a few random MCMC draws initially monopolize the raw
importance weights (the high blue dots). If we calculated ELPD using
those raw weights, our cross-validation score would be highly erratic
and dictated by statistical noise. The Pareto smoothing (purple dots)
gently compresses the extreme tail of these weights into a more stable
distribution.

Mathematically, an extreme raw importance weight means the approximation
is unstable. Cognitively, an extreme importance weight means the model
is utterly shocked by this specific participant's choice. We will use
this dual mathematical/cognitive interpretation to flag influential
observations later.

But first let's get to the thick of it and start simulating data.

\section{Simulation Setup for Model
Recovery}\label{simulation-setup-for-model-recovery}

We simulate two populations of agents playing against a shared opponent
using the \textbf{reversal design} first validated in Ch. 6 and used
throughout Ch. 7: the opponent plays 80\% right for the first half of
the session, then switches to 20\% right for the second half. We use the
exact sequence loaded from Ch. 7 above (\texttt{opponent\_reversal}),
taking the first 120 trials (60 + 60) for this chapter's shorter
sessions.

Why this design? If the opponent plays a constant bias, the memory
agent's internal running average quickly settles near that value and
stays flat. Behaviorally, an agent responding to a flat memory signal is
mathematically indistinguishable from a biased agent --- both produce a
fixed right-leaning rate. The reversal forces the memory signal to
change sign at trial 61: memory agents reverse their behavior while
biased agents do not. This contrast is what makes the two models
theoretically separable, and why Ch. 6 discovered that a constant-bias
design made \texttt{bias} and \texttt{beta} unidentifiable.

\emph{Note on recovery failure:} We simulate a population with strong
memory sensitivity (\(\mu_\beta = 1.5\)). With weak memory
(\(\mu_\beta \approx 0.3\)), the behavioral difference would be subtle
enough that the simpler biased model would win the ELPD comparison by
default due to its lower complexity penalty --- a correct finding, not a
method failure.

The key insight for model recovery is that we fit \emph{both} models to
\emph{both} datasets, giving a 2×2 matrix of fits:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Biased model on biased data} --- the correctly-specified
  model.
\item
  \textbf{Memory model on biased data} --- the over-specified model; it
  has an extra parameter (\(\beta\)) it does not need.
\item
  \textbf{Biased model on memory data} --- the under-specified model; it
  lacks the flexibility to capture the reversal.
\item
  \textbf{Memory model on memory data} --- the correctly-specified
  model.
\end{itemize}

PSIS-LOO should prefer the correct model in each column. This is
\textbf{model recovery}: the analogue of parameter recovery one level
higher --- instead of asking ``can we recover the right parameter
values?'', we ask ``can we identify the right model?''

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{simulate\_model\_recovery\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n\_agents         =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{,  }\AttributeTok{n\_trials      =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_theta         =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{sigma\_theta   =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha         =} \FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{mu\_beta       =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_alpha      =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{sigma\_beta    =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{rho              =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,   }
  \AttributeTok{seed             =} \DecValTok{42}
\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}

  \CommentTok{\# Reversal block design: 80\% right for t 1:60, 20\% right for t 61:120.}
  \CommentTok{\# If a fixed sequence is supplied (from Ch. 7), use it directly so that}
  \CommentTok{\# all chapters share the same opponent history.}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(opponent\_choices)) \{}
    \FunctionTok{stopifnot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(opponent\_choices) }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\NormalTok{ n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{    opp\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ opponent\_choices[}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)]}
\NormalTok{  \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    opp\_choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
      \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{),}
      \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_trials }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Perfect unweighted memory (cumulative average)}
\NormalTok{  opp\_rate\_prev    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{  opp\_rate\_prev[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (n\_trials }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{    raw\_rates         }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(opp\_choices[}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{n\_trials]) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    opp\_rate\_prev[}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.01}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{, raw\_rates)) }\CommentTok{\# to avoid singularities}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \CommentTok{\# Generating the hierarchical biased behaviors}
  \CommentTok{\# Generating the hierarchical biased behaviors}
\NormalTok{  theta\_j }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_agents, mu\_theta, sigma\_theta)}
  \CommentTok{\# Inject extreme outliers to naturally trigger high Pareto{-}k values}
\NormalTok{  theta\_j[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{4.5}  \CommentTok{\# Agent 1 is extremely biased right}
\NormalTok{  theta\_j[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{4.5} \CommentTok{\# Agent 2 is extremely biased left}
\NormalTok{  biased\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_agents), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(j) \{}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =}\NormalTok{ j, }\AttributeTok{trial =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials),}
           \AttributeTok{choice =} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(theta\_j[j])),}
           \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ opp\_rate\_prev, }\AttributeTok{agent\_type =} \StringTok{"biased"}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{true\_theta =}\NormalTok{ theta\_j[j])}
\NormalTok{  \})}

  \CommentTok{\# Generating the parameters for the hierarchical memory behaviors with correlation}
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# We follow the NCP parametrization to show you how, but also because it\textquotesingle{}s more}
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# efficient for sampling correlated bias and beta.}
  
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# First we define the Cholesky factor for the correlation matrix:}
\NormalTok{  L\_Omega  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, rho, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ rho}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# Then we need to convert it to the covariance matrix by pre{-}multiplying by }
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# the scale parameters (correlations are z{-}scored, so to get the actual }
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# values we need to put the scales back in):}
\NormalTok{  Scale    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(sigma\_alpha, sigma\_beta)) }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ L\_Omega}
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# Then we create a matrix of independent, standard normal draws}
\NormalTok{  Z        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ n\_agents), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, n\_agents)}
  \DocumentationTok{\#\# Then we apply our rescaling to the standard normal draws and add the group{-}level means to get the individual{-}level parameters}
\NormalTok{  indiv    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{t}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(mu\_alpha, mu\_beta), }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}
                  \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, n\_agents) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Scale }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ Z)}

  \DocumentationTok{\#\# Then we generate the data for the memory model}
\NormalTok{  memory\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_agents), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(j) \{}
\NormalTok{    logit\_p }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ indiv[j, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ indiv[j, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(opp\_rate\_prev)}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =}\NormalTok{ j, }\AttributeTok{trial =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials),}
           \AttributeTok{choice =} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(logit\_p)),}
           \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ opp\_rate\_prev, }\AttributeTok{agent\_type =} \StringTok{"memory"}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{true\_alpha =}\NormalTok{ indiv[j, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], }\AttributeTok{true\_beta =}\NormalTok{ indiv[j, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{])}
\NormalTok{  \})}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{biased =}\NormalTok{ biased\_data, }\AttributeTok{memory =}\NormalTok{ memory\_data,}
       \AttributeTok{hyperparams =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu\_theta =}\NormalTok{ mu\_theta, }\AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_theta,}
                          \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha =}\NormalTok{ mu\_alpha, }\AttributeTok{mu\_beta =}\NormalTok{ mu\_beta,}
                          \AttributeTok{sigma\_alpha =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_alpha, }\AttributeTok{sigma\_beta =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_beta,}
                          \AttributeTok{rho =}\NormalTok{ rho))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{sim\_data\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simdata"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_model\_recovery\_data.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_model\_recovery\_data}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{opponent\_choices =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_reversal   }\CommentTok{\# Use Ch. 7 reversal schedule}
\NormalTok{  )}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(sim\_data, sim\_data\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sim\_data\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{d\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{biased}
\NormalTok{d\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{memory}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Memory data: N ="}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d\_memory), }\StringTok{"from"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{n\_distinct}\NormalTok{(d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id), }\StringTok{"agents}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
