\begin{verbatim}
Memory data: N = 6000 from 50 agents
\end{verbatim}

Let us visualize a sample of agents from both populations before fitting
any models. This is a useful sanity check, and part of any sensible
workflow. Check your data and simulations, folks!

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sampled\_agents }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(d\_biased, d\_memory) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_type, agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_type) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{slice\_sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =} \DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}

\FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(d\_biased, d\_memory) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{semi\_join}\NormalTok{(sampled\_agents, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"agent\_type"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(agent\_type, agent\_id, trial) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_type, agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cum\_rate =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(choice) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ trial) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ cum\_rate, }\AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ agent\_id, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ agent\_type)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray60"}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{agent\_type, }\AttributeTok{labeller =} \FunctionTok{as\_labeller}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{biased =} \StringTok{"Biased Agents (fixed bias)"}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{memory =} \StringTok{"Memory Agents (track opponent)"}\NormalTok{))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{guide =} \StringTok{"none"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_y\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Cumulative Choice Rates: Biased vs. Memory Agents"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Both populations play the reversal design (80\% right → 20\% right at trial 61). Memory agents shift; biased agents don\textquotesingle{}t."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cumulative proportion \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{}"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-visual-inspection-1.pdf}
\end{center}

We should see a difference: Both populations initially drift upwards
(rightwards in a sense) one because it is biased that way, the other
because it initially plays against a 80\%-right opponent. Memory agents
then start drifting downwards as they track the reversal in the
opponent's behavior, while biased agents stabilize. But it is not always
easy to tell them apart by eye, especially if we look at individual
agents rather than averages. This is why we need formal model comparison
techniques.

Now we prepare the four Stan data lists for our 2x2 comparison. Note
that when fitting the memory model we need to add the
\texttt{opp\_rate\_prev} even to the biased-generated data, as the model
requires it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{make\_stan\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(d)}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d), }\AttributeTok{J =} \FunctionTok{n\_distinct}\NormalTok{(d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id),}
       \AttributeTok{agent =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id, }\AttributeTok{h =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice))}

\NormalTok{make\_stan\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(d)}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d), }\AttributeTok{J =} \FunctionTok{n\_distinct}\NormalTok{(d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id),}
       \AttributeTok{agent =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id, }\AttributeTok{h =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice),}
       \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev)}

\NormalTok{sd\_biased\_on\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_stan\_biased}\NormalTok{(d\_biased)}
\NormalTok{sd\_memory\_on\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_stan\_memory}\NormalTok{(d\_biased)}
\NormalTok{sd\_biased\_on\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_stan\_biased}\NormalTok{(d\_memory)}
\NormalTok{sd\_memory\_on\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_stan\_memory}\NormalTok{(d\_memory)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

We reuse the NCP Stan models from Chapter 7. The key feature we need for
model comparison is the \texttt{log\_lik} vector in the
\texttt{generated\ quantities} block - this is the pointwise
log-likelihood for each observation that PSIS-LOO requires. This is why
we designed those models with \texttt{log\_lik} from the beginning: the
Chapter 7 workflow was already leading here.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_file\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_multilevel\_biased\_ncp.stan"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_biased)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{\textquotesingle{}}
\StringTok{data \{ int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N; int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=1,upper=J\textgreater{} agent;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0,upper=1\textgreater{} h; \}}
\StringTok{parameters \{ real mu\_theta; real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} sigma\_theta; vector[J] z\_theta; \}}
\StringTok{transformed parameters \{ vector[J] theta\_logit = mu\_theta + z\_theta*sigma\_theta; \}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_theta|0,1.5);}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma\_theta|1);}
\StringTok{  target += std\_normal\_lpdf(z\_theta);}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h|theta\_logit[agent]); \}}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  real lprior = normal\_lpdf(mu\_theta|0,1.5)+exponential\_lpdf(sigma\_theta|1)}
\StringTok{              + std\_normal\_lpdf(z\_theta);}
\StringTok{  vector[N] log\_lik; array[N] int h\_post\_rep;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    log\_lik[i]    = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i]|theta\_logit[agent[i]]);}
\StringTok{    h\_post\_rep[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(theta\_logit[agent[i]]); \} \}\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    stan\_file\_biased)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{stan\_file\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch07\_multilevel\_memory\_ncp.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_memory)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{\textquotesingle{}}
\StringTok{data \{ int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N; int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=1,upper=J\textgreater{} agent; array[N] int\textless{}lower=0,upper=1\textgreater{} h;}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0.01,upper=0.99\textgreater{}[N] opp\_rate\_prev; \}}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real mu\_alpha; real mu\_beta; vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}[2] sigma;}
\StringTok{  cholesky\_factor\_corr[2] L\_Omega; matrix[2,J] z; \}}
\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[J,2] indiv\_params;}
\StringTok{  \{ matrix[2,J] dev = diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma,L\_Omega)*z;}
\StringTok{    for (j in 1:J) \{}
\StringTok{      indiv\_params[j,1] = mu\_alpha+dev[1,j];}
\StringTok{      indiv\_params[j,2] = mu\_beta+dev[2,j]; \} \} \}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_alpha|0,1); target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_beta|0,1);}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma|1);}
\StringTok{  target += lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega|2);}
\StringTok{  target += std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z));}
\StringTok{  vector[N] logit\_p;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N)}
\StringTok{    logit\_p[i] = indiv\_params[agent[i],1]}
\StringTok{               + indiv\_params[agent[i],2]*logit(opp\_rate\_prev[i]);}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h|logit\_p); \}}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[2,2] Omega = multiply\_lower\_tri\_self\_transpose(L\_Omega);}
\StringTok{  real rho = Omega[1,2];}
\StringTok{  real lprior = normal\_lpdf(mu\_alpha|0,1)+normal\_lpdf(mu\_beta|0,1)}
\StringTok{              + exponential\_lpdf(sigma|1)+lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega|2)}
\StringTok{              + std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z));}
\StringTok{  vector[N] log\_lik; array[N] int h\_post\_rep;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    real lp = indiv\_params[agent[i],1]}
\StringTok{            + indiv\_params[agent[i],2]*logit(opp\_rate\_prev[i]);}
\StringTok{    log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i]|lp);}
\StringTok{    h\_post\_rep[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(lp); \} \}\textquotesingle{}}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{    stan\_file\_memory)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{mod\_biased\_ncp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_biased)}
\NormalTok{mod\_memory\_ncp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_memory)}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Both NCP models compiled.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Both NCP models compiled.
\end{verbatim}

\section{Fitting the Four Model-Data
Combinations}\label{fitting-the-four-model-data-combinations}

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why fit both models to both datasets?} Fitting only the true model
to its own data tells us the model works --- but it cannot tell us
whether ELPD actually discriminates. For model comparison to be
meaningful, the wrong model must score \emph{worse} than the right one.
Fitting all four combinations gives us a 2×2 confusion matrix: if
PSIS-LOO correctly selects the generating model in each column, the
method has recovered the truth. If it selects the wrong model, that is
scientifically informative too --- it tells us the experimental design
is not discriminative enough at this sample size.
\end{quote}

We now fit all four combinations. This is the computationally expensive
step, but it only needs to be run once --- thereafter, results are
loaded from disk. The \texttt{fit\_or\_load()} helper encapsulates this
logic.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit\_or\_load }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(label, model, data\_list, file\_path, init\_fn, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(file\_path)) \{}
    \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Fitting: "}\NormalTok{, label)}
\NormalTok{    fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ model}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_list, }\AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ seed, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{init =} \FunctionTok{init\_fn}\NormalTok{(data\_list),}
      \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{max\_treedepth =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(file\_path)}
\NormalTok{  \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(file\_path)}
    \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded: "}\NormalTok{, label)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{  fit}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{init\_biased }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(data\_list) \{}
\NormalTok{  J }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data\_list}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{J}
  \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) }\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu\_theta =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{sigma\_theta =} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{z\_theta =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(J,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{init\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(data\_list) \{}
\NormalTok{  J }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data\_list}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{J}
  \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) }\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mu\_alpha =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{mu\_beta =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{sigma =} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{L\_Omega =} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{z =} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol =}\NormalTok{ J)))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{fit\_b\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{fit\_or\_load}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{, mod\_biased\_ncp, sd\_biased\_on\_biased,}
\NormalTok{  here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch08\_fit\_b\_on\_b.rds"}\NormalTok{), init\_biased)}
\NormalTok{fit\_m\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{fit\_or\_load}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{, mod\_memory\_ncp, sd\_memory\_on\_biased,}
\NormalTok{  here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch08\_fit\_m\_on\_b.rds"}\NormalTok{), init\_memory)}
\NormalTok{fit\_b\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{fit\_or\_load}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{, mod\_biased\_ncp, sd\_biased\_on\_memory,}
\NormalTok{  here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch08\_fit\_b\_on\_m.rds"}\NormalTok{), init\_biased)}
\NormalTok{fit\_m\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{fit\_or\_load}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{, mod\_memory\_ncp, sd\_memory\_on\_memory,}
\NormalTok{  here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch08\_fit\_m\_on\_m.rds"}\NormalTok{), init\_memory)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Before computing any model comparison statistics, we verify that all
four fits pass the standard geometry diagnostics from Chapter 6. This
step matters especially for the misspecified fits. When we fit the
memory model to biased data, we are asking a model to find a
memory-tracking signal where none exists - it may strain to do so, which
can sometimes produce divergences or poor mixing.

If we find issues, we need to think critically. On the one hand,
comparing a well fitted model to a poorly fitted one is not a strong
test of their relative performance. On the other hand, if the
misspecified model is so difficult to fit that it fails basic
diagnostics, that is itself a sign of poor performance. So as we
investigate the sources of the issues, we need to critically evaluate
whether they are a sign of a fundamental mismatch between the model and
the data-generating mechanism or just a technical issue that can be
resolved with more careful fitting.

Remember that we already run the full bayesian workflow on these models
in the previous chapters, so here we only briefly check whether these
new fitting processes show any sign of issues. If these were new models,
we would need to do a thorough validation first!

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fits }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ fit\_b\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ fit\_m\_on\_b,}
             \StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ fit\_b\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ fit\_m\_on\_m)}

\FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{names}\NormalTok{(fits), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(label) \{}
\NormalTok{  f }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fits[[label]]}
\NormalTok{  sm }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ f}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{quiet =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Compute both metrics in a single pass}
\NormalTok{  post\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{summarise\_draws}\NormalTok{(f}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(), }\StringTok{"rhat"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ess\_bulk"}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{Combination  =}\NormalTok{ label,}
    \AttributeTok{Divergences  =} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(sm}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{num\_divergent),}
    \AttributeTok{Max\_Rhat     =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(post\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rhat, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{Min\_ESS\_bulk =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(post\_summary}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ess\_bulk, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{caption =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}
    \StringTok{"Pre{-}comparison diagnostics. Required: Divergences = 0, R{-}hat \textless{} 1.01, ESS \textgreater{} 400."}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"Misspecified fits (Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased, Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory) may show slightly worse ESS"}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"because the model is searching for structure that does not exist in the data."}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"This is informative: severe degradation here would itself be evidence against the model."}
\NormalTok{  ))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrr@{}}
\caption{Pre-comparison diagnostics. Required: Divergences = 0, R-hat
\textless{} 1.01, ESS \textgreater{} 400. Misspecified fits
(Memory-\textgreater Biased, Biased-\textgreater Memory) may show

does not exist in the data. This is informative: severe degradation here
would itself be evidence against the model.}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}

\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead

\endhead

\endlastfoot
Biased-\textgreater Biased & 0 & 1.006 & 868 \\
Memory-\textgreater Biased & 0 & 1.006 & 963 \\

\section{Computing PSIS-LOO}\label{computing-psis-loo}

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{The Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) Diagnostic: Which
Observations Are
Influential?}{The Pareto-\textbackslash hat\{k\} Diagnostic: Which Observations Are Influential?}}\label{the-pareto-hatk-diagnostic-which-observations-are-influential}

The \texttt{loo()} function performs two critical operations: it
estimates the Expected Log Predictive Density (ELPD) via Pareto-smoothed
importance sampling, and it quantifies the mathematical reliability of
this approximation using the Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) diagnostic.

Recall that PSIS-LOO approximates exact leave-one-out cross-validation
by reweighting draws from the full-data posterior. The raw importance
weights are the inverse of the pointwise likelihoods. If an observation
\(y_i\) is highly unexpected by the model, its importance weight becomes
astronomically large. If a few posterior draws capture most of the
weight, the Monte Carlo approximation collapses (cannot represent the
full dataset).

To stabilize this, the algorithm fits a Generalized Pareto Distribution
to the right tail of these raw weights. The \(\hat{k}\) statistic is the
estimated shape parameter of this distribution, directly measuring the
heaviness of the tail. Conceptually, if removing a single trial would
drastically shift the posterior, extreme importance weights are required
to compensate, causing \(\hat{k}\) to blow up. Thus, \(\hat{k}\) serves
two vital purposes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Statistical diagnostic: It flags specific observations where the
  reweighting shortcut is mathematically unreliable.
\item
  Cognitive diagnostic: It identifies highly influential or surprising
  trials that violate the model's structural expectations.
\end{itemize}

We execute the function with save\_psis = TRUE to retain the smoothed
weights matrix, which is strictly required for calibration checks later
in the workflow (LOO-PIT, which will be explained then).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{get\_raw\_loo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(fit\_obj, file\_path) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(file\_path)) \{}
    \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Computing and saving raw LOO to "}\NormalTok{, file\_path)}
\NormalTok{    loo\_obj }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{save\_psis =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
    \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj, file\_path)}
    \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj)}
\NormalTok{  \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
    \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loading cached raw LOO from "}\NormalTok{, file\_path)}
    \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(file\_path))}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{loo\_b\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{get\_raw\_loo}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_b, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_raw\_b\_on\_b.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_m\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{get\_raw\_loo}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_b, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_raw\_m\_on\_b.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_b\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{get\_raw\_loo}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_m, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_raw\_b\_on\_m.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_m\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{get\_raw\_loo}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_m, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_raw\_m\_on\_m.rds"}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{summarise\_pareto\_k }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj, label) \{}
\NormalTok{  k     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k}
\NormalTok{  p\_loo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"p\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{]}
\NormalTok{  n     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(k)}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Combination=}\NormalTok{label, }\AttributeTok{N=}\NormalTok{n,}
         \AttributeTok{k\_below\_05=}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(k}\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{k\_ch06\_07=}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(k}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\FloatTok{0.5}\SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{k}\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{k\_ch08\_10=}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(k}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\FloatTok{0.7}\SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{k}\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{k\_above\_10=}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(k}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{Max\_k=}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(k),}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{p\_hat\_loo=}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(p\_loo,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{ratio=}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(p\_loo}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{n,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{ELPD=}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{],}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{SE=}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{],}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{pareto\_k\_table }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_pareto\_k}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_b,}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_pareto\_k}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_b,}\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_pareto\_k}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_m,}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{summarise\_pareto\_k}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_m,}\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(pareto\_k\_table,}
  \AttributeTok{caption =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Pareto{-}k diagnostics. ratio = p\_hat\_loo/N measures model"}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"complexity relative to dataset size {-} we will use this for LOO{-}PIT below."}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Biased-\textgreater Biased & 6000 & 6000 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.162 & 41.1 &
0.007 & -3393.8 & 36.0 \\
Memory-\textgreater Biased & 6000 & 5998 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0.521 & 47.0 &
0.008 & -3395.1 & 36.0 \\
Biased-\textgreater Memory & 6000 & 6000 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.190 & 35.6 &
0.006 & -4038.3 & 15.4 \\
Memory-\textgreater Memory & 6000 & 5969 & 25 & 6 & 0 & 0.759 & 61.7 &
0.010 & -3700.3 & 27.3 \\
\end{longtable}

The action we take depends on how large \(\hat{k}\) is. The thresholds
below correspond roughly to how well the Pareto distribution fits the
tail of the importance weights --- which determines how reliable the
approximation is:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lll@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\(\hat{k}\) & Interpretation & Action \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\(< 0.5\) & Reliable & Accept PSIS-LOO \\
\([0.5, 0.7)\) & Acceptable & But proceed with caution \\
\([0.7, 1.0)\) & Problematic & Apply \texttt{loo\_moment\_match()}
first \\
\(\geq 1.0\) & Very problematic & Use actual K-fold \\
\end{longtable}

The \texttt{loo\_moment\_match()} refits the model excluding the
influential observations one by one, and assesses their out-of-sample
error under the refitted model. This brings two concerns: i)
computational feasibility: can we actually afford to refit the model
multiple times? ii) if there are multiple influential observations, they
might not be independent and only removing one would not capture the
full influence of whatever is causing the underlying issue. So once
again, we need to be critical and do our detective work. However, this
time we are lucky and it seems all is in place.

Notice also the \texttt{p\_hat\_loo} and \texttt{ratio} columns. These
are the effective number of parameters estimated by LOO and its ratio to
total observations - we will need these shortly when checking LOO-PIT
calibration.

The Effective Number of Parameters (\(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}}\)): You may
be familiar with Information Criteria like AIC or BIC, which penalize
models based on their raw parameter count (\(k\)). In Bayesian
hierarchical models, the ``true'' number of parameters is ambiguous
because of shrinkage. Instead, \(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}}\) measures the
model's optimism: the difference between how well the model fits the
training data vs.~how well it predicts held-out
data.\(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}} \approx\) total parameters: The model is
learning very little from the population
prior.\(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}} <\) total parameters: The hierarchical
prior is effectively ``narrowing'' the model's flexibility.PSIS-LOO is
essentially a more robust version of WAIC (Widely Applicable Information
Criterion) that includes the Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) diagnostic to tell you
when the penalty is unreliable.

The ratio \(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}}/N\) tells us the effective flexibility
per observation. This metric is critical for our next step: checking
predictive calibration via LOO-PIT. If this ratio is non-negligible,
removing a single observation drastically shifts the posterior, meaning
our \(N\) leave-one-out predictive distributions are highly correlated.
As Tesso and Vehtari (2026) demonstrated, this dependency invalidates
standard statistical tests for uniformity. We will explicitly rely on
this ratio to justify using specialized dependent-PIT tests (like POT-C)
to accurately diagnose model calibration.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Characterizing Influential Observations:
\emph{Why} Are They
Influential?}{Characterizing Influential Observations: Why Are They Influential?}}\label{characterizing-influential-observations-why-are-they-influential}

Finding that some observations have high \(\hat{k}\) is only the
beginning of the story. The scientifically interesting question is
\emph{why} those particular observations are influential. In a
hierarchical cognitive model, there are two structurally distinct
reasons an observation might be hard to predict, with different
theoretical implications:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The agent is an outlier from the population.} Their individual
  parameters (say, a very high memory sensitivity \(\beta_j\)) are far
  from the population mean. The model is surprised by their behavior not
  because anything unusual happened on that trial, but because the
  hierarchical prior, which uses all other agents to constrain this
  agent's parameters, is pulling the estimate toward the center. Finding
  this pattern suggests the population-level variance may be
  underestimated, or that some participants genuinely follow a different
  strategy than the majority.
\item
  \textbf{The trial itself is unusual for that agent.} Given the agent's
  estimated parameters, this specific observation is surprising, perhaps
  the memory signal was strong (opponent rate 90\%) and the model
  predicted the agent would strongly track it, but instead the agent
  chose randomly. This is a cognitively interesting failure: it
  identifies a trial where our model's assumptions break down,
  potentially revealing strategic flexibility, lapses of attention, or
  simply that agents sometimes deviate from their average behavior
  (e.g.~becoming distracted).
\end{enumerate}

To distinguish these two causes, we join the \(\hat{k}\) values back to
the raw data and look for structure across three dimensions: agent
identity, trial position, and the strength of the memory signal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{k\_mem\_on\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{k             =}\NormalTok{ loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k,}
  \AttributeTok{agent\_id      =}\NormalTok{ d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
  \AttributeTok{trial         =}\NormalTok{ d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{trial,}
  \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev,}
  \AttributeTok{fit           =} \StringTok{"Memory {-}\textgreater{} Memory"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{k\_bias\_on\_mem }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{k             =}\NormalTok{ loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k,}
  \AttributeTok{agent\_id      =}\NormalTok{ d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
  \AttributeTok{trial         =}\NormalTok{ d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{trial,}
  \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev,}
  \AttributeTok{fit           =} \StringTok{"Biased {-}\textgreater{} Memory"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{k\_annotated }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(k\_mem\_on\_mem, k\_bias\_on\_mem)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-} Panel 1: Does influence cluster within specific agents? {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{p\_by\_agent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ k\_annotated }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(fit, agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_high =} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(k }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{reorder}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(agent\_id), }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{n\_high),}
             \AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ n\_high, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ fit)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_col}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{position =} \StringTok{"dodge"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"High{-}k Observations per Agent (k \textgreater{}= 0.5)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Concentration in specific agents suggests they deviate from"}\NormalTok{,}
                        \StringTok{"the population distribution."}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Agent ID (sorted by count)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Number of high{-}k trials"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{axis.text.x =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{angle =} \DecValTok{90}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-} Panel 2: Does influence cluster at early trials? {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Early trials have uninformative memory (opp\_rate\_prev \textasciitilde{}= 0.5) so the}
\CommentTok{\# memory model has nothing to predict from, making any outcome surprising.}
\NormalTok{p\_by\_trial }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(k\_annotated, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ k, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ fit)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{method =} \StringTok{"gam"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \FunctionTok{s}\NormalTok{(x, }\AttributeTok{bs =} \StringTok{"cs"}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{se =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Pareto{-}k by Trial Position"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Both models should show higher k early: the memory signal"}\NormalTok{,}
                        \StringTok{"is uninformative (opp\_rate \textasciitilde{}= 0.5) so predictions are uncertain."}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{hat}\NormalTok{(k))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-} Panel 3: Does influence cluster at extreme opponent rates? {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# When opp\_rate\_prev is extreme (near 0 or 1), the memory model makes a}
\CommentTok{\# strong, confident prediction. If the agent does not follow the signal,}
\CommentTok{\# k will be high {-}{-} these are the most cognitively informative failures.}
\NormalTok{p\_by\_opp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(k\_annotated, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ opp\_rate\_prev, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ k, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ fit)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{method =} \StringTok{"gam"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ y }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \FunctionTok{s}\NormalTok{(x, }\AttributeTok{bs =} \StringTok{"cs"}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{se =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_brewer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{palette =} \StringTok{"Set1"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Pareto{-}k by Opponent\textquotesingle{}s Running Rate"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"At extreme rates the memory model makes confident predictions."}\NormalTok{,}
                        \StringTok{"High k here = the agent did not follow the signal."}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Opponent running choice rate (the memory signal)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{hat}\NormalTok{(k))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_by\_agent }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_by\_trial }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_by\_opp }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{plot\_annotation}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Characterizing Influential Observations"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Reading all three panels together reveals the cognitive structure"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"of model influence:}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"Panel 1: person{-}level outliers | Panel 2: temporal learning effects |"}\NormalTok{,}
      \StringTok{"Panel 3: overconfident predictions"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-characterize-influential-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Reading these three panels together, we can build a cognitive story
about model influence.

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Panel 1 tells us whether specific agents are outliers from the group
  --- if a handful of agents account for most of the high-\(\hat{k}\)
  observations, those individuals may be genuinely using a different
  strategy from the majority.
\item
  Panel 2 tells us whether the model struggles early in a session,
  before it has accumulated useful opponent history: at trial 1, the
  running opponent rate is exactly 0.5 (the model knows nothing about
  the opponent), so any outcome is equally surprising --- this is
  expected and not a sign of model failure.
\item
  Panel 3 is the most cognitively interesting: when the opponent's
  running rate is extreme (say, 90\% right), the memory model predicts
  the agent will strongly track this. If instead the agent chose
  randomly or in the opposite direction, the model is very surprised ---
  \(\hat{k}\) spikes.
\end{itemize}

These are cognitively informative failures: they identify trials where
our model's assumption that agents track opponent history breaks down.
In our concrete case, we see that early trials are the most problematic.
Given the relatively high bias in the opponent and the few trials,
memory can quickly go to the extreme values and the model has a hard
time dealing with that.

But let's imagine we saw some agents being particularly influential ---
say, they had 10+ high-\(\hat{k}\) observations. This would suggest that
those agents are outliers from the population distribution --- perhaps
they have a very high memory sensitivity \(\beta_j\) that the
hierarchical prior is struggling to accommodate. In this case, we would
want to look at the posterior estimates for those agents and see if they
indeed have extreme parameter values. We could also consider refitting
the model with a more flexible hierarchical structure (e.g.,
heavier-tailed distributions) or even excluding those agents if we
believe they represent a qualitatively different strategy.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Extract estimated parameters and transform to probability space (Median and 90\% HDI)}
\NormalTok{agent\_estimates }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"indiv\_params"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{spread\_draws}\NormalTok{(indiv\_params[agent\_id, param\_idx]) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \CommentTok{\# Pivot to have alpha and beta in the same row per draw}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_wider}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{names\_from =}\NormalTok{ param\_idx, }\AttributeTok{values\_from =}\NormalTok{ indiv\_params, }\AttributeTok{names\_prefix =} \StringTok{"param\_"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{rename}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =}\NormalTok{ param\_1, }\AttributeTok{beta =}\NormalTok{ param\_2) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Probability transformations evaluated at experimental block extremes}
    \AttributeTok{p\_bias   =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha),                           }\CommentTok{\# Baseline probability (opp = 0.5)}
    \AttributeTok{p\_block1 =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{)),      }\CommentTok{\# Prob of Right during 80\% block}
    \AttributeTok{p\_block2 =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{))       }\CommentTok{\# Prob of Right during 20\% block}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{cols =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(alpha, beta, p\_bias, p\_block1, p\_block2), }
    \AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"metric"}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"value"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, metric) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{median\_hdi}\NormalTok{(value, }\AttributeTok{.width =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \CommentTok{\# Format as "Median [Lower, Upper]"}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{estimate\_label =} \FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\%.2f [\%.2f, \%.2f]"}\NormalTok{, value, .lower, .upper)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, metric, estimate\_label) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_wider}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{names\_from =}\NormalTok{ metric, }\AttributeTok{values\_from =}\NormalTok{ estimate\_label, }\AttributeTok{names\_prefix =} \StringTok{"est\_"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Extract true parameters and compute true probabilities}
\NormalTok{agent\_truths }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{distinct}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, true\_alpha, true\_beta) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{true\_p\_bias   =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(true\_alpha), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{true\_p\_block1 =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(true\_alpha }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ true\_beta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{)), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{true\_p\_block2 =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(true\_alpha }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ true\_beta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{)), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{true\_alpha    =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(true\_alpha, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{true\_beta     =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(true\_beta, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Join with the Pareto{-}k summary}
\NormalTok{k\_annotated }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(fit }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Memory {-}\textgreater{} Memory"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_high\_k =} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(k }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{), }
    \AttributeTok{max\_k    =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(k), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{.groups  =} \StringTok{"drop"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{desc}\NormalTok{(n\_high\_k)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{slice\_head}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =} \DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(agent\_truths, }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(agent\_estimates, }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \CommentTok{\# Select columns in a logical display order}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    agent\_id, n\_high\_k, max\_k, }
    \FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"true\_alpha"}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"est\_alpha"}\NormalTok{),}
    \FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"true\_beta"}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"est\_beta"}\NormalTok{),}
    \FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"true\_p\_block1"}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"est\_p\_block1"}\NormalTok{),}
    \FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"true\_p\_block2"}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"est\_p\_block2"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{caption =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}
    \StringTok{"Top 8 agents by number of high{-}k observations."}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"Probability space metrics (p\_block1, p\_block2) demonstrate agent determinism at environmental extremes."}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"Values near 1.00 or 0.00 with narrow HDIs indicate near{-}deterministic behavioral expectations,"}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"which might trigger high Pareto{-}k values upon any contradictory response."}
\NormalTok{  ))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Moment Matching for Problematic
Observations}\label{moment-matching-for-problematic-observations}

When some observations have \(\hat{k} \geq 0.7\), the standard PSIS
approximation is unreliable for those specific data points. The first
line of defense is \textbf{moment matching} (Paananen et al., 2021): a
method that refines the importance weights by applying a transformation
to the posterior draws so that the moments of the reweighted
distribution better match what the LOO posterior should look like.

Think of it as improving the reweighting without running a full MCMC
from scratch. Instead of asking ``what would the posterior look like if
we had never seen observation \(i\)?'', moment matching asks ``can we
adjust the existing draws so they better approximate that LOO
posterior?'' This is substantially cheaper than refitting and works well
for moderately influential observations. Only if moment matching fails
to bring \(\hat{k}\) below 1.0 should we resort to agent-level K-fold
cross-validation (which we implement next).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{apply\_moment\_match\_if\_needed }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj, fit\_obj, label, file\_path) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# 1. If the cached file exists, load it immediately}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&} \FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(file\_path)) \{}
    \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(label, }\StringTok{": Loading cached LOO..."}\NormalTok{)}
    \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(file\_path))}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  
\NormalTok{  n\_bad }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (n\_bad }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) \{}
    \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(label, }\StringTok{": All k \textless{} 0.7. PSIS{-}LOO is reliable."}\NormalTok{)}
    \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj, file\_path)}
    \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj)}
\NormalTok{  \} }
  
  \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(label, }\StringTok{": WARNING! "}\NormalTok{, n\_bad, }\StringTok{" observations with k \textgreater{}= 0.7."}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"          Running CmdStanR moment matching to refine importance weights..."}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 2. Run the actual moment matching algorithm}
  \CommentTok{\# (Requires cmdstanr \textgreater{}= 0.7.0 and models that can be unconstrained)}
\NormalTok{  loo\_mm }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{loo\_moment\_match}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    fit\_obj, }
    \AttributeTok{loo =}\NormalTok{ loo\_obj}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
  \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"          Max k reduced from "}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }
          \StringTok{" to "}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(loo\_mm}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{diagnostics}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pareto\_k), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))}
          
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(loo\_mm, file\_path)}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(loo\_mm)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Execute the caching function }
\NormalTok{loo\_b\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{apply\_moment\_match\_if\_needed}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_b, fit\_b\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_mm\_b\_on\_b.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_m\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{apply\_moment\_match\_if\_needed}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_b, fit\_m\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_mm\_m\_on\_b.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_b\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{apply\_moment\_match\_if\_needed}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_m, fit\_b\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_mm\_b\_on\_m.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_m\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{apply\_moment\_match\_if\_needed}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_m, fit\_m\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_loo\_mm\_m\_on\_m.rds"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{The Limits of PSIS: Agent-Level
LOO}\label{the-limits-of-psis-agent-level-loo}

If cognitive scientists are primarily interested in generalizing to new
participants, you might wonder: why can't we just calculate PSIS-LOO at
the participant level?

We actually can. To do this, we simply take our trial-level
log-likelihood matrix and sum the log-likelihoods of all 120 trials for
each agent \emph{before} passing the matrix to the \texttt{loo()}
function. This asks the PSIS algorithm to approximate what the posterior
would look like if an entire agent's dataset had been excluded.

Let's try this on our well-fitting
\texttt{Memory\ -\textgreater{}\ Memory} model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Extract the trial{-}level log{-}likelihood matrix (S draws x N trials)}
\NormalTok{ll\_trial\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Create an empty matrix for agent{-}level log{-}likelihoods (S draws x J agents)}
\NormalTok{J }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{n\_distinct}\NormalTok{(d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id)}
\NormalTok{S }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(ll\_trial\_m)}
\NormalTok{ll\_agent\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{nrow =}\NormalTok{ S, }\AttributeTok{ncol =}\NormalTok{ J)}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Sum the trial log{-}likelihoods for each agent}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (j }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{J) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Find which columns in the trial matrix belong to agent j}
\NormalTok{  trial\_cols }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{which}\NormalTok{(d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ j)}
  \CommentTok{\# Sum across those columns for every MCMC draw}
\NormalTok{  ll\_agent\_m[, j] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rowSums}\NormalTok{(ll\_trial\_m[, trial\_cols])}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Run PSIS{-}LOO at the agent level}
\NormalTok{loo\_agent\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{(ll\_agent\_m)}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(loo\_agent\_m)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

Computed from 4000 by 50 log-likelihood matrix.

         Estimate   SE
elpd_loo  -3711.2 48.6
p_loo        57.1  4.1
looic      7422.4 97.2
------
MCSE of elpd_loo is NA.
MCSE and ESS estimates assume independent draws (r_eff=1).

Pareto k diagnostic values:
                         Count Pct.    Min. ESS
(-Inf, 0.7]   (good)     12    24.0%   136     
   (0.7, 1]   (bad)      30    60.0%   <NA>    
   (1, Inf)   (very bad)  8    16.0%   <NA>    
See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details.
\end{verbatim}

I f you run this code, the console should scream at you with warnings.
You will see that a massive percentage of the Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) values
are \(> 0.7\), and many are \(> 1.0\).This is a spectacular mathematical
failure, and it teaches us a crucial lesson about the limits of
Importance Sampling. The PSIS shortcut only works when the
``Leave-One-Out'' posterior is relatively similar to the full-data
posterior. Removing a single trial from a dataset of 6,000 barely shifts
the parameters. But removing an entire agent (and their 120 unique
trials) radically changes the population-level estimates. The shortcut
completely breaks down because the full-data posterior simply does not
contain enough information about what the population would look like
without that agent. This failure provides the exact justification for
our next step. When we want to test generalization to new agents, we are
forced to abandon the PSIS shortcut entirely and perform exact
cross-validation.

\section{Agent-Level K-Fold: Generalizing to New
Participants}\label{agent-level-k-fold-generalizing-to-new-participants}

Cross-validation requires us to make a fundamental choice: what defines
a ``data point'' when we hold data out?

Up until now, we have performed \textbf{trial-level} cross-validation
using the PSIS-LOO shortcut. Trial-level CV asks: \emph{``How well does
the model predict a held-out trial for a participant whose other 119
trials we have already observed and learned from?''}

But cognitive scientists usually want to answer a different question:
\emph{``How well does the model generalize to completely new
participants?''}. To answer this, we must perform \textbf{agent-level}
cross-validation, holding out entire individuals from the training set.

It is crucial to understand that the \emph{level} of cross-validation
(trial vs.~agent) is entirely separate from the \emph{computational
method} we use (PSIS approximation vs.~exact refitting).

In theory, we could perform agent-level LOO-CV (Leave-One-Agent-Out). We
could even try to use our PSIS mathematical shortcut to do it without
refitting the model. However, leaving out an entire agent's worth of
data (120 trials) radically changes the shape of the posterior
distribution. When the LOO posterior is that different from the
full-data posterior, the PSIS importance weights become wildly unstable
(\(\hat{k} \gg 1.0\)), and the shortcut completely fails.

Therefore, when we want to test generalization to new agents, we are
forced to abandon the PSIS shortcut and perform \textbf{exact
cross-validation}, where we actually recompile and refit the Stan model
on the training splits. Because exact Leave-One-Agent-Out would require
fitting the model \(J=50\) times, we compromise to save computation time
and use \textbf{exact agent-level K-fold CV}.

In agent-level K-fold, we hold out \(1/K\) of the agents entirely from
the training set, fit the model to the remaining agents, and then ask:
given \emph{only} the population parameters estimated from the training
group, how well can we predict the held-out strangers' behavior?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Biased K{-}Fold Model}
\CommentTok{\# Draws ONE parameter per test agent from the population distribution.}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_train; }
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J\_train;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_train] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=J\_train\textgreater{} agent\_train;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_train] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h\_train;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_test; }
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J\_test;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_test] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=J\_test\textgreater{} agent\_test;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_test] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h\_test;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real mu\_theta; }
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} sigma\_theta; }
\StringTok{  vector[J\_train] z\_theta;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[J\_train] theta\_logit = mu\_theta + z\_theta * sigma\_theta;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_theta | 0, 1.5);}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma\_theta | 1);}
\StringTok{  target += std\_normal\_lpdf(z\_theta);}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h\_train | theta\_logit[agent\_train]);}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[J\_test] theta\_new;}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_test] log\_lik\_test;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (j in 1:J\_test) \{}
\StringTok{    theta\_new[j] = normal\_rng(mu\_theta, sigma\_theta);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (n in 1:N\_test) \{}
\StringTok{    log\_lik\_test[n] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h\_test[n] | theta\_new[agent\_test[n]]);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}"}\NormalTok{, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_biased\_kfold.stan"}\NormalTok{))}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Memory K{-}Fold Model}
\CommentTok{\# Samples correlated parameter pairs for each test agent from the population prior.}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_train; }
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J\_train;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_train] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=J\_train\textgreater{} agent\_train;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_train] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h\_train;}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0.01, upper=0.99\textgreater{}[N\_train] opp\_rate\_prev\_train;}

\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_test; }
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J\_test;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_test] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=J\_test\textgreater{} agent\_test;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_test] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h\_test;}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0.01, upper=0.99\textgreater{}[N\_test] opp\_rate\_prev\_test;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real mu\_alpha; }
\StringTok{  real mu\_beta; }
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}[2] sigma;}
\StringTok{  cholesky\_factor\_corr[2] L\_Omega; }
\StringTok{  matrix[2, J\_train] z;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[J\_train, 2] indiv\_params;}
\StringTok{  \{}
\StringTok{    matrix[2, J\_train] dev = diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma, L\_Omega) * z;}
\StringTok{    for (j in 1:J\_train) \{}
\StringTok{      indiv\_params[j, 1] = mu\_alpha + dev[1, j];}
\StringTok{      indiv\_params[j, 2] = mu\_beta + dev[2, j];}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_alpha | 0, 1); }
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(mu\_beta | 0, 1);}
\StringTok{  target += exponential\_lpdf(sigma | 1);}
\StringTok{  target += lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega | 2);}
\StringTok{  target += std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z));}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  vector[N\_train] logit\_p;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N\_train) \{}
\StringTok{    logit\_p[i] = indiv\_params[agent\_train[i], 1] + }
\StringTok{                 indiv\_params[agent\_train[i], 2] * logit(opp\_rate\_prev\_train[i]);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h\_train | logit\_p);}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[2, J\_test] new\_indiv;}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_test] log\_lik\_test;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (j in 1:J\_test) \{}
\StringTok{    vector[2] z\_new;}
\StringTok{    z\_new[1] = std\_normal\_rng();}
\StringTok{    z\_new[2] = std\_normal\_rng();}
\StringTok{    vector[2] param\_new = [mu\_alpha, mu\_beta]\textquotesingle{} + diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma, L\_Omega) * z\_new;}
\StringTok{    new\_indiv[1, j] = param\_new[1];}
\StringTok{    new\_indiv[2, j] = param\_new[2];}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (n in 1:N\_test) \{}
\StringTok{    real lp = new\_indiv[1, agent\_test[n]] + }
\StringTok{              new\_indiv[2, agent\_test[n]] * logit(opp\_rate\_prev\_test[n]);}
\StringTok{    log\_lik\_test[n] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h\_test[n] | lp);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}"}\NormalTok{, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_memory\_kfold.stan"}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{mod\_biased\_kfold }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_biased\_kfold.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{mod\_memory\_kfold }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch08\_memory\_kfold.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Both K{-}fold Stan models compiled.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Both K-fold Stan models compiled.
\end{verbatim}

\NormalTok{kfold\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"ch08\_kfold\_comparison.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{K }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{; }\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{21}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# We will run this on the Memory data to see the true model win out of sample}
\NormalTok{agent\_ids   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sort}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{unique}\NormalTok{(d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id))}
\NormalTok{fold\_assign }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{K, }\AttributeTok{length.out=}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(agent\_ids)))}
\NormalTok{agent\_folds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id=}\NormalTok{agent\_ids, }\AttributeTok{fold=}\NormalTok{fold\_assign)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_kfold }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(kfold\_path)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Running 5{-}fold CV for Biased and Memory models. This takes a moment..."}\NormalTok{)}
  
\NormalTok{  kfold\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{K, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(k) \{}
\NormalTok{    test\_ids  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_folds}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id[agent\_folds}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{fold }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ k]}
\NormalTok{    train\_ids }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ agent\_folds}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id[agent\_folds}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{fold }\SpecialCharTok{!=}\NormalTok{ k]}
    
\NormalTok{    d\_train }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(agent\_id }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%}\NormalTok{ train\_ids) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
               \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_train =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(agent\_id)))}
\NormalTok{    d\_test  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(agent\_id }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%}\NormalTok{ test\_ids) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
               \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_test =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(agent\_id)))}

\NormalTok{    sdata\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}

\NormalTok{    )}
    
\NormalTok{    sdata\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}

      \AttributeTok{agent\_train =}\NormalTok{ d\_train}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_train, }\AttributeTok{h\_train =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(d\_train}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice),}
      \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev\_train =}\NormalTok{ d\_train}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev,}

      \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev\_test =}\NormalTok{ d\_test}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev}
\NormalTok{    )}
    
    \CommentTok{\# Fit models (using 2 chains to save rendering time)}
\NormalTok{    fit\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_biased\_kfold}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ sdata\_b, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{200}\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{k, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }

                                     \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }
                                     \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
    
\NormalTok{    ll\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik\_test"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    ll\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik\_test"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    idx  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{which}\NormalTok{(d\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%}\NormalTok{ test\_ids)}
    
    \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ll\_b =}\NormalTok{ ll\_b, }\AttributeTok{ll\_m =}\NormalTok{ ll\_m, }\AttributeTok{indices =}\NormalTok{ idx, }\AttributeTok{k =}\NormalTok{ k)}
\NormalTok{  \})}
  
\NormalTok{  S\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(kfold\_list[[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]]}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ll\_b)}
\NormalTok{  S\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(kfold\_list[[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]]}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ll\_m)}
\NormalTok{  ll\_kfold\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA\_real\_}\NormalTok{, S\_b, }\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d\_memory))}
\NormalTok{  ll\_kfold\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA\_real\_}\NormalTok{, S\_m, }\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d\_memory))}

\NormalTok{    ll\_kfold\_b[, res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{indices] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ll\_b}
\NormalTok{    ll\_kfold\_m[, res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{indices] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ll\_m}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{biased =}\NormalTok{ ll\_kfold\_b, }\AttributeTok{memory =}\NormalTok{ ll\_kfold\_m), kfold\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  kfold\_res  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(kfold\_path)}
\NormalTok{  ll\_kfold\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ kfold\_res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{biased}
\NormalTok{  ll\_kfold\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ kfold\_res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{memory}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{    loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{]))}

\NormalTok{    loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{]))}

\NormalTok{    elpd\_kf\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], elpd\_kf\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{]))}

\NormalTok{\}}
