\begin{verbatim}
=== Trial-level LOO vs. Agent-level K-fold (Memory Data) ===

Biased Model - PSIS-LOO:  -4038.3 (SE=15.4)
Biased Model - K-fold:    -4075.0 (SE=12.8)

Memory Model - PSIS-LOO:  -3700.3 (SE=27.3)
Memory Model - K-fold:    -3773.2 (SE=25.2)

=== K-Fold Model Comparison ===
       elpd_diff se_diff
model1    0.0       0.0 
model2 -301.7      22.2 
\end{verbatim}

The Agent-Level K-fold ELPD will almost always be lower (worse) than the
trial-level LOO ELPD for both models. This is mathematically expected
and cognitively informative. Trial-level LOO asks: ``How well can I
predict trial 120 for an agent whose personal bias and memory
sensitivity I have already estimated from 119 trials?'' Agent-level
K-fold asks: ``How well can I predict behavior for a complete stranger,
relying purely on population averages?''

The gap between the two ELPDs quantifies exactly how much
individual-level variation matters. If the gap is massive, human
individuality dominates the cognitive process.

\section{LOO-PIT: Calibration Checks for Predictive
Distributions}\label{loo-pit-calibration-checks-for-predictive-distributions}

\subsection{What LOO-PIT Measures}\label{what-loo-pit-measures}

The Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) values tell us whether the \emph{importance
weights} are reliable: whether the PSIS approximation is numerically
stable. LOO-PIT tells us something different and complementary: whether
the model's \emph{predictive distributions} are well-calibrated: whether
the model's uncertainty matches reality.

A model can have perfectly reliable \(\hat{k}\) values and still be
systematically miscalibrated. For example, a biased model fitted to
memory data might produce low \(\hat{k}\) for every observation (the
model is not dramatically wrong on any single trial) while still being
consistently wrong in a systematic, diffuse way across all trials. The
Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) would not flag this; LOO-PIT will.

The Probability Integral Transform (PIT) builds on a simple but powerful
idea. Consider the LOO predictive distribution for observation \(y_i\):
the distribution the model assigns to \(y_i\) before seeing it, having
been fitted on all other observations. We ask: where does the actual
observed \(y_i\) fall within this distribution? Specifically, we compute
\(u_i\), the cumulative probability up to the observed value:

\[u_i = \int_{-\infty}^{y_i} p(\tilde{y} \mid y_{-i}) \, d\tilde{y}\]

This is the \emph{quantile} of the observation within its own LOO
predictive distribution. If the model assigned most of its predictive
probability below \(y_i\), then \(u_i\) is close to 1 --- the model was
surprised, expecting a lower value but getting a higher one. If the
model assigned most of its probability above \(y_i\), then \(u_i\) is
close to 0.

For a perfectly calibrated model, the model's predictions should be
neither systematically too high nor too low. Half the observations
should fall in the lower half of their predicted distributions, half in
the upper half. More precisely, the \(u_i\) values should be
\emph{uniformly distributed} on \([0, 1]\). Departures from uniformity
reveal specific failure modes:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{U-shaped distribution} (too many values near 0 and 1): The
  model is \emph{overconfident} --- its predictive distributions are too
  narrow. Observations consistently fall in the tails, surprising the
  model.
\item
  \textbf{Bell-shaped distribution} (too many values near 0.5): The
  model is \emph{underconfident} --- its predictive distributions are
  too wide, spreading probability mass over values that never occur.
\item
  \textbf{Skewed distribution} (more mass on one side): The model is
  \emph{biased}, systematically predicting too high or too low.
\end{itemize}

If this sounds familiar, you have been paying attention! It's the same
principle as in simulation-based-calibration, but applied to
observations instead of parameters. The difference is that in SBC we ask
``where does the true parameter value fall within the posterior
distribution?'' In LOO-PIT we ask ``where does the observed data point
fall within the predictive distribution?'' Both are checks of
calibration --- whether the model's uncertainty matches reality --- but
they apply to different distributions and reveal different failure
modes.

While SBC catches computational bugs and prior-likelihood conflicts,
LOO-PIT catches structural misspecification in the predictions
themselves.

\subsection{The Discrete Data Problem: Randomization for Bernoulli
Outcomes}\label{the-discrete-data-problem-randomization-for-bernoulli-outcomes}

Our outcome is a binary choice: \(h_{it} \in \{0, 1\}\). The PIT as
defined above requires a continuous predictive distribution, but
Bernoulli predictions are discrete. The CDF of a Bernoulli distribution
can only jump between two values: the predicted probability of \(h = 0\)
is \(1 - \hat{p}_i\), and the predicted probability of \(h = 1\) is
\(1.0\). So the raw PIT value is either \(1 - \hat{p}_i\) (when
\(h_i = 0\)) or \(1.0\) (when \(h_i = 1\)). These two families of values
cannot be uniformly distributed on \([0, 1]\), even for a perfectly
correct model.

The standard solution (Czado et al., 2009) is \textbf{randomization}.
Instead of using the CDF value directly, we spread each observation's
PIT value uniformly across the interval the CDF jumps over at that
observation. We draw \(v_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)\) and compute:

\[u_i = F(y_i - 1 \mid y_{-i}) + v_i \cdot \left[F(y_i \mid y_{-i}) - F(y_i - 1 \mid y_{-i})\right]\]

For \(h_i = 1\): \(u_i = (1 - \hat{p}_i) + v_i \cdot \hat{p}_i\), which
spreads uniformly across \([1-\hat{p}_i, 1]\). For \(h_i = 0\):
\(u_i = v_i \cdot (1-\hat{p}_i)\), which spreads uniformly across
\([0, 1-\hat{p}_i]\).

Under a calibrated model, these randomized \(u_i\) values end up
marginally uniform on \([0,1]\). The \texttt{bayesplot} package applies
this randomization automatically when it detects binary data. But
understanding the reasoning matters: without randomization, a PIT plot
for Bernoulli data would look non-uniform even for a perfectly correct
model, which would be deeply misleading.

\subsection{A Critical Warning: The Standard Visual May Miss
Miscalibration}\label{a-critical-warning-the-standard-visual-may-miss-miscalibration}

Here is a subtlety that matters specifically for hierarchical cognitive
models, established recently by Tesso \& Vehtari (2026).

The standard LOO-PIT visualization - the ECDF (empirical cumulative
distribution function) envelope from Sailynoja et al.~(2022) -
constructs its confidence band assuming the \(u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_N\)
values are \emph{independent}. In a simple model with few parameters,
this is approximately true. But in a hierarchical model fitted to
hundreds or thousands of observations, the LOO-PIT values are
\textbf{correlated with one another}.

Why? Each LOO predictive distribution \(p(y_i \mid y_{-i})\) is
determined by the model fitted to the other \(N-1\) observations. Those
\(N-1\) datasets differ from each other by only one data point --- they
are nearly identical. All the LOO predictive distributions therefore
share nearly the same information, and the resulting \(u_i\) values
inherit this shared structure.

Tesso \& Vehtari (2026) prove that this correlation scales with model
complexity: the more effective parameters a model has relative to the
dataset size, the more correlated the LOO-PIT values are, and the
narrower the true null distribution of the ECDF becomes. When we use the
independence-based confidence envelope, that envelope is \emph{too wide}
- it accommodates more deviation than a properly-calibrated null
distribution would allow. The practical consequence is that the standard
visual has \textbf{reduced statistical power}: it fails to flag
miscalibration that is genuinely present. For simple models this is not
a serious problem. For our hierarchical models it can be.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Step 1: The \(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}} / N\)
Complexity
Ratio}{Step 1: The \textbackslash hat\{p\}\_\{\textbackslash text\{loo\}\} / N Complexity Ratio}}\label{step-1-the-hatp_textloo-n-complexity-ratio}

Before interpreting any LOO-PIT result, we compute one diagnostic
number: the ratio of the effective number of parameters
(\(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}}\)) to the total number of observations (\(N\)).
Tesso \& Vehtari (2026) show that:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  When \(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}} / N\) is close to zero, the LOO-PIT values
  are nearly independent and the standard visual is reliable.
\item
  When the ratio is non-negligible --- say, above 0.05 --- the
  independence assumption inflates the envelope width and a formal test
  is needed to supplement the visual.
\end{itemize}

Conveniently, \(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}}\) is already reported in our
\texttt{loo()} output. For a hierarchical model with \(J = 50\) agents,
\(\hat{p}_{\text{loo}}\) is roughly proportional to \(J\) (each agent
contributes approximately one effective parameter for their individual
parameters), giving a ratio around \(50 / 6000 \approx 0.008\) in our
case. This is small --- but not zero, and the exact value matters when
the dataset is smaller or the model is more complex.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{complexity\_table }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ pareto\_k\_table }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(Combination, N, p\_hat\_loo, ratio) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{visual\_ok =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(ratio }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"Standard visual acceptable"}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"Formal test recommended (POT{-}C)"}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(complexity\_table, }\AttributeTok{caption =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{"Model complexity ratios. For hierarchical models with J=50 agents,"}\NormalTok{,}
  \StringTok{"p\_hat\_loo grows with J: each agent contributes roughly one effective parameter."}\NormalTok{,}
  \StringTok{"The ratio p\_hat\_loo/N determines whether the standard visual is sufficient."}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrrl@{}}

effective parameter. The ratio p\_hat\_loo/N determines whether the
standard visual is sufficient.}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
Combination & N & p\_hat\_loo & ratio & visual\_ok \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endfirsthead
\toprule\noalign{}
Combination & N & p\_hat\_loo & ratio & visual\_ok \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Biased-\textgreater Biased & 6000 & 41.1 & 0.007 & Standard visual
acceptable \\
Memory-\textgreater Biased & 6000 & 47.0 & 0.008 & Standard visual
acceptable \\
Biased-\textgreater Memory & 6000 & 35.6 & 0.006 & Standard visual
acceptable \\
Memory-\textgreater Memory & 6000 & 61.7 & 0.010 & Standard visual
acceptable \\
\end{longtable}

Examine the \texttt{visual\_ok} column. For most of our fits the ratio
is small enough that the standard visual is acceptable --- but the
formal test below provides additional confidence, and is essential
whenever the ratio is larger.

\subsection{Step 2: The Randomized ECDF Visual
Check}\label{step-2-the-randomized-ecdf-visual-check}

We always produce the visual check, regardless of whether the ratio is
small. The visual is indispensable for understanding the \emph{shape} of
any miscalibration: is the model overconfident? Biased in one direction?

Because our Matching Pennies data is binary (\(y \in \{0, 1\}\)), the
true predictive CDF is a discrete step function. Applying standard
continuous Density Overlays to this generates severe mathematical
artifacts. To evaluate calibration accurately, we rely on the
\textbf{Randomized Probability Integral Transform}. We mathematically
restore continuity by injecting uniform noise strictly between the
discrete CDF steps. We then plot the Empirical Cumulative Distribution
Function (ECDF) of these randomized values. If the model is
well-calibrated, the ECDF will closely track the diagonal and remain
completely inside the shaded confidence envelope.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{color\_scheme\_set}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"blue"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 1. Define a rigorous function to compute Randomized PIT for Bernoulli data}
\NormalTok{compute\_randomized\_pit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(y, loo\_obj, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{101}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
\NormalTok{  p\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pointwise[, }\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{])}
  \CommentTok{\# Inject uniform noise between the discrete CDF steps}
\NormalTok{  pit\_vals }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{runif}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(y), }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_obs, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{runif}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(y), }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, p\_obs))}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(pit\_vals)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Extract outcomes and compute randomized PIT values }
\NormalTok{pit\_b\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_randomized\_pit}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(sd\_biased\_on\_biased}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h), loo\_b\_on\_b)}
\NormalTok{pit\_b\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_randomized\_pit}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(sd\_biased\_on\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h), loo\_b\_on\_m)}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Helper function to generate the ECDF plots}
\NormalTok{plot\_pit\_ecdf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(y, pit\_vals, plot\_title) \{}
  \FunctionTok{ppc\_pit\_ecdf}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y, }\AttributeTok{pit =}\NormalTok{ pit\_vals, }\AttributeTok{pit\_bounds =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =}\NormalTok{ plot\_title) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Generate plots for the true model and the miscalibrated model}
\NormalTok{p\_pit1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plot\_pit\_ecdf}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(sd\_biased\_on\_biased}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h), pit\_b\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Biased {-}\textgreater{} Biased (Calibrated)"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p\_pit2 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plot\_pit\_ecdf}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(sd\_biased\_on\_memory}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h), pit\_b\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Biased {-}\textgreater{} Memory (Miscalibrated)"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_pit1 }\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{ p\_pit2 }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{plot\_annotation}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Randomized LOO{-}PIT ECDF Calibration Checks"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Lines inside the shaded envelope indicate uniformity (well{-}calibrated model)."}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-loo-pit-visual-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The flat, nearly-uniform overlay for the well-specified fits
(Biased-\textgreater Biased and Memory-\textgreater Memory) is a
reassuring sign. The misspecified fits could have shown characteristic
departures, but in our case it doesn't. So we can move forward.

\subsection{Step 3: The POT-C Formal Test -- Valid Under
Dependence}\label{step-3-the-pot-c-formal-test-valid-under-dependence}

The visual check is useful for identifying the \emph{shape} of
miscalibration, but it can miss subtle violations when LOO-PIT values
are correlated. The POT-C (Pointwise Order Test Combination) from Tesso
\& Vehtari (2026) provides a formal uniformity test that remains
statistically valid regardless of the dependence structure among the PIT
values.

\textbf{The intuition behind POT-C in three steps:}

\emph{Step 1 - Individual tests per order statistic.} Imagine sorting
all \(N\) PIT values from smallest to largest:
\(u_{(1)} \leq u_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq u_{(N)}\). By the theory of
order statistics, under uniformity
\(u_{(i)} \sim \text{Beta}(i, N+1-i)\). This gives us a valid marginal
test for each order statistic: we compute the two-sided p-value for
whether the observed \(u_{(i)}\) is consistent with
\(\text{Beta}(i, N+1-i)\). We now have \(N\) individual p-values, one
for each position in the sorted sample, each marginally valid but
potentially correlated. p-values, you read correctly. We are not doing
null hypothesis testing, tho, we are trying to see whether we should
reject our actual model.

\emph{Step 2 - Combining correlated p-values via the Cauchy method.}
Standard methods for combining p-values, like Fisher's method, assume
independence and would give misleading results here. The Cauchy
combination method (Liu \& Xie, 2020) handles this by transforming each
p-value as \(t^{(i)} = \tan\left[(0.5 - p^{(i)}) \pi\right]\). A p-value
of exactly 0.5 (no evidence against uniformity) contributes zero. A very
small p-value contributes a large positive value. The test statistic is
the average: \[T = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} t^{(i)}\] The key
mathematical result (Liu \& Xie, 2020) is that the tail probabilities of
\(T\) approximate those of a standard Cauchy distribution even under
arbitrary correlation structures. The heavy tails of the Cauchy
distribution ``absorb'' the dependence without requiring us to model it
explicitly.

\emph{Step 3 - Shapley values for localization.} Beyond asking ``is the
model miscalibrated?'', we want to know ``where is it miscalibrated?''.
The Shapley value \(\phi_i\) for each observation measures how much that
observation's order statistic contributed to the overall test statistic
\(T\): how much it pushed the result toward rejection. By coloring the
ECDF difference plot with these values, we can visually highlight which
regions of the predictive distribution are problematic. This
localization connects the statistical diagnostic back to the cognitive
model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-} POT{-}C implementation (Tesso \& Vehtari, 2026) {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}

\NormalTok{pot\_c\_test }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(u\_vec, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  u   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sort}\NormalTok{(u\_vec)}
\NormalTok{  n   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(u)}

  \CommentTok{\# Step 1: Beta{-}based p{-}value for each order statistic}
\NormalTok{  p\_vals }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dbl}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) \{}
\NormalTok{    cdf\_val }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pbeta}\NormalTok{(u[i], }\AttributeTok{shape1 =}\NormalTok{ i, }\AttributeTok{shape2 =}\NormalTok{ n }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ i)}
    \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(cdf\_val, }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ cdf\_val)}
\NormalTok{  \})}

  \CommentTok{\# Step 2: Cauchy transformation and global p{-}value}
\NormalTok{  t\_vals }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tan}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_vals) }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pi)}
\NormalTok{  T\_stat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(t\_vals)}
\NormalTok{  p\_global }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{pcauchy}\NormalTok{(T\_stat)}

  \CommentTok{\# Step 3: Shapley values {-}{-} each observation\textquotesingle{}s marginal contribution to T}
  \CommentTok{\# phi\_i = (1/n)*t\_i + (H\_n{-}1)/n * (t\_i {-} mean of other t\_j)}
\NormalTok{  H\_n      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n))}
\NormalTok{  t\_sum    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(t\_vals)}
\NormalTok{  phi\_vals }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dbl}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) \{}
\NormalTok{    t\_i }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ t\_vals[i]}
\NormalTok{    (}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{n) }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ t\_i }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ((H\_n }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{n) }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (t\_i }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ (t\_sum }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ t\_i) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (n }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{  \})}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{p\_global=}\NormalTok{p\_global, }\AttributeTok{T\_stat=}\NormalTok{T\_stat, }\AttributeTok{p\_vals=}\NormalTok{p\_vals, }\AttributeTok{t\_vals=}\NormalTok{t\_vals,}
       \AttributeTok{phi=}\NormalTok{phi\_vals, }\AttributeTok{u\_sorted=}\NormalTok{u, }\AttributeTok{n=}\NormalTok{n)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-} Randomized LOO{-}PIT for Bernoulli data via PSIS weights {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# We approximate the LOO predictive P(h\_i=1|y\_\{{-}i\}) as a weighted mean}
\CommentTok{\# of the posterior predictive draws, using the PSIS log{-}weights.}
\CommentTok{\# The resulting u\_i values are then randomized per Czado et al. (2009).}

\NormalTok{compute\_loo\_pit\_bernoulli }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(y, yrep, psis\_lw, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{99}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
\NormalTok{  w\_mat   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(psis\_lw)                           }\CommentTok{\# S x N probability weights}
\NormalTok{  p\_loo\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{colSums}\NormalTok{(w\_mat }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ yrep) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{colSums}\NormalTok{(w\_mat) }\CommentTok{\# weighted mean per observation}
\NormalTok{  p\_loo\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{1e{-}6}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{1e{-}6}\NormalTok{, p\_loo\_1))}

  \CommentTok{\# Randomized PIT:}
  \CommentTok{\# h=1: u\_i \textasciitilde{} Uniform(1{-}p\_i, 1),   i.e. (1{-}p) + v*p}
  \CommentTok{\# h=0: u\_i \textasciitilde{} Uniform(0, 1{-}p\_i),   i.e. v*(1{-}p)}
\NormalTok{  V }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{runif}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(y))}
  \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{         (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_loo\_1) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ V }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_loo\_1,}
\NormalTok{         V }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_loo\_1))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{run\_pit\_analysis }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(fit\_obj, data\_list, loo\_obj, label) \{}
\NormalTok{  y      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(data\_list}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h)}
\NormalTok{  yrep   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"h\_post\_rep"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  lw     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{weights}\NormalTok{(loo\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{psis\_object)}
\NormalTok{  u\_vec  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_loo\_pit\_bernoulli}\NormalTok{(y, yrep, lw)}
\NormalTok{  result }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pot\_c\_test}\NormalTok{(u\_vec)}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ label, }\AttributeTok{u =}\NormalTok{ u\_vec, }\AttributeTok{result =}\NormalTok{ result)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{pit\_b\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{run\_pit\_analysis}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_b, sd\_biased\_on\_biased, loo\_b\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{pit\_m\_on\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{run\_pit\_analysis}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_b, sd\_memory\_on\_biased, loo\_m\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{pit\_b\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{run\_pit\_analysis}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_m, sd\_biased\_on\_memory, loo\_b\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{pit\_m\_on\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{run\_pit\_analysis}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_m, sd\_memory\_on\_memory, loo\_m\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(pit\_b\_on\_b, pit\_m\_on\_b, pit\_b\_on\_m, pit\_m\_on\_m), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(x) \{}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Fit =}\NormalTok{ x}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{label, }\AttributeTok{p\_global =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(x}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global,}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{T\_stat =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(x}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T\_stat,}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{Verdict =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(x}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{,}
                        \StringTok{"REJECT {-}{-} miscalibrated"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Fail to reject {-}{-} acceptable"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{\}) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{caption =} \FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}
    \StringTok{"POT{-}C formal uniformity test for LOO{-}PIT values."}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"Valid even when LOO{-}PIT values are correlated."}\NormalTok{,}
    \StringTok{"p \textless{} 0.05 = significant departure from calibration."}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrl@{}}
\caption{POT-C formal uniformity test for LOO-PIT values. Valid even
when LOO-PIT values are correlated. p \textless{} 0.05 = significant
departure from calibration.}\tabularnewline
\toprule\noalign{}
Fit & p\_global & T\_stat & Verdict \\

\endfirsthead

Fit & p\_global & T\_stat & Verdict \\

miscalibrated \\

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-} Shapley{-}colored ECDF difference plots {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# The ECDF difference plot shows F\_hat(u) {-} u (deviation from perfect uniform).}
\CommentTok{\# Perfect calibration = horizontal line at y=0.}
\CommentTok{\# We color each point by its Shapley value:}
\CommentTok{\#   RED  = positive phi (pushes the test statistic toward rejection)}
\CommentTok{\#   BLUE = neutral or negative phi (consistent with uniformity)}

\NormalTok{make\_shapley\_ecdf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(pit\_res) \{}
\NormalTok{  r   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ pit\_res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}
\NormalTok{  n   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ r}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n}
  
  \CommentTok{\# Corrected namespace assignment}
\NormalTok{  df  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{u\_sorted    =}\NormalTok{ r}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{u\_sorted, }
    \AttributeTok{phi         =}\NormalTok{ r}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{phi,}
    \AttributeTok{ecdf\_y      =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ n,}
    \AttributeTok{ecdf\_diff   =}\NormalTok{ ecdf\_y }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ u\_sorted,}
    \AttributeTok{influential =}\NormalTok{ phi }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
  
\NormalTok{  p\_label }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(r}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \FloatTok{0.001}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"p \textless{} 0.001"}\NormalTok{,}
                    \FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"p = \%.3f"}\NormalTok{, r}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global))}
  
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ u\_sorted, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ ecdf\_diff)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{geom\_step}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray30"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ influential), }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"TRUE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{),}
      \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"Neutral"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"TRUE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"Drives rejection"}\NormalTok{),}
      \AttributeTok{name   =} \StringTok{"Shapley influence"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x=}\FloatTok{0.02}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y=}\ConstantTok{Inf}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{hjust=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{vjust=}\FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{label=}\NormalTok{p\_label, }\AttributeTok{fontface=}\StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size=}\FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =}\NormalTok{ pit\_res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{label,}
         \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"ECDF(u) {-} u: zero everywhere = perfect calibration"}\NormalTok{,}
         \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"LOO{-}PIT value u"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"ECDF(u) {-} u"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
    \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Plot only the correctly specified and the explicitly miscalibrated model}
\FunctionTok{make\_shapley\_ecdf}\NormalTok{(pit\_b\_on\_b) }\SpecialCharTok{|} \FunctionTok{make\_shapley\_ecdf}\NormalTok{(pit\_b\_on\_m) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{plot\_annotation}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Shapley{-}Colored ECDF Difference Plots (POT{-}C)"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-pot-c-implementation-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The ECDF difference plot is a centered version of the usual ECDF:
instead of plotting \(\hat{F}(u)\) against \(u\), we plot
\(\hat{F}(u) - u\). Perfect calibration corresponds to a horizontal line
at \(y = 0\). Deviations reveal the shape of the problem:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Red in the left tail (near \(u = 0\)): The model was confidently wrong
  when the agent chose ``left'' (\(y=0\)). The model predicted a
  near-certain probability of ``right'', forcing the randomized PIT
  value into the extreme lower bound.
\item
  Red in the right tail (near \(u = 1\)): The model was confidently
  wrong when the agent chose ``right'' (\(y=1\)). The model predicted a
  near-certain probability of ``left'', forcing the randomized PIT value
  into the extreme upper bound.
\item
  A global slope or S-curve: The model exhibits a systematic bias in
  base rates. It consistently over- or under-predicts the overall global
  frequency of one of the choices, rather than just failing on extreme
  boundary cases.
\end{itemize}

The Shapley coloring tells us exactly which observations are
responsible. Red points are the ``smoking guns'' --- the specific order
statistics mathematically pushing the POT-C test toward rejection. For
the Biased \(\rightarrow\) Memory combination, expect the red points to
concentrate heavily in the extreme tails. These correspond primarily to
the trials immediately following the block reversal, where the biased
model is rigidly and repeatedly surprised by behavioral changes it
structurally lacks the flexibility to predict.

\subsection{Connecting LOO-PIT to the Cognitive
Theory}\label{connecting-loo-pit-to-the-cognitive-theory}

Consider what happens when we fit the biased model to memory data. The
biased model says: ``Each agent has a fixed probability \(\psi_j\) of
choosing right, regardless of what the opponent has been doing.'' When
memory agents play against the reversal design, their trial-level choice
probabilities are anything but fixed. In the first block (trials 1--60),
as the running opponent rate climbs toward 0.8, memory agents
increasingly track it and choose right more often. At the reversal
(trial 61), the opponent flips to 20\% right: the memory signal inverts,
and memory agents start choosing left. The biased model cannot represent
either the rise or the reversal --- it is committed to a single
probability \(\psi_j\) that tries to average across both phases.

The predictive failure is asymmetric. In the first block the biased
model may do tolerably: its fixed \(\psi_j\) roughly matches the
early-session average. But immediately after the reversal, memory agents
start choosing left while the biased model is still confidently
predicting right. On those trials the biased model assigns high
probability to ``right'' and the agent chooses ``left'' --- exactly the
kind of systematic surprise that drives LOO-PIT values into the tails
and produces a large Pareto-\(\hat{k}\). This is the overconfidence
signature --- not overconfidence in the sense of arrogance, but in the
sense of structural rigidity: the model makes narrow, committed
predictions that the reversal repeatedly proves wrong.

\subsection{Agent-Level Calibration: A Transparent
Complement}\label{agent-level-calibration-a-transparent-complement}

LOO-PIT operates at the trial level, checking whether the model's
uncertainty about individual choices is well-calibrated. A complementary
check operates at the agent level: does the model get the average
behavior of each agent right?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{agent\_calibration }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(fit\_obj, data\_df, label) \{}
\NormalTok{  obs\_rates }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{obs\_rate =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(choice), }\AttributeTok{.groups=}\StringTok{"drop"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  yrep      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"h\_post\_rep"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format=}\StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  agent\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ data\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id}
\NormalTok{  pred\_mean }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{map\_dbl}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sort}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{unique}\NormalTok{(agent\_vec)), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(j)}
    \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(yrep[, }\FunctionTok{which}\NormalTok{(agent\_vec }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ j)]))}
\NormalTok{  obs\_rates }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{pred\_rate=}\NormalTok{pred\_mean, }\AttributeTok{label=}\NormalTok{label)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{agent\_calibration}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_b, d\_biased, }\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{agent\_calibration}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_b, d\_biased, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{agent\_calibration}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_m, d\_memory, }\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{agent\_calibration}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_m, d\_memory, }\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x=}\NormalTok{obs\_rate, }\AttributeTok{y=}\NormalTok{pred\_rate)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype=}\StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color=}\StringTok{"gray50"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color=}\StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha=}\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{label, }\AttributeTok{ncol=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{labels=}\NormalTok{scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_y\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{labels=}\NormalTok{scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Agent{-}Level Calibration: Observed vs. Predicted Choice Rates"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"LOO{-}PIT checks trial{-}level calibration; this checks agent{-}level means. Both matter."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x=}\StringTok{"Observed proportion \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{}"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y=}\StringTok{"Posterior mean predicted proportion \textquotesingle{}right\textquotesingle{}"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-agent-calibration-1.pdf}
\end{center}

This plot answers a different question from LOO-PIT: not ``is the
model's predictive \emph{uncertainty} correct?'' but ``is the model
getting the agent-level \emph{means} right?'' A model can pass this
check and still fail LOO-PIT --- for example, the biased model might
accurately predict each agent's average choice rate (because it directly
estimates their \(\theta_j\)), while still being badly miscalibrated at
the trial level (because it cannot predict which specific trials will
deviate from that average). Both checks are necessary for a complete
picture.

\section{Formal Model Comparison}\label{formal-model-comparison}

\begin{quote}
\emph{Why ELPD/LOO rather than Bayes Factors?} Bayes Factors compare the
marginal likelihood of the data under each model --- an elegant idea,
but one that requires integrating over the \emph{entire prior}. For
hierarchical models, this integral is computationally intractable and
the result is exquisitely sensitive to the exact width of every prior.
PSIS-LOO avoids this by focusing entirely on predictive accuracy for
held-out observations --- a criterion that is robust to prior choices
and provides the Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) as a built-in reliability
diagnostic. The trade-off is philosophical: LOO measures pragmatic
predictive utility, not the probability that a model is true. For most
applied cognitive modeling questions, pragmatic predictive utility is
what we actually care about.
\end{quote}

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Reading \texttt{loo\_compare()}
Correctly}{Reading loo\_compare() Correctly}}\label{reading-loo_compare-correctly}

Now we come to the main event: using PSIS-LOO to compare the two models
on each dataset. The \texttt{loo\_compare()} function ranks models by
their ELPD estimates and reports the differences. Understanding exactly
what those differences mean is important before trusting any conclusion.

The output has two key columns:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \texttt{elpd\_diff}: The difference in ELPD between this model and the
  best-ranked model (which always appears in row 1, with
  \texttt{elpd\_diff\ =\ 0} by definition). For lower-ranked models,
  \texttt{elpd\_diff} is negative --- they predict worse. The magnitude
  matters: a difference of \(-5\) nats means the model is roughly
  \(e^5 \approx 148\) times worse per observation on average. A
  difference of \(-0.5\) is much less decisive.
\item
  \texttt{se\_diff}: The standard error of that difference. This is
  estimated directly from the \emph{pointwise} ELPD contributions,
  treating each observation as an independent draw from the distribution
  of possible pointwise differences. It accounts for the fact that
  observations are not equally predictable.
\end{itemize}

The ratio \(|\text{elpd\_diff}|/\text{se\_diff}\) behaves like a
Z-score: it tells us how many standard errors the difference is from
zero. As a rough guide:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Below 2: inconclusive --- the models are not decisively separable at
  this data size.
\item
  Between 2 and 4: suggestive --- there is a preference but it may not
  be reliable.
\item
  Above 4: a practically meaningful difference that is unlikely to be
  sampling noise.
\end{itemize}

What if the result is inconclusive (\(< 2\))? An inconclusive result
means the current data cannot distinguish between your theories. In this
case, do not simply pick the model with the slightly higher ELPD.
Instead:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Redesign the task: Create a more discriminative opponent (like our
  reversal design) to force the models apart.
\item
  Collect more data: Use your Model Recovery power analysis to see how
  many more participants and/or trials you need.
\item
  Check PPCs: One model might have a better ELPD but fail to capture a
  specific qualitative feature of the behavior.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"=== BIASED DATA (truth = Biased) ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
=== BIASED DATA (truth = Biased) ===
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{compare\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{loo\_compare}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_b, loo\_m\_on\_b)}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(compare\_b)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
       elpd_diff se_diff
model1  0.0       0.0   
model2 -1.3       1.6   
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{z\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(compare\_b[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"elpd\_diff"}\NormalTok{]}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{compare\_b[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"se\_diff"}\NormalTok{])}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{|elpd\_diff|/SE = \%.1f  {-}\textgreater{}  \%s}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{, z\_b,}
  \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(z\_b}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Clear preference."}\NormalTok{,}\FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(z\_b}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Weak preference."}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Inconclusive."}\NormalTok{))))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

|elpd_diff|/SE = 0.8  ->  Inconclusive.
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"=== MEMORY DATA (truth = Memory) ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
=== MEMORY DATA (truth = Memory) ===
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{compare\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{loo\_compare}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_m, loo\_b\_on\_m)}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(compare\_m)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
       elpd_diff se_diff
model1    0.0       0.0 
model2 -337.9      23.4 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{z\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(compare\_m[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"elpd\_diff"}\NormalTok{]}\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{compare\_m[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"se\_diff"}\NormalTok{])}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{|elpd\_diff|/SE = \%.1f  {-}\textgreater{}  \%s}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{, z\_m,}
  \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(z\_m}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Clear preference."}\NormalTok{,}\FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(z\_m}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Weak preference."}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Inconclusive."}\NormalTok{))))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

|elpd_diff|/SE = 14.4  ->  Clear preference.
\end{verbatim}

Reading the output with the Z-score rule
(\(|\text{elpd\_diff}|/\text{se\_diff}\)):

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Biased data:} The memory model is slightly worse, but the
  ratio is likely below 2 --- \emph{inconclusive}. This is the correct
  scientific answer: when data come from a purely biased agent, a memory
  model with \(\beta \approx 0\) produces indistinguishable predictions.
  The PSIS-LOO score is not broken; it is honestly reporting that the
  simpler model is sufficient here.
\item
  \textbf{Memory data:} The biased model is decisively worse, with a
  ratio well above 4 --- \emph{clear preference}. The reversal at trial
  61 produces behavioral changes that the biased model cannot track, and
  PSIS-LOO detects this.
\end{itemize}

This is \textbf{successful model recovery}: the method correctly
identifies the true generating model in the column where detection is
possible, and honestly reports uncertainty in the column where the
models overlap. Both outcomes are scientifically valid.

\subsection{Pointwise ELPD Differences: Where Does the Advantage Come
From?}\label{pointwise-elpd-differences-where-does-the-advantage-come-from}

An aggregate ELPD difference tells us \emph{that} one model predicts
better overall. But often the most scientifically interesting question
is \emph{where} that difference comes from: on which types of trials
does the advantage concentrate?

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{make\_pointwise\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(loo\_a, loo\_b, data\_df, label)}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{obs =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(data\_df)),}
         \AttributeTok{elpd\_diff=}\NormalTok{loo\_a}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pointwise[,}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{]}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{loo\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pointwise[,}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{],}
         \AttributeTok{trial =}\NormalTok{ data\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{trial, }\AttributeTok{agent\_id=}\NormalTok{data\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id, }\AttributeTok{label=}\NormalTok{label)}

\NormalTok{pw\_all }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{make\_pointwise\_df}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_b, loo\_m\_on\_b, d\_biased, }\StringTok{"Biased data: Biased {-} Memory"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{make\_pointwise\_df}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_m, loo\_b\_on\_m, d\_memory, }\StringTok{"Memory data: Memory {-} Biased"}\NormalTok{))}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(pw\_all, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x=}\NormalTok{trial, }\AttributeTok{y=}\NormalTok{elpd\_diff)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype=}\StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color=}\StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color=}\NormalTok{elpd\_diff}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{size=}\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha=}\FloatTok{0.35}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_smooth}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{method=}\StringTok{"gam"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{formula=}\NormalTok{y}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\FunctionTok{s}\NormalTok{(x,}\AttributeTok{bs=}\StringTok{"cs"}\NormalTok{),}
              \AttributeTok{color=}\StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{se=}\ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{label) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"TRUE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{labels=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"Favors alternative"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"TRUE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"Favors true model"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{name=}\ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Pointwise ELPD Differences Across Trials"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-pointwise-elpd-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The growing advantage of the memory model over trials is a direct
cognitive signature: early in the game the running opponent rate is near
0.5 (uninformative), so both models predict roughly equally. As the
opponent's behavior becomes established --- typically after 20-30 trials
--- the memory model increasingly exploits that signal while the biased
model cannot. Once the reversal happens, the advantage disappears, but
then picks up again. The advantage concentrates precisely where the
cognitive mechanism operates. This is model comparison doing what it is
supposed to do: not just telling us which model wins, but illuminating
\emph{why} and \emph{when}.

\subsection{Bayesian Stacking: Combining Models Into an
Ensemble}\label{bayesian-stacking-combining-models-into-an-ensemble}

\texttt{loo\_compare()} produces a ranking, but it does not tell us how
much to trust each model. \textbf{Bayesian stacking} (Yao et al., 2018)
goes further by finding the \emph{optimal mixture} of the competing
models for prediction.

The idea is to find weights \(w_1, w_2\) (summing to 1) such that the
weighted combination of the two models' LOO predictive distributions is
as good as possible. Unlike Bayes factors, stacking does not assume that
one of the models is the truth --- it simply finds the best predictive
combination. A weight of nearly 1.0 for one model means the other adds
nothing useful. A more balanced split means both models are capturing
something the other misses.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stack\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{loo\_model\_weights}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased"} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ loo\_b\_on\_b, }\StringTok{"Memory"} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ loo\_m\_on\_b),}
                             \AttributeTok{method =} \StringTok{"stacking"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{stack\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{loo\_model\_weights}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Memory"}\OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{loo\_m\_on\_m, }\StringTok{"Biased"}\OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{loo\_b\_on\_m),}
                             \AttributeTok{method=}\StringTok{"stacking"}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Dataset  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Model    =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Stacking =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(stack\_b[}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{],stack\_b[}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{],}
\NormalTok{                     stack\_m[}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{],stack\_m[}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{]), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{True\_model =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{,}\ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{,}\ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{,}\ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x=}\NormalTok{Model, }\AttributeTok{y=}\NormalTok{Stacking, }\AttributeTok{fill=}\NormalTok{True\_model)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_col}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Add labels, offset slightly above the bar}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_text}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{percent}\NormalTok{(Stacking, }\AttributeTok{accuracy =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)), }
            \AttributeTok{vjust =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Dataset, }\AttributeTok{labeller=}\NormalTok{label\_both) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"gray70"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"TRUE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{labels=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"FALSE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"Alternative"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"TRUE"}\OtherTok{=}\StringTok{"True model"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{name=}\ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Expand the upper y{-}limit to 1.1 so the 100\% labels are not clipped}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_y\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{labels=}\NormalTok{scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent, }\AttributeTok{limits=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Bayesian Stacking Weights"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Successful recovery: true model receives near{-}100\% weight."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x=}\ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y=}\StringTok{"Model weight"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position=}\StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-stacking-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\section{Evaluating Our Pipeline: Full Model
Recovery}\label{evaluating-our-pipeline-full-model-recovery}

Let us now pull together everything into the model recovery matrix ---
the central test of whether PSIS-LOO is doing its job. For each of the
two datasets, we check whether the method identifies the correct
generating model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{elpd\_matrix }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tribble}\NormalTok{(}
  \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Model,   }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Data,    }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ELPD,       }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{SE,}
  \StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{, loo\_b\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], loo\_b\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{],}
  \StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{, loo\_m\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], loo\_m\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{],}
  \StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{, loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{],}
  \StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{, loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{], loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"SE"}\NormalTok{]}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{is\_true=}\NormalTok{(Model}\SpecialCharTok{==}\StringTok{"Biased"}\SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{Data}\SpecialCharTok{==}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{(Model}\SpecialCharTok{==}\StringTok{"Memory"}\SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{Data}\SpecialCharTok{==}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{),}
         \AttributeTok{label=}\FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\%.1f}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{(SE=\%.1f)"}\NormalTok{, ELPD, SE))}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(elpd\_matrix, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x=}\NormalTok{Data, }\AttributeTok{y=}\NormalTok{Model, }\AttributeTok{fill=}\NormalTok{ELPD)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_tile}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color=}\StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth=}\FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_text}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{label=}\NormalTok{label), }\AttributeTok{size=}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fontface=}\StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_tile}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data=}\FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(elpd\_matrix,is\_true),}
            \AttributeTok{color=}\StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill=}\ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth=}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_viridis\_c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option=}\StringTok{"D"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name=}\StringTok{"ELPD"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{guide=}\FunctionTok{guide\_colorbar}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{barwidth=}\DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{barheight=}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Model Recovery Matrix"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Blue borders = true generating model. Higher ELPD = better prediction."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x=}\StringTok{"Data{-}generating model"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y=}\StringTok{"Fitted model"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position=}\StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-recovery-matrix-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Each column of this matrix answers a different scientific question. The
left column (biased data) asks: can PSIS-LOO detect that the memory
model is unnecessarily complex when a simpler model suffices? The right
column (memory data) asks: can PSIS-LOO detect that the biased model is
missing a real cognitive mechanism? Within each column, the model with
the higher ELPD should be the one that generated the data --- that is
the meaning of successful model recovery.

For context, the Ch. 7 memory model (fitted to the full 50-agent
reversal dataset with 80--240 trials per agent) had ELPD = -4233 (SE =
44.3). The Ch. 8 memory-on-memory fit uses a shorter, fixed-length
session (120 trials vs.~80--240), so its absolute ELPD is not directly
comparable --- but the sign and direction of all comparisons should be
consistent.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{final\_table }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{Fit}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}           \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Biased"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Biased{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Memory{-}\textgreater{}Memory"}\NormalTok{),}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{ELPD}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}          \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ pareto\_k\_table}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ELPD,}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{SE}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}            \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ pareto\_k\_table}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{SE,}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{p\_loo/N}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}       \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ pareto\_k\_table}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ratio,}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{POT{-}C p}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}       \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(pit\_b\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
                       \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(pit\_m\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
                       \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(pit\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
                       \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(pit\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_global,}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)),}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{True model?}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}   \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"YES"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"YES"}\NormalTok{),}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{LOO preferred?}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Row 1: Does Biased beat Memory (on Biased data)?}
    \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{           loo\_m\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{],}\StringTok{"YES"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{),}
    
    \CommentTok{\# Row 2: Does Memory beat Biased (on Biased data)?}
    \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{           loo\_b\_on\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{],}\StringTok{"YES"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{),}
    
    \CommentTok{\# Row 3: Does Biased beat Memory (on Memory data)?  \textless{}{-}{-} FIXED THIS ONE}
    \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{           loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{],}\StringTok{"YES"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{),}
    
    \CommentTok{\# Row 4: Does Memory beat Biased (on Memory data)?}
    \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(loo\_m\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{           loo\_b\_on\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{estimates[}\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"Estimate"}\NormalTok{],}\StringTok{"YES"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(final\_table, }\AttributeTok{caption=}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{"Full comparison summary. p\_loo/N: use POT{-}C if \textgreater{} 0.05."}\NormalTok{,}
  \StringTok{"POT{-}C p: formal uniformity test, valid under dependence."}\NormalTok{,}
  \StringTok{"Recovery: \textquotesingle{}True model?\textquotesingle{} should match \textquotesingle{}LOO preferred?\textquotesingle{}."}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}

Biased-\textgreater Biased & -3393.8 & 36.0 & 0.007 & 0 & YES & YES \\
