\subsection{Power Analysis and Experimental
Design}\label{power-analysis-and-experimental-design}

e just saw a successful single instance of model recovery: for our
specific simulated dataset, loo\_compare() correctly preferred the true
generating model in both cases. But one lucky simulation does not
guarantee our experimental design is robust.

If a participant truly uses a simple Biased strategy, is our
experimental design rich enough for LOO cross-validation to correctly
prefer the Biased model over the Memory model? Conversely, if they truly
use the Memory model, do we have enough trials to confidently detect
that complexity, or will the simpler Biased model win by default due to
its lower complexity penalty?

Testing this is called a Model Recovery Analysis, and it serves as the
Bayesian cognitive modeling equivalent of a statistical power analysis.

\subsection{Measuring Power: The F1
Score}\label{measuring-power-the-f1-score}

To run a model recovery study, we simulate hundreds of datasets from
both models across a variety of experimental designs (varying the number
of participants and trials) and cross-fit both models to see which
wins.Because model comparison penalizes complexity, the errors are
rarely symmetrical. A complex model (like Memory) might be hard to
detect if the effect is weak (low Recall), but if LOO does select it, it
is almost certainly the true model (high Precision). Conversely, the
simpler model (Biased) acts as a default fallback: it is easily
recovered when true, but is often falsely selected when the true Memory
mechanism is too weak to detect.To capture this asymmetry in a single
metric, we calculate the F1 Score for each model at every experimental
design point. The F1 score is the harmonic mean of Precision and
Recall:\[F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}\]
An F1 score near 1.0 means we perfectly and reliably identify the model.
An F1 score below 0.80 suggests our experimental design is underpowered
for that specific cognitive mechanism.

\subsection{Designing the Experiment: Trials, Participants, and Task
Structure}\label{designing-the-experiment-trials-participants-and-task-structure}

In hierarchical cognitive modeling, we have two distinct sample sizes to
worry about: the number of trials per participant (\(N\)), and the
number of participants (\(J\)).These two dimensions govern different
types of statistical power:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  More Trials (\(N\)): Increases our ability to accurately estimate
  individual-level parameters. If we only have 30 trials, we cannot
  confidently detect the subtle influence of the Memory parameter within
  a single person's noisy choices.
\item
  More Participants (\(J\)): Increases our ability to accurately
  estimate the population-level distribution.
\end{itemize}

To conduct a formal power analysis for model comparison, we
systematically repeat our recovery pipeline across a grid of design
choices. We then plot the F1 score as a 2D heatmap to find the optimal
experimental threshold where both models can be reliably identified.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Conceptual Power Trade{-}off Matrix (F1 Scores) {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{power\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N\_Trials =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{90}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{N\_Participants =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{40}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Simulate Recall (True Positive Rate)}
    \CommentTok{\# Memory is hard to detect with few trials.}
    \AttributeTok{Recall\_Memory =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.025} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.02} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants),}
    \CommentTok{\# Biased is easy to detect when true.}
    \AttributeTok{Recall\_Biased =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.01} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.01} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants), }

    \CommentTok{\# Simulate Precision (Positive Predictive Value)}
    \CommentTok{\# If LOO picks Memory, it\textquotesingle{}s almost certainly right (complex models rarely win by accident).}
    \AttributeTok{Precision\_Memory =} \FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.80} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.001} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.001} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants),}
    \CommentTok{\# If LOO picks Biased, it might be a weak Memory model falling back (lower precision).}
    \AttributeTok{Precision\_Biased =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.015} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.02} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Calculate F1 Score}
    \AttributeTok{F1\_Memory =} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (Precision\_Memory }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ Recall\_Memory) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (Precision\_Memory }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Recall\_Memory),}
    \AttributeTok{F1\_Biased =} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (Precision\_Biased }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ Recall\_Biased) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (Precision\_Biased }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Recall\_Biased)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{across}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"F1"}\NormalTok{), }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(.x, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(N\_Trials, N\_Participants, F1\_Memory, F1\_Biased) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cols =} \FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"F1"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"Model"}\NormalTok{, }
               \AttributeTok{names\_prefix =} \StringTok{"F1\_"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"F1\_Score"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Plot the surfaces side{-}by{-}side}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(power\_grid, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(N\_Trials), }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(N\_Participants), }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ F1\_Score)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_tile}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_text}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{percent}\NormalTok{(F1\_Score, }\AttributeTok{accuracy =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)), }
            \AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fontface =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{, }
            \AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(power\_grid}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{F1\_Score }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{0.80}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Model, }\AttributeTok{labeller =} \FunctionTok{as\_labeller}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Biased =} \StringTok{"Biased Model Recovery (F1)"}\NormalTok{, }
                                              \AttributeTok{Memory =} \StringTok{"Memory Model Recovery (F1)"}\NormalTok{))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_viridis\_c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"mako"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{direction =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }
                       \AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
       \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Model Recovery Power Analysis: Trials × Participants"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"F1 \textgreater{} 80\% (white cells) = reliably discriminable design. "}\NormalTok{,}
                         \StringTok{"Memory is harder to detect than Biased — it needs both more trials AND more participants."}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Number of Trials per Participant (N)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Number of Participants (J)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"F1 Score"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{panel.grid =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{(),}
        \AttributeTok{axis.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{axis.title =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{strip.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-model-recovery-matrix-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Looking at this trade-off matrix, we can definitively make a decision:
``To reliably distinguish between Biased and Memory strategies (e.g., F1
\textgreater{} 80\% for both models), we cannot rely on large
participant counts alone. Running 100 participants for only 30 trials
yields inadequate power for recovering the Memory mechanism (F1 = 66\%).
We must ensure sufficient within-subject data, requiring a minimum
design of roughly 60 participants completing 90 trials each to achieve
reliable discriminability across the board.''

\subsection{The Impact of Task Design: Better Data vs.~More
Data}\label{the-impact-of-task-design-better-data-vs.-more-data}

But power in cognitive modeling isn't just about collecting more data;
it is often about collecting better data. We can vary the experimental
setup itself.Consider why we explicitly forced the opponent to switch
from an 80\%-right bias to a 20\%-right bias halfway through our
Matching Pennies simulation. What would happen if we just let the
opponent play a constant 80\%-right bias for the entire game?If the
opponent's behavior is constant, the Memory agent's internal running
average (opp\_rate\_prev) quickly settles near 0.8 and stays there.
Behaviorally, an agent responding to a flat memory signal is
mathematically indistinguishable from a Biased agent with a fixed
probability. Because the independent variable (the memory signal) lacks
variance, the two models become collinear. When models are collinear,
PSIS-LOO will heavily penalize the Memory model for its extra \(\beta\)
parameter, and the simpler Biased model will falsely ``win'' the
comparison.We can formalize this by adding ``Task Design'' as an
explicit dimension in our power analysis. Below, we simulate the F1
recovery score for the Memory model under two different experimental
designs: a Constant 80\% Bias and an 80\% \(\to\) 20\% Reversal.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Conceptual Power Trade{-}off: Task Design Impact {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{design\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N\_Trials =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{90}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{N\_Participants =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{40}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{80}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Task\_Design =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Constant Bias (80\%)"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Reversal (80\% {-}\textgreater{} 20\%)"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Simulate Recall: Constant bias severely caps the ability to detect memory}
    \AttributeTok{Recall =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(Task\_Design }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Reversal (80\% {-}\textgreater{} 20\%)"}\NormalTok{,}
                    \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.025} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.02} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants),}
                    \FloatTok{0.60} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.01} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.01} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants)),}
    
    \CommentTok{\# Simulate Precision: Constant bias makes the model uncertain even when it wins}
    \AttributeTok{Precision =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(Task\_Design }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Reversal (80\% {-}\textgreater{} 20\%)"}\NormalTok{,}
                       \FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.80} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.001} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.001} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants),}
                       \FloatTok{0.70} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.0005} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Trials }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.0005} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ N\_Participants)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{F1\_Score =} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (Precision }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ Recall) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (Precision }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Recall),}
    \AttributeTok{F1\_Score =} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.99}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(F1\_Score, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))) }\CommentTok{\# Bound between 0 and 0.99}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Plot the impact of Task Design}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(design\_grid, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(N\_Trials), }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(N\_Participants), }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ F1\_Score)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_tile}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_text}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{percent}\NormalTok{(F1\_Score, }\AttributeTok{accuracy =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)), }
            \AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fontface =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{, }
            \AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(design\_grid}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{F1\_Score }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Task\_Design) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_viridis\_c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"mako"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{direction =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }
                       \AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"The Impact of Task Design on Model Recovery"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"F1 Score for recovering the Memory Model. A poor design cannot be saved by sample size."}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Number of Trials per Participant (N)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Number of Participants (J)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"F1 Score"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{panel.grid =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{(),}
        \AttributeTok{axis.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{axis.title =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{strip.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/ch08_power_task_design_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

This visualization delivers a stark warning. If you use a Constant Bias
design, even running a massive, expensive study with 100 participants
and 150 trials yields a dismal F1 score of roughly 65\%. The models are
structurally overlapping, and throwing more data at the problem barely
moves the needle.By simply changing the code of the task to include a
Reversal (costing exactly \(0\) extra dollars and \(0\) extra minutes of
participant time), a much smaller study of just 40 participants and 90
trials achieves a highly reliable 84\% F1 score.

\subsection{Beyond Binary Recovery: Simulation-Based Calibration Checks
for
Models}\label{beyond-binary-recovery-simulation-based-calibration-checks-for-models}

\subsection{From Binary Wins to Probabilistic
Power}\label{from-binary-wins-to-probabilistic-power}

Traditional model recovery treats model comparison as a binary,
``winner-takes-all'' election. If ELPD\_A \textgreater{} ELPD\_B by even
0.1 nats, Model A gets a ``1'' for accuracy, and Model B gets a ``0''.
This binary approach is mathematically impoverished because it hides our
uncertainty.

In a fully Bayesian workflow, we prefer a probabilistic approach.
Instead of asking ``Did the true model win?'', we can ask ``How much
stacking weight does the true model receive?''

This transforms our understanding of statistical power. Instead of a
binary accuracy curve, we can plot the average stacking weight assigned
to the true model across simulations. If the Biased model generated the
data, but its average stacking weight is only 0.55, our experimental
design is highly uncertain and under-powered, even if the Biased model
technically ``wins'' most of the time. Using continuous stacking weights
introduces a natural, continuous gradient of uncertainty into our model
identification. By using stacking weights, we move from a discrete
`Correct/Incorrect' recovery metric to a continuous measure of
probabilistic power. This allows us to quantify exactly how much
uncertainty remains in our model identification even when the true model
`wins' the comparison.

This realization leads us directly to the ultimate check of our entire
model comparison pipeline: Model Simulation-Based Calibration Checks
(Model SBC).

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{A Philosophical Technical Note:
\(\mathcal{M}\)-closed
vs.~\(\mathcal{M}\)-open}{A Philosophical Technical Note: \textbackslash mathcal\{M\}-closed vs.~\textbackslash mathcal\{M\}-open}}\label{a-philosophical-technical-note-mathcalm-closed-vs.-mathcalm-open}

There is a philosophical friction in this exercise. By simulating data
from a specific model, we are operating in an \(\mathcal{M}\)-closed
framework---we know for an absolute fact that one of our models is the
true data-generating process. However, we evaluate the recovery using
Bayesian stacking weights, which are designed for the
\(\mathcal{M}\)-open framework (the assumption that all models are
false, and we merely want the optimal predictive mixture).We use
stacking weights here anyway because they are continuous. Rather than
forcing a binary ``win/loss,'' stacking weights tell us how much
predictive variance uniquely belongs to each architecture.

\subsection{Running Model SBC: The Code
Pipeline}\label{running-model-sbc-the-code-pipeline}

The generative process for Model SBC extends parameter SBC to the model
level:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Sample a true model \(M\) (e.g., a coin flip between Biased and
  Memory).
\item
  Sample parameters strictly from that specific model's prior.
\item
  Simulate data from the data model. Fit all competing models to the
  data and compute their Bayesian stacking weights.
\item
  Repeat this thousands of times.
\end{itemize}

Because running 2,000 simulations and fitting two multilevel Stan models
to each would take hours (or days) on a standard laptop, we will not
execute it live here. However, to maintain the hands-on promise of this
workflow, here is the exact furrr parallelization code you would use.
Notice how it elevates the target to loo::loo\_model\_weights().

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(multisession, }\AttributeTok{workers =}\NormalTok{ parallel}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{detectCores}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{n\_sims }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2000}

\NormalTok{model\_sbc\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{future\_map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_sims, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sim\_id) \{}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 1. Flip a coin to choose the true generative model}
\NormalTok{  true\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 2. Simulate data exactly from the chosen model\textquotesingle{}s PRIORS}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (true\_model }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{    sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_biased\_from\_prior}\NormalTok{() }
\NormalTok{    stan\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_stan\_biased}\NormalTok{(sim\_data)}
\NormalTok{  \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    sim\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_memory\_from\_prior}\NormalTok{() }
\NormalTok{    stan\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_stan\_memory}\NormalTok{(sim\_data)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 3. Fit BOTH candidate models to the simulated dataset}
\NormalTok{  fit\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_biased\_ncp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_list, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }
                                 \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  fit\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_memory\_ncp}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_list, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }
                                 \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 4. Compute LOO and Bayesian Stacking Weights}
\NormalTok{  loo\_b }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_b}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{  loo\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{()}
  
\NormalTok{  weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{loo\_model\_weights}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Biased =}\NormalTok{ loo\_b, }\AttributeTok{Memory =}\NormalTok{ loo\_m), }
                                    \AttributeTok{method =} \StringTok{"stacking"}\NormalTok{)}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 5. Return the result for this specific simulation iteration}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{sim\_id =}\NormalTok{ sim\_id,}
    \AttributeTok{true\_model =}\NormalTok{ true\_model,}
    \AttributeTok{weight\_biased =} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(weights[}\StringTok{"Biased"}\NormalTok{]),}
    \AttributeTok{weight\_memory =} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(weights[}\StringTok{"Memory"}\NormalTok{])}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}, }\AttributeTok{.options =} \FunctionTok{furrr\_options}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{12345}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{How Do We Assess Model
Calibration?}\label{how-do-we-assess-model-calibration}

To assess if our model comparison is well-calibrated, we treat the
stacking weights as a measure of predictive confidence. However,
``calibration'' in this context is frequently misunderstood.Calibration
does not mean that the average stacking weight should match the prior
base rate of the models. Calibration means that the weight must honestly
reflect the empirical reality of its own claims:
\[P(\text{Model is True} \mid \text{Weight} = w) = w\]

If we find that across all datasets where the Memory model received a
weight of \(0.70\), it was actually the true model only \(40\%\) of the
time, our workflow is systematically overconfident. It is claiming a
unique predictive contribution for a model that isn't actually there.
Conversely, if it was the true model \(90\%\) of the time, our metric is
underconfident.

\subsubsection{Why do we use a 50/50 prior for
SBC?}\label{why-do-we-use-a-5050-prior-for-sbc}

This definition of calibration explains why we sample the ``True Model''
using a uniform 50/50 coin flip in our SBC pipeline. Imagine if we
instead simulated 1,000 datasets where the Memory model was true 100\%
of the time. For datasets with very few trials (uninformative data), the
model comparison would correctly output a cautious weight of \(0.50\).
But because the Memory model is always true in this skewed universe, a
weight of \(0.50\) would look massively underconfident on a calibration
plot. By feeding the simulation a 50/50 split, we force the calibration
check to evaluate only the information content of the data itself,
neutralizing the base rate.

\subsubsection{Decoding the Calibration
Curves}\label{decoding-the-calibration-curves}

Since we aren't running the full 2,000-fit simulation locally right now,
I have generated a sample plot to show exactly what the calibration
curves produced by the code above would look like under different
diagnostic scenarios.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Simulate the data, explicitly including the number of datasets per bin (N)}
\NormalTok{calibration\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{bin\_mid =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{length.out =} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# In model comparison, weights often pile up at the extremes (0 or 1) }
    \CommentTok{\# and fewer datasets land in the ambiguous middle.}
    \AttributeTok{N\_datasets   =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{500} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{4} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (bin\_mid }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{)),}
    
    \AttributeTok{Perfect      =}\NormalTok{ bin\_mid,}
    \AttributeTok{Overconf     =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{1.5} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(bin\_mid)))),}
    \AttributeTok{Underconf    =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(bin\_mid)))),}
    \AttributeTok{Miscalibrate =} \FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, bin\_mid }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.15} \SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{sin}\NormalTok{(bin\_mid }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pi }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)))}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cols =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(Perfect, Overconf, Underconf, Miscalibrate), }
               \AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"Scenario"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"Observed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# Calculate standard errors for the binomial proportion}
    \AttributeTok{SE =} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{((Observed }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ Observed)) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ N\_datasets),}
    \CommentTok{\# 95\% Confidence Intervals}
    \AttributeTok{Lower =} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, Observed }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{1.96} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ SE),}
    \AttributeTok{Upper =} \FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, Observed }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{1.96} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ SE)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# 2. The Calibration Curve with Confidence Ribbons}
\NormalTok{p\_curve }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(calibration\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ bin\_mid, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Observed, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ Scenario)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \CommentTok{\# Add the variance ribbon!}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ Lower, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ Upper, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ Scenario), }
              \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.15}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linetype =}\NormalTok{ Scenario), }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Miscalibrate"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Overconf"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"orange"}\NormalTok{, }
                                \StringTok{"Underconf"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"purple"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Perfect"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Miscalibrate"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Overconf"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"orange"}\NormalTok{, }
                               \StringTok{"Underconf"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"purple"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Perfect"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent, }\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_y\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent, }\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"SBC Model Calibration with Uncertainty"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Ribbons show 95\% CI. Wider ribbons in the middle reflect fewer ambiguous datasets."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Empirical Frequency (Ground Truth)"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"none"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{axis.text.x =} \FunctionTok{element\_blank}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# 3. The Marginal Histogram (Showing the distribution of weights)}
\NormalTok{p\_hist }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ calibration\_data }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(Scenario }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Perfect"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \CommentTok{\# Just to get the Ns once}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ bin\_mid, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ N\_datasets)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_col}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"gray70"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent, }\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Stacking Weight (Model\textquotesingle{}s Confidence)"}\NormalTok{, }
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Count (N)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_minimal}\NormalTok{()}

\CommentTok{\# Combine them using patchwork}
\NormalTok{p\_curve }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_hist }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{plot\_layout}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{heights =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/ch08_calibration_curves_demo-1.pdf}
\end{center}

When you plot the binned stacking weights (X-axis) against the actual
frequency that the model was the true data-generating process (Y-axis),
the geometry of the curve diagnoses your workflow:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Perfectly Calibrated (Steel Blue Diagonal): The model's predictive
  confidence perfectly matches reality. If the data is uninformative,
  the weights go to \(0.5\) and the true model is a coin flip. If the
  data is rich, the weights go to \(1.0\) and the model is always right.
\item
  Overconfident (Orange S-Curve): The model is ``loud.'' Look at the
  \(80\%\) mark on the X-axis: the model claims high confidence, but the
  true frequency is only \(65\%\) (it falls below the diagonal). The
  model is overestimating its own predictive uniqueness. This is the
  most common enemy in cognitive modeling, typically occurring when a
  flexible hierarchical model overfits the idiosyncrasies (noise) of a
  small dataset.
\item
  Underconfident (Purple Reverse S-Curve): The model is ``shy.'' At a
  cautious \(60\%\) weight, the true frequency is already \(80\%\) (it
  sits above the diagonal). The model is failing to commit. This often
  happens if you apply overly aggressive regularization or priors that
  are too skeptical of extreme cognitive effects.
\item
  Miscalibrated (Red Wavy Line): This represents a structural breakdown
  in the model comparison. Notice the flat plateau in the middle: the
  model's weight oscillates between \(35\%\) and \(65\%\), but the
  ground truth stays stubbornly flat at \(50\%\). The model thinks it is
  picking up a subtle signal, but it is actually just hallucinating
  patterns in the noise. Unlike smooth S-curves (which just need more
  data or tuning), a wavy line indicates severe issues like lumpy
  priors, MCMC divergence failures, or models hitting hard mathematical
  boundaries.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{The Danger of Averages: Embracing the
Distribution}\label{the-danger-of-averages-embracing-the-distribution}

Looking at a simple line on a calibration curve can be deceiving. A
single dot representing the ``mean empirical frequency'' hides the
underlying distribution of the stacking weights, which can lead to
dangerously false confidence. A rigorous calibration check must
visualize two distinct types of variance:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Vertical Variance (Binomial Uncertainty) Not all bins are created
  equal. Suppose our model assigns a stacking weight of \(0.50\). If our
  experimental design is highly decisive, it might be very rare for the
  model to be this confused; perhaps only 15 simulated datasets out of
  2,000 land in the \(0.50\) bin. If 8 of them are the Memory model, the
  mean frequency is roughly \(50\%\), but our mathematical uncertainty
  around that mean is massive.By adding a 95\% confidence ribbon around
  our curves, we map exactly how trustworthy the diagnostic is. Where
  the ribbon is narrow, we have high sample density and high certainty.
  Where it balloons outward, the calibration curve is mostly statistical
  noise.
\item
  Horizontal Variance (The Weight Density) We also need to know the base
  distribution of the weights themselves. Does the model usually output
  highly decisive weights (near \(0\%\) and \(100\%\)), or is it
  frequently returning ambiguous splits (near \(50\%\))?
\end{enumerate}

By plotting a marginal histogram beneath the calibration curve, we
reveal the model's decisiveness. In a well-designed experiment, we
expect to see a U-shaped distribution: the majority of the datasets
should pile up at the \(0\%\) and \(100\%\) extremes, indicating that
the task successfully forces the models to diverge and allows the math
to confidently pick a winner. If the histogram shows a massive spike in
the center at \(50\%\), it is a fatal diagnosis of your experimental
design: your task is simply not generating data rich enough to tell your
theories apart.

\begin{quote}
Technical Note: The Flaw of Binning \& Counting (and the CORP Solution)
If you attempt to generate the calibration curves we just discussed
using your own simulated Model SBC data, you will immediately run into a
severe methodological trap: the ``binning and counting'' problem. To
plot empirical frequencies, you have to group your continuous stacking
weights into discrete bins (e.g., \(0.40\) to \(0.50\)). If you use
fixed, evenly spaced bins, you will inevitably end up with ``sparse
bins''---regions where the model rarely outputs a weight. The empirical
frequency inside a sparse bin is subject to massive estimation variance.
If you plot this, your calibration line will spike wildly up and down,
making a perfectly calibrated model look like it has suffered a
structural breakdown (the red ``Miscalibrated'' wavy line from our
conceptual plot). To solve this, Dimitriadis, Gneiting, and Jordan
(2021) introduced the CORP approach (Consistent, Optimally binned,
Reproducible, and PAV-based). Instead of forcing data into arbitrary,
fixed bins, CORP lets the data determine its own optimal bins using
nonparametric isotonic regression.It accomplishes this via the elegant
Pool-Adjacent-Violators (PAV) algorithm. Here is how it works for Model
SBC:
\end{quote}

\begin{quote}
\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Sort: Sort all 2,000 simulated datasets from the lowest assigned
  stacking weight to the highest.
\item
  Assume Monotonicity: A logical model comparison metric must be
  monotonic: a higher stacking weight should correspond to an equal or
  higher true empirical frequency.
\item
  Pool the Violators: The algorithm walks along the sorted data. If it
  finds a ``violator''---a case where a higher stacking weight actually
  has a lower true empirical rate than the point before it (due to noise
  in sparse regions)---it ``pools'' those adjacent points together and
  averages them into a single, wider bin. The result is pure magic: PAV
  automatically creates wide, stable bins where your data is sparse, and
  fine-grained, precise steps where your data is dense. It also
  mathematically decomposes the Brier score of your predictions into
  three stable metrics: Miscalibration (MCB), Discrimination (DSC), and
  Uncertainty (UNC).Here is how you can apply the CORP approach to your
  own Model SBC data using the authors' reliabilitydiag R package:
\end{itemize}
\end{quote}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pacman}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{p\_load}\NormalTok{(reliabilitydiag)}

\CommentTok{\# 1. Simulate a mock Model SBC output (2,000 fits)}
\NormalTok{n\_sims }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2000}

\CommentTok{\# Simulate stacking weights (piling up at the extremes)}
\NormalTok{mock\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbeta}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }

\CommentTok{\# Simulate the Ground Truth (1 = Memory is True, 0 = Biased is True)}
\CommentTok{\# We deliberately inject "Overconfidence" into the DGP: }
\CommentTok{\# The true probability is pulled closer to 0.5 than the model\textquotesingle{}s weight claims.}
\NormalTok{true\_probs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(mock\_weights) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }
\NormalTok{mock\_truth }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, true\_probs)}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Apply the CORP approach (PAV Algorithm)}
\CommentTok{\# \textquotesingle{}x\textquotesingle{} is our continuous prediction (stacking weight)}
\CommentTok{\# \textquotesingle{}y\textquotesingle{} is the binary ground truth (was it actually the Memory model?)}
\NormalTok{rd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{reliabilitydiag}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Overconfident\_Model =}\NormalTok{ mock\_weights, }
  \AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mock\_truth}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Plot the optimal reliability diagram}
\FunctionTok{autoplot}\NormalTok{(rd) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"SBC Model Calibration (CORP Approach)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Optimal dynamic binning via the PAV algorithm. Notice the S{-}curve of overconfidence."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Forecast Value (Bayesian Stacking Weight)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Conditional Event Probability (True Model Frequency)"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/ch08_corp_pav_demo-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsubsection{When a 50/50 Split is a Perfect
Diagnosis}\label{when-a-5050-split-is-a-perfect-diagnosis}

Imagine the SBC process selects the Memory model, and then draws a
population memory sensitivity parameter from the prior that happens to
be very close to zero (\(\mu_\beta \approx 0\)). The simulated agents
will completely ignore the opponent's history. Mathematically, the data
generated by this Memory model is entirely indistinguishable from data
generated by a Biased model.When we fit both models to this specific
dataset, the Bayesian stacking weights will likely split right down the
middle, hovering around \(0.5\) for each model.Students often panic when
they see this, assuming the model comparison has ``failed'' to recover
the true model. In reality, a weight of \(0.5\) is a mathematically
perfect diagnosis of structural non-identifiability under the prior. The
metric is working exactly as intended: it is honestly reporting that, in
the parameter space near \(\beta = 0\), the models make identical
predictions. A continuous metric allows us to map exactly where in the
parameter space our cognitive theories are distinct, and where they
collapse into one another.

\section{Prior Sensitivity at the Comparison
Level}\label{prior-sensitivity-at-the-comparison-level}

We applied prior sensitivity analysis to individual models in Chapter 6.
At the model comparison stage, the question becomes slightly different:
even if each model's individual parameters are sensitive to prior
choices, does the \emph{comparison} between models remain stable?

Consider the following thought experiment: scaling the prior on
\(\sigma_\theta\) shifts the biased model's ELPD by 5 nats, but also
shifts the memory model's ELPD by roughly the same 5 nats. The
difference --- the quantity that drives the comparison --- would remain
stable. This would be a good outcome: the comparison is robust even
though individual models are sensitive. The problematic scenario is
asymmetric sensitivity: one model's ELPD shifts substantially while the
other's stays put. In that case, our preference for one model over the
other depends on which prior happened to be chosen --- which is not a
principled basis for a scientific conclusion.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"=== Prior sensitivity: Biased model ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
=== Prior sensitivity: Biased model ===
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_sensitivity}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_b,}
  \AttributeTok{variable=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Sensitivity based on cjs_dist
Prior selection: all priors
Likelihood selection: all data

    variable prior likelihood                     diagnosis
    mu_theta 0.025      0.035                             -
 sigma_theta 0.681      0.127 potential prior-data conflict
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{=== Prior sensitivity: Memory model ===}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

=== Prior sensitivity: Memory model ===
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_sensitivity}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_m,}
  \AttributeTok{variable=}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Sensitivity based on cjs_dist
Prior selection: all priors
Likelihood selection: all data

 variable prior likelihood diagnosis
 mu_alpha 0.041      0.064         -
  mu_beta 0.027      0.091         -
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_dens}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_sequence}\NormalTok{(fit\_b\_on\_b, }\AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"sigma\_theta"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Biased Model: Prior Sensitivity"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p2 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_dens}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_sequence}\NormalTok{(fit\_m\_on\_m, }\AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mu\_beta"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme\_classic}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Memory Model: Prior Sensitivity"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Combine using patchwork}
\NormalTok{p1 }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{07-ModelComparison_files/figure-pdf/07-prior-sensitivity-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The console output above directly tests this: the priorsense tables
include a diagnostic column that warns you if the LOO/ELPD computation
is highly sensitive to the prior. Meanwhile, the density perturbation
plots show why that sensitivity might be happening by revealing how the
posterior on each parameter shifts as we increase or decrease the prior
weight. If the biased model's ELPD is flagged as sensitive but the
memory model's is not, then our model preference is partly an artifact
of our prior choice rather than genuine predictive superiority.

\section{When Standard LOO Is Wrong: Sequential
Dependencies}\label{when-standard-loo-is-wrong-sequential-dependencies}

There is an important limitation to PSIS-LOO that is easy to overlook.
Standard LOO treats observations as \emph{exchangeable} --- it assumes
that the order of observations does not matter, and that leaving out
observation \(i\) only affects predictions for \(y_i\) itself, not for
any other observation.

For the biased agent model this is entirely correct: each trial is
independent given the agent's parameters. For our memory model as
implemented, it is also technically valid: the memory signal
\texttt{opp\_rate\_prev} is a pre-computed covariate that we pass into
Stan as fixed data. Leaving out trial \(i\) from the LOO computation
does not change \texttt{opp\_rate\_prev} for any other trial --- the
memory features were already computed before Stan ever ran.

However, many cognitively interesting models have genuine temporal
dependencies that standard LOO cannot handle. Consider a Rescorla-Wagner
reinforcement learning model where the learning state at trial \(t\)
depends on the prediction error at trial \(t-1\). Or a drift-diffusion
model with trial-by-trial fatigue effects. In these models, leaving out
trial \(i\) from the LOO set would also change the learning state at
trials \(i+1, i+2, \ldots\) --- we cannot simply remove one observation
without unraveling the whole sequential structure. Standard LOO would
then be ``peeking into the future'' by conditioning on observations that
come after the one being predicted.

For such models, the correct approach is \textbf{Leave-Future-Out
cross-validation (LFO-CV)} (Burkner, Gabry \& Vehtari, 2020). Instead of
leaving out one observation at a time in isolation, LFO-CV asks: at each
time point \(t\), how well does the model predict the next trial given
all past trials \(y_{1:t-1}\)? This respects the temporal structure and
gives an honest measure of prospective predictive accuracy.

Decision rule: if temporal structure is handled externally via
pre-computed features passed as data (as in our memory model), standard
LOO is appropriate. If the sequential dynamics are inside Stan's
\texttt{transformed\ data} or \texttt{model} blocks, use LFO-CV.

\section{What Model Comparison Cannot
Do}\label{what-model-comparison-cannot-do}

We have spent this chapter learning how to use PSIS-LOO to compare
models. It is equally important to understand what it \emph{cannot} do.

\textbf{It measures internal training generalization, not external
transfer.} We need to think carefully about what we mean by ``out of
sample.'' There are actually two distinct types of test sets we might
consider: internal and external.

Internal test sets come from the same data collection effort as our
training data - for example, we might randomly set aside 20\% of our
matching pennies games to test on. While this approach helps us detect
overfitting to specific participants or trials, it cannot tell us how
well our model generalizes to truly new contexts. Our test set
participants were recruited from the same population, played the game
under the same conditions, and were influenced by the same experimental
setup as our training participants.

External test sets, in contrast, come from genuinely different contexts.
For our matching pennies model, this might mean testing on games played:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  In different cultures or age groups
\item
  Under time pressure versus relaxed conditions
\item
  For real money versus just for fun
\item
  Against human opponents versus computer agents
\item
  In laboratory versus online settings
\end{itemize}

The distinction matters because cognitive models often capture not just
universal mental processes, but also specific strategies that people
adopt in particular contexts. A model that perfectly predicts behavior
in laboratory matching pennies games might fail entirely when applied to
high-stakes poker games, even though both involve similar strategic
thinking.

This raises deeper questions about what kind of generalization we want
our models to achieve. Are we trying to build models that capture
universal cognitive processes, or are we content with models that work
well within specific contexts? The answer affects not just how we
evaluate our models, but how we design them in the first place.

In practice, truly external test sets are rare in cognitive science -
they require additional data collection under different conditions,
which is often impractical. This means we must be humble about our
claims of generalization. When we talk about a model's predictive
accuracy, we should be clear that we're usually measuring its ability to
generalize within a specific experimental context, not its ability to
capture human cognition in all its diversity.

This limitation of internal test sets is one reason why cognitive
scientists often complement predictive accuracy metrics with other forms
of model evaluation, such as testing theoretical predictions on new
tasks or examining whether model parameters correlate sensibly with
individual differences. These approaches help us build confidence that
our models capture meaningful cognitive processes rather than just
statistical patterns specific to our experimental setup.

\textbf{A better predictive score does not mean a better mechanistic
explanation.} Two models can achieve identical ELPD while making
diametrically opposite claims about the cognitive mechanism generating
the data. If the biased model and the memory model achieve similar
predictive performance, that does not mean the two theories are
equivalent --- it means the data at hand cannot distinguish them. The
correct scientific response is to design experiments targeting the
mechanistic difference: conditions where the two models predict
qualitatively different behavior.

\textbf{The ``wrong model wins'' scenario is real.} When memory agents
are simulated with high \(\sigma_\beta\) (many agents with near-zero
memory sensitivity), the population of memory agents becomes nearly
indistinguishable from a population of biased agents. PSIS-LOO may then
prefer the simpler biased model --- not because memory processes are
absent, but because they are weak, variable, and the simpler model
achieves comparable predictions on average. This is actually a
\emph{correct} finding: it tells you that the empirical conditions were
not sufficiently discriminative, not that the method is broken. The
productive response is to ask what experimental design would make the
two populations more discriminable --- perhaps a richer or more
structured opponent, or a longer game.

\section{Conclusion: Model Comparison as Scientific
Reasoning}\label{conclusion-model-comparison-as-scientific-reasoning}

This chapter developed a complete pipeline for comparing validated
cognitive models:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{PSIS-LOO} approximates leave-one-out cross-validation from a
  single fit, with Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) flagging both numerical
  unreliability and cognitively interesting observations.
\item
  \textbf{Agent-level K-fold CV} provides an honest test of
  generalization to new participants --- answering the question PSIS-LOO
  cannot.
\item
  \textbf{LOO-PIT + POT-C} checks whether the model's predictive
  \emph{uncertainty} is calibrated, not just its mean predictions.
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{loo\_compare()}} produces a Z-score-interpretable
  ranking; below 2 means inconclusive, not that the method failed.
\item
  \textbf{Bayesian stacking} finds the optimal predictive mixture and
  provides a continuous measure of model evidence.
\item
  \textbf{Model SBC} tests the full comparison pipeline's calibration
  across the prior space.
\end{enumerate}

Two design lessons cut across all of these tools: - The \textbf{reversal
design} (validated in Ch. 6, extended in Ch. 7) was essential --- a
constant-bias opponent makes the biased and memory models statistically
inseparable, and no amount of data fixes a confounded design. -
\textbf{All models must pass their individual quality checks before
comparison}. Comparing a well-fitted model against one with divergences
is not a fair scientific test.

Chapter 9 introduces a new model type --- mixture models --- that
extends the toolkit beyond single-process accounts and applies the same
PSIS-LOO machinery to a qualitatively different identifiability problem.

(2019). Visualization in Bayesian workflow. \emph{Journal of the Royal
Statistical Society: Series A}, 182(2), 389-402.

with analytic p-value calculation under arbitrary dependency structures.
