\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Mixture Models}\label{sec-mixture-models}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chs. 1--7
modelled behaviour as arising from a \emph{single} cognitive process per
agent. This chapter relaxes that assumption: behaviour can be a weighted
mixture of distinct processes (e.g., deliberate strategy and attentional
lapses). Mixture models extend the toolkit from Ch. 7 (hierarchical
structures) and Ch. 8 (model comparison via LOO) to this new model
class. The key new challenge is that discrete latent variables --- which
process generated this trial? --- cannot be sampled by HMC and must be
analytically integrated out before any Stan code is written. The full
course roadmap is in Ch. 1, Section ``The Course Roadmap''.
\end{quote}

When we model human cognition and behavior, people do not always follow
a single, consistent strategy. A participant in a matching-pennies game
might sometimes carefully exploit a detected pattern in the opponent's
behavior, and other times respond essentially at random when attention
lapses. Traditional single-process models are structurally misspecified
when this is the case: they attribute lapse-driven variance to the
strategic parameter, inflating its uncertainty and biasing its location.

Mixture models provide an elegant and principled solution. Rather than
assuming behavior reflects one cognitive process, they represent
observations as draws from a \textbf{weighted combination} of component
distributions, each corresponding to a distinct process. This reframes
the question addressed in Chapter 8 --- \emph{which model is correct?}
--- into a richer question: \emph{how much does each process contribute,
and for which observations?}

Because each component must be marginalized analytically before writing
Stan code, mixture models also serve as a concrete introduction to a
class of computational constraint that applies broadly: \textbf{discrete
parameters cannot be sampled by HMC and must be integrated out}.
Mastering this principle is prerequisite for hidden Markov models,
contamination models, and finite mixture clusters.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-7}

After completing this chapter you will be able to:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Specify a mixture model as a marginalized sum of component
  log-likelihoods and articulate \emph{why} marginalization is required
  in Stan.
\item
  Identify and correct the vectorisation error that makes the mixture
  likelihood computationally wrong when component densities are applied
  to arrays.
\item
  Validate a mixture model with SBC, extracting both calibration
  diagnostics and parameter recovery from the same simulation bank.
\item
  Characterize the likelihood-level identifiability constraint that
  limits what binary choice data can tell us about mixture parameters,
  and visualise the posterior aliasing manifold.
\item
  Extend the single-agent model to a hierarchical mixture model with a
  non-centred parameterisation.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Motivation: When are Mixtures
Visible?}\label{motivation-when-are-mixtures-visible}

Before developing the mathematics, consider a concrete case where a
mixture is visually transparent. In many response-time tasks, a
participant sometimes deliberates carefully (producing a log-normal RT
distribution) and sometimes responds inattentively (producing a roughly
uniform RT distribution). The resulting marginal distribution is a
weighted blend.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{n\_obs     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{500}
\NormalTok{pi\_delib  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.80}       \CommentTok{\# 80 \% deliberate}
\NormalTok{mu\_log    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{6.2}        \CommentTok{\# mean of log(RT), deliberate process ≈ 500 ms}
\NormalTok{sigma\_log }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.20}
\NormalTok{min\_lapse }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{200}
\NormalTok{max\_lapse }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2000}

\NormalTok{component }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_obs, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, pi\_delib)  }\CommentTok{\# 1 = deliberate, 0 = lapse}

\NormalTok{rt\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{rt        =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(component }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
                     \FunctionTok{rlnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_obs, mu\_log, sigma\_log),}
                     \FunctionTok{runif}\NormalTok{(n\_obs, min\_lapse, max\_lapse)),}
  \AttributeTok{log\_rt    =} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(rt),}
  \AttributeTok{process   =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(component }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Deliberate"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Lapse"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# RT scale}
\NormalTok{p1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(rt\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ rt)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{after\_stat}\NormalTok{(density)),}
                 \AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey80"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ process, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ process),}
               \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ rt\_data }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(process }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Deliberate"}\NormalTok{),}
               \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_segment}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ min\_lapse, }\AttributeTok{xend =}\NormalTok{ max\_lapse,}
        \AttributeTok{y =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (max\_lapse }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ min\_lapse),}
        \AttributeTok{yend =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (max\_lapse }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ min\_lapse),}
        \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"Lapse"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Deliberate =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{Lapse =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{),}
                      \AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Deliberate =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{Lapse =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{),}
                    \AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{guide =} \StringTok{"none"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{coord\_cartesian}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xlim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Reaction{-}time distribution"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"A mixture of deliberate (log{-}normal) and lapse (uniform) processes"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"RT (ms)"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"top"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Log{-}RT scale}
\NormalTok{p2 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(rt\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ log\_rt)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{after\_stat}\NormalTok{(density)),}
                 \AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey80"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ process, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ process),}
               \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ rt\_data }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(process }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"Deliberate"}\NormalTok{),}
               \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Deliberate =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Deliberate =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{name =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Log{-}RT distribution"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Log transformation clarifies the deliberate component"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"log RT"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"top"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p1 }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_rt-motivation-1.pdf}
\end{center}

In the RT case, the two components have genuinely different support
structures --- the lapse process contributes probability mass across a
1,800 ms window, while the deliberate process is tightly concentrated.
Each observation therefore carries real information about which
component generated it, so the mixture is well-identified from the
marginal distribution alone.

We will return to this point in Section 6. For binary choice data, the
identifiability picture is dramatically different.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Theoretical Framework}\label{theoretical-framework}

\subsection{The Mixture Density}\label{the-mixture-density}

A \(K\)-component mixture model assigns the following marginal density
(or mass function) to each observation \(y_i\):

\[
p(y_i) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, p_k(y_i \mid \theta_k),
\qquad \pi_k \ge 0, \quad \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1.
\tag{1}
\]

Each \(p_k(\cdot \mid \theta_k)\) is a component distribution with its
own parameter vector \(\theta_k\), and \(\pi_k\) is the corresponding
mixing weight. Given independent observations, the joint likelihood is

\[
p(y \mid \pi, \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, p_k(y_i \mid \theta_k).
\tag{2}
\]

The log-likelihood therefore contains a \textbf{sum inside a log} ---
this cannot be factored into independent additive contributions per
component, which makes mixture models more computationally demanding
than standard regression.

\subsection{The Latent-Variable
Augmentation}\label{the-latent-variable-augmentation}

An equivalent and conceptually useful representation introduces a latent
component indicator \(z_i \in \{1, \ldots, K\}\):

\[
z_i \sim \text{Categorical}(\boldsymbol{\pi}), \qquad
y_i \mid z_i = k \sim p_k(y_i \mid \theta_k).
\tag{3}
\]

If we could observe \(z_i\) the model would decompose into \(K\)
separate standard likelihoods. Unfortunately \(z_i\) is unobserved, so
we sum (integrate) over all possible component assignments to recover
equation (1). This is the \textbf{marginalisation} step.

\subsection{Why Stan Requires
Marginalisation}\label{why-stan-requires-marginalisation}

Stan's Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampler operates on a continuous,
differentiable log posterior. It cannot directly sample discrete random
variables such as \(z_i\). \textbf{Marginalising \(z_i\) before writing
any Stan code is not optional; it is a structural requirement.} The
joint model \((z_i, y_i)\) must be reduced to the marginal model
\(p(y_i)\) expressed entirely through continuous parameters.

Stan provides \texttt{log\_mix()} as a numerically stable implementation
of the per-observation mixture contribution:

\[
\texttt{log\_mix}(\pi, \ell_1, \ell_2) \;=\; \log\!\bigl[\pi \, e^{\ell_1} + (1-\pi) \, e^{\ell_2}\bigr],
\tag{4}
\]

where \(\ell_1 = \log p_1(y_i \mid \theta_1)\) and
\(\ell_2 = \log p_2(y_i \mid \theta_2)\).

\subsection{Our Running Model: Bias--Noise Mixture for Binary
Choices}\label{our-running-model-biasnoise-mixture-for-binary-choices}

We model binary choices as a mixture of a \textbf{biased process} and a
\textbf{random process}:

\[
y_i \sim (1-\pi)\,\text{Bernoulli}(\theta) + \pi\,\text{Bernoulli}(0.5),
\tag{5}
\]

where \(\theta = \text{logit}^{-1}(\alpha)\) is the probability of
choosing ``right'' under the biased process, and
\(\pi = \text{logit}^{-1}(\nu)\) is the probability that any given trial
is generated by the random process. Both are modelled on the logit
scale:

\[
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(0,\, 1), \qquad
\nu \sim \text{Normal}(-1,\, 1).
\tag{6}
\]

The centring of \(\nu\) at \(-1\) weakly favours noise below 50 \%,
reflecting the prior belief that most trials are deliberate.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Prior Predictive Check}\label{prior-predictive-check-1}

Before writing any Stan code, we simulate from the prior to verify that
the implied distribution over observable behaviour is scientifically
plausible.

\begin{quote}
Why check priors before writing Stan code? A prior that assigns
\textgreater30\% mass to noise rates above π = 0.5 means we expect most
trials to be random --- a scientifically implausible starting point that
would corrupt the posterior before data are ever observed.
\end{quote}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{n\_prior }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{10000}

\NormalTok{prior\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{alpha    =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_prior, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,  }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{nu       =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_prior, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{theta    =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha),}
  \AttributeTok{pi\_noise =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(nu),}
  \CommentTok{\# Marginalised effective probability of choosing "right"}
  \AttributeTok{theta\_eff =}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(nu)) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(nu)}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_bias }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{after\_stat}\NormalTok{(density)),}
                 \AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior on "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ theta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{" (bias)"}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Normal(0, 1) on logit scale"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(theta), }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{p\_noise }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ pi\_noise)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{after\_stat}\NormalTok{(density)),}
                 \AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior on "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pi }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{" (noise weight)"}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Normal({-}1, 1) on logit scale"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(pi), }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{p\_eff }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta\_eff)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{after\_stat}\NormalTok{(density)),}
                 \AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{60}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey40"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior on "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ theta[eff] }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{" = (1{-}"} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pi }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{")"} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ theta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{" + 0.5"} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pi),}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Marginalised effective choice probability"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(theta[eff]), }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{(p\_bias }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ p\_noise) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_eff}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_prior-predictive-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The prior on \(\theta\) is diffuse but centred at 0.5, spanning the full
plausible range of biases. The noise prior concentrates most mass below
\(\pi = 0.5\), reflecting the scientific expectation that participants
are mostly deliberate. The marginalised effective probability
\(\theta_{\text{eff}}\) is correspondingly diffuse but slightly shrunk
toward 0.5. These are all scientifically defensible starting points.

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Note:} If you found that the noise prior placed more than ≈ 30
\% of its mass above \(\pi = 0.4\), that would indicate the prior is too
permissive. This is the value of checking the prior predictive before
fitting: it costs nothing and can prevent days of debugging a
misspecified model.
\end{quote}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Stan Implementation: Single-Agent
Model}\label{stan-implementation-single-agent-model}

\subsection{A Critical Vectorisation
Trap}\label{a-critical-vectorisation-trap}

The Stan code in the original version of this chapter contained a subtle
but consequential error. It called:

\begin{verbatim}
target += log_sum_exp(
  log(pi) + bernoulli_logit_lpmf(h | 0),
  log(1-pi) + bernoulli_logit_lpmf(h | bias)
);
\end{verbatim}

where \texttt{h} is a \textbf{vector}.
\texttt{bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h\ \textbar{}\ 0)} with a vector
\texttt{h} returns the \textbf{scalar} \(\sum_i \log p_1(y_i)\) --- the
joint log-probability of all observations under component 1.
\texttt{log\_sum\_exp} then computes:

\[
\log\!\Bigl[\pi \prod_i p_1(y_i) \;+\; (1-\pi) \prod_i p_2(y_i)\Bigr].
\]

This is not a mixture model. It is a model in which \textbf{all \(n\)
observations simultaneously come from one component or the other} ---
equivalent to a global discrete model-selection variable, not per-trial
latent class assignment. The correct log-likelihood is:

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log\!\Bigl[\pi \, p_1(y_i) + (1-\pi) \, p_2(y_i)\Bigr],
\]

which requires iterating \texttt{log\_mix()} \textbf{inside a loop over
observations}.

\subsection{The Correct Stan Model}\label{the-correct-stan-model}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Technique --- Marginalizing Discrete Parameters with
\texttt{log\_mix}}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Stan's HMC sampler requires a \textbf{continuous, differentiable}
log-posterior. A latent component indicator \(z_i \in \{0, 1\}\) (which
component generated trial \(i\)) is \textbf{discrete} --- HMC cannot
sample it directly.

The solution is \textbf{analytic marginalization}: instead of sampling
\(z_i\), we sum its log-probability over both possible values. For a
two-component mixture this is exactly what \texttt{log\_mix} does:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ log\_mix(pi,          }\CommentTok{// mixing weight (probability of component 1)}
\NormalTok{                  lp1,         }\CommentTok{// log p(obs | component 1)}
\NormalTok{                  lp2);        }\CommentTok{// log p(obs | component 2)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

which computes \(\log\!\left[\pi \cdot p_1 + (1-\pi) \cdot p_2\right]\)
in a numerically stable way.

\textbf{Block placement note.} The per-observation \texttt{log\_mix} sum
lives inside the \texttt{model} block as a local computation --- it
contributes to \texttt{target} but \texttt{z\_i} is never stored. The
parameters we do sample (\texttt{alpha}, \texttt{nu}) are continuous and
HMC can take gradients through them. In \texttt{generated\ quantities},
we can \emph{recover} posterior expectations of \(z_i\) via a Bayes-rule
calculation if needed, but we do not sample it.

\textbf{Why the loop is necessary here.} Unlike a pure Bernoulli
likelihood, \texttt{log\_mix} operates on a single observation at a
time. There is no vectorized Stan function for mixture likelihoods
across observations (because each call is itself a sum of two terms that
depend on the observation), so the explicit \texttt{for\ (i\ in\ 1:n)}
loop is correct and unavoidable for the single-subject model. For the
multilevel model we will see how to structure this loop across subjects
to enable future parallelization.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{single\_mixture\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_alpha\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_alpha\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_nu\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_nu\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real alpha;        // logit{-}scale bias (biased component)}
\StringTok{  real nu;           // logit{-}scale mixing weight (random component)}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  real noise\_p = inv\_logit(nu);}

\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(alpha | prior\_alpha\_mu, prior\_alpha\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(nu    | prior\_nu\_mu,    prior\_nu\_sigma);}

\StringTok{  // Marginalise the discrete component indicator analytically via log\_mix.}
\StringTok{  // One call per observation — vectorisation is not available for log\_mix.}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:n)}
\StringTok{    target += log\_mix(noise\_p,}
\StringTok{                      bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | 0.0),}
\StringTok{                      bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | alpha));}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} theta   = inv\_logit(alpha);}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} pi\_hat  = inv\_logit(nu);}
\StringTok{  real lprior = normal\_lpdf(alpha | prior\_alpha\_mu, prior\_alpha\_sigma)}
\StringTok{              + normal\_lpdf(nu    | prior\_nu\_mu,    prior\_nu\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  vector[n] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int pred\_choice;}
\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    for (i in 1:n) \{}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[i] = log\_mix(pi\_hat,}
\StringTok{                           bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | 0.0),}
\StringTok{                           bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | alpha));}
\StringTok{      if (bernoulli\_rng(pi\_hat))}
\StringTok{        pred\_choice[i] = bernoulli\_rng(0.5);}
\StringTok{      else}
\StringTok{        pred\_choice[i] = bernoulli\_rng(theta);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(single\_mixture\_stan, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch09\_mixture\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch09_mixture_single.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{"stan/ch09\_mixture\_single.stan"}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Data}\label{data}

We use the simulated matching-pennies data introduced in earlier
chapters, selecting the agent with rate 0.8 and noise 0.1. This gives us
a ground truth against which to assess recovery: \(\theta = 0.8\),
\(\pi = 0.1\).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{d    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{read\_csv}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simdata"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch05\_randomnoise.csv"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{show\_col\_types =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{dd   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(true\_rate }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, true\_noise }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{n    =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(dd),}
  \AttributeTok{h    =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(dd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice),}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_alpha\_mu    =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,   }\AttributeTok{prior\_alpha\_sigma =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_nu\_mu       =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,  }\AttributeTok{prior\_nu\_sigma    =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics   =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Trials:"}\NormalTok{, data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n, }\StringTok{"| Proportion \textquotesingle{}1\textquotesingle{}:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Trials: 120 | Proportion '1': 0.775 
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Fitting with Pathfinder
Initialisation}\label{fitting-with-pathfinder-initialisation}

Mixture model posteriors can have multimodal or ridge-shaped geometry
(see Section 6). Pathfinder --- a multi-path variational approximation
--- provides stable starting points and avoids the Uniform(−2, 2)
default initialisation that often fails when parameters are on very
different scales or interact.

If Pathfinder itself fails (e.g., because the variational approximation
diverges near the ridge), the fallback must not be \texttt{NULL}.
Passing \texttt{NULL} to \texttt{init} triggers Stan's Uniform(−2, 2)
default, which places chains at random points that may lie far from the
high-probability region and immediately generate divergent transitions.
Instead we define an explicit per-chain jittered initialisation centred
on the prior medians: \(\alpha = 0\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0.5\)) and
\(\nu = -1\) (i.e., \(\pi \approx 0.27\)). A small Gaussian jitter of SD
0.1 ensures chains start at distinct points, which is necessary for
\(\hat{R}\) to be meaningful.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{single\_fit\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simmodels/ch09\_single\_mixture.RDS"}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(single\_fit\_file)) \{}

  \CommentTok{\# Explicit per{-}chain jittered initialisation at prior medians.}
  \CommentTok{\# Used as Pathfinder fallback; safe for ridge{-}shaped posteriors.}
\NormalTok{  safe\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) }\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{alpha =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{nu    =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ))}

\NormalTok{  init\_pf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    mod\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{pathfinder}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{data      =}\NormalTok{ data,}
      \AttributeTok{seed      =} \DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{num\_paths =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{refresh   =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{    ),}
    \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) \{}
      \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Pathfinder failed — falling back to jittered prior{-}centre initialisation."}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      safe\_init}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  fit\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data            =}\NormalTok{ data,}
    \AttributeTok{init            =}\NormalTok{ init\_pf,}
    \AttributeTok{seed            =} \DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains          =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup     =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling   =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh         =} \DecValTok{500}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(single\_fit\_file)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(single\_fit\_file)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"pi\_hat"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 4 x 10
  variable   mean median     sd    mad      q5    q95  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail
  <chr>     <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 alpha     1.58   1.52  0.417  0.380   1.00    2.36   1.00    3114.    2450.
2 nu       -1.55  -1.48  0.745  0.734  -2.87   -0.465  1.00    2943.    2738.
3 theta     0.821  0.821 0.0547 0.0562  0.732   0.914  1.00    3114.    2450.
4 pi_hat    0.198  0.185 0.104  0.109   0.0536  0.386  1.00    2943.    2738.
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Geometry Diagnostics}\label{geometry-diagnostics}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{draws\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# Required diagnostic thresholds (cheatsheet):}
\CommentTok{\# Divergences = 0 | R{-}hat \textless{} 1.01 | Bulk \& Tail ESS \textgreater{} 400 | E{-}BFMI \textgreater{} 0.2}
\NormalTok{diag }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Divergences:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(diag}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{num\_divergent), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Divergences: 0 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Max R{-}hat:  "}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rhat, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

\end{verbatim}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Min ESS:    "}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ess\_bulk, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Trace plots for the two sampled parameters}
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_trace}\NormalTok{(draws\_single, }\AttributeTok{pars =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ggtitle}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Trace plots: logit{-}scale parameters"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"mako"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{begin =} \FloatTok{0.15}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{end =} \FloatTok{0.85}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{guide =} \StringTok{"none"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
