\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Pairwise geometry on the probability scale — reveals ridge if present}
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_pairs}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(),}
  \AttributeTok{pars      =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"theta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"pi\_hat"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{off\_diag\_fun =} \StringTok{"scatter"}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{off\_diag\_args =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Posterior geometry: bias × noise"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"A ridge along the aliasing manifold is expected for binary data (see Section 6)"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
NULL
\end{verbatim}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Validation: Simulation-Based Calibration
Checks}\label{validation-simulation-based-calibration-checks}

SBC simultaneously certifies two properties. When rank statistics are
uniform, the sampler is \textbf{computationally unbiased} (calibration).
The joint distribution of true values and posterior summaries across all
SBC datasets provides \textbf{parameter recovery} --- the same
information conveyed by a recovery grid, but derived from a principled
random draw over the full prior rather than a hand-chosen set of grid
points.

\subsection{Generator and Backend}\label{generator-and-backend}

\begin{quote}
Why SBC for mixture models specifically? Mixture posteriors are often
ridge-shaped or multimodal, which means the sampler can fail silently
--- producing low divergences but biased posteriors. SBC's
rank-uniformity test catches this structural failure that standard
diagnostics miss.
\end{quote}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbc\_gen\_fn }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{() \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Draw from prior (logit scale — must match Stan parameter names exactly)}
\NormalTok{  alpha\_true }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  nu\_true    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{  theta\_true }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha\_true)}
\NormalTok{  pi\_true    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(nu\_true)}

\NormalTok{  n }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{120}
\NormalTok{  component }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, pi\_true)}
\NormalTok{  h }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    component }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
    \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, theta\_true)}
\NormalTok{  ))}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_true, }\AttributeTok{nu =}\NormalTok{ nu\_true),}
    \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =}\NormalTok{ n, }\AttributeTok{h =}\NormalTok{ h)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{sbc\_generator }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(sbc\_gen\_fn)}

\NormalTok{sbc\_backend }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  mod\_single,}
  \AttributeTok{chains        =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup   =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{refresh       =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{show\_messages =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbc\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simmodels/ch09\_sbc\_results.RDS"}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sbc\_file)) \{}
\NormalTok{  future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{multisession, }\AttributeTok{workers =} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, parallel}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{detectCores}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))}

  \CommentTok{\# S = 1000 certifies the rank statistic at the 95 \% level.}
  \CommentTok{\# For development, reduce to 200.}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_datasets }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_generator, }\AttributeTok{n\_sims =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(sbc\_datasets, sbc\_backend)}

\NormalTok{  future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(future}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{sequential)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results, sbc\_file)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sbc\_file)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Calibration: ECDF-Difference
Plots}\label{calibration-ecdf-difference-plots}

The primary calibration diagnostic is the ECDF-difference plot
(Säilynoja et al.~2022). A well-calibrated sampler produces rank
statistics that are uniform on \(\{0, 1, \ldots, S\}\), so the ECDF
should fall along the horizontal zero line. Deviations outside the 95 \%
envelope indicate structural bias or over/underconfidence.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"SBC calibration: ECDF{-}difference plots"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Deviations outside the grey band indicate a miscalibrated sampler"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{strip.background =} \FunctionTok{element\_rect}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey95"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{strip.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_sbc-ecdf-diff-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plot\_rank\_hist}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"SBC calibration: rank histograms"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"U{-}shape → overconfident; hump → underconfident; skew → biased"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{strip.background =} \FunctionTok{element\_rect}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey95"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{strip.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_sbc-rank-hist-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Parameter Recovery}\label{parameter-recovery}

The \texttt{stats} component of the SBC output contains the
\texttt{simulated\_value} (the true prior draw) alongside posterior
summaries for each simulation. Plotting posterior mean against true
value directly provides the recovery assessment --- no separate
grid-based recovery study is required.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{recovery\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{stats }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{param\_label =}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{recode}\NormalTok{(variable,}
      \AttributeTok{alpha =} \StringTok{"alpha (logit bias)"}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{nu    =} \StringTok{"nu (logit noise)"}
\NormalTok{    ),}
    \CommentTok{\# Calibration coverage: does the 95 \% CrI contain the truth?}
    \AttributeTok{covered\_95 =}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\NormalTok{ q5 }\SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}=}\NormalTok{ q95}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Coverage summary}
\NormalTok{recovery\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(param\_label) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{coverage\_95 =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(covered\_95),}
    \AttributeTok{rmse        =} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{((mean }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 2 x 3
  param_label        coverage_95  rmse
  <chr>                    <dbl> <dbl>
1 alpha (logit bias)       0.893 0.403
2 nu (logit noise)         0.897 0.953
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Recovery scatter: posterior mean vs. true value}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ covered\_95), }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_linerange}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95, }\AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ covered\_95),}
                 \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
              \AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"black"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{param\_label, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"TRUE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"FALSE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{name   =} \StringTok{"95 \% CrI covers truth"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Parameter recovery from SBC simulations"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Each point is one SBC dataset; dashed line = perfect recovery (y = x)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"True value (prior draw)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Posterior mean"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{legend.position    =} \StringTok{"top"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{strip.background   =} \FunctionTok{element\_rect}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey95"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{strip.text         =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{panel.grid.major   =} \FunctionTok{element\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"grey92"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_sbc-recovery-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Recovery should be unbiased across the full prior range. The fraction of
95 \% credible intervals covering the truth should be close to 0.95.
Systematic deviations --- for instance, the noise parameter \(\nu\)
recovering poorly near \(\nu \approx -2\) (i.e., \(\pi \approx 0.12\))
--- would indicate that 120 trials provide insufficient information to
distinguish very low noise from zero noise, a legitimate finding about
the experimental design, not a modelling failure.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Identifiability: The Bernoulli Aliasing
Problem}\label{identifiability-the-bernoulli-aliasing-problem}

\subsection{The Algebraic Constraint}\label{the-algebraic-constraint}

\begin{quote}
Why prove identifiability analytically before checking it empirically?
The aliasing manifold exists regardless of sample size or model quality.
No amount of data or better priors can break a fundamental algebraic
degeneracy. Understanding the constraint analytically tells us exactly
what additional information (RT, condition manipulation) would be
required --- rather than leaving us puzzled when LOO shows no
preference.
\end{quote}

For a single binary trial the probability of observing \(y_i = 1\) under
the bias--noise mixture is:

\[
P(y_i = 1) = (1 - \pi)\,\theta + \pi \cdot 0.5 = \theta - \pi(\theta - 0.5).
\tag{7}
\]

This depends on \((\theta, \pi)\) only through the linear combination
\(\theta_{\text{eff}} = (1-\pi)\theta + 0.5\pi\). For i.i.d. binary
observations, the \textbf{entire dataset} carries information only about
this one-dimensional quantity. Consequently, infinitely many
\((\theta, \pi)\) pairs are observationally equivalent: for any fixed
observed proportion \(\bar{y}\), the set

\[
\bigl\{(\theta, \pi) : (1-\pi)\,\theta + 0.5\,\pi = \bar{y}\bigr\}
\tag{8}
\]

is a curve in parameter space, and the posterior will be smeared along
this curve. This is the \textbf{aliasing manifold}.

The mixture model is not wrong --- it is simply not identified from the
marginal binary likelihood alone. The bias and noise parameters are
individually interpretable and scientifically meaningful; the data just
cannot separate them without additional structure (e.g., trial-level
covariates, joint RT and accuracy modelling, or experimental
manipulations of the noise rate).

\subsection{Visualising the Posterior
Ridge}\label{visualising-the-posterior-ridge}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{draws\_prob }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(theta, pi\_hat)}

\NormalTok{obs\_prop   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h)}

\CommentTok{\# Aliasing manifold: all (theta, pi) consistent with observed proportion}
\NormalTok{pi\_grid    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.002}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{theta\_mani }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ (obs\_prop }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pi\_grid) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ pi\_grid)}
\NormalTok{manifold   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{pi\_hat =}\NormalTok{ pi\_grid, }\AttributeTok{theta =}\NormalTok{ theta\_mani) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(theta }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, theta }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}=} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(draws\_prob, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ pi\_hat, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ theta)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.06}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ manifold, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \FloatTok{0.45}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \FloatTok{0.92}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{hjust =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{label =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{hat}\NormalTok{(theta)[eff] }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FunctionTok{bar}\NormalTok{(y))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Posterior geometry and the aliasing manifold"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Red curve: all (π, θ) pairs consistent with the observed choice proportion"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(pi }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"(noise weight)"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(theta }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"(bias)"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{coord\_cartesian}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xlim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{ylim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_aliasing-manifold-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The posterior concentrates along the red aliasing manifold rather than
converging to a point. This is not a sampling pathology --- it is the
correct Bayesian representation of what the data actually contain. The
prior does provide some regularisation (the posterior does not extend to
the full manifold), but with 120 binary observations and diffuse priors,
the two parameters remain substantially correlated.

\subsection{Why the RT Case is
Different}\label{why-the-rt-case-is-different}

Recall from Section 1 that the RT mixture was visually transparent. The
reason is that the two components have \textbf{different likelihoods for
the same observation value}. A 400 ms response is extremely unlikely
under the uniform lapse process but highly probable under the log-normal
deliberate process. This trial-level likelihood contrast provides
information about \(z_i\) for each observation, breaking the aliasing.
With binary choices, both the biased process (at some \(\theta\)) and
the random process (at 0.5) assign non-zero probability to both
outcomes, so the aliasing cannot be broken by observation values alone.

This comparison motivates a practical principle: if you want a mixture
model's components to be individually identified, ensure that the
components are distinguishable at the level of individual observations,
not merely at the level of marginal statistics. Two strategies
accomplish this in practice.

\textbf{Strategy A: Joint modelling of complementary outcome types.} If
reaction time \(rt_i\) is observed alongside each binary choice \(y_i\),
the per-trial joint likelihood becomes:

\[
p(y_i, rt_i) = (1-\pi)\; p_{\text{bias}}(y_i \mid \theta)\; p_{\text{LN}}(rt_i \mid \mu, \sigma) \;+\; \pi\; p_{\text{rand}}(y_i \mid 0.5)\; p_{\text{unif}}(rt_i \mid a, b).
\tag{8a}
\]

Because
\(p_{\text{LN}}(rt_i \mid \mu, \sigma) / p_{\text{unif}}(rt_i \mid a, b)\)
varies continuously with \(rt_i\), every observation now provides direct
evidence about \(z_i\). A short RT carries high likelihood under the
log-normal deliberate component and low likelihood under the uniform
lapse component, or vice versa for an unusually long RT. The aliasing
manifold collapses to a point: the two-dimensional parameter space
\((\theta, \pi)\) is identified by the joint data stream, not just the
marginal choice proportion.

\textbf{Strategy B: Experimental manipulation of the noise rate.}
Suppose participants complete a single-task block (\(c_i = 0\)) and a
dual-task block (\(c_i = 1\)), where the additional cognitive load is
expected to increase lapse frequency but leave the deliberate bias
intact. We model \(\pi\) as a function of condition while holding
\(\theta\) constant:

\[
\nu_i = \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 c_i, \qquad \pi_i = \text{logit}^{-1}(\nu_i).
\tag{8b}
\]

The aliasing constraint now applies separately within each condition:

\[
(1 - \pi_0)\,\theta + 0.5\,\pi_0 = \bar{y}_0, \qquad
(1 - \pi_1)\,\theta + 0.5\,\pi_1 = \bar{y}_1.
\tag{8c}
\]

These are two linear equations in the three unknowns
\((\theta, \pi_0, \pi_1)\) --- or equivalently
\((\theta, \gamma_0, \gamma_1)\). If \(\pi_0 \ne \pi_1\) (i.e., if the
dual-task manipulation actually changes the noise rate), the two
manifolds intersect at a unique point and all three parameters are
identified. The identifiability here does not require RT data at all:
the experimental design does the work by breaking the single-condition
degeneracy.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Prior Sensitivity Analysis}\label{prior-sensitivity-analysis}

Section 6 established analytically that the binary likelihood cannot
separate \(\theta\) and \(\pi\): the data constrain only
\(\theta_{\text{eff}} = (1-\pi)\theta + 0.5\pi\). A direct corollary is
that the posterior for at least one of the individual parameters must be
sensitive to the prior. \texttt{priorsense::powerscale\_sensitivity()}
quantifies this sensitivity without refitting the model. It uses
Pareto-smoothed importance sampling to evaluate the perturbed posterior:

\[
p_{\alpha,\beta}(\theta, \pi \mid y) \propto p(y \mid \theta, \pi)^{\alpha}\; p(\theta, \pi)^{\beta},
\tag{11}
\]

where \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are power-scaling factors. Sensitivity to
the prior corresponds to varying \(\beta\) while holding \(\alpha = 1\);
sensitivity to the likelihood corresponds to varying \(\alpha\) while
holding \(\beta = 1\). The summary statistic \(\hat{\psi}\) measures how
much the posterior mean shifts (in units of posterior SD) as the scaling
factor departs from 1. Values above \(\approx 0.05\) indicate
non-negligible sensitivity.

The \texttt{priorsense} workflow requires a \texttt{lprior} variable in
the Stan model's \texttt{generated\ quantities} block, which accumulates
the log prior density. This was added to the single-agent Stan model in
§4.2 and will be added to the multilevel model in §8.2. The
\texttt{powerscale\_sensitivity()} call then reads \texttt{lprior} as
the prior contribution and uses \texttt{log\_lik} as the likelihood
contribution.

\begin{quote}
Why expect high sensitivity for the noise parameter? Section 6 showed
algebraically that binary i.i.d. data constrain only θ\_eff = (1-π)θ +
0.5π. The noise weight π therefore receives almost no direct information
from the data --- its posterior is largely proportional to its prior. A
high ψ̂ for ν is not a model failure; it is the quantitative fingerprint
of this degeneracy.
\end{quote}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# powerscale\_sensitivity works with CmdStanFit objects via the posterior package.}
\CommentTok{\# It reads \textquotesingle{}lprior\textquotesingle{} and \textquotesingle{}log\_lik\textquotesingle{} from generated quantities automatically.}
\NormalTok{sensitivity }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_sensitivity}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_single,}
  \AttributeTok{variable =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(sensitivity)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Sensitivity based on cjs_dist
Prior selection: all priors
Likelihood selection: all data

 variable prior likelihood                                diagnosis
    alpha 0.140      0.063            potential prior-data conflict
       nu 0.104      0.027 potential strong prior / weak likelihood
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Density deformation under power{-}scaling shows which parameters are affected.}
\NormalTok{priorsense}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{powerscale\_plot\_dens}\NormalTok{(fit\_single, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"alpha"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"nu"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prior sensitivity: power{-}scaled posterior densities"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}
      \StringTok{"Stronger deformation = greater dependence on the prior"}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{strip.background =} \FunctionTok{element\_rect}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"grey95"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{strip.text       =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{legend.position  =} \StringTok{"bottom"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_priorsense-plot-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The expected pattern is instructive: \(\hat{\psi}\) for \(\nu\) (noise,
logit scale) should be substantially higher than \(\hat{\psi}\) for
\(\alpha\) (bias, logit scale). This is the quantitative signature of
the aliasing manifold. The bias \(\theta\) is constrained by the data
through \(\theta_{\text{eff}} \approx \bar{y}\), so the posterior mean
of \(\alpha\) is relatively stable under prior perturbation. The noise
weight \(\pi\), by contrast, receives almost no direct information from
binary i.i.d. data, so its posterior is nearly proportional to its
prior. A strong \(\hat{\psi}\) for \(\nu\) is therefore not a modelling
failure --- it is an empirical confirmation of the §6 identifiability
analysis and should be reported alongside it.

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Workflow note.} If \texttt{powerscale\_sensitivity()} returns a
Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) warning for the prior scaling direction, it signals
that the prior and likelihood are in strong conflict --- the perturbed
posterior is too far from the fitted posterior to be reliably
approximated by importance sampling. In that case, refit with scaled
priors explicitly. For the noise parameter under typical experimental
conditions this warning is unlikely because the prior is doing most of
the work; conflict warnings are more characteristic of well-identified
parameters with informative priors.
\end{quote}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Posterior Predictive Checks and
LOO}\label{posterior-predictive-checks-and-loo}

\subsection{Posterior Predictive
Checks}\label{posterior-predictive-checks}

Posterior predictive draws are computed in the
\texttt{generated\ quantities} block and extracted directly from the
fitted object. This avoids the manual simulation loop in the original
chapter, which re-implemented in R what Stan already computed.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# S x n matrix of posterior predictive choices (0/1)}
\NormalTok{yrep }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"pred\_choice"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{y    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{h)}

\CommentTok{\# PPC 1: proportion of "1" choices}
\NormalTok{p\_mean }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ bayesplot}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{ppc\_stat}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{y    =}\NormalTok{ y,}
  \AttributeTok{yrep =}\NormalTok{ yrep,}
  \AttributeTok{stat =} \StringTok{"mean"}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"PPC: proportion of right choices"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Mean choice"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# PPC 2: mean run length — sensitive to serial structure}
\NormalTok{obs\_run  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rle}\NormalTok{(y)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{lengths)}
\NormalTok{pred\_run }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{apply}\NormalTok{(yrep, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(x) }\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rle}\NormalTok{(x)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{lengths))}

\NormalTok{p\_run }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{run =}\NormalTok{ pred\_run), }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ run)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{after\_stat}\NormalTok{(density)),}
                 \AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{40}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =}\NormalTok{ obs\_run, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ obs\_run }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \ConstantTok{Inf}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{vjust =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{label =} \StringTok{"Observed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{hjust =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"PPC: mean run length"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Sensitive to sequential autocorrelation the mean cannot detect"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Mean run length"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_mean }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_run}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_ppc-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Interpretation}: If the run-length check fails (observed value
in the tail of the predictive distribution) while the mean check passes,
the data contain sequential dependence that a fully i.i.d. mixture model
cannot reproduce. In that case, consider a hidden Markov model with
Markov-dependent \(z_i\) (Section 10).
\end{quote}

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{PSIS-LOO and Pareto-\(\hat{k}\)
Diagnostics}{PSIS-LOO and Pareto-\textbackslash hat\{k\} Diagnostics}}\label{psis-loo-and-pareto-hatk-diagnostics}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{log\_lik\_mat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"matrix"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{r\_eff       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{relative\_eff}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_lik\_mat),}
                                  \AttributeTok{chain\_id =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{each =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{loo\_single  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{(log\_lik\_mat, }\AttributeTok{r\_eff =}\NormalTok{ r\_eff, }\AttributeTok{save\_psis =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(loo\_single)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

Computed from 8000 by 120 log-likelihood matrix.

         Estimate   SE
elpd_loo    -65.0  5.3
p_loo         0.9  0.1
looic       130.0 10.5
------
MCSE of elpd_loo is 0.0.
MCSE and ESS estimates assume MCMC draws (r_eff in [0.9, 0.9]).

All Pareto k estimates are good (k < 0.7).
See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details.
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{plot}\NormalTok{(loo\_single, }\AttributeTok{label\_points =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{,}
     \AttributeTok{main =} \StringTok{"PSIS{-}LOO Pareto{-}k diagnostics"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_loo-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Pareto-\(\hat{k}\) values above 0.7 indicate observations for which the
full-data posterior is a poor approximation to the leave-one-out
posterior. In a mixture model, such observations are often concentrated
near \(y = 0.5\) (where the biased and random components are most
confounded) or in long runs of the same choice (which the i.i.d. model
cannot anticipate).

\subsection{LOO-PIT}\label{loo-pit}

For continuous outcomes, LOO-PIT values should be uniform. For binary
outcomes the CDF is a step function, so the standard PIT is discrete and
requires randomisation to achieve uniformity. The
\texttt{rstantools}::loo\_pit()` function applies this randomisation
automatically.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{psis\_obj }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{psis\_object}
\NormalTok{lw       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{weights}\NormalTok{(psis\_obj, }\AttributeTok{log =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{normalize =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)  }\CommentTok{\# draws × obs}

\NormalTok{yrep\_int }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(yrep), }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(yrep))}
\NormalTok{pit\_vals }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ rstantools}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{loo\_pit}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{object =}\NormalTok{ yrep\_int, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y, }\AttributeTok{lw =}\NormalTok{ lw)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Multilevel Mixture Model}\label{multilevel-mixture-model}

\subsection{Generative Model}\label{generative-model}

When data come from \(J\) participants, we extend the model
hierarchically. Each participant \(j\) has their own bias \(\alpha_j\)
and noise \(\nu_j\), which are drawn from a bivariate normal population
distribution:

\[
\begin{pmatrix} \alpha_j \\ \nu_j \end{pmatrix}
= \begin{pmatrix} \mu_\alpha \\ \mu_\nu \end{pmatrix}
+ \mathbf{L}\,\boldsymbol{z}_j,
\qquad
\boldsymbol{z}_j \sim \text{Normal}_2(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{I}),
\tag{9}
\]

where \(\mathbf{L} = \text{diag}(\tau) \cdot \mathbf{L}_\Omega\) is the
Cholesky factor of the parameter covariance matrix, \(\tau\) is the
vector of population standard deviations, and \(\mathbf{L}_\Omega\) is
the Cholesky factor of the correlation matrix. This is the non-centred
parameterisation (NCP) introduced in Chapter 7.

\textbf{Priors:}

\[
\mu_\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(0, 1), \qquad
\mu_\nu \sim \text{Normal}(-1, 0.5),
\] \[
\tau_k \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0, 0.3), \quad k = 1, 2,
\qquad
\mathbf{L}_\Omega \sim \text{LKJ}(2).
\]

\textbf{Per-trial likelihood} (same correction as in Section 4.1):

\[
y_{t,j} \sim \log\text{-mix}\!\bigl(\text{logit}^{-1}(\nu_j),\; \text{Bern}(0.5),\; \text{Bern}(\text{logit}^{-1}(\alpha_j))\bigr).
\tag{10}
\]

\subsection{Stan Code}\label{stan-code}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{multilevel\_mixture\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} trials;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} agents;}
\StringTok{  array[trials, agents] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h;}
\StringTok{  // Priors as data — change without recompiling}
\StringTok{  real prior\_muA\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_muA\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_muN\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_muN\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_tau\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_lkj\_shape;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real muA;}
\StringTok{  real muN;}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}[2] tau;}
\StringTok{  matrix[2, agents] z\_IDs;}
\StringTok{  cholesky\_factor\_corr[2] L\_Omega;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  matrix[agents, 2] IDs = (diag\_pre\_multiply(tau, L\_Omega) * z\_IDs)\textquotesingle{};}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(muA | prior\_muA\_mu,  prior\_muA\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(muN | prior\_muN\_mu,  prior\_muN\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(tau | 0, prior\_tau\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega | prior\_lkj\_shape);}
\StringTok{  target += std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z\_IDs));}

\StringTok{  for (i in 1:agents) \{}
\StringTok{    real noise\_i = inv\_logit(muN + IDs[i, 2]);}
\StringTok{    real alpha\_i = muA + IDs[i, 1];}
\StringTok{    for (t in 1:trials)}
\StringTok{      target += log\_mix(noise\_i,}
\StringTok{                        bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[t, i] | 0.0),}
\StringTok{                        bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[t, i] | alpha\_i));}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} pop\_bias  = inv\_logit(muA);}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} pop\_noise = inv\_logit(muN);}
\StringTok{  corr\_matrix[2] Omega             = multiply\_lower\_tri\_self\_transpose(L\_Omega);}
\StringTok{  real bias\_noise\_corr             = Omega[1, 2];}
\StringTok{  real lprior = normal\_lpdf(muA | prior\_muA\_mu,  prior\_muA\_sigma)}
\StringTok{              + normal\_lpdf(muN | prior\_muN\_mu,  prior\_muN\_sigma)}
\StringTok{              + normal\_lpdf(tau | 0, prior\_tau\_sigma)}
\StringTok{              + lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf(L\_Omega | prior\_lkj\_shape)}
\StringTok{              + std\_normal\_lpdf(to\_vector(z\_IDs));}

\StringTok{  array[agents] real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} agent\_bias;}
\StringTok{  array[agents] real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} agent\_noise;}
\StringTok{  array[trials, agents] real log\_lik;}

\StringTok{  for (i in 1:agents) \{}
\StringTok{    real alpha\_i = muA + IDs[i, 1];}
\StringTok{    real noise\_i = inv\_logit(muN + IDs[i, 2]);}
\StringTok{    agent\_bias[i]  = inv\_logit(alpha\_i);}
\StringTok{    agent\_noise[i] = noise\_i;}
\StringTok{    if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{      for (t in 1:trials)}
\StringTok{        log\_lik[t, i] = log\_mix(noise\_i,}
\StringTok{                                bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[t, i] | 0.0),}
\StringTok{                                bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[t, i] | alpha\_i));}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(multilevel\_mixture\_stan,}
                \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch09\_mixture\_multilevel.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch09_mixture_multilevel.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod\_multi }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{"stan/ch09\_mixture\_multilevel.stan"}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Simulating Multilevel
Data}\label{simulating-multilevel-data}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{801}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{n\_agents }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{30}
\NormalTok{n\_trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{120}

\CommentTok{\# Population parameters (logit scale)}
\NormalTok{muA\_true   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{)   }\CommentTok{\# mean bias ≈ 0.75}
\NormalTok{muN\_true   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.12}\NormalTok{)   }\CommentTok{\# mean noise ≈ 0.12}
\NormalTok{tauA\_true  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.50}
\NormalTok{tauN\_true  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.40}

\CommentTok{\# Individual parameters (logit scale)}
\NormalTok{alpha\_j }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_agents, muA\_true, tauA\_true)}
\NormalTok{nu\_j    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_agents, muN\_true, tauN\_true)}

\CommentTok{\# Generate choice matrix [trials x agents]}
\NormalTok{h\_matrix }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA\_integer\_}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{nrow =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials, }\AttributeTok{ncol =}\NormalTok{ n\_agents)}

\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (j }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_agents)) \{}
\NormalTok{  theta\_j }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha\_j[j])}
\NormalTok{  pi\_j    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(nu\_j[j])}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)) \{}
\NormalTok{    h\_matrix[t, j] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, pi\_j) }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
      \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
    \ControlFlowTok{else}
      \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, theta\_j)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{data\_multi }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{trials            =}\NormalTok{ n\_trials,}
  \AttributeTok{agents            =}\NormalTok{ n\_agents,}
  \AttributeTok{h                 =}\NormalTok{ h\_matrix,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_muA\_mu      =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,    }\AttributeTok{prior\_muA\_sigma   =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_muN\_mu      =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,   }\AttributeTok{prior\_muN\_sigma   =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_tau\_sigma   =} \FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_lkj\_shape   =} \FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics   =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# True individual parameters for later recovery plots}
\NormalTok{true\_params }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{agent     =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_agents),}
  \AttributeTok{alpha\_true =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_j,}
  \AttributeTok{nu\_true    =}\NormalTok{ nu\_j,}
  \AttributeTok{theta\_true =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(alpha\_j),}
  \AttributeTok{pi\_true    =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(nu\_j),}
  \CommentTok{\# No{-}pooling "estimate": raw data mean per agent}
  \AttributeTok{nopool\_theta =} \FunctionTok{colMeans}\NormalTok{(h\_matrix)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Population bias  (prob scale):"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(muA\_true), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Population bias  (prob scale): 0.75 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Population noise (prob scale):"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(muN\_true), }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Population noise (prob scale): 0.12 
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Fitting}\label{fitting}

The same Pathfinder-with-safe-fallback pattern from §4.4 applies here,
with one important difference: the multilevel model has additional
structure that the fallback initialisation must respect. Setting
\texttt{tau} to a small positive value (rather than drawing from the
full half-normal prior) avoids starting near the funnel geometry.
Setting \texttt{z\_IDs} to near-zero ensures all individual parameters
begin at their population means. Setting \texttt{L\_Omega} to the
identity matrix provides a neutral starting correlation. All values are
jittered slightly across chains.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{multi\_fit\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"simmodels/ch09\_multilevel\_mixture.RDS"}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(multi\_fit\_file)) \{}

  \CommentTok{\# Explicit per{-}chain jittered initialisation at prior{-}centre values.}
  \CommentTok{\# tau near zero avoids the NCP funnel; z\_IDs = 0 starts all agents}
  \CommentTok{\# at the population mean; L\_Omega = I provides neutral correlation.}
\NormalTok{  safe\_init\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) }\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{muA     =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,  }\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{muN     =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{tau     =} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.02}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{z\_IDs   =} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ n\_agents, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, n\_agents),}
    \AttributeTok{L\_Omega =} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ))}

\NormalTok{  init\_pf\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    mod\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{pathfinder}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ data\_multi, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{801}\NormalTok{,}
                         \AttributeTok{num\_paths =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) \{}
      \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Pathfinder failed — falling back to jittered prior{-}centre initialisation."}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      safe\_init\_m}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  fit\_multi }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data            =}\NormalTok{ data\_multi,}
    \AttributeTok{init            =}\NormalTok{ init\_pf\_m,}
    \AttributeTok{seed            =} \DecValTok{801}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains          =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup     =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling   =} \DecValTok{2000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh         =} \DecValTok{500}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  fit\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(multi\_fit\_file)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_multi }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(multi\_fit\_file)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{fit\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"pop\_bias"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"pop\_noise"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"tau"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias\_noise\_corr"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}

  variable       mean median     sd    mad      q5   q95  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail
  <chr>         <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 pop_bias      0.805  0.803 0.0325 0.0312  0.756  0.864  1.00     865.     410.
2 pop_noise     0.205  0.199 0.0637 0.0609  0.111  0.322  1.00     794.     299.
3 tau[1]        0.597  0.590 0.133  0.122   0.398  0.824  1.01     634.     160.
4 tau[2]        0.267  0.220 0.207  0.192   0.0223 0.687  1.01     632.     280.
5 bias_noise_~ -0.119 -0.147 0.433  0.495  -0.785  0.618  1.00    4038.    4740.
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{diag\_m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Divergences:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(diag\_m}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{num\_divergent), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Max R{-}hat:  "}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(fit\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{rhat, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Max R-hat:   1.0106 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Min ESS:    "}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(fit\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{()}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ess\_bulk, }\AttributeTok{na.rm =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Min ESS:     524 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Debugging note.} If you see divergences in the multilevel
mixture model, the most common cause is the noise SD \(\tau_\nu\)
approaching zero, which creates a funnel geometry in the NCP. Tighten
the half-normal prior on \(\tau_\nu\) (e.g., from
\(\text{Half-Normal}(0, 0.3)\) to \(\text{Half-Normal}(0, 0.2)\)) and
check whether the SBC ECDF-difference for \(\nu\) shows a U-shape
(overconfidence). Do \textbf{not} respond by increasing
\texttt{adapt\_delta} without first investigating the parameterisation.
Note that the safe fallback initialisation in §9.4 already places
\texttt{tau} away from zero; if divergences persist despite that, the
prior itself needs tightening.
\end{quote}

\subsection{Partial-Pooling Shrinkage}\label{partial-pooling-shrinkage}

A hallmark of hierarchical models is that individual estimates are
shrunk toward the group mean, with more shrinkage for agents whose data
are less informative. The shrinkage plot below compares the raw
per-agent data mean (no-pooling) with the posterior mean of
\texttt{agent\_bias{[}j{]}} (partial pooling).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{draws\_multi }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as\_draws\_df}\NormalTok{(fit\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{())}

\CommentTok{\# Extract posterior means of individual bias parameters}
\NormalTok{agent\_cols }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"agent\_bias["}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_agents), }\StringTok{"]"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{post\_bias\_means }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_multi }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{all\_of}\NormalTok{(agent\_cols)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{across}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  tidyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(),}
                      \AttributeTok{names\_to  =} \StringTok{"param"}\NormalTok{,}
                      \AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"post\_mean"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    stringr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{str\_extract}\NormalTok{(param, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{d+"}\NormalTok{)))}

\NormalTok{shrinkage\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ true\_params }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(post\_bias\_means, }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{pop\_mean\_est }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(draws\_multi}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pop\_bias)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(shrinkage\_df) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_segment}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ nopool\_theta, }\AttributeTok{xend =}\NormalTok{ post\_mean, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ agent, }\AttributeTok{yend =}\NormalTok{ agent),}
    \AttributeTok{arrow     =} \FunctionTok{arrow}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{length =} \FunctionTok{unit}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.18}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"cm"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{type =} \StringTok{"closed"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{colour    =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{alpha     =} \FloatTok{0.7}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ nopool\_theta, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ agent), }\AttributeTok{shape =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ post\_mean, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ agent), }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#2166AC"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta\_true, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ agent), }\AttributeTok{shape =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#D6604D"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =}\NormalTok{ pop\_mean\_est,}
             \AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"grey50"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Partial{-}pooling shrinkage"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Open circles: no{-}pooling (data mean); blue filled: posterior mean; red ×: true value"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"Bias parameter (probability scale)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Agent"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{08-MixtureModels_files/figure-pdf/ch09_shrinkage-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Agents whose raw data means are far from the group mean are pulled back
more strongly --- they are more likely to be statistical outliers than
genuinely extreme individuals. Note also that the posterior means are
systematically closer to the true values (red crosses) than the raw data
means, confirming that partial pooling improves estimation accuracy.

Recovery}\label{population-parameter-recovery}

\section{Model Comparison}\label{model-comparison}

\subsection{Single-Process Baseline}\label{single-process-baseline}

We fit a simple biased-agent model --- no noise component --- to the
same data used in Section 4. This is the model that emerges when we
constrain \(\pi = 0\).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{biased\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} n;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} h;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real alpha;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(alpha | 0, 1);}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h | alpha);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} theta = inv\_logit(alpha);}
\StringTok{  vector[n] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[n] int pred\_choice;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:n) \{}
\StringTok{    log\_lik[i]    = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | alpha);}
\StringTok{    pred\_choice[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(alpha);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(biased\_stan, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch09\_biased\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{Highlighting}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mixture =}\NormalTok{ loo\_single, }\AttributeTok{biased =}\NormalTok{ loo\_biased),}
  \AttributeTok{method =} \StringTok{"stacking"}
\NormalTok{)}
\FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(weights\_stacking)}

------
        weight
mixture 0.000 
biased  1.000 
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Interpretation}\label{interpretation}

The comparison result should show a near-zero ELPD difference with a
standard error larger than the difference itself. This is the correct
outcome, for reasons established in Section 6. Any
\((1-\pi)\text{Bern}(\theta) + \pi\text{Bern}(0.5)\) mixture is
\textbf{likelihood-equivalent} to \(\text{Bern}(\theta_{\text{eff}})\)
for i.i.d. binary data. The biased model, in effect, estimates
\(\hat{\theta}_{\text{eff}}\) directly. PSIS-LOO has no basis on which
to prefer one parameterisation over the other, and Bayesian stacking
weights will similarly be indeterminate. The prior sensitivity analysis
in Section 7 already made this outcome predictable: high \(\hat{\psi}\)
for the noise parameter is the per-fit signal of the same degeneracy
that manifests here as indistinguishable ELPD.

This finding does \emph{not} mean the mixture model is useless. It means
that to identify the components separately, the analysis requires one of
the structural interventions described in Section 6.3: joint modelling
of RT and accuracy, a between-condition predictor on \(\pi\), or
trial-level covariates that predict component membership. In practice,
the mixture model is scientifically justified by theory and produces
richer parameter estimates that are interpretable under the generative
story --- even when LOO cannot disambiguate models, theoretical
grounding should guide model selection.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Extensions}\label{extensions}

\textbf{Mixtures with \(K > 2\) components.} The framework extends
naturally: replace the scalar mixing weight \(\pi\) with a \(K\)-simplex
\(\boldsymbol{\pi} \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\boldsymbol{\alpha})\) and loop
over \(K\) terms in \texttt{log\_sum\_exp}. The label-switching problem
(components can permute their identities between chains) becomes more
severe as \(K\) grows; post-processing the draws to enforce a canonical
ordering (e.g., \(\theta_1 < \theta_2 < \ldots\)) or placing informative
asymmetric priors on the components is required.

\textbf{Hidden Markov Models.} If the latent component \(z_i\) is not
i.i.d. but follows a first-order Markov chain, the model is an HMC with
a transition matrix. The forward algorithm marginalises over all state
sequences in \(\mathcal{O}(nK^2)\) time, and is implementable in Stan's
\texttt{functions} block. HMMs are the natural model when behavioural
strategies change gradually across trials, as in online learning or
sequential decision-making tasks.

\textbf{Contamination models for RT data.} The deliberate + lapse
structure in Section 1 is a special case of the \emph{contamination
model} (Ratcliff \& Tuerlinckx 2002), widely used in RT modelling. The
deliberate component is typically an ex-Gaussian or a shifted
log-normal; the contamination component is uniform over a
physiologically plausible range. Because the components have different
support structures, the mixture is well-identified even from marginal RT
data alone --- unlike the binary case.

\textbf{Finite mixture clustering.} When the component means
\(\theta_k\) are themselves unknown parameters, the model becomes a
finite mixture cluster model. Each individual is assigned (softly) to
one of \(K\) latent subgroups with distinct cognitive parameters. This
is a principled alternative to post-hoc clustering of posterior means,
and produces coherent uncertainty over cluster membership.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Conclusion}\label{conclusion}

Mixture models are a powerful and principled tool for cognitive
modelling when behaviour reflects more than one underlying process.
Several key lessons from this chapter should inform their use:

\textbf{Discrete parameters must be marginalised before writing Stan
code.} The latent component indicator \(z_i\) cannot be sampled by HMC.
Summing over components using \texttt{log\_mix()} inside a
per-observation loop is not optional. The vectorised form --- passing a
vector to the component density function before calling
\texttt{log\_sum\_exp} --- produces a structurally incorrect likelihood
that models all-or-nothing component assignment rather than per-trial
mixing.

\textbf{Scale consistency inside \texttt{log\_mix()} matters.} Both
component log-likelihoods should be evaluated on the same scale. Use
\texttt{bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h{[}i{]}\ \textbar{}\ 0.0)} for a random
component at 0.5, not
\texttt{bernoulli\_lpmf(h{[}i{]}\ \textbar{}\ 0.5)}. The results are
numerically identical but the logit-scale form is consistent, avoids an
implicit transformation, and keeps the model's parameterisation
coherent.

\textbf{Constrained parameter declarations absorb truncation
automatically.} Declaring
\texttt{vector\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}{[}2{]}\ tau} means Stan
applies the log Jacobian of the constraint transform internally.
Manually subtracting \texttt{normal\_lccdf()} is redundant and adds
unnecessary autodiff nodes. This applies to any constrained parameter:
the truncation correction is only required when placing a truncated
prior on an \emph{unconstrained} quantity.

\textbf{SBC is the right validation tool.} Mixture models are harder to
calibrate than standard regression models because the posterior is often
ridge-shaped and component-specific parameters may be weakly identified.
Running 1,000 SBC datasets and inspecting ECDF-difference plots for both
calibration and parameter recovery is the minimum validation required
before fitting to empirical data. A U-shaped ECDF-difference for the
noise parameter is a common failure mode that signals overconfidence.

\textbf{Prior sensitivity quantifies what identifiability analysis
predicts.} Section 6 showed analytically that \(\pi\) is not identified
from binary i.i.d. data. Section 7's \texttt{priorsense} analysis made
this empirical: a high \(\hat{\psi}\) for the noise logit \(\nu\) and a
low \(\hat{\psi}\) for the bias logit \(\alpha\) is the expected
fingerprint. Reporting both analyses together --- the manifold plot and
the power-scaling sensitivities --- gives the reader a complete picture
of what the data do and do not constrain.

\textbf{Initialisation is not a detail.} Mixture model posteriors can
have ridge or funnel geometry that causes Stan's Uniform(−2, 2) default
initialisation to fail immediately. Use Pathfinder as the primary
initialisation method and define an explicit jittered prior-centre
fallback. For hierarchical models, initialise the population SD vector
\texttt{tau} at small positive values, not at zero or at wide prior
draws.

\textbf{A model that loses a LOO comparison is not necessarily wrong.}
In the binary case, the mixture model and the biased model have
equivalent ELPD because they are parameterisations of the same
likelihood. Theory should guide model choice here, not predictive scores
alone. Model comparison and theoretical justification are complementary,
not competing, sources of evidence. The degeneracy-breaking strategies
in Section 6.3 describe the experimental and modelling decisions that
would, in a future study, make LOO comparison informative.

\textbf{Coming up in Chapter 10:} We leave the matching-pennies domain
entirely and apply the same six-phase workflow to a social learning task
--- the marble task --- where participants integrate direct evidence
about a jar's composition with social information from another person.
This introduces two genuinely new cognitive models (the Simple and
Weighted Bayesian Agents), a Beta-Binomial likelihood, and the
Leave-Future-Out cross-validation technique needed when trial order
creates causal dependencies that standard LOO cannot handle.

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Reading Other Minds: Theory-of-Mind
Models}\label{sec-theory-of-mind}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chapters
1--6 built, validated, and scaled single-process models of choice
behavior. Chapter 7 taught us how to compare competing theoretical
accounts using out-of-sample predictive performance. In all of that, our
agent reacted to the \emph{statistics} of the environment: a biased
coin, a rate that occasionally reversed, an opponent treated as a
stochastic source to be tracked. We now take the step that actually
makes the environment social: our agent reasons about the \emph{mind}
producing the behavior on the other side of the table. This chapter
develops the \(k\)-level Theory of Mind (\(k\)-ToM) family --- both a
simple 0-ToM tracker and a genuinely recursive 1-ToM --- applies the
full six-phase validation battery from Ch. 5--6 to them, performs an
Occam's-razor model-comparison study in the spirit of Ch. 7, and then
fits a joint hierarchical model to seven primate species (Devaine et
al., 2017) that regresses individual-level ToM parameters on
standardized endocranial volume (ECV) and social group size. Those two
covariates are the empirical stand-ins for the cognitive scaffolding
hypothesis and the Machiavellian intelligence hypothesis, respectively.
\end{quote}

\section{Introduction}\label{introduction-2}

So far every model we have written shares a structural assumption: there
is an environment, it produces observations with some statistical
regularity, and our agent learns that regularity. The Biased Agent
assumed a fixed coin. The Memory Agent tracked a rate that could drift
or reverse. In both, the \emph{opponent} --- if we even called it that
--- was a passive data-generating process.

This assumption breaks the moment we play a competitive game with
another adaptive agent. If I hide a coin and you try to guess where it
is, then the optimal thing for me to do depends on what I think
\emph{you} think I will do. And the optimal thing for you to do depends
on what you think \emph{I} think you will do. The statistics of the
environment are no longer exogenous --- they are the output of a mind
that is trying to anticipate mine.

Theory of Mind (ToM) is the psychology-and-cognitive-science label for
the capacity to represent other agents as having mental states (beliefs,
desires, intentions) and to use those representations to predict their
behavior. The concept is old, verbal, and famously under-specified
(Schaafsma et al., 2015; Quesque \& Rossetti, 2020). What Devaine,
Hollard, and Daunizeau (2014a, 2014b) and the \texttt{tomsup} package
(Waade et al., 2022) contribute is a \emph{computational} definition: a
family of agents, indexed by a recursion depth \(k\), that formally
specifies what it means to mentalize at level 0, 1, 2, and beyond. Armed
with such a definition we can do what we have done in every previous
chapter --- simulate, recover, validate, and compare --- and use those
tools to ask whether a particular dataset is better explained by agents
who mentalize or agents who do not.

\subsection{The estimand}\label{the-estimand}

Before writing a single line of code, let us be precise about what we
are trying to learn. Our target quantity has two layers:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Individual level.} For each individual (here, each primate),
  what are the posterior distributions over their \(k\)-ToM parameters
  --- volatility \(\sigma\), behavioural temperature \(\beta\), bias
  \(b\) --- when we fit 0-ToM and when we fit 1-ToM? Which model
  predicts that individual's trial-by-trial choices better out of
  sample?
\item
  \textbf{Population level.} Does individual-level behavioural
  temperature (the parameter that most directly controls how strongly an
  agent's expected-utility computation translates into action) vary
  systematically with species-level covariates? Two candidates are
  pre-registered by competing evolutionary hypotheses: social group size
  (\emph{Machiavellian intelligence}) and endocranial volume
  (\emph{cognitive scaffolding}). The only way to arbitrate is to fit
  the individual-level model and then regress the individual-level
  posteriors on both covariates \emph{jointly}, so that each coefficient
  is estimated with the other held constant.
\end{enumerate}

Both layers matter. Fitting \(k\)-ToM to one participant and declaring
them a ``2-ToM'' would be Ch. 2-style estimation: a point estimate that
ignores uncertainty and population structure. Running a single logistic
regression of win rate on ECV would be a pre-Ch. 5 shortcut that throws
away the mechanistic model entirely. The estimand only makes sense if we
carry individual-level posterior uncertainty through to the
species-level inference --- exactly the architecture Ch. 6 built.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-8}

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Motivate recursive ToM} by contrasting it with statistical
  learning, and articulate why a competitive game with an adaptive
  opponent breaks the passive-environment assumption.
\item
  \textbf{Specify the 0-ToM learning rule} as a Kalman-filter-like
  update on the log-odds of the opponent's choice probability, with
  three parameters: volatility \(\sigma\), behavioural temperature
  \(\beta\), and bias \(b\).
\item
  \textbf{Specify a true recursive 1-ToM} that tracks the opponent's
  belief about the agent itself, and understand why this is
  qualitatively different from influence learning (Hampton et al.,
  2008), which we treat as an intermediate \emph{proto-ToM} reference.
\item
  \textbf{Simulate all three agents in R} against each other and against
  non-ToM baselines.
\item
  \textbf{Implement 0-ToM and 1-ToM in Stan} with the recursive latent
  state unrolled in \texttt{transformed\ parameters}, priors on
  log-scale hyperparameters, and \texttt{log\_lik} for PSIS-LOO.
\item
  \textbf{Execute the six-phase validation battery}: prior predictive
  checks, parameter recovery, Simulation-Based Calibration Checks (SBC),
  posterior predictive checks, prior sensitivity, and between-model
  PSIS-LOO comparison --- including a targeted \emph{Occam's razor
  simulation study} that shows 1-ToM is correctly penalized on data
  generated from simpler mechanisms.
\item
  \textbf{Fit a joint hierarchical model} to the primate dataset that
  regresses individual-level \(\log\beta\) on standardized ECV and group
  size, with non-centered parameterization at both the species and
  individual levels.
\item
  \textbf{Interpret} the regression coefficients as evidence for or
  against the cognitive scaffolding and Machiavellian intelligence
  hypotheses, with appropriate uncertainty and appropriate humility
  about what a 2×2 game can identify.
\end{itemize}

\section{The game and the data}\label{the-game-and-the-data}

The ``hide and seek'' / matching-pennies paradigm is deliberately
minimal. On each trial, one player (the opponent, played by a familiar
zookeeper following the instructions of a computer algorithm) hides a
piece of food in one of two hands. The other player (the primate)
chooses a hand. If the choice matches, the primate wins.

We code the choice binary: \(c^{\text{self}}_t \in \{0, 1\}\) for the
primate's choice, \(c^{\text{op}}_t \in \{0, 1\}\) for the opponent's
hidden-reward location. In matching pennies the primate wants
\(c^{\text{self}}_t = c^{\text{op}}_t\) and the opponent wants the
opposite. The payoff to the primate is \(U(c^{\text{self}},
c^{\text{op}}) = 1\) if they match and \(-1\) if not, flipped for the
opponent.

There are three opponent conditions:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{RS (random bias, coded \texttt{-1})}: a 65/35 fixed-bias
  pseudo-random sequence. Baseline. A primate can win by tracking the
  bias.
\item
  \textbf{0-ToM (coded \texttt{0})}: the opponent itself is a 0-ToM
  learner that tracks the primate's choice frequency and hides where the
  primate is \emph{least} likely to look.
\item
  \textbf{1-ToM (coded \texttt{1})}: the opponent models the primate as
  a 0-ToM and best-responds to that simulated agent.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Loading and
sanity-checking}\label{loading-and-sanity-checking}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{read\_csv}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"data"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"mp\_primates.csv"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{show\_col\_types =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{id        =}\NormalTok{ ID,}
    \AttributeTok{species   =}\NormalTok{ commonName,}
    \AttributeTok{trial     =}\NormalTok{ Trial,}
    \AttributeTok{choice    =}\NormalTok{ Decision,}

    \AttributeTok{opp\_cond  =}\NormalTok{ opponent\_level,}
    \AttributeTok{bot       =}\NormalTok{ BotStrategy,}
\NormalTok{    ECV, neocortex, groupsize\_mean}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(choice), }\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.na}\NormalTok{(op\_choice)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}

  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Win rate"}\NormalTok{,}

       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Dashed line = chance"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Opponent"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{axis.text.x =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{angle =} \DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{hjust =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_perf_plot-1.pdf}

}

\caption{Per-individual win rates by species and opponent condition.
Points are individuals, bars are 95\% bootstrap CIs of the species mean.
Against RS a bias-tracker can win; against 0-ToM and 1-ToM, performance
clusters around chance because the caregiver-opponent is partially read
as cooperative (Devaine et al., 2017).}

\end{figure}%

Two things are already visible. First, against the biased RS opponent
most species clear chance, confirming they have grasped the
choice-reward contingency. Second, against the two interactive opponents
win rates cluster near or slightly below \(0.5\) --- consistent with
Devaine et al.'s (2017) interpretation that the primates have partially
read the interaction as cooperative and that their mentalizing, if
present, is directed at a different goal than winning. This is a useful
early warning: performance alone cannot tell us whether an individual is
mentalizing. That distinction has to come from the trial-by-trial
\emph{structure} of their choices --- which is exactly what \(k\)-ToM
lets us measure.

Species-level covariates (one row per species):
