\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{covariates }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mp }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(species) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarize}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{n\_ind     =} \FunctionTok{n\_distinct}\NormalTok{(id),}
    \AttributeTok{ECV       =} \FunctionTok{first}\NormalTok{(ECV),}
    \AttributeTok{groupsize =} \FunctionTok{first}\NormalTok{(groupsize\_mean),}
    \AttributeTok{.groups   =} \StringTok{"drop"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(covariates, }\AttributeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrr@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
species & n\_ind & ECV & groupsize \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
L macaques & 4 & 80.0 & 30.7 \\
R macaques & 5 & 88.3 & 34.8 \\
chimpanzees & 6 & 357.6 & 43.1 \\
gorillas & 5 & 501.5 & 8.5 \\
lemurs & 4 & 22.9 & 15.0 \\
mangabeys & 8 & 94.7 & 54.1 \\
orangutans & 7 & 349.3 & 1.7 \\
\end{longtable}

The two covariates that will drive the evolutionary regression have very
different profiles: gorillas have large brains (high ECV) but live in
small groups, while mangabeys have modest brains in relatively large
groups. This dissociation is exactly what makes the dataset suitable for
arbitrating between the two hypotheses. The cross-species log-log
correlation between ECV and group size across these seven species is
only about \(-0.37\), so the two predictors are statistically separable.

\section{Model 1: the 0-ToM learner}\label{model-1-the-0-tom-learner}

\subsection{The cognitive intuition}\label{the-cognitive-intuition}

A 0-ToM agent treats its opponent as a biased coin whose bias can slowly
drift. That is almost exactly the Memory Agent from Ch. 6 with a
reversal --- a model we know well. The one new ingredient is a softmax
decision rule that connects the inferred opponent bias back to the
agent's own choice via the payoff matrix.

Let \(\mu_t\) be the agent's current estimate of the log-odds that the
opponent will choose option 1 on trial \(t\), and \(\Sigma_t\) its
uncertainty. After observing \(c^{\text{op}}_t\), the agent applies
Devaine et al.'s (2014a) approximate log-odds Kalman update:

\[
\Sigma_t \;\approx\; \left[\frac{1}{\Sigma_{t-1} + \sigma} + s(\mu_{t-1})\,(1 - s(\mu_{t-1}))\right]^{-1},
\] \[
\mu_t \;\approx\; \mu_{t-1} + \Sigma_t\left(c^{\text{op}}_{t-1} - s(\mu_{t-1})\right),
\]

where \(s(\cdot)\) is the sigmoid and \(\sigma > 0\) is the
\emph{volatility} parameter. The point-estimate prediction for the
opponent's next choice folds uncertainty in:

\[
p^{\text{op}}_t \;\approx\; s\!\left(\frac{\mu_t}{\sqrt{1 + 0.36\,\Sigma_t}}\right).
\]

In matching pennies the expected-utility difference for choosing 1 over
0 collapses to \(\Delta V_t = 2 p^{\text{op}}_t - 1\), and the agent's
own choice probability is a softmax with behavioural temperature
\(\beta > 0\) and bias \(b\):

\[
P(c^{\text{self}}_t = 1) \;=\; s\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_t + b}{\beta}\right).
\]

Three parameters per agent: \(\sigma\) (volatility), \(\beta\)
(decisiveness), \(b\) (handedness). Throughout we work with \(\log
\sigma\) and \(\log \beta\) on the unconstrained scale, and \(b\) in
logit-utility units, consistent with Ch. 6.

\subsection{R simulator}\label{r-simulator}

Because the update is deterministic given the data, we can compute the
whole latent trajectory in vectorized R --- useful for simulation and
for prior predictive checks.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Compute 0{-}ToM latent state trajectory for one opponent sequence.}
\NormalTok{tom0\_latent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(op\_choices, log\_sigma) \{}
\NormalTok{  sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_sigma)}
\NormalTok{  N }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(op\_choices)}
\NormalTok{  mu    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(N }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{); Sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(N }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  mu[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{; Sigma[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1}   \CommentTok{\# agnostic prior (matches tomsup / VBA)}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N)) \{}
\NormalTok{    s\_mu }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(mu[t])}
\NormalTok{    Sigma[t }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (Sigma[t] }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ sigma) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ s\_mu }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ s\_mu))}
\NormalTok{    mu[t }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mu[t] }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Sigma[t }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (op\_choices[t] }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ s\_mu)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{  mu\_pred    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mu[}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{N]}
\NormalTok{  Sigma\_pred }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ Sigma[}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{N]}
\NormalTok{  p\_op       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(mu\_pred }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.36} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ Sigma\_pred))}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{t =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N), }\AttributeTok{mu =}\NormalTok{ mu\_pred, }\AttributeTok{Sigma =}\NormalTok{ Sigma\_pred,}
         \AttributeTok{p\_op =}\NormalTok{ p\_op, }\AttributeTok{dV =} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_op }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Simulate one 0{-}ToM agent against a given opponent choice sequence.}
\NormalTok{simulate\_tom0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(op\_choices, log\_sigma, log\_beta, bias, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(seed)) }\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
\NormalTok{  lat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tom0\_latent}\NormalTok{(op\_choices, log\_sigma)}
\NormalTok{  p\_self }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{((lat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{dV }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ bias) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_beta))}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{t =}\NormalTok{ lat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{t, }\AttributeTok{choice =} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(p\_self), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, p\_self),}
         \AttributeTok{op\_choice =}\NormalTok{ op\_choices, }\AttributeTok{p\_self =}\NormalTok{ p\_self, }\AttributeTok{p\_op =}\NormalTok{ lat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{p\_op)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Phase 1 --- prior predictive
check}\label{phase-1-prior-predictive-check}

Priors for unbounded parameters that feed through sigmoids are easy to
mis-specify: a ``weakly informative'' \(\mathcal{N}(0, 5)\) on
\texttt{log\_sigma} would place substantial mass at
\(\sigma \approx e^{5}
\approx 150\), which corresponds to an agent whose belief about the
opponent bias resets to the prior on every trial. We therefore choose:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{log\_sigma\ \textasciitilde{}\ normal(-2,\ 1)} --- centered at
  \(\sigma \approx 0.14\), the tomsup/VBA default.
\item
  \texttt{log\_beta\ \ \textasciitilde{}\ normal(-1,\ 1)} --- centered
  at \(\beta \approx 0.37\), a mildly deterministic decision rule.
\item
  \texttt{bias\ \ \ \ \ \ \textasciitilde{}\ normal(0,\ 1)}.
\end{itemize}

Do these priors generate plausible choice sequences? Let us draw
parameters from them and simulate.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2026}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{n\_sims }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{9}
\NormalTok{op\_seq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{prior\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{sim       =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_sims,}
  \AttributeTok{log\_sigma =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{log\_beta  =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{bias      =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_sims,  }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{rowwise}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sim\_dat =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{simulate\_tom0}\NormalTok{(op\_seq, log\_sigma, log\_beta, bias,}
                                      \AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ sim))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{unnest}\NormalTok{(sim\_dat)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_draws, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ t)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_step}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ choice), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"firebrick3"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ p\_self), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{sim, }\AttributeTok{labeller =}\NormalTok{ label\_both) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Choice / P(choice=1)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Prior predictive: 0{-}ToM choices across 9 prior draws"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Red step = sampled choice, blue = model P(choice=1)"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_prior_pc_tom0-1.pdf}

}

\caption{Prior predictive check for 0-ToM. Each panel shows one
simulated agent against a 70/30 biased opponent. Priors should produce
heterogeneous but non-pathological choice sequences.}

\end{figure}%

The simulations show a variety of behaviors --- some agents track the
opponent bias tightly, some are noisy, some show a strong handedness
bias --- and crucially none collapses to a constant 0 or 1 on every
trial, which would indicate a prior forcing boundary behavior. We
proceed.

\subsection{Phase 2 --- Stan implementation (single
agent)}\label{phase-2-stan-implementation-single-agent}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Technique --- Sequential State Unrolling in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters}}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The 0-ToM model's belief state \((\mu_t, \Sigma_t)\) is a recursive
function of the previous state and the opponent's most recent choice. It
depends on the free parameter \texttt{log\_sigma} (the volatility), so
it \textbf{cannot} live in \texttt{transformed\ data}. It also cannot be
a local variable in \texttt{model}, because the likelihood at every
trial depends on it and we need Stan's autodiff to propagate gradients
back through the entire sequence.

The solution is to unroll the recursion in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters}:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[N\_total] dV;          }\CommentTok{// saved for the likelihood and generated quantities}
\NormalTok{  \{}
    \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ mu = }\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{; }\DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ Sig = }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{;    }\CommentTok{// local state — not saved between draws}
    \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ sigma = exp(log\_sigma);}
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N\_total) \{}
\NormalTok{      dV[t] = f(mu, Sig);             }\CommentTok{// deterministic function of the belief state}
\NormalTok{      Sig   = update\_Sig(Sig, sigma, mu);}
\NormalTok{      mu    = update\_mu(mu, Sig, other[t]);}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The \texttt{\{\}} inner block keeps \texttt{mu} and \texttt{Sig}
\textbf{local} --- they are stack-allocated scratch variables that Stan
discards after each gradient evaluation. Only \texttt{dV} is lifted to
the saved parameter space, because the likelihood needs it. This is the
minimal footprint: the gradient tape tracks
\texttt{log\_sigma\ →\ sigma\ →\ Sig\ →\ mu\ →\ dV{[}t{]}}, but does not
store the full sequence of \((\mu_t, \Sigma_t)\) in the posterior draws.

\textbf{Why \texttt{transformed\ parameters} rather than
\texttt{model}?} We need \texttt{dV} in \texttt{generated\ quantities}
to compute \texttt{log\_lik} and \texttt{y\_rep}. If \texttt{dV} were a
local variable in \texttt{model}, it would be invisible to
\texttt{generated\ quantities}. The solution is to declare it in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} (where it is visible to all later
blocks) and keep the volatile state variables local inside
\texttt{\{\}}.

\textbf{Computational cost.} Unrolling \(T\) steps in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} means running the recursion once per
leapfrog evaluation --- the same cost as running it in \texttt{model}.
The only overhead compared to a local-variable approach is that
\texttt{dV} (a vector of length \(T\)) is written to the saved-draw tape
once per posterior sample, adding \(T\) doubles per draw. For
\(T = 120\) this is negligible.

\end{tcolorbox}

We unroll the Kalman update inside \texttt{transformed\ parameters} so
that \texttt{log\_sigma} is a real free parameter (not a fixed
covariate) and is learned jointly with the decision parameters. The
Bernoulli logit likelihood and the \texttt{log\_lik} block are standard.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_tom0\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_total;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} y;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} other;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  real prior\_log\_sigma\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_log\_sigma\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_log\_beta\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_log\_beta\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_bias\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_bias\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real log\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real log\_beta;}
\StringTok{  real bias;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] dV;}
\StringTok{  \{}
\StringTok{    real sigma = exp(log\_sigma);}
\StringTok{    real mu    = 0.0;}
\StringTok{    real Sig   = 1.0;}
\StringTok{    for (t in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      real s\_mu = inv\_logit(mu);}
\StringTok{      real p\_op = inv\_logit(mu / sqrt(1.0 + 0.36 * Sig));}
\StringTok{      dV[t] = 2.0 * p\_op {-} 1.0;}
\StringTok{      Sig = 1.0 / (1.0 / (Sig + sigma) + s\_mu * (1.0 {-} s\_mu));}
\StringTok{      mu  = mu + Sig * (other[t] {-} s\_mu);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(log\_sigma | prior\_log\_sigma\_mu, prior\_log\_sigma\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(log\_beta  | prior\_log\_beta\_mu,  prior\_log\_beta\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(bias      | prior\_bias\_mu,      prior\_bias\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | (dV + bias) / exp(log\_beta));}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int y\_rep;}
\StringTok{  real lprior =   normal\_lpdf(log\_sigma | prior\_log\_sigma\_mu, prior\_log\_sigma\_sigma)}
\StringTok{                + normal\_lpdf(log\_beta  | prior\_log\_beta\_mu,  prior\_log\_beta\_sigma)}
\StringTok{                + normal\_lpdf(bias      | prior\_bias\_mu,      prior\_bias\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    for (t in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[t] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[t] | (dV[t] + bias) / exp(log\_beta));}
\StringTok{      y\_rep[t]   = bernoulli\_logit\_rng((dV[t] + bias) / exp(log\_beta));}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_tom0\_single, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{mod\_tom0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Phase 3 --- parameter
recovery}\label{phase-3-parameter-recovery}

A 20-agent recovery study with 120 trials each, which matches the
average trial count we see per individual per condition in the primate
dataset.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{recovery\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_recovery.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(recovery\_path)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{42}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  truth }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{agent     =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{log\_sigma =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{log\_beta  =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{bias      =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{,  }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
  \CommentTok{\# Every synthetic agent plays the same 120{-}trial biased opponent}
\NormalTok{  op\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.65}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{  recovery }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ truth }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{rowwise}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sim\_dat =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{simulate\_tom0}\NormalTok{(op\_vec, log\_sigma, log\_beta,}
\NormalTok{                                        bias, }\AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ agent))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}

\NormalTok{  fit\_one }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(d) \{}
\NormalTok{    fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_tom0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{data =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{N\_total =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d), }
        \AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice, }
        \AttributeTok{other =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{op\_choice,}
        \AttributeTok{prior\_log\_sigma\_mu =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prior\_log\_sigma\_sigma =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
        \AttributeTok{prior\_log\_beta\_mu =} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,  }\AttributeTok{prior\_log\_beta\_sigma =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
        \AttributeTok{prior\_bias\_mu =} \FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{,       }\AttributeTok{prior\_bias\_sigma =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
        \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{      ),}
      \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{show\_messages =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{    fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_sigma"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(variable, mean, q5, q95)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

\NormalTok{  recovery\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ recovery }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{rowwise}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{post =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{fit\_one}\NormalTok{(sim\_dat))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent, log\_sigma, log\_beta, bias, post) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{unnest}\NormalTok{(post)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(recovery\_summary, recovery\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  recovery\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(recovery\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{recovery\_long }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ recovery\_summary }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(log\_sigma, log\_beta, bias),}
               \AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"param"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"true"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ param)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery\_long, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ true, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{,}
                \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{,}
              \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{param, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True value"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior mean"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"0{-}ToM parameter recovery (20 synthetic agents, 120 trials)"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_recovery_tom0_plot-1.pdf}

}

\caption{0-ToM parameter recovery. Points = posterior means, bars = 90\%
CIs, dashed line = identity. Recovery is clean for bias and log\_beta;
log\_sigma is noisier with mild shrinkage to the prior at 120 trials ---
a design finding we will confirm under the more rigorous SBC next.}

\end{figure}%

\subsection{Phase 4 --- Simulation-Based Calibration Checks
(SBC)}\label{phase-4-simulation-based-calibration-checks-sbc}

Parameter recovery on a single dataset is anecdotal: we may have sampled
a benign region of parameter space. SBC (Talts et al., 2018) asks the
stronger question --- across the \emph{entire prior} --- is the
posterior calibrated? If we generate \(\theta_{\text{true}}\) from the
prior, simulate data from the data model, and compute the rank of
\(\theta_{\text{true}}\) in the posterior draws, that rank must be
uniform. A U-shaped rank histogram means overconfidence; a bump-shape
means underconfidence; any asymmetry means bias.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbc\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_sbc.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{generate\_sbc\_tom0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{op\_bias =} \FloatTok{0.65}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  log\_sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  log\_beta  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  bias      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,  }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  op\_vec    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(N, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, op\_bias)}
\NormalTok{  sim\_dat   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_tom0}\NormalTok{(op\_vec, log\_sigma, log\_beta, bias)}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{log\_sigma =}\NormalTok{ log\_sigma,}
                     \AttributeTok{log\_beta  =}\NormalTok{ log\_beta,}
                     \AttributeTok{bias      =}\NormalTok{ bias),}
    \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N,}
                     \AttributeTok{choice    =}\NormalTok{ sim\_dat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
                     \AttributeTok{op\_choice =}\NormalTok{ sim\_dat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{op\_choice)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_sbc }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sbc\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  n\_sbc }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{150}   \CommentTok{\# 200+ preferred in production; 150 for chapter compute}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_gen }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(generate\_sbc\_tom0, }\AttributeTok{N =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{op\_bias =} \FloatTok{0.65}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_backend }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    mod\_tom0,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
  \FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(sequential)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_ds      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_gen, n\_sbc)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{datasets =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_ds, }\AttributeTok{backend =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_backend,}
                             \AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ds =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_ds, }\AttributeTok{results =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results), sbc\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_obj     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sbc\_path)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_ds      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ds}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{results}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p\_hist }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plot\_rank\_hist}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results,}
                         \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_sigma"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Rank histograms"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p\_ecdf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_results,}
                         \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_sigma"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"ECDF difference"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{p\_hist }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_ecdf}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_sbc_tom0_plot-1.pdf}

}

\caption{Simulation-Based Calibration Checks for 0-ToM. Top row: rank
histograms (flat = calibrated). Bottom row: ECDF difference (blue line
inside grey band = calibrated). log\_sigma shows the mildest excursion,
consistent with the recovery plot: its posterior is only partially
data-driven at 120 trials; log\_beta and bias are clean.}

\end{figure}%

The SBC results confirm the recovery diagnosis: \texttt{log\_beta} and
\texttt{bias} are cleanly calibrated, \texttt{log\_sigma} shows the
signature of mild underidentification with 120 trials per sequence. We
will therefore interpret species-level posteriors on \texttt{log\_sigma}
with explicit caution, and place the scientific weight on
\texttt{log\_beta}.

\section{Model 2: influence learning
(proto-ToM)}\label{model-2-influence-learning-proto-tom}

The simplest step beyond 0-ToM is to notice that your opponent is
\emph{reacting} to you. Hampton, Bossaerts, and O'Doherty (2008)
formalized this as \textbf{influence learning}: augment the 0-ToM
prediction error with a term that captures how your own current choice
will shift the opponent's next prediction of you.

For a competitive matching-pennies opponent:

\[
p^{\text{op}}_{t+1} = p^{\text{op}}_t
+ \eta \bigl( c^{\text{op}}_t - p^{\text{op}}_t \bigr)
- \lambda\, p^{\text{op}}_t(1 - p^{\text{op}}_t)\Bigl(2 c^{\text{self}}_t - \beta_{\text{op}}\,\text{logit}(p^{\text{op}}_t) - 1\Bigr),
\]

where \(\eta\) is the baseline learning rate, \(\lambda\) is the
influence weight, and the bracketed factor encodes the competitive
incentive. Importantly, there is \emph{no} recursive belief updating:
the agent does not maintain a posterior over the opponent's parameters,
it only adjusts a heuristic rule. Devaine et al.~(2017) classify
influence learning as proto-ToM precisely for this reason --- it is
aware of self-influence without mentalizing per se.

We implement influence learning as a parallel simulator and Stan
observation model. The code structure mirrors 0-ToM; we include the R
simulator here and omit the Stan duplicate for space (it differs only in
the \texttt{transformed\ parameters} block: the scalar \(p^{\text{op}}\)
update equation replaces the Kalman block). A working parallel
\texttt{ch09\_influence\_single.stan} is included in the chapter's
supplementary repository.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{simulate\_inf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(op\_choices, log\_eta, log\_lambda, log\_beta, bias,}
                         \AttributeTok{beta\_op =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(seed)) }\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
\NormalTok{  eta    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(log\_eta)     }\CommentTok{\# eta, lambda bounded in (0,1)}
\NormalTok{  lambda }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(log\_lambda)}
\NormalTok{  beta   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_beta)}
\NormalTok{  N   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(op\_choices)}
\NormalTok{  p\_op     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(N }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{); p\_op[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}
\NormalTok{  choice   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{integer}\NormalTok{(N)}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N)) \{}
\NormalTok{    dV }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_op[t] }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{    p\_self\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{((dV }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ bias) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ beta)}
\NormalTok{    choice[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, p\_self\_t)}
    \CommentTok{\# Influence update for next{-}trial prediction}
\NormalTok{    pe      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ op\_choices[t] }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_op[t]}
\NormalTok{    logit\_p }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{qlogis}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(p\_op[t], }\FloatTok{1e{-}6}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{1e{-}6}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{    infl    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ lambda }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_op[t] }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_op[t]) }\SpecialCharTok{*}
\NormalTok{               (}\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ choice[t] }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ beta\_op }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ logit\_p }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    p\_op[t }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(p\_op[t] }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ eta }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ pe }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ infl, }\FloatTok{1e{-}4}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{1e{-}4}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{t =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N), }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choice, }\AttributeTok{op\_choice =}\NormalTok{ op\_choices,}
         \AttributeTok{p\_op =}\NormalTok{ p\_op[}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{N])}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\section{Model 3: true recursive
1-ToM}\label{model-3-true-recursive-1-tom}

Now the real thing. A 1-ToM agent believes its opponent is a 0-ToM
agent, and \emph{represents the opponent's belief about itself}. That
recursive representation is what elevates 1-ToM above influence
learning: the agent does not just adjust for the opponent's reactivity
with a heuristic, it simulates the opponent's cognitive update
internally.

\subsection{The mechanism}\label{the-mechanism}

Let us denote 1-ToM's belief state about \emph{how the opponent views
the 1-ToM agent itself} by
\((\mu^{\text{self}}, \Sigma^{\text{self}})\). This pair evolves exactly
like a 0-ToM's belief, but the ``observed choice'' that drives the
update is the 1-ToM agent's \emph{own} past choice \(c^{\text{self}}_t\)
(because that is what the opponent observes):

\[
\Sigma^{\text{self}}_t \approx \left[\frac{1}{\Sigma^{\text{self}}_{t-1} + \sigma_{\text{op}}} + s(\mu^{\text{self}}_{t-1})(1 - s(\mu^{\text{self}}_{t-1}))\right]^{-1},
\] \[
\mu^{\text{self}}_t \approx \mu^{\text{self}}_{t-1} + \Sigma^{\text{self}}_t \bigl( c^{\text{self}}_{t-1} - s(\mu^{\text{self}}_{t-1}) \bigr).
\]

Here \(\sigma_{\text{op}}\) is the \emph{opponent's} volatility --- as
believed by the 1-ToM. Given this state, the 1-ToM predicts what the
opponent will do on trial \(t\) by simulating the opponent's decision
rule. The opponent (as a 0-ToM) wants to \emph{mismatch} the primate, so
its expected utility of choosing 1 is
\(\Delta V^{\text{op}}_t = 1 - 2 \hat{p}^{\text{self}}_t\), and its
choice probability is:

\[
\hat{p}^{\text{op}}_t = s\!\left(\frac{1 - 2 \hat{p}^{\text{self}}_t}{\beta_{\text{op}}}\right),
\qquad
\hat{p}^{\text{self}}_t = s\!\left(\frac{\mu^{\text{self}}_t}{\sqrt{1 + 0.36\,\Sigma^{\text{self}}_t}}\right).
\]

The 1-ToM agent then plugs \(\hat{p}^{\text{op}}_t\) into its own
expected-utility calculation and softmaxes to produce a choice. Four
parameters: \(\log \sigma_{\text{op}}\) (how volatile the 1-ToM thinks
its opponent thinks it is), \(\log \beta_{\text{op}}\) (opponent's
decision temperature as believed by 1-ToM), \(\log \beta\) (1-ToM's own
decision temperature), and \(b\) (bias).

Note the recursive structure: this is qualitatively different from
influence learning. 1-ToM maintains a \emph{full belief state} about
what the opponent thinks of it, with uncertainty, and updates that
belief via a Bayesian filter. Influence learning updates a scalar
\(p^{\text{op}}\) with a correction term. The two are behaviorally close
in many scenarios (Devaine et al., 2017, Fig. 7), but mechanistically
distinct --- and the Occam's-razor test below shows that data can
sometimes distinguish them.

\subsection{What this implementation commits
to}\label{what-this-implementation-commits-to}

A candid methodological note before we proceed. Our 1-ToM agent tracks
the first two moments \((\mu^{\text{self}}, \Sigma^{\text{self}})\) of
its belief about the opponent's belief about itself, \emph{conditional
on point values} of the opponent's volatility \(\sigma_{\text{op}}\) and
decision temperature \(\beta_{\text{op}}\). The agent does not represent
uncertainty over those opponent parameters. The full variational scheme
of Devaine et al.~(2014a) maintains a Gaussian variational posterior
over \((\sigma_{\text{op}},
\beta_{\text{op}})\) as well, and propagates that second-order
uncertainty through the recursion via moment matching. That is the more
complete cognitive commitment and also the more expensive one.

We are therefore implementing what is most accurately called a
\textbf{fixed-parameter 1-ToM}: an agent that believes its opponent has
fixed, known \((\sigma_{\text{op}}, \beta_{\text{op}})\) and updates
only its beliefs about the opponent's moment-to-moment \emph{state}
\(\mu^{\text{self}}\). The posterior we will obtain from Stan over
\((\log\sigma_{\text{op}}, \log\beta_{\text{op}})\) reflects \emph{our}
uncertainty over which fixed values best describe this particular
primate's internal opponent model, \emph{not} the primate's own
representational uncertainty about its opponent. This is a simpler
cognitive model than full variational 1-ToM, and it is what is actually
tractable inside an HMC sampler without a specialized message-passing
backend. Readers who want the Devaine et al.~scheme in full should
consult the \texttt{VBA} toolbox (Daunizeau, 2014).

\textbf{Graceful degradation.} One concern with the point-estimate
approximation is how sensitive 1-ToM predictions are to
mis-specification of \(\sigma_{\text{op}}\). The recursion has a stable
fixed point \(\Sigma^\star\) set by the balance of drift
\(\sigma_{\text{op}}\) and the sigmoid second moment (bounded above by
\(1/4\)). For \(\sigma_{\text{op}}\) ranging from \(10^{-3}\) to
\(10^1\), \(\Sigma^\star\) lies in roughly \([0.1, 2]\), and the
predicted \(\hat{p}^{\text{op}}_t\) is a compressed, monotone transform
of the underlying \(\mu\)-trajectory. The consequence is that a tenfold
error in the internal \(\sigma_{\text{op}}\) produces a bounded and
usually small change in \(dV_t\) --- a feature from a robustness
standpoint, but a \emph{bug} from an identifiability standpoint, because
it means the data will not constrain this parameter strongly. We return
to this in Phase 4b.

\subsection{R simulator}\label{r-simulator-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} Compute 1{-}ToM\textquotesingle{}s predicted p\_op sequence given agent parameters AND}
\CommentTok{\#\textquotesingle{} given the agent\textquotesingle{}s own past choices. Used by simulator and by Stan.}
\NormalTok{tom1\_p\_op }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(choices\_self, log\_sigma\_op, log\_beta\_op) \{}
\NormalTok{  sigma\_op }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_sigma\_op)}
\NormalTok{  beta\_op  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_beta\_op)}
\NormalTok{  N  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(choices\_self)}
\NormalTok{  mu\_s    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{; Sig\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{  p\_op    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(N)}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p\_self\_from\_op }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(mu\_s }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.36} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ Sig\_s))}
\NormalTok{    p\_op[t] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{((}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_self\_from\_op) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ beta\_op)}
    \CommentTok{\# Update opponent\textquotesingle{}s belief about self, using self\textquotesingle{}s past choice}
\NormalTok{    s\_ps  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_self\_from\_op}
\NormalTok{    Sig\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (Sig\_s }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ sigma\_op) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ s\_ps }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ s\_ps))}
\NormalTok{    mu\_s  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mu\_s }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Sig\_s }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (choices\_self[t] }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ s\_ps)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{  p\_op}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{simulate\_tom1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(op\_choices, log\_sigma\_op, log\_beta\_op,}
\NormalTok{                          log\_beta, bias, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(seed)) }\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
\NormalTok{  beta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_beta)}
\NormalTok{  N  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(op\_choices)}
\NormalTok{  mu\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{; Sig\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{  choice }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{integer}\NormalTok{(N); p\_op\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(N)}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N)) \{}
\NormalTok{    p\_self\_from\_op }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(mu\_s }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.36} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ Sig\_s))}
\NormalTok{    p\_op\_t         }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{((}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_self\_from\_op) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_beta\_op))}
\NormalTok{    p\_op\_vec[t]    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_op\_t}
\NormalTok{    dV\_t           }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ p\_op\_t }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{    p\_self\_t       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{((dV\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ bias) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ beta)}
\NormalTok{    choice[t]      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, p\_self\_t)}
    \CommentTok{\# Update after observing own choice}
\NormalTok{    s\_ps }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_self\_from\_op}
\NormalTok{    Sig\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (Sig\_s }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_sigma\_op)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ s\_ps }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ s\_ps))}
\NormalTok{    mu\_s  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mu\_s }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ Sig\_s }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (choice[t] }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ s\_ps)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{t =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(N), }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choice, }\AttributeTok{op\_choice =}\NormalTok{ op\_choices,}
         \AttributeTok{p\_op =}\NormalTok{ p\_op\_vec)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Stan implementation}\label{stan-implementation}

The Stan model unrolls the same recursion inside
\texttt{transformed\ parameters}. A subtle but important point: the
opponent's belief state depends on \texttt{choice{[}t{]}}, which is
\emph{data}, not a parameter. This keeps the recursion within a single
leapfrog step cheap.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_tom1\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} choice;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} op\_choice;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real log\_sigma\_op;}
\StringTok{  real log\_beta\_op;}
\StringTok{  real log\_beta;}
\StringTok{  real bias;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N] dV;}
\StringTok{  \{}
\StringTok{    real sigma\_op = exp(log\_sigma\_op);}
\StringTok{    real beta\_op  = exp(log\_beta\_op);}
\StringTok{    real mu\_s     = 0.0;}
\StringTok{    real Sig\_s    = 1.0;}
\StringTok{    for (t in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{      real p\_self\_from\_op = inv\_logit(mu\_s / sqrt(1 + 0.36 * Sig\_s));}
\StringTok{      real p\_op           = inv\_logit((1 {-} 2 * p\_self\_from\_op) / beta\_op);}
\StringTok{      dV[t] = 2 * p\_op {-} 1;}
\StringTok{      // After observing self\textquotesingle{}s choice, opponent updates its belief}
\StringTok{      real s\_ps = p\_self\_from\_op;}
\StringTok{      Sig\_s = 1.0 / (1.0 / (Sig\_s + sigma\_op) + s\_ps * (1 {-} s\_ps));}
\StringTok{      mu\_s  = mu\_s + Sig\_s * (choice[t] {-} s\_ps);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  log\_sigma\_op \textasciitilde{} normal({-}2, 1);}
\StringTok{  log\_beta\_op  \textasciitilde{} normal({-}1, 0.7);}
\StringTok{  log\_beta     \textasciitilde{} normal({-}1, 1);}
\StringTok{  bias         \textasciitilde{} normal( 0, 1);}
\StringTok{  choice \textasciitilde{} bernoulli\_logit((dV + bias) / exp(log\_beta));}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  real lprior =   normal\_lpdf(log\_sigma\_op | {-}2, 1)}
\StringTok{                + normal\_lpdf(log\_beta\_op  | {-}1, 0.7)}
\StringTok{                + normal\_lpdf(log\_beta     | {-}1, 1)}
\StringTok{                + normal\_lpdf(bias         |  0, 1);}
\StringTok{  for (t in 1:N)}
\StringTok{    log\_lik[t] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(choice[t] |}
\StringTok{                  (dV[t] + bias) / exp(log\_beta));}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_tom1\_single, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom1\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{mod\_tom1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom1\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Two remarks on priors. First, \texttt{log\_beta\_op} is held somewhat
tighter than the primate's own \texttt{log\_beta} because this parameter
represents the primate's \emph{belief} about the opponent's decisiveness
and is identified only indirectly through predictions --- a tight prior
avoids fishing in regions the data cannot constrain. Second,
\texttt{log\_sigma\_op} and \texttt{log\_beta\_op} enter the likelihood
only through the recursion, making them the least well-identified
parameters. Do not be surprised if their posteriors are close to the
prior on short sequences.

\subsection{1-ToM recovery}\label{tom-recovery}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{recovery\_tom1\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom1\_recovery.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(recovery\_tom1\_path)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2027}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  truth1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{agent        =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{log\_sigma\_op =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{log\_beta\_op  =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{log\_beta     =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{bias         =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{15}\NormalTok{,  }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  op\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.65}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{  fit\_one\_tom1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(ls\_op, lb\_op, lb, bs, a) \{}
\NormalTok{    dd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_tom1}\NormalTok{(op\_vec, ls\_op, lb\_op, lb, bs, }\AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ a)}
\NormalTok{    fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_tom1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{data =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(dd), }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ dd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice, }\AttributeTok{op\_choice =}\NormalTok{ dd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{op\_choice),}
      \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{show\_messages =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{    fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_sigma\_op"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"log\_beta\_op"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"log\_beta"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{      dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(variable, mean, q5, q95)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

\NormalTok{  recovery1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ truth1 }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{rowwise}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{post =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{fit\_one\_tom1}\NormalTok{(log\_sigma\_op, log\_beta\_op,}
\NormalTok{                                    log\_beta, bias, agent))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent, log\_sigma\_op, log\_beta\_op, log\_beta, bias, post) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{unnest}\NormalTok{(post)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(recovery1, recovery\_tom1\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  recovery1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(recovery\_tom1\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{recovery1\_long }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ recovery1 }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(log\_sigma\_op, log\_beta\_op, log\_beta, bias),}
               \AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"param"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"true"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ param)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(recovery1\_long, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ true, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.4}\NormalTok{,}
                \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{param, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True value"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior mean"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"1{-}ToM parameter recovery (15 synthetic agents, 150 trials)"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_recovery_tom1_plot-1.pdf}

}

\caption{1-ToM parameter recovery. The agent's own log\_beta and bias
are well recovered. The opponent-model parameters log\_sigma\_op and
log\_beta\_op are noisier --- they enter the likelihood only through the
recursion, and a 150-trial sequence only weakly constrains them.}

\end{figure}%

The pattern is exactly what we should expect from the theory. The
agent's \emph{own} decision parameters (\texttt{log\_beta},
\texttt{bias}) are identified directly from the choice likelihood and
recover cleanly. The \emph{opponent-model} parameters
(\texttt{log\_sigma\_op}, \texttt{log\_beta\_op}) are identified only
through the recursive belief update that feeds into \(\Delta V\), and
their recovery is correspondingly weaker. But a single recovery study
cannot say \emph{how} weak --- recovery evaluated at the prior mode can
look fine even when the posterior is grossly miscalibrated elsewhere in
parameter space. That is what SBC is for.

\section{Phase 4b --- SBC for 1-ToM}\label{phase-4b-sbc-for-1-tom}

Skipping SBC on the model whose recovery is weakest would invert the
logic of the battery. The whole point of Phase 4 is to \emph{diagnose}
the geometry of models whose single-dataset recovery is ambiguous. We
therefore run SBC on 1-ToM with exactly the same machinery, same number
of simulations, and same trial count we used for 0-ToM. The structural
prediction from the fixed-point analysis above is that
\texttt{log\_beta} and \texttt{bias} will calibrate cleanly, while
\texttt{log\_sigma\_op} and \texttt{log\_beta\_op} should show
substantial ECDF excursions --- their posteriors are weakly updated from
the prior because matching pennies does not provide a regime change
sharp enough to pin them down.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{sbc\_tom1\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom1\_sbc.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{generate\_sbc\_tom1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =} \DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{op\_bias =} \FloatTok{0.65}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  log\_sigma\_op }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  log\_beta\_op  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  log\_beta     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  bias         }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,  }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  op\_vec       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(N, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, op\_bias)}
\NormalTok{  sim\_dat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_tom1}\NormalTok{(op\_vec, log\_sigma\_op, log\_beta\_op,}
\NormalTok{                           log\_beta, bias)}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{log\_sigma\_op =}\NormalTok{ log\_sigma\_op,}
                     \AttributeTok{log\_beta\_op  =}\NormalTok{ log\_beta\_op,}
                     \AttributeTok{log\_beta     =}\NormalTok{ log\_beta,}
                     \AttributeTok{bias         =}\NormalTok{ bias),}
    \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N,}
                     \AttributeTok{choice    =}\NormalTok{ sim\_dat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
                     \AttributeTok{op\_choice =}\NormalTok{ sim\_dat}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{op\_choice)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_sbc }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sbc\_tom1\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  n\_sbc\_tom1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{150}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_gen1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(generate\_sbc\_tom1,}
                                     \AttributeTok{N =} \DecValTok{150}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{op\_bias =} \FloatTok{0.65}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_backend1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    mod\_tom1,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_ds1      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_gen1, n\_sbc\_tom1)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_results1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{datasets =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_ds1, }\AttributeTok{backend =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_backend1,}
                              \AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ds =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_ds1, }\AttributeTok{results =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_results1), sbc\_tom1\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_obj1     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(sbc\_tom1\_path)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_ds1      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_obj1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ds}
