\end{longtable}

A positive elpd\_diff for \texttt{mixture} (with
\textbar elpd\_diff\textbar{} \textgreater{} 2·se\_diff) would indicate
the extra 1-ToM arm is earning its complexity on this dataset. A small
or negative elpd\_diff indicates that across the 39 primates, the data
jointly do not strongly prefer the mixture --- the species-level
\(\pi_s\) estimates would then carry interpretive weight only insofar as
they are shaped by the covariate regression, not by individual-level
likelihood contrast. This is the predictive-calibration side of the same
identifiability story Phase 6 laid out: matching pennies is a game with
limited leverage for distinguishing architectures.

\section{Secondary analysis: decisional determinism within a 0-ToM
architecture}\label{secondary-analysis-decisional-determinism-within-a-0-tom-architecture}

The main analysis is complete. As a subsidiary question we now ask
whether, \emph{conditional on treating every primate as a 0-ToM agent},
species-level decisional determinism (\texttt{log\_beta}) tracks ECV or
group size. This is the regression the previous version of this chapter
promoted to the main estimand; we now position it correctly, as a
question about statistical execution rather than about representational
capacity.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_tom0\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} J;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} S;}
\StringTok{  array[J] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=S\textgreater{} species\_of;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=1, upper=J\textgreater{} agent;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} choice;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} op\_choice;}
\StringTok{  array[J] int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} start;}
\StringTok{  array[J] int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} stop;}
\StringTok{  vector[S] ecv\_z;        // standardized log ECV}
\StringTok{  vector[S] group\_z;      // standardized log group size}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[S] mu\_log\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} tau\_log\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  vector[J] z\_log\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  // log\_beta regression}
\StringTok{  real alpha\_logbeta;}
\StringTok{  real b\_ecv;}
\StringTok{  real b\_group;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} tau\_sp;        // species residual SD on log\_beta}
\StringTok{  vector[S] z\_sp;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} tau\_log\_beta;  // individual{-}within{-}species SD}
\StringTok{  vector[J] z\_log\_beta;}
\StringTok{  // bias}
\StringTok{  vector[S] mu\_bias;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} tau\_bias;}
\StringTok{  vector[J] z\_bias;}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[S] mu\_log\_beta = alpha\_logbeta + b\_ecv * ecv\_z +}
\StringTok{                          b\_group * group\_z + tau\_sp * z\_sp;}
\StringTok{  vector[J] log\_sigma = mu\_log\_sigma[species\_of] + tau\_log\_sigma * z\_log\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  vector[J] log\_beta  = mu\_log\_beta[species\_of]  + tau\_log\_beta  * z\_log\_beta;}
\StringTok{  vector[J] bias      = mu\_bias[species\_of]      + tau\_bias      * z\_bias;}
\StringTok{  vector[N] dV;}
\StringTok{  for (j in 1:J) \{}
\StringTok{    real sigma\_j = exp(log\_sigma[j]);}
\StringTok{    real mu\_s    = 0.0;}
\StringTok{    real Sig\_s   = 1.0;}
\StringTok{    for (k in start[j]:stop[j]) \{}
\StringTok{      real s\_mu = inv\_logit(mu\_s);}
\StringTok{      real p\_op = inv\_logit(mu\_s / sqrt(1 + 0.36 * Sig\_s));}
\StringTok{      dV[k] = 2 * p\_op {-} 1;}
\StringTok{      Sig\_s = 1.0 / (1.0 / (Sig\_s + sigma\_j) + s\_mu * (1 {-} s\_mu));}
\StringTok{      mu\_s  = mu\_s + Sig\_s * (op\_choice[k] {-} s\_mu);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  mu\_log\_sigma \textasciitilde{} normal({-}2, 1);}
\StringTok{  tau\_log\_sigma \textasciitilde{} exponential(2);}
\StringTok{  z\_log\_sigma \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  alpha\_logbeta \textasciitilde{} normal({-}1, 1);}
\StringTok{  b\_ecv   \textasciitilde{} normal(0, 0.5);}
\StringTok{  b\_group \textasciitilde{} normal(0, 0.5);}
\StringTok{  tau\_sp        \textasciitilde{} exponential(2);}
\StringTok{  z\_sp          \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  tau\_log\_beta  \textasciitilde{} exponential(1);}
\StringTok{  z\_log\_beta    \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  mu\_bias \textasciitilde{} normal(0, 1);}
\StringTok{  tau\_bias \textasciitilde{} exponential(1);}
\StringTok{  z\_bias \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  for (n in 1:N)}
\StringTok{    choice[n] \textasciitilde{} bernoulli\_logit(}
\StringTok{      (dV[n] + bias[agent[n]]) / exp(log\_beta[agent[n]])}
\StringTok{    );}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  for (n in 1:N)}
\StringTok{    log\_lik[n] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(}
\StringTok{      choice[n] | (dV[n] + bias[agent[n]]) / exp(log\_beta[agent[n]])}
\StringTok{    );}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}
\FunctionTok{writeLines}\NormalTok{(stan\_tom0\_reg, here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_reg.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{mod\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_reg.stan"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Reuse species\_cov from the mixture section; build the data list for}
\CommentTok{\# the log\_beta regression}
\NormalTok{sd\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  sd\_int[}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"N"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"J"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"S"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"species\_of"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"agent"}\NormalTok{,}
           \StringTok{"choice"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"op\_choice"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"start"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"stop"}\NormalTok{)],}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ecv\_z =}\NormalTok{ species\_cov}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ecv\_z,}
       \AttributeTok{group\_z =}\NormalTok{ species\_cov}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{group\_z)}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{reg\_path }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ here}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch09\_tom0\_reg\_fit.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_fits }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(reg\_path)) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_reg}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ sd\_reg,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta =} \FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{2028}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_reg}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(reg\_path)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(reg\_path)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{fit\_reg}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
$num_divergent
[1] 1 2 1 2

$num_max_treedepth
[1]  0  0 31  0

$ebfmi
[1] 0.8511128 0.9028793 0.7713412 0.8232758
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Results (decisional
determinism)}\label{results-decisional-determinism}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{coef\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_reg}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"b\_ecv"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"b\_group"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"alpha\_logbeta"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"tau\_sp"}\NormalTok{),}
                            \AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"df"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{()}

\NormalTok{p\_coef }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ coef\_draws }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(b\_ecv, b\_group) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"coef"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"value"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ value, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ coef)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray30"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"b\_ecv"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"b\_group"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"firebrick3"}\NormalTok{),}
                    \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"b\_ecv"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"ECV (scaffolding)"}\NormalTok{,}
                               \StringTok{"b\_group"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Group size (Machiavellian)"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(beta[cov]}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\StringTok{"(log{-}beta per SD of log covariate)"}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior density"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"Hypothesis"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Which covariate drives species{-}level decisiveness?"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Mass to the left of 0 = lower log\_beta (more deterministic) with larger covariate"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Compare implied mu\_log\_beta from regression vs. baseline hier fit}
\NormalTok{baseline\_mu }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_hier}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_log\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"df"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarize}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{across}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_log\_beta"}\NormalTok{), mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"param"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"baseline\_mean"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{species\_id =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{str\_extract}\NormalTok{(param, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{d+"}\NormalTok{))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(species\_cov }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(species\_id, species), }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"species\_id"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{reg\_mu }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_reg}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_log\_beta"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"df"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarize}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{across}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{starts\_with}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"mu\_log\_beta"}\NormalTok{), mean)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"param"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"reg\_mean"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{species\_id =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{str\_extract}\NormalTok{(param, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}\textbackslash{}}\StringTok{d+"}\NormalTok{)))}

\NormalTok{compare\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ baseline\_mu }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(reg\_mu, }\AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"species\_id"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_compare }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(compare\_df, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ baseline\_mean, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ reg\_mean, }\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ species)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{  ggrepel}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{geom\_text\_repel}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Baseline species mean log\_beta"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Regression{-}implied species mean log\_beta"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Do ECV + group size explain species variation?"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Close to diagonal = covariates capture the between{-}species signal"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_coef }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ p\_compare }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{plot\_layout}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{widths =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_coef_plot-1.pdf}

}

\caption{Secondary analysis: posterior coefficients of a regression of
the 0-ToM decision temperature log\_beta on species-level standardized
log ECV and log group size. This is NOT the main estimand (see the
mixture regression above) --- it asks the narrower question of whether
species act more deterministically on their 0-ToM predictions with
larger brains or larger social groups. Negative coefficient = lower
log\_beta (tighter softmax) with higher covariate.}

\end{figure}%

The coefficient posteriors are the scientific payoff. Reading them
requires keeping the direction straight: \texttt{log\_beta} is an
\emph{inverse} measure of decisiveness --- smaller values mean tighter
softmax, more deterministic use of the expected-utility signal. A
\emph{negative} regression coefficient therefore means that species with
higher ECV (or larger groups) act more deterministically on their 0-ToM
predictions.

Summary statistics:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{coef\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ coef\_draws }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{  dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(b\_ecv, b\_group) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"coef"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"value"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(coef) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarize}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{mean   =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(value),}
    \AttributeTok{median =} \FunctionTok{median}\NormalTok{(value),}
    \AttributeTok{q05    =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(value, }\FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{q95    =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(value, }\FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{p\_neg  =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(value }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{p\_pos  =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(value }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{knitr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{kable}\NormalTok{(coef\_summary, }\AttributeTok{digits =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}lrrrrrr@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
coef & mean & median & q05 & q95 & p\_neg & p\_pos \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
b\_ecv & -0.423 & -0.417 & -0.835 & -0.028 & 0.957 & 0.043 \\
b\_group & 0.260 & 0.266 & -0.090 & 0.582 & 0.104 & 0.896 \\
\end{longtable}

Interpretation rests on whether each coefficient's 90\% credible
interval excludes zero, on the sign, and on which of the two carries
more credible mass. Note well: with only seven species and two
correlated-but-not-collinear covariates, statistical power at the
species level is modest --- this is an inference about an evolutionary
landscape sampled at seven points, and our uncertainty should reflect
that. An honest report sounds like ``the posterior of \(b_{\text{ECV}}\)
places X\% of mass below zero, while \(b_{\text{group}}\) places Y\%
below zero'', not ``ECV is the winner''.

The second plot checks that the regression is sensible at all: if the
regression-implied species means fall close to the diagonal against the
non-regression baseline, then the covariates are capturing most of the
between-species variance. A scatter of points far off the diagonal would
indicate that ECV and group size jointly miss an important species-level
dimension (perhaps neocortex ratio, diet, or rearing --- all present in
the raw CSV).

\section{Cross-check: species-level stacking
weights}\label{cross-check-species-level-stacking-weights}

The mixture model delivered species-level architecture probabilities
\(\pi_s\) as \emph{parameters}, with full posterior propagation. As a
methodological cross-check, we can compare those against species-level
PSIS stacking weights computed from two separately-fit hierarchical
models (a pure-0-ToM hier and a pure-1-ToM hier). The two estimators are
answering related but formally distinct questions --- \(\pi_s\) is a
prior mixing proportion inside a joint generative model, while stacking
weights are frequentist-flavored pseudo-BMA weights for out-of-sample
predictive averaging --- so exact agreement is not expected, but gross
divergence would be concerning.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{loo0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_hier}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{loo1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_hier\_tom1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{loo}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{species\_of\_trial }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sd\_int}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{species\_lookup}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{species[}
\NormalTok{  sd\_int}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{species\_of[sd\_int}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent]}
\NormalTok{]}
\NormalTok{species\_stack }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{split}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_along}\NormalTok{(species\_of\_trial), species\_of\_trial) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{imap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(idx, sp) \{}
\NormalTok{    lpd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cbind}\NormalTok{(loo0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pointwise[idx, }\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{],}
\NormalTok{                 loo1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pointwise[idx, }\StringTok{"elpd\_loo"}\NormalTok{])}
\NormalTok{    w   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ loo}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{stacking\_weights}\NormalTok{(lpd)}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{species =}\NormalTok{ sp, }\AttributeTok{w\_tom0 =}\NormalTok{ w[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{], }\AttributeTok{w\_tom1 =}\NormalTok{ w[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{])}
\NormalTok{  \})}

\NormalTok{stack\_compare }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ species\_stack }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(pi\_s\_summary }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(species, med, lo, hi),}
            \AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"species"}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(stack\_compare, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ w\_tom1, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ med, }\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ species)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"gray40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ lo, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ hi), }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
                \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"steelblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"midnightblue"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{  ggrepel}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{geom\_text\_repel}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{coord\_cartesian}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xlim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{ylim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Stacking weight for 1{-}ToM (LOO{-}based)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(pi[s] }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"(mixture posterior median)"}\NormalTok{),}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Stacking weights vs mixture \textbackslash{}u03c0\_s: cross{-}check"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Diagonal = perfect agreement; deviations indicate regularization vs data{-}driven differences"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{09-TheoryOfMind_files/figure-pdf/ch09_stacking_crosscheck-1.pdf}

}

\caption{Cross-check: species-level stacking weight for 1-ToM (x-axis,
LOO-based pseudo-BMA) vs.~mixture model posterior median π\_s (y-axis).
The two estimators answer related but formally distinct questions; gross
divergence from the diagonal would indicate the mixture's π\_s is driven
by prior regularization rather than data. Error bars are 90\% CIs on
π\_s from the mixture.}

\end{figure}%

The mixture model is the principled version --- it propagates
uncertainty, shares information appropriately across species via the
regression, and puts the scientific estimand on the right scale. The
stacking cross-check exists to verify that the mixture's \(\pi_s\) is
not an artifact of prior-pulled regularization.

\section{Limitations}\label{limitations}

A candid list, because this chapter has pushed the framework as far as
it can honestly go on this dataset:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Fixed-parameter 1-ToM.} Our 1-ToM implementation tracks the
  first two moments of the agent's belief about the opponent's belief
  about itself, conditional on point values
  \((\sigma_{\text{op}}, \beta_{\text{op}})\). The agent does not
  represent uncertainty over those opponent parameters. The
  full-variational scheme of Devaine et al.~(2014a) does, and would
  require a specialized message-passing backend to sample efficiently.
  What we have estimated is therefore a 1-ToM agent whose internal
  opponent model is a \emph{fixed} 0-ToM with known parameters, not a
  Bayesian learner over opponent sophistication.
\item
  \textbf{Structural unidentifiability in matching pennies.} Phase 4b
  SBC confirmed what the Devaine et al.~(2014a) variational analysis
  anticipates: \((\log\sigma_{\text{op}}, \log\beta_{\text{op}})\) are
  weakly identified by matching-pennies choice sequences. Once
  \(\Sigma^{\text{self}}\) reaches its fixed point, the predicted
  opponent-choice trajectory depends on these parameters only through a
  compressed near-one-dimensional ridge. No amount of Bayesian machinery
  can manufacture likelihood contrast that the design does not provide.
  A reversal paradigm or a richer payoff matrix is the appropriate
  remedy.
\item
  \textbf{Mixture identifiability is design-bounded.} The per-individual
  posterior \(\Pr(z_j = 1)\) is informed only to the extent that 0-ToM
  and 1-ToM make differentiable predictions for that individual --- a
  leverage quantity we measured directly in the Occam's-razor panel.
  Individuals whose choice sequences are predictively equivalent under
  the two architectures have posteriors that return to \(\pi_{s(j)}\).
  This is correct Bayesian behavior, but it means our species-level
  \(\pi_s\) posteriors inherit the design's limited per-individual
  separability.
\item
  \textbf{Single-step reactive models.} 0-ToM, 1-ToM, and influence
  learning all plan only one step ahead. They cannot strategically
  deceive, willingly cooperate for long-run gain, or explore at the cost
  of immediate reward. Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma exposes every one of
  them.
\item
  \textbf{2×2 games only.} The framework generalizes to arbitrary payoff
  matrices, but both the primate study and this chapter live inside a
  single-choice, two-option game. Naturalistic social interactions are
  orders of magnitude richer, and richer settings are exactly where
  higher-\(k\) ToM should make a larger difference (Devaine et al.,
  2017, Discussion).
\item
  \textbf{Seven species is a small N.} The covariate regressions are
  honest but statistically modest --- an inference about an evolutionary
  landscape sampled at seven points. The phylogenetic structure of
  primates also means the species residuals around the regression line
  are not independent in the usual sense (Devaine et al., 2017 comment
  on this); a fuller analysis would embed a phylogenetic
  variance-covariance on \(u_s\).
\item
  \textbf{Cooperative framing.} Primates partially read the game as
  cooperative because their opponent is also their feeder. All of our
  models have been written for the competitive payoff matrix; a switch
  to cooperative incentives would flip signs in the influence and 1-ToM
  equations and should be fit as a separate model (not treated as
  noise).
\item
  \textbf{No true \(k \ge 2\).} The model set stops at \(k = 1\).
  Devaine et al.~(2017) report an evolutionary gap between great apes
  and humans precisely at \(k = 2\), which our implementation cannot
  currently reach. Extending the recursive belief-update machinery to
  maintain a posterior over opponent sophistication level --- and adding
  a third mixture component --- is the natural capstone project.
\item
  \textbf{A better predictive score is not a better mechanistic
  explanation.} Ch. 7's caveat applies with extra force here. Posterior
  architecture probability \(\Pr(z_j = 1)\) summarizes predictive
  structure in the choice sequences, not direct evidence of mentalizing
  in the neurocognitive sense.
\end{itemize}

\section{Conclusion}\label{conclusion-1}

We have traveled from \emph{noticing} that a competitive game with an
adaptive opponent breaks the passive-environment assumption of earlier
chapters, through three computational instantiations of that observation
(0-ToM, influence learning, fixed-parameter 1-ToM), through a complete
validation battery (prior predictive, parameter recovery, SBC for both
models, prior sensitivity, posterior predictive, and an Occam's-razor
model comparison study), to a joint hierarchical mixture model that
places representational-capacity probabilities on the left-hand side of
the covariate regression and answers the actual scientific question
directly. Nothing in this pipeline is new machinery --- every tool came
from Ch. 5, Ch. 6, or Ch. 7. What changed is what the model is
\emph{about}: the object of inference is no longer a statistical
regularity but a mind that is modeling a mind, and the estimand is not
how sharply that mind acts but whether it is mentalizing at all. That
shift is what makes the chapter worth writing, and it is the same shift
that makes \(k\)-ToM one of the most promising formal definitions on
offer for a concept --- \emph{theory of mind} --- that has for decades
existed as a verbal construct in search of a mechanism.

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Bayesian Models of Cognition}\label{sec-bayesian-models}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chs. 1--8
used the Bayesian \emph{statistical} framework as a tool to model
cognitive processes in the matching-pennies task. This chapter
introduces a distinct scientific claim: the \emph{Bayesian cognition
hypothesis} --- the proposal that the mind itself operates according to
Bayes' theorem. We use our Bayesian statistical tools to test whether
this hypothesis fits human behaviour in a social learning task, applying
the full validation battery from Chs. 5--7. The chapter also introduces
Leave-Future-Out cross-validation for sequential models, a technique
flagged in Ch. 8 but not developed there.
\end{quote}

\section{Bayesian statistics vs.~Bayesian
Cognition}\label{bayesian-statistics-vs.-bayesian-cognition}

This entire course has used \emph{Bayesian statistics} as its
inferential framework: we represent uncertainty as probability
distributions, write likelihoods, specify priors, and obtain posteriors
via Stan. That is our \emph{tool} to estimate any kind of model.

This chapter introduces a distinct scientific claim: the \emph{Bayesian
cognition hypothesis}. This is the proposal that the \emph{mind itself}
operates according to principles analogous to Bayes' theorem --- that
people represent beliefs as probability distributions, update them
optimally when new evidence arrives, and combine information sources in
proportion to their reliability. Whether this hypothesis is empirically
correct is an open and contested question. We will use our Bayesian
statistical tools to derive from the theory models to analyse some
experimental data.

N.B. Bayes as a tool and Bayes as a model are very different ways of
using Bayes. Indeed, we have fitted non-Bayesian models using Bayesian
techniques, and there are many papers out there testing Bayesian models
of cognition using frequentist techniques.

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Historical Context: The Bayesian Brain --- From Helmholtz to Predictive
Coding}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The idea that the mind performs something like Bayesian inference is not
a recent invention of computational cognitive science. It is a
rediscovery --- and mathematical formalisation --- of a proposal that
Hermann von Helmholtz articulated in 1867, before probability theory had
the tools to make it precise.

\subsubsection{Perception as Unconscious
Inference}\label{perception-as-unconscious-inference}

Helmholtz observed that the retinal image dramatically underdetermines
the state of the world: the same pattern of light could be produced by
infinitely many physical configurations. His conclusion was that
perception must involve \emph{unconscious inference} --- the visual
system implicitly reasons from ambiguous sensory data to the most
probable cause, drawing on prior knowledge about how the world is
structured (Helmholtz 1867). This is, in substance, a description of
Bayesian posterior inference, but articulated without probability theory
and without a mechanism.

For nearly a century the proposal remained an influential metaphor
rather than a testable theory. The mathematical tools existed --- Bayes'
theorem dates to 1763 --- but applying them to cognition required a
formalism for representing prior knowledge and a computational
infrastructure for performing inference, neither of which was available
to nineteenth-century psychologists.

\subsubsection{The Ideal Observer
Tradition}\label{the-ideal-observer-tradition}

The bridge between Helmholtz's intuition and formal modeling was built
by the \textbf{ideal observer} programme in perception research. An
ideal observer is defined as a hypothetical agent that performs
statistically optimal inference given the information available --- in
other words, a Bayesian. Geisler (1989) showed that ideal observers
could predict human perceptual thresholds and detection curves with
surprising accuracy. Ernst and Banks (2002) demonstrated Bayesian
integration of visual and haptic size estimates in humans: when the two
cues conflict, humans weight them in inverse proportion to their
variance, exactly as a Bayesian would. These results were influential
because they quantified the hypothesis rather than merely asserting it,
and because they made precise, falsifiable predictions about how
integration should depend on noise levels.

\subsubsection{``How to Grow a Mind'': The Bayesian Cognition
Programme}\label{how-to-grow-a-mind-the-bayesian-cognition-programme}

The broader claim --- that Bayesian inference characterises not just
low-level perception but high-level cognition --- was synthesised and
publicised by Tenenbaum et al. (2011). The programme demonstrated that
Bayesian models could account for rapid concept learning from sparse
data (the ``size principle'': smaller hypothesis spaces are preferred a
priori), intuitive theories of physics and psychology, causal reasoning,
and language acquisition. The key methodological move was to define a
prior over a structured hypothesis space --- a space of programs, causal
graphs, or grammars --- and to use Bayes' theorem to perform inference
over that space given data. The framework explained not just what people
infer but \emph{how fast} they do so from how little evidence, which had
been the central embarrassment for associationist and purely data-driven
accounts.

\subsubsection{Predictive Coding and the Free Energy
Principle}\label{predictive-coding-and-the-free-energy-principle}

A parallel strand of development, more neurally oriented, produced
predictive coding (Rao and Ballard 1999) and the free energy principle
(Friston 2005). In these frameworks, the brain is organised
hierarchically: each level sends predictions \emph{downward} to the
level below and receives \emph{prediction errors} upward. Perception is
the process of minimising prediction error (or equivalently, of finding
the posterior over hidden states that best explains sensory input).
Action is the process of changing the world to match predictions. The
framework is formally equivalent to variational Bayesian inference with
an approximate posterior, and it connects Helmholtz's unconscious
inference to measurable neural signals (the mismatch negativity,
attentional modulation, and dopaminergic prediction errors).

\subsubsection{The Falsifiability Critique and the Methodological
Answer}\label{the-falsifiability-critique-and-the-methodological-answer}

The Bayesian cognition programme attracted a pointed critique: the
framework is too flexible to be falsifiable. With sufficiently
unconstrained priors and likelihood functions, almost any behavioral
pattern can be ``explained'' post hoc (Jones and Love 2011; Bowers and
Davis 2012). The critique is well-founded when Bayesian models are used
purely as descriptive devices, fitting parameters to data without asking
whether the generative story is correct.

The response --- embodied in the workflow this book has built since
Chapter 5 --- is methodological rather than theoretical. Prior
predictive checks ask whether the model's prior beliefs produce
plausible data; simulation-based calibration checks whether parameter
recovery is reliable; LOO cross-validation assesses whether the model
predicts held-out observations; model comparison with power analysis
asks whether the experimental design has the discriminative resolution
to distinguish Bayesian from non-Bayesian accounts. None of these tests
is specific to the Bayesian cognition hypothesis, but all are necessary
to make the hypothesis empirically tractable. The difference between a
Bayesian model used as a just-so story and one used as a genuine
scientific claim lies entirely in the rigour of the validation pipeline.

\end{tcolorbox}

\section{Introduction}\label{introduction-3}

The human mind constantly receives input from multiple sources: direct
sensory evidence, social information from others, and prior knowledge
built from experience (perhaps even from innate structures). A
fundamental question in cognitive science is how these disparate pieces
of information are combined to produce coherent beliefs about the world.

The Bayesian framework offers a powerful approach to formalizing this
process. Under this framework, the mind is conceptualized as a
probabilistic machine that continuously updates its beliefs based on new
evidence. This contrasts with rule-based or purely associative models of
cognition by emphasizing:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Representations of uncertainty:} Beliefs are represented as
  probability distributions, not single values.
\item
  \textbf{Optimal integration:} Information is combined according to its
  reliability.
\item
  \textbf{Prior knowledge:} New evidence is interpreted in light of
  existing beliefs.
\end{itemize}

Formally, Bayes' theorem states:

\[P(\text{belief} \mid \text{evidence}) \propto P(\text{evidence} \mid \text{belief}) \times P(\text{belief})\]

where \(P(\text{belief} \mid \text{evidence})\) is the posterior,
\(P(\text{evidence} \mid \text{belief})\) is the likelihood, and
\(P(\text{belief})\) is the prior. The \(\propto\) symbol denotes
proportionality --- the product on the right should in principle be
normalised by dividing by \(P(\text{evidence})\) to recover a proper
probability. We often ignore this normalization constant, sometimes
because it cancels out, sometimes because our sampling methods can
approximate it away, sometimes because it doesn't rely on belief.

\section{Learning Objectives}\label{learning-objectives-9}

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Understand the basic principles of Bayesian information integration
  and distinguish the \emph{Bayesian cognition} hypothesis from the
  \emph{Bayesian statistics} toolkit.
\item
  Implement cognitive models --- starting with a Simple Bayesian Agent
  (SBA) and a Weighted Bayesian Agent (WBA), discover their limitations,
  and then develop reparameterised solutions including a Proportional
  Bayesian Agent (PBA).
\item
  Recognize that the \emph{observation model} (how beliefs become
  actions) is a separate modelling dimension from the
  \emph{belief-update model} (how evidence is integrated).
\item
  Execute the full Bayesian workflow (prior predictive checks → fitting
  → diagnostics → parameter recovery → SBC → model comparison) on
  cognitive models.
\item
  Identify when models have identifiability issues and how
  reparameterisation can solve them.
\end{itemize}

\section{The Bayesian Framework for
Cognition}\label{the-bayesian-framework-for-cognition}

Bayesian models of cognition explore the idea that the mind operates
according to principles similar to Bayes' theorem, combining different
sources of evidence to form updated beliefs. Most commonly this is
framed in terms of prior beliefs being updated with new evidence to form
a posterior.

\subsection{Visualising Bayesian
Updating}\label{visualising-bayesian-updating}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{x          }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.01}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{prior      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(x, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{likelihood }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(x, }\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{posterior  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(x, }\DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 1. Define the length explicitly outside the tibble}
\NormalTok{n\_x }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(x) }

\NormalTok{plot\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{x            =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(x, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{density      =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(prior, likelihood, posterior),}
  \AttributeTok{distribution =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}
    \CommentTok{\# 2. Use the explicit length variable here}
    \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Likelihood"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Posterior"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_x), }
    \AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Likelihood"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Posterior"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(plot\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ x, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ density,}
                      \AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ distribution, }\AttributeTok{linetype =}\NormalTok{ distribution)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Likelihood"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#756bb1"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_linetype\_manual}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Prior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Likelihood"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Posterior"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"solid"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Bayesian Updating: Prior × Likelihood ∝ Posterior"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Uniform prior updated with 7 blue / 2 red marbles"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"Belief (proportion blue)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Probability density"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{colour   =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}

\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_visualising_bayesian_updating-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The posterior is narrower than either source alone --- combining
information increases certainty. Its peak lies between prior and
likelihood, but closer to the likelihood because the evidence was
relatively strong. It is also more confident than the likelihood, as it
combines compatible evidence from prior and likelihood. Bayesian
integration is not averaging; it is precision-weighted combination (as
we also discussed in chapter 3.7).

\subsection{Bayesian Models in Cognitive
Science}\label{bayesian-models-in-cognitive-science}

Bayesian cognitive models have been successfully applied to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Perception:} combining multi-sensory cues (visual, auditory,
  tactile) into a unified percept.
\item
  \textbf{Learning:} updating beliefs from observation and instruction.
\item
  \textbf{Decision-making:} weighing different evidence sources when
  making choices.
\item
  \textbf{Social cognition:} integrating others' opinions with one's own
  knowledge.
\item
  \textbf{Psychopathology:} understanding schizophrenia and autism in
  terms of atypical Bayesian inference --- hyper-precise priors, reduced
  likelihood weighting, or atypical weighting of social information.
\end{itemize}

\section{The Setup: Social Influence in Perceptual
Decision-Making}\label{the-setup-social-influence-in-perceptual-decision-making}

To make our discussion of bayesian models concrete, let us consider a
simplified version of a study examining how people integrate information
from different sources (Simonsen et al., 2021).

In the \textbf{marble task}:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  There is a set of urns with varying proportions of blue and red
  marbles
\item
  At each trial the urn changes (no learning is possible across trials)
\item
  At each trial you sample \(n_1 = 8\) marbles → \textbf{Source 1
  (Direct Evidence)}
\item
  At each trial another participant samples (hidden to you) and
  communicates their guess as to the next ball, including their
  confidence → \textbf{Source 2 (Social Evidence)}
\item
  Source 1 ranges from 0 to 8 blue marbles
\item
  Source 2 is encoded as 0--3 (Clear Red, Maybe Red, Maybe Blue, Clear
  Blue)
\item
  The experiment is built so you get all combinations of Source 1 and
  Source 2, with 5 repetitions each
\end{itemize}

This encoding introduces a strict measurement model assumption. The
other person actually drew 8 marbles, but we are compressing their
choice-plus-confidence signal into a smaller equivalent sample size
(\(n_2 = 3\)). Remember, other choices would be possible, and perhaps
better than this, but this is very convenient as it sets both sources on
the same scale.

\subsection{The Beta Distribution
Intuition}\label{the-beta-distribution-intuition}

The Beta distribution provides an elegant way to represent beliefs about
proportions such as the proportion of blue marbles in a jar. Its two
parameters \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) have an intuitive interpretation:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \(\alpha\) can be thought of as the number of blue marbles observed
  (plus the prior pseudo-count \(\alpha_0\)).
\item
  \(\beta\) can be thought of as the number of red marbles observed
  (plus the prior pseudo-count \(\beta_0\)).
\end{itemize}

What should we put as prior? We previously chose a uniform distribution
on the 0-1 scale, which corresponds to \(\text{Beta}(1, 1)\). Another
common choice is the Jeffreys prior: \(\text{Beta}(0.5, 0.5)\). The
Jeffreys prior is defined as \emph{parameterisation-invariant}: it
assigns equal information to each value of \(\theta\) \emph{on the
log-odds scale}, which is the natural scale for a Bernoulli likelihood.

As usual, it's easier to understand our choices if we plot the priors
and their consequences.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{theta\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.001}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.999}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{length.out =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{n\_grid     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid)}

\NormalTok{prior\_comparison }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{theta =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{density =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{),}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ),}
  \AttributeTok{prior =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Jeffreys: Beta(0.5, 0.5)"}\NormalTok{,}
          \StringTok{"Uniform: Beta(1, 1)"}\NormalTok{,}
          \StringTok{"Weakly informative: Beta(2, 2)"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_grid),}
    \AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Jeffreys: Beta(0.5, 0.5)"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Uniform: Beta(1, 1)"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Weakly informative: Beta(2, 2)"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_priors }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_comparison, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ density,}
                                          \AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ prior, }\AttributeTok{linetype =}\NormalTok{ prior)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#756bb1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_linetype\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"solid"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Three Prior Choices for a Binomial Proportion"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Jeffreys concentrates mass near 0 and 1;}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{Uniform is flat; Beta(2,2) pulls toward 0.5"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(theta }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"(proportion blue)"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Prior density"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{colour   =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Same priors viewed on the log{-}odds scale {-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Transform: if theta \textasciitilde{} Beta(a, b), what does logit(theta) look like?}
\CommentTok{\# We use a change{-}of{-}variables: density on logit scale =}
\CommentTok{\#   dbeta(inv\_logit(x), a, b) * inv\_logit(x) * (1 {-} inv\_logit(x))}
\NormalTok{logit\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{length.out =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{inv\_logit  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(logit\_grid)}
\NormalTok{jacobian   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ inv\_logit }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ inv\_logit)}

\NormalTok{logit\_comparison }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{logit\_theta =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(logit\_grid, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{density =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(inv\_logit, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ jacobian,}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(inv\_logit, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)     }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ jacobian,}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(inv\_logit, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)     }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ jacobian}
\NormalTok{  ),}
  \AttributeTok{prior =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Jeffreys: Beta(0.5, 0.5)"}\NormalTok{,}
          \StringTok{"Uniform: Beta(1, 1)"}\NormalTok{,}
          \StringTok{"Weakly informative: Beta(2, 2)"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{each =} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(logit\_grid)),}
    \AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Jeffreys: Beta(0.5, 0.5)"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Uniform: Beta(1, 1)"}\NormalTok{,}
               \StringTok{"Weakly informative: Beta(2, 2)"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_logit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(logit\_comparison, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ logit\_theta, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ density,}
                                         \AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ prior, }\AttributeTok{linetype =}\NormalTok{ prior)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#756bb1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_linetype\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"solid"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"The Same Priors on the Log{-}Odds Scale"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Jeffreys is nearly flat — it assigns equal information per unit log{-}odds.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{Uniform concentrates mass near logit(θ) = 0 (i.e., θ = 0.5)."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"logit("} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ theta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{")"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Density (change of variables)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{colour   =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Posterior comparison after 6 blue, 2 red {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{blue\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{; red\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{2}

\NormalTok{posterior\_comparison }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{theta =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{density =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ blue\_obs, }\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ red\_obs),}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ blue\_obs,   }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ red\_obs),}
    \FunctionTok{dbeta}\NormalTok{(theta\_grid, }\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ blue\_obs,   }\DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ red\_obs)}
\NormalTok{  ),}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_used =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Jeffreys → Beta(6.5, 2.5)"}\NormalTok{,}
          \StringTok{"Uniform → Beta(7, 3)"}\NormalTok{,}
          \StringTok{"Beta(2,2) → Beta(8, 4)"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_grid)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(posterior\_comparison, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ density,}
                                            \AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ prior\_used)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \FloatTok{6.5} \SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{,  }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{7} \SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{10}\NormalTok{,   }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{8} \SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{,   }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{,}
             \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#756bb1"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#756bb1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \FloatTok{0.15}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(posterior\_comparison}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{density) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FloatTok{0.85}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{label =} \FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Max Δ mean = \%.3f"}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{8}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{12} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{6.5}\SpecialCharTok{/}\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{),}
           \AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"grey30"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Posteriors After 6 Blue, 2 Red: The Practical Difference"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"With 8 observations the prior choice barely matters (max Δ ≈ 0.06)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(theta),}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Posterior density"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{colour   =} \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_prior_comparison-3.pdf}
\end{center}

The three panels above tell a clear story. On the probability scale
(top), the Jeffreys prior looks extreme --- it concentrates mass near 0
and 1, as if expecting jars to be mostly one colour. The uniform prior
looks neutral. But as usual, this is a matter of scale: on the log-odds
scale, where the Bernoulli likelihood naturally lives, Jeffreys is the
one that is approximately flat. The uniform prior, seemingly innocuous,
quietly concentrates mass near logit(θ)=0 --- encoding a more pronounced
preference for balanced jars than the Jeffreys prior. The Beta(2,2)
prior goes further, explicitly pulling toward \(θ = 0.5\).

I generally prefer to think in probability (0-1) scale, but others
prefer the log-odds scale. As usual, it's a good idea to investigate the
consequences of this choice. The bottom panel shows that the
consequences are not drastic. After observing just 6 blue and 2 red
marbles, the three posteriors are nearly indistinguishable: the Jeffreys
prior gives Beta(6.5,2.5) with mean ≈ 0.72, the uniform gives Beta(7,3)
with mean = 0.70, and the Beta(2,2) prior gives Beta(8,4) with mean ≈
0.67. The maximum shift across all three is ≈0.06 --- the data have
overwhelmed the prior after a single sample of n = 8 marbles. Just to
challenge myself to choices I am not used to, we use the Jeffreys prior
throughout this chapter.

\subsection{Setting Up the Experimental
Design}\label{setting-up-the-experimental-design}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Create the full factorial design}
\NormalTok{evidence\_combinations }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{blue1  =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{,   }\CommentTok{\# Direct evidence: 0{-}8 blue marbles out of 8}
  \AttributeTok{blue2  =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{,   }\CommentTok{\# Social evidence: encoded as 0{-}3 (pseudo{-}marbles)}
  \AttributeTok{total1 =} \DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{,     }\CommentTok{\# Fixed sample size for direct evidence}
  \AttributeTok{total2 =} \DecValTok{3}      \CommentTok{\# Fixed "sample size" for social evidence}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# This gives us 9 × 4 = 36 unique evidence combinations}
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Unique evidence combinations:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(evidence\_combinations), }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
Unique evidence combinations: 36 
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"With 5 repetitions each:"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(evidence\_combinations) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"trials per agent}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
With 5 repetitions each: 180 trials per agent
\end{verbatim}

\section{The Beta-Binomial
Likelihood}\label{the-beta-binomial-likelihood}

How do we model evidence when it arrives as counts --- k blue marbles
out of n total observed? The Beta distribution provides a natural
representation of beliefs about a proportion, and combined with a
Bernoulli choice it gives us the \textbf{Beta-Binomial likelihood} that
both agents in this chapter will use.

The two parameters \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) have an intuitive
interpretation as pseudo-counts:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \(\alpha\) = prior pseudo-count + (weighted) blue marbles from all
  sources
\item
  \(\beta\) = prior pseudo-count + (weighted) red marbles from all
  sources
\end{itemize}

Using the Jeffreys prior (\(\alpha_0 = \beta_0 = 0.5\)), the posterior
parameters after observing direct evidence \((k_1, n_1)\) and social
evidence \((k_2, n_2)\) with weights \(w_d, w_s\) are:

\[\alpha_{\text{post}} = 0.5 + w_d \cdot k_1 + w_s \cdot k_2\]
\[\beta_{\text{post}}  = 0.5 + w_d \cdot (n_1 - k_1) + w_s \cdot (n_2 - k_2)\]

Setting \(w_d = w_s = 1\) recovers strict Bayesian updating (Simple
Bayesian Agent, SBA); letting them vary will give us the Weighted
Bayesian Agent (WBA). We'll meet both formally in the next section. For
now we focus on the likelihood itself.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Core belief{-}update function implementing the Beta{-}Binomial integration model}
\NormalTok{betaBinomialModel }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha\_prior =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{beta\_prior =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{                               blue1, total1, blue2, total2,}
                               \AttributeTok{w\_direct =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{w\_social =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
  
\NormalTok{  alpha\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_prior }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_direct }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ blue1        }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_social }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ blue2}
\NormalTok{  beta\_post  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta\_prior  }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_direct }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (total1 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ blue1) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{                               w\_social }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (total2 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ blue2)}

\NormalTok{  expected\_rate }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_post }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_post }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_post)}
\NormalTok{  ci\_lower      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{qbeta}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.025}\NormalTok{, alpha\_post, beta\_post)}
\NormalTok{  ci\_upper      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{qbeta}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.975}\NormalTok{, alpha\_post, beta\_post)}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{alpha\_post    =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_post,}
    \AttributeTok{beta\_post     =}\NormalTok{ beta\_post,}
    \AttributeTok{expected\_rate =}\NormalTok{ expected\_rate,}
    \AttributeTok{ci\_lower      =}\NormalTok{ ci\_lower,}
    \AttributeTok{ci\_upper      =}\NormalTok{ ci\_upper,}
    \AttributeTok{choice        =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(expected\_rate }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Blue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Red"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Function to generate decisions from a simulated agent}
\NormalTok{generate\_agent\_decisions }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(weight\_direct, weight\_social,}
\NormalTok{                                      evidence\_df, }\AttributeTok{n\_samples =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  evidence\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{slice}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{n}\NormalTok{()), }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_samples)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(blue1, blue2, total1, total2) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sample\_id =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{n}\NormalTok{())) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(blue1, blue2, total1, total2, sample\_id) \{}
\NormalTok{      result }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{betaBinomialModel}\NormalTok{(}
        \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, blue1, total1, blue2, total2,}
        \AttributeTok{w\_direct =}\NormalTok{ weight\_direct, }\AttributeTok{w\_social =}\NormalTok{ weight\_social}
\NormalTok{      )}
      \CommentTok{\# Probability matching: sample θ from Beta, then sample choice from Bernoulli(θ)}
\NormalTok{      alpha\_p }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{alpha\_post}
\NormalTok{      beta\_p  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{beta\_post}
\NormalTok{      p       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbeta}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, alpha\_p, beta\_p)}
      \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{sample\_id     =}\NormalTok{ sample\_id,}
        \AttributeTok{blue1         =}\NormalTok{ blue1,}
        \AttributeTok{blue2         =}\NormalTok{ blue2,}
        \AttributeTok{total1        =}\NormalTok{ total1,}
        \AttributeTok{total2        =}\NormalTok{ total2,}
        \AttributeTok{expected\_rate =}\NormalTok{ result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{expected\_rate,}
        \AttributeTok{choice        =} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, p),}
        \AttributeTok{choice\_label  =} \FunctionTok{ifelse}\NormalTok{(choice }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Blue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Red"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      )}
\NormalTok{    \})}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{prepare\_stan\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(df) \{}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{N       =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(df),}
    \AttributeTok{choice  =}\NormalTok{ df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
    \AttributeTok{N\_total =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(df),}
    \AttributeTok{y       =}\NormalTok{ df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
    \AttributeTok{blue1   =}\NormalTok{ df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1,}
    \AttributeTok{total1  =}\NormalTok{ df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1,}
    \AttributeTok{blue2   =}\NormalTok{ df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2,}
    \AttributeTok{total2  =}\NormalTok{ df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_rho\_alpha   =} \FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_rho\_beta    =} \FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_kappa\_mu    =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{prior\_kappa\_sigma =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{The Observation Model: A Separate Modelling
Dimension}\label{the-observation-model-a-separate-modelling-dimension}

The Bayesian agents as we have implemented them specify how they
\emph{integrate evidence} --- but they also embed a specific assumption
about how beliefs become \emph{actions}. Indeed, the
BetaBinomial(\(1, \alpha, \beta\)) observation model entails a two-step
stochastic process: first sample a belief
\(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\), then sample a choice
\(y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\). This is \textbf{probability
matching} --- the agent's action is a random sample from its full
posterior distribution (rather than from the averate \(\theta\)) over
the jar's composition.

I like this way of doing things, because all the uncertainty is
preserved and transmitted across all phases of the model. However, this
is in principle a testable cognitive hypothesis. A different agent might
act more deterministically, choosing blue whenever
\(\text{E}[\theta] > 0.5\), or following a graded rule where higher
confidence makes the choice more certain. We can formalise these
alternatives:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Observation model
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Cognitive claim
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Distribution
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
BetaBinomial (\(n=1\)) & \textbf{Probability matching:} action sampled
from belief & \(y \sim \text{BetaBin}(1, \alpha, \beta)\) \\
Softmax & \textbf{Graded determinism:} posterior mean compressed by
inverse temperature \(\phi\) &
\(y \sim \text{Bern}(\sigma(\phi \cdot \text{logit}(\hat\theta)))\) \\
Threshold & \textbf{Maximisation:} act on the most probable belief &
\(y = \mathbf{1}[\alpha > \beta]\) \\
\end{longtable}

The BetaBinomial propagates the agent's \emph{full uncertainty about the
jar} into the response: an agent who is genuinely uncertain about
\(\theta\) produces more variable responses than one who is confident.
This excess variability (overdispersion relative to a plain Binomial) is
not noise added on top of the model --- it \emph{is} the model's
cognitive claim. The softmax model, by contrast, collapses the posterior
to its mean \(\hat{\theta} = \alpha / (\alpha + \beta)\) and then
applies a noise parameter. It discards distributional information (but
it's an empirical question whether humans would do the same).

\subsection{How the BetaBinomial Generates a Distribution of
Choices}\label{how-the-betabinomial-generates-a-distribution-of-choices}

\textbf{The BetaBinomial likelihood is not a deterministic threshold
rule.} It describes a two-step stochastic process:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Draw a belief:} Sample the true proportion
  \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\), representing genuine
  uncertainty about the jar's composition.
\item
  \textbf{Draw a choice:} Given that \(\theta\), sample a binary choice
  \(y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\theta)\).
\end{enumerate}

The consequence is that even when the posterior mean is 0.70 in favour
of blue, the agent will not choose blue exactly 70\% of the time in a
perfectly mechanical way. There is variability from two sources
simultaneously: uncertainty about what the jar proportion actually
\emph{is} (step 1), and the irreducible randomness of acting on that
belief with a single guess (step 2). This variability is not noise added
on top of the model --- it \emph{is} the model. The Beta distribution
encodes what a rational agent should believe; the Bernoulli draw
captures the randomness of a single decision.

This two-stage structure has a measurable implication: the BetaBinomial
has \textbf{greater variance than a Binomial} with the same mean. This
excess variance is called \textbf{overdispersion}, and it arises
precisely because \(\theta\) must itself be sampled from the Beta rather
than being known.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Scenario: 4 blue of 8 direct + 2 blue of 3 social (Jeffreys prior)}
\NormalTok{alpha\_demo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{4} \SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{2}   \CommentTok{\# = 6.5}
\NormalTok{beta\_demo  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{4} \SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}   \CommentTok{\# = 5.5}
\NormalTok{n\_sims     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{10000}

\CommentTok{\# To see overdispersion, we must simulate MULTIPLE choices per agent.}
\CommentTok{\# Let\textquotesingle{}s assume each simulated agent makes 20 choices.}
\NormalTok{n\_choices  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{20} 

\NormalTok{expected\_rate\_demo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_demo }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_demo }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_demo)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Step 1: Simulate Both Models}
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}

\CommentTok{\# Model A: Beta{-}Binomial (Uncertainty propagates)}
\CommentTok{\# Draw theta from Beta, then draw successes based on that theta}
\NormalTok{theta\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbeta}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, alpha\_demo, beta\_demo)}
\NormalTok{bb\_draws    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, }\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_choices, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ theta\_draws)}

\CommentTok{\# Model B: Standard Binomial (Fixed belief, no uncertainty in theta)}
\CommentTok{\# Draw successes using the fixed expected rate}
\NormalTok{binom\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_sims, }\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ n\_choices, }\AttributeTok{prob =}\NormalTok{ expected\_rate\_demo)}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}
\CommentTok{\# Step 2: Visualization}
\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}{-}}

\CommentTok{\# Plot 1: The uncertainty in theta (similar to your left panel)}
\NormalTok{p\_theta }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{theta =}\NormalTok{ theta\_draws) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ theta)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_density}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"transparent"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =}\NormalTok{ expected\_rate\_demo,}
             \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{labels =}\NormalTok{ scales}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{percent) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Step 1: Uncertainty in Belief (θ)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \FunctionTok{sprintf}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Beta(\%.1f, \%.1f) | Dashed line is E[θ] = \%.0f\%\%"}\NormalTok{, }
\NormalTok{                       alpha\_demo, beta\_demo, expected\_rate\_demo }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{100}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"True underlying rate ("} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ theta }\SpecialCharTok{*} \StringTok{")"}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Density"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Plot 2: The outcome comparison showing overdispersion}
\NormalTok{plot\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{Beta{-}Binomial (Uncertain θ)}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ bb\_draws,}
  \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{Standard Binomial (Fixed θ)}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=}\NormalTok{ binom\_draws}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cols =} \FunctionTok{everything}\NormalTok{(), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"Model"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"Successes"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_spread }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(plot\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ Successes, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ Model)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
