\subsection{Visualising the Classification
Boundary}\label{visualising-the-classification-boundary}

What separates correctly classified agents from misclassified ones? We
plot the agents in the estimated \((\rho, \kappa)\) space, marking
classifications.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mix\_class\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mix\_recovery }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(agent\_elpd }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, best\_model, elpd\_advantage),}
            \AttributeTok{by =} \StringTok{"agent\_id"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{correct =}\NormalTok{ (true\_strategy }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"SBA"} \SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{ best\_model }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"SBA"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|}
\NormalTok{                   (true\_strategy }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"WBA"} \SpecialCharTok{\&}\NormalTok{ best\_model }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"PBA"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"WBA"}\NormalTok{)))}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(mix\_class\_data,}
       \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ est\_rho, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ est\_kappa,}
           \AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ true\_strategy, }\AttributeTok{shape =}\NormalTok{ correct)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"grey60"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dotted"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"grey60"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_colour\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"SBA"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#4daf4a"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"WBA"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#984ea3"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_shape\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"TRUE"} \OtherTok{=} \DecValTok{16}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"FALSE"} \OtherTok{=} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{),}
                     \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"TRUE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Correct"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"FALSE"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"Misclassified"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Classification in Estimated Parameter Space"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Dotted lines show SBA reference values (ρ = 0.5, κ = 2.0). Crosses = misclassified."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Estimated "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ rho[j]),}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Estimated "} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ kappa[j]),}
    \AttributeTok{colour   =} \StringTok{"True Strategy"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{shape =} \StringTok{"Classification"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_mixed_classification_boundary-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Misclassifications, when they occur, concentrate near the boundary
between the two strategy clusters --- agents whose estimated \(\rho\)
sits between 0.35 and 0.5, where the SBA and WBA strategies are hardest
to discriminate.

\subsection{Discussion: What the Mixed-Population Analysis
Reveals}\label{discussion-what-the-mixed-population-analysis-reveals}

This analysis demonstrates three important lessons for applied cognitive
modelling:

\textbf{1. Population parameters are misleading for heterogeneous
groups.} The ML-WBA's estimated population mean \(\rho\) averaged over
two qualitatively different strategies to produce a summary that
describes nobody. This is not a failure of the hierarchical model
\emph{per se} --- it is a structural consequence of fitting a unimodal
population distribution to a bimodal mixture. When you suspect strategy
heterogeneity, do not interpret population means at face value.

\textbf{2. Individual-level recovery succeeds despite population-level
misspecification.} The ML-WBA, though structurally unable to represent a
mixture population, still recovers individual \(\rho_j\) and
\(\kappa_j\) values well enough to separate the two groups. The
estimated population SD absorbs the between-group variance, and the
partial pooling is mild enough that the bimodal structure in the
individual estimates is preserved. A formal mixture model (as in Ch. 9)
would be more appropriate --- it would estimate the mixing proportion
and the within-group population distributions directly --- but the
standard hierarchical model is a surprisingly robust approximation.

\textbf{3. Per-agent model comparison identifies strategy types.} By
decomposing the overall LOO into agent-level contributions, we can
classify individuals as SBA-type or WBA-type with reasonable accuracy.
This connects directly to the mixture modelling approach from Ch. 9:
where a mixture model infers strategy membership \emph{within} the
generative model, per-agent LOO achieves a similar classification
\emph{post hoc} through predictive comparison.

The practical implication for researchers: if you suspect your
participants use different strategies, always look beyond
population-level summaries. Examine individual parameter estimates for
multimodality, and use per-agent model comparison to classify
participants before interpreting group effects.

\section{Beyond Weighted Bayes: Circular
Inference}\label{beyond-weighted-bayes-circular-inference}

The models we have explored so far --- SBA, PBA, WBA --- share a common
structural assumption: each source of evidence is counted exactly once.
Simonsen et al.~(2021), following Jardri et al.~(2017), found that this
assumption is systematically violated in human reasoning ---
particularly in patients with schizophrenia. Their best-fitting model
was \emph{circular inference}: evidence from one source is fed back into
the system and counted multiple times.

\subsection{The Log-Odds Transformation
Function}\label{the-log-odds-transformation-function}

To properly model circular inference, pseudocounts (\(\alpha, \beta\))
are not enough. We must shift our integration into the \textbf{log-odds
space}. If we express our evidence \(L\) as log-odds, Jardri and
colleagues define a specific non-linear transformation function
\(F(L, w)\) that applies a weight \(w \in [0.5, 1]\) to that evidence:

\[F(L,w) = \log \frac{w e^L + 1 - w}{(1 - w)e^L + w}\]

Let's visualize how this function behaves in R.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# The core Jardri integration function}
\NormalTok{JardriF }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(L, w) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# L is evidence in log{-}odds}
  \CommentTok{\# w is the weight (0.5 = no effect, 1 = full trust)}
\NormalTok{  num }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ w }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(L) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ w)}
\NormalTok{  den }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ w) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(L) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(num }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ den))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Visualize the effect of varying weights}
\FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{L =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{w =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{F\_L =} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(L, w),}
    \AttributeTok{Prob =} \FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(F\_L) }\CommentTok{\# Convert log{-}odds back to probability}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ L, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ Prob, }\AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(w))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_vline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xintercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"plasma"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{end =} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Jardri Transformation Function F(L, w)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Higher weights approach the true evidence (S{-}curve), w=0.5 ignores it (flat)."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Evidence (L) in Log{-}Odds"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Probability"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Weight (w)"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_jardri_function-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Building the Models
Pedagogically}\label{building-the-models-pedagogically}

We will now build the Circular Inference family of models in three
stages, restricted to two sources of information: Direct Evidence
(\(L_1\)) and Social Evidence (\(L_2\)).

\subsubsection{Stage 1: Weights Only (Weighted Bayes in
Log-Odds)}\label{stage-1-weights-only-weighted-bayes-in-log-odds}

If an agent only applies weights but does not overcount evidence, the
posterior log-odds is simply the sum of the transformed
evidence:\[L_{post} = F(L_1, w_1) + F(L_2, w_2)\]

\subsubsection{Stage 2: Weights and Loops (No
Interference)}\label{stage-2-weights-and-loops-no-interference}

Circular inference claims that information reverberates in the network.
We model this by multiplying the evidence by a loop parameter \(\alpha\)
before the transformation. An \(\alpha > 1\) represents
overcounting.\[L_{post} = F(\alpha_1 L_1, w_1) + F(\alpha_2 L_2, w_2)\]

\subsubsection{Stage 3: Weights, Loops, and Interference (Full Circular
Inference)}\label{stage-3-weights-loops-and-interference-full-circular-inference}

The complete Jardri model notes that overcounted evidence causes
interference. The reverberated information corrupts the original
sources, rendering them non-separable. We first calculate the total
interference \(I\), and then add it to the base evidence inside the
transformation
function:\[I = F(\alpha_1 L_1, w_1) + F(\alpha_2 L_2, w_2)\]\[L_{post} = F(L_1 + I, w_1) + F(L_2 + I, w_2)\]Let's
write generative R functions for all three stages.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Stage 1: Weights Only}
\NormalTok{model\_weights\_only }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(L1, L2, w1, w2) \{}
\NormalTok{  L\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(L1, w1) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(L2, w2)}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(L\_post))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Stage 2: Weights and Loops}
\NormalTok{model\_loops }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(L1, L2, w1, w2, alpha1, alpha2) \{}
\NormalTok{  L\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(alpha1 }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ L1, w1) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(alpha2 }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ L2, w2)}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(L\_post))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Stage 3: Weights, Loops, and Interference}
\NormalTok{model\_interference }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(L1, L2, w1, w2, alpha1, alpha2) \{}
\NormalTok{  I }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(alpha1 }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ L1, w1) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(alpha2 }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ L2, w2)}
\NormalTok{  L\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(L1 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ I, w1) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{JardriF}\NormalTok{(L2 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ I, w2)}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(L\_post))}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Visualizing and Testing the Three
Models}\label{visualizing-and-testing-the-three-models}

How do these structural differences manifest in behaviour? To test this,
let's simulate how an agent integrates Direct Evidence (\(L_1\)) and
Social Evidence (\(L_2\)) under each of the three architectures.We will
give the agent realistic parameters: moderate trust in themselves
(\(w_1 = 0.85\)) and moderate trust in others (\(w_2 = 0.70\)). For the
circular models, we assume they heavily overcount their own direct
experience (\(\alpha_1 = 3.5\)) and slightly overcount social
information (\(\alpha_2 = 1.5\)).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Convert counts to log odds mapping (avoiding strict 0 and 1)}
\NormalTok{to\_log\_odds }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(blue, total) \{}
  \CommentTok{\# Add small epsilon to prevent infinite log{-}odds}
\NormalTok{  p }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ (blue }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (total }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(p }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p)))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Create a testing grid}
\NormalTok{ci\_test\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{blue1 =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{blue2 =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{3}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{L1 =} \FunctionTok{to\_log\_odds}\NormalTok{(blue1, }\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{L2 =} \FunctionTok{to\_log\_odds}\NormalTok{(blue2, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
    \CommentTok{\# Agent parameters}
    \AttributeTok{w1 =} \FloatTok{0.85}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{w2 =} \FloatTok{0.70}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{a1 =} \FloatTok{3.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{a2 =} \FloatTok{1.5}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \CommentTok{\# Generate predictions for all three models}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{1. Weights Only}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{pmap\_dbl}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(L1, L2, w1, w2), model\_weights\_only),}
    \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{2. Weights + Loops}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{pmap\_dbl}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(L1, L2, w1, w2, a1, a2), model\_loops),}
    \StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{3. Loops + Interference}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}} \OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{pmap\_dbl}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(L1, L2, w1, w2, a1, a2), model\_interference),}
    \AttributeTok{social\_label =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(blue2, }\AttributeTok{levels =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }
                          \AttributeTok{labels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Clear Red"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Maybe Red"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Maybe Blue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Clear Blue"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \CommentTok{\# FIX: Explicitly select all three model columns}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{cols =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{1. Weights Only}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{2. Weights + Loops}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\AttributeTok{3. Loops + Interference}\StringTok{\textasciigrave{}}\NormalTok{), }
    \AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"Model"}\NormalTok{, }
    \AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"P\_Blue"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\CommentTok{\# Plot the comparison}
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(ci\_test\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ blue1, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ P\_Blue, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ social\_label)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \FloatTok{1.2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{Model) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#fdae61"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#abd9e9"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_x\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{breaks =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Testing Circular Inference Architectures"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Notice how loops create steep, overconfident \textquotesingle{}S{-}curves\textquotesingle{}, while interference compresses them together."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Direct Evidence (Blue Marbles out of 8)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"P(Choose Blue)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"Social Evidence"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_evidence_integration_sim-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Discussion: Interpreting the
Visualizations}\label{discussion-interpreting-the-visualizations}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  Weights Only: This model resembles our earlier log-odds integration
  plots. The agent smoothly transitions from choosing Red to Blue based
  on their direct sample, and the Social Evidence simply shifts the
  curve up or down linearly in log-odds space.
\item
  Weights + Loops: By adding an \(\alpha_1 = 3.5\) loop to direct
  evidence, the agent overcounts their own observations. This results in
  a much steeper curve in the center. A minor shift in direct evidence
  triggers extreme overconfidence, causing the agent to `jump to
  conclusions'.
\item
  Loops + Interference: Adding the interference term means that the
  reverberating direct evidence structurally corrupts the social
  evidence. You can see the curves tightly bunch together at the
  extremes because the massive internal echo of the direct evidence
  completely drowns out the social information.This final model
  (Interference) is what Simonsen et al.~(2021) found best fit the
  behaviour of patients with schizophrenia, explaining both their
  tendency to ignore others and their high confidence in subjective
  experiences.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Stan Implementation: The Circular Inference
Family}\label{stan-implementation-the-circular-inference-family}

To translate our three pedagogical models into Stan, we need to handle a
few new structural elements compared to the Beta-Binomial models we used
earlier in the chapter:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The \texttt{functions} block:} We must define our custom
  non-linear \texttt{jardri\_f} transformation function natively in Stan
  so we can call it repeatedly.
\item
  \textbf{The \texttt{transformed\ data} block:} The Jardri function
  operates strictly in log-odds space. Instead of converting raw marble
  counts to log-odds on every single Markov Chain iteration, we can do
  this once up front to drastically speed up sampling.
\item
  \textbf{The Parameter Bounds:} Following the methodology in Simonsen
  et al.~(2021), weights (\(w\)) are constrained between \texttt{0.5}
  (completely ignoring the source) and \texttt{1.0} (taking it at face
  value). Loops (\(\alpha\)) are strictly \(\ge 1\), which we enforce by
  sampling an excess parameter \texttt{alpha\_m1} \(\ge 0\).
\end{enumerate}

Let's build the three models.

\subsubsection{1. Stage 1: Weights Only}\label{stage-1-weights-only}

This is our baseline. It strictly integrates the evidence without any
reverberation (overcounting) or interference.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{CI\_Weights\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{functions \{}
\StringTok{  // The core Jardri transformation function}
\StringTok{  real jardri\_f(real L, real w) \{}
\StringTok{    real num = w * exp(L) + (1.0 {-} w);}
\StringTok{    real den = (1.0 {-} w) * exp(L) + w;}
\StringTok{    return log(num / den);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_total;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} y;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} blue1;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} total1;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} blue2;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} total2;}

\StringTok{  real prior\_w\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_w\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed data \{}
\StringTok{  // Convert raw counts to log{-}odds once to save computation}
\StringTok{  real alpha0 = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  real beta0  = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] real lo\_self;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] real lo\_social;}

\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{    lo\_self[i]   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) / (beta0 + total1[i] {-} blue1[i]));}
\StringTok{    lo\_social[i] = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) / (beta0 + total2[i] {-} blue2[i]));}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  // Scaled between 0.5 (no trust) and 1.0 (full trust)}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0.5, upper=1.0\textgreater{} w\_self;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0.5, upper=1.0\textgreater{} w\_other;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Weakly informative priors centered between 0.5 and 1.0}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(w\_self | prior\_w\_mu, prior\_w\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(w\_other | prior\_w\_mu, prior\_w\_sigma);}

\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{    real L\_post = jardri\_f(lo\_self[i], w\_self) + }
\StringTok{                  jardri\_f(lo\_social[i], w\_other);}
\StringTok{    target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | L\_post);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int y\_rep;}

\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      real L\_post = jardri\_f(lo\_self[i], w\_self) + }
\StringTok{                    jardri\_f(lo\_social[i], w\_other);}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | L\_post);}
\StringTok{      y\_rep[i]   = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(L\_post);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}


\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(CI\_Weights\_stan, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch10\_ci\_weights\_only.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch10_ci_weights_only.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\subsubsection{2. Stage 2: Weights + Loops}\label{stage-2-weights-loops}

Now we introduce the loop parameters (\(\alpha\)). We estimate the
excess over 1 (alpha\_m1) using a half-normal prior (since lower=0).
This creates a gentle, scientifically conservative shrinkage that
assumes the agent does not overcount unless the data explicitly demands
it.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{CI\_Loops\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{functions \{}
\StringTok{  real jardri\_f(real L, real w) \{}
\StringTok{    real num = w * exp(L) + (1.0 {-} w);}
\StringTok{    real den = (1.0 {-} w) * exp(L) + w;}
\StringTok{    return log(num / den);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} choice;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} blue1;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} total1;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} blue2;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} total2;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed data \{}
\StringTok{  real alpha0 = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  real beta0  = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  array[N] real lo\_self;}
\StringTok{  array[N] real lo\_social;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    lo\_self[i]   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) / (beta0 + total1[i] {-} blue1[i]));}
\StringTok{    lo\_social[i] = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) / (beta0 + total2[i] {-} blue2[i]));}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0.5, upper=1.0\textgreater{} w\_self;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0.5, upper=1.0\textgreater{} w\_other;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} alpha\_self\_m1;    // Excess over 1}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} alpha\_other\_m1;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  // Reconstruct the actual loop multiplier}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} alpha\_self  = 1.0 + alpha\_self\_m1;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} alpha\_other = 1.0 + alpha\_other\_m1;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  w\_self  \textasciitilde{} normal(0.75, 0.25);}
\StringTok{  w\_other \textasciitilde{} normal(0.75, 0.25);}
\StringTok{  // Half{-}normal priors shrinking towards 1 (no overcounting)}
\StringTok{  alpha\_self\_m1  \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  alpha\_other\_m1 \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    // Evidence is multiplied by the loop parameter BEFORE weighting}
\StringTok{    real L\_post = jardri\_f(alpha\_self * lo\_self[i], w\_self) + }
\StringTok{                  jardri\_f(alpha\_other * lo\_social[i], w\_other);}
\StringTok{                  }
\StringTok{    target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(choice[i] | L\_post);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int posterior\_pred;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    real L\_post = jardri\_f(alpha\_self * lo\_self[i], w\_self) + }
\StringTok{                  jardri\_f(alpha\_other * lo\_social[i], w\_other);}
\StringTok{                  }
\StringTok{    log\_lik[i]        = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(choice[i] | L\_post);}
\StringTok{    posterior\_pred[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(L\_post);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(CI\_Loops\_stan, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch10\_ci\_loops.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch10_ci_loops.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{3. Stage 3: Weights + Loops +
Interference}\label{stage-3-weights-loops-interference}

Finally, the full model. This exactly maps to the math we established:
the total reverberated evidence (I) is calculated first, and then it is
added back into the base evidence for a second pass through the
weighting transformation.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{CI\_Interference\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{functions \{}
\StringTok{  real jardri\_f(real L, real w) \{}
\StringTok{    real num = w * exp(L) + (1.0 {-} w);}
\StringTok{    real den = (1.0 {-} w) * exp(L) + w;}
\StringTok{    return log(num / den);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} choice;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} blue1;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} total1;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} blue2;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} total2;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed data \{}
\StringTok{  real alpha0 = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  real beta0  = 0.5;}
\StringTok{  array[N] real lo\_self;}
\StringTok{  array[N] real lo\_social;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    lo\_self[i]   = log((alpha0 + blue1[i]) / (beta0 + total1[i] {-} blue1[i]));}
\StringTok{    lo\_social[i] = log((alpha0 + blue2[i]) / (beta0 + total2[i] {-} blue2[i]));}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0.5, upper=1.0\textgreater{} w\_self;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0.5, upper=1.0\textgreater{} w\_other;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} alpha\_self\_m1;    }
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} alpha\_other\_m1;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} alpha\_self  = 1.0 + alpha\_self\_m1;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} alpha\_other = 1.0 + alpha\_other\_m1;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  w\_self  \textasciitilde{} normal(0.75, 0.25);}
\StringTok{  w\_other \textasciitilde{} normal(0.75, 0.25);}
\StringTok{  alpha\_self\_m1  \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  alpha\_other\_m1 \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    // Calculate Total Interference first}
\StringTok{    real I = jardri\_f(alpha\_self * lo\_self[i], w\_self) + }
\StringTok{             jardri\_f(alpha\_other * lo\_social[i], w\_other);}
\StringTok{             }
\StringTok{    // Add Interference to base evidence, then weight}
\StringTok{    real L\_post = jardri\_f(lo\_self[i] + I, w\_self) + }
\StringTok{                  jardri\_f(lo\_social[i] + I, w\_other);}
\StringTok{                  }
\StringTok{    target += bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(choice[i] | L\_post);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int posterior\_pred;}
\StringTok{  }
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    real I = jardri\_f(alpha\_self * lo\_self[i], w\_self) + }
\StringTok{             jardri\_f(alpha\_other * lo\_social[i], w\_other);}
\StringTok{             }
\StringTok{    real L\_post = jardri\_f(lo\_self[i] + I, w\_self) + }
\StringTok{                  jardri\_f(lo\_social[i] + I, w\_other);}
\StringTok{                  }
\StringTok{    log\_lik[i]        = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(choice[i] | L\_post);}
\StringTok{    posterior\_pred[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(L\_post);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(CI\_Interference\_stan, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch10\_ci\_interference.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch10_ci_interference.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\subsection{Simulation-Based Calibration Checks (SBC): Pinpointing the
Source of
Complexity}\label{simulation-based-calibration-checks-sbc-pinpointing-the-source-of-complexity}

With our three models written natively in Stan, we face a significant
computational challenge. The \texttt{jardri\_f} transformation is highly
non-linear. Furthermore, the loop parameters (\(\alpha\)) multiply the
evidence \emph{before} it passes through this transformation, and the
interference model creates feedback loops between the two sources.

When parameters interact multiplicatively inside non-linear bounds,
\textbf{identifiability issues} are almost guaranteed. To see exactly
where these problems emerge, we will run Simulation-Based Calibration
Checks (SBC) on all three models in our pedagogical sequence: 1. Weights
Only 2. Weights + Loops 3. Full Interference

We will simulate datasets from the priors of each model, fit the
corresponding Stan model to them, and compare their calibration and
parameter recovery side-by-side.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Ensure all models are compiled}
\NormalTok{mod\_ci\_weights      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan/ch10\_ci\_weights\_only.stan"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{mod\_ci\_loops        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan/ch10\_ci\_loops.stan"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{mod\_ci\_interference }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan/ch10\_ci\_interference.stan"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch10\_sbc\_ci\_all\_results.rds"}\NormalTok{)) \{}
  
  \CommentTok{\# 1. Define the R equivalent of the Jardri function for the generators}
\NormalTok{  jardri\_f }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(L, w) \{}
\NormalTok{    num }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ w }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(L) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{1.0} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ w)}
\NormalTok{    den }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{1.0} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ w) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(L) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w}
    \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(num }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ den))}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \CommentTok{\# Helper to sample from truncated normal [0.5, 1.0] for weights}
\NormalTok{  draw\_w }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{() \{}
\NormalTok{    w }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}
    \ControlFlowTok{while}\NormalTok{(w }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{||}\NormalTok{ w }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{) w }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{)}
    \FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(w)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

\NormalTok{  N\_sbc\_trials }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(evidence\_combinations) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \DecValTok{5} \CommentTok{\# 180 trials per simulated agent}

  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} GENERATOR 1: Weights Only {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_gen\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(}
    \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(N) \{}
\NormalTok{      ws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{draw\_w}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{      wo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{draw\_w}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{      ev }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ evidence\_combinations }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{slice\_sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{replace =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
      
\NormalTok{      lo\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1))}
\NormalTok{      lo\_o }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2))}
      
\NormalTok{      L\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(lo\_s, ws) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(lo\_o, wo)}
\NormalTok{      choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(N, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(L\_post))}

      \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{w\_self =}\NormalTok{ ws, }\AttributeTok{w\_other =}\NormalTok{ wo),}
        \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choices, }\AttributeTok{blue1 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1, }
                         \AttributeTok{total1 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1, }\AttributeTok{blue2 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2, }\AttributeTok{total2 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2)}
\NormalTok{      )}
\NormalTok{    \}, }\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N\_sbc\_trials}
\NormalTok{  )}

  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} GENERATOR 2: Weights + Loops {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_gen\_loops }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(}
    \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(N) \{}
\NormalTok{      ws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{draw\_w}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{      wo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{draw\_w}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{      as\_m1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)); ao\_m1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{      as\_ }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{1.0} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ as\_m1; ao\_ }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{1.0} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ao\_m1}

\NormalTok{      ev }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ evidence\_combinations }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{slice\_sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{replace =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      lo\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1))}
\NormalTok{      lo\_o }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2))}
      
\NormalTok{      L\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(as\_ }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ lo\_s, ws) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(ao\_ }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ lo\_o, wo)}
\NormalTok{      choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(N, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(L\_post))}

      \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{w\_self =}\NormalTok{ ws, }\AttributeTok{w\_other =}\NormalTok{ wo, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_self\_m1 =}\NormalTok{ as\_m1, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_other\_m1 =}\NormalTok{ ao\_m1),}
        \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choices, }\AttributeTok{blue1 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1, }
                         \AttributeTok{total1 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1, }\AttributeTok{blue2 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2, }\AttributeTok{total2 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2)}
\NormalTok{      )}
\NormalTok{    \}, }\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N\_sbc\_trials}
\NormalTok{  )}

  \CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} GENERATOR 3: Full Interference {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_gen\_interference }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(}
    \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(N) \{}
\NormalTok{      ws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{draw\_w}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{      wo }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{draw\_w}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{      as\_m1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)); ao\_m1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{      as\_ }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{1.0} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ as\_m1; ao\_ }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{1.0} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ao\_m1}

\NormalTok{      ev }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ evidence\_combinations }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{slice\_sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{replace =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      lo\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1))}
\NormalTok{      lo\_o }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{((}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2) }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (}\FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2))}
      
\NormalTok{      I }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(as\_ }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ lo\_s, ws) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(ao\_ }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ lo\_o, wo)}
\NormalTok{      L\_post }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(lo\_s }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ I, ws) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{jardri\_f}\NormalTok{(lo\_o }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ I, wo)}
\NormalTok{      choices }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(N, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{plogis}\NormalTok{(L\_post))}

      \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{w\_self =}\NormalTok{ ws, }\AttributeTok{w\_other =}\NormalTok{ wo, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_self\_m1 =}\NormalTok{ as\_m1, }\AttributeTok{alpha\_other\_m1 =}\NormalTok{ ao\_m1),}
        \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{choice =}\NormalTok{ choices, }\AttributeTok{blue1 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue1, }
                         \AttributeTok{total1 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total1, }\AttributeTok{blue2 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{blue2, }\AttributeTok{total2 =}\NormalTok{ ev}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{total2)}
\NormalTok{      )}
\NormalTok{    \}, }\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N\_sbc\_trials}
\NormalTok{  )}

  \CommentTok{\# 2. Generate datasets}
\NormalTok{  n\_sbc }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{400}
\NormalTok{  datasets\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_gen\_weights, n\_sbc)}
\NormalTok{  datasets\_loops   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_gen\_loops, n\_sbc)}
\NormalTok{  datasets\_interf  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{generate\_datasets}\NormalTok{(sbc\_gen\_interference, n\_sbc)}

  \CommentTok{\# 3. Setup Backends}
\NormalTok{  sampler\_args }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
  
\NormalTok{  backend\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{do.call}\NormalTok{(SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample, }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{model =}\NormalTok{ mod\_ci\_weights), sampler\_args))}
\NormalTok{  backend\_loops   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{do.call}\NormalTok{(SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample, }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{model =}\NormalTok{ mod\_ci\_loops), sampler\_args))}
\NormalTok{  backend\_interf  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{do.call}\NormalTok{(SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\NormalTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample, }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{model =}\NormalTok{ mod\_ci\_interference), sampler\_args))}

  \CommentTok{\# 4. Compute SBC}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_res\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(datasets\_weights, backend\_weights, }\AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_res\_loops   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(datasets\_loops, backend\_loops, }\AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_res\_interf  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{compute\_SBC}\NormalTok{(datasets\_interf, backend\_interf, }\AttributeTok{keep\_fits =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}

  \FunctionTok{saveRDS}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{weights =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_res\_weights, }\AttributeTok{loops =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_res\_loops, }\AttributeTok{interf =}\NormalTok{ sbc\_res\_interf), }
          \StringTok{"simmodels/ch10\_sbc\_ci\_all\_results.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_all }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch10\_sbc\_ci\_all\_results.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_res\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_all}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{weights}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_res\_loops   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_all}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{loops}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_res\_interf  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sbc\_all}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{interf}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsubsection{Checking Posterior Calibration
(ECDF)}\label{checking-posterior-calibration-ecdf}

First, we check the Empirical Cumulative Distribution Function (ECDF)
plots to see if the sampler explores the parameter space without bias
across all three models.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{p\_ecdf\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_res\_weights, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"w\_self"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w\_other"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"1. Weights Only Calibration"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_ecdf\_loops }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_res\_loops, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"w\_self"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w\_other"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha\_self\_m1"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha\_other\_m1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"2. Weights + Loops Calibration"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_ecdf\_interf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ SBC}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{plot\_ecdf\_diff}\NormalTok{(sbc\_res\_interf, }\AttributeTok{variables =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"w\_self"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w\_other"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha\_self\_m1"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha\_other\_m1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"3. Full Interference Calibration"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{(p\_ecdf\_weights }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_ecdf\_loops }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_ecdf\_interf) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{plot\_annotation}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"SBC Calibration Across the Circular Inference Family"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"If lines stray outside the blue bands, the posterior is systematically biased."}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_sbc_ci_ecdf_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The ECDF plots generally stay within the blue envelopes, though you will
likely see the loop parameters (alpha\_*\_m1) gently push against the
edges in the Interference model. This indicates that Stan's NUTS sampler
is working hard but succeeding at unbiased exploration.

\subsubsection{Parameter Recovery: The Asymptote Problem
Emerges}\label{parameter-recovery-the-asymptote-problem-emerges}

Calibration tells us if the posterior captures the true value on
average, but Parameter Recovery tells us how precise those estimates
are. By plotting all three models together, we can see exactly what
structural feature destroys our precision.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Extract and label recovery stats}
\NormalTok{prep\_recovery }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sbc\_obj, model\_name) \{}
\NormalTok{  sbc\_obj}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{stats }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(variable }\SpecialCharTok{\%in\%} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"w\_self"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w\_other"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha\_self\_m1"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"alpha\_other\_m1"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{Model =}\NormalTok{ model\_name,}
      \AttributeTok{param\_label =} \FunctionTok{case\_when}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{        variable }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"w\_self"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"Weight Self (w\_s)"}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{        variable }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"w\_other"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"Weight Other (w\_o)"}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{        variable }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"alpha\_self\_m1"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"Excess Loops Self (\textbackslash{}u03b1\_s {-} 1)"}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{        variable }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"alpha\_other\_m1"} \SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}} \StringTok{"Excess Loops Other (\textbackslash{}u03b1\_o {-} 1)"}
\NormalTok{      )}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{rec\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{prep\_recovery}\NormalTok{(sbc\_res\_weights, }\StringTok{"1. Weights Only"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{prep\_recovery}\NormalTok{(sbc\_res\_loops, }\StringTok{"2. Weights + Loops"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{prep\_recovery}\NormalTok{(sbc\_res\_interf, }\StringTok{"3. Full Interference"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{Model =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(Model, }\AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"1. Weights Only"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"2. Weights + Loops"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"3. Full Interference"}\NormalTok{)))}

\CommentTok{\# {-}{-}{-} Plot 1: Weights Only (Strictly bounded 0.5 to 1.0) {-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{p\_rec\_weights }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ rec\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} 
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{str\_detect}\NormalTok{(variable, }\StringTok{"w\_"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ simulated\_value, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ median)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"grey50"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}

  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"\#d73027"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}

  \FunctionTok{coord\_cartesian}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{xlim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{ylim =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Parameter Recovery: Trust Weights (w)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Weights recover well, though interference adds visible noise to the estimates."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True Simulated Value"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}

  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(Model }\SpecialCharTok{!=} \StringTok{"1. Weights Only"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}

  \FunctionTok{geom\_errorbar}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q5, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.15}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{width =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"\#4575b4"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}

  \FunctionTok{facet\_grid}\NormalTok{(Model }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ param\_label, }\AttributeTok{scales =} \StringTok{"free"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"Parameter Recovery: Loop Parameters (\textbackslash{}u03b1 {-} 1)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Notice the severe \textquotesingle{}asymptotic flattening\textquotesingle{} and massive uncertainty at higher values."}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"True Simulated Value"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Posterior Median"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{strip.background =} \FunctionTok{element\_rect}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"gray95"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \ConstantTok{NA}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{strip.text =} \FunctionTok{element\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{face =} \StringTok{"bold"}\NormalTok{))}

\CommentTok{\# Combine with patchwork}
\NormalTok{p\_rec\_weights }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_rec\_loops}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-BayesianModels_files/figure-pdf/ch10_sbc_ci_recovery-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Weights are easy to recover, but loops not. What can we do? My hunch
again is that we should collect data on participants' confidence, as
that should give us better resolution.

\subsection{Discussion: When to Use Each
Parameterisation}\label{discussion-when-to-use-each-parameterisation}

The parameterisations explored in this chapter make different claims
about the data-generating process:

\textbf{Pseudocount (SBA/PBA/WBA):} The mind treats each source as an
independent binomial sample. Evidence is counted once, with optional
rescaling. The BetaBinomial observation model claims probability
matching --- actions sample from the full belief distribution.

\textbf{Circular inference (Jardri \& Denève 2013):} Evidence is not
merely weighted but potentially amplified beyond its face value. The
Bernoulli observation model collapses the belief to a single log-odds
value; distributional uncertainty is captured by the posterior over
parameters. The key predictive signature --- steeper response curves for
the amplified source --- cannot be reproduced by pseudocount weighting
alone.

The choice between models should be driven by the scientific question
and validated by LOO comparison.

\section{What the Bayesian Workflow
Adds}\label{what-the-bayesian-workflow-adds}

This chapter has done more than fit cognitive models --- it has
\emph{validated} them through a disciplined pipeline. Prior predictive
checks established that the priors of all three models are cognitively
plausible. SBC certified that the Stan samplers for the WBA and PBA are
unbiased. Single-fit recovery confirmed identifiability. LOO-PIT and
sensitivity analysis characterised the limits of the fitted posteriors.
The three-way PSIS-LOO comparison arbitrated between competing cognitive
hypotheses in a way that penalises complexity and estimates genuine
predictive performance.

This pipeline is not bureaucracy. Without SBC, a subtle Stan
implementation bug could produce attractive posteriors that are
systematically biased. Without LOO-PIT, we cannot distinguish a model
that fits from a model that truly predicts. Without the three-way
comparison, we cannot know whether the WBA's additional parameter
reflects a real cognitive structure or mere overfitting. And without
parameter recovery, we would never have discovered that the raw
\((w_d, w_s)\) parameterisation was structurally unidentifiable --- a
problem invisible to standard MCMC diagnostics.

\subsection{Limitations of the Current
Models}\label{limitations-of-the-current-models}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The observation model is itself a hypothesis.} The
  BetaBinomial claims participants probability-match --- sampling their
  action from the full posterior over \(\theta\). If participants are
  more deterministic (choosing blue whenever
  \(\text{E}[\theta] > 0.5\)), the BetaBinomial will overestimate
  response variability. A softmax observation model with an
  inverse-temperature parameter would test this alternative; the LOO-PIT
  hump pattern is the diagnostic signature to watch for.
\item
  \textbf{Trial independence in non-sequential models.} The SBA, PBA,
  and WBA assume each trial involves a fresh, independent jar. Applying
  these models to paradigms with across-trial structure requires the
  sequential extension --- with LFO-CV replacing standard LOO.
\item
  \textbf{Fixed prior pseudo-count.} We fix \(\alpha_0 = \beta_0 = 0.5\)
  (Jeffreys prior), assuming no prior knowledge about the jar. A
  hierarchical extension would estimate a population distribution over
  \((\alpha_0, \beta_0)\), allowing for individual differences in
  baseline expectations.
\item
  \textbf{The PBA's identifiability boundary.} When \(p \to 0\) or
  \(p \to 1\), one evidence source is entirely ignored. The Beta(2,2)
  prior keeps the sampler away from these boundaries, but check tail ESS
  whenever the PBA is fitted to strongly asymmetric agents.
\item
  \textbf{Social evidence encoding and pool-size asymmetry.} Social
  evidence is encoded as \texttt{total2\ =\ 3} pseudo-marbles, making
  direct and social evidence structurally asymmetric. Recovered weights
  should be interpreted relative to the encoding, not as absolute
  measures of social reliance.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Connections and
Extensions}\label{connections-and-extensions}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Rational inattention models} relax the assumption that all
  evidence is perfectly observed --- the agent may allocate limited
  attentional resources to each source.
\item
  \textbf{Resource-rational models} (Griffiths et al.~2015) ask: what
  integration strategy is optimal given limited computational resources?
  The WBA can be interpreted as an approximation to full Bayesian
  inference under resource constraints.
\item
  \textbf{Active inference} (Friston 2010) frames perception and action
  as a unified process of minimising variational free energy, offering a
  different theoretical foundation for the same empirical phenomena.
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Stan Engineering: What We Have Built Across Chapters 4--10}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Across this book's first ten chapters we have introduced a layered set
of Stan programming techniques. Before moving to the categorization
chapters --- where all of them combine --- it is worth having the full
picture in one place.

\textbf{Stage I (Ch. 4--5): Foundations}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Technique
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Rule
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Block placement & \texttt{transformed\ data} for data-only quantities;
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} for parameter-dependent state;
\texttt{model} for local accumulators; \texttt{generated\ quantities}
for post-sampling diagnostics \\
\texttt{run\_diagnostics} flag & Gate expensive
\texttt{generated\ quantities} (PPC draws, \texttt{log\_lik}) behind an
\texttt{int\textless{}lower=0,upper=1\textgreater{}} data flag; set to 0
in SBC backends \\
Priors as data & Pass prior hyperparameters through the \texttt{data}
block --- no recompilation when adjusting priors; SBC stays in sync
automatically \\
Pathfinder warm-start & Run \texttt{mod\$pathfinder(...)} first; pass
the result as \texttt{init} to \texttt{mod\$sample(...)} to cut warm-up
by 30--60\% on complex models \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Stage II (Ch. 6): Population Structure}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Technique
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Rule
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Non-centered parameterization (NCP) & Sample standardized offsets
\(z_j \sim N(0,1)\); reconstruct cognitive parameters deterministically
in \texttt{transformed\ parameters}. Eliminates Neal's Funnel in
sparse-data hierarchical models \\
Subject indexing & \texttt{subj\_id} array (scatter-index) for
exchangeable models; \texttt{subj\_start/subj\_end} contiguous-block for
sequential models (Ch. 9+) \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Stage III (Ch. 8--10): Process Mechanisms}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Technique
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Rule
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Discrete marginalization & Never sample discrete parameters with HMC;
use \texttt{log\_sum\_exp} or \texttt{log\_mix} to analytically
integrate them out in the \texttt{model} block \\
Sequential state unrolling & Recursive belief/memory state goes in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} with volatile state kept local in
\texttt{\{\}}; only the quantities needed downstream are lifted to the
saved-draw tape \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Stage IV (Ch. 11): High-Performance}

The categorization chapters introduce the final layer: precomputing
pairwise stimulus distances in \texttt{transformed\ data} (computed once
rather than thousands of times), NCP for all individual parameters
including attention weights (eliminating Dirichlet funnels), and
\texttt{reduce\_sum} for multi-threaded parallelism across subjects
within a chain. The next chapter puts all of these together.

\end{tcolorbox}

\textbf{This is the end of the core modelling sequence.} Chapters 1--10
have traced the complete Bayesian cognitive modelling workflow: from
pizza-stone physics (Ch. 1), through verbal and formal models of
strategic behaviour (Chs. 2--3), parameter estimation (Ch. 4), model
validation (Ch. 5), hierarchical extensions (Ch. 6), model comparison
(Ch. 7), mixture structures (Ch. 8), sequential cognition (Ch. 9), and
Bayesian cognitive theory (Ch. 10). The tools and habits of mind
established here --- generative modelling, principled validation, honest
uncertainty --- apply directly to any new domain or model you encounter.
