\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{The Generalized Context
Model}\label{sec-categorization-exemplars}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chapters
1--10 built the complete inference toolkit: parameter estimation (Ch.
4), the six-phase validation pipeline (Ch. 5), hierarchical models (Ch.
6), model comparison via LOO (Ch. 7), mixture models with discrete
marginalization (Ch. 8), and hierarchical Bayesian cognition (Ch.
9--10). This and the following 2 chapters apply the full toolkit to
\emph{categorization}, asking not only what parameters describe a
participant's behavior, but which \emph{cognitive mechanism} best
accounts for how people learn to assign objects to kinds. We begin with
the exemplar approach: the Generalized Context Model (GCM). The
following chapters will introduce prototype and rule-based models, and
then compare all three approaches head-to-head using the full validation
battery.

Two important caveats. First, the categorization models learn, that is,
are \textbf{path-dependent} --- the likelihood at trial \(t\) depends on
the full sequence of stimuli on trials \(1, \ldots, t-1\) through the
exemplar memory store. Standard LOO-CV is therefore technically invalid
here. We met this issue when discussing sequential Bayes. Here we
introduce leave-future-out cross-validation (LFO-CV; Bürkner, Gabry, \&
Vehtari, 2020). Second, the rule-based models we will discuss cannot
really be fit using MCMC (we'll see why), so we need to introduce a new
slower method: Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC).

\textbf{Trilogy structure.} Chapters 11, 12, and 13 form a parallel
trilogy. Each chapter builds a base cognitive model, extends it with a
single non-stationarity parameter (\(\lambda\) here, \(q\) in Ch. 12,
\(\mu\) in Ch. 13), and then validates across the same three
environmental scenarios in the same order: static Kruschke (baseline),
performance-contingent abrupt shifts (misspecified non-stationarity),
and smooth drift (assumption-matched non-stationarity). The ordering
reflects increasing assumption match between the scenario's
data-generating process and each model's structural commitments. Chapter
13 closes with a synthesis of what the three analyses jointly imply
about identifiability and model-task alignment.
\end{quote}

\section{Introduction to Categorization
Models}\label{introduction-to-categorization-models}

\subsection{The Fundamental Problem of
Categorization}\label{the-fundamental-problem-of-categorization}

Categorization is one of the most fundamental cognitive abilities that
humans possess. From early childhood, we learn to organize the world
around us into meaningful categories: distinguishing food from non-food,
safe from dangerous, or one letter from another. This process of
assigning objects to categories is complex, involving perception,
memory, attention, and decision-making.

How do humans learn categories and make categorization decisions? This
question has generated decades of research and theoretical debate in
cognitive science.

\subsection{Why Model Categorization?}\label{why-model-categorization}

Computational models let us formalize competing theories, generate
precise predictions, and test them against human behavior. This chapter
focuses on the \textbf{exemplar approach} --- the Generalized Context
Model (GCM). Chapters 12 and 13 introduce prototype and rule-based
models respectively, with a full cross-model comparison in Chapter 13.

\subsection{Historical Development of Categorization
Models}\label{historical-development-of-categorization-models}

\subsubsection{Early Views: The Classical
Approach}\label{early-views-the-classical-approach}

Early theories of categorization followed what is now called the
classical view, where categories were defined by necessary and
sufficient features. In this view, category membership is an
all-or-nothing affair: an object either satisfies the logical criteria
or it doesn't. Some examples from Aristotle:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  a human is a rational animal.
\item
  a bird has feathers, wings, and a beak; and lays eggs.
\item
  a fish lives in water; has scales; and breathes through gills.
\end{itemize}

You need all of these features to be part of one category, and having
them makes you part of that category.

While intuitive, this approach struggled to explain many aspects of
human categorization, such as:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Graded category membership (some members seem ``more typical'' than
  others)
\item
  Unclear boundaries between categories.
\item
  Context-dependent categorization
\item
  Family resemblance structures (where no single feature is necessary)
\end{itemize}

Think of the traditional definition of a species: a set of organisms
that can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. However, there are
many cases of organism A being able to interbreed with B, and B with C,
but A and C cannot interbreed (for instance Californian Ensatina
Salamanders have such phenomena). This creates a fuzzy boundary that the
classical view cannot easily accommodate.

\subsubsection{The Prototype Revolution}\label{the-prototype-revolution}

In the 1970s, Eleanor Rosch's pioneering work on prototypes challenged
the classical view. She demonstrated that categories appear to be
organized around central tendencies or ``prototypes,'' with membership
determined by similarity to these prototypes. Objects closer to the
prototype are categorized more quickly and consistently.

\subsubsection{The Exemplar Alternative}\label{the-exemplar-alternative}

In the late 1970s and 1980s, researchers like Douglas Medin and Robert
Nosofsky proposed that rather than abstracting prototypes, people might
store individual exemplars of categories and make judgments based on
similarity to these stored examples.

The Generalized Context Model (GCM), developed by Nosofsky, became the
standard exemplar model. It proposes that categorization decisions are
based on the summed similarity of a new stimulus to all stored exemplars
of each category, weighted by attention to different stimulus
dimensions.

\subsubsection{Rule-Based Models}\label{rule-based-models}

While similarity-based approaches gained prominence, other researchers
argued that people sometimes use explicit rules for categorization
(e.g.~in scientific reasoning, but not only). Models like Bayesian
particle filters, multinomial trees, COVIS and RULEX formalize these
ideas, suggesting that rule learning and application form a core part of
human categorization, particularly for well-defined categories.

\subsubsection{Hybrid Approaches}\label{hybrid-approaches}

More recent work has focused on hybrid models that incorporate elements
of multiple approaches. Models like SUSTAIN (Love, Medin, and Gureckis
2004) and ATRIUM (Erickson and Kruschke 1998) propose that humans can
flexibly switch between strategies or that different systems operate in
parallel.

\subsection{A Systematization Framework for Categorization
Models}\label{a-systematization-framework-for-categorization-models}

To pedagogically navigate and compare the different approaches, we can
deconstruct the cognitive process of categorization into a sequential
six-step pipeline:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Input Representation:} How are stimuli represented? (e.g.,
  continuous geometry vs.~discrete binary features).
\item
  \textbf{Attentional Processes:} How are features prioritized? Not all
  features are equally relevant given a task, and attention is often
  modeled as weights summing to 1.
\item
  \textbf{Intermediate Representations:} How is the category internally
  stored? (e.g., as raw exemplars, abstract prototypes, or explicit
  rules).
\item
  \textbf{Evidential Mechanisms:} How is the incoming stimulus compared
  to the representation to gather evidence? (e.g., calculating average
  similarity with an exponential decay over distance, or evidence
  accumulation).
\item
  \textbf{Decision Rules:} How is evidence translated into a choice?
  (e.g., maximum similarity, or a probabilistic Luce choice axiom).
\item
  \textbf{Learning:} How do representations, attentional weights, or
  rule structures update over time through exposure, memory
  accumulation, and feedback?
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{Our Implementation
Approach}\label{our-implementation-approach}

Each chapter in the trilogy follows the same validation structure:
forward simulator → Stan model → diagnostic battery → prior/posterior
predictive checks → parameter recovery → prior sensitivity → SBC. This
parallel structure makes it possible to isolate what differs between the
cognitive mechanisms rather than conflating model differences with
implementation choices.

\subsection{Categorization (Cognitive Science) vs.~Classification
(Machine
Learning)}\label{categorization-cognitive-science-vs.-classification-machine-learning}

Because the mathematical tools are often identical, it is tempting to
view cognitive models of categorization simply as early, inefficient
machine learning classifiers. Both fields rely on the exact same
underlying mechanics to map inputs to outputs: calculating distances in
multi-dimensional feature space, applying functions (like Luce or
softmax) to translate evidence into probabilities, and using
gradient-based weight updates or Bayesian filters to learn from
feedback.

However, despite this shared mathematical framework, they serve
fundamentally different goals:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{Classification (Machine Learning):} The primary goal is
  \emph{engineering}. It focuses on drawing the optimal decision
  boundary to maximize predictive accuracy and minimize loss on a given
  dataset. The internal mechanism (e.g., a deep neural network's hidden
  layers) doesn't need to resemble human thought---it just needs to work
  efficiently and accurately.
\item
  \textbf{Categorization (Cognitive Modeling):} The primary goal is
  \emph{reverse-engineering}. Categorization is about understanding the
  knowledge structures that enable a biological agent to make predictive
  inferences. We ask not only what parameters describe behavior, but
  which \emph{cognitive mechanism} best accounts for how people actually
  learn, including the specific mistakes they make.
\end{itemize}

We can summarize this distinction along three key dimensions:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Dimension
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Classification (Machine Learning)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Categorization (Cognitive Modeling)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{Primary Goal} & Maximize out-of-sample accuracy. & Explain human
behavior, including systematic human errors. \\
\textbf{Data Processing} & Often batch-processed over massive, static
datasets. & Inherently \textbf{path-dependent}; learns sequentially
trial-by-trial. \\
\textbf{Constraints} & Hardware limits and computational efficiency. &
Psychological plausibility (bounded memory, selective attention,
cognitive load). \\
\end{longtable}

In short: A machine learning classifier asks, \emph{``What is the best
mathematical way to separate Category A from Category B?''} A cognitive
model asks, \emph{``How do humans separate A from B, and what are the
internal mental representations that allow them to do so?''}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{The Generalized Context Model
(GCM)}\label{the-generalized-context-model-gcm}

\subsection{Mathematical Foundations and Cognitive
Principles}\label{mathematical-foundations-and-cognitive-principles}

The Generalized Context Model (GCM), developed by Robert Nosofsky
(Nosofsky 1986) in the 1980s, represents one of the most influential
exemplar-based approaches to categorization. Unlike models that abstract
category information into prototypes or rules, the GCM proposes that
people store individual exemplars in memory and make categorization
decisions based on similarity to these stored examples.

\subsubsection{The GCM Mapped to the Categorization
Pipeline}\label{the-gcm-mapped-to-the-categorization-pipeline}

The GCM translates the 6-step cognitive pipeline into a formal
mathematical structure as follows:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{1. Input Representation (Geometric Space):} Stimuli are
  represented as continuous points in a multi-dimensional psychological
  space.
\item
  \textbf{2. Attentional Processes (Selective Attention):} Per-dimension
  weights \(w\) (summing to 1) rescale the psychological space before
  distance is computed, prioritizing task-relevant features.
\item
  \textbf{3. Intermediate Representations (Memory for Instances):} The
  model explicitly skips abstraction. Every encountered example is
  stored directly in memory with its category label.
\item
  \textbf{4. Evidential Mechanisms (Similarity Computation):} Distance
  is calculated using a city-block metric (Manhattan distance).
  Similarity between a new stimulus and each stored exemplar is an
  exponentially decreasing function of their distance, governed by a
  sensitivity parameter \(c\). Evidence is then aggregated as average
  similarity for each category.
\item
  \textbf{5. Decision Rules (Choice Probability):} The decision relies
  on the Luce choice axiom. The probability of assigning a stimulus to a
  category is proportional to its summed similarity to exemplars of that
  category, modified by a baseline category bias \(\beta\).
\item
  \textbf{6. Learning (Memory Accumulation):} The model learns
  sequentially by adding each newly categorized and feedback-evaluated
  stimulus directly into its exemplar memory store, altering the
  evidential landscape for future trials.
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Historical Context: From Binary Logic to Psychological Space}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The evolution of the Generalized Context Model (GCM) reflects a broader
shift in cognitive science: moving from models constrained by
computational simplicity toward those more capable of capturing the
``messy'' richness of human perception.

\subsubsection{The Era of Binary
Constraints}\label{the-era-of-binary-constraints}

In the late 1970s, categorization research was dominated by the
``small-set'' paradigm. Stimuli were often constructed from a few
discrete, binary features (e.g., ``large vs.~small,'' ``red vs.~blue'').
The pioneering \textbf{Context Model} (Medin and Schaffer 1978) was
designed for these environments, calculating similarity based on simple
feature overlap. While revolutionary, it was computationally and
theoretically ``flat'': it couldn't account for the fact that some
dimensions are more perceptually salient than others, or that the
psychological distance between features isn't always linear.

\subsubsection{The Geometric Revolution}\label{the-geometric-revolution}

The introduction of the GCM (Nosofsky 1986) broke these constraints by
synthesizing exemplar theory with \textbf{Multidimensional Scaling
(MDS)}. Instead of binary features, stimuli became points in a
continuous, multi-dimensional psychological space. The core innovation
was the weighted Minkowski metric:
\[d_{ij} = c \left[ \sum w_k |x_{ik} - x_{jk}|^r \right]^{1/r}\] This
formula allowed the model to ``stretch'' or ``shrink'' psychological
space based on selective attention (\(w_k\)). It transformed
categorization from a logical matching process into a geometric one,
where the ``geometry of thought'' could be empirically mapped.

\subsubsection{The ``Ecological'' Turn: Summed vs.~Mean
Similarity}\label{the-ecological-turn-summed-vs.-mean-similarity}

As the field moved toward experiments with larger and unbalanced
category sizes, a debate arose over whether evidence should be summed or
averaged across exemplars. See §Summed vs.~Mean Similarity below for the
full argument and a concrete numerical demonstration; that section
motivates why our implementation adopts the \textbf{mean-similarity}
formulation.

\subsubsection{The Process Revolution: Sequential Accumulation and
Path-Dependency}\label{the-process-revolution-sequential-accumulation-and-path-dependency}

Early models were ``static'': they were used to fit terminal,
steady-state data from the end of an experiment. The conceptual shift
toward process models began by acknowledging that categorization is a
sequential learning experience. The critical first step is simply
modeling memory as it grows: exemplars are accumulated into the memory
store one trial at a time.

This transition highlights the fundamentally \textbf{path-dependent}
nature of cognition: your response at trial \(t\) depends strictly on
the specific sequence of exemplars and feedback you encountered in
trials \(1 \dots t-1\). While later models like \textbf{ALCOVE} (John K.
Kruschke 1992) attempted to model trial-by-trial, error-driven updates
to the attention weights themselves, in my experience such extensions
often yield terrible parameter recovery and fail stringent modern
validation checks. Consequently, our approach grounds the ``Process
Revolution'' in robust sequential exemplar accumulation. We then
conceptually extend this path-dependency by introducing a memory decay
parameter, providing a mechanism for agents to unlearn and adapt in
non-stationary environments without sacrificing model identifiability.
{[}It would be a very good learning exercise to try to build and
validate ALCOVE and other process models, but that is beyond the scope
of this chapter and we will not do it here.{]}

\end{tcolorbox}

\subsubsection{Parameters: Cognitive Commitment, Algebraic Role, Stan
Parameterization}\label{parameters-cognitive-commitment-algebraic-role-stan-parameterization}

\textbf{Attention weights \(w\).} Cognitively, \(w\) is a simplex of
dimension-specific attention weights. Algebraically, \(w\) enters the
distance formula as the per-dimension coefficient in the
attention-weighted city-block metric. At the Stan level \(w\) is
declared as \texttt{simplex{[}N\_features{]}}, which enforces
\(w_m \ge 0\) and \(\sum_m w_m = 1\) directly.

\textbf{Sensitivity \(c\).} Cognitively, \(c\) represents perceptual
discriminability. Algebraically, \(c\) is the exponential decay rate in
\(\eta_{ij} = e^{-c\, d_{ij}}\). At the Stan level, we sample an
unconstrained \texttt{real\ log\_c} and set \texttt{c\ =\ exp(log\_c)}
in \texttt{transformed\ parameters}.

\textbf{Response bias \(\beta\).} Cognitively, \(\beta\) is a baseline
preference for category A independent of stimulus similarity;
\(\beta = 0.5\) is unbiased. In this chapter \(\beta\) also serves as
the cold-start fallback. At the Stan level \(\beta\) is declared as
\texttt{real\textless{}lower=0,\ upper=1\textgreater{}} with a Beta
prior.

\subsection{Mathematical Formulation}\label{mathematical-formulation}

\textbf{1. Distance Calculation}

\[d_{ij} = \left[ \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_m |x_{im} - x_{jm}|^r \right]^{\frac{1}{r}}\]

We use the city-block metric (\(r = 1\)), appropriate for separable
stimulus dimensions:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# City{-}block distance (r = 1), attention{-}weighted}
\NormalTok{distance }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(vect1, vect2, w) \{}
  \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(w }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(vect1 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ vect2))}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{2. Similarity Computation}

\[\eta_{ij} = e^{-c \cdot d_{ij}}\]

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Exponential similarity kernel}
\NormalTok{similarity }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(dist\_val, c) \{}
  \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{c }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ dist\_val)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Let's visualize how similarity changes with distance for different
values of \(c\):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{dd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{distance  =} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{c         =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{3.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{6.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{10.0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{sim     =} \FunctionTok{similarity}\NormalTok{(distance, c),}
    \AttributeTok{c\_label =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(c)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(dd, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ distance, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ sim, }\AttributeTok{color =}\NormalTok{ c\_label, }\AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ c\_label)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"plasma"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Sensitivity (c)"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Effect of Sensitivity Parameter (c) on Similarity"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"c is on the natural (original) scale; prior places most mass on c ∈ (0.3, 15)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x        =} \StringTok{"Psychological Distance"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{y        =} \StringTok{"Similarity"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"bottom"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{11-CategorizationModelsExemplars_files/figure-pdf/ch12_visualizing_sensitivity-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\textbf{3. Category Response Probability}

\[P(A|i) = \frac{\beta \; \bar\eta_{i,A}}{\beta \; \bar\eta_{i,A} + (1-\beta) \; \bar\eta_{i,B}}, \qquad \bar\eta_{i,A} = \frac{1}{N_A} \sum_{j \in A} \eta_{ij}\]

where \(\bar\eta_{i,A}\) is the \textbf{mean} similarity of the current
stimulus to the exemplars stored under category \(A\), and \(N_A\) is
the count of those stored exemplars. The cold-start fallback applies
when either category has no stored exemplars yet: \(P(A|i) = \beta\).

\subsection{Summed vs.~Mean Similarity: A Caveat on the Canonical
Formulation}\label{summed-vs.-mean-similarity-a-caveat-on-the-canonical-formulation}

The original GCM (Nosofsky 1986) and the ALCOVE extensions (John K.
Kruschke 1992) use the \emph{summed} form \(\sum_{j \in A} \eta_{ij}\)
rather than the mean. That choice carries a subtle but consequential
assumption: whichever category has more stored exemplars acquires a
systematic advantage, independent of how similar any of those exemplars
is to the current stimulus.

A concrete failure. Suppose category A has 12 stored exemplars at
distance roughly \(d = 2\) from the current stimulus (so each
contributes \(\eta_{ij} \approx e^{-2c}\)) and category B has 1 stored
exemplar at distance \(d = 0.2\) (contributing
\(\eta_{ij} \approx e^{-0.2c}\)). At a moderate sensitivity \(c = 1\):

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Summed: \(S_A = 12 \cdot e^{-2} \approx 1.62\) vs
  \(S_B = 1 \cdot e^{-0.2} \approx 0.82\). Category A wins.
\item
  Mean: \(\bar\eta_A = e^{-2} \approx 0.14\) vs
  \(\bar\eta_B = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.82\). Category B wins, which is what
  our intuition expects: the closest neighbour belongs to B.
\end{itemize}

The bias parameter \(\beta\) cannot repair this. Fitting \(\beta\) to
the data will compensate for the \emph{average} frequency imbalance, but
cannot fix it trial-by-trial because the imbalance itself grows and
shrinks as the memory store evolves.

This chapter therefore uses the \textbf{mean-similarity} formulation
throughout, which is the form adopted by F. Gregory Ashby and Maddox
(1993) and by several later papers that explicitly address this point.
At \(N_A = N_B\) (the canonical Kruschke task with balanced training)
the two formulations produce effectively identical behaviour, so
replication of published Kruschke-era results is unaffected. On data
with unequal base rates, partial trial sequences, or the non-stationary
scenarios introduced later in this chapter --- where memory composition
changes meaningfully over trials --- the difference becomes both
measurable and interpretable: with mean similarity, \(\beta\) becomes a
clean response-bias parameter rather than a fitted compensator for
implicit base-rate effects.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Toy demonstration of the summed{-}vs{-}mean choice with deliberately}
\CommentTok{\# unbalanced memory contents. 12 cat{-}A exemplars far away, 1 cat{-}B}
\CommentTok{\# exemplar close by. We show choice probabilities under both forms.}
\NormalTok{demo\_memory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{category =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{sim      =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }\DecValTok{12}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{))  }\CommentTok{\# pre{-}computed for c = 1}
\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{demo\_sums }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ demo\_memory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(category) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{sum\_sim =} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(sim), }\AttributeTok{mean\_sim =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(sim), }\AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{demo\_sums }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{p\_cat1\_summed =}\NormalTok{ sum\_sim  }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(sum\_sim),}
    \AttributeTok{p\_cat1\_mean   =}\NormalTok{ mean\_sim }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(mean\_sim)}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 2 x 5
  category sum_sim mean_sim p_cat1_summed p_cat1_mean
     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>         <dbl>       <dbl>
1        0   1.62     0.135         0.665       0.142
2        1   0.819    0.819         0.335       0.858
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Summed: P(cat 1) ≈ 0.34 — the 12 distant exemplars in category 0 win.}
\CommentTok{\# Mean:   P(cat 1) ≈ 0.85 — the 1 close exemplar in category 1 correctly wins.}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The demonstration above uses bias \(\beta = 0.5\) (neutral) so the
asymmetry comes purely from the aggregation choice. All figures,
recovery results, and SBC checks in the rest of this chapter use the
mean-similarity form. Readers who want to reproduce a published
summed-similarity study can swap the aggregation line in
\texttt{gcm\_simulate()} (replace each \texttt{mean()} with
\texttt{sum()}) and the corresponding two lines in the Stan model
(remove the division by \texttt{n1} and \texttt{n0}) without any other
changes.

\subsection{The GCM Agent Implementation (R
Simulation)}\label{the-gcm-agent-implementation-r-simulation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Generative GCM model function.}
\NormalTok{gcm\_simulate }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(w,            }\CommentTok{\# attention weights (sums to 1)}
\NormalTok{                         c,            }\CommentTok{\# sensitivity (natural scale, strictly positive)}
\NormalTok{                         bias,         }\CommentTok{\# response bias towards category 1}
\NormalTok{                         obs,          }\CommentTok{\# matrix of stimulus features (trials x features)}
\NormalTok{                         cat\_feedback, }\CommentTok{\# true category labels (0 or 1)}
                         \AttributeTok{quiet =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  ntrials   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(obs)}
\NormalTok{  nfeatures }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ncol}\NormalTok{(obs)}

\NormalTok{  prob\_cat1    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(ntrials)}
\NormalTok{  sim\_response }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(ntrials)}

\NormalTok{  memory\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NA\_real\_}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol =}\NormalTok{ nfeatures)}
\NormalTok{  memory\_cat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}

  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(ntrials)) \{}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{quiet }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ i }\SpecialCharTok{\%\%} \DecValTok{20} \SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Simulating trial:"}\NormalTok{, i, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{    current\_stim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(obs[i, ])}
\NormalTok{    n\_mem        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(memory\_obs)}
\NormalTok{    has\_cat0     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{any}\NormalTok{(memory\_cat }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    has\_cat1     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{any}\NormalTok{(memory\_cat }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (n\_mem }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0} \SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{has\_cat0 }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{has\_cat1) \{}
\NormalTok{      prob\_cat1[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ bias}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{      sims }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{vapply}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_mem), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) \{}
        \FunctionTok{similarity}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{distance}\NormalTok{(current\_stim, memory\_obs[e, ], w), c)}
\NormalTok{      \}, }\FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

      \CommentTok{\# Mean similarity per category — removes category{-}frequency bias.}
\NormalTok{      m1  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(sims[memory\_cat }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{])}
\NormalTok{      m0  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(sims[memory\_cat }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{])}
\NormalTok{      num }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ bias }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ m1}
\NormalTok{      den }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ num }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ bias) }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ m0}

\NormalTok{      prob\_cat1[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (den }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{1e{-}9}\NormalTok{) num }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ den }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ bias}
\NormalTok{      prob\_cat1[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{1e{-}9}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{1e{-}9}\NormalTok{, prob\_cat1[i]))}
\NormalTok{    \}}

\NormalTok{    sim\_response[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, prob\_cat1[i])}

\NormalTok{    memory\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbind}\NormalTok{(memory\_obs, current\_stim)}
\NormalTok{    memory\_cat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(memory\_cat, cat\_feedback[i])}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{prob\_cat1 =}\NormalTok{ prob\_cat1, }\AttributeTok{sim\_response =}\NormalTok{ sim\_response)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{Experimental Setup: Why John K. Kruschke
(1993)?}\label{experimental-setup-why-kruschke1993human}

To test our GCM implementation, we need a stimulus environment that is
more complex than a simple linear separation but structured enough to
reveal cognitive principles. We adopt the setup from John K. Kruschke
(1993) for three specific reasons:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{Continuous Dimension Benchmarking:} Unlike earlier
  binary-feature tasks (e.g., Medin and Schaffer (1978)), Kruschke uses
  stimuli (rectangles varying in height and position) that occupy a
  continuous 2D psychological space. This is the natural environment for
  the GCM's Minkowski distance metrics.
\item
  \textbf{Testing Selective Attention:} The task is designed so that
  both dimensions (height and position) are relevant, but their
  importance depends on the specific stimuli. This allows us to observe
  whether our model can ``discover'' the optimal attention weights
  (\(w\)) to minimize categorization error---a core feature of the
  ALCOVE extension to the GCM.
\item
  \textbf{Non-Linearity \& Abstraction:} The category structure (plotted
  below) is not perfectly separable by a single horizontal or vertical
  line. It requires the agent to either learn a diagonal boundary or to
  store the specific exemplars that form the ``corners'' of each
  category. This makes it a diagnostic benchmark for comparing exemplar
  models against the prototype models we'll see in Chapter 12.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stimulus\_info }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{stimulus      =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{height        =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{position      =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{category\_true =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ position, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ height,}
                          \AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(category\_true), }\AttributeTok{label =}\NormalTok{ stimulus)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_text}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{nudge\_y =} \FloatTok{0.18}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_manual}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{values =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"0"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#0072B2"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"1"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"\#D55E00"}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Category"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"@kruschke1993human Stimulus Structure"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Position"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Height"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{coord\_fixed}\NormalTok{()}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Per-Subject Schedule
Generation}\label{per-subject-schedule-generation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{make\_subject\_schedule }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, seed) \{}
  \FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(seed)}
\NormalTok{  n\_stim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info)}

\NormalTok{  sequence }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{unlist}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_blocks), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(b) \{}
    \FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{stimulus, n\_stim, }\AttributeTok{replace =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}))}

  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{trial\_within\_subject =} \FunctionTok{seq\_along}\NormalTok{(sequence),}
    \AttributeTok{block                =} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_blocks), }\AttributeTok{each =}\NormalTok{ n\_stim),}
    \AttributeTok{stimulus\_id          =}\NormalTok{ sequence}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{left\_join}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stimulus\_id"} \OtherTok{=} \StringTok{"stimulus"}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{rename}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{category\_feedback =}\NormalTok{ category\_true) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
\NormalTok{    dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(trial\_within\_subject, block, stimulus\_id,}
\NormalTok{                  height, position, category\_feedback)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Simulating Behavior with the
GCM}\label{simulating-behavior-with-the-gcm}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{simulate\_gcm\_agent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, w\_true, c\_true, bias\_true,}
\NormalTok{                               stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, subject\_seed) \{}
\NormalTok{  schedule }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_subject\_schedule}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, }\AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ subject\_seed)}
\NormalTok{  obs      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ schedule }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(height, position)}
\NormalTok{  feedback }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ schedule}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{category\_feedback}

\NormalTok{  sim\_results }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{gcm\_simulate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{w =}\NormalTok{ w\_true, }\AttributeTok{c =}\NormalTok{ c\_true, }\AttributeTok{bias =}\NormalTok{ bias\_true,}
    \AttributeTok{obs =}\NormalTok{ obs, }\AttributeTok{cat\_feedback =}\NormalTok{ feedback, }\AttributeTok{quiet =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  schedule }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{agent\_id     =}\NormalTok{ agent\_id,}
      \AttributeTok{w1\_true      =}\NormalTok{ w\_true[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{],}
      \AttributeTok{w2\_true      =}\NormalTok{ w\_true[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{],}
      \AttributeTok{c\_true       =}\NormalTok{ c\_true,}
      \AttributeTok{log\_c\_true   =} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(c\_true),}
      \AttributeTok{bias\_true    =}\NormalTok{ bias\_true,}
      \AttributeTok{prob\_cat1    =}\NormalTok{ sim\_results}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{prob\_cat1,}
      \AttributeTok{sim\_response =}\NormalTok{ sim\_results}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sim\_response,}
      \AttributeTok{correct      =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(category\_feedback }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ sim\_response)}
\NormalTok{    ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{performance =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(correct) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{param\_grid }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{w\_setting    =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{)),}
  \AttributeTok{c\_setting    =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{4.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{5.0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{15.0}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{bias\_setting =} \FloatTok{0.5}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{setting\_id =} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{(),}
    \AttributeTok{w\_label    =} \FunctionTok{map\_chr}\NormalTok{(w\_setting, }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\FunctionTok{paste}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(.x, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{), }\AttributeTok{collapse =} \StringTok{","}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{c\_label    =} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"c≈"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(c\_setting, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{label      =} \FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"w=("}\NormalTok{, w\_label, }\StringTok{"), "}\NormalTok{, c\_label)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{n\_agents\_per\_setting }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{5}

\NormalTok{simulation\_plan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ param\_grid }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{uncount}\NormalTok{(n\_agents\_per\_setting) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{())}

\NormalTok{sim\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simdata"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch12\_gcm\_simulated\_responses.csv"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_file)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(multisession, }\AttributeTok{workers =} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{availableCores}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{  simulated\_responses }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{future\_pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{agent\_id  =}\NormalTok{ simulation\_plan}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
      \AttributeTok{w\_true    =}\NormalTok{ simulation\_plan}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{w\_setting,}
      \AttributeTok{c\_true    =}\NormalTok{ simulation\_plan}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{c\_setting,}
      \AttributeTok{bias\_true =}\NormalTok{ simulation\_plan}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{bias\_setting}
\NormalTok{    ),}
\NormalTok{    simulate\_gcm\_agent,}
    \AttributeTok{stimulus\_info =}\NormalTok{ stimulus\_info,}
    \AttributeTok{n\_blocks      =}\NormalTok{ n\_blocks,}
    \AttributeTok{subject\_seed  =}\NormalTok{ simulation\_plan}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
    \AttributeTok{.options =} \FunctionTok{furrr\_options}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{seed =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}

  \FunctionTok{plan}\NormalTok{(sequential)}
  \FunctionTok{write\_csv}\NormalTok{(simulated\_responses, sim\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Simulation complete. Results saved.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  simulated\_responses }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{read\_csv}\NormalTok{(sim\_file, }\AttributeTok{show\_col\_types =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing simulation results.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\subsection{Prior Predictive Check}\label{prior-predictive-check-2}

We need to define the priors for the Stan model already here:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \(w \sim \text{Dirichlet}(1, 1)\) --- uniform over the simplex
\item
  \(\log c \sim \text{Normal}(\log(2), 1)\) --- implies
  \(c \in (0.27, 14.8)\) at \(\pm 2\) SD
\item
  \(\text{bias} \sim \text{Beta}(1, 1)\) --- uniform over \((0, 1)\)
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{n\_prior\_samples }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{500}

\NormalTok{prior\_draws }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{sample\_id =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_prior\_samples),}
  \AttributeTok{log\_c     =} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(n\_prior\_samples, LOG\_C\_PRIOR\_MEAN, LOG\_C\_PRIOR\_SD),}
  \AttributeTok{w1        =} \FunctionTok{runif}\NormalTok{(n\_prior\_samples),}
  \AttributeTok{bias      =} \FunctionTok{rbeta}\NormalTok{(n\_prior\_samples, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{c\_true =} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_c), }\AttributeTok{w2 =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ w1)}

\NormalTok{ppc\_schedule }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_subject\_schedule}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{999}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{prior\_pred\_curves }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{sample\_id =}\NormalTok{ prior\_draws}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sample\_id,}
    \AttributeTok{log\_c     =}\NormalTok{ prior\_draws}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{log\_c,}
    \AttributeTok{w1        =}\NormalTok{ prior\_draws}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{w1,}
    \AttributeTok{bias      =}\NormalTok{ prior\_draws}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{bias}
\NormalTok{  ),}
  \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(sample\_id, log\_c, w1, bias) \{}
\NormalTok{    c }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_c)}
\NormalTok{    sim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{gcm\_simulate}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{w =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(w1, }\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ w1), }\AttributeTok{c =}\NormalTok{ c, }\AttributeTok{bias =}\NormalTok{ bias,}
      \AttributeTok{obs          =}\NormalTok{ ppc\_schedule }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{select}\NormalTok{(height, position),}
      \AttributeTok{cat\_feedback =}\NormalTok{ ppc\_schedule}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{category\_feedback}
\NormalTok{    )}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{sample\_id =}\NormalTok{ sample\_id,}
      \AttributeTok{trial     =} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(ppc\_schedule)),}
      \AttributeTok{correct   =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(sim}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sim\_response }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ ppc\_schedule}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{category\_feedback)}
\NormalTok{    ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
      \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{cum\_acc =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(correct) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{())}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{prior\_pred\_summary }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prior\_pred\_curves }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(trial) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{q05 =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(cum\_acc, }\FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{q25 =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(cum\_acc, }\FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{q50 =} \FunctionTok{median}\NormalTok{(cum\_acc),}
    \AttributeTok{q75 =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(cum\_acc, }\FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{q95 =} \FunctionTok{quantile}\NormalTok{(cum\_acc, }\FloatTok{0.95}\NormalTok{),}
    \AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}
\NormalTok{  )}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prior\_pred\_summary, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q05, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q95), }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"\#56B4E9"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.20}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_ribbon}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ymin =}\NormalTok{ q25, }\AttributeTok{ymax =}\NormalTok{ q75), }\AttributeTok{fill =} \StringTok{"\#56B4E9"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.40}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ q50), }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"\#0072B2"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{color =} \StringTok{"grey40"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_y\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{limits =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prior Predictive Check: GCM Learning Curves"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Ribbons: 50\% and 90\% prior predictive intervals; line: median}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{Priors: log(c) \textasciitilde{} Normal(log 2, 1),  w \textasciitilde{} Dirichlet(1,1),  bias \textasciitilde{} Beta(1,1)"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cumulative Accuracy"}
\NormalTok{  )}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{11-CategorizationModelsExemplars_files/figure-pdf/ch12_prior_predictive-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\subsection{Implementing the GCM in Stan for Parameter
Estimation}\label{implementing-the-gcm-in-stan-for-parameter-estimation}

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{Architectural choice:
\texttt{prob\_cat1{[}i{]}} in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters}}{Architectural choice: prob\_cat1{[}i{]} in transformed parameters}}\label{architectural-choice-prob_cat1i-in-transformed-parameters}

Because the GCM's per-trial choice probability \(p_i\) is a
deterministic function of the parameters \((w, c, \beta)\) and the data
sequence, it belongs in \texttt{transformed\ parameters} as a single
source of truth. The \texttt{model\{\}} block then becomes a one-line
vectorised Bernoulli likelihood, and \texttt{generated\ quantities\{\}}
becomes a lookup against the already-computed \texttt{prob\_cat1}
vector.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}usetikzlibrary}\NormalTok{\{bayesnet\}}
\KeywordTok{\textbackslash{}begin}\NormalTok{\{}\ExtensionTok{tikzpicture}\NormalTok{\}}
  \CommentTok{\% ── Hyperparameters (const) ─────────────────────────────}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[const] (alpha\_w) at (0, 0)   \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}boldsymbol}\SpecialStringTok{\{}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}alpha}\SpecialStringTok{\}\_w$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[const] (hyp\_c)   at (4, 0)   \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}mu}\SpecialStringTok{\_c,}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{},\textbackslash{}sigma}\SpecialStringTok{\_c$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[const] (hyp\_b)   at (8, 0)   \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}alpha}\SpecialStringTok{\_}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}beta}\SpecialStringTok{,}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{},\textbackslash{}beta}\SpecialStringTok{\_}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}beta}\SpecialStringTok{$}\NormalTok{\};}

  \CommentTok{\% ── Latent parameters ───────────────────────────────────}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[latent] (w)    at (0, {-}2)  \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}mathbf}\SpecialStringTok{\{w\}$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[latent] (logc) at (4, {-}2)  \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}log}\SpecialStringTok{ c$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[latent] (bias) at (8, {-}2)  \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}beta}\SpecialStringTok{$}\NormalTok{\};}

  \CommentTok{\% ── Trial{-}level nodes (inside plate) ────────────────────}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[obs] (xi) at (2, {-}4.5) \{}\SpecialStringTok{$}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}mathbf}\SpecialStringTok{\{x\}\_i$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[obs] (zi) at (4, {-}4.5) \{}\SpecialStringTok{$z\_\{\textless{}i\}$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[det] (pi) at (6, {-}4.5) \{}\SpecialStringTok{$p\_i$}\NormalTok{\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}node}\NormalTok{[obs] (yi) at (6, {-}6.5) \{}\SpecialStringTok{$y\_i$}\NormalTok{\};}

  \CommentTok{\% ── Edges ───────────────────────────────────────────────}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}edge}\NormalTok{ \{alpha\_w\}        \{w\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}edge}\NormalTok{ \{hyp\_c\}          \{logc\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}edge}\NormalTok{ \{hyp\_b\}          \{bias\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}edge}\NormalTok{ \{w, logc, bias\}  \{pi\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}edge}\NormalTok{ \{xi, zi\}         \{pi\};}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}edge}\NormalTok{ \{pi\}             \{yi\};}

  \CommentTok{\% ── Plate ───────────────────────────────────────────────}
  \FunctionTok{\textbackslash{}plate}\NormalTok{ \{trial\} \{(xi)(zi)(pi)(yi)\} \{}\SpecialStringTok{$N$}\NormalTok{ trials\};}
\KeywordTok{\textbackslash{}end}\NormalTok{\{}\ExtensionTok{tikzpicture}\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

{\centering \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{11-CategorizationModelsExemplars_files/figure-pdf/gcm-single-dag-1.pdf}

}

\caption{DAG for the Single-Subject GCM. Hyperparameters (dots) feed
into latent parameters (circles): attention weights \(\mathbf{w}\), log
sensitivity \(\log c\), and response bias \(\beta\). Inside the plate,
the stimulus \(\mathbf{x}_i\) and accumulated exemplar memory \(z_{<i}\)
(past category feedback) combine with the parameters to yield the
deterministic choice probability \(p_i\) (double circle), from which the
response \(y_i\) is drawn.}

\end{figure}%

The DAG formalizes the four-stage structure from §Mathematical
Formulation above: priors on \((w, \log c, \beta)\) feed into
attention-weighted city-block distances, which yield per-category mean
similarities, which enter the Luce choice rule to produce \(p_i\); the
response \(y_i\) is then a Bernoulli draw.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{gcm\_single\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Generalized Context Model — Single Subject (refactored architecture)}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_total;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N\_features;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} y;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total, N\_features] real obs;}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} cat\_feedback;}

\StringTok{  vector[N\_features] prior\_w\_alpha;}
\StringTok{  real prior\_log\_c\_mu;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_log\_c\_sigma;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_bias\_alpha;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} prior\_bias\_beta;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed data \{}
\StringTok{  array[N\_total, N\_total, N\_features] real abs\_diff;}
\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{    for (j in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      for (f in 1:N\_features) \{}
\StringTok{        abs\_diff[i, j, f] = abs(obs[i, f] {-} obs[j, f]);}
\StringTok{      \}}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  simplex[N\_features] w;}
\StringTok{  real log\_c;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} bias;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} c = exp(log\_c);}
\StringTok{  vector\textless{}lower=1e{-}9, upper=1{-}1e{-}9\textgreater{}[N\_total] prob\_cat1;}

\StringTok{  \{}
\StringTok{    array[N\_total] int memory\_trial\_idx;}
\StringTok{    array[N\_total] int memory\_cat;}
\StringTok{    int n\_mem = 0;}

\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N\_total) \{}
\StringTok{      real p\_i;}
\StringTok{      int has\_cat0 = 0;}
\StringTok{      int has\_cat1 = 0;}

\StringTok{      for (k in 1:n\_mem) \{}
\StringTok{        if (memory\_cat[k] == 0) has\_cat0 = 1;}
\StringTok{        if (memory\_cat[k] == 1) has\_cat1 = 1;}
\StringTok{      \}}

\StringTok{      if (n\_mem == 0 || has\_cat0 == 0 || has\_cat1 == 0) \{}
\StringTok{        p\_i = bias;}
\StringTok{      \} else \{}
\StringTok{        real s1 = 0; real s0 = 0;}
\StringTok{        int n1 = 0;  int n0 = 0;}
\StringTok{        for (e in 1:n\_mem) \{}
\StringTok{          real d = 0;}
\StringTok{          int past\_i = memory\_trial\_idx[e];}
\StringTok{          for (f in 1:N\_features)}
\StringTok{            d += w[f] * abs\_diff[i, past\_i, f];}
\StringTok{          real sim = exp({-}c * d);}
\StringTok{          if (memory\_cat[e] == 1) \{ s1 += sim; n1 += 1; \}}
\StringTok{          else                    \{ s0 += sim; n0 += 1; \}}
\StringTok{        \}}
\StringTok{        real m1 = s1 / n1;}
\StringTok{        real m0 = s0 / n0;}
\StringTok{        real num = bias * m1;}
\StringTok{        real den = num + (1 {-} bias) * m0;}
\StringTok{        p\_i = (den \textgreater{} 1e{-}9) ? num / den : bias;}
\StringTok{      \}}

\StringTok{      prob\_cat1[i] = fmax(1e{-}9, fmin(1 {-} 1e{-}9, p\_i));}

\StringTok{      n\_mem += 1;}
\StringTok{      memory\_trial\_idx[n\_mem] = i;}
\StringTok{      memory\_cat[n\_mem] = cat\_feedback[i];}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += dirichlet\_lpdf(w    | prior\_w\_alpha);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(log\_c   | prior\_log\_c\_mu, prior\_log\_c\_sigma);}
\StringTok{  target += beta\_lpdf(bias      | prior\_bias\_alpha,  prior\_bias\_beta);}

\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_lpmf(y | prob\_cat1);}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{generated quantities \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N\_total] log\_lik;}
\StringTok{  real lprior;}
\StringTok{  if (run\_diagnostics) \{}
\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N\_total)}
\StringTok{      log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_lpmf(y[i] | prob\_cat1[i]);}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{  lprior = dirichlet\_lpdf(w | prior\_w\_alpha) +}
\StringTok{           normal\_lpdf(log\_c | prior\_log\_c\_mu, prior\_log\_c\_sigma) +}
\StringTok{           beta\_lpdf(bias | prior\_bias\_alpha, prior\_bias\_beta);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\NormalTok{stan\_file\_gcm\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"stan/ch12\_gcm\_single.stan"}
\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(gcm\_single\_stan, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch12\_gcm\_single.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch12_gcm_single.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod\_gcm\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file\_gcm\_single)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsubsection{What each block does:}\label{what-each-block-does}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{transformed\ data}}: Builds the
  \texttt{abs\_diff{[}i,\ j,\ f{]}} tensor. Evaluated exactly once per
  chain.
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{parameters}}: \texttt{simplex{[}N\_features{]}\ w},
  unconstrained \texttt{real\ log\_c}, and
  \texttt{real\textless{}lower=0,\ upper=1\textgreater{}\ bias}.
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{transformed\ parameters}}: Brings back the constraints
  on the parameters, and caches \texttt{prob\_cat1{[}i{]}} in a single
  forward pass over trials.
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{model}}: Priors followed by a vectorised one-line
  likelihood.
\item
  \textbf{\texttt{generated\ quantities}}: \texttt{log\_lik{[}i{]}} as a
  per-trial lookup, and \texttt{lprior} for \texttt{priorsense}.
\end{itemize}

\begin{quote}
A note on reduce\_sum and parallelization: The GCM's inner loop cannot
be parallelized with reduce\_sum because trial \(t\)'s contribution to
target depends on the memory state accumulated sequentially from trials
\(1, \ldots, t-1\).
\end{quote}

\subsubsection{Fitting the Single-Subject
GCM}\label{fitting-the-single-subject-gcm}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Select one simulated agent: w ≈ (0.9, 0.1), c ≈ 2}
\NormalTok{agent\_to\_fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ simulated\_responses }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(w1\_true, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{,}
    \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(log\_c\_true }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(log\_c\_true }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{)))}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(agent\_id }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(agent\_id)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{slice}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{total\_trials)}

\FunctionTok{stopifnot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(agent\_to\_fit) }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ total\_trials)}

\NormalTok{gcm\_data\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N\_total           =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(agent\_to\_fit),}
  \AttributeTok{N\_features        =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{L,}
  \AttributeTok{y                 =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(agent\_to\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sim\_response),}
  \AttributeTok{obs               =} \FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(agent\_to\_fit[, }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"height"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"position"}\NormalTok{)]),}
  \AttributeTok{cat\_feedback      =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(agent\_to\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{category\_feedback),}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_w\_alpha     =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_log\_c\_mu    =}\NormalTok{ LOG\_C\_PRIOR\_MEAN,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_log\_c\_sigma =}\NormalTok{ LOG\_C\_PRIOR\_SD,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_bias\_alpha  =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{prior\_bias\_beta   =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics   =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{fit\_filepath\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch12\_gcm\_single\_fit.rds"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(fit\_filepath\_single)) \{}
\NormalTok{  pf\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    mod\_gcm\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{pathfinder}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{data      =}\NormalTok{ gcm\_data\_single,}
      \AttributeTok{seed      =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{num\_paths =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{refresh   =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{    ),}
    \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) \{}
      \FunctionTok{message}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Pathfinder failed — falling back to default initialisation."}\NormalTok{)}
      \ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  fit\_gcm\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_gcm\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data            =}\NormalTok{ gcm\_data\_single,}
    \AttributeTok{init            =}\NormalTok{ pf\_single,}
    \AttributeTok{seed            =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains          =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \FunctionTok{min}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{availableCores}\NormalTok{()),}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup     =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling   =} \DecValTok{1500}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh         =} \DecValTok{300}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta     =} \FloatTok{0.9}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_gcm\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(fit\_filepath\_single)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Single{-}agent model fitted and saved.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_gcm\_single }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(fit\_filepath\_single)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Loaded existing single{-}agent fit.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Loaded existing single-agent fit.
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(fit\_gcm\_single)) \{}
\NormalTok{  diag\_tbl }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diagnostic\_summary\_table}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    fit\_gcm\_single,}
    \AttributeTok{params =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"w[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w[2]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_c"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"c"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
  \FunctionTok{print}\NormalTok{(diag\_tbl)}

  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{all}\NormalTok{(diag\_tbl}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{pass)) \{}
    \FunctionTok{warning}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Diagnostic battery has failures — interpret posteriors with caution."}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  \}}

\NormalTok{  bayesplot}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{mcmc\_trace}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    fit\_gcm\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_c"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{facet\_args =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  bayesplot}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{mcmc\_rank\_overlay}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    fit\_gcm\_single}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_c"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"w[1]"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"bias"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}
