\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{11-CategorizationModelsExemplars_files/figure-pdf/ch12_ml_decay_subject_recovery-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The \texttt{log\_c} row should be tight along the identity across
scenarios. The \texttt{log\_lambda} row is the diagnostic: scattered and
pulled toward the population mean under static (visible shrinkage when
an individual's data is uninformative), tighter along the diagonal under
drift, and tightest under contingent-shift --- consistent with the
single-subject recovery results from earlier in the chapter.

\subsection{Multilevel Simulation-Based Calibration
(SBC)}\label{multilevel-simulation-based-calibration-sbc}

Multilevel SBC is the most rigorous way to check that the hierarchical
priors and the non-centered parameterisation are working as intended.
Because fitting a multilevel model hundreds of times is computationally
intensive, we provide the code here as a template for students wishing
to perform an exhaustive validation of their own hierarchical
extensions.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# 1. Generator: Draws population parameters from priors, then subject }
\CommentTok{\#    parameters from those hierarchies, and finally simulates trial data.}
\NormalTok{make\_ml\_decay\_sbc\_generator }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(scenario, }\AttributeTok{n\_subjects =} \DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{) \{}
  \FunctionTok{SBC\_generator\_function}\NormalTok{(}
    \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{() \{}
      \CommentTok{\# Draw population hyperparameters from the priors}
\NormalTok{      pop\_logit\_w\_mean    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_logit\_w\_sd      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_log\_c\_mean      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, LOG\_C\_PRIOR\_MEAN, LOG\_C\_PRIOR\_SD)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_log\_c\_sd        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_log\_lambda\_mean }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, LOG\_LAMBDA\_PRIOR\_MEAN, LOG\_LAMBDA\_PRIOR\_SD)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_log\_lambda\_sd   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_logit\_bias\_mean }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rnorm}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      pop\_logit\_bias\_sd   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rexp}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
      
      \CommentTok{\# Simulate one multilevel dataset}
\NormalTok{      sim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_multilevel\_decay\_gcm}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{n\_subjects          =}\NormalTok{ n\_subjects,}
        \AttributeTok{stimulus\_info       =}\NormalTok{ stimulus\_info,}
        \AttributeTok{n\_blocks            =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\CommentTok{\# Fewer blocks for faster SBC}
        \AttributeTok{scenario            =}\NormalTok{ scenario,}
        \AttributeTok{base\_seed           =} \FunctionTok{sample.int}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{1e6}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_logit\_w\_mean    =}\NormalTok{ pop\_logit\_w\_mean,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_logit\_w\_sd      =}\NormalTok{ pop\_logit\_w\_sd,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_log\_c\_mean      =}\NormalTok{ pop\_log\_c\_mean,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_log\_c\_sd        =}\NormalTok{ pop\_log\_c\_sd,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_log\_lambda\_mean =}\NormalTok{ pop\_log\_lambda\_mean,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_log\_lambda\_sd   =}\NormalTok{ pop\_log\_lambda\_sd,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_logit\_bias\_mean =}\NormalTok{ pop\_logit\_bias\_mean,}
        \AttributeTok{pop\_logit\_bias\_sd   =}\NormalTok{ pop\_logit\_bias\_sd}
\NormalTok{      )}
      
      \CommentTok{\# Return parameters and the list for Stan}
      \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
        \AttributeTok{parameters =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
          \AttributeTok{pop\_logit\_w\_mean    =}\NormalTok{ pop\_logit\_w\_mean,}
          \AttributeTok{pop\_log\_c\_mean      =}\NormalTok{ pop\_log\_c\_mean,}
          \AttributeTok{pop\_log\_lambda\_mean =}\NormalTok{ pop\_log\_lambda\_mean,}
          \AttributeTok{pop\_logit\_bias\_mean =}\NormalTok{ pop\_logit\_bias\_mean}
\NormalTok{        ),}
        \AttributeTok{generated =} \FunctionTok{build\_ml\_stan\_data}\NormalTok{(sim}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{data, stimulus\_info)}
\NormalTok{      )}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# 2. Backend: Uses the compiled multilevel Stan model}
\NormalTok{ml\_decay\_sbc\_backend }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{SBC\_backend\_cmdstan\_sample}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  mod\_gcm\_ml\_decay,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{800}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{800}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}
\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# 3. Compute SBC (Uncomment to run)}
\CommentTok{\# ml\_sbc\_results \textless{}{-} compute\_SBC(}
\CommentTok{\#   make\_ml\_decay\_sbc\_generator("drift", n\_subjects = 5),}
\CommentTok{\#   ml\_decay\_sbc\_backend,}
\CommentTok{\#   n\_sims = 50,}
\CommentTok{\#   cache\_location = here("simdata", "ch12\_ml\_decay\_sbc\_cache")}
\CommentTok{\# )}

\CommentTok{\# 4. Visualize Results}
\CommentTok{\# plot\_rank\_hist(ml\_sbc\_results)}
\CommentTok{\# plot\_ecdf\_diff(ml\_sbc\_results)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{What changes when you scale
up}\label{what-changes-when-you-scale-up}

Three things to keep in mind when adapting this model to real data:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Sample size enters twice.} With more subjects, the population
  SDs become better identified, which shrinks subject-level estimates
  more aggressively toward the population mean --- desirable when
  individual-level data is noisy, but worth checking visually.
\item
  \textbf{Sequential-trial costs scale linearly.} Doubling trials per
  subject doubles per-leapfrog cost; doubling subjects doubles it again.
  Going from this 10×64 toy to a 100×200 dataset is a \textasciitilde30×
  increase in wall time, not 30 different fits to manage.
\item
  \textbf{Static-scenario behaviour is the canary.} If
  \texttt{pop\_log\_lambda\_mean} and \texttt{pop\_log\_lambda\_sd}
  start to diverge or drag at \texttt{max\_treedepth} on real data, your
  task is probably structurally closer to ``static'' than you think ---
  there is not enough non-stationarity for \(\lambda\) to be identified
  at the population level. Reduce the model or accept that \(\lambda\)
  is pinned by the prior and report it as such.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{GCM in Context: Strengths and
Limitations}\label{gcm-in-context-strengths-and-limitations}

\subsubsection{Strengths}\label{strengths}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Empirical support}: The GCM has successfully fit human
  categorization data across numerous experiments.
\item
  \textbf{Psychological plausibility}: The model's parameters correspond
  to psychologically meaningful constructs (attention,
  sensitivity/generalization, bias, and now forgetting).
\item
  \textbf{Flexibility}: It can account for a wide range of category
  structures and learning phenomena, including non-stationary
  environments.
\item
  \textbf{Individual differences}: The multilevel framework allows
  principled modeling of variations across people.
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Limitations}\label{limitations-1}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Memory requirements}: Storing all exemplars becomes
  computationally demanding for large numbers of stimuli. How memory is
  bounded over long timescales is not fully specified.
\item
  \textbf{Conflation of generalisation and decisional precision}: The
  standard GCM has no separate softmax temperature; sensitivity \(c\)
  doubles as a generalisation parameter and as a decisional precision
  parameter.
\item
  \textbf{Complex categories}: The standard GCM struggles with
  categories defined by abstract rules, relations, or logical
  combinations of features.
\item
  \textbf{Sequential structure and LOO}: Because the GCM is
  path-dependent, standard LOO-CV is technically invalid --- but this
  chapter implements LFO-CV (Bürkner et al., 2020) as the canonical
  sequential-model cross-validation.
\end{itemize}

In the next two chapters we explore alternative approaches --- prototype
models (Ch. 12) and rule-based models (Ch. 13) --- that address some of
these limitations while introducing their own strengths and challenges.

\subsection{Looking Ahead to Chapter 12: The Six-Step Pipeline in the
Prototype
Model}\label{looking-ahead-to-chapter-12-the-six-step-pipeline-in-the-prototype-model}

Chapter 12 applies the same six-step pipeline to a prototype-based
model. Before moving on, it is worth tracing explicitly how each stage
maps from the GCM to the Kalman filter prototype model --- because the
chapters are designed as a matched pair, and the contrasts at each stage
are the core theoretical content.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Pipeline Stage
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
GCM (this chapter)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Kalman Filter Prototype (Ch. 12)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\textbf{1. Input representation} & Stimuli as points in continuous
feature space (height, position). & Same feature space; stimuli used as
Kalman observations. \\
\textbf{2. Attention} & Explicit attention weights \(w_f\) summing to 1;
estimated from data. & No explicit attention weights; covariance
structure implicitly weights dimensions. \\
\textbf{3. Intermediate representation} & \textbf{All} past exemplars
stored verbatim; memory grows trial by trial. & One prototype
\(\vec{\mu}_k\) per category, updated by each observation; memory is
constant-size. \\
\textbf{4. Evidential mechanism} & Similarity to each stored exemplar,
exponentially decaying with distance and (in the extended model) trial
age. & Mahalanobis-like distance from the current stimulus to each
prototype, scaled by the running covariance \(\Sigma_k\). \\
\textbf{5. Decision rule} & Luce choice axiom:
\(p(\text{cat}_1) = \frac{\beta \cdot \bar{\eta}_1}{\beta \cdot \bar{\eta}_1 + (1-\beta) \cdot \bar{\eta}_0}\).
& Same Luce choice axiom; the per-trial probability is computed from the
Kalman-filter likelihood ratio. \\
\textbf{6. Learning} & Accumulate: each trial's exemplar enters
permanent (but decaying) memory. No structural update. & Update: the
Kalman gain \(K\) shifts \(\vec{\mu}_k\) toward the new observation and
reduces \(\Sigma_k\); the prediction step (process noise \(Q\)) can
re-inflate \(\Sigma_k\) between trials. \\
\end{longtable}

The key tension is at \textbf{Stage 3 and Stage 6}. The GCM treats every
trial as permanently informative (modulo forgetting); the prototype
model treats each trial as an observation that refines a running
summary. As a result:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  The GCM is maximally sensitive to specific exemplars and their spatial
  arrangement. The prototype model is sensitive only to the category
  centroid and spread.
\item
  The GCM's non-stationarity mechanism (decay \(\lambda\)) operates
  \emph{retrospectively} --- old exemplars become less influential as
  time passes. The Kalman filter's mechanism (process noise \(q\))
  operates \emph{prospectively} --- future observations are expected to
  diverge from the current prototype.
\item
  Both mechanisms are phenomenologically similar under smooth drift.
  They diverge most sharply under abrupt shifts: the GCM gradually
  forgets old contradicting exemplars, while the Kalman filter
  re-inflates uncertainty after surprising observations and quickly
  reacquires the new prototype.
\end{itemize}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{What to watch for when reading Chapter 12}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{The \(q\)/\(r\) identifiability problem.} The Kalman filter's
  two parameters (\(r\) = observation noise, \(q\) = process noise) are
  more tightly confounded under static data than the GCM's
  \(c\)/\(\lambda\) pair --- they both scale the \emph{same}
  decision-time likelihood rather than operating on orthogonal axes (see
  §``The \(c\)/\(\lambda\) Joint Posterior'' above). Ch. 12 introduces
  an explicit reparameterization (the SNR parameter \(\rho = q/r\)) to
  diagnose and partially resolve this.
\item
  \textbf{The scenario order is deliberately reversed.} Ch. 12 arranges
  scenarios as static → contingent shift → drift (increasing assumption
  match for the Kalman filter), while this chapter used static → drift →
  contingent shift (increasing \(\lambda\) preference). The three
  scenarios are identical; only the ordering logic differs.
\item
  \textbf{No attention weights.} Removing \(w\) and \(\beta\) from the
  parameter set makes the prototype model simpler to diagnose --- SBC
  rank histograms have two curves instead of four, and pair plots have
  one panel instead of six. That simplicity is \emph{why} Ch. 12 is the
  right place to introduce LFO-CV in detail: the algorithm is most
  legible when the model is lean.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\bookmarksetup{startatroot}

\chapter{Prototype Models: Learning Category
Averages}\label{prototype-models-learning-category-averages}

\begin{quote}
\textbf{📍 Where we are in the Bayesian modeling workflow:} Chapter 11
built the GCM --- an exemplar model that stores every encountered
stimulus and decides by summed similarity to memory. This chapter
introduces a structurally different account: the \textbf{prototype
model}, which abstracts each category to a single running estimate (its
mean and uncertainty) rather than storing individual instances. We
implement prototype learning as a multivariate Kalman filter and then
add a \textbf{Stan parameter-estimation model} for the prototype
architecture.

The full validation pipeline from Ch. 5 --- prior predictive check,
Pathfinder initialization, mandatory MCMC diagnostic battery, posterior
predictive check, prior--posterior update plot, randomised LOO-PIT,
LFO-CV, precision analysis, prior sensitivity, and SBC --- is applied to
this new model. Crucially, the \textbf{LFO-CV machinery implemented in
Chapter 11} (\texttt{psis\_lfo\_gcm}) is generalised here as
\texttt{psis\_lfo\_kalman} and applied to the prototype model. The
chapter then extends to a \textbf{multilevel prototype model} with
population-level priors on \texttt{log\_r}, per-subject NCP offsets, and
the full multilevel SBC battery (population and individual calibration).
\end{quote}

\section{Rethinking Category Representation: From Examples to
Averages}\label{rethinking-category-representation-from-examples-to-averages}

In the previous chapter, we explored the Generalized Context Model
(GCM), an exemplar model where every encountered example is stored in
memory. This approach is powerful but raises questions about cognitive
efficiency. Do we really store every single instance we encounter?

Consider learning about common categories like ``birds'' or ``chairs''.
While specific examples matter, we also seem to develop a general sense
of what constitutes a typical member. Prototype theory offers an
alternative perspective: instead of storing individual exemplars, the
mind abstracts a summary representation --- a prototype --- for each
category. This prototype often represents the central tendency or
``average'' member.

\section{Core Ideas of Prototype
Models}\label{core-ideas-of-prototype-models}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Abstraction, Not Storage}: The primary representation is a
  summary (the prototype), not a collection of instances.
\item
  \textbf{Cognitive Economy}: Storing one prototype per category is
  potentially much more memory-efficient than storing numerous
  exemplars.
\item
  \textbf{Similarity to Prototype}: New items are categorized based on
  their similarity to the stored prototypes of different categories.
\item
  \textbf{Typicality}: Items closer to a category's prototype are judged
  as more ``typical'' members and are often categorized faster and more
  accurately. This contrasts with exemplar models, which emphasize
  detailed memory for specific instances.
\end{itemize}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Feature
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Prototype Models
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Exemplar Models (GCM)
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Representation & Abstract summary (e.g., average) & Collection of
individual examples \\
Memory Cost & Low (one prototype per category) & High (potentially all
examples) \\
Key Information & Central tendency \& variance & Specific instances \&
their labels \\
Sensitivity & Less sensitive to specific examples & Highly sensitive to
specific examples \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Historical Context: From Typicality Effects to Dynamic Prototypes}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The prototype approach to categorization did not emerge fully formed. It
arose as the solution to a specific failure mode of the dominant view
--- and then itself had to be rescued from static simplicity by
importing engineering mathematics.

\subsubsection{The Classical View and Its
Discontents}\label{the-classical-view-and-its-discontents}

Through the 1950s and into the 1960s, both philosophers and
psychologists took it for granted that concepts had \emph{defining
features}: necessary and sufficient conditions that every member must
satisfy. A bachelor is an unmarried adult male --- full stop.
Categorization, on this view, was logical: check the features, apply the
rule. The model is appealingly crisp, but Wittgenstein had already noted
the trouble in \emph{Philosophical Investigations} (1953): most ordinary
categories --- \emph{game}, \emph{furniture}, \emph{bird} --- resist
such definitions. Family resemblance, not shared essence, seemed to be
the operative structure.

The empirical broadside came from Eleanor Rosch (E. H. Rosch 1973; E.
Rosch 1975; E. Rosch et al. 1976). In a series of influential
experiments she documented \textbf{typicality effects}: robins are
judged faster and more confidently as birds than penguins are; desk
chairs are more chair-like than beanbags. Categories showed graded
membership rather than all-or-nothing inclusion. Rosch also identified a
privileged \textbf{basic level} --- the level of abstraction (dog, not
animal and not poodle) at which objects are most efficiently perceived,
named, and remembered. Neither observation is compatible with the
classical view, which predicts uniform membership within a category and
no principled distinction across levels.

\subsubsection{The Prototype as Psychological
Mechanism}\label{the-prototype-as-psychological-mechanism}

Rosch's work raised the question of \emph{representation}: if not a
rule, what mental structure supports graded membership? The answer on
offer was a \textbf{prototype} --- a summary representation encoding the
central tendency of encountered category members. New objects are
categorized by their distance from stored prototypes; items close to the
prototype are typical, items distant are atypical.

Formal prototype models predated Rosch's program. Posner and Keele
(1968) showed that subjects who had studied distorted dot patterns
without ever seeing the prototype nonetheless recognized the prototype
at the end as highly familiar --- abstraction was occurring implicitly.
Reed (1972) implemented an explicit prototype-matching classifier and
showed it outperformed several alternatives on face-classification data.
These early models computed a \textbf{static average} from training
examples: the prototype was fixed after learning and then used as a
lookup.

\subsubsection{Static Prototypes and the Exemplar
Challenge}\label{static-prototypes-and-the-exemplar-challenge}

The clean elegance of static prototype models made them obvious targets
for empirical attack. Medin and Schaffer (1978) showed that, on certain
category structures, the Context Model (a pure exemplar model) fit human
data significantly better than a prototype model could. The critical
cases were \textbf{exception items}: an atypical member of one category
that closely resembles the other category's prototype. Exemplar models
accommodate exceptions naturally --- the deviant item is stored
individually. A static prototype, by definition, assimilates the deviant
item into the average, smoothing away the very information that drives
behavior.

Through the 1980s and early 1990s, the prototype-versus-exemplar debate
generated substantial empirical literature. The consensus, codified in
Smith and Minda (1998), was that neither account was universally
superior: prototype-like effects dominated in some conditions (large
training sets, brief study times, transfer to novel stimuli) while
exemplar-like effects dominated in others (small training sets, extended
learning, highly distinctive items). The field was left with a
theoretical draw that demanded either a hybrid architecture or a shift
in the question being asked.

\subsubsection{From Static Averaging to Bayesian
Updating}\label{from-static-averaging-to-bayesian-updating}

The decisive conceptual move was to ask not \emph{what is stored} but
\emph{how the representation is updated}. A static average treats all
observed exemplars symmetrarily regardless of when they were
encountered. This is inconsistent with the well-documented finding that
recent experiences carry more weight than remote ones (Gluck and Bower
1988; Estes 1994). It is also inconsistent with the ability to track
categories that drift over time --- the clothing you learn is
``fashionable'' today may not be fashionable in five years.

The Kalman filter (Kalman 1960), developed originally for aerospace
navigation, provided an off-the-shelf solution. It is the
\textbf{minimum-variance unbiased estimator} for a linear-Gaussian
dynamical system: at each step it computes exactly how much weight to
place on the new observation versus the accumulated prior, using the
relative magnitudes of measurement noise \(R\) and process noise \(Q\).
When \(Q = 0\) the prototype is assumed static and learning rate decays
monotonically --- recovering the classical static average as a special
case. When \(Q > 0\) the prototype can drift and the steady-state Kalman
gain remains positive, allowing perpetual sensitivity to new
observations.

Applications of Kalman-style updating to human learning appear across
multiple domains: classical conditioning (Dayan, Kakade, and Montague
2000), interval timing, and reward prediction. The prototype-learning
context is a natural fit because the Kalman filter formalises the very
trade-off prototype theory requires: balance between the accumulated
representation (prior) and the informational content of the newest
instance (likelihood). The two-parameter family \((r, q)\) instantiates
the entire spectrum from pure exemplar-like responsiveness (\(r\) small,
steep gain) to heavily smoothed prototype abstraction (\(r\) large,
shallow gain, slow forgetting).

\subsubsection{The Bayesian Identity}\label{the-bayesian-identity}

The Kalman filter is not merely an engineering heuristic applied to
psychology: it is the exact Bayesian posterior for a Gaussian
state-space model. The prototype at trial \(t\) is the posterior mean
\(\mathbb{E}[\mu_t \mid x_{1:t}]\); the covariance \(\Sigma_t\) is the
posterior variance. Recognising this identity connects the prototype
model to the broader programme of \textbf{rational analysis} (Anderson
1991) and \textbf{Bayesian models of cognition} (Tenenbaum et al. 2011):
the learner is performing optimal inference about a latent category
structure given noisy, sequentially arriving evidence. The parameters
\(r\) and \(q\) then carry clear psychological interpretations ---
observation noise (how variable are category members around the
prototype?) and process noise (how rapidly does the category itself
change?) --- rather than being purely phenomenological fitting
coefficients.

This framing also clarifies the key difference from the GCM: where the
GCM stores the full history and retrieves it at decision time, the
Kalman prototype model \textbf{compresses} the history into sufficient
statistics (mean and covariance) that are updated recursively. The
compression is lossless when the generative model is Gaussian and linear
--- which is why recovery and SBC are cleaner here than in most
cognitive models of comparable scope.

\end{tcolorbox}

\section{Modeling Dynamic Prototypes: The Kalman
Filter}\label{modeling-dynamic-prototypes-the-kalman-filter}

Early prototype models often calculated a static average. However, human
learning is dynamic; our understanding evolves with experience. How can
we model a prototype that updates incrementally as new examples are
encountered?

The Kalman filter provides a suitable mathematical framework. Originally
developed for tracking physical systems amidst noise, it's well-suited
for modeling prototype learning because it allows us to:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Track an Estimate}: The category prototype (its average
  feature values).
\item
  \textbf{Represent Uncertainty}: Maintain not just the prototype's
  location but also our uncertainty about it.
\item
  \textbf{Update Incrementally}: Refine the prototype with each new
  example.
\item
  \textbf{Balance Old and New}: Optimally combine the current prototype
  (prior belief) with the new example (evidence).
\item
  \textbf{Implement an Adaptive Learning Rate}: Automatically adjust how
  much influence a new example has (the ``Kalman gain'') based on
  current uncertainty. Learning is faster when uncertainty is high and
  slows as confidence grows.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{The Kalman Filter Logic: Updating an
Estimate}\label{the-kalman-filter-logic-updating-an-estimate}

Imagine tracking a prototype based on a single feature (e.g., average
height). The Kalman filter maintains your Current State. At any moment,
you have two key pieces of information:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Your Best Guess (\(\mu\)):} This is your current estimate of
  the prototype's average height.
\item
  \textbf{Your Confidence (\(\sigma^2\)):} This isn't just \emph{if}
  you're confident, but \emph{how much}. It's the \emph{variance} around
  your guess. A small variance means you're quite sure; a large variance
  means you're very unsure.
\end{itemize}

When a new category member (observation \(x\)) arrives, you measure its
height.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{The Catch:} Your measurement tool (or the example itself)
  isn't perfect. There's some inherent ``noise'' or variability in how
  examples relate to the true prototype. We represent the uncertainty of
  this \emph{measurement process} with another variance, often called
  \(R\).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{The Core Problem:} You have your guess (with
\(\sigma^2_{prev}\)) and a new measurement (with \(R\)). How do you
combine them to get a \emph{new, better guess} and an \emph{updated
level of uncertainty}?

\textbf{Enter the Kalman Gain (\(K\)): The ``Trust'' Knob}

The Kalman filter calculates the Kalman Gain \(K\), which tells you how
much to trust the \emph{new measurement} compared to your \emph{current
guess}:

\[K = \frac{\sigma^2_{prev}}{\sigma^2_{prev} + R}\]

If \(\sigma^2_{prev}\) is large relative to \(R\), then \(K \to 1\):
trust the measurement almost completely. If \(\sigma^2_{prev}\) is small
relative to \(R\), then \(K \to 0\): stick closer to your previous
guess.

\textbf{Updating Your Guess:}

You nudge your old guess towards the new measurement. The size of the
nudge is determined by the Kalman Gain \(K\):

\[\mu_{new} = \mu_{prev} + K \cdot (x - \mu_{prev})\]

The term \((x - \mu_{prev})\) is the ``prediction error'' or
``innovation'' --- how much the new data differs from what you expected.

\textbf{Updating Your Confidence (Uncertainty):}

After incorporating new information, uncertainty decreases:

\[\sigma^2_{new} = (1 - K) \cdot \sigma^2_{prev}\]

Since \(K \in (0, 1)\), multiplying by \((1 - K)\) always shrinks
uncertainty. The more you trusted the measurement (higher \(K\)), the
more your uncertainty shrinks.

\textbf{Extending to drifting targets.} The scalar Kalman filter above
assumes a static target: uncertainty monotonically decreases because
there is no mechanism for it to grow back. The prototype model in this
chapter adds a \textbf{process noise term} \(Q\). Before each
measurement update, a \emph{prediction step} is applied:

\[\sigma^2_{k|k-1} = \sigma^2_{k-1|k-1} + Q\]

This increases uncertainty between observations, reflecting the
possibility that the true prototype has shifted since the last trial.
The subsequent measurement update then decreases uncertainty via the
Kalman gain, exactly as before. At steady state, the two forces balance
and uncertainty converges to a fixed value rather than shrinking to
zero. When \(Q = 0\) the model reduces to the static-target filter. We
treat the scalar \(q\) (the diagonal of \(Q = q \cdot I\)) as a free
parameter to be estimated from data, alongside the observation noise
\(r\).

\subsection{Extending to Multiple Features: Multivariate Kalman
Filter}\label{extending-to-multiple-features-multivariate-kalman-filter}

Our stimuli have two features (height and position). We need a
multivariate version:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Your Current State (Vectors and Matrices):}

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \textbf{Your Best Guess (\(\vec{\mu}\)):} Now a \emph{vector}
    containing your best guess for each feature.
  \item
    \textbf{Your Confidence (\(\Sigma\)):} Now a \emph{covariance
    matrix}. The diagonal elements give the variance for each feature
    individually; the off-diagonal elements give their covariance ---
    for example, whether taller prototypes are also typically further to
    the right.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{A New Hint Arrives:} Your new observation (\(\vec{x}\)) is
  also a vector.

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \textbf{Measurement Noise (\(R\)):} Also a covariance matrix. Often
    simplified to a diagonal matrix, assuming measurement errors on
    different features are independent.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  \textbf{The Logic Stays the Same, the Math Uses Matrices:}

  \begin{itemize}
  \tightlist
  \item
    \textbf{Kalman Gain (\(K\)):} A \emph{matrix}:
    \(K = \Sigma_{prev} (\Sigma_{prev} + R)^{-1}\)
  \item
    \textbf{Updating Your Guess (\(\vec{\mu}\)):}
    \(\vec{\mu}_{new} = \vec{\mu}_{prev} + K (\vec{x} - \vec{\mu}_{prev})\)
  \item
    \textbf{Updating Your Confidence (\(\Sigma\)):} We use the
    numerically stable Joseph form:
    \(\Sigma_{new} = (I - K)\Sigma_{prev}(I - K)^T + KRK^T\). While it
    looks complex, the intuition is the same as the scalar case:
    uncertainty decreases after each update. The Joseph form is
    preferred because it guarantees the result remains symmetric and
    positive semi-definite even in the presence of floating-point
    rounding errors.
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{In a Nutshell}\label{in-a-nutshell}

The Kalman filter is a recursive algorithm that provides the
mathematically optimal way to estimate the state of a system (like the
features of a prototype) given a series of noisy measurements. It works
by:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  Maintaining an estimate (\(\vec{\mu}\)) and uncertainty (\(\Sigma\)).
\item
  Calculating a Kalman Gain \(K\) that balances trust in the current
  estimate against trust in the new measurement.
\item
  Updating the estimate by moving it towards the new measurement,
  weighted by \(K\).
\item
  Updating (reducing) the uncertainty based on the information gained.
\end{enumerate}

The multivariate version simply uses vectors and matrices to do this for
multiple, potentially correlated variables simultaneously.

\subsection{Implementing the Multivariate Update in
R}\label{implementing-the-multivariate-update-in-r}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# One update step of a multivariate Kalman filter.}
\CommentTok{\# Returns updated mean vector, covariance matrix, and Kalman gain.}
\NormalTok{multivariate\_kalman\_update }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(mu\_prev,      }\CommentTok{\# previous mean vector}
\NormalTok{                                       sigma\_prev,    }\CommentTok{\# previous covariance matrix}
\NormalTok{                                       observation,   }\CommentTok{\# observed feature vector}
\NormalTok{                                       r\_matrix) \{    }\CommentTok{\# observation noise matrix}
\NormalTok{  mu\_prev     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(mu\_prev)}
\NormalTok{  observation }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(observation)}
\NormalTok{  sigma\_prev  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(sigma\_prev)}
\NormalTok{  r\_matrix    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(r\_matrix)}

\NormalTok{  n\_dim }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(mu\_prev)}
\NormalTok{  I     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(n\_dim)}

  \CommentTok{\# Kalman gain: K = Sigma\_prev * (Sigma\_prev + R)\^{}\{{-}1\}}
\NormalTok{  S     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sigma\_prev }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ r\_matrix}
\NormalTok{  S\_inv }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{solve}\NormalTok{(S),}
    \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) \{}
      \FunctionTok{warning}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Matrix inversion failed; using pseudo{-}inverse."}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{call. =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{      MASS}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{ginv}\NormalTok{(S)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  K }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sigma\_prev }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ S\_inv}

  \CommentTok{\# Update mean}
\NormalTok{  innovation }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ observation }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ mu\_prev}
\NormalTok{  mu\_new     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(mu\_prev }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ K }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ innovation)}

  \CommentTok{\# Update covariance — Joseph form for numerical stability}
\NormalTok{  IK        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ I }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ K}
\NormalTok{  sigma\_new }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ IK }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ sigma\_prev }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%} \FunctionTok{t}\NormalTok{(IK) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ K }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%}\NormalTok{ r\_matrix }\SpecialCharTok{\%*\%} \FunctionTok{t}\NormalTok{(K)}

  \CommentTok{\# Enforce symmetry (floating{-}point can introduce tiny asymmetry)}
\NormalTok{  sigma\_new }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ (sigma\_new }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{t}\NormalTok{(sigma\_new)) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{2}

  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu =}\NormalTok{ mu\_new, }\AttributeTok{sigma =}\NormalTok{ sigma\_new, }\AttributeTok{k =}\NormalTok{ K)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This function performs a single update for one category's prototype when
it observes a new member.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

We are fitting a \emph{two-parameter} model: \(r\) controls both Kalman
gain and decisional precision; \(q\) controls prototype drift speed and
steady-state uncertainty. Both are inferred from data on the log scale.
There is no category bias, fixed initial means, fixed initial
covariances, and diagonal noise structures for both \(R\) and \(Q\). The
Stan implementation in §``The Prototype Model in Stan'' implements
exactly this.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Building the Full Categorization
Model}\label{building-the-full-categorization-model}

We now integrate this update mechanism into a full agent that learns
prototypes for two categories and makes categorization decisions.

\subsection{Initialization}\label{initialization}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  We need separate prototypes (\(\mu_0, \Sigma_0\) and
  \(\mu_1, \Sigma_1\)) for Category 0 and Category 1.
\item
  We start with high uncertainty (large initial \(\Sigma\)) and
  uninformative means at the center of the feature space.
\item
  We define the observation noise matrix \(R = r_\text{val} \cdot I\).
  The parameter \(r_\text{val}\) represents how much variability we
  assume exists within categories or in our perception of stimuli.
\item
  We define the process noise matrix \(Q = q_\text{val} \cdot I\). The
  parameter \(q_\text{val}\) controls how much the category prototype
  may drift between successive observations. Small \(q\) means
  near-static categories; large \(q\) means rapidly shifting ones.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Categorization Decision}\label{categorization-decision}

How does the model decide the category for a new stimulus \(\vec{x}\)?
It compares \(\vec{x}\) to both prototypes, considering their current
means (\(\vec{\mu}\)) and uncertainties (\(\Sigma\)). We use the
probability density of the multivariate normal distribution:

\[P(\vec{x} \mid \text{Category } C) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\vec{x} - \vec{\mu}_C)^T (\Sigma_C + R)^{-1} (\vec{x} - \vec{\mu}_C)\right)\]

The probability of choosing Category 1 is then calculated using the Luce
choice rule (softmax):

\[P(\text{Choose Cat 1} \mid \vec{x}) = \frac{P(\vec{x} \mid \text{Category } 1)}{P(\vec{x} \mid \text{Category } 0) + P(\vec{x} \mid \text{Category } 1)}\]

We work with log-probabilities for numerical stability. Note that the
same \(r\) that drives the Kalman update also enters here through
\((\Sigma_C + R)^{-1}\) --- there is no separate softmax temperature.

\subsection{The Learning Loop}\label{the-learning-loop}

The agent processes trials sequentially:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Predict}: Add \(Q\) to both category covariances ---
  \(\Sigma_{0} \mathrel{+}= Q\), \(\Sigma_{1} \mathrel{+}= Q\) ---
  reflecting that each prototype may have drifted since the last
  observation.
\item
  \textbf{Decide}: Calculate \(P(\text{Choose Cat 1} \mid \vec{x}_i)\)
  based on the post-prediction \(\mu_0, \Sigma_0, \mu_1, \Sigma_1\).
\item
  \textbf{Respond}: Generate a response.
\item
  \textbf{Receive feedback}: Observe true category \(y_i\).
\item
  \textbf{Update}: Apply the Kalman measurement update to the correct
  category only.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{R Implementation of the Prototype
Agent}\label{r-implementation-of-the-prototype-agent}

The function now accepts \texttt{subject\_seed} and calls
\texttt{make\_subject\_schedule} internally --- consistent with the
path-dependence fix from Ch. 11. Each agent's learning trajectory
depends on the order in which stimuli are encountered, and different
randomizations produce genuinely different trajectories.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Prototype model agent using Kalman filter with process noise for categorization.}
\CommentTok{\# r\_value   : observation noise variance (scalar \textgreater{} 0)}
\CommentTok{\# q\_value   : process noise variance / prototype drift rate (scalar \textgreater{}= 0)}
\CommentTok{\# obs       : matrix of observations (trials x features)}
\CommentTok{\# cat\_one   : vector of true category labels (0 or 1) for feedback}
\CommentTok{\# Returns:  tibble with prob\_cat1 and sim\_response}
\NormalTok{prototype\_kalman }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(r\_value,}
\NormalTok{                             q\_value,}
\NormalTok{                             obs,}
\NormalTok{                             cat\_one,}
                             \AttributeTok{initial\_mu         =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{,}
                             \AttributeTok{initial\_sigma\_diag =} \FloatTok{10.0}\NormalTok{,}
                             \AttributeTok{quiet              =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  n\_trials   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(obs)}
\NormalTok{  n\_features }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ncol}\NormalTok{(obs)}

  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(initial\_mu)) \{}
\NormalTok{    mu0\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, n\_features)}
\NormalTok{    mu1\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, n\_features)}
\NormalTok{  \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    mu0\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ initial\_mu[[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]]}
\NormalTok{    mu1\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ initial\_mu[[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{]]}
\NormalTok{  \}}

\NormalTok{  prototype\_cat\_0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu =}\NormalTok{ mu0\_init, }\AttributeTok{sigma =} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(initial\_sigma\_diag, n\_features))}
\NormalTok{  prototype\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu =}\NormalTok{ mu1\_init, }\AttributeTok{sigma =} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(initial\_sigma\_diag, n\_features))}
\NormalTok{  r\_matrix        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(r\_value, n\_features)}
\NormalTok{  q\_matrix        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(q\_value, n\_features)}

\NormalTok{  response\_probs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{numeric}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)}

\NormalTok{  log\_sum\_exp }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(v) \{}
\NormalTok{    m }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(v)}
\NormalTok{    m }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(v }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ m)))}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)) \{}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{quiet }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ i }\SpecialCharTok{\%\%} \DecValTok{20} \SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{, i, }\StringTok{"}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{    current\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(obs[i, ])}

    \CommentTok{\# ── Prediction step: add process noise to both prototypes ──────────────}
    \CommentTok{\# Reflects potential drift in category location since last trial.}
    \CommentTok{\# When q\_value = 0 this reduces to the static{-}target filter.}
\NormalTok{    prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ q\_matrix}
\NormalTok{    prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ q\_matrix}

    \CommentTok{\# ── Decision ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────}
\NormalTok{    cov\_cat\_0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ r\_matrix}
\NormalTok{    cov\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ r\_matrix}

\NormalTok{    log\_prob\_0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{      mvtnorm}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{dmvnorm}\NormalTok{(current\_obs, }\AttributeTok{mean =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu,}
                       \AttributeTok{sigma =}\NormalTok{ cov\_cat\_0, }\AttributeTok{log =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
      \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\ConstantTok{Inf}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{    log\_prob\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{      mvtnorm}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{dmvnorm}\NormalTok{(current\_obs, }\AttributeTok{mean =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu,}
                       \AttributeTok{sigma =}\NormalTok{ cov\_cat\_1, }\AttributeTok{log =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
      \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\ConstantTok{Inf}
\NormalTok{    )}

    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.finite}\NormalTok{(log\_prob\_0) }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.finite}\NormalTok{(log\_prob\_1)) \{}
\NormalTok{      prob\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.finite}\NormalTok{(log\_prob\_0)) \{}
\NormalTok{      prob\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{1.0}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else} \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{is.finite}\NormalTok{(log\_prob\_1)) \{}
\NormalTok{      prob\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.0}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{      prob\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(log\_prob\_1 }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{log\_sum\_exp}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(log\_prob\_0, log\_prob\_1)))}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{    response\_probs[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{1e{-}9}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{pmin}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{1e{-}9}\NormalTok{, prob\_cat\_1))}

    \CommentTok{\# ── Update (measurement update for the correct category only) ────────────}
\NormalTok{    true\_cat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ cat\_one[i]}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (true\_cat }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{      upd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{multivariate\_kalman\_update}\NormalTok{(prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu, prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma,}
\NormalTok{                                        current\_obs, r\_matrix)}
\NormalTok{      prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu}
\NormalTok{      prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma}
\NormalTok{    \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{      upd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{multivariate\_kalman\_update}\NormalTok{(prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu, prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma,}
\NormalTok{                                        current\_obs, r\_matrix)}
\NormalTok{      prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu}
\NormalTok{      prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  \}}

  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{prob\_cat1    =}\NormalTok{ response\_probs,}
    \AttributeTok{sim\_response =} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, response\_probs)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Wrapper: generates per{-}agent schedule then calls prototype\_kalman}
\NormalTok{simulate\_prototype\_agent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(agent\_id, r\_value, q\_value,}
\NormalTok{                                     stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, subject\_seed) \{}
\NormalTok{  schedule }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_subject\_schedule}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, }\AttributeTok{seed =}\NormalTok{ subject\_seed)}
\NormalTok{  obs      }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(schedule[, }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"height"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"position"}\NormalTok{)])}
\NormalTok{  cat\_one  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ schedule}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{category\_feedback}

\NormalTok{  result }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{prototype\_kalman}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{r\_value            =}\NormalTok{ r\_value,}
    \AttributeTok{q\_value            =}\NormalTok{ q\_value,}
    \AttributeTok{obs                =}\NormalTok{ obs,}
    \AttributeTok{cat\_one            =}\NormalTok{ cat\_one,}
    \AttributeTok{initial\_mu         =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{initial\_sigma\_diag =} \FloatTok{10.0}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{quiet              =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}
\NormalTok{  )}

\NormalTok{  schedule }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{agent\_id     =}\NormalTok{ agent\_id,}
      \AttributeTok{r\_value\_true =}\NormalTok{ r\_value,}
      \AttributeTok{q\_value\_true =}\NormalTok{ q\_value,}
      \AttributeTok{log\_r\_true   =} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(r\_value),}
      \AttributeTok{log\_q\_true   =} \FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(q\_value),}
      \AttributeTok{prob\_cat1    =}\NormalTok{ result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{prob\_cat1,}
      \AttributeTok{sim\_response =}\NormalTok{ result}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sim\_response,}
      \AttributeTok{correct      =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(category\_feedback }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ sim\_response)}
\NormalTok{    ) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(agent\_id) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{performance =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(correct) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{row\_number}\NormalTok{()) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Simulating Categorization
Behavior}\label{simulating-categorization-behavior}

Let's simulate behavior on the Kruschke (1993) task. The key parameter
here is \texttt{r\_value}, the observation noise. This parameter
reflects the model's assumption about how much variability exists within
categories or arises from perceptual noise.

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Low \texttt{r\_value}}: The model assumes observations are
  precise representations of the category; each new example exerts
  strong influence and prototypes update rapidly.
\item
  \textbf{High \texttt{r\_value}}: The model assumes observations are
  noisy; learning is slower as each example has less influence, but the
  model generalizes more broadly.
\end{itemize}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{param\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{expand\_grid}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{agent\_id  =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{r\_value   =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{q\_value   =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.001}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{subject\_seed =}\NormalTok{ agent\_id)}

\NormalTok{sim\_file }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{here}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simdata"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"ch13\_prototype\_simulated\_responses.csv"}\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(sim\_file)) \{}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Regenerating prototype simulations...}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  prototype\_responses }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{future\_pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}
    \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{agent\_id     =}\NormalTok{ param\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{agent\_id,}
      \AttributeTok{r\_value      =}\NormalTok{ param\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{r\_value,}
      \AttributeTok{q\_value      =}\NormalTok{ param\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{q\_value,}
      \AttributeTok{subject\_seed =}\NormalTok{ param\_df}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{subject\_seed}
\NormalTok{    ),}
\NormalTok{    simulate\_prototype\_agent,}
    \AttributeTok{stimulus\_info =}\NormalTok{ stimulus\_info,}
    \AttributeTok{n\_blocks      =}\NormalTok{ n\_blocks,}
    \AttributeTok{.options =} \FunctionTok{furrr\_options}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{seed =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
  \FunctionTok{write\_csv}\NormalTok{(prototype\_responses, sim\_file)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Simulations saved.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  prototype\_responses }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{read\_csv}\NormalTok{(sim\_file, }\AttributeTok{show\_col\_types =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{cat}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Simulations loaded.}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(prototype\_responses,}
       \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ trial\_within\_subject, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ performance, }\AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(r\_value\_true))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{stat\_summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fun =}\NormalTok{ mean, }\AttributeTok{geom =} \StringTok{"line"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{stat\_summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fun.data =}\NormalTok{ mean\_se, }\AttributeTok{geom =} \StringTok{"ribbon"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.15}\NormalTok{,}
               \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fill =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(r\_value\_true))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_color\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"plasma"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"r"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_fill\_viridis\_d}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{option =} \StringTok{"plasma"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"r"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"q = "}\NormalTok{, q\_value\_true), }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{title    =} \StringTok{"Prototype Model Learning Performance"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{subtitle =} \StringTok{"Columns: process noise q; colors: observation noise r; mean ± SE across agents"}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cumulative Accuracy"}
\NormalTok{  ) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ylim}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.3}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{12-CategorizationModelsPrototypes_files/figure-pdf/ch13_simulate_prototype_responses-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\textbf{Interpretation}: The panel structure separates the effects of
\(r\) (colors) and \(q\) (columns). Along a row (fixed \(q\)), higher
\(r\) slows learning and lowers asymptotic accuracy. Down a column
(fixed \(r\)), higher \(q\) introduces persistent uncertainty --- the
model cannot converge to a stable prototype, so accuracy may plateau
below ceiling or even oscillate slightly. When \(q = 0\) the model
reduces to the static-target filter from previous drafts of this
chapter. The key pedagogical point: \(q\) and \(r\) are not redundant
--- they affect different phases of the learning curve and, in
non-stationary environments, become separately identifiable.

\subsection{Visualizing Prototype
Evolution}\label{visualizing-prototype-evolution}

Where do the prototypes end up? Let's track their movement and
uncertainty over trials.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Track prototype means and covariance matrices over the course of learning.}
\CommentTok{\# Returns a tibble with one row per (trial × category).}
\NormalTok{track\_prototypes }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(r\_value, obs, cat\_one,}
                             \AttributeTok{q\_value =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
                             \AttributeTok{initial\_mu =} \ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{initial\_sigma\_diag =} \FloatTok{10.0}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  n\_trials  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(obs)}
\NormalTok{  n\_features }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{ncol}\NormalTok{(obs)}

  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(initial\_mu)) \{}
\NormalTok{    mu0\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, n\_features)}
\NormalTok{    mu1\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, n\_features)}
\NormalTok{  \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{    mu0\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ initial\_mu[[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]]}
\NormalTok{    mu1\_init }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ initial\_mu[[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{]]}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{  prototype\_cat\_0 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu =}\NormalTok{ mu0\_init, }\AttributeTok{sigma =} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(initial\_sigma\_diag, n\_features))}
\NormalTok{  prototype\_cat\_1 }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{mu =}\NormalTok{ mu1\_init, }\AttributeTok{sigma =} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(initial\_sigma\_diag, n\_features))}
\NormalTok{  r\_matrix        }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(r\_value, n\_features)}

\NormalTok{  history }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{()}

  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) \{}
\NormalTok{    history[[}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(history) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{trial      =}\NormalTok{ i, }\AttributeTok{category =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{feature1\_mean =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{],}
      \AttributeTok{feature2\_mean =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{],}
      \AttributeTok{cov\_matrix    =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma)}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{    history[[}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(history) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{trial      =}\NormalTok{ i, }\AttributeTok{category =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
      \AttributeTok{feature1\_mean =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{],}
      \AttributeTok{feature2\_mean =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{],}
      \AttributeTok{cov\_matrix    =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma)}
\NormalTok{    )}

    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (i }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}=}\NormalTok{ n\_trials) \{}
      \CommentTok{\# Prediction step}
\NormalTok{      prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(q\_value, n\_features)}
\NormalTok{      prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(q\_value, n\_features)}

\NormalTok{      current\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(obs[i, ])}
\NormalTok{      true\_cat    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ cat\_one[i]}
      \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (true\_cat }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{        upd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{multivariate\_kalman\_update}\NormalTok{(prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu, prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma,}
\NormalTok{                                          current\_obs, r\_matrix)}
\NormalTok{        prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu}
\NormalTok{        prototype\_cat\_1}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma}
\NormalTok{      \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{        upd }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{multivariate\_kalman\_update}\NormalTok{(prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu, prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma,}
\NormalTok{                                          current\_obs, r\_matrix)}
\NormalTok{        prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{mu}
\NormalTok{        prototype\_cat\_0}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ upd}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sigma}
\NormalTok{      \}}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(history)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Ellipse helper for uncertainty visualization}
\NormalTok{get\_ellipse }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(mu, sigma, }\AttributeTok{level =} \FloatTok{0.68}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  mu    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.numeric}\NormalTok{(mu)}
\NormalTok{  sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(sigma)}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(mu) }\SpecialCharTok{!=} \DecValTok{2} \SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{all}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{dim}\NormalTok{(sigma) }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{))) }\FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ev }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{eigen}\NormalTok{(sigma, }\AttributeTok{symmetric =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{only.values =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{values}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{any}\NormalTok{(ev }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}=} \FloatTok{1e{-}6}\NormalTok{)) sigma }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sigma }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{diag}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{ncol}\NormalTok{(sigma)) }\SpecialCharTok{*} \FloatTok{1e{-}6}
\NormalTok{  pts }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tryCatch}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{    ellipse}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{ellipse}\NormalTok{(sigma, }\AttributeTok{centre =}\NormalTok{ mu, }\AttributeTok{level =}\NormalTok{ level),}
    \AttributeTok{error =} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(e) }\ConstantTok{NULL}
\NormalTok{  )}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{is.null}\NormalTok{(pts)) }\FunctionTok{return}\NormalTok{(}\ConstantTok{NULL}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{as\_tibble}\NormalTok{(pts) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{setNames}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"feature1\_mean"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"feature2\_mean"}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Use a fixed seed{-}1 schedule for the trajectory visualization}
\NormalTok{traj\_schedule }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_subject\_schedule}\NormalTok{(stimulus\_info, n\_blocks, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{prototype\_trajectory }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{track\_prototypes}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{r\_value            =} \FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{q\_value            =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{obs                =} \FunctionTok{as.matrix}\NormalTok{(traj\_schedule[, }\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"height"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"position"}\NormalTok{)]),}

  \AttributeTok{initial\_mu         =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{), }\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{2.5}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)),}
  \AttributeTok{initial\_sigma\_diag =} \FloatTok{10.0}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{final\_prototypes    }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ prototype\_trajectory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(trial }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(trial))}

\NormalTok{ellipse\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ final\_prototypes }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{rowwise}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{ell =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{get\_ellipse}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(feature1\_mean, feature2\_mean), cov\_matrix[[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]]))) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{map\_lgl}\NormalTok{(ell, is.null)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{unnest}\NormalTok{(ell)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stimulus\_info,}
             \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ position, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ height, }\AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(category\_true),}
                 \AttributeTok{shape =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(category\_true)),}

  \FunctionTok{geom\_path}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ prototype\_trajectory }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(trial }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}=}\NormalTok{ total\_trials),}
    \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ feature2\_mean, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ feature1\_mean,}
        \AttributeTok{group =}\NormalTok{ category, }\AttributeTok{color =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(category)),}
