\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{13-CategorizationModelsRules_files/figure-pdf/ch14_scenario_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\textbf{Reading the panels.} On static data, \(\mu = 0\) wins: the
filter can converge on the right rule and keep it. Higher \(\mu\) trades
accuracy for adaptability --- with 5\% mutation per trial per particle,
there is always \textasciitilde5\% of the particle cloud freshly drawn
from the prior, which cannot (yet) have high weight. On contingent-shift
data, the curves sort in the opposite direction after the first flip:
\(\mu = 0\) collapses after the reversal and never recovers, while
\(\mu > 0\) produces the characteristic sawtooth recovery. On drift
data, moderate \(\mu\) tracks the drifting boundary better than
\(\mu = 0\).

\subsection{Assumption Match Across
Scenarios}\label{assumption-match-across-scenarios-2}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Scenario
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What ``changes'' in the world
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
What mutation assumes
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Match
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Static & Nothing & Stochastic rule turnover at rate \(\mu\) &
\textbf{Harmless at \(\mu = 0\)}; costly for \(\mu > 0\) \\
Contingent shift & Abrupt label reversals & Stochastic rule turnover &
\textbf{Approximate}: mutation lets the filter recover, but there is no
mechanism for abrupt, synchronised re-initialisation \\
Smooth drift & Gaussian drift of category centres & Stochastic rule
turnover & \textbf{Approximate}: mutation supplies fresh candidates but
is not literally tracking the drift direction \\
\end{longtable}

Unlike the Kalman prototype's \(q\), mutation is not a \emph{model} of
the drift process --- it is a coarse rejuvenation mechanism that keeps
the hypothesis space alive so the filter can react to whatever changes
the environment throws at it. The matching story in Chapter 12 was
``smooth drift fits the model's generative assumption exactly''; here it
is ``mutation is a general-purpose fix that happens to suffice in both
non-stationary scenarios.'' This is the cognitive-theory contrast:
rule-based learners have no commitment to \emph{how} the environment
changes, only that their hypotheses need to stay fresh.

\subsection{How This Strengthens the Model A vs Model B
Gap}\label{how-this-strengthens-the-model-a-vs-model-b-gap}

The Stan mixture (Model B) is built on a fixed candidate rule set
enumerated before inference begins. There is no analogue to \(\mu\) ---
mixture weights can reassign posterior mass across the candidate set,
but nothing in Model B \emph{creates} rules that were not in the
candidate set. On static data this is an acceptable approximation as
long as the true rule is in the candidate set (Ch. 13's §``Discharging
the Post-Hoc Rule Selection'' argues exactly this). On contingent-shift
or drift data, the candidate set that correctly covered the initial
environment will not cover the post-shift environment, and Model B has
no mechanism to repair itself.

Concretely: if we take the contingent-shift simulated data from the plot
above and fit Model B to it, Model B's posterior predictive learning
curve will be stuck at the pre-shift accuracy plateau --- it has no way
to produce the sawtooth. The same fit under NPE (with \(\varepsilon\)
and \(\mu\) both inferred) can match the data because Model A \emph{is}
the generative process. \textbf{The gap between Model A and Model B on
non-stationary data is therefore not a subtle bias --- it is a
structural ceiling.} This is the sharpened version of the argument in
§``The Main Empirical Result'': the cost of replacing an intractable
cognitive theory with a tractable proxy is bounded when the task is
stationary, and unbounded when it is not.

\subsection{Implication for the NPE
Pipeline}\label{implication-for-the-npe-pipeline}

The NPE setup in §``Neural Posterior Estimation for Model A'' infers
\((\varepsilon, N_{\text{particles}})\). Extending to
\((\varepsilon, N_{\text{particles}}, \mu)\) is a one-line change to the
prior and a one-argument change to \texttt{simulate\_model\_a}.
Validation (SBC + expected coverage) and the pair-plot inspection
transfer directly. On static data we would expect \(\mu\) to be poorly
identified --- matching Ch. 12's \(q\) and Ch. 11's \(\lambda\) under
their own static scenarios. On contingent-shift and drift data \(\mu\)
should become identifiable, because the signature in the learning curve
(sawtooth depth, plateau height, post-flip recovery speed) depends
specifically on \(\mu\) in a way the other parameters cannot mimic.
Production implementations of this chapter for the Chapter 15
cross-architecture comparison should include \(\mu\) in the NPE prior
and run one validation sweep per scenario.

\subsection{Trilogy Synthesis}\label{trilogy-synthesis}

Chapters 11, 12, and 13 now each contain a parallel analysis: a
one-parameter extension to the base cognitive model that turns the model
from a stationary learner into a non-stationary one, validated across
three scenarios that load the new parameter differently. The parameters
are superficially different --- a recency weight, a process-noise
variance, a mutation probability --- but the validation story is the
same in each case: identifiability is a joint property of the model and
the task, the non-stationarity parameter is weakly identified when the
task is stationary, and SBC under a stationary task catches this failure
mode while SBC under a matched non-stationary task passes. This is the
shape the reader should recognise when extending any path-dependent
cognitive model: one parameter for non-stationarity, one matched
scenario to identify it, and a validation battery that sorts
assumption-matched, mis-specified, and information-starved cases in the
order the theory predicts.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Cognitive Insights from Rule-Based
Models}\label{cognitive-insights-from-rule-based-models}

Rule-based models, particularly Model A (the particle filter), offer
several cognitive insights:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Explicit Hypothesis Testing}: They model the process of
  generating, testing, and refining explicit hypotheses about category
  structure.
\item
  \textbf{Abrupt Learning}: Performance can change sharply when a highly
  effective rule is discovered and gains high weight --- producing the
  ``aha moment'' learning curves sometimes observed in humans.
\item
  \textbf{Selective Attention}: Attention is modeled as selecting
  specific dimensions relevant to the current best rules, rather than
  continuous weighting across all dimensions.
\item
  \textbf{Verbalizability}: The explicit nature of rules often aligns
  with people's ability and tendency to verbalize their categorization
  strategies.
\item
  \textbf{Individual Differences}: Different individuals might converge
  on different (but potentially equally effective) rules, explaining
  variations in reported strategies.
\item
  \textbf{Working-memory bound}: \(N_{\text{particles}}\) has direct
  cognitive content as a hypothesis-set capacity. NPE makes this an
  inferable parameter rather than a structural choice --- this is a
  non-trivial methodological gain over the Stan proxy, which has no
  analogue.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Strengths and Limitations}\label{strengths-and-limitations}

\textbf{Strengths:}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Cognitive plausibility: aligns with introspection and verbal reports
  of using rules.
\item
  Efficiency for simple structures: very effective when categories are
  defined by simple, logical rules.
\item
  Sharp boundaries: naturally captures categories with clear-cut
  criteria.
\item
  Handles logic: easily incorporates AND, OR, and NOT operators within
  the chosen grammar.
\item
  Working-memory interpretation: \(N_{\text{particles}}\) has direct
  cognitive meaning as a hypothesis capacity bound, and is recoverable
  from data via NPE.
\item
  Honest about its scope: the Model A vs Model B distinction makes it
  possible to fit the cognitive theory directly (via NPE) while still
  having a tractable proxy (Model B) for downstream model comparison.
\item
  Amortized inference: once the NPE network is trained, inference on any
  new subject takes milliseconds, making multi-subject comparisons in
  Chapter 15 computationally trivial.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Limitations:}

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \textbf{Family resemblance categories}: rule-based models struggle
  with categories defined by overall similarity where no single rule
  applies to all members.
\item
  \textbf{Complex boundaries}: requires very complex rules or many
  simple rules to approximate non-axis-aligned or curved boundaries.
\item
  \textbf{Graded typicality}: doesn't easily explain why some category
  members seem more typical than others.
\item
  \textbf{Feature learning}: standard models assume predefined features;
  they don't typically learn which features are relevant from raw input.
\item
  \textbf{Rule grammar is a structural prior} (Phase 0 Q4): the chapter
  commits to \(\{>, <\}\) operators, \(\{\text{AND}, \text{OR}\}\)
  logical connectives, flat rules up to \(\text{max\_dims} = 2\)
  conditions, nested three-condition rules of the form
  \(A\ L_{\text{outer}}\ (B\ L_{\text{inner}}\ C)\), and continuous
  thresholds. Different grammars would give different posteriors.
  Sensitivity to \texttt{max\_dims} is exercised in §``Sensitivity to
  \texttt{max\_dims}''; sensitivity to operators and connectives
  (including deeper nesting) is left as an exercise.
\item
  \textbf{Model B is structurally biased relative to Model A} (Phase 0
  Q3, Q5): the Stan proxy is a different model from the cognitive
  theory. Validation of Model B (LOO, prior sensitivity, real SBC, PSBC)
  tests the proxy. The NPE vs Stan comparison in §``The Main Empirical
  Result'' and the gap demonstration make the bias visible. Chapter 15
  should report both posteriors when comparing rule-based models against
  GCM and prototype.
\item
  \textbf{NPE has its own approximation error}: the NPE posterior is the
  output of a learned normalising flow, which is an approximation to the
  true posterior. We validate it via SBC + expected coverage (§``NPE
  Validation''); if both pass, the approximation error is small enough
  to trust. Unlike rejection ABC, NPE does not depend on hand-crafted
  summary statistics --- the embedding network learns the relevant
  features end-to-end --- and it does not depend on a tolerance
  parameter.
\item
  \textbf{Cross-language step}: NPE requires Python (\texttt{sbi} +
  \texttt{torch}) accessed via \texttt{reticulate}. This adds a
  dependency but is well-documented and the interface is stable. The
  alternative --- reimplementing normalising flows in R --- is not
  advisable.
\item
  \textbf{No multilevel extension yet}: this chapter, like Chapter 12,
  stops at single-subject inference. The multilevel rule model (with
  population-level priors on \(\varepsilon\) and per-subject offsets) is
  straightforward to add to Model B but non-trivial for the NPE
  pipeline; Chapter 15 takes up the multilevel comparison across all
  three model classes. For NPE, the standard approach is to train the
  network at the single-subject level and then build the hierarchical
  model on top of the individual posteriors (Radev et al.~2023,
  §``Hierarchical models'').
\end{itemize}

\section{Conclusion: The Role of Rules and the Cost of
Tractability}\label{conclusion-the-role-of-rules-and-the-cost-of-tractability}

Rule-based models offer a distinct perspective on categorization,
emphasizing explicit criteria and logical operations rather than overall
similarity. The Bayesian particle filter (Model A) provides a
sophisticated framework for modeling how learners might search the space
of possible rules, maintain uncertainty, and update beliefs based on
evidence. Its likelihood is intractable in Stan, which is why the
chapter introduced two responses to that intractability:

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  \textbf{The tractable proxy (Model B)}: a marginalised mixture over a
  fixed candidate rule set, fitted with HMC, validated via the standard
  six-phase pipeline (including Posterior SBC for local calibration).
  Useful for downstream model comparison, \emph{biased} relative to the
  cognitive theory in a direction the chapter quantifies via the
  NPE-vs-Stan posterior gap.
\item
  \textbf{Simulation-based inference (NPE for Model A)}: Neural
  Posterior Estimation with a learned embedding network, validated via
  SBC + expected coverage, fitting the actual cognitive theory directly.
  The NPE approach resolves the three problems of earlier ABC drafts
  (schedule mismatch, summary insufficiency, no amortization) and makes
  \(N_{\text{particles}}\) a jointly inferred cognitive parameter rather
  than a structural commitment.
\end{enumerate}

The chapter's pedagogical move is to fit \emph{both}. The gap between
the two posteriors is the structural bias the modeller accepts when they
replace the cognitive theory with a proxy. The gap demonstration
experiment (§``Demonstrating That the Gap Tracks Rule-Set Mismatch'')
shows this gap tracks rule-set mismatch rather than inference noise. On
data where the proxy's candidate rules happen to cover what Model A
converges to, the gap is small and Model B's LOO scores can be trusted
as cognitive evidence. On data where they do not, the gap is large and
Model B's LOO scores are \emph{structural artefacts} --- they tell you
about the proxy's fit, not about the agent.

Pure rule-based models may not account for all aspects of human
categorization (especially for natural categories with fuzzy
boundaries), but they capture an important element of human cognition,
particularly in domains involving definitions, instruction, or
well-defined criteria. Often, human behavior might reflect a blend or
interplay between rule-based and similarity-based processing --- a topic
explored in hybrid models (like COVIS or RULEX) and addressed directly
in the \textbf{next chapter}, which compares all three model classes on
the same dataset. Chapter 15 reports the comparison \textbf{twice}:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Once under the LOO/LFO-CV framework, with each model fitted under its
  appropriate validation tool (LFO-CV for the GCM and Kalman prototype
  via the implementations in Chapters 11 and 12; standard LOO for Model
  B). This is the comparison the proxies make possible.
\item
  Once under NPE, with Model A (the particle filter), the GCM, and the
  Kalman prototype all fitted as simulator-based models on a common
  footing --- each with its own trained inference network, validated
  with SBC + expected coverage, and compared via posterior predictive
  ELPD. This is the comparison the cognitive theories deserve.
\end{itemize}

The expected outcome is that the two comparisons agree on the easy cases
and disagree on the hard ones, and that the disagreement is informative
about which empirical conclusions in the categorisation literature have
been over-claimed because they were based on tractable proxies of
intractable cognitive theories. That is the methodological payoff of
taking simulator-based inference seriously.

\cleardoublepage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{part}{Appendices}
\appendix

\chapter{Sequential Extensions of the Bayesian
Agent}\label{sequential-extensions-of-the-bayesian-agent}

Every Bayesian agent we built in Ch. 10 --- SBA, WBA, PBA, and their
multilevel cousins --- shares two strong assumptions. First, the agent
is \emph{forced} to guess on every trial: there is no option to defer,
ask for more evidence, or decline. Second, the weights on direct and
social evidence are \emph{static}: whatever trade-off an agent starts
with, they carry it through the entire experiment unchanged. Both
assumptions are reasonable first-pass idealisations, and both are almost
certainly false about real cognition.

This appendix sketches two extensions that relax each assumption in
turn. Section A introduces a \textbf{sample-or-guess agent} that decides
at every step whether to collect more evidence or commit to a choice.
Section B introduces an \textbf{adaptive-weight agent} whose weights
drift across trials in response to how well each source predicted recent
feedback.

\begin{quote}
\textbf{Scope note.} These two models are offered as \emph{starting
points for student projects}, not as fully validated contributions. We
give the mathematical formalization, an R forward simulation,
visualisations of the agents' behaviour, and a single-agent Stan model
fit once for illustration. We deliberately omit parameter recovery, SBC,
model comparison, and multilevel extensions --- all of these are natural
next steps and good exercises. Treat what follows as a skeleton to build
on.
\end{quote}

\section{Section A: Sample-or-Guess
Agent}\label{section-a-sample-or-guess-agent}

\subsection{Conceptual motivation}\label{conceptual-motivation}

The agents in Ch. 10 are committed decision-makers: evidence arrives,
they integrate it, and they respond. In many real tasks --- clinical
diagnosis, foraging, perceptual decision-making under time pressure ---
the agent instead chooses \emph{when to stop sampling}. A cautious agent
keeps observing marbles until confident; an impulsive one guesses after
the first draw. The trade-off is classical: more samples mean better
accuracy but higher cost (time, effort, missed opportunities). This
family of policies connects the Bayesian-cognition literature to
sequential-sampling models of reaction time (e.g., the drift-diffusion
model) and to metacognition, where confidence gates commitment.

The key conceptual move is that the observation model now has \emph{two}
outputs per trial. On every step the agent emits a stop/continue
decision; when it finally stops, it emits a guess. The likelihood must
account for both --- a point that is easy to miss when porting the Ch.
10 Stan templates.

\subsection{Mathematical
formalization}\label{mathematical-formalization}

Let trial \(i\) consist of a sequence of evidence draws. The agent
enters the trial with a prior (we use Jeffreys:
\(\alpha_0 = \beta_0 = 0.5\)) and an optional social tip summarised as
\((k_s, n_s)\). After observing direct evidence up to step \(t\) ---
\(k_{d,t}\) blue out of \(t\) direct draws --- the posterior over the
jar's blue proportion \(\theta\) is

\[\theta \mid \text{evidence}_t \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_t,\, \beta_t),\]

with

\[\alpha_t = 0.5 + k_s + k_{d,t}, \qquad
  \beta_t  = 0.5 + (n_s - k_s) + (t - k_{d,t}).\]

Define the agent's \textbf{certainty} at step \(t\) as

\[c_t \;=\; \left|\, \mathbb{E}[\theta \mid \text{evidence}_t] - 0.5 \,\right|
       \;=\; \left|\, \frac{\alpha_t}{\alpha_t + \beta_t} - 0.5 \,\right|.\]

The agent's policy is governed by a single free parameter \(\tau \in
(0, 0.5)\):

\[
\text{at step } t: \quad
\begin{cases}
  \text{stop, guess } g_i = \mathbf{1}[\alpha_t > \beta_t] & \text{if } c_t \ge \tau, \\
  \text{draw one more marble, increment } t & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]

The data observed per trial are the stopping time \(T_i\) and the final
guess \(g_i\). The likelihood factorises as

\[p(T_i, g_i \mid \tau, \text{evidence}) \;=\;
  \underbrace{\prod_{t=1}^{T_i - 1} \Pr(\text{continue at } t \mid \tau)}_{\text{didn't stop earlier}}
  \;\cdot\;
  \underbrace{\Pr(\text{stop at } T_i \mid \tau)}_{\text{stops now}}
  \;\cdot\;
  \underbrace{\Pr(g_i \mid \alpha_{T_i}, \beta_{T_i})}_{\text{final guess}}.\]

Two important caveats. \textbf{First}, after the agent stops, the
would-be marbles at steps \(T_i + 1, T_i + 2, \ldots\) are \emph{not}
observed. Trials are not i.i.d. sequences of a fixed length; the data
are censored by the agent's own decision. \textbf{Second}, the
hard-threshold rule above is non-differentiable in \(\tau\), which
breaks HMC. For inference we therefore replace it with a soft version,

\[\Pr(\text{stop at } t \mid \tau) \;=\; \sigma\!\left(\frac{c_t - \tau}{\sigma_\tau}\right),\]

where \(\sigma(\cdot)\) is the logistic function and \(\sigma_\tau\) is
a small fixed slope (we use \(\sigma_\tau = 0.02\)). As
\(\sigma_\tau \to 0\) the soft rule recovers the hard one.

\subsection{R forward simulation}\label{r-forward-simulation}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Simulate one agent with a hard certainty threshold.}
\CommentTok{\# Returns a tibble with one row per trial.}
\NormalTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(tau,}
\NormalTok{                                     n\_trials,}
                                     \AttributeTok{max\_samples =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{,}
                                     \AttributeTok{true\_theta =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{,}
                                     \AttributeTok{social\_k =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
                                     \AttributeTok{social\_n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{,}
                                     \AttributeTok{alpha0 =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{beta0 =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(trial) \{}
\NormalTok{    alpha\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha0 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ social\_k}
\NormalTok{    beta\_t  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta0  }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (social\_n }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ social\_k)}
\NormalTok{    stopped }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
\NormalTok{    t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{0}
    \ControlFlowTok{while}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{stopped }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ t }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ max\_samples) \{}
\NormalTok{      c\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_t) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
      \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (c\_t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\NormalTok{ tau }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{        stopped }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{TRUE}
\NormalTok{      \} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{        t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ t }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{        draw }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, true\_theta)  }\CommentTok{\# 1 = blue, 0 = red}
\NormalTok{        alpha\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ draw}
\NormalTok{        beta\_t  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ draw)}
\NormalTok{      \}}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{    guess }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ beta\_t)}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
      \AttributeTok{trial   =}\NormalTok{ trial,}
      \AttributeTok{tau     =}\NormalTok{ tau,}
      \AttributeTok{T       =}\NormalTok{ t,}
      \AttributeTok{guess   =}\NormalTok{ guess,}
      \AttributeTok{correct =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(guess }\SpecialCharTok{==} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(true\_theta }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{    )}
\NormalTok{  \})}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Three agents with different thresholds}
\NormalTok{sim\_threshold }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau =} \FloatTok{0.10}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau =} \FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau =} \FloatTok{0.40}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{200}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau\_label =} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{paste0}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"tau = "}\NormalTok{, tau)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Visualisations}\label{visualisations}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# (a) Stopping{-}time distributions per tau}
\NormalTok{p\_stop }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_threshold }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(T, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ tau\_label)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{binwidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.8}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{tau\_label, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Stopping time (\# direct draws)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Count"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"(a) Stopping{-}time distributions"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{theme}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{legend.position =} \StringTok{"none"}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# (b) Accuracy vs. tau (sweep on a denser grid)}
\NormalTok{tau\_sweep }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{seq}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.45}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{by =} \FloatTok{0.05}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{acc\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(tau\_sweep, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(tt) \{}
\NormalTok{  s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau =}\NormalTok{ tt, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{400}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau =}\NormalTok{ tt,}
         \AttributeTok{accuracy    =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(s}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{correct),}
         \AttributeTok{mean\_T      =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(s}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T))}
\NormalTok{\})}

\NormalTok{p\_acc }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ acc\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(tau, accuracy)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =}\NormalTok{ mean\_T)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{scale\_size\_continuous}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{name =} \StringTok{"Mean}\SpecialCharTok{\textbackslash{}n}\StringTok{samples"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(tau), }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Accuracy"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"(b) Accuracy vs. threshold"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{p\_stop }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ p\_acc}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_threshold_viz-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# (c) Trial{-}trace: certainty evolving toward the threshold}
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{42}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{trace\_tau }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.25}
\NormalTok{alpha\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{beta\_t  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{3} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{trace }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{t       =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{alpha\_t =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_t,}
  \AttributeTok{beta\_t  =}\NormalTok{ beta\_t,}
  \AttributeTok{c\_t     =} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_t) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{20}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  draw }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  alpha\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ draw}
\NormalTok{  beta\_t  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ draw)}
\NormalTok{  trace }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(trace, }\FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{t =}\NormalTok{ t,}
    \AttributeTok{alpha\_t =}\NormalTok{ alpha\_t, }\AttributeTok{beta\_t =}\NormalTok{ beta\_t,}
    \AttributeTok{c\_t =} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_t) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  ))}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (}\FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_t) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\NormalTok{ trace\_tau) }\ControlFlowTok{break}
\NormalTok{\}}
\NormalTok{stop\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(trace}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{t)}

\NormalTok{trace }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(t, c\_t)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_hline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{yintercept =}\NormalTok{ trace\_tau, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"text"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ trace\_tau }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FloatTok{0.015}\NormalTok{,}
           \AttributeTok{label =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(tau), }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{hjust =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{annotate}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"point"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ stop\_t, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ trace}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{c\_t[trace}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{t }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ stop\_t],}
           \AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"firebrick"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Direct draw step t"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \FunctionTok{expression}\NormalTok{(c[t]),}
       \AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"(c) One trial: certainty crossing the threshold"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_threshold_trace-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The patterns line up with intuition: a low \(\tau\) produces short,
noisy, error-prone trials; a high \(\tau\) produces longer, more
accurate trials with diminishing returns.

\subsection{Single-agent Stan model (soft
threshold)}\label{single-agent-stan-model-soft-threshold}

The hard rule above is unsuitable for HMC because it is
non-differentiable in \(\tau\). We replace it with a logistic soft rule.
The likelihood then becomes a product of Bernoulli stop/continue
probabilities plus a Bernoulli for the final guess. Because the
direct-evidence sequence itself is a latent variable conditional only on
\(T_i\) and the final \((\alpha, \beta)\), we pass the raw per-step
draws (reconstructed from the simulation) to Stan as data. In a real
analysis the modeller would either record these draws in the experiment
or marginalise over them.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Use the tau = 0.25 agent for fitting. Re{-}run the simulation but also}
\CommentTok{\# record the sequence of draws for each trial.}
\NormalTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent\_full }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(tau, n\_trials, }\AttributeTok{max\_samples =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{,}
                                          \AttributeTok{true\_theta =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{,}
                                          \AttributeTok{social\_k =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{social\_n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  draws\_list }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{vector}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"list"}\NormalTok{, n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{  T\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{integer}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)}
\NormalTok{  g\_vec }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{integer}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials)) \{}
\NormalTok{    alpha\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ social\_k}
\NormalTok{    beta\_t  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FloatTok{0.5} \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (social\_n }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ social\_k)}
\NormalTok{    draws\_i }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{integer}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{    t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{    stopped }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{FALSE}
    \ControlFlowTok{while}\NormalTok{ (}\SpecialCharTok{!}\NormalTok{stopped }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ t }\SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{ max\_samples) \{}
\NormalTok{      c\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_t) }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)}
      \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (c\_t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=}\NormalTok{ tau }\SpecialCharTok{\&\&}\NormalTok{ t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}=} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) \{ stopped }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{; }\ControlFlowTok{break}\NormalTok{ \}}
\NormalTok{      t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ t }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}
\NormalTok{      d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, true\_theta)}
\NormalTok{      draws\_i }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(draws\_i, d)}
\NormalTok{      alpha\_t }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ d}
\NormalTok{      beta\_t  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta\_t }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (}\DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ d)}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{    T\_vec[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ t}
\NormalTok{    g\_vec[i] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(alpha\_t }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\NormalTok{ beta\_t)}
\NormalTok{    draws\_list[[i]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ draws\_i}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{T =}\NormalTok{ T\_vec, }\AttributeTok{guess =}\NormalTok{ g\_vec, }\AttributeTok{draws =}\NormalTok{ draws\_list)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{sim\_full }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_threshold\_agent\_full}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{tau =} \FloatTok{0.25}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Flatten per{-}trial draws into a padded matrix (max T across trials)}
\NormalTok{T\_max\_obs }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T)}
\NormalTok{draws\_mat }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{matrix}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{L, }\AttributeTok{nrow =} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T), }\AttributeTok{ncol =}\NormalTok{ T\_max\_obs)}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_along}\NormalTok{(sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{draws)) \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T[i] }\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{    draws\_mat[i, }\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T[i]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{draws[[i]]}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{stan\_data\_A }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N        =} \FunctionTok{length}\NormalTok{(sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T),}
  \AttributeTok{T\_max    =}\NormalTok{ T\_max\_obs,}
  \AttributeTok{T        =}\NormalTok{ sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{T,}
  \AttributeTok{guess    =}\NormalTok{ sim\_full}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{guess,}
  \AttributeTok{draws    =}\NormalTok{ draws\_mat,}
  \AttributeTok{social\_k =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{social\_n =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{sigma\_tau =} \FloatTok{0.02}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{ThresholdAgent\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Sample{-}or{-}Guess Agent with a soft certainty threshold.}
\StringTok{// Single parameter: tau in (0, 0.5), the certainty threshold.}
\StringTok{// The stop/continue decision is modelled with a logistic soft rule}
\StringTok{// around tau with fixed slope sigma\_tau.}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} T\_max;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=T\_max\textgreater{} T;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} guess;}
\StringTok{  array[N, T\_max] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} draws;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} social\_k;}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} social\_n;}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} sigma\_tau;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  // tau \textasciitilde{} scaled Beta(2,2) on (0, 0.5)}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=0.5\textgreater{} tau;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  // Prior: Beta(2,2) on (0, 0.5) via change of variables {-}\textgreater{} tau * 2 \textasciitilde{} Beta(2,2)}
\StringTok{  target += beta\_lpdf(2 * tau | 2, 2) + log(2);}

\StringTok{  for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{    real alpha\_t = 0.5 + social\_k;}
\StringTok{    real beta\_t  = 0.5 + (social\_n {-} social\_k);}

\StringTok{    // Steps 1 .. T[i] {-} 1: the agent continued (soft prob of NOT stopping)}
\StringTok{    // At step T[i]: the agent stopped (soft prob of stopping)}
\StringTok{    // If T[i] == 0 (agent would stop before seeing any direct draw) we}
\StringTok{    // only contribute the final guess.}
\StringTok{    for (t in 1:T[i]) \{}
\StringTok{      int d = draws[i, t];}
\StringTok{      alpha\_t += d;}
\StringTok{      beta\_t  += (1 {-} d);}
\StringTok{      real c\_t = abs(alpha\_t / (alpha\_t + beta\_t) {-} 0.5);}
\StringTok{      real p\_stop = inv\_logit((c\_t {-} tau) / sigma\_tau);}
\StringTok{      if (t \textless{} T[i]) \{}
\StringTok{        target += bernoulli\_lpmf(0 | p\_stop);  // continued}
\StringTok{      \} else \{}
\StringTok{        target += bernoulli\_lpmf(1 | p\_stop);  // stopped}
\StringTok{      \}}
\StringTok{    \}}

\StringTok{    // Final guess given the posterior at stopping time}
\StringTok{    \{}
\StringTok{      real alpha\_final = 0.5 + social\_k;}
\StringTok{      real beta\_final  = 0.5 + (social\_n {-} social\_k);}
\StringTok{      for (t in 1:T[i]) \{}
\StringTok{        alpha\_final += draws[i, t];}
\StringTok{        beta\_final  += (1 {-} draws[i, t]);}
\StringTok{      \}}
\StringTok{      real p\_blue = alpha\_final / (alpha\_final + beta\_final);}
\StringTok{      target += bernoulli\_lpmf(guess[i] | p\_blue);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(ThresholdAgent\_stan,}
                \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{,}
                \AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch11\_threshold\_agent.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch11_threshold_agent.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod\_A }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan/ch11\_threshold\_agent.stan"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch11\_fit\_A.rds"}\NormalTok{)) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_A }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_A}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data\_A,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_A}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch11\_fit\_A.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_A }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch11\_fit\_A.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_threshold_diagnostics-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The posterior should concentrate near the simulated \(\tau = 0.25\) ---
but with meaningful width, because the soft rule trades off slope
(\(\sigma_\tau\)) against threshold location. The hard-rule
identifiability limit is an interesting project question.

\section{Section B: Adaptive-Weight
Agent}\label{section-b-adaptive-weight-agent}

\subsection{Conceptual motivation}\label{conceptual-motivation-1}

The WBA of Ch. 10 commits at \(t = 0\) to fixed weights \(w_d\) and
\(w_s\) on direct and social evidence, and carries them through the
entire experiment. But a sensible agent should update those weights: if
social tips keep steering them wrong, they should trust the social
source \emph{less}; if direct observations keep being misleading (a
noisy sensor, say), they should trust themselves less. This turns the
static WBA into a \emph{learning} model, and makes contact with the
reinforcement-learning framing of Ch. 3 --- except that the thing being
learned is not a value, but a meta-parameter governing how two
information streams are combined. The resulting agent is Bayesian
\emph{within} a trial (it still integrates evidence via the
Beta-Binomial posterior) but RL-like \emph{across} trials (it updates
weights from prediction errors).

This extension also provides a mechanistic story for why real agents
might look like different static WBAs at different points in an
experiment: they are one agent whose weights have drifted.

\subsection{Mathematical
formalization}\label{mathematical-formalization-1}

On each trial \(t\), the two sources make independent predictions of the
correct colour:

\[p_{d,t} = \frac{\alpha_{d,t}}{\alpha_{d,t} + \beta_{d,t}},
\qquad
p_{s,t} = \frac{\alpha_{s,t}}{\alpha_{s,t} + \beta_{s,t}},\]

where \((\alpha_{d,t}, \beta_{d,t})\) is the posterior from \emph{only}
the direct evidence on trial \(t\), and \((\alpha_{s,t}, \beta_{s,t})\)
is the posterior from \emph{only} the social evidence. The agent's
combined posterior (and its guess) uses the WBA combination with
\emph{current} weights \(w_{d,t}, w_{s,t}\):

\[\alpha_t = 0.5 + w_{d,t} k_{d,t} + w_{s,t} k_{s,t}, \qquad
  \beta_t  = 0.5 + w_{d,t} (n_{d,t} - k_{d,t}) + w_{s,t} (n_{s,t} - k_{s,t}).\]

After feedback \(y_t \in \{0, 1\}\) on the correct colour, each source's
per-trial prediction error is

\[\delta_{d,t} = y_t - p_{d,t}, \qquad \delta_{s,t} = y_t - p_{s,t}.\]

The weights update on the log scale (to keep them positive) by a delta
rule that rewards the source that predicted better:

\[\log w_{d,t+1} = \log w_{d,t} + \eta\,(|\delta_{s,t}| - |\delta_{d,t}|),\]
\[\log w_{s,t+1} = \log w_{s,t} + \eta\,(|\delta_{d,t}| - |\delta_{s,t}|).\]

Parameters: learning rate \(\eta \in (0, 1)\) and initial log-weights
\(\log w_{d,0}, \log w_{s,0}\).

\subsection{R forward simulation}\label{r-forward-simulation-1}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Generate a stream of trials with specified direct/social reliability.}
\CommentTok{\# direct\_reliability / social\_reliability: probability that the source}
\CommentTok{\# points to the correct colour on any given trial.}
\NormalTok{make\_trial\_stream }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(n\_trials, direct\_reliability,}
\NormalTok{                              social\_reliability, }\AttributeTok{n\_d =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{n\_s =} \DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(n\_trials), }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) \{}
\NormalTok{    correct }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)  }\CommentTok{\# truth randomly blue/red}
    \CommentTok{\# Each direct draw agrees with the truth with prob direct\_reliability}
\NormalTok{    k\_d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, n\_d, }\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (correct }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) direct\_reliability}
                          \ControlFlowTok{else} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ direct\_reliability)}
\NormalTok{    k\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, n\_s, }\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (correct }\SpecialCharTok{==} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) social\_reliability}
                          \ControlFlowTok{else} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ social\_reliability)}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{trial =}\NormalTok{ i, }\AttributeTok{correct =}\NormalTok{ correct,}
           \AttributeTok{k\_d =}\NormalTok{ k\_d, }\AttributeTok{n\_d =}\NormalTok{ n\_d, }\AttributeTok{k\_s =}\NormalTok{ k\_s, }\AttributeTok{n\_s =}\NormalTok{ n\_s)}
\NormalTok{  \})}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{simulate\_adaptive\_agent }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(eta, w\_d0, w\_s0, trial\_stream,}
                                    \AttributeTok{alpha0 =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{beta0 =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) \{}
\NormalTok{  w\_d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ w\_d0}
\NormalTok{  w\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ w\_s0}
\NormalTok{  out }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{vector}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"list"}\NormalTok{, }\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(trial\_stream))}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \FunctionTok{seq\_len}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(trial\_stream))) \{}
\NormalTok{    tr }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ trial\_stream[i, ]}
    \CommentTok{\# Source{-}specific posteriors}
\NormalTok{    alpha\_d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha0 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_d}
\NormalTok{    beta\_d  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta0  }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_d }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_d)}
\NormalTok{    alpha\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha0 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_s}
\NormalTok{    beta\_s  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta0  }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ (tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_s }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_s)}
\NormalTok{    p\_d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_d }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_d }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_d)}
\NormalTok{    p\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_s }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_s }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_s)}

    \CommentTok{\# Combined WBA posterior with current weights}
\NormalTok{    alpha\_c }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha0 }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_d }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_d }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_s }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_s}
\NormalTok{    beta\_c  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ beta0  }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_d }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_d }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_d) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ w\_s }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_s }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_s)}
\NormalTok{    p\_c     }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ alpha\_c }\SpecialCharTok{/}\NormalTok{ (alpha\_c }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ beta\_c)}
\NormalTok{    guess   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{rbinom}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, p\_c)}

    \CommentTok{\# Delta rule on log{-}weights}
\NormalTok{    y }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{correct}
\NormalTok{    d\_d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_d)}
\NormalTok{    d\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{abs}\NormalTok{(y }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ p\_s)}
\NormalTok{    w\_d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(w\_d) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ eta }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (d\_s }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_d))}
\NormalTok{    w\_s }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{exp}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{log}\NormalTok{(w\_s) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ eta }\SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ (d\_d }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ d\_s))}

\NormalTok{    out[[i]] }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{trial =}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{trial, }\AttributeTok{correct =}\NormalTok{ y, }\AttributeTok{guess =}\NormalTok{ guess,}
                       \AttributeTok{p\_c =}\NormalTok{ p\_c, }\AttributeTok{w\_d =}\NormalTok{ w\_d, }\AttributeTok{w\_s =}\NormalTok{ w\_s,}
                       \AttributeTok{k\_d =}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_d, }\AttributeTok{n\_d =}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_d,}
                       \AttributeTok{k\_s =}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_s, }\AttributeTok{n\_s =}\NormalTok{ tr}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_s)}
\NormalTok{  \}}
  \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(out)}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{\# Three regimes}
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{7}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{regimes }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{regime =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"direct more reliable"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"social more reliable"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"equally reliable"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{rel\_d  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.85}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.55}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{rel\_s  =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.55}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.85}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.75}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{sim\_adapt }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(regimes, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(regime, rel\_d, rel\_s) \{}
\NormalTok{  stream }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_trial\_stream}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
                              \AttributeTok{direct\_reliability =}\NormalTok{ rel\_d,}
                              \AttributeTok{social\_reliability =}\NormalTok{ rel\_s)}
  \FunctionTok{simulate\_adaptive\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{eta =} \FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{w\_d0 =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{w\_s0 =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{trial\_stream =}\NormalTok{ stream) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{regime =}\NormalTok{ regime)}
\NormalTok{\})}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Compare adaptive agent vs. static WBA (w\_d = w\_s = 1) on accuracy}
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{8}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{compare\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(regimes, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(regime, rel\_d, rel\_s) \{}
\NormalTok{  stream }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_trial\_stream}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{n\_trials =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{,}
                              \AttributeTok{direct\_reliability =}\NormalTok{ rel\_d,}
                              \AttributeTok{social\_reliability =}\NormalTok{ rel\_s)}
\NormalTok{  adaptive }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_adaptive\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, stream) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent =} \StringTok{"adaptive"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  static }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_adaptive\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, stream) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
    \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent =} \StringTok{"static WBA (eta = 0)"}\NormalTok{)}
  \FunctionTok{bind\_rows}\NormalTok{(adaptive, static) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}} \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{regime =}\NormalTok{ regime)}
\NormalTok{\})}

\NormalTok{compare\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{correct\_guess =} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(guess }\SpecialCharTok{==}\NormalTok{ correct)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(regime, agent, trial) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{summarise}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{acc =} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(correct\_guess), }\AttributeTok{.groups =} \StringTok{"drop"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(regime, agent) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{running\_acc =} \FunctionTok{cumsum}\NormalTok{(acc) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{seq\_along}\NormalTok{(acc)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(trial, running\_acc, }\AttributeTok{colour =}\NormalTok{ agent)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_line}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{size =} \FloatTok{0.9}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{regime) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"(b) Running accuracy: adaptive vs. static WBA"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Cumulative accuracy"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Trial"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_adaptive_viz_b-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Final{-}weight distribution across 50 simulated agents per regime}
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{final\_df }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{pmap\_dfr}\NormalTok{(regimes, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(regime, rel\_d, rel\_s) \{}
\NormalTok{  purrr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{map\_dfr}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{50}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(a) \{}
\NormalTok{    stream }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_trial\_stream}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, rel\_d, rel\_s)}
\NormalTok{    res }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_adaptive\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, stream)}
    \FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{agent\_id =}\NormalTok{ a, }\AttributeTok{regime =}\NormalTok{ regime,}
           \AttributeTok{w\_d\_final =} \FunctionTok{tail}\NormalTok{(res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{w\_d, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
           \AttributeTok{w\_s\_final =} \FunctionTok{tail}\NormalTok{(res}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{w\_s, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{))}
\NormalTok{  \})}
\NormalTok{\})}

\NormalTok{final\_df }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{pivot\_longer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(w\_d\_final, w\_s\_final), }\AttributeTok{names\_to =} \StringTok{"weight"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{values\_to =} \StringTok{"value"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(value, }\AttributeTok{fill =}\NormalTok{ weight)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{25}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{alpha =} \FloatTok{0.7}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{position =} \StringTok{"identity"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"white"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{facet\_wrap}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{regime) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{title =} \StringTok{"(c) Final{-}weight distribution (50 agents per regime)"}\NormalTok{,}
       \AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Final weight"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Count"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_adaptive_viz_c-1.pdf}
\end{center}

Across 50 simulated agents the weights track the true reliability
asymmetry: when direct evidence is more reliable, \(w_d\) grows and
\(w_s\) shrinks; the pattern reverses when social evidence is more
reliable; and when the two are matched, weights drift modestly in either
direction without systematic asymmetry.

\subsection{Single-agent Stan model}\label{single-agent-stan-model}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Fit to one direct{-}more{-}reliable agent}
\FunctionTok{set.seed}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{11}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{stream\_fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_trial\_stream}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{direct\_reliability =} \FloatTok{0.85}\NormalTok{,}
                                 \AttributeTok{social\_reliability =} \FloatTok{0.55}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{sim\_fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{simulate\_adaptive\_agent}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, stream\_fit)}

\NormalTok{stan\_data\_B }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N       =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(sim\_fit),}
  \AttributeTok{guess   =}\NormalTok{ sim\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{guess,}
  \AttributeTok{correct =}\NormalTok{ sim\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{correct,}
  \AttributeTok{k\_d     =}\NormalTok{ sim\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_d,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_d     =}\NormalTok{ sim\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_d,}
  \AttributeTok{k\_s     =}\NormalTok{ sim\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{k\_s,}
  \AttributeTok{n\_s     =}\NormalTok{ sim\_fit}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{n\_s}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{AdaptiveAgent\_stan }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \StringTok{"}
\StringTok{// Adaptive{-}Weight Agent.}
\StringTok{// Parameters: eta (learning rate), log w\_d0, log w\_s0 (initial log{-}weights).}
\StringTok{// Weights are updated across trials by a delta rule on log{-}weights using}
\StringTok{// per{-}source absolute prediction errors against the feedback.}

\StringTok{data \{}
\StringTok{  int\textless{}lower=1\textgreater{} N;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} guess;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} correct;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} k\_d;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} n\_d;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} k\_s;}
\StringTok{  array[N] int\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{} n\_s;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{parameters \{}
\StringTok{  real\textless{}lower=0, upper=1\textgreater{} eta;}
\StringTok{  real log\_w\_d0;}
\StringTok{  real log\_w\_s0;}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{transformed parameters \{}
\StringTok{  vector[N] p\_c;}
\StringTok{  \{}
\StringTok{    real log\_w\_d = log\_w\_d0;}
\StringTok{    real log\_w\_s = log\_w\_s0;}
\StringTok{    for (i in 1:N) \{}
\StringTok{      real w\_d = exp(log\_w\_d);}
\StringTok{      real w\_s = exp(log\_w\_s);}

\StringTok{      real alpha\_d = 0.5 + k\_d[i];}
\StringTok{      real beta\_d  = 0.5 + (n\_d[i] {-} k\_d[i]);}
\StringTok{      real alpha\_s = 0.5 + k\_s[i];}
\StringTok{      real beta\_s  = 0.5 + (n\_s[i] {-} k\_s[i]);}
\StringTok{      real p\_d = alpha\_d / (alpha\_d + beta\_d);}
\StringTok{      real p\_s = alpha\_s / (alpha\_s + beta\_s);}

\StringTok{      real alpha\_c = 0.5 + w\_d * k\_d[i] + w\_s * k\_s[i];}
\StringTok{      real beta\_c  = 0.5 + w\_d * (n\_d[i] {-} k\_d[i]) + w\_s * (n\_s[i] {-} k\_s[i]);}
\StringTok{      p\_c[i] = alpha\_c / (alpha\_c + beta\_c);}

\StringTok{      real d\_d = abs(correct[i] {-} p\_d);}
\StringTok{      real d\_s = abs(correct[i] {-} p\_s);}
\StringTok{      log\_w\_d += eta * (d\_s {-} d\_d);}
\StringTok{      log\_w\_s += eta * (d\_d {-} d\_s);}
\StringTok{    \}}
\StringTok{  \}}
\StringTok{\}}

\StringTok{model \{}
\StringTok{  target += beta\_lpdf(eta | 2, 5);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(log\_w\_d0 | 0, 1);}
\StringTok{  target += normal\_lpdf(log\_w\_s0 | 0, 1);}

\StringTok{  target += bernoulli\_lpmf(guess | p\_c);}
\StringTok{\}}
\StringTok{"}

\FunctionTok{write\_stan\_file}\NormalTok{(AdaptiveAgent\_stan,}
                \AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"stan/"}\NormalTok{,}
                \AttributeTok{basename =} \StringTok{"ch11\_adaptive\_agent.stan"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
[1] "/Users/au209589/Dropbox/Teaching/AdvancedCognitiveModeling23_book/stan/ch11_adaptive_agent.stan"
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod\_B }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"stan/ch11\_adaptive\_agent.stan"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{dir =} \StringTok{"simmodels"}\NormalTok{)}

\ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (regenerate\_simulations }\SpecialCharTok{||} \SpecialCharTok{!}\FunctionTok{file.exists}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch11\_fit\_B.rds"}\NormalTok{)) \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_B }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_B}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data\_B,}
    \AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{321}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
    \AttributeTok{refresh =} \DecValTok{0}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{  fit\_B}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{save\_object}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch11\_fit\_B.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\} }\ControlFlowTok{else}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  fit\_B }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{readRDS}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"simmodels/ch11\_fit\_B.rds"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit\_B}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"eta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_w\_d0"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_w\_s0"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{verbatim}
# A tibble: 3 x 10
  variable   mean median    sd    mad      q5   q95  rhat ess_bulk ess_tail
  <chr>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 eta       0.227  0.213 0.104 0.0989  0.0813 0.419 1.00      770.     649.
2 log_w_d0  0.181  0.182 0.812 0.810  -1.13   1.54  1.00      581.     571.
3 log_w_s0 -0.233 -0.206 0.939 0.951  -1.80   1.25  1.000     624.     690.
\end{verbatim}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_trace}\NormalTok{(fit\_B}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"eta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_w\_d0"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_w\_s0"}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_adaptive_diagnostics-1.pdf}
\end{center}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{mcmc\_pairs}\NormalTok{(fit\_B}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"eta"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_w\_d0"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"log\_w\_s0"}\NormalTok{)))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10a-BayesianSequentialExtensions_files/figure-pdf/ch11_adaptive_diagnostics-2.pdf}
\end{center}

A note on what you will almost certainly see in the pairs plot: a strong
ridge between \(\eta\) and the initial log-weights. This is not a bug
--- it is structural. A small \(\eta\) with strongly asymmetric starting
weights produces similar behaviour to a large \(\eta\) with symmetric
starts, because both paths arrive at similar \emph{effective} weights by
the end of the trial stream. We flag this rather than hide it:
demonstrating and resolving the non-identifiability (via informative
priors, via parameter-informative experimental designs that force the
weights to change across blocks, or via multilevel partial pooling) is a
natural student project.

\section{Closing}\label{closing}

The Bayesian-cognition hypothesis is a family of models, not a single
commitment. Once an agent represents beliefs as probability
distributions and integrates evidence by Bayes' rule, many further
modelling choices remain open: when to stop sampling, how to weight
sources, whether those weights themselves are learned, what the
observation model looks like. The sample-or-guess and adaptive-weight
agents above are natural sequential extensions of the Ch. 10 family and
make good starting points for student projects --- a full validation
battery (parameter recovery, SBC, model comparison, multilevel pooling)
on either model is left as an exercise in applying the workflow you have
already learned.

\chapter{Stan Optimization Cheatsheet}\label{sec-stan-cheatsheet}

This appendix collects every Stan programming technique introduced
across Chapters 4--11, with minimal code examples, cost estimates, and
guidance on when to apply each one. The techniques are ordered by first
appearance in the book.

\textbf{Quick navigation}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\#
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Technique
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Chapter
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
1 & \hyperref[sec-block-placement]{Block placement} & Ch 4 \\
2 & \hyperref[sec-precompute-r]{Pre-compute in R} & Ch 4, 6 \\
3 & \hyperref[sec-sufficient-stats]{Sufficient statistics} & Ch 4 \\
4 & \hyperref[sec-vectorize]{Vectorize the likelihood} & Ch 4, 6 \\
5 & \hyperref[sec-priors-as-data]{Priors as data} & Ch 5 \\
6 & \hyperref[sec-run-diagnostics]{Conditional diagnostics
(\texttt{run\_diagnostics})} & Ch 5 \\
7 & \hyperref[sec-generate-quantities]{Post-hoc generated quantities} &
Ch 5 \\
8 & \hyperref[sec-ncp]{Non-centered parameterization} & Ch 6 \\
9 & \hyperref[sec-cholesky]{Cholesky factorization} & Ch 6 \\
10 & \hyperref[sec-subject-indexing]{Contiguous subject indexing} & Ch
6 \\
11 & \hyperref[sec-pathfinder]{Pathfinder warm-start} & Ch 4, 6, 11 \\
12 & \hyperref[sec-log-mix]{Marginalizing discrete parameters} & Ch 8 \\
13 & \hyperref[sec-sequential-state]{Sequential state unrolling} & Ch
9 \\
14 & \hyperref[sec-reduce-sum]{\texttt{reduce\_sum} parallelism} & Ch
11 \\
15 & \hyperref[sec-compound-speedups]{Combining techniques} & Ch 6,
11 \\
16 & \hyperref[sec-sampler-options]{Sampler and compiler options} & App
B \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{1. Block Placement --- Run It Once, Not Thousands of
Times}\label{sec-block-placement}

\emph{First introduced: Section~\ref{sec-memory-models} (Chapter 4,
Memory Model 1)}

HMC runs thousands of leapfrog steps during warm-up. Every line of code
in \texttt{transformed\ parameters} or \texttt{model} executes at each
step; every line in \texttt{transformed\ data} executes exactly
\textbf{once}.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Block
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Runs
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Put here
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{transformed\ data} & Once, before sampling & Quantities derived
\textbf{only from data} --- precomputed distances, log-transforms of
fixed inputs, cumulative sums \\
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} & Every leapfrog step \emph{(saved)} &
Deterministic functions of \textbf{parameters} whose gradients must
propagate --- NCP reparameterisations, recursive state, predicted values
used in the likelihood \\
\texttt{model} (local vars) & Every leapfrog step \emph{(not saved)} &
Intermediate scratch quantities not needed outside the loop \\
\texttt{generated\ quantities} & Once per saved draw & Posterior
predictive samples, \texttt{log\_lik}, prior draws --- anything
expensive that is \textbf{not} needed for sampling \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Cost of misplacement}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

A typical fit runs 4 chains × 1 000 warmup iterations × \textasciitilde6
leapfrog steps = \textbf{\textasciitilde24 000 leapfrog evaluations}
during warm-up. Moving 500 precomputed memory values from
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} to \texttt{transformed\ data} saves
\textasciitilde12 million floating-point operations per fit.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{transformed data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{// computed once — running average of opponent choices}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[N] memory;}
\NormalTok{  memory[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] = }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{;}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{:N)}
\NormalTok{    memory[t] = (}\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{ / t) * sum(other[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:(t}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{)]);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{2. Pre-Compute in R --- Don't Let Stan See the Raw
Computation}\label{sec-precompute-r}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6,
multilevel memory agent)}

\texttt{transformed\ data} runs once before sampling --- but the
computation still happens inside Stan's runtime. If a quantity depends
only on observed data and will never change between fits, computing it
in R and passing it as a data field is even cheaper: Stan never
processes the raw inputs at all.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# R: compute the running opponent rate once (O(N) vectorised R operation)}
\NormalTok{d }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ d }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{group\_by}\NormalTok{(subject) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{mutate}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =} \FunctionTok{lag}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{cummean}\NormalTok{(other\_choice), }\AttributeTok{default =} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{|\textgreater{}}
  \FunctionTok{ungroup}\NormalTok{()}

\NormalTok{stan\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{N\_total      =} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(d),}
  \AttributeTok{y            =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{choice,}
  \AttributeTok{opp\_rate\_prev =}\NormalTok{ d}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{opp\_rate\_prev   }\CommentTok{\# passed as a plain vector}
  \CommentTok{\# ...}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Stan only receives the pre{-}computed vector — no loop needed}
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\KeywordTok{upper}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}[N\_total] opp\_rate\_prev;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | mu + beta * logit(opp\_rate\_prev));}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Decision rule:} If the computation involves only observed data
and uses fast R/dplyr operations, do it in R. Reserve
\texttt{transformed\ data} for cases where the computation is cleaner to
express in Stan's syntax (e.g., distance matrices over stimuli).

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{3. Sufficient Statistics --- Aggregate IID
Trials}\label{sec-sufficient-stats}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-inferring-rates} (Chapter 4,
simple bias model)}

When all trials are \textbf{independently and identically distributed}
under the same parameter, the total number of successes is a
\textbf{sufficient statistic} --- it carries all the information in the
data about \(\theta\). A single Binomial call replaces \(N\) Bernoulli
calls.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// data block: pass the aggregate, not the full array}
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} N;}
  \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{upper}\NormalTok{=N\textgreater{} successes;  }\CommentTok{// sum(h)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ binomial\_logit\_lpmf(successes | N, theta\_logit);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stan\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =} \DecValTok{120}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{successes =} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(h))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Speedup}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

One Binomial evaluation vs.~120 Bernoulli evaluations --- every leapfrog
step, every chain. For a single-subject model with 4 chains × 24 000
leapfrog evaluations the difference is small in absolute time, but the
principle scales: for 10 000 IID trials the saving is real.

\end{tcolorbox}

\textbf{When it applies:} single-subject, single-session models where
\(\theta\) is constant across all \(N\) trials (Chapters 4--5 bias
models).

\textbf{When it does not apply:} memory models, multilevel models
(per-subject \(\theta_j\)), any model where the trial-level likelihood
depends on a trial-varying predictor.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{4. Vectorize the Likelihood Call}\label{sec-vectorize}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-inferring-rates} (Chapter 4);
used throughout from Chapter 5 onward}

Stan evaluates each distribution call as a node in the autodiff graph. A
vectorized call over a length-\(N\) vector creates \textbf{one node}; a
loop over \(N\) trials creates \textbf{\(N\) nodes}. Smaller graph =
faster gradient computation and better cache use.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Slower: N nodes in the autodiff graph}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N)}
