  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h[i] | theta\_logit);}

\CommentTok{// Faster: one node — mathematically identical}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(h | theta\_logit);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In multilevel models, scatter-indexing by subject makes the entire trial
pool vectorizable:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// N\_total trials, J subjects — one call instead of N\_total calls}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | theta\_logit[subj\_id]);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{When vectorization is not available}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

\texttt{log\_mix()} for mixture likelihoods (Chapter 8) operates on
\textbf{one observation at a time} because each call mixes two
observation-specific log-probabilities. There is no vectorized
\texttt{log\_mix} --- the per-observation loop is correct and
unavoidable there.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{5. Priors as Data --- Avoid
Recompilation}\label{sec-priors-as-data}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-model-quality-checks} (Chapter
5)}

Pass prior hyperparameters through the \texttt{data} block instead of
hard-coding them. Stan recompiles whenever the \textbf{code} changes,
never for new \textbf{data}. This lets you run prior sensitivity
analyses and SBC sweeps without recompiling.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} N;}
  \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[N] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ y;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{             prior\_mu\_mean;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{}    prior\_mu\_sigma;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  mu \textasciitilde{} normal(prior\_mu\_mean, prior\_mu\_sigma);}
\NormalTok{  y  \textasciitilde{} bernoulli\_logit(mu);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{N =}\NormalTok{ N, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ y,}
             \AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_mean =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{prior\_mu\_sigma =} \FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{6. The \texttt{run\_diagnostics} Flag ---
Conditional Generated
Quantities}{6. The run\_diagnostics Flag --- Conditional Generated Quantities}}\label{sec-run-diagnostics}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-model-quality-checks} (Chapter
5)}

Computing \texttt{log\_lik} (N values per draw) and \texttt{y\_rep} in
\texttt{generated\ quantities} can double run-time. Gate them behind an
integer flag so SBC backends can turn them off.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\KeywordTok{upper}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} run\_diagnostics;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{generated quantities}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[N] log\_lik = rep\_vector(}\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{, N);}
  \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[N] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ y\_rep;}
  \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (run\_diagnostics) \{}
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N) \{}
\NormalTok{      log\_lik[i] = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | mu);}
\NormalTok{      y\_rep[i]   = bernoulli\_logit\_rng(mu);}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Full fit — diagnostics on}
\NormalTok{data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L)}

\CommentTok{\# SBC backend — diagnostics off, \textasciitilde{}2× faster generated quantities}
\NormalTok{sbc\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{modifyList}\NormalTok{(data, }\FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{L))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{When does it matter most?}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

For 1 000 trials, 4 chains, 1 000 sampling draws,
\texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 1} generates and saves 4 million
\texttt{log\_lik} values. During SBC (which only needs the parameters),
setting \texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 0} eliminates that cost entirely.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{7. Post-Hoc Generated Quantities with
\texttt{\$generate\_quantities()}}{7. Post-Hoc Generated Quantities with \$generate\_quantities()}}\label{sec-generate-quantities}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-model-quality-checks} (Chapter
5)}

The \texttt{run\_diagnostics} flag lets you skip \texttt{log\_lik} and
\texttt{y\_rep} during SBC. A complementary pattern avoids the trade-off
entirely: \textbf{fit once with diagnostics off, then replay the saved
parameter draws through the \texttt{generated\ quantities} block on
demand}, without re-running MCMC.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Fast fit — no log\_lik or y\_rep during sampling}
\NormalTok{fit\_fast }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{L), ...)}

\CommentTok{\# Later: add log\_lik and y\_rep from the saved draws}
\NormalTok{fit\_gq }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{generate\_quantities}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{fitted\_params =}\NormalTok{ fit\_fast,}
  \AttributeTok{data          =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{run\_diagnostics =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L)}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{log\_lik }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"log\_lik"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{y\_rep   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ fit\_gq}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"y\_rep"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\texttt{\$generate\_quantities()} runs only the
\texttt{generated\ quantities} block --- the sampler is never touched.
Key consequences:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  LOO and PPC can be \textbf{deferred} until you actually need them.
\item
  A fit saved with \texttt{readRDS()} from a previous session can be
  replayed: pass the loaded object as \texttt{fitted\_params}.
\item
  SBC backends always run with \texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 0}; call
  \texttt{\$generate\_quantities()} only on the small subset of fits you
  want to inspect in detail.
\end{itemize}

The compiled model object must be the same for both the original fit and
the \texttt{\$generate\_quantities()} call.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{8. Non-Centered Parameterization (NCP)}\label{sec-ncp}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6,
multilevel biased agent)} \emph{Extended to simplex parameters:
Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars} (Chapter 11, multilevel decay
GCM)}

Centered parameterizations create \textbf{funnel geometries} when the
group-level variance (\texttt{sigma}) is small: individual-level
parameters collapse toward the mean, forming a narrow neck that HMC
cannot traverse efficiently, producing divergences, low E-BFMI, and
\texttt{max\_treedepth} warnings.

\textbf{Centered (problematic):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ mu;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} sigma;}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[J] theta;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  theta \textasciitilde{} normal(mu, sigma);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

~

\textbf{Non-Centered (preferred):}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ mu;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} sigma;}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[J] z;          }\CommentTok{// N(0,1) offset}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[J] theta = mu + sigma * z;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  z \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Logit-Normal NCP for Simplex
Parameters}\label{logit-normal-ncp-for-simplex-parameters}

For attention weights or mixing proportions modeled as a simplex, avoid
the Dirichlet + kappa parameterization. Small kappa values produce
nearly-flat Dirichlet distributions, creating the same funnel that
centered normal models do. For a 2-element simplex use a
\textbf{logit-normal NCP} instead:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{          pop\_logit\_w\_mean;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} pop\_logit\_w\_sd;}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[J]     z\_w;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[J] }\DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] w;}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (j }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:J) \{}
    \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lw  = pop\_logit\_w\_mean + pop\_logit\_w\_sd * z\_w[j];}
\NormalTok{    w[j][}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]  = inv\_logit(lw);}
\NormalTok{    w[j][}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{]  = }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{ {-} w[j][}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{];}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  z\_w \textasciitilde{} std\_normal();}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-warning-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-warning-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-warning-color}{\faExclamationTriangle}\hspace{0.5em}{The cost of ignoring NCP}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

The centered Dirichlet +
\texttt{kappa\ \textasciitilde{}\ exponential(0.1)} parameterization in
Chapter 11's multilevel decay GCM produced \textbf{4 000 max\_treedepth
warnings per run} and failed to complete 10 iterations overnight at
\texttt{adapt\_delta\ =\ 0.99,\ max\_treedepth\ =\ 20}. Switching to
logit-normal NCP eliminated the funnel geometry entirely.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{When centered parameterization is preferred}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

NCP is not always the right choice. When the data are highly informative
relative to the prior, the \emph{centered} parameterization can be more
efficient. In that regime, the group-level variance \(\sigma\) is
well-constrained by the data, so the individual-level parameters
\(\theta_j\) are tightly funneled by the likelihood rather than the
prior --- and HMC can navigate the centered geometry directly without
the stretched \(z\)-offsets that NCP introduces.

A practical heuristic (the \textbf{park rule}): prefer NCP when the
per-group sample size is small relative to the between-group variance
(data are weak); prefer CP when data are plentiful and the posterior for
\(\sigma\) is far from zero. When in doubt, fit both and compare
diagnostics.

See
\href{https://avehtari.github.io/Bayesian-Workflow/park_rule/park_rule.html}{Vehtari
et al.~--- the park rule} for a formal treatment with diagnostics to
guide the choice.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{9. Cholesky Factorization for Correlation
Matrices}\label{sec-cholesky}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6,
multilevel memory agent with correlated parameters)}

Whenever a model estimates a \textbf{correlation matrix} \(\Omega\) over
individual-level parameters, use the Cholesky-factorized form rather
than sampling \(\Omega\) directly.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Parameterization
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Leapfrog cost
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Why prefer/avoid
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{corr\_matrix{[}K{]}\ Omega} + \texttt{lkj\_corr\_lpdf} &
\(O(K^3)\) matrix operations + positive-definiteness check & Expensive;
unconstrained transform requires matrix square root \\
\texttt{cholesky\_factor\_corr{[}K{]}\ L\_Omega} +
\texttt{lkj\_corr\_cholesky\_lpdf} & \(O(K^2)\) & Lies directly on its
natural manifold; no matrix inversion needed \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Avoid}
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{ }\DataTypeTok{corr\_matrix}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] Omega; \}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{      \{ Omega \textasciitilde{} lkj\_corr(}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{); \}}

\CommentTok{// Prefer}
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{ }\DataTypeTok{cholesky\_factor\_corr}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] L\_Omega; \}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{      \{ L\_Omega \textasciitilde{} lkj\_corr\_cholesky(}\FloatTok{2.0}\NormalTok{); \}}

\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{// O(K\^{}2) multiply — no square root}
  \DataTypeTok{matrix}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, J] deviations = diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma, L\_Omega) * z;}
\NormalTok{\}}

\KeywordTok{generated quantities}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{// Reconstruct Omega once per saved draw, not every leapfrog step}
  \DataTypeTok{matrix}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{] Omega = multiply\_lower\_tri\_self\_transpose(L\_Omega);}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ rho = Omega[}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{];}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Key point:} \texttt{diag\_pre\_multiply(sigma,\ L\_Omega)\ *\ z}
produces individual-level NCP deviations directly from the Cholesky
factor --- the full correlation matrix \(\Omega\) is only recovered in
\texttt{generated\ quantities}, running once per saved draw rather than
thousands of times during warm-up.

For \(K = 2\) parameters the cost difference is modest; for \(K \geq 5\)
(e.g., five per-subject RL parameters) it becomes substantial.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{10. Contiguous Subject Indexing ---
\texttt{subj\_start} /
\texttt{subj\_end}}{10. Contiguous Subject Indexing --- subj\_start / subj\_end}}\label{sec-subject-indexing}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6,
multilevel memory model)}

Two strategies for multi-subject trial data:

\textbf{Strategy A --- \texttt{subj\_id} lookup} (simple; works only
when trials are independent):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{ }\DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[N\_total] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ subj\_id; \}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N\_total)}
    \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_lpmf(y[i] | f(theta[subj\_id[i]], x[i]));}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Strategy B --- Contiguous block indexing} (required for
sequential models and \texttt{reduce\_sum}):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[J] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ subj\_start;}
  \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[J] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ subj\_end;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (j }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:J)}
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in}\NormalTok{ subj\_start[j]:subj\_end[j])}
      \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_lpmf(y[t] | f(theta[j], x[t]));}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Build the index in R:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{subj\_start }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{tapply}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_along}\NormalTok{(subj\_id), subj\_id, min))}
\NormalTok{subj\_end   }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.integer}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{tapply}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{seq\_along}\NormalTok{(subj\_id), subj\_id, max))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Use Strategy B whenever:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  Trial \(t\) depends on state accumulated from trials
  \(1, \ldots, t-1\) (memory, belief updating, RL)
\item
  You intend to use \texttt{reduce\_sum} for within-chain parallelism
  (see Section~\ref{sec-reduce-sum})
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{11. Pathfinder Warm-Start Initialisation}\label{sec-pathfinder}

\emph{First benchmarked: Chapter~\ref{sec-inferring-rates} (Chapter 4)}
\emph{Timing comparison for multilevel models:
Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6)} \emph{Three-way
benchmark: Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars} (Chapter 11)}

Stan's default initialisation draws chain starts from Uniform(−2, 2) in
unconstrained space --- far from the posterior on hierarchical models.
\textbf{Pathfinder} runs L-BFGS from each start, fits Gaussian
approximations along the path, and returns approximate posterior draws.
Passing these as \texttt{init} to \texttt{\$sample()} lets HMC skip most
of the warm-up trajectory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Step 1 — fast variational approximation}
\NormalTok{pf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{pathfinder}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data, }\AttributeTok{num\_paths =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{draws =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{seed =} \DecValTok{123}\NormalTok{)}

\CommentTok{\# Step 2 — draw one starting point per chain}
\NormalTok{init\_pf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) }\FunctionTok{as.list}\NormalTok{(pf}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()[i, , }\AttributeTok{drop =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{]))}

\CommentTok{\# Step 3 — full MCMC from warm starts}
\NormalTok{fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data, }\AttributeTok{init =}\NormalTok{ init\_pf,}
                  \AttributeTok{chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_warmup =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{iter\_sampling =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{When Pathfinder helps most}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Pathfinder gives the largest benefit on \textbf{hierarchical models} and
any model with correlated population parameters. It saves 20--50\% of
total wall time by shortening the warmup phase. On simple,
well-conditioned models the warm-up is already fast --- the Pathfinder
overhead may outweigh the saving.

\textbf{Pathfinder output is not a gold-standard posterior.} Always run
full MCMC and check diagnostics.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{12. Marginalizing Discrete Parameters with
\texttt{log\_mix}}{12. Marginalizing Discrete Parameters with log\_mix}}\label{sec-log-mix}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-mixture-models} (Chapter 8,
single and multilevel mixture models)}

HMC requires continuous, differentiable parameters. Discrete latent
variables (component indicators, regime switches) must be
\textbf{analytically marginalized} out of the likelihood before
sampling.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// two{-}component mixture}
\DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lp1 = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | alpha);}
\DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lp2 = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y | nu);}
\CommentTok{// log( pi*exp(lp1) + (1{-}pi)*exp(lp2) )}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ log\_mix(pi, lp1, lp2);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

For \(K > 2\) components use \texttt{log\_sum\_exp} directly:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[K] lps;}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (k }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:K)}
\NormalTok{  lps[k] = log(pi[k]) + component\_lpmf(y | theta[k]);}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ log\_sum\_exp(lps);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-warning-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-warning-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-warning-color}{\faExclamationTriangle}\hspace{0.5em}{Never sample discrete indicators directly}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// WRONG — HMC cannot compute gradients of discrete parameters}
\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ z \textasciitilde{} categorical(pi);}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_lpmf(y | theta[z]);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This will compile but produce incorrect results or sampler errors at
runtime. Always marginalise.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{13. Sequential State Unrolling in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters}}{13. Sequential State Unrolling in transformed parameters}}\label{sec-sequential-state}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-theory-of-mind} (Chapter 9,
weighted and proportional agent models)}

When the likelihood depends on a latent state that evolves
trial-by-trial (belief, RL value, running weight), unroll the recursion
in \texttt{transformed\ parameters} so the gradient flows back through
the entire trajectory.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[N] predicted\_choice;}
\NormalTok{  \{}
    \CommentTok{// local scope: scratch variable allocated and freed each leapfrog step}
    \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ belief = }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N) \{}
\NormalTok{      predicted\_choice[t] = inv\_logit(weight * (belief {-} }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{));}
\NormalTok{      belief += alpha * (feedback[t] {-} belief);}
\NormalTok{    \}}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ bernoulli\_lpmf(y | predicted\_choice);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Why \texttt{transformed\ parameters} and not \texttt{model}?}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

State that enters the likelihood needs its gradient tracked by autodiff.
Declare only the \textbf{output vector} (\texttt{predicted\_choice}) as
a \texttt{transformed\ parameters} variable; keep loop-internal scratch
variables inside a \texttt{\{\}} local block to avoid placing them on
the saved-draw tape.

\textbf{Exception:} If the loop is independent across subjects, move it
into a \texttt{partial\_sum\_lp} function and use \texttt{reduce\_sum}
for across-subject parallelism (see Section~\ref{sec-reduce-sum}).

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{\texorpdfstring{14. Intra-Chain Parallelism with
\texttt{reduce\_sum}}{14. Intra-Chain Parallelism with reduce\_sum}}\label{sec-reduce-sum}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars}
(Chapter 11, multilevel decay GCM)}

\texttt{reduce\_sum} exploits \textbf{conditional independence across
subjects}: given population parameters, subject \(j\)'s trial loop
shares no mutable state with subject \(k\)'s loop. The subject dimension
can therefore be distributed across CPU threads \textbf{within a single
chain}, giving near-linear speedup up to the number of subjects.

\textbf{Step 1 --- Write a \texttt{partial\_sum\_lp} function in the
\texttt{functions} block:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{functions}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ partial\_sum\_lp(}
    \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ subj\_slice, }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ start, }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ end,}
    \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{ log\_c,}
    \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ subj\_start\_g, }\DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ subj\_end\_g,}
    \DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[] }\DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ y, }\DataTypeTok{array}\NormalTok{[,] }\DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ x}
\NormalTok{  ) \{}
    \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lp = }\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{;}
    \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (idx }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:size(subj\_slice)) \{}
      \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{ j = subj\_slice[idx];}
      \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in}\NormalTok{ subj\_start\_g[j]:subj\_end\_g[j])}
\NormalTok{        lp += bernoulli\_lpmf(y[t] | f(exp(log\_c[j]), x[t]));}
\NormalTok{    \}}
    \ControlFlowTok{return}\NormalTok{ lp;}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Step 2 --- Replace the subject loop with \texttt{reduce\_sum} in
the \texttt{model} block:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \CommentTok{// subject\_ids = \{1, 2, ..., J\}}
  \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ reduce\_sum(partial\_sum\_lp, subject\_ids, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{,}
\NormalTok{                       log\_c, subj\_start, subj\_end, y, x);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Step 3 --- Compile with threading support and sample:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod\_par }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(stan\_file,}
                         \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{))}

\NormalTok{n\_threads }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L, parallel}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{detectCores}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{\%/\%} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{L)}

\NormalTok{fit\_par }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_par}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{data             =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data,}
  \AttributeTok{chains           =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains  =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{threads\_per\_chain =}\NormalTok{ n\_threads,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup      =} \DecValTok{500}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling    =} \DecValTok{1000}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{When is \texttt{reduce\_sum} worth it?}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Condition
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Effect
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
≥ 20 subjects with expensive per-subject loops & Large speedup
(near-linear up to J) \\
Few subjects or very cheap loops & Threading overhead may dominate \\
Within-subject sequential dependency & Still valid --- parallelises
\emph{across} subjects, not \emph{within} \\
Contiguous \texttt{subj\_start}/\texttt{subj\_end} indexing &
\textbf{Required} (see Section~\ref{sec-subject-indexing}) \\
\end{longtable}

The grainsize argument (second integer in \texttt{reduce\_sum}) controls
the minimum slice size. Use \texttt{1} as a starting point; increase if
the per-subject work is small.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Within-subject loops cannot be parallelised with \texttt{reduce\_sum}}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

In the single-subject GCM (Chapter 11), trial \(t\) depends on the
memory state accumulated from trials \(1, \ldots, t-1\). This sequential
dependency is \textbf{within} a subject, so \texttt{reduce\_sum} cannot
help there. Parallelism across subjects is always safe because subjects
are conditionally independent.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{15. Combining Techniques --- Compound
Speedups}\label{sec-compound-speedups}

\emph{Three-way timing benchmark:
Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars} (Chapter 11)}

The techniques above stack multiplicatively on hierarchical models:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Technique
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Typical saving
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
When to apply
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
NCP instead of CP & 5--100× fewer divergences & Any multilevel model ---
always prefer NCP \\
\texttt{transformed\ data} precompute & 10--100× fewer flops & Static
quantities derived from data only \\
\texttt{run\_diagnostics\ =\ 0} in SBC & \textasciitilde2× faster
\texttt{generated\ quantities} & All SBC backends \\
Pathfinder warm-start & 20--50\% less wall time & Hierarchical models;
correlated posteriors \\
\texttt{reduce\_sum} (10 subjects, 4-core laptop) & 2--3× within-chain &
≥ 20 subjects, expensive loops \\
Pathfinder + \texttt{reduce\_sum} together & 3--5× vs.~serial baseline &
Large multilevel models, multi-core hardware \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Benchmark template} (used in Chapters 6 and 11):

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{time\_serial }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{system.time}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_serial }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data, ...)}
\NormalTok{)[[}\StringTok{"elapsed"}\NormalTok{]]}

\NormalTok{pf       }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{pathfinder}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data, }\AttributeTok{num\_paths =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{draws =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{init\_pf  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lapply}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{, }\ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(i) }\FunctionTok{as.list}\NormalTok{(pf}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{draws}\NormalTok{()[i, , }\AttributeTok{drop =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{]))}

\NormalTok{time\_serial\_pf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{system.time}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_pf }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data, }\AttributeTok{init =}\NormalTok{ init\_pf, ...)}
\NormalTok{)[[}\StringTok{"elapsed"}\NormalTok{]]}

\NormalTok{n\_threads }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{max}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{L, parallel}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{detectCores}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{\%/\%} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{L)}
\NormalTok{time\_par  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{system.time}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  fit\_par }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod\_par}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data, }\AttributeTok{init =}\NormalTok{ init\_pf,}
    \AttributeTok{threads\_per\_chain =}\NormalTok{ n\_threads, ...)}
\NormalTok{)[[}\StringTok{"elapsed"}\NormalTok{]]}

\FunctionTok{tibble}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{Strategy =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Serial (baseline)"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Serial + Pathfinder"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Parallel + Pathfinder"}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{Time\_sec =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(time\_serial, time\_serial\_pf, time\_par),}
  \AttributeTok{Speedup  =} \FunctionTok{round}\NormalTok{(time\_serial }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(time\_serial, time\_serial\_pf, time\_par), }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{16. Sampler and Compiler Options}\label{sec-sampler-options}

\emph{Reference:
\href{https://blog.mc-stan.org/2022/08/03/options-for-improving-stan-sampling-speed/}{Options
for improving Stan sampling speed}}

The techniques in §1--15 reduce the \emph{work per leapfrog step}. The
options below change how the sampler and compiler behave around those
steps. They are cheap to try and often give meaningful savings with no
code changes.

\subsection{\texorpdfstring{Sampler options (passed to
\texttt{\$sample()})}{Sampler options (passed to \$sample())}}\label{sampler-options-passed-to-sample}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Option
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Default
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Guidance
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\texttt{adapt\_delta} & 0.80 & Raise to 0.90--0.95 when divergences
persist after NCP; higher values force smaller step sizes and more
leapfrog steps per transition \\
\texttt{max\_treedepth} & 10 & Raise to 12--15 only when
\texttt{max\_treedepth} warnings dominate; raising without fixing
geometry just burns time \\
\texttt{iter\_warmup} & 1000 & Lower to 500 on well-conditioned models;
raise to 2000 when E-BFMI warnings persist across chains \\
\texttt{iter\_sampling} & 1000 & Raise when posterior tails matter
(e.g., rare-event predictions) or when \(\hat{R}\) instability is
numerical rather than geometric \\
\texttt{chains} & 4 & Never run fewer than 4 --- \(\hat{R}\) requires
between-chain comparison \\
\texttt{parallel\_chains} & 1 & Set to \texttt{chains} when fitting a
single model; reduce when running many parallel fits \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(}
  \AttributeTok{data           =}\NormalTok{ stan\_data,}
  \AttributeTok{chains         =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{parallel\_chains =} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_warmup    =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{iter\_sampling  =} \DecValTok{1000}\NormalTok{,}
  \AttributeTok{adapt\_delta    =} \FloatTok{0.90}\NormalTok{,     }\CommentTok{\# raise from 0.80 if divergences persist}
  \AttributeTok{max\_treedepth  =} \DecValTok{12}        \CommentTok{\# raise from 10 if treedepth warnings dominate}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection{Compiler optimizations}\label{compiler-optimizations}

Stan models are compiled to C++. Enabling processor-specific
optimizations costs nothing at runtime but can save 10--30\% on
algebraically intensive models (GCMs, ToM).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{mod }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{cmdstan\_model}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  stan\_file,}
  \AttributeTok{cpp\_options =} \FunctionTok{list}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{stan\_threads =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{,          }\CommentTok{\# required for reduce\_sum (§14)}
    \AttributeTok{CXXFLAGS =} \StringTok{"{-}O3 {-}march=native"} \CommentTok{\# processor{-}specific vectorization}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-warning-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-warning-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-warning-color}{\faExclamationTriangle}\hspace{0.5em}{\texttt{-march=native} portability}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Binaries compiled with \texttt{-march=native} use CPU instructions
specific to the build machine. Do not share compiled Stan objects across
machines with different processor families. Recompile on the target
machine.

\end{tcolorbox}

\subsection{Output size}\label{output-size}

By default Stan saves 8-significant-figure draws. For large models with
many generated quantities, reduce to 6 to halve \texttt{.csv} file sizes
with negligible precision loss:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{fit }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ mod}\SpecialCharTok{$}\FunctionTok{sample}\NormalTok{(..., }\AttributeTok{sig\_figs =} \DecValTok{6}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Quick Reference}\label{sec-stan-quick-ref}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Problem observed
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Technique
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Section
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Redundant computation during leapfrog & Block placement →
\texttt{transformed\ data} & \hyperref[sec-block-placement]{§1} \\
Quantity derived from data only, easier to compute in R & Pre-compute in
R & \hyperref[sec-precompute-r]{§2} \\
Many IID trials, one parameter per session & Sufficient statistics →
\texttt{binomial\_lpmf} & \hyperref[sec-sufficient-stats]{§3} \\
Trial loop over identical distribution & Vectorize the likelihood call &
\hyperref[sec-vectorize]{§4} \\
Changing priors requires recompiling & Priors as data &
\hyperref[sec-priors-as-data]{§5} \\
SBC / LOO is slow & \texttt{run\_diagnostics} flag &
\hyperref[sec-run-diagnostics]{§6} \\
Want PPC/LOO without re-running MCMC & \texttt{\$generate\_quantities()}
post-hoc & \hyperref[sec-generate-quantities]{§7} \\
Divergences / low E-BFMI in multilevel model & Non-centered
parameterization (NCP) & \hyperref[sec-ncp]{§8} \\
Simplex + small kappa → funnel geometry & Logit-normal NCP for simplex &
\hyperref[sec-ncp]{§8} \\
Model has correlation matrix \(\Omega\) & Cholesky factorization
(\texttt{lkj\_corr\_cholesky}) & \hyperref[sec-cholesky]{§9} \\
Sequential model with per-subject trial loops & Contiguous
\texttt{subj\_start}/\texttt{subj\_end} indexing &
\hyperref[sec-subject-indexing]{§10} \\
Slow warm-up on complex / hierarchical model & Pathfinder warm-start &
\hyperref[sec-pathfinder]{§11} \\
Discrete latent variables (indicators, regimes) & Marginalise with
\texttt{log\_mix} & \hyperref[sec-log-mix]{§12} \\
Trial-to-trial latent state in likelihood & Sequential unrolling in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} &
\hyperref[sec-sequential-state]{§13} \\
Many subjects, multi-core machine & \texttt{reduce\_sum} intra-chain
parallelism & \hyperref[sec-reduce-sum]{§14} \\
Divergences / treedepth / slow warm-up & Sampler options
(\texttt{adapt\_delta}, \texttt{max\_treedepth}) &
\hyperref[sec-sampler-options]{§16} \\
Slow compilation or runtime on algebraic models & Compiler flags
(\texttt{-O3\ -march=native}) & \hyperref[sec-sampler-options]{§16} \\
Large \texttt{.csv} output files & Reduce \texttt{sig\_figs} to 6 &
\hyperref[sec-sampler-options]{§16} \\
\end{longtable}

\chapter{Reparameterization Techniques}\label{sec-reparameterization}

A \textbf{reparameterization} replaces one mathematical form of a
parameter with another that is equivalent for inference but better
behaved for HMC, easier to place priors on, or more directly
interpretable in cognitive terms. This appendix collects every
reparameterization used across the book, organized by the type of
constraint the original parameter imposes.

The key intuition throughout: \textbf{HMC works in unconstrained space}.
Stan handles the Jacobian of any declared constraint
(\texttt{\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}}, \texttt{simplex}, etc.)
automatically, but the efficiency of the sampler depends on the
\emph{shape} of the posterior in that unconstrained space. A good
reparameterization flattens and regularizes that shape.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Part I --- Mapping Bounded Parameters to Unbounded
Space}\label{sec-reparam-bounded}

\subsection{1. Probabilities {[}0, 1{]} --- the Logit
Transform}\label{sec-logit}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-inferring-rates} (Chapter 4,
logit bias model)}

A probability \(\theta \in [0,1]\) cannot use a Normal prior directly
--- Normal has infinite support, and the boundaries create hard walls
that distort HMC trajectories. The logit transform maps \([0,1]\) to
\((-\infty, +\infty)\):

\[\text{logit}(\theta) = \log\!\left(\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}\right), \qquad
  \text{logit}^{-1}(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} \equiv \texttt{inv\_logit}(x)\]

\textbf{Stan pattern:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ theta\_logit;           }\CommentTok{// unconstrained — Normal prior works cleanly}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\KeywordTok{upper}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} theta = inv\_logit(theta\_logit);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  theta\_logit \textasciitilde{} normal(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{1.5}\NormalTok{);   }\CommentTok{// ≈ uniform on probability scale}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Choosing the prior width on the logit scale:}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\texttt{normal(0,\ σ)} on logit scale
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Implied prior on probability scale
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
σ = 0.5 & Concentrated near 0.5; rarely below 0.2 or above 0.8 \\
σ = 1.0 & Moderately diffuse \\
σ = 1.5 & Approximately uniform on {[}0,1{]} --- the default for
uninformative use \\
σ = 3.0 & Heavy mass near 0 and 1; implies extreme determinism \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Cognitive uses:} choice bias, probability-matching weight,
mixing proportion, learning rate, any rate parameter bounded by
{[}0,1{]}.

\textbf{Stan shortcut:}
\texttt{bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y\ \textbar{}\ theta\_logit)} accepts the
log-odds directly, so the \texttt{inv\_logit} transform in
\texttt{transformed\ parameters} is only needed when you want to report
\(\theta\) on the probability scale in \texttt{generated\ quantities}.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{2. Positive-Only Parameters --- the Log
Transform}\label{sec-log}

\emph{First introduced: Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars}
(Chapter 11, GCM sensitivity \(c\) and decay \(\lambda\))}

A parameter that must be strictly positive (\(\theta > 0\)) ---
sensitivity, scale, precision, rate --- can be declared as
\texttt{real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}}, but this creates a
boundary at zero that HMC must negotiate. An unconstrained
\texttt{real\ log\_theta} with a Normal prior is geometrically smoother.

\[\log\theta \in (-\infty, +\infty), \qquad \theta = e^{\log\theta} > 0\]

\textbf{Stan pattern:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ log\_c;          }\CommentTok{// log sensitivity — unconstrained}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ log\_lambda;     }\CommentTok{// log decay rate — unconstrained}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} c      = exp(log\_c);}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} lambda = exp(log\_lambda);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  log\_c      \textasciitilde{} normal(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{);    }\CommentTok{// prior on log scale}
\NormalTok{  log\_lambda \textasciitilde{} normal({-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{);   }\CommentTok{// informative: moderate decay expected}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Interpreting the log-scale prior:}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Prior on log θ
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Implied range for θ
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Cognitive interpretation
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
normal(0, 1) & ≈ {[}0.05, 20{]} (2 SD) & Broad uninformative prior for a
scale parameter \\
normal(log(2), 0.5) & Centred at 2, roughly {[}0.7, 5.5{]} &
Informative: moderate sensitivity expected \\
normal(-1, 0.5) & Centred at 0.37, roughly {[}0.15, 0.9{]} & Slow decay
/ weak forgetting \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Cognitive uses:} GCM sensitivity \(c\), exponential decay rate
\(\lambda\), RL learning rate \(\alpha\) (if modeled as positive but not
bounded above 1), noise precision, inverse temperature \(\beta\).

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Log transform vs.~\texttt{\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}} declaration}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Declaring \texttt{real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}\ sigma} in
\texttt{parameters} applies an exponential-map transform internally, so
it is \emph{mathematically equivalent} to declaring
\texttt{real\ log\_sigma} and computing
\texttt{sigma\ =\ exp(log\_sigma)}. The difference is:

\begin{itemize}
\tightlist
\item
  \texttt{real\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}\ sigma} → Stan chooses
  the unconstrained transform; you place the prior directly on
  \(\sigma\).
\item
  \texttt{real\ log\_sigma} → you control the transform and place the
  prior on \(\log\sigma\).
\end{itemize}

The second form is preferred when you have \textbf{informative prior
beliefs expressible on the log scale} or when the gradient geometry near
zero is poor.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{3. Parameters Bounded in {[}0, 1{]} with a Structural
Interpretation --- the Beta Reparameterization}\label{sec-beta-reparam}

\emph{Used in: Chapter~\ref{sec-inferring-rates} (Chapter 4, forgetting
rate), Chapter~\ref{sec-bayesian-models} (Chapter 10, evidence weight
\(\rho\), allocation \(p\))}

Sometimes a {[}0,1{]} parameter is best understood as a \textbf{mean
with an associated uncertainty} rather than as a log-odds. The Beta
distribution has two equivalent parameterizations:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Parameterization
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Parameters
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Interpretation
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Standard & \(\alpha, \beta > 0\) & Shape parameters --- not directly
interpretable \\
Mean + concentration & \(\mu \in (0,1)\), \(\kappa > 0\) & Mean
\(= \mu\); concentration \(= \kappa\); variance
\(= \mu(1-\mu)/(\kappa+1)\) \\
\end{longtable}

\[\alpha = \mu\kappa, \qquad \beta = (1-\mu)\kappa\]

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Mean+concentration parameterization — more interpretable priors}
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,}\KeywordTok{upper}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} mu;     }\CommentTok{// expected value}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} kappa;          }\CommentTok{// concentration (larger = tighter around mu)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} alpha = mu * kappa;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} beta  = (}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{ {-} mu) * kappa;}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  mu    \textasciitilde{} beta(}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{);          }\CommentTok{// soft peak at 0.5; any value plausible}
\NormalTok{  kappa \textasciitilde{} exponential(}\FloatTok{0.1}\NormalTok{);    }\CommentTok{// most agents moderately uncertain}
\NormalTok{  y     \textasciitilde{} beta(alpha, beta);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{When to prefer mean+concentration over logit:} When the
cognitive claim \emph{is} about the mean allocation (e.g., ``this agent
allocates \(\rho\) of its weight to direct evidence''), and you want to
reason separately about average tendency (\(\mu\)) and consistency
(\(\kappa\)).

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{4. Weight Vectors Summing to One --- Simplex
Reparameterizations}\label{sec-simplex}

\emph{Used in: Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars} (Chapter 11,
GCM attention weights \(\mathbf{w}\))}

A \(K\)-dimensional \textbf{simplex} (\(w_k \geq 0\),
\(\sum_k w_k = 1\)) can be parameterized in several ways. For \(K = 2\)
(two features), logit-normal NCP is always preferred (see
Section~\ref{sec-reparameterization-ncp}). For \(K > 2\), the choice is
less clear-cut.

\subsubsection{4a. Dirichlet directly (simple, but problematic for
hierarchical
models)}\label{a.-dirichlet-directly-simple-but-problematic-for-hierarchical-models}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{ }\DataTypeTok{simplex}\NormalTok{[K] w; \}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{      \{ w \textasciitilde{} dirichlet(alpha); \}  }\CommentTok{// alpha: K{-}vector of concentration hyperparameters}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Works well for single-subject models where concentration \texttt{alpha}
is fixed. \textbf{Avoid in hierarchical models}: the Dirichlet +
hyperprior-on-concentration geometry creates funnels identical to the
NCP problem (Ch. 11 case study).

\subsubsection{4b. Stick-breaking (unconstrained representation, K−1
free
parameters)}\label{b.-stick-breaking-unconstrained-representation-k1-free-parameters}

Decompose a \(K\)-simplex into \(K-1\) unconstrained values using the
sequential stick-breaking transform. Stan implements this automatically
for \texttt{simplex} declarations --- you never need to code it
manually. But understanding it helps with custom implementations:

\[v_k = \text{logit}(w_k^* + \epsilon_k), \quad
  w_k = w_k^* \cdot \prod_{j<k}(1 - w_j^*), \quad w_K = 1 - \sum_{k<K} w_k\]

For \(K = 2\): reduces exactly to the logit transform on \(w_1\), with
\(w_2 = 1 - w_1\).

\subsubsection{\texorpdfstring{4c. Logit-normal NCP for \(K = 2\)
(recommended for hierarchical
models)}{4c. Logit-normal NCP for K = 2 (recommended for hierarchical models)}}\label{c.-logit-normal-ncp-for-k-2-recommended-for-hierarchical-models}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{          pop\_logit\_w\_mean;}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} pop\_logit\_w\_sd;}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[J]     z\_w;                        }\CommentTok{// standard{-}normal offsets}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (j }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:J) \{}
    \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lw  = pop\_logit\_w\_mean + pop\_logit\_w\_sd * z\_w[j];}
\NormalTok{    w[j][}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]  = inv\_logit(lw);}
\NormalTok{    w[j][}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{]  = }\FloatTok{1.0}\NormalTok{ {-} w[j][}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{];}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{ z\_w \textasciitilde{} std\_normal(); \}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This was the fix for the 4 000 max\_treedepth warnings in the multilevel
decay GCM (Chapter 11). The centered Dirichlet +
\texttt{kappa\ \textasciitilde{}\ exponential(0.1)} produced a funnel;
the logit-normal NCP eliminated it entirely.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{5. K-Way Choice Probabilities --- the Softmax
Transform}\label{sec-softmax}

\emph{Mentioned in: Chapter~\ref{sec-theory-of-mind} (Chapter 9),
Chapter~\ref{sec-categorization-exemplars} (Chapter 11)}

When an agent chooses among \(K > 2\) options and the evidence for each
option is a real-valued score \(v_k\), the \textbf{softmax} maps scores
to probabilities:

\[p_k = \frac{e^{v_k / \tau}}{\sum_{j=1}^K e^{v_j / \tau}}\]

where \(\tau > 0\) is an \textbf{inverse temperature} (higher \(\tau\) =
more random; lower = more deterministic). For \(K = 2\), softmax reduces
to the logistic function.

\textbf{Stan:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// v is a vector of K utility/evidence values; tau is inverse temperature}
\DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[K] p = softmax(v / tau);}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ categorical\_lpmf(choice | p);}

\CommentTok{// Or equivalently, in log space (more numerically stable):}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ categorical\_logit\_lpmf(choice | v / tau);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{Sampling \texttt{tau} vs.~sampling \texttt{beta\ =\ 1/tau}}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Convention varies across the literature. Stan's
\texttt{categorical\_logit\_lpmf} takes the \textbf{log-odds vector}
directly, so it is natural to sample \(\beta = 1/\tau\) (inverse
temperature) on the log scale:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ log\_beta;                        }\CommentTok{// log inverse{-}temperature}
\CommentTok{// ...}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ categorical\_logit\_lpmf(choice | exp(log\_beta) * v);}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This places a Normal prior on \(\log\beta\), keeping sampling
unconstrained.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Part II --- Priors on Scale Parameters}\label{sec-scale-priors}

Scale parameters (\(\sigma\), \(\tau\), \(\kappa\)) must be positive and
appear in hierarchical models as between-subject standard deviations.
The prior on these parameters strongly affects sampling geometry near
zero.

\subsection{6. Exponential, Half-Normal, and
Half-Cauchy}\label{sec-scale-param-priors}

\emph{Used in: Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6,
\texttt{sigma\_theta\ \textasciitilde{}\ exponential(lambda)})}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Prior
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Stan syntax
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Shape
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Recommendation
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Exponential(\(\lambda\)) &
\texttt{sigma\ \textasciitilde{}\ exponential(lambda)} & Monotone
decreasing from 0; most mass near 0 & Default in this book; works well
when small \(\sigma\) is plausible \\
Half-Normal(\(\sigma_0\)) &
\texttt{sigma\ \textasciitilde{}\ normal(0,\ sigma\_0)\ T{[}0,{]}} &
Bell-shaped; puts more mass away from 0 & Better when you expect
moderate between-subject variance \\
Half-Cauchy(\(s\)) &
\texttt{sigma\ \textasciitilde{}\ cauchy(0,\ s)\ T{[}0,{]}} & Heavy
tails & Use when outlier subjects are plausible \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{The book's choice} (exponential throughout) reflects a
regularizing stance: most cognitive-parameter hierarchies are relatively
homogeneous, so placing more mass near \(\sigma = 0\) acts as a mild
shrinkage prior that prevents the posterior from drifting to implausibly
large between-subject variances.

\textbf{Parameterizing the exponential:} \texttt{exponential(lambda)}
has mean \(1/\lambda\). Setting \texttt{lambda\ =\ 1} means the prior
expects \(\sigma \approx 1\) on the log-odds scale. For parameters whose
population mean is on a different scale, adjust accordingly.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Exponential prior for between{-}subject SD on the logit scale}
\NormalTok{sigma \textasciitilde{} exponential(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{);    }\CommentTok{// mean = 1 log{-}odds unit ≈ ±25 percentage points}

\CommentTok{// Half{-}Normal alternative — more permissive}
\NormalTok{sigma \textasciitilde{} normal(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{) }\KeywordTok{T}\NormalTok{[}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{,];  }\CommentTok{// 95\% prior mass below \textasciitilde{}1 log{-}odds unit}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-note-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-note-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-note-color}{\faInfo}\hspace{0.5em}{Why not \texttt{\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}\ real\ sigma} +
\texttt{normal(0,\ σ₀)}?}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Stan accepts \texttt{sigma\ \textasciitilde{}\ normal(0,\ 0.5)} even
when \texttt{sigma} is declared
\texttt{\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}}. This is a \textbf{truncated
normal} --- Stan evaluates the density only on \([0, \infty)\). For HMC,
declaring the constraint \texttt{\textless{}lower=0\textgreater{}} is
important; the prior statement handles the shape. The explicit
\texttt{T{[}0,{]}} truncation notation is only needed inside
\texttt{stan\_code} blocks where Stan cannot infer the constraint from
the declaration.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Part III --- Structural
Reparameterizations}\label{sec-structural-reparam}

\subsection{7. Non-Centered Parameterization
(NCP)}\label{sec-reparameterization-ncp}

\emph{Covered in detail in Section~\ref{sec-ncp} (Appendix B)}

The NCP separates the \textbf{location} (\(\mu\)) and \textbf{scale}
(\(\sigma\)) of a hierarchical parameter from the individual-level
deviations (\(z_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\)).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Centered: theta\_j \textasciitilde{} normal(mu, sigma)  ← funnel when sigma → 0}
\CommentTok{// Non{-}Centered: theta\_j = mu + sigma * z\_j, z\_j \textasciitilde{} std\_normal()  ← always Gaussian}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The NCP is the single most impactful reparameterization for hierarchical
cognitive models. See Section~\ref{sec-ncp} for full details, the
K-simplex extension, and the Chapter 11 case study.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{8. Sum-to-Zero Constraint for Group
Effects}\label{sec-sum-to-zero}

\emph{Relevant context:
\href{https://avehtari.github.io/Bayesian-Workflow/park_rule/park_rule.html}{Vehtari
et al.~--- the park rule}}

The standard \textbf{corner constraint} for ANOVA-style group effects
fixes one group as the reference (its effect is zero) and estimates all
others relative to it. This is a valid identification strategy but it
creates an asymmetry: the reference group's effect is absorbed into the
intercept, making the intercept's prior inadvertently informative about
one specific group.

The \textbf{sum-to-zero constraint} (\(\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k = 0\)) is a
symmetric alternative. Every group effect is estimated relative to the
grand mean, the intercept retains a clean interpretation as the overall
mean, and priors on group effects are exchangeable.

\textbf{Stan implementation via QR decomposition:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{data}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{int}\NormalTok{\textless{}}\KeywordTok{lower}\NormalTok{=}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{\textgreater{} K;                   }\CommentTok{// number of groups}
  \DataTypeTok{matrix}\NormalTok{[K}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{, K] A;                 }\CommentTok{// sum{-}to{-}zero contrast matrix (precomputed in R)}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[K}\DecValTok{{-}1}\NormalTok{] alpha\_raw;            }\CommentTok{// K{-}1 free parameters}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{transformed parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{vector}\NormalTok{[K] alpha = A\textquotesingle{} * alpha\_raw; }\CommentTok{// K effects summing to zero}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  alpha\_raw \textasciitilde{} normal(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{);}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Build the contrast matrix in R:}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{\# Helmert{-}style QR contrast matrix enforcing sum{-}to{-}zero}
\NormalTok{make\_sum\_zero\_matrix }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(K) \{}
\NormalTok{  Q }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{qr.Q}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{qr}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{rep}\NormalTok{(}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, K)), }\AttributeTok{complete =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{)[, }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{]}
  \FunctionTok{t}\NormalTok{(Q)   }\CommentTok{\# (K{-}1) x K matrix: A \%*\% alpha = 0 iff sum(alpha) = 0}
\NormalTok{\}}

\NormalTok{A }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{make\_sum\_zero\_matrix}\NormalTok{(K)   }\CommentTok{\# pass this as data to Stan}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-tip-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-tip-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-tip-color}{\faLightbulb}\hspace{0.5em}{When to prefer sum-to-zero over corner constraints}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

Use sum-to-zero when (a) no group is a natural reference, (b) you want
exchangeable priors on all group effects, or (c) the intercept should
represent the grand mean rather than one group's baseline. The
constraint has no effect on the likelihood --- only on identifiability
and prior geometry.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{10. Probit Link --- Signal Detection
Theory}\label{sec-probit}

The \textbf{probit link} uses the standard Normal CDF \(\Phi\) in place
of the logistic function:

\[p = \Phi(d'), \qquad d' = \Phi^{-1}(p) \equiv \text{probit}(p)\]

Stan function: \texttt{Phi(x)} (Normal CDF), \texttt{Phi\_approx(x)}
(fast rational approximation).

The probit link is the natural choice whenever the cognitive model is
derived from \textbf{signal detection theory (SDT)}: the sensitivity
parameter \(d'\) is directly the standardized distance between signal
and noise distributions, and the criterion \(c\) is the decision
threshold in the same units.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\KeywordTok{parameters}\NormalTok{ \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ d\_prime;     }\CommentTok{// sensitivity (positive = above chance)}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ criterion;   }\CommentTok{// decision criterion}
\NormalTok{\}}
\KeywordTok{model}\NormalTok{ \{}
\NormalTok{  d\_prime   \textasciitilde{} normal(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{);}
\NormalTok{  criterion \textasciitilde{} normal(}\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{);}
  \ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N) \{}
    \ControlFlowTok{if}\NormalTok{ (signal[i] == }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}
      \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ log(Phi( d\_prime/}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ {-} criterion));   }\CommentTok{// hit}
    \ControlFlowTok{else}
      \KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ log(Phi({-}d\_prime/}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ {-} criterion));   }\CommentTok{// correct rejection}
\NormalTok{  \}}
\NormalTok{\}}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\textbf{Logit vs.~probit in practice:} The two links are very similar
numerically (probit ≈ logit × 0.607), and prior sensitivity analyses
rarely distinguish them. Choose probit when the theoretical framework is
SDT and interpretability in \(d'\) units matters; choose logit for all
other binary-outcome models because \texttt{bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf} is
faster and numerically more stable.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\subsection{11. Correlation Matrices --- LKJ and
Cholesky}\label{sec-lkj}

\emph{Covered in Section~\ref{sec-cholesky} (Appendix B); introduced in
Chapter~\ref{sec-multilevel-models} (Chapter 6)}

The \textbf{LKJ distribution} is the natural prior for correlation
matrices. Its single parameter \(\eta\) controls how much mass is placed
near the identity matrix:

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 2\tabcolsep) * \real{0.5000}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
\(\eta\)
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Implied prior on correlations
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\(\eta = 1\) & Uniform over all valid correlation matrices \\
\(\eta = 2\) & Mild regularization toward identity (off-diagonal
correlations shrunk toward 0) \\
\(\eta \to \infty\) & All mass on the identity (zero correlations) \\
\end{longtable}

\textbf{Always} sample the Cholesky factor
\texttt{cholesky\_factor\_corr{[}K{]}\ L\_Omega} and apply the
\texttt{lkj\_corr\_cholesky} prior. Recover
\(\Omega = L_\Omega L_\Omega^\top\) in \texttt{generated\ quantities}.
See Section~\ref{sec-cholesky} for full code and a cost comparison.

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Part IV --- Numerical Stability}\label{sec-numerical-stability}

\subsection{10. Log-Space Arithmetic}\label{sec-log-space}

\emph{Used throughout: \texttt{log\_mix}, \texttt{log\_sum\_exp},
\texttt{log1m} in Chapters 8--11}

Computing probabilities directly (multiplying small numbers together,
then taking logs) leads to underflow for long trial sequences. Staying
in \textbf{log space} throughout avoids this.

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 4\tabcolsep) * \real{0.3333}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Operation
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Unstable form
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Stable Stan function
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
\(\log(p_1 + p_2)\) & \texttt{log(exp(lp1)\ +\ exp(lp2))} &
\texttt{log\_sum\_exp(lp1,\ lp2)} \\
\(\log(\pi p_1 + (1-\pi) p_2)\) &
\texttt{log(pi*exp(lp1)\ +\ (1-pi)*exp(lp2))} &
\texttt{log\_mix(pi,\ lp1,\ lp2)} \\
\(\log(1 + e^x)\) & \texttt{log(1\ +\ exp(x))} &
\texttt{log1p\_exp(x)} \\
\(\log(1 - e^x)\) (requires \(x < 0\)) & \texttt{log(1\ -\ exp(x))} &
\texttt{log1m\_exp(x)} \\
\(\log(1 - p)\) & \texttt{log(1\ -\ p)} & \texttt{log1m(p)} \\
\(\log \Phi(x)\) (Normal CDF) & \texttt{log(Phi(x))} &
\texttt{log(Phi\_approx(x))} or
\texttt{normal\_lcdf(x\ \textbackslash{}\textbar{}\ 0,1)} \\
\end{longtable}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\CommentTok{// Mixture likelihood in log space — numerically stable}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (i }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:N) \{}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lp1 = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | alpha);}
  \DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ lp2 = bernoulli\_logit\_lpmf(y[i] | nu);}
  \KeywordTok{target  +=}\NormalTok{ log\_mix(pi, lp1, lp2);   }\CommentTok{// never calls exp() on small values}
\NormalTok{\}}

\CommentTok{// Accumulated log{-}probability for a trajectory}
\DataTypeTok{real}\NormalTok{ log\_p = }\FloatTok{0.0}\NormalTok{;}
\ControlFlowTok{for}\NormalTok{ (t }\ControlFlowTok{in} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{:T)}
\NormalTok{  log\_p += log\_mix(pi[t], lp\_A[t], lp\_B[t]);}
\KeywordTok{target +=}\NormalTok{ log\_p;}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{tcolorbox}[enhanced jigsaw, toprule=.15mm, toptitle=1mm, bottomtitle=1mm, leftrule=.75mm, rightrule=.15mm, colframe=quarto-callout-warning-color-frame, left=2mm, colback=white, titlerule=0mm, coltitle=black, colbacktitle=quarto-callout-warning-color!10!white, opacityback=0, title=\textcolor{quarto-callout-warning-color}{\faExclamationTriangle}\hspace{0.5em}{When \texttt{log\_sum\_exp} is not available}, arc=.35mm, opacitybacktitle=0.6, breakable, bottomrule=.15mm]

For vectors: \texttt{log\_sum\_exp(v)} sums all elements of \texttt{v}
in log-space. For accumulation inside a loop: maintain a running
\texttt{log\_p} and add each term. Never compute
\texttt{sum(exp(log\_probs))} and then take the log --- this is the
numerically unstable version that Stan's log-space functions exist to
replace.

\end{tcolorbox}

\begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.5pt}\end{center}

\section{Quick Reference}\label{sec-reparam-quick-ref}

\begin{longtable}[]{@{}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}
  >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{(\linewidth - 6\tabcolsep) * \real{0.2500}}@{}}
\toprule\noalign{}
\begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Parameter type
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Constraint
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Recommended reparameterization
\end{minipage} & \begin{minipage}[b]{\linewidth}\raggedright
Chapter
\end{minipage} \\
\midrule\noalign{}
\endhead
\bottomrule\noalign{}
\endlastfoot
Probability / rate & {[}0, 1{]} & Logit → \texttt{real\ theta\_logit},
\texttt{inv\_logit} in TP & Ch 4 \\
Positive scale / rate & \textgreater{} 0 & Log →
\texttt{real\ log\_theta}, \texttt{exp} in TP & Ch 11 \\
{[}0,1{]} with mean+variance story & {[}0, 1{]} & Beta
mean+concentration (μ, κ) & Ch 10 \\
Two-feature attention weight & simplex{[}2{]} & Logit-normal NCP & Ch
11 \\
K-feature attention weight & simplex{[}K{]} & Stan \texttt{simplex}
(auto stick-break); NCP for hierarchical & Ch 11 \\
K-way choice probability & probability K-vector & Softmax of
unconstrained scores / \texttt{categorical\_logit} & Ch 9 \\
Individual params drawn from population & real & Non-centered:
\(z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\), \(\theta = \mu + \sigma z\) & Ch 6 \\
Between-subject standard deviation & \textgreater{} 0 &
\texttt{exponential(1)} or \texttt{normal(0,\ 0.5)\ T{[}0,{]}} & Ch 6 \\
Correlation matrix & positive-definite, diag = 1 & Cholesky factor + LKJ
prior & Ch 6 \\
Group effects (no natural reference) & sum-to-zero & QR contrast matrix
→ \texttt{A\textquotesingle{}\ *\ alpha\_raw} & App C §8 \\
SDT sensitivity & unconstrained & Probit: \texttt{Phi(d\_prime)} &
--- \\
Sum of log-probabilities & \((-\infty, 0]\) & \texttt{log\_sum\_exp},
\texttt{log\_mix}, \texttt{log1p\_exp} & Ch 8--11 \\
\end{longtable}
